13 Tips To Stop Being An Amateur Graphic Designer FOREVER

0:00 Study and learn each of these 13 graphic
0:02 design tips today to never create an
0:04 amateur design ever again. And that
0:07 starts with learning about something
0:08 called relief zones and how they affect
0:10 your work.
0:12 If everything's feeling a bit heavy on
0:14 your design, nothing will hit properly
0:17 to your audience or to the client.
0:19 Sometimes a poster or editorial spread
0:22 is jammed with big names, bold type,
0:24 high energy, and as a consumer of this
0:26 design, your brain will get tired.
0:29 Enter the cognitive relief zone. This
0:33 can be a low density section on your
0:35 layout. Maybe it's a warm gray block.
0:38 Maybe it's just a soft type, an empty
0:40 space. And the job here is to give the
0:42 mind a space to breathe. It helps reset
0:45 visual energy so the next loud moment
0:47 hits a bit harder.
0:49 And a pro tip here is that relief zones
0:52 don't mean white space. They mean
0:54 intentional calm. So you can use warm
0:57 tone, soft structure, a limit of
0:58 contrast. It's the visual version of
1:01 silence in music.
1:07 Here's the messy museum exhibition
1:09 poster. We have a giant title, three
1:12 taglines, two images, a sponsor, creator
1:15 names, dates, hours, map icon, QR,
1:18 social headlines, 12 micro choices.
1:22 That's just too much. So, I'm going to
1:23 cut things down to four decisions.
1:26 What is it? The exhibition title,
1:30 what to look at, one hero artwork, when
1:34 the dates, everything else in a
1:36 proximity group. Everything else gets
1:39 grouped into a tiny footer strip. The
1:41 sponsors go monotone in one line.
1:44 And now for the 10-second test. Show
1:47 your graphic design to a fresh pair of
1:49 eyes and ask, "What is the one idea on
1:51 this design?" You want something like
1:53 the Bow House retrospective at the
1:55 design museum October 12th to January
1:58 5th. If they say anything broader or get
2:01 stuck listing extras, we cut again. The
2:04 goal is fewer decisions blocking the
2:06 main gist of the design and you need to
2:08 consider the CTA which of course on this
2:10 is simply to inform people of when,
2:13 where, and so on.
2:15 Graphic design is a lot about rhythm and
2:18 in a big way spatial rhythm.
2:21 Let's say you've got a large image block
2:23 top left of your poster with 60 pixels
2:26 of a margin top and left. Now further
2:29 down in the layout, you've got a quote
2:31 block. Try echoing that 60 pixel margin
2:34 top and left again down here. The viewer
2:37 doesn't know what you did, but they feel
2:39 it. This is a spatial echo. It's when
2:43 you repeat spacing from one part of your
2:45 layout in a totally separate area, and
2:47 it creates this kind of subconscious
2:49 structure like the design is actually
2:51 breathing in tempo.
2:54 Now, another example would be to imagine
2:56 that you're working on a record vinyl
2:58 sleeve cover design. The artist's name
3:00 is spaced exactly 40 pixels from the
3:03 bottom on the front cover. But flipping
3:06 things over to the back cover, instead
3:08 of guessing your spacing for the track
3:10 list, use the same 40 pixels from the
3:12 top for the title. And then we can
3:14 double up that 40 pixels to make 80
3:17 pixels for the song name after the
3:19 title. No lines needed, no rules, just a
3:23 repetition. And this is harmony through
3:25 invisible rhythm. And then of course at
3:28 the bottom a logo or a motif can be 40
3:31 pixels from that bottom edge.
3:37 So you're designing for a client's
3:39 Instagram account. You've got what
3:42 1080x350
3:44 pixels to work with and the audience
3:46 notoriously swipes quite fast on IG.
3:49 That means your hierarchy needs to be
3:51 vertical with quick entry points, clear
3:54 rhythm, and strong anchors in the first
3:57 third of the image. But on LinkedIn, the
4:00 layout shifts. You'll need more
4:02 breathing space and a better use of
4:04 white space to hold attention in scroll
4:06 stop conditions. And the first skill
4:09 here is layout fluency. It is that
4:12 ability to adapt a design across
4:15 different platforms, different contexts.
4:17 And that's actually it's quite a rare
4:19 skill in designers this day and age.
4:21 It's something hugely important.
4:23 However, learn what type of layout
4:26 structures thrive on each platform.
4:28 Stacked verse horizontal, fast versus
4:31 slow pacing, compact versus expansive
4:33 composition. What you're developing here
4:36 is platform fluency. And in 2025,
4:39 designers who can adapt layouts with
4:41 platform first logic will lead the pack.
4:43 And that's because many clients,
4:45 especially the higherp paying ones,
4:47 don't just want one great design. They
4:49 want a system that works everywhere
4:51 fluently.
4:58 Next, we're looking at something so cool
5:00 and yet so easy to use. We're going to
5:03 create one isolation vontoff aspect to
5:06 our graphic design workflow.
5:08 And so on this album cover, I'll make
5:10 the band name The Star using exactly one
5:13 isolation tactic. Option A is a unique
5:17 hue, so everything else is muted, but
5:19 the title with its own isolation hue.
5:22 Option B, unexpected scale, so oversize
5:25 the title so it bleeds to the edge.
5:29 Option C, subtle motion. And so for
5:31 prints, that could be like a motion blur
5:33 or some kind of trail.
5:36 Now, you can create three artboards or
5:38 three canvases side by side and then lay
5:40 each design out against each other. This
5:43 will let you see which one works best.
5:45 And doing this helps you create a clear
5:47 and concise focal point which drags a
5:50 viewer right into our design. But this
5:53 is only the start of the journey on a
5:55 graphic design. So graphic designers who
5:57 have transcended the amateur level of
5:59 thinking know that every strong layout
6:01 tells a threebeat story because that's
6:03 how the eye makes sense of a page. It
6:06 lands, it verifies, and then hopefully
6:08 it acts. So on a blood donation
6:11 billboard, put the entry where left to
6:14 right readers naturally start, so top
6:16 left, and make it the highest value
6:18 contrast and the largest type on the
6:20 board.
6:23 Keep it short, so ideally under seven
6:25 words, but of course it depends what
6:27 you're working with from the client. And

14:18 shape psychology, we absolutely have to
14:21 100% be clear on what each shape evokes
14:24 psychologically speaking. And first we
14:27 have the geometric shapes. And we're
14:29 going to start with circles which are
14:31 very heavily psychological in graphic
14:33 design. They have no beginning or no
14:36 end. They are symmetrical, smooth, and
14:38 enclosed. Circles are often associated
14:41 with unity, wholeness, and harmony. They
14:44 feel comforting, stable, and protective
14:46 because of their infinite form. Pepsi
14:49 circular logo tries to convey
14:52 friendliness and community matching its
14:54 inclusive, refreshing brand identity,
14:56 and car brands often use circles in the
14:59 logos because of the secure and safety
15:01 factor. Next, we have squares. Now,
15:04 squares represent stability,
15:06 reliability, and professionalism. They
15:09 often convey a sense of order,
15:11 structure, and trustworthiness.
15:13 Microsoft's Square logo shows a brand's
15:15 four core products while reinforcing a
15:18 sense of structure, and consistency, and
15:20 this is ideal for tech giants.
15:24 Triangles are quite interesting as they
15:26 can represent a few different things
15:28 depending on which way they are
15:29 pointing. Triangles can signify action,
15:32 energy, or movement depending on their
15:34 orientation.
15:36 Upward triangles can suggest stability
15:38 and progress, while downward triangles
15:40 can imply instability or focus. The play
15:44 button icon, typically a triangle
15:46 pointing right, universally conveys
15:48 movement and forward progression, and
15:50 this enhances its function for media and
15:52 entertainment purposes.
15:55 Like squares, rectangles feel sturdy and
15:58 dependable, but add a kind of
16:00 directional elements that can lead the
16:01 viewer's eye. They feel secure and
16:04 organized.

16:08 border frames its content with a sense
16:10 of exploration and focus, encapsulating
16:12 the brand's emphasis on storytelling.
16:16 Now, hexagons imply connection and

16:28 represent the collaboration that does
16:30 define the brand, and this hints at a
16:32 teamwork and communication element.
16:35 Ovals feel organic and feel gentle,
16:37 often symbolizing motion or the flow of
16:40 time, and that's with a calming and

16:54 And that moves us briskly into the next

22:58 And now to dispel the myth that shape
23:00 psychology is a bunch of hoie and
23:02 doesn't have any place or relevance in
23:04 reality and graphic design because it
23:06 does. Now it's been shown in scans that
23:09 the brain has dedicated areas for
23:10 processing basic visual elements
23:13 including shapes. The primary visual
23:15 cortex and regions of the optical and
23:17 parietal loes help us interpret shapes
23:20 and assign meaning based on learned and
23:22 intuitive associations.
23:25 For example, fMRI scans show heightened
23:28 brain activity in the amydala, a center
23:30 for emotions, and that's when viewing
23:32 angular or sharp shapes. This supports
23:35 the idea that these shapes evoke
23:37 stronger, often more intense responses.
23:40 But get this, the influence of logo
23:43 shapes, including those of brands like
23:45 Pepsi, has been studied to understand
23:47 consumer perception. Research often
23:50 cited in this field includes a 2015
23:52 study by Dr. Milica Mormon and Dr.
23:55 Andrew A. Mitchell and this examined how
23:58 shape affects brand perception. Their
24:01 study shapes, colors, and brand identity
24:04 exploring the role of visual properties
24:06 in consumers which was published in
24:08 journal of marketing research found that
24:10 circular logos tend to evoke feelings of
24:13 warmth, community, and harmony whereas
24:15 angular logos were associated with
24:17 durability, strength, and
24:19 professionalism.
24:21 But the researchers of a different study
24:22 in 2013 which was published in the
24:25 journal of experimental social
24:26 psychology presented participants with
24:29 images containing basic geometric shapes
24:32 such as circles, squares and triangles
24:34 and this was in neutral colors as
24:36 example black or gray. The simplicity of
24:38 the shapes helped isolate the emotional
24:40 impact specifically related to the form
24:43 rather than other design elements like
24:45 color or texture. Participants also
24:48 viewed a mix of smoothged and sharpedged
24:50 shapes to analyze the psychological
24:52 effects of curvature versus angularity
24:55 in design.
24:56 The primary tool for measuring emotion
24:59 was the positive and negative effect
25:01 schedule or panas scale. And this
25:03 allowed participants to rate how each
25:05 shape made them feel in terms of
25:07 emotions like calmness, excitement,
25:09 anxiety or trust.
25:11 This approach helped quantify emotional
25:14 reactions on a standard scale, making
25:16 the response comparable across
25:18 participants and cultures. The results
25:21 concluded that shapes do evoke specific
25:23 emotion that can transcend cultural
25:25 boundaries, though cultural nuances
25:28 influence how strongly these emotions
25:30 are felt.
25:31 They suggest that designers can utilize
25:33 these findings to create more targeted
25:35 visuals, choosing shapes that align with
25:37 the desired emotional impact of a brand
25:39 or message, such as circles for
25:41 inclusivity or squares for stability.
25:44 So, when you're next looking at a
25:46 graphic design, or better yet, looking
25:48 at your screen while designing your next
25:50 project, really do consider how shape
25:52 impacts psychology.

25:56 and shapes are some of the most

26:02 to our audiences. And they won't be able

27:42 with just a few pixels.

27:47 impossible or even crazy, but hear me
27:50 out. It's about translating brand
27:53 values, product features, or emotions

28:01 a designer. So what is the core message?

1:39:19 really nothing to lose here. Actually
1:39:20 making tweaks and then saving those
1:39:22 changes is going to save you more time
1:39:24 and also more of your session limit than
1:39:26 if you're just having a conversation
1:39:27 back and forth with Claude, because we
1:39:29 all know how that can go if you just
1:39:30 shoot off prompt after prompt after
1:39:32 prompt after prompt. Because also think
1:39:33 about the fact that if you were doing
1:39:35 this in Claude code and you had all
1:39:36 these tweaks that you wanted to play
1:39:37 with, this is basically like, "Hey, I
1:39:38 wonder what it would look like if the
1:39:40 background was blue." And then you would
1:39:41 have to go submit the prompt, Claude
1:39:43 code would change the background, it
1:39:45 would put it on a local host, and then
1:39:46 you'd look at it and be like, "Ah, I
1:39:47 actually don't like that." So, then you
1:39:49 would have to like revert back to the
1:39:51 old version. And so, that could
1:39:52 potentially be two prompts. Whereas
1:39:54 here, we can see so much stuff and test
1:39:56 it out in a way that doesn't actually
1:39:58 make us send more prompts and send more
1:40:00 prompts. Now, one thing I noticed is I

1:40:02 button. In Lovable, you can just like
1:40:04 revert back to previous prompts, which

1:40:22 not doing it. So, once you're good with

1:50:19 on a paid plan. You cannot be on a free
1:50:20 plan. So, let's talk about the two meter
1:50:22 system. First of all, Claude design has
1:50:24 its own weekly quota. So, if I go back

1:50:31 all models, you have Sonnet only, and

1:50:35 And this is what I blew through pretty
1:50:36 quick. And so, what do the burn rates
1:50:38 look like? People have been complaining
1:50:39 that they're getting their session eaten
1:50:41 up really quick. So, if you're on 20
1:50:43 bucks a month, after one big website and
1:50:45 one big design system, you might already
1:50:47 be almost done. If you're on the max
1:50:49 five times plan, then you're going to
1:50:50 have, you know, maybe four to five
1:50:51 series prompts and you might be able to,
1:50:53 you know, hit the limit pretty quick.
1:50:55 And then if you're on the 20x plan,
1:50:56 which I'm on, I was able to build
1:50:58 probably
1:50:59 a few different video projects and a few
1:51:01 different websites and then I started to
1:51:02 get really close to the end of my limit.
1:51:04 So, what actually drains your limits the
1:51:05 fastest? Well, there are a few things.
1:51:07 First of all, if you do every single
1:51:09 thing with 4.7, then you're obviously
1:51:10 going to be eating more tokens than if
1:51:12 you were on Sonnet or even Haiku for
1:51:14 some very minor changes. I personally
1:51:16 haven't done this myself. I've haven't
1:51:18 really jumped into the wireframes. I
1:51:20 always just started with a high
1:51:22 fidelity, but I have seen some people on
1:51:24 X talking about that if you do the
1:51:26 wireframe first, you're able to
1:51:27 basically convert it better to high
1:51:30 fidelity with less iteration. And I
1:51:32 honestly that makes sense. I mean, if
1:51:33 you're in Claude Code, I always talk
1:51:34 about how the planning phase is so
1:51:36 important. So, get clear on your idea,
1:51:38 give as much context up front as far as
1:51:40 like a sketch, and, you know, maybe like
1:51:42 that Claude spec that we saw earlier in
1:51:44 the live example. This information was
1:51:47 able to make it so much easier for
1:51:48 Claude design to build what we wanted on
1:51:50 the first try rather than having to
1:51:52 iterate so much cuz that's where you're
1:51:53 going to burn a lot of tokens. Also,
1:51:54 setting up a design system without a
1:51:55 URL. In general though, design systems
1:51:58 will eat a lot. So, if I go back to the
1:52:01 um app and I go into like creating a
1:52:04 design system, which if I went right
1:52:05 here,
1:52:06 what this does is it lets you set up a
1:52:08 system for your brand. So, your name,
1:52:10 you can put in a GitHub repo, you can
1:52:12 put in other folders, other files, and
1:52:14 other logos and assets, and it will
1:52:16 create you basically a template so that
1:52:18 if you ever want to make anything,
1:52:20 videos, slides, websites, you can create
1:52:23 it with that brand. So, for example, I
1:52:25 made one with AI Automation Society,
1:52:26 which is what you see right here. So,
1:52:28 every time I use this design system,
1:52:30 it's going to make everything with that
1:52:31 typography, with those colors, with that
1:52:33 background, the color scheme, all that
1:52:35 kind of stuff. Now, when you create a
1:52:36 design system, once you put in all this
1:52:38 information, it has to ingest it and it
1:52:40 has to create that actual spec. And this
1:52:42 can eat a decent amount of tokens
1:52:44 because it's going to take like 10 to 15
1:52:45 minutes. Now, what's interesting is if
1:52:47 you actually export this,
1:52:49 you can put that into Claude Code and
1:52:51 you can get all of that value already.
1:52:53 If you come into your design system, you
1:52:54 can see the type of information that's
1:52:55 in here. You can iterate on it. But, you
1:52:57 can actually go ahead and share this
1:52:58 with your team, but you could also just
1:53:00 download this as a zip. And then you
1:53:02 could give that zip to Claude Code sort
1:53:03 of like we did earlier. And now you
1:53:05 could say, "Hey, this is the design
1:53:05 system for AI Automation Society. Help
1:53:07 me build some other stuff." And you
1:53:08 could do that then in Claude Code as
1:53:10 well. It's really nothing new. The whole
1:53:11 concept of having like a design system
1:53:13 or a design MD, it's not a new concept.
1:53:15 It's just kind of the way that it's
1:53:17 packaged up here in Claude design makes
1:53:20 it a lot easier. And then of course,
1:53:21 like I showed you guys, the ability to
1:53:22 tweak stuff in the canvas. We talked
1:53:24 about this, right? Being inside of a
1:53:26 project and using tweaks and using, you
1:53:28 know, the comments and the edits, that's
1:53:30 going to be more precise and it's going
1:53:32 to give Claude a better idea of what you
1:53:33 actually want to change. So, then it
1:53:34 doesn't go down the wrong path and waste
1:53:36 your time and your session. And then
1:53:37 just think about the fact that
1:53:39 you might get into some really, really
1:53:41 long threads.
1:53:42 Um I haven't proven this for a fact, but
1:53:44 just based on the way that Claude uses
1:53:45 tokens, what I found is that if you are
1:53:47 in a project, right, and it's getting
1:53:49 really, really long and you're still not
1:53:51 getting where you want, maybe you want
1:53:52 to export this and then open up a fresh
1:53:55 session and you're kind of not really
1:53:57 starting at square one again. You're
1:53:59 iterating upon something that's already
1:54:01 been built and you're making some
1:54:02 changes, but now you have fresh context
1:54:04 because this is likely all starting to
1:54:06 pollute the context window and it might
1:54:08 be leading to some rot and it might be
1:54:10 leading to tokens becoming uncached and
1:54:12 then you're basically just processing
1:54:14 them again. So, that's something to keep
1:54:16 in mind. Now, you could also come in
1:54:17 here and do a {slash} clear and it says,
1:54:19 "Hey, cleared. We're ready for the next
1:54:20 instruction." So, that's something else
1:54:21 to also play around with, but I can't
1:54:23 say with certainty that that actually
1:54:24 clears all of this from the context
1:54:25 window. I haven't seen for sure yet. So,
1:54:28 okay, so best practices to stretch
1:54:29 limits. We kind of already talked a lot
1:54:30 about that. I just showed you guys the
1:54:32 zip export. When you're zipping either
1:54:34 your actual project or a design system.
1:54:36 We talked about model by stage. So,
1:54:38 maybe you can use Opus 4.7 at the
1:54:39 beginning to plan a little bit or when
1:54:41 you need some really legit changes, but
1:54:44 switch over to Sonnet 4.6. I was on
1:54:46 Sonnet 4.6 for um a lot of the different
1:54:48 builds and it was able to generate the
1:54:50 video and it was able to create the
1:54:51 animations and create the slide decks
1:54:53 and it was working just fine. I think at
1:54:54 the end of the day, like Sonnet 4.6 is
1:54:56 really good. And if you are super clear
1:54:59 and super specific about what you want,
1:55:01 then you can probably get a lot of the
1:55:02 way done with Sonnet 4.6. So, it's
1:55:05 definitely worth testing out. And then
1:55:06 some other real quick tips, one visual
1:55:08 dimension per prompt. So, don't try to
1:55:10 throw in a mega prompt with, "Hey,
1:55:12 change
1:55:13 XYZ, ABC, DEF, and G." Now, I know that
1:55:17 some people might disagree with this
1:55:18 because sometimes in Claude Code, you
1:55:19 want to send off one big prompt so that
1:55:21 it can create its to-do list and it can
1:55:23 start to iterate. But, what I found here
1:55:25 is that when I ask for multiple
1:55:26 different big changes in one prompt, it
1:55:29 only actually does like one or two of
1:55:31 them well and the rest of them just kind
1:55:32 of get disregarded. So, I've found more
1:55:34 success and when I talk about being
1:55:36 efficient with my prompting, I found
1:55:38 more success doing one main big change
1:55:41 per prompt. It's also good to do
1:55:42 negatives. So, tell it what you don't
1:55:44 want. Tell it if you don't want these
1:55:45 fonts or if you don't want these color
1:55:47 schemes, that way you don't have to
1:55:49 correct it later. You can also reference
1:55:50 real things. So, saying something like
1:55:52 linear 2023 with higher density rather
1:55:54 than just saying, "Hey, make this
1:55:55 clean." or make this minimalistic.
1:55:57 Obviously, pointing and commenting on
1:55:58 things and drawing on things and then
1:56:01 asking for prototypes, flows, demos. And
1:56:03 then of course, you can go ahead and
1:56:04 export that entire HTML. You could
1:56:06 export it to Figma, you could export it
1:56:08 to Canva, you could export it to
1:56:09 PowerPoint. You can get it out of there
1:56:12 and then make the tweaks you need to if
1:56:13 you don't actually need to use Claude
1:56:14 design for things that you could do
1:56:16 pretty easily and pretty quickly by
1:56:17 hand. So, some other things to keep in
1:56:18 mind, file uploads. So,
1:56:21 I have been able to upload MP4s, like
1:56:23 you guys have seen. I've been able to
1:56:25 edit some videos, but there is a cap and
1:56:26 I think it might be 30 megabytes, it
1:56:28 might be 40, it might be a little more,
1:56:30 but if you try to go and upload like a
1:56:32 5-minute video, it's probably not going
1:56:33 to let you.
1:56:35 There also might have just been a bug
1:56:36 because this is in research preview and
1:56:37 because everyone's using it. But, for
1:56:39 the most part, you can upload lots of
1:56:41 different assets and the more things
1:56:43 that you're able to upload right away,
1:56:44 the more context it's going to have and
1:56:45 the less you're going to have to repeat
1:56:46 yourself and fix things. And then here
1:56:49 are just some other quick limitations to
1:56:51 flag. Not going to read all these off
1:56:53 cuz I feel like I've hit on a lot of
1:56:54 these already. But, take a quick pause
1:56:56 if you want to look at this. And like I
1:56:58 said, that free doc that I drop in the
1:57:00 community
1:57:02 will have a lot more of this kind of
1:57:03 stuff as well.
1:57:04 All right. So, that is going to do it
1:57:05 for today. Now, what you can do next is
1:57:08 watch this video right up here where I
1:57:09 talk about other website hacks and
1:57:11 specifically in Claude Code. Because
1:57:12 what might happen is if you burn through
1:57:14 your limit in Claude design, but you
1:57:16 want to keep iterating, you want to keep
1:57:17 building, just do what I showed you.
1:57:18 Export your Claude design project, get
1:57:20 it into Claude Code, and now you can
1:57:22 just keep working on Claude Code because
1:57:23 you have more usage. And then just wait
1:57:25 till your usage resets on design and
1:57:27 then take it back into design. But, that
1:57:28 is going to do it for today's
1:57:30 masterclass. I hope that you guys
1:57:31 enjoyed. And if you did, please give it
1:57:33 a like. It helps me out a ton. Don't
1:57:34 forget to join my free school community
1:57:36 if you want to check out the resource
1:57:37 guide from this video as well as every
1:57:39 other free resource that I've ever given
1:57:40 out on YouTube. Once again, that is
1:57:42 completely free to join. So, go ahead
1:57:44 and consider subscribing if this helped
1:57:46 and check out a ton of the other videos
1:57:47 on my channel where I break down very
1:57:49 similar stuff. So, as always, I
1:57:51 appreciate you guys making it to the end
1:57:52 of the video and I will see you on the
1:57:53 next one.
1:57:54 Thanks, everyone.


---

Design Custom Websites in Claude Code Based on AI-Generated Inspiration

0:00 So, I have an event coming up on the
0:01 26th of May. I use Claude to kind of
0:04 help me generate a text. I obviously
0:06 didn't like the design that Claude gave
0:08 me for a poster, and Canva is still a
0:10 very template-first tool. You browse and
0:13 you look for something that looks close
0:14 enough, and then you're back to that
0:16 manual design process. But, what if you
0:18 can just grab this text, find a
0:20 reference like something like this, and
0:22 then eventually come up with your own
0:23 custom design based on that inspiration,
0:25 but with that original text that I
0:27 needed for my own design? And that's
0:29 what Vizual AI does. It doesn't just
0:32 give you the nice output, but it also
0:34 gives you the ability to edit the text
0:36 over here, continue chatting with an AI
0:38 to further improve your design, and
0:40 eventually grab this frame and export it
0:42 as an HTML file, which you can then
0:45 eventually bring into Claude code and
0:47 build out your own custom website based
0:50 on that initial design that Vizual gave
0:52 you. And guys, by the way, my name is
0:53 Lucas, and if you're into these types of
0:54 topics, please feel free to join my
0:56 Discord community. Link is down below
0:58 where the Creator Network we get
0:59 together every single weekday to talk
1:00 about different tools and topics. So,
1:02 yeah guys, if you want to join, link is
1:04 down below. All right guys, so what
1:05 actually is Vizual AI? Vizual is a
1:08 content-first design tool, and I
1:09 actually want to break down what that
1:10 means. Like I said in the beginning, if
1:12 you were to go to canva.com, you have a
1:15 bunch of templates that you can explore.
1:17 Let's say for example, we we like this
1:18 one.
1:19 We can click on customize a template.
1:21 And once we're in, we can, you know,
1:22 change the text, we can change the
1:24 images, we can even ask Canva to do
1:26 certain changes for this template, but
1:28 we can't really like reference this
1:30 template and start from something
1:32 scratch. With Vizual, it's actually a
1:33 little bit different. We start with the
1:35 content rather than choose a template
1:37 first, and then change the content at
1:39 the end. You can paste in your words,
1:41 you can paste in docs, reference
1:43 designs. You just describe what you
1:45 need, and this tool builds it. And as
1:47 you can see down here, it supports
1:48 different formats for social media, for
1:51 marketing, for print. You can create
1:53 LinkedIn carousels, Instagram carousel,
1:55 basically every single marketing asset
1:57 that you would need. All right, so I'm
1:59 going to walk you through a few examples
2:01 and just pay attention to two things.
2:02 How fast the output gets there and how
2:04 much control you have once the design is
2:07 generated. So, for example, when we're
2:08 going to go up here to the top left and
2:10 click on magic templates. And under
2:12 magic templates, you have a bunch of
2:15 different categories from social media
2:17 to album covers to health care uh
2:20 themes. Under social media, you have a
2:22 bunch of different examples of social
2:25 media posts that you can kind of copy
2:27 and remix. And this is like the cleanest
2:29 way to kind of understanding what Viso
2:31 does, so I'm starting here. And let's
2:32 say for example, we really like this
2:34 one. We can copy this style and then we
2:36 can paste that style in the prompt in
2:38 the original prompt box and also add in
2:41 extra text just to give it some more
2:43 context as to, you know, who the main
2:45 subject is, what the title should be,
2:47 what the main headline should be. And
2:49 then we want to paste that in the in the
2:50 original prompt box here in the main
2:52 site and also add an extra text to kind
2:55 of give Viso additional context as to
2:58 who the main subject should be, the
2:59 titles, the headlines, the dates, the
3:01 background details. And then again, you
3:03 can go down here and you can adjust the
3:04 size. I'm just going to keep this to
3:06 auto and then we can go ahead and click
3:07 on generate design. And once we hit
3:09 generate design, this is the first
3:12 result that we get. So, as you can see,
3:14 we have our date, we have our
3:15 sub-headline, we have our main headline,
3:17 we have the secondary headline, we have
3:19 the paragraph text over here. I can go
3:21 ahead and select certain things. I can
3:23 put my name over here if I wanted to. I
3:25 can delete certain things like this, for
3:26 example, move things around. I can
3:29 change the fonts of certain text over
3:31 here. And then again, if you want to,
3:33 you know, make certain changes, you can
3:35 do so over here in the chat. And if
3:36 you're done with your design, you can go
3:38 ahead and share this. I'm going to be
3:39 doing this in a couple of minutes. But
3:41 for example number two, I'm going to
3:42 start working with a
3:44 much more complicated piece of text. It
3:46 has two pages, it has multiple
3:48 paragraphs, it has a lot of context. And
3:50 then what I'm going to do is I'm going
3:51 to go back to the prompt and import this
3:54 document. Just going to write something
3:55 simple, create a multi-page deck for
3:57 this PDF document. We're going to make
3:59 sure that we have multi-page brochure
4:00 selected over here. And for the aspect
4:02 ratio, I'm going to choose landscape.
4:04 And what we get is the following. We get
4:06 four different pages, the genetic shift.
4:08 We have page number one, page number
4:09 two, and then we have the back cover.
4:11 But what if we wanted to make the style
4:14 of this this multi-page brief that we
4:16 just created and match it to this
4:18 poster, right? Because it's all going to
4:19 be in the same brand. Well, what we can
4:21 do is we can reference that design. So
4:22 you can go over here and again click on
4:24 share and export this as a PNG. Then you
4:27 can just simply add that to the chat
4:29 over here and say, "Please adjust the
4:30 design to match this style." And lo and
4:33 behold, we have our new design with our
4:34 new pages. So actually, they're the same
4:36 pages, but just the design the new
4:38 design has been applied. So as you can
4:40 see, um basically new font, new colors,
4:43 same layout, just this new adjustment
4:46 that was brought in from that poster
4:48 that we created earlier. And again, just
4:50 like with the poster, everything is, you
4:52 know, you can edit anything. So you we
4:54 can click on this. We can change the
4:56 font, we can change the size, we can
4:58 make it bold, italic, change the color,
5:01 you know, pure control for, you know,
5:03 for customizing everything. Now for the
5:04 final example, what I want to do is I
5:06 have this text that I want to convert
5:08 into a LinkedIn carousel to share with
5:11 my LinkedIn network that are local and,
5:14 you know, want to be motivated to to
5:16 join this event that I'm hosting. And
5:17 this text was generated by AI, so I want
5:19 to simple way to kind of convert that AI
5:23 text into an actual LinkedIn carousel.
5:27 And what I can do here is again, I can I
5:29 can, you know, download this into a PDF
5:32 file. And then we can go back into
5:35 Vizard and select the carousel and then
5:37 we can obviously import the content from
5:40 our docs. And once we have that, we can
5:42 select social media and then scroll down
5:44 to where we see LinkedIn. So we have
5:46 LinkedIn post. and you can also select
5:49 this option over here, which basically
5:51 gives you like a
5:53 a template of how you can structure each
5:55 slide, but I'm just going to start this
5:57 from scratch. I'm going to go ahead and
5:58 click on generate design. And for the
6:00 first look, it looks really good. I
6:01 mean, obviously it doesn't have our
6:03 design
6:05 you know, our original design style with
6:07 the colors and all that applied to this,
6:10 but it looks good. It gives me a good
6:11 direction, gives me some good ideas as
6:14 to, you know, what design we can we can
6:16 eventually do.
6:18 We have like little posted notes that I
6:19 like over here. But then again, just
6:21 like with the other one, make the design
6:23 fit the style, add that reference that
6:25 we saved that we exported from that
6:27 original poster. And then as you can
6:29 see, we have our new design applied to
6:30 it. Beautiful stuff, new font, we have
6:33 our image from before. You can scroll
6:35 down. Matches the same style that we
6:37 want. We have these like new gray
6:39 sections that I really like. Lean in or
6:41 get left behind. Rewire your workflow,
6:43 which is basically like the call to
6:45 action. And perfect. So, we have some
6:47 cool designs that I like. Now, what I'm
6:49 going to do is I'm going to actually
6:50 select certain frames like this one and
6:53 like this one. And then we're going to
6:55 go ahead and click on share and click on
6:57 export. And we can manually export the
6:59 ones that we want to, you know, export.
7:01 So, I'm going to choose these. Let's say
7:03 these three over here and select the
7:06 format to be HTML. And let's go ahead
7:09 and export this. And then inside of
7:11 CloudCode, you can start up a new
7:12 session. Again, you just want to go over
7:14 here and you want to select the folder
7:15 where you want to work in. And then as
7:17 you can see over here, the first thing
7:18 that I did was I just referenced that
7:21 file. I literally just attached it over
7:23 here to the to the prompt.
7:25 And I said, "Can you create a landing
7:27 page based on the contents and design
7:29 from this?" And literally, I'm not
7:30 joking, the first try we get this, which
7:33 looks amazing, really. Maybe some of the
7:36 fonts are wrong, but all in all the
7:37 design looks really, really fantastic.
7:40 And again, over here if you want to
7:41 change certain things, we can go back to
7:43 Vizzle and select this font that I
7:45 really like, the VT323. And we can say
7:47 change the header font to be VT323. And
7:50 then we start getting this new custom
7:52 font throughout our site. So yeah, guys,
7:54 that's pretty much the entire workflow
7:56 using Vizzle to look for inspiration and
7:58 to actually create designs based on your
8:01 content, going ahead and creating these
8:03 designs as you can see over here, and
8:04 then simply just exporting the designs
8:06 that you want and importing them into
8:09 CloudCode where you can then eventually
8:11 build up your own landing page or your
8:12 own web app based on that original
8:14 design. So it's a cool little like
8:15 CloudCode design hack that you can try
8:18 out today. So yeah, guys, if you have
8:19 any comments or questions, please let me
8:21 know down below. And like always, thank
8:22 you all so much for watching. Hope to
8:24 see you next time. Goodbye.

## Source: everything-you-need-to-know-about-mobile-app-ui-s-in-8-minutes-beginner-friendly.txt

- Original path: `addintional info to add\everything-you-need-to-know-about-mobile-app-ui-s-in-8-minutes-beginner-friendly.txt`
- Size: `9796` bytes
- SHA256: `A71350F72D3651C516FC9C4169501CFEBDFD9550942D1EE93147641753A8E37C`

---

Everything you need to know about Mobile App UI’s in 8 minutes (beginner friendly)

0:00 When it comes to UI, mobile is just so
0:02 different from desktop that it results
0:03 in some pretty questionable designs. So,
0:06 in this video, I'm going to show you how
0:07 to design your very first mobile UI
0:09 completely from scratch in a way that's
0:11 dynamic, purposeful, and effortless to
0:13 use. And by the end, you won't just have
0:15 a stunning mobile design. You'll
0:17 understand why the best apps are
0:18 designed the way they are. The first
0:20 difference of desktop to mobile is
0:23 obviously the navigation. Where we had
0:24 an expansive sidebar, we now definitely
0:27 do not. So, we have two options. If we
0:29 can consolidate our sidebar links down
0:31 to just a few key icons, we can go with
0:33 a nice bottom bar, which nowadays is
0:35 typically floating with the important
0:37 action broken out just like this. Five
0:39 links down here is pretty much the
0:41 definite limit, and three or four is
0:43 much more ideal. But, either way, this
0:45 means that we'll always maintain over a
0:47 44-pixel target since people do have fat
0:49 fingers. It's important to note too that
0:51 depending on the context you're in,
0:53 these actions can and do change, which
0:55 we'll touch on when we start building
0:56 out a few pages. That typically is the
0:59 standard. However, sometimes you just
1:01 have too many important things in the
1:02 sidebar to really fit things in here, in
1:04 which case another valid form of
1:06 navigation is to take the sidebar and
1:08 turn that into a whole page that sort of
1:10 becomes the home page. In this case,
1:12 we'll add in some recent notes to the
1:14 top and actions and counts to the right
1:16 side, so it doesn't feel so lopsided. It
1:19 also conveniently clears up the bottom
1:21 for a nice big search bar or action
1:22 button, which is what apps like Notion
1:24 do. Moving up to the top, a bell and
1:26 more menu is a good place to start, but
1:28 these actions are also highly contextual
1:30 based on the page. Now, when it comes
1:32 time to actually start building,
1:33 squishing things down and trying to fit
1:35 as much on the screen as possible is
1:37 pretty natural. This all looks fairly
1:39 normal, actually, until I zoom out and
1:41 show you the other normal-sized apps for
1:43 comparison. Despite our screen getting
1:46 much, much smaller, the type scale and
1:48 spacing remains relatively similar to
1:50 desktop, even getting a bit larger. iOS
1:52 actually has a base font size of 17
1:55 pixels, while macOS has a base of only
1:57 13 pixels. So, more often than not,
1:59 things actually get larger on these
2:01 smaller screens. And it's a tough
2:03 realization that on desktop, where you
2:04 could have had an action bar, a gallery
2:06 of recent notes, a full calendar, tasks,
2:09 and a scratch pad, now you can pick one
2:11 of those things. And because we have to
2:13 pick just one, the way we start to lay
2:15 out content starts to change a little.
2:17 Dashboards and desktop designs can lay
2:19 out content in two directions at the
2:21 same time. What that means is this
2:23 layout has two columns and three rows,
2:25 obviously extending in both directions.
2:28 However, on mobile, you can choose one
2:30 direction to move in per section. Either
2:32 you can stack each note vertically, or
2:34 you can make it horizontal off the page,
2:36 but of course, you can't have both like
2:37 you reasonably could on desktop. This is
2:40 a useful guideline when taking content
2:41 from dashboards and making it
2:43 mobile-optimized. And if you understand
2:46 that concept, things really start to
2:47 become a piece of cake. In addition,
2:49 ignoring our floating menus and actions,
2:51 there's really only four types of
2:53 building blocks for an app. The biggest
2:55 and most important are cards. Basically,
2:57 everything on here is a card in some way
2:59 or another because it allows us to
3:00 flexibly group content in place of white
3:03 space, which we don't typically have a
3:04 lot of here. The others are text or
3:06 links, of course, images, and inputs.
3:09 And of course, any of those can be put
3:11 into cards as well. One important note
3:13 about cards is to try and avoid
3:15 double-nesting them, kind of like this.
3:17 It's not always avoidable, but it
3:19 creates padding on padding, which really
3:21 restricts the space you have and ends up
3:23 cramping things. Often, you can group
3:25 with white space instead of a container
3:27 and somewhat alleviate this problem.
3:29 Because we are so space-constrained on
3:31 mobile apps, I'd like to introduce the
3:33 important idea that one screen does one
3:36 thing, and that's it. Obviously, the
3:38 very notable exception is our home
3:40 screen, but otherwise, the settings is
3:42 just settings, and the notes editor is
3:44 just a notes editor. We don't throw in
3:46 clutter like recent notes or suggested
3:48 templates at the top. Instead, whenever
3:50 you need to add something new, don't
3:52 reach for a different layout, reach for
3:53 a different page altogether. Though,
3:55 this example about templates brings up
3:57 an interesting point about context.
3:59 We're editing a note, so we don't want
4:01 to be ripped away to a new page to
4:02 select a template, but we don't have
4:04 enough room on screen. This is where
4:06 bottom sheets become super useful. This
4:09 is a bottom sheet. You've probably seen
4:11 it a bunch, and now we can simply add in
4:13 a title, search bar, and a check and X,
4:16 and all of our templates, too. These can
4:17 be any height, but they keep the user in
4:19 context and are easy to navigate with
4:21 gestures, which we'll talk about in just
4:24 a second. Another good example is the
4:25 typical use of this plus button, which
4:27 either opens a little menu or opens with
4:30 an input to just immediately start
4:32 typing. But, before we get to gestures,
4:34 sometimes when you're designing, you
4:36 need some inspiration. So, a tool I use
4:38 all the time and actually used to make
4:40 this video is Mobbin, the sponsor of
4:42 today's video. Mobbin curates hundreds
4:44 of thousands of videos, images, and user
4:47 flows, all from top apps like Notion,
4:49 Craft, Headspace, and many more. It
4:52 often helps me to see how top teams
4:53 design flows like onboarding or to check
4:56 out specific UI elements just like
4:58 bottom sheets. You can filter by
4:59 categories, screens, UI elements, and
5:01 user flows, so you can always find
5:03 what's relevant to you. If you're
5:05 interested, you can check out Mobbin
5:07 using my link in description to get 20%
5:09 off. Now, back to the video. Now,
5:11 gestures is where mobile apps start to
5:13 get really interesting. One of the most
5:15 common is swiping right to go back like
5:17 this. But, if we move the background
5:19 left by about 35% and then also animate
5:22 it right, we get a super smooth
5:24 transition. Same idea with our bottom
5:26 sheets. As the bottom sheet comes up, we
5:28 often zoom out the background and zoom
5:30 back in on swipe down. Swipes are
5:32 everywhere in modern mobile apps, so as
5:34 long as you educate the user on how to
5:36 use them, they can be relied on pretty
5:38 heavily. A good example is a handy swipe
5:40 up to search, which is used by Slack as
5:42 well as sort of by Apple, too. And I'd
5:45 be remiss if I didn't mention the long
5:47 press, the mobile equivalent of the
5:48 right click. It's typical to blur the
5:51 rest of the screen and just show the
5:52 actions in addition to some slight zoom
5:55 on the element itself. Or, we can take
5:56 it even a step further and turn this
5:58 into a more dynamic preview like how iOS
6:01 does it. Now, also because of our
6:03 limited space, actions need to come and
6:05 go as needed. They can't always be
6:07 persistent. For example, clicking into
6:09 one of our notes hides our navbar and
6:11 reveals other actions specific to note
6:13 editing, such as text formatting or
6:14 sharing up at the top. Or, when we go to
6:16 select templates, all of that is hidden
6:18 for really just two actions, a
6:20 confirmation button and an X. And of
6:22 course, how these actions animate in and
6:25 out is half the fun, too. Finally, what
6:27 we've designed here is an ideal state.
6:30 The app is filled with content, but
6:31 don't forget what the user sees the
6:33 first time they log in when there is no
6:35 content. We could have cards inviting
6:37 user to start adding in events, tasks,
6:39 and notes, but instead, let's draw
6:41 attention to the plus button, which is
6:43 our main action, and add in a
6:45 full-screen empty state to simplify
6:47 things. Then, we have a simple popover
6:49 instructing them on how things actually
6:52 work. But, there is also another type of
6:54 empty state, which is when we search for
6:56 a note, for example, and get no results.
6:58 Instead of just an instruction, we
7:00 should have some nice imagery, and we
7:02 need to acknowledge that there are no
7:03 notes matching the existing keywords,
7:06 and then perhaps give them a few
7:07 suggestions in case of a typo, and an
7:09 action to exit the empty state. There is
7:12 a ton of different apps and mobile
7:13 designs you could build, but this should
7:15 give you the foundation to start
7:17 creating some really pro-level app
7:19 designs super easily. If you're
7:21 interested in checking out Mobbin, it'll
7:23 be the very first link down below, and
7:24 the link to all of the Figma assets used
7:26 in this video will be down there, too.
7:28 Thank you so much for watching, and I'll
7:30 see you in the next one.

5:34 So let's get started with the first principle, balance.

8:05 You can see seamless patterns predominantly in interior design when

8:11 Next, we have rhythm.

29:01 Legibility is how a typeface functions.
29:04 It is a measure of how easy it is to distinguish letters or
29:09 words from one another and how easy blocks of text are read.
29:14 There are different factors like x-height, character, width, weight, and
29:19 other design traits that can determine if a font is legible.
29:22 Size, the smaller the type size, the more difficult it is to read,
29:27 especially if your audience includes children or elderly people.
29:31 Always consider who you're designing the content for.
29:35 Line length or column width, as a rule of thumb,
29:38 there should be between 45 and 70 characters per line.
29:43 When the column width is too narrow, it can result in many hyphenated words,
29:48 forcing the eyes to jump to the next line too often.
29:52 On the other hand, long lines can cause confusion for
29:55 the reader when they go from line to line.
29:58 Leading or line space, the amount of letting you will need in a text box is
30:02 based on the type size and the x-height of the font you're using.
30:07 To maximize readability, make sure there is enough line spacing,
30:10 because too little line spacing can result on the lines crashing into each other.
30:15 Tracking or letter spacing,
30:17 this refers to the space between a group of letters in a line of text.
30:22 Adjusting the tracking can enhance readability and
30:26 change the texture of a text box.
30:28 Kerning is the adjustment of the space between two individual characters
30:33 within a word.
30:34 It is mostly used in logos or headings to enhance legibility.
30:38 Alignments, in paragraphs of Western text,
30:41 alignment refers to the invisible vertical lines implied by a block of type.
30:47 When type is left-aligned, each new line begins from the same point on the x-axis.
30:52 Right alignment isn't used that often, and
30:55 center alignment should only be used for short form text.
30:59 Rags, these are the uneven vertical emergence on a block of
31:04 text when it is left-aligned or right-aligned.
31:08 You can fix them by hyphenating words where necessary or
31:11 doing a soft return for a new line break.
31:14 Rags are more about visual appearance, but
31:17 the reading phase needs to also be considered here.
31:20 Rivers, these are gaps that appear to run through a block of text,
31:25 which usually appear in justified text.
31:29 A common way to fix this is by unjustifying the text or
31:32 typesetting each line with hyphenation and soft returns.
31:37 Orphans and widows, an orphan is a paragraph opening line at
31:41 the beginning of a page or column that separated from the rest of the text.
31:46 And a widow is a single word or
31:48 line at the end of a paragraph that's separated from the rest of the text.
31:53 These two problems can be solved by adjusting the kerning or tracking or
31:58 by adding manual line breaks.
32:00 And last, font case.
32:02 Uppercase is when every character is capitalized in a sentence
32:07 Lower case is when no letters are capitalized.
32:10 Sentence case is when the first letter of the first word in
32:14 a sentence is capitalized.
32:17 And title case is the first letter of each word capitalized.
32:21 And that's it.

32:23 you learned about one of the most essential elements of graphic design.

59:49 today can be created by painting in Adobe Photoshop or feeding words to an AI.
59:54 Nowadays, we can take amazing photographs not only with cameras,
59:59 but also with our phones.
1:00:00 Connectivity has made it interesting for many designers to work remotely.
1:00:04 Adobe has made it easy for freelance designers to work anywhere in the world
1:00:09 while still collaborating on design files without being in house.
1:00:14 When it comes to fonts,
1:00:16 in the past we needed multiple refined files or multiple files.
1:00:20 Now we have variable fonts, this is the latest digital type addition,
1:00:25 where one font file contains multiple typographic styles that vary in
1:00:30 weighed with style and size.
1:00:32 And last technology has made the field if graphic design expand by
1:00:37 organically creating overlapping disciplines.
1:00:40 UI and UX wouldn't have happened without the Internet revolution.
1:00:44 The traditional graphic designer was known for
1:00:48 creating packaging or ads for marketing.
1:00:51 It now has evolved into a wide range of disciplines,
1:00:54 all which have communication and technology as the common denominator.
1:00:59 Graphic design is an essential tool for business growth and
1:01:03 it will continue to evolve as a result of advances in technology and online mediums.
1:01:09 I think it is important to emphasize here that graphic design has a purpose.
1:01:15 Every design problem has a brief to work off from and a problem to solve.
1:01:21 And that's what makes graphic design, graphic design.
1:01:24 In the next chapter,
1:01:25 we will take an overlook at everything you learned in this course.
1:01:30 Graphic design is the creation of visual content with a communication purpose.
1:01:35 You will learn about the different visual elements that are involved in creating
1:01:39 a design.
1:01:40 We looked at the basic principles of design so
1:01:43 you can organize the limits on the page color theory and its concepts and
1:01:48 an overview of typography, history and its classification.
1:01:52 We also looked at the different design disciplines that exist presently and
1:01:55 the more traditional ones.
1:01:57 You also saw examples of the different design choices you can make based on
1:02:01 the discipline.
1:02:02 To make your design process easier,
1:02:05 we show you tools that can help you have a seamless workflow.
1:02:09 We also show you everything from a subscription software alternatives.
1:02:13 Wood boarding and websites to help you with color development.
1:02:17 Last, we took a look at how technology has evolved and affected our industry.
1:02:22 While technology development has moved quickly,
1:02:25 we must remind ourselves the poor job of a graphic designer.
1:02:28 It all lies in a balance between making something look visually stunning or
1:02:32 organizing complex information that's being delivered in a comprehensible way.
1:02:36 At the end of the day, communicating with clarity is priority.
1:02:41 My name is Laura Keung from all of us Envato Tuts+,
1:02:44 we hope you enjoyed this course, and we'll see you in the next one.

How to Actually Learn UI/UX Design On Your Own (+ day in the life)

0:03 [Music]
0:12 So, I get this question all the time.
0:14 How did you learn UIUX design? How did
0:16 you get started? My college majors were
0:18 completely unrelated to the field of
0:19 design because I was a premed and a
0:21 studio art double major. I do think the
0:23 art major helped a little bit, but I
0:24 started learning design my last year of
0:26 college completely on my own. And that
0:28 resulted in me getting a design job
0:29 right out of college. And now years
0:31 later, after switching to software
0:32 engineering and then returning to
0:34 design, I've learned what actually
0:35 works. Now, of course, the job market
0:37 and everything might be a little bit
0:38 different right now, but I was able to
0:39 break into the world of design without a
0:41 degree in it. So, today I'm sharing what
0:43 I would do if I was starting over and
0:44 didn't know anything about UI or UX or
0:46 product design and wanted to learn.
0:48 Because honestly, if I could learn this
0:50 completely on my own, you definitely
0:51 can, too.

5:00 you design. It's the importance of
5:02 recognizing patterns. Most apps have
5:04 white backgrounds. Most buttons are
5:05 rounded. Most navigation is at the
5:07 bottom on mobile. Most primary actions
5:09 are on the right. These are not
5:10 accidents. These are all patterns that
5:12 users expect. A great example is a UX
5:14 rule called Fitz's law where buttons and
5:16 navigation on mobile are placed close to
5:18 where the thumb naturally sits. Learn
5:20 the rules first. Then once you
5:22 understand why they exist, you can break
5:23 them intentionally. But for now,
5:25 understand and practice the rules before
5:27 trying to stand out. Laws of UX is an

5:30 a really quick place to start and it has

15:05 because you'll have everything you need.

15:11 start out. This is also per project so
15:13 you can continue to add more projects if
15:14 needed. If you're a student with aedu
15:16 email, I think you get the pro plan for
15:18 free which is such a perk. Here are some
15:20 free UI kits to learn from. A big one is
15:22 the untitled UI kit. Apple's iOS design
15:24 kit. You can probably find the latest
15:26 one and that also goes for Google's
15:27 material design kit. Some free plugins
15:29 that I love to use. Unsplash for photos.
15:31 Iconify for all of my icon needs. Icons
15:34 8 for some more icons and a lot of
15:36 illustrations. Lauram Ipsum for some
15:38 filler copy and so so so much more.
15:40 Also, when you're taking a look at these
15:42 design kits, you can really look at how
15:44 components work, break them apart,
15:45 understand the spacing, the colors.
15:47 Again, it's really good for recognizing
15:49 patterns. It's also like reverse
15:51 engineering design.
15:53 [Music]
16:00 All right. So, let me touch more on
16:01 finding inspiration. It comes in all
16:04 forms. Watch a music video or a movie
16:06 scene that's beautiful. Go to the park
16:07 and look at the way the blue sky matches
16:09 so well with the white clouds and the
16:11 green trees. Go to the bookstore and
16:13 find some really good book covers with
16:14 really cool art styles and colors. Going
16:16 on Pinterest or Instagram and finding
16:18 some very aesthetic photos. The coffee
16:20 shop you go to might have some really
16:21 cool typography on their menu. Start
16:23 noticing design everywhere. The next
16:25 time you see something that you like,
16:26 take note of it and try to figure out
16:28 why you like it. Is it the colors, the
16:30 art style, the composition, the
16:31 typography, and use that to either
16:33 inspire or implement in your next
16:35 designs? Sometimes it might not look
16:36 good on a certain idea, but it could in
16:38 a future one. Also important,
16:40 recognizing design patterns for
16:41 different industries. This has a lot to
16:43 do with color theory as well. I've also
16:44 noticed that designing for corporate
16:46 versus non-corporate is a little bit
16:47 different. Some different industry
16:48 examples include designing for AI, which
16:50 usually includes purples and gradients
16:52 and a lot of moving elements for that
16:54 futuristic feel. Crypto loves very dark
16:57 themes with neon accents. Food apps use
16:59 a lot of warm colors like oranges, reds,
17:01 and yellows, which happen to be appetite
17:03 triggers, and financial apps need trust.
17:05 Blues, whites, and a good amount of
17:07 space. Not to mention the shopping
17:08 industry, which needs a sense of
17:10 urgency, which means a lot of countdowns
17:12 and some sales tax. There's also the
17:13 difference between web and mobile. And
17:15 with mobile, there's native iOS and
17:17 native Android. Design is a little bit
17:19 different for each one depending on your
17:20 platform. And also keep in mind the term
17:22 responsive design. Mobile design is
17:24 thumb friendly, limited space, quick
17:26 tasks. Web is mouse-driven, large
17:28 screens, complex workflows. iOS has very
17:31 specific guidelines and Android has
17:33 specific guidelines as well. Okay, so
17:35 let's talk about realistic timelines
17:36 because nobody really discusses this.
17:38 The easiest way to learn is to divide
17:40 each topic up and tackle a little bit
17:41 every day. Start with 20 minutes a day
17:43 if that's all you have learning about a
17:45 specific design topic or go full
17:47 immersion deep diving on a topic and
17:48 practicing for 6 to 8 hours if you are
17:50 in a rush to learn. I did both at
17:52 different times. Again, this does depend
17:54 on how fast you want or need to learn,
17:56 but again, I promise you, if I can do
17:58 it, you can do it as well. For me, I
18:00 mainly used reading, watching, and
18:02 learning by doing because that's how I
18:03 learned best. And of course, social
18:05 learning with Christian, online
18:06 communities, and my first design job. I
18:08 also wanted to save you from some of the
18:10 mistakes I made. One, learning too many
18:12 tools at once. Just pick one. Figma,
18:15 maybe Fig Jam for whiteboarding, and
18:16 that's it. Two, skipping fundamentals.
18:19 Typography, color theory, spacing. They
18:21 might seem boring and basic, but they
18:23 are very essential. Number three,
18:24 designing in isolation. Share your work
18:26 early and often. It's scary, but it is
18:28 very, very necessary. Number four,
18:30 thinking you need to know everything
18:32 before starting. You learn by doing and
18:33 not preparing to do it. Number five,
18:35 perfectionism paralysis. Set those time
18:38 limits. Number six, not documenting your
18:40 process. Number seven, only copying
18:42 without understanding why. Eight,
18:44 ignoring accessibility. Nine, comparing
18:46 yourself to senior designers who have
18:47 been doing this for years. And last but
18:49 not least, and my number one thing,
18:51 don't let the imposter syndrome get to
18:52 you, especially when starting out. When
18:54 you see a beautiful design, which you
18:56 always will, don't think, wow, I could
18:58 never do that. It's so good. Which is
19:00 what I used to do and I'm still guilty
19:01 of doing even to this day, change your
19:03 thinking to, "Wow, that looks so good. I
19:05 cannot wait to be that good." Or, "I
19:06 wonder how they did that on Figma." And
19:08 try to recreate it and try to learn from
19:10 it.
19:16 Part of the job of being a designer is
19:18 always learning. Always keeping up with
19:20 design trends, whether you implement
19:21 them or not, but just knowing them and

19:24 that pop up all the time, improving your
19:26 design skills and process. What worked
19:28 in 2020 looks very dated. Now, this is
19:30 similar to software engineering where
19:31 you are always learning and it's an
19:33 aspect about this career that I love. It
19:34 never gets boring. There's always
19:36 something new to try, some problem to
19:37 solve, some way to make something just a
19:39 little bit better. So, there you have
19:41 it. how I started and everything I wish
19:42 I knew when I was starting. Remember, I

19:58 minutes. Find one community to join.

How To Actually Think Like A Graphic Designer!

0:00 there are three core ways that top level and good graphic designers think like and you really do not
0:05 want to ignore these three different ways of thinking as a graphic designer because if you
0:09 do try and brush them off or avoid them you're not going to get very far in this industry and
0:13 here's the first thing that you might already have experienced a client might come to you
0:17 saying we need a website that feels fresh or we want a logo that stands out but still feels
0:22 professional now this is where your problem solving mindset should be kicking in and this
0:27 is the first core way of thinking as a designer and if you don't fully understand or know that
0:33 don't worry because right now we're going to look at some real life examples so Airbnb the
0:39 world-renowned travel platform it was struggling with the same issue their old logo just wasn't
0:45 cutting it and it looked like a mattress brand and this confused its target audience the problem
0:51 they faced was not just designing something quote unquote pretty but crafting a logo that
0:56 communicated a complex message and that was belonging how do you visually communicate
1:01 belonging when the designers began working on this they were probably asking themselves how
1:08 do we make something that doesn't just look cool but actually evokes a message this is the type
1:13 of problem solving that goes beyond Aesthetics and that's the challenge we as designers have
1:18 to rise up to so the designers started to break down what Airbnb really was about it wasn't just
1:26 about homes or renting properties it was about creating a space where any anyone from anywhere
1:30 in the world could feel like they belong this led them to develop the simple yet iconic a symbol
1:37 which is more than just an initial the design is actually multi-layered the a shape doubles
1:43 as a location pin where you are basically a heart which is belonging or love and even an
1:49 abstract person in some opinions each piece was intentionally designed to represent airbnb's
1:55 brand values and solve the communication issue so even if you're Ting your ability to communicate
2:01 visually remember that problem solving is just a process airbnb's logo didn't just magically
2:07 happen it took research trial error and a deep understanding of the message to get there and
2:14 that's what we as designers are here to do solve problems in a creative and meaningful
2:20 way for this approach you need to identify the problem within the breathe for the project and
2:31 then hash out some potential solutions for the problem or problems now if you can do this you
2:36 will have access to Concepts really quickly and let me show you how that actually looks now to
2:43 demonstrate how problem Sprints work check out this logo here now I'm sure only a handful of
2:49 people know what this logo is or shall I say was so this logo was actually the design for
2:55 Uber pre 2018 and if we look at this logo what problems can we identify on the design itself
3:03 firstly it looks more like an app icon and it's not very iconic and near the irony in
3:08 that wording but it has a lot of detail and it would become kind of lost at smaller scales it
3:13 also looks a bit generic and a bit outdated to be honest Uber really wanted to become an identified
3:19 brand away from just an app and so we have those problems and to create design ideas and Concepts
3:25 we just try and fix those problems in Uber's case the removed color and strip things back to black
3:31 and white for Simplicity modernity and sleekness they also removed all of the chaotic detail and
3:37 opted for a typographic logo again for Simplicity and brand recognition so for this first approach
3:44 simply identify problems and then create fixes for those problems and that will reveal Concepts and
3:50 ideas for your project so don't for a single second Overlook or ignore the power of design
3:55 thinking in terms of problem solving visually speaking it's such a core fundamental aspect
4:01 of being a graphic designer now I'm a busy man and a busy designer and I never learned how to
4:07 code so one way that I managed to actually create websites or marketing materials that actually look
4:12 good is by using framer one thing that helps me of saving time is the library of 700 plus templates
4:18 which gives us a lot of starting options so for example this 3D template is looking pretty dope
4:27 now like I said with framer everything is just so so quick and so easy even if you're someone
4:32 like myself who just doesn't want to mess around with code and I can make Social Links here way
4:37 more interesting compared to the static appearance they have now and I can actually come in and add a
4:42 hover effect here under the effects panel there are some settings to play around with but if
4:49 you come up and hit play here look how smooth and how neat this animation looks after the
4:54 fact but if you really want to draw attention to your ctas I like the loop effect which constantly
5:03 plays out on your site and this will surely drive those clicks now there are so many more features
5:12 more tools and different ways to create stunning websites with framer and if you do want to make
5:16 these cool websites these effective websites for framer do click the link down below and check out
5:21 framer today let me ask you something have you ever looked at a design and felt something stir
5:28 inside you per perhaps Joy Nostalgia maybe even sadness that emotional impact it isn't some kind
5:35 of fluke or accident not by a long shot let's take an example we're all familiar with and
5:40 that is Coca-Cola now I don't really care about the brand or the product so don't get caught up
5:44 in those narratives we're just looking at the brand through the lens of a designer on the
5:49 video why does the advertising always evoke feelings of Happiness celebration and warmth
5:56 it's not just the iconic red color although that does does help it's the whole visual
6:02 package the typography is playful and friendly evoking a sense of nostalgia and tradition the
6:09 imagery of people smiling sharing moments together this reinforces that Coca-Cola is
6:14 about the experience and togetherness it's not just a drink in their mind that's because the
6:20 design team who put this brand identity together actually used emotional impacts via storytelling
6:27 in a graphic design visual way and it is very very important color has an incredible ability
6:32 to influence people's mood think about movie posters horror films typically use dark Moody
6:39 colors with sharp contrast to create suspense and fear while romantic comedies often use warm
6:46 inviting tones to set a light-hearted happier mood as designers we need to ask ourselves
6:54 what emotions do we want to evoke and how can we use color typography and imagery to achieve that
7:01 when you approach a project start by asking what emotional response am I aiming for here
7:07 dive deep into the psychology of color explore how typography choices can change the tone of
7:12 a message and pick imagery that reinforces the emotion you want to evoke that's how you
7:18 become not just a designer but a Storyteller if you've ever doubted your ability to connect to
7:25 an audience emotionally with your designs know that it's actually a learnable skill
7:30 look at every piece of design as an opportunity to tell a story and Spark an emotional connection and
7:36 that's the magic of visual storytelling in graphic design and when you do actually tap into it your
7:42 designs won't just speak they will move people now let's be honest here have you ever just slapped
7:48 some color onto your projects applied a couple of fonts and said okay that's the end that's the
7:53 project finished it's easy to do especially when deadlines Loom just over on the horizon but here's
8:00 the hard truth without intention behind every design element your work can easily fall flat and
8:07 if that happens you risk missing the mark entirely with your audience and your client every good
8:13 designer thinks in terms of every design Choice must have a purpose maybe you felt that doubt
8:20 creep in am I just making this look good or is there more to it that uncertainty can weigh heavy
8:27 on your mind as a designer but here's the key thing every single element in your design should
8:33 have a purpose nothing should be random no color should be applied just because you like that color
8:40 and no line should be drawn without considering how that impacts the final message of the design
8:45 think about the Nike logo it's more than just a swoosh right it's Sleek it's fast and it's Dynamic
8:53 the curved forward moving shape reinforces the Brand's message of Athletics speed and Victory
8:59 there's purpose in that simple design right there the logo isn't just visually appealing it actually
9:05 kind of tells a story if you think about it now you might be wondering how do I design with that
9:11 kind of intention behind it what does it even mean to have purpose in every single element well that
9:18 curiosity is exactly what's going to push you forward as a designer start asking questions
9:24 like why this color what does this symbolize why use this shape how does it direct the eye
9:31 or support the Brand's message why should I use this type face does it feel bold delicate
9:37 traditional or modern asking all these questions will help you further in your projects and take
9:43 color for example if you're designing a brand identity for a children's toy company you wouldn't
9:48 use muted Grays or blacks you'd lean towards bright playful Hues because those colors are
9:55 Vogue energy excitement and fun they speak to the audience and that's why intentional design is all
10:01 about communication every decision you make in the design process should be geared towards a purpose
10:08 and once you start designing with intention everything just becomes clearer you'll feel more
10:13 confident in your choices and your designs will start to resonate more deeply with your audiences
10:18 and don't get it twisted you're not designing for your client you are designing for your client's
10:22 audience and so remember it's not just about making things look pretty it's about making them
10:27 matter when you design with purpose every element becomes a piece of the puzzle working together to
10:33 deliver the right message now if you can actually approach design projects and graphic design in
10:38 general when considering these three different ways of thinking not only are you going to see
10:42 your work improve but you will be miles ahead of most designers out there while most people just
10:48 consume almost useless reals about a Photoshop tool you will have gained some deep and advanced
10:53 knowledge of how to approach actual design projects at the level of top tier agencies but
10:59 yeah don't forget that and if you want to continue your education as a graphic designer today just
11:04 click one of those videos on screen but until next time guys design your future today peace

33:59 that are pretty new to Framer and a lot
34:02 of you, you know, ask me certain things.
34:04 So I kind of find different patterns and
34:06 that's how I make my videos. So, if you
34:08 have any more ideas, any more issues
34:10 that you're dealing with, please let me
34:11 know in the comments below and I'll be
34:13 happy to to make another video about
34:17 those issues. Um, also feel free to join
34:19 my Discord. We're a lot of framework
34:21 developers there and, you know, we're
34:23 very passionate about what we do and
34:25 we're passionate in helping others, you
34:27 know, get jobs or or get clients um or,
34:32 you know, improve our our own work or
34:35 portfolios. So yeah, feel free to join
34:37 that as well. And yeah, hope you guys
34:39 have a wonderful day. Thank you so much
34:41 for watching.

50:07 this middle section of a landing page
50:09 hero sections every landing page needs
50:14 one it's extremely important to get your
50:17 hero section correct because the hero
50:19 section answers a couple things first
50:22 what is this page about and it also
50:25 gives them a clear Direct a call to
50:27 action of some sort so a typical hero
50:30 section is consisting of just three
50:32 different elements or four if you think
50:33 about it first the headline the headline
50:36 has to be a sentence or just a few words
50:40 that really speaks to the audience and
50:42 typically it's the largest element on
50:44 the page because it's the first thing
50:45 you want people to read then you have a
50:47 sub headline which will go into more
50:49 details about the page and then finally
50:52 you have a call to action a button of
50:54 some sort or a form field to fill out
50:56 out or a scroll indicator icon these are
50:58 all calls to action actions we want the
51:01 user to take and then we also typically
51:03 have an illustration or a photograph of
