# shadcn/ui

> shadcn/ui is a collection of beautifully-designed, accessible components and a code distribution platform. It is built with TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, and Radix UI primitives. It supports multiple frameworks including Next.js, Vite, Remix, Astro, and more. Open Source. Open Code. AI-Ready. It also comes with a command-line tool to install and manage components and a registry system to publish and distribute code.

## Overview

- [Introduction](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs): Core principles—Open Code, Composition, Distribution, Beautiful Defaults, and AI-Ready design.
- [CLI](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/cli): Command-line tool for installing and managing components.
- [components.json](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components-json): Configuration file for customizing the CLI and component installation.
- [Theming](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/theming): Guide to customizing colors, typography, and design tokens.
- [Changelog](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/changelog): Release notes and version history.
- [Skills](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/skills): Deep shadcn/ui knowledge for AI assistants like Claude Code.
- [Directory](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/directory): Community registries built into the CLI.

## Installation

- [Next.js](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/installation/next): Install shadcn/ui in a Next.js project.
- [Vite](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/installation/vite): Install shadcn/ui in a Vite project.
- [Remix](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/installation/remix): Install shadcn/ui in a Remix project.
- [Astro](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/installation/astro): Install shadcn/ui in an Astro project.
- [Laravel](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/installation/laravel): Install shadcn/ui in a Laravel project.
- [Gatsby](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/installation/gatsby): Install shadcn/ui in a Gatsby project.
- [React Router](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/installation/react-router): Install shadcn/ui in a React Router project.
- [TanStack Router](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/installation/tanstack-router): Install shadcn/ui in a TanStack Router project.
- [TanStack Start](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/installation/tanstack): Install shadcn/ui in a TanStack Start project.
- [Manual Installation](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/installation/manual): Manually install shadcn/ui without the CLI.

## Components

### Form & Input

- [Field](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/field): Field component for form inputs with labels and error messages.
- [Button](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/button): Button component with multiple variants.
- [Button Group](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/button-group): Group multiple buttons together.
- [Input](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/input): Text input component.
- [Input Group](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/input-group): Input component with prefix and suffix addons.
- [Input OTP](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/input-otp): One-time password input component.
- [Textarea](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/textarea): Multi-line text input component.
- [Checkbox](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/checkbox): Checkbox input component.
- [Radio Group](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/radio-group): Radio button group component.
- [Select](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/select): Select dropdown component.
- [Native Select](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/native-select): Styled native HTML select element.
- [Switch](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/switch): Toggle switch component.
- [Slider](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/slider): Slider input component.
- [Calendar](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/calendar): Calendar component for date selection.
- [Date Picker](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/date-picker): Date picker component combining input and calendar.
- [Combobox](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/combobox): Searchable select component with autocomplete.
- [Label](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/label): Form label component.

### Layout & Navigation

- [Accordion](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/accordion): Collapsible accordion component.
- [Breadcrumb](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/breadcrumb): Breadcrumb navigation component.
- [Navigation Menu](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/navigation-menu): Accessible navigation menu with dropdowns.
- [Sidebar](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/sidebar): Collapsible sidebar component for app layouts.
- [Tabs](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/tabs): Tabbed interface component.
- [Separator](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/separator): Visual divider between content sections.
- [Scroll Area](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/scroll-area): Custom scrollable area with styled scrollbars.
- [Resizable](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/resizable): Resizable panel layout component.

### Overlays & Dialogs

- [Dialog](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/dialog): Modal dialog component.
- [Alert Dialog](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/alert-dialog): Alert dialog for confirmation prompts.
- [Sheet](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/sheet): Slide-out panel component (drawer).
- [Drawer](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/drawer): Mobile-friendly drawer component using Vaul.
- [Popover](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/popover): Floating popover component.
- [Tooltip](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/tooltip): Tooltip component for additional context.
- [Hover Card](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/hover-card): Card that appears on hover.
- [Context Menu](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/context-menu): Right-click context menu.
- [Dropdown Menu](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/dropdown-menu): Dropdown menu component.
- [Menubar](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/menubar): Horizontal menubar component.
- [Command](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/command): Command palette component (cmdk).

### Feedback & Status

- [Alert](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/alert): Alert component for messages and notifications.
- [Toast](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/toast): Toast notification component using Sonner.
- [Sonner](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/sonner): Opinionated toast component for React.
- [Progress](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/progress): Progress bar component.
- [Spinner](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/spinner): Loading spinner component.
- [Skeleton](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/skeleton): Skeleton loading placeholder.
- [Badge](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/badge): Badge component for labels and status indicators.
- [Empty](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/empty): Empty state component for no data scenarios.

### Display & Media

- [Avatar](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/avatar): Avatar component for user profiles.
- [Card](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/card): Card container component.
- [Table](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/table): Table component for displaying data.
- [Data Table](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/data-table): Advanced data table with sorting, filtering, and pagination.
- [Chart](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/chart): Chart components using Recharts.
- [Carousel](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/carousel): Carousel component using Embla Carousel.
- [Aspect Ratio](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/aspect-ratio): Container that maintains aspect ratio.
- [Typography](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/typography): Typography styles and components.
- [Item](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/item): Generic item component for lists and menus.
- [Kbd](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/kbd): Keyboard shortcut display component.

### Misc

- [Collapsible](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/collapsible): Collapsible container component.
- [Toggle](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/toggle): Toggle button component.
- [Toggle Group](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/toggle-group): Group of toggle buttons.
- [Pagination](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/pagination): Pagination component for lists and tables.
- [Direction](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/direction): Text direction provider for RTL support.

## Dark Mode

- [Dark Mode](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/dark-mode): Overview of dark mode implementation.
- [Dark Mode - Next.js](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/dark-mode/next): Dark mode setup for Next.js.
- [Dark Mode - Vite](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/dark-mode/vite): Dark mode setup for Vite.
- [Dark Mode - Astro](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/dark-mode/astro): Dark mode setup for Astro.
- [Dark Mode - Remix](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/dark-mode/remix): Dark mode setup for Remix.

## RTL

- [RTL](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/rtl): Overview of right-to-left language support.
- [RTL - Next.js](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/rtl/next): RTL setup for Next.js.
- [RTL - Vite](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/rtl/vite): RTL setup for Vite.
- [RTL - TanStack Start](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/rtl/start): RTL setup for TanStack Start.

## Forms

- [Forms Overview](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/forms): Guide to building forms with shadcn/ui.
- [React Hook Form](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/forms/react-hook-form): Using shadcn/ui with React Hook Form.
- [TanStack Form](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/forms/tanstack-form): Using shadcn/ui with TanStack Form.
- [Formisch](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/forms/formisch): Using shadcn/ui with Formisch.
- [Forms - Next.js](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/forms/next): Building forms in Next.js with Server Actions.

## Advanced

- [Monorepo](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/monorepo): Using shadcn/ui in a monorepo setup.
- [React 19](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/react-19): React 19 support and migration guide.
- [Tailwind CSS v4](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/tailwind-v4): Tailwind CSS v4 support and setup.
- [JavaScript](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/javascript): Using shadcn/ui with JavaScript (no TypeScript).
- [Figma](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/figma): Figma design resources.
- [v0](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/v0): Generating UI with v0 by Vercel.

## MCP Server

- [MCP Server](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/mcp): Model Context Protocol server for AI integrations. Allows AI assistants to browse, search, and install components from registries using natural language. Works with Claude Code, Cursor, VS Code (GitHub Copilot), Codex and more.

## Registry

- [Registry Overview](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/registry): Creating and publishing your own component registry.
- [Getting Started](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/registry/getting-started): Set up your own registry.
- [Examples](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/registry/examples): Example registries.
- [FAQ](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/registry/faq): Common questions about registries.
- [Authentication](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/registry/authentication): Adding authentication to your registry.
- [Registry MCP](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/registry/mcp): MCP integration for registries.
- [Namespaces](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/registry/namespace): Using multiple registries with namespace support.
- [Add a Registry](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/registry/registry-index): Open source registry index and how to submit yours.
- [Open in v0](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/registry/open-in-v0): Integrating your registry with Open in v0.
- [registry.json](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/registry/registry-json): `registry.json` schema for your own registry.
- [registry-item.json](https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/registry/registry-item-json): `registry-item.json` specification for registry items.

### Registry Schemas

- [Registry Schema](https://ui.shadcn.com/schema/registry.json): JSON Schema for registry index files. Defines the structure for a collection of components, hooks, pages, etc. Requires name, homepage, and items array.
- [Registry Item Schema](https://ui.shadcn.com/schema/registry-item.json): JSON Schema for individual registry items. Defines components, hooks, themes, and other distributable code with properties for dependencies, files, Tailwind config, CSS variables, and more.

6:46 consider peer review as excellent

7:31 peer review tips

7:35 parts of the design process
7:37 they're an essential way to see if your
7:38 design is communicating what you
7:40 intended to an audience
7:42 so in this video i'm going to outline a
7:44 few tips for completing peer review
7:46 assignments successfully in this class
7:49 participation in peer review
7:50 thoughtfully and meaningfully will help
7:52 you practice these indispensable skills
7:56 submitting assignments
7:59 read the instructions carefully
8:01 make sure you take a look at the review
8:03 criteria so you know how your assignment
8:06 will be assessed
8:07 review any examples your instructor may
8:09 have provided and upload exactly what
8:12 you are asked to do
8:13 if something in the instructions isn't
8:15 clear post your question in the course
8:17 forums so staff can assist you
8:21 make it your best work
8:23 this is your creation and your
8:25 creativity
8:26 and should be an exercise that
8:27 demonstrates what you can do
8:30 practice assignments should be
8:32 opportunities to fail but a final
8:34 assignment should be something worthy of
8:36 your professional portfolio
8:38 ask for specific feedback
8:41 clarify what you need from your peers in
8:43 your review where applicable use the
8:46 designated comment field to ask for
8:48 specific feedback on your submission
8:52 submit on time refer to the due date for
8:55 submitting your assignment on the grades
8:57 page within the course
8:59 if you're too early or too late the peer
9:02 review process may not work as intended
9:06 reviewing submissions
9:09 take your time with reviewing
9:11 don't rush this
9:12 look at each part of your peers
9:14 submission carefully and compare what
9:17 has been submitted to the expectations
9:19 set out in the rubric
9:22 be objective
9:23 when reviewing consider your role as a
9:26 viewer or reader of the work
9:28 focus only on what the rubric asks you
9:30 to evaluate and try to limit your
9:32 personal opinions about the work in your
9:35 comments
9:37 be clear and informative
9:39 generic feedback such as good or okay
9:42 are unhelpful comments for peer reviews
9:45 because they don't give a your peer any
9:47 specific information about what was
9:50 working in their submission
9:52 likewise it doesn't help to say it
9:54 doesn't work or i don't like it because
9:57 it doesn't give your peer enough
9:59 information to help them reassess their
10:01 designs
10:02 try to articulate a detailed response
10:05 that helps to affirm your peers choices
10:07 in their submission or guides your peer
10:10 towards the goal of an assignment if it
10:12 appears that they are off track
10:15 be constructive
10:17 your feedback should motivate your peer
10:19 to make adjustments and work towards
10:21 improvement
10:23 if there's a need to correct your peer
10:25 be honest but it's more helpful to
10:27 include specific recommendations or
10:30 strategies to help the learner improve
10:33 be generous
10:35 recognize that everyone comes to this
10:37 course with a different level of
10:39 experience as well as a different
10:41 approach to making work
10:43 honor and value these differences
10:46 bad grammar and spelling shouldn't
10:48 contribute to a bad grade please be
10:50 generous
10:51 likewise please don't penalize students
10:54 for small mistakes
10:57 flag plagiarism and dishonest behavior
11:00 these violate coursera's honor code
11:03 if you're asked to review an assignment
11:05 and it appears to be plagiarized you can
11:07 flag it so coursera learner support are
11:10 notified
11:13 complete your reviews on time
11:16 refer to the due date for completing the
11:18 minimum number of peer reviews on the
11:20 grades page within the course
11:22 if you're too early or too late the peer
11:25 review process may not work as intended
11:29 consider reviewing more than the minimum
11:31 number of peers for a given assignment
11:34 not only is this helpful for your fellow
11:36 peers but it's also instructive for you
11:39 to see a greater range of submissions to
11:42 inform your own work
11:43 and the more you practice critique the
11:46 better you will get at it and ultimately
11:48 the better your own work will be as you
11:50 integrate your critical abilities into
11:53 your own design process

12:33 by engaging in peer review you're

12:39 it's important to understand how an

12:45 peer feedback should never feel like a

13:06 that's okay it's not ideal but don't let

13:31 negative feedback might let you know

13:58 if you receive a low grade consider why

32:48 wait a minute if you press them
32:50 nothing really happens
32:52 how do you know if you've actually
32:54 done anything by pressing the button so
32:57 straight away we can understand that
32:58 when we interact with an interface we
33:00 need to have some kind of
33:02 feedback some kind of result otherwise
33:05 how do we know if the interface is
33:06 working how can we trust that interface
33:09 so even in this very simple model there
33:11 has to be some feedback so if we press a
33:13 key for example we want to see on a
33:16 separate screen that those numbers
33:17 appear so that we know that our
33:19 interaction has actually worked
33:22 but what happens if we do something
33:23 wrong what happens if we press the wrong
33:25 number
33:26 well we're going to need an extra key i
33:28 think we're going to need a cancel key
33:31 for example in case we make a mistake
33:34 and we might also need an ok key for
33:36 when we press the right number
33:39 so even from this very simple idea of
33:41 looking at how a keypad works we figured
33:44 out some very important principles for
33:46 interactivity the fact that we need to
33:48 have feedback as a user the fact that if
33:51 we make a mistake we need to be able to
33:53 correct it and the fact that we need to
33:55 say that things are okay when we've done
33:56 them right
33:59 in this segment we're going to look at
34:01 interface conventions
34:02 and an easy way to do this is to take a
34:04 very basic interface
34:06 for instance our keypad and try and use
34:08 that to examine something more
34:10 complicated because even this simple
34:12 keypad the interactions with it a lot
34:15 more complex than they seem
34:17 from these basic interactions we can
34:19 pull out a set of rules or ideas about
34:22 how a user interface should work
34:24 and a lot of ui ux designers online will
34:27 have their top 10 tips
34:29 for things that ui designers should look
34:31 at so here are some of mine
34:33 we're going to take this list and
34:34 examine them one at a time and see how
34:37 they work when applied to even a very
34:40 simple interface like the keypad but we
34:42 could take those ideas and also think
34:44 about how they might work with something
34:46 much more complicated so first of all
34:48 let's look at real world knowledge real
34:51 world knowledge is based on analog
34:52 models early interfaces were clearly
34:55 based on these real-world models where
34:58 they were simulations of physical things
35:01 and objects that existed in the real
35:03 world
35:04 it's less so with contemporary
35:05 interfaces but some of that vocabulary
35:08 still lingers on we still talk about
35:10 buttons and menus for example that are
35:12 physical things with our keypad for
35:14 instance we recognize digital buttons
35:17 because we've experienced buttons in the
35:19 real world so we have an idea about how
35:22 they're going to work and this brings us
35:24 on to our next topic which is learned
35:26 behavior or conventions
35:28 because we already know how interface
35:30 elements work for instance the button
35:33 even though this is a flat
35:34 representation on the screen rather than
35:37 being physical
35:38 we draw a correlation between how the
35:41 button works in the real world and then
35:43 knowing how the button is going to work
35:45 in the digital world cause and effect
35:47 looks at action and reaction
35:49 when we press a button we know that
35:51 something is going to happen we have an
35:53 expectation already about how it works
35:56 often the thing that happens is going to
35:58 happen somewhere else but if that button
36:00 is combined with words or numbers or
36:04 symbols that are associated with it then
36:06 we have an expectation of what the
36:08 button is going to do of what's actually
36:11 going to happen when we press it
36:13 consistency looks at a logical or
36:15 systematic approach to design if our
36:18 buttons look the same then they should
36:20 act in a similar or systematic way
36:23 if you press one button and you learn
36:24 how it works then you have a pretty good
36:27 idea about how the next button is going
36:29 to work buttons shouldn't look the same
36:31 but then work in a different way
36:34 we're really trying to figure out a
36:36 consistent system
36:38 so if we know we press this button that
36:39 is a 1 when we press the 8 button we
36:42 know that the 8 is going to work in the
36:43 same way
36:45 interaction should be seamless
36:47 it should be fast and it should be easy
36:49 there should be as few steps as possible
36:52 when you're asking a user to interact
36:54 with something
36:55 when you interact with an interface you
36:57 want immediate results without delay you
36:59 want whatever you want to happen and you
37:01 want it to happen now there's nothing
37:03 worse than clicking or tapping
37:06 and nothing happening it breeds
37:08 frustration in the user and leads to
37:10 dissatisfaction with the interface
37:13 immediate intuition
37:15 in other words a few interactions can
37:17 teach a lot
37:18 once the users learn how a button works
37:21 they can intuitively know how a whole
37:24 set of buttons work by extension they
37:26 might also know how similar digital
37:28 buttons work even if they're going to
37:31 look and function in a different way
37:34 so once you've figured out a way of
37:36 interacting that way can be applied to a
37:39 lot of different forms
37:41 in other words if you know how this
37:43 button works then you know how all
37:45 buttons work and of course this is very
37:47 useful because those kind of things
37:49 build into interface conventions that
37:51 are consistent across a lot of platforms
37:54 so if we know how to swipe in one app
37:56 for instance we know how to swipe in all
37:58 apps no matter what kind of thing we're
38:00 doing with the swiping whether we're
38:02 shopping or going on a date or ordering
38:04 food the swipe is always still the same
38:07 we only need to learn how to do that
38:09 once regardless of content or context
38:13 fulfillment in other words payoff or
38:16 results if we take the time to press a
38:18 button to interact with an interface we
38:21 expect something to happen we expect a
38:24 small sense of gratification or
38:26 fulfillment we want to believe that a
38:29 screen based digital interaction has
38:31 real value
38:33 if we take the time to press the button
38:35 we expect something in return as a user
38:38 it gives us a sense of value with the
38:40 interface that we're interacting with it
38:42 gives us a sense that we're important
38:44 that whatever we do we're going to get
38:46 something back from the interface itself
38:48 for our investment
38:50 undo redo the user wants to feel safe
38:54 to know that if they make a mistake
38:56 they can nearly always undo or redo it
38:58 in the digital world
39:00 it's a key thing that we love about the
39:02 digital world as opposed to the real
39:04 world in the digital world we have an
39:07 ability to undo our actions
39:09 if we broke something in a digital
39:11 experience we could hit command z and
39:13 undo it and it wouldn't be broken
39:15 anymore but if we break something in the
39:17 real world in the analog world it's
39:19 broken for good
39:21 so we like to know that if we press a
39:23 button nothing bad is going to happen we
39:26 like to feel safe in that digital
39:28 environment
39:29 it's really important to understand
39:31 these basic concepts of how interaction
39:33 works because they're a foundation to
39:35 understanding that the blank screen that
39:37 we start with when we design an
39:39 interface is not necessarily a blank
39:42 slate
39:43 there are pre-existing modes of
39:45 interacting and pre-existing interface
39:47 conventions that we need to consider we
39:50 need to think about what does the user
39:53 bring to the interface in terms of what
39:56 the user already knows about how
39:57 interfaces work how interaction works
40:01 you can't present a user with a totally
40:03 alien interface every time they're
40:05 interacting with the different content
40:07 where they have to learn how to interact
40:10 in totally different and new ways but at
40:12 the same time you don't want to give
40:14 them such a conventional interface that
40:16 there's nothing interesting or engaging
40:18 or original about that experience
40:22 in this section we're going to look at
40:23 interface conventions and we're going to
40:25 break it down into two different
40:27 categories
40:29 the first category is going to be the
40:31 interface and how it relates to us as
40:34 users as physical human beings there are
40:37 certain conventions here to do with the
40:39 arrangement and positioning of
40:40 information on the screen if we take our
40:42 basic keypad again and think about how
40:45 that works there's a set of numbers keys
40:48 to push
40:49 a screen where we see the results but if
40:52 we just take those elements without
40:54 thinking about the design of the
40:55 elements and reconfigure them
40:58 the keypad becomes very dysfunctional
41:00 we're not really sure how to use it
41:02 anymore and we're not really sure we
41:04 recognize it as a keypad anymore and
41:06 there's a couple of reasons for this one
41:08 is because we're used to seeing keypads
41:10 arranged in a certain way we're used to
41:13 seeing a calculator for example and we
41:15 used to sing that look in a certain
41:17 format and that format was came from a
41:20 physical calculator that came out of the
41:22 1970s and the 1980s although if we look
41:25 at a digital calculator on say the
41:27 iphone for instance the design of it is
41:29 actually exactly the same it's not the
41:31 graphic elements that are changing the
41:34 buttons are still the same some of the
41:36 shapes might change the colors might
41:38 change but it's really the arrangement
41:40 that is causing disorientation for the
41:43 user because it changes how we
41:45 physically interact with the keypad and
41:47 this is a very important thing for us to
41:49 look at in terms of thinking about
41:51 interface conventions physical interface
41:53 conventions that is it's to do with the
41:56 size of the screen that we have in
41:58 relationship with the human form
42:00 and our basic human interactions and
42:03 human ergonomics have had a strong
42:05 impact on what kinds of things can
42:07 actually happen in the interface on the
42:09 screen because we've rearranged the
42:11 elements we've made it quite
42:12 dysfunctional if we put it back to how
42:15 it was we can look and see what happens
42:17 when we as human beings press a key
42:20 straight away we need to be able to
42:21 think about the key in relationship to
42:23 the size of our fingers as well as the
42:25 keypad in relationship to the size of
42:27 our hand
42:29 so the size and shape of the buttons
42:31 might actually have some relationship to
42:33 the human body to the human hand if we
42:36 think about how we interact with these
42:37 keypad depressed numbers the human hand
42:40 gets in the way of a lot of the numbers
42:42 when we're pressing them particularly
42:44 the numbers at the top then we can't see
42:46 the ones at the bottom they're hidden by
42:47 the hand so straight away we realize
42:49 that we have to have the screen
42:51 showing the result of our interaction
42:53 above the keypad otherwise the hand is
42:56 going to be hiding the screen and we
42:58 won't be able to see what we're pressing
43:00 so there's a very simple relationship
43:02 between our physicality as human beings
43:05 and the machinery the screens that we
43:07 interact with you could think about the
43:09 keypad the scale of it in relationship
43:12 to the scale of the human hand if a
43:14 calculator for instance fits in a hand
43:17 in a comfortable way then it's going to
43:19 make our interactions much more easy
43:21 it's going to feel comfortable to
43:23 interact with this becomes relevant when
43:25 we think about how the scale of the
43:27 calculator for instance in relationship
43:29 to the human body also relates to the
43:32 scale of your phone currently for
43:33 instance both devices are held in the
43:36 hand at a comfortable distance and we're
43:38 able to interact with the screen or the
43:40 interface with our fingers to get the
43:42 information we want in the easiest way
43:45 possible when we interact with a
43:46 handheld device we're really just using
43:49 our fingers we're not really using any
43:51 other parts of our body to interact with
43:53 it but when we have different size
43:55 screens we start to interact with them
43:57 in different ways and we hold these
43:59 screens at different distances from our
44:01 body so they fill our visual feel in
44:03 different ways as well
44:05 we hold and look at a phone very
44:07 differently from how we hold and look at
44:10 and interact with an ipad for instance
44:13 if we look at another screen if we
44:15 increase the size of that screen even
44:17 more then we can see how our interaction
44:19 changes even more so for instance with
44:21 the laptop our hands are much more
44:23 active in a different way we're not
44:25 touching the screen anymore now we're
44:27 touching a keypad so we've got a
44:30 different kind of physical movement
44:32 we're also not holding the computer
44:34 anymore in the way that we hold an ipad
44:36 or a phone for instance now it's resting
44:39 and we've got two hands to interact with
44:41 the keyboard now and these different
44:43 kinds of interactions with physical
44:45 devices explains a little bit why we
44:48 like to have different size screens for
44:50 different kinds of functions and
44:51 different kinds of interactions
44:54 some are more portable than others but
44:56 the ones that are not portable allow us
44:57 to have a much larger screen and perhaps
45:00 allow us to do much more robust kinds of
45:02 work in a fixed situation the physical
45:05 size of the computer has a relationship
45:07 to our bodies
45:09 if we think about a desktop computer
45:11 it's more removed than even the laptop
45:13 is because now the keypad is a separate
45:15 item our screen is even bigger and it's
45:18 definitely much less mobile the phone
45:20 you can take everywhere but a desktop
45:22 computer stays in one place these
45:25 physical conventions all have a
45:27 relationship to the human body and how
45:29 we interact with different sizes of
45:31 screens and how we have different tasks
45:34 that we complete on those different
45:35 screens those screens are based on a
45:38 relationship to our whole human body and
45:41 being able to carry screens around but
45:43 our interaction with the screens takes
45:45 place mainly through the hand and
45:47 through the eye and these are both
45:49 conduits to our thinking device our cpu
45:52 in other words our brain the eye lets us
45:54 see what we've done and the hand lets us
45:57 interact with the computer it's
45:59 important to know about these physical
46:00 conventions because these lead into the
46:02 digital conventions over the years
46:05 interface designers have taken into
46:06 account a user's physicality to develop
46:09 the best solutions of how interfaces
46:11 should work and these digital
46:13 conventions have become standardized to
46:16 the point of interface invisibility we
46:18 never really think anymore why our
46:20 navigation menus are at the top of our
46:22 screen we just take it for granted that
46:24 that's where they're going to live it's
46:25 become such a convention that has become
46:27 invisible to us
46:29 so our navigation choices normally go at
46:31 the top of the computer if we want to
46:33 see a long menu when we click on these
46:35 it needs to have space for the menu to
46:36 fall down so being at the top seems a
46:39 logical place for it to be it's also a
46:41 convenient place to put something where
46:43 you can ignore it if you want to we're
46:45 very used to a certain arrangement and
46:48 hierarchy of information in the
46:50 interface
46:51 our branding or identity might be very
46:53 visible sitting under the menu choices
46:55 at the top of the screen and then we
46:57 probably have an area
46:59 taking up most of the rest of the screen
47:01 that shows variable content or whatever
47:03 specialized things that particular
47:05 website can do and then down at the
47:07 bottom quite often the secondary
47:09 information
47:10 all the things you need to find when
47:12 there's a problem with the website
47:14 menu items that are at a lower
47:15 hierarchical level than the ones at the
47:17 top so even just by putting these four
47:20 things together we have something that
47:21 looks like a fairly generic and familiar
47:24 website
47:25 we could shrink our menu down to make it
47:27 have a better relationship to the size
47:30 of the screen and we could introduce
47:31 another convention
47:33 how do we search the information on our
47:35 website how do we find something so if
47:37 we have a live text entry box with the
47:39 search icon even the icon itself is a
47:42 convention we know what the icon of the
47:45 magnifying glass means we know it's not
47:48 actually for a magnifying glass we know
47:50 it's for looking or searching for
47:52 something so it's another digital
47:54 convention in this segment we're going
47:56 to look at two different approaches to
47:57 user interface design
47:59 one which is much more template based
48:02 working with conventions versus one that
48:04 is more content-based if we go back to
48:06 our generic web structure
48:08 we can see that in many ways the
48:10 template equals a distilled version of
48:13 conventions this can be useful because
48:15 of its familiarity and its ubiquity but
48:18 it can also be a little bit boring and
48:19 sometimes predictable due to the same
48:21 factors the template doesn't take
48:24 content into account the template is
48:27 content agnostic and while this offers a
48:29 level of functionality it also offers a
48:32 level of predictability and sometimes
48:34 that experience means it's not that
48:36 exciting it doesn't matter what kind of
48:38 content you flow into a generic
48:41 structure the framework never changes if
48:44 we're making a website about the shapes
48:45 of animals for instance it might very
48:48 little if our content was how to bake a
48:50 cake or how to buy a t-shirt but
48:52 adversely the generic interface does
48:55 have some great advantages first of all
48:58 it's fast and it's easy and it offers
49:00 some flexibility so even though it's
49:02 generic it can still be customized it's
49:04 good for repetitive similar kinds of
49:07 content for instance here's a website
49:09 built using squarespace which has
49:11 standardized templates this is a website
49:14 for an artist
49:15 where you can see it's easy to
49:17 categorize the information into certain
49:19 bodies of work and it's easy to navigate
49:22 them and choose which body of work you
49:24 want to look at it's very clean and
49:26 functional it's not confusing it's very
49:29 clear so there are other conventions we
49:31 can see here there are thumbnails of
49:33 work that you can click on you can
49:35 scroll through images from top to bottom
49:38 so thinking about what kind of areas a
49:40 template might be successful in
49:42 something like this website where the
49:45 content is visually complex but needs to
49:47 be organized in an easy to understand
49:49 and accessible way might be good for a
49:51 template there's also not a lot of user
49:54 interaction here apart from looking at
49:56 pictures of things so here again a
49:59 template would work very well for that
50:01 we can see similarities with how that
50:03 might be a structure that also works for
50:05 something like our animal shapes website
50:08 where the content is very very similar
50:10 so it makes sense for it to be organized
50:12 in a flat way
50:14 but let's see what happens when we
50:16 change that content into something that
50:18 has a little more variation for instance
50:20 here's some publications that i've
50:22 designed and i might decide i want to
50:23 make a website of my book designs
50:26 these are very three-dimensional and
50:28 they have different kinds of formats so
50:31 suddenly it's a lot harder for me to
50:33 arrange these i'm not working with
50:35 regular squares as part of my interface
50:38 elements i'm working with irregular
50:40 objects so the generic interface starts
50:42 to work a little bit less but i can take
50:45 this idea of a fairly generic functional
50:47 website and i can start to customize it
50:50 i can add color to my website for
50:51 instance i can create a division between
50:54 the navigation and the content area i
50:57 can choose the color of my typography
50:59 all of these things are options that
51:00 might be available in a template and i
51:03 might be able to choose something where
51:04 there's much less of a visible grid in
51:06 the structure for instance that creates
51:08 a different kind of experience but it
51:10 still feels like a relatively generic
51:12 solution when we're working within the
51:15 constraints of the template even my
51:17 aesthetic design decisions are still
51:20 contained under the umbrella of what the
51:22 template can do so here's what that
51:24 website actually looks like when it
51:26 might be made it's taking some of the
51:28 ideas of the template in terms of
51:30 structure accessibility and logic
51:34 but it's creating something that has a
51:36 little more visual interest by creating
51:38 a user interface that is a little more
51:40 specific to this particular kind of
51:43 content here are the same kind of books
51:46 but they're structured in a way
51:48 where there's a different color coding
51:49 system and the typography
51:52 works in a more visually interesting way
51:55 but the logic and structure of the site
51:57 is still very much based on how a
51:59 template works where you can peruse
52:01 different sections and zoom into them
52:04 and look at details but it's actually
52:06 the details of the design and the
52:08 interface design that make the
52:09 difference to this experience it makes
52:12 it more rich it makes it a more
52:14 idiosyncratic experience than if a user
52:17 was looking at a purely generic website
52:20 but at the same time it retains some of
52:22 the functionality and familiarity the
52:25 ease of use that you want to have from a
52:28 generic or functional website and that's
52:30 something that the generic website can
52:32 offer it's essential to remember that in
52:34 this way one size does not fit all a
52:37 template can be very useful in terms of
52:39 its functionality but it has to be
52:41 modified in order to take certain kinds
52:44 of content into account or on the other
52:46 hand the template could be ignored
52:48 entirely and we could take some of those
52:50 conventions and turn them into a much
52:52 more content specific design and what we
52:55 mean by this is really looking at what
52:57 you're building an app a website or an
52:59 interface for and trying to think about
53:01 what is the best kind of interface
53:04 possible for this kind of content and we
53:06 often call this context specific design
53:09 because it's taking into account other
53:11 things as well as just the content
53:13 context specific design has a
53:16 relationship to specific content
53:18 in other words the way that a visual
53:21 experience looks has a direct
53:23 correlation to what the content is or
53:26 what the message of that experience is
53:28 but the interface is also shaped by
53:30 other factors there might be a certain
53:32 user group that is specific to this
53:34 particular digital experience and that
53:36 might change how the interface looks and
53:38 how the interface feels for instance if
53:41 i knew that my user group were purely
53:43 people that listened only to heavy metal
53:45 music that might dictate the kind of
53:48 typography or the kind of imagery that i
53:50 might want to use but if i knew that it
53:52 was for people that liked all different
53:53 kinds of music i'd have to create
53:55 something that had a more general
53:57 feeling to it and a less specific
53:59 feeling the design can also be tempered
54:02 by the digital experience having certain
54:04 goals or outcomes that are desired and
54:06 this might drive how the user interface
54:08 works and also how it looks and these
54:11 external factors can be different for
54:14 each different website so it makes us
54:16 think about when to use a template and
54:18 when not to use a template
54:20 when the content is strong or has a
54:22 specific user group or specific goals a
54:25 template might not always be the best
54:27 solution we're looking at a balance
54:29 between the functionality of generic
54:31 design and interface conventions
54:34 and the specificity of different kinds
54:37 of content or experiences that we want a
54:39 user to have
54:41 a lot of interface designers strive to
54:44 have an invisible experience for the
54:46 interface to be invisible what this
54:48 means is they don't want the interface
54:50 to get in the way of the content and
54:52 that invisible experience really equates
54:55 to prioritizing the functionality above
54:58 all but the danger of the invisible
55:00 experience is that it can become a
55:02 generic experience
55:04 another way to think about it is that
55:06 the specificity of any digital
55:08 experience comes through the aesthetics
55:10 of that experience
55:12 whereas the functionality is coming from
55:14 the interface conventions or the utility
55:17 when you combine the aesthetics and the
55:19 functionality together you end up with
55:21 the complete digital experience where
55:24 you feel like you have something
55:25 idiosyncratic and personal but you also
55:28 have something that is functional
55:31 in this segment we're going to look at
55:32 aesthetics and functionality and this
55:34 correlates quite strongly to some of the
55:36 things we've already looked at internet
55:38 conventions which are really about
55:40 functionality of the user interface and
55:42 aesthetics which are about the formal
55:45 resolution of how something looks which
55:47 is often connected to the content we can
55:49 think about this in a very simple way
55:51 when building a user interface in terms
55:53 of how it looks and how it works and a
55:56 user interface designer tends to work
55:58 with both of these things together so
56:00 we're going to pull these things apart
56:01 to look at them separately so if we
56:03 think about how to turn this into a
56:05 button for instance we could make some
56:07 design moves that are purely aesthetic
56:09 to make this happen we might create a
56:11 background shape behind the type so
56:14 we've got a physical area that we know
56:15 is interactive we might add some text so
56:18 we know what the button is going to do
56:20 we might decide we want the button to
56:22 look three-dimensional to make it look
56:24 physical we could change the typography
56:27 we could change the color of the
56:28 typography as well
56:30 all of these things might make part of
56:32 our interface look a certain way but it
56:35 might not necessarily change how it
56:38 works
56:39 if we look at how it works we could take
56:41 the same shape but the how it works part
56:44 might be more about changing the states
56:46 of the button so if a mouse hovers over
56:49 it it might change color and when it's
56:51 clicked it might change color again
56:54 so how it works and how it looks are not
56:57 mutually exclusive
56:59 if we look at a more contemporary
57:00 example of that and we take a much more
57:02 reduced version of an interface a more
57:05 minimal interface we could look at the
57:07 difference between how things look and
57:10 how things work with a much more minimal
57:12 language
57:14 here how it looks is actually very
57:16 similar to how it works
57:18 we don't need to try and create
57:20 something that physically looks like a
57:21 button because we already understand
57:24 that everything on the screen is going
57:25 to be interactive due to interface
57:27 conventions that we're already familiar
57:29 with so how it looks and how it works
57:32 become fused into one thing the
57:34 aesthetics and the functionality
57:36 become merged together
57:38 as one interface element
57:41 and in contemporary interface design
57:43 this is a good way to think about when
57:45 something is successful
57:47 when both the aesthetics and the
57:48 functionality are working together in a
57:51 very harmonious way to form the
57:53 interface to use some of the same
57:55 language we've already looked at the
57:56 aesthetic part of the interface design
57:59 is about giving the interface a sense of
58:01 individuality whereas the functional
58:03 part of the interface design is about
58:06 giving it some familiarity some ease of
58:08 use and when you put these two things
58:11 together it can be very powerful so the
58:13 aesthetics are creating an individual
58:15 feeling but the functionality of the
58:17 interface the familiarity of it is
58:20 making it easy to use as well so you
58:22 have both functionality and visual
58:24 interest and putting these things
58:26 together is really the key to making a
58:28 successful user interface

1:03:30 for it
1:03:30 more than anything you need to be the

1:03:37 you should be the expert as a designer
1:03:39 but also be the expert in terms of the
1:03:42 content that you're working with

1:03:54 of how design works in a ui project the
1:03:57 look and feel of the project another way

1:04:10 this project was to be finished
1:04:12 that's often done in motion projects in
1:04:14 print projects there's also a thing
1:04:16 called a mood board where you try and
1:04:18 paint a broad picture of research and
1:04:20 the look and feel of a project what all
1:04:22 of these things are trying to do
1:04:24 and they're fairly interchangeable
1:04:25 expressions is that they're trying to
1:04:28 envision the overall design direction
1:04:31 and this is still part of pre-design
1:04:33 we're not really working with words now
1:04:35 but we're working with very broad visual
1:04:38 strokes
1:04:39 it gets us into a mode of pre-production
1:04:42 it's and it's before we've really
1:04:44 produced anything it's pre-commitment as
1:04:46 well it's before we've really made any
1:04:49 design decisions that we have to stick
1:04:51 to and what that means is it's before
1:04:53 we've really spent any money
1:04:55 you have to get all the components right
1:04:58 before a project goes into production
1:05:00 and might cost millions of dollars where
1:05:02 thousands of people might be using it
1:05:04 and hundreds of people beta testing it
1:05:06 so it's important to get these things

1:05:17 imagining that design trying to give it

1:05:21 really exist in a lot of ways

1:15:19 expressions and think about how we put

1:15:33 so for instance we might look at the

1:15:47 probably isn't
1:15:50 so let's compare our

1:15:58 we're going to call

1:16:03 can think about how we use words in a

1:16:08 we could think about words as navigation

1:17:37 so language can set the mood and the

1:17:49 in this section we're going to look at
1:17:51 how color and shape can work as formal
1:17:53 elements of your interface design
1:17:56 so let's start out by looking at color
1:17:57 in a little more detail
1:17:59 one of the main ways that color works is
1:18:02 by creating an overall mood and this can
1:18:05 be really useful in an app because again
1:18:07 it can set the tone if you want
1:18:08 something to be serious or more fun you
1:18:11 might find a color palette that makes
1:18:12 your app more believable
1:18:14 as human beings we generally respond to
1:18:17 colors
1:18:18 and we respond to them in a very
1:18:19 subjective and emotive way
1:18:21 so let's start out by doing a few
1:18:23 exercises and looking at how color works
1:18:25 and let's take our scientific interface
1:18:28 and let's take all the color away from
1:18:30 it and start out with just black and
1:18:31 white
1:18:32 when everything is just black and white
1:18:34 we often have connotations with that of
1:18:36 design being very serious
1:18:39 and that stems from cultural
1:18:40 connotations of black and white printing
1:18:43 newspapers early photography
1:18:45 but just by adding a simple color we can
1:18:48 change those connotations when we put
1:18:50 yellow and black together for instance
1:18:52 we're used to seeing that to represent
1:18:54 danger
1:18:55 when we see a bright color like magenta
1:18:58 we think that that's something that's
1:18:59 perhaps going to be fun
1:19:01 so you can see how even just changing
1:19:03 one color
1:19:04 can create an emotion
1:19:06 if we start to put together a palette
1:19:08 where we've got a range of colors
1:19:10 working together in harmony we could
1:19:12 have a cool palette that might create a
1:19:14 very icy or cold feeling and when we
1:19:17 start to change those colors we could
1:19:19 create other connotations for me red
1:19:22 white and blue represents england for
1:19:25 other people it might represent a
1:19:26 different country
1:19:28 we could take a palette that comes out
1:19:29 of nature for instance browns
1:19:32 or greens and we can build a palette
1:19:35 that makes us feel
1:19:36 maybe calm or makes us feel a connection
1:19:38 to nature
1:19:40 so there are some connotations that come
1:19:43 from the real world and some that are
1:19:44 fabricated
1:19:46 we see blue things we might think it's a
1:19:48 design for a boy and pink is a design
1:19:50 for a girl
1:19:51 but those are just cultural conventions
1:19:54 color is quite relative in a lot of ways
1:19:57 and quite subjective so we have to
1:19:59 figure out how to control that and how
1:20:01 to try and control that subjectivity a
1:20:03 little bit in order to get it to do what
1:20:06 we want it to do for our interface in
1:20:08 order to help create an atmosphere and a
1:20:11 mood as well as a function
1:20:14 and in terms of function we can use
1:20:15 color in many different ways and a
1:20:17 primary one is to think about how color
1:20:19 works as navigation within our interface
1:20:22 so for instance if you have these three
1:20:24 buttons and i ask you to pick one i'm
1:20:26 pretty sure you're going to press the
1:20:27 green button just because it looks like
1:20:30 it's active we're used to thinking that
1:20:32 perhaps green makes it want to be picked
1:20:34 but also it's different from the other
1:20:36 buttons
1:20:37 and we can use color to both create
1:20:39 difference and similarity so just by
1:20:42 changing the other buttons here the
1:20:44 inactive buttons from gray to a lighter
1:20:47 shade of green we build a different kind
1:20:49 of relationship now they feel like they
1:20:52 have the possibility to all be pressed
1:20:54 and turn to the darker shade of green
1:20:57 and basically what we're doing here is
1:20:59 creating a system that has differences
1:21:01 and similarities and that can also work
1:21:04 in terms of association with other
1:21:06 things that we've experienced before
1:21:08 either in interfaces or the real world
1:21:11 we think about green as being go or
1:21:13 being active and red as being stop or
1:21:16 being danger
1:21:18 if we use colors that have a similar
1:21:20 relationship they can also build a sense
1:21:23 of similar functionality
1:21:25 so here we've got three buttons going
1:21:27 from light to dark with a similar hue to
1:21:30 them and they might feel like they do
1:21:32 the same thing but to maybe a different
1:21:35 extent
1:21:36 whereas if we have three different hues
1:21:39 we feel like that these three different
1:21:41 buttons are going to have three
1:21:42 different functions
1:21:44 we can use color in numerous ways
1:21:46 we could use it to denote something
1:21:48 being active
1:21:50 here just the green that's different
1:21:52 from the blues suggests that something
1:21:54 has been turned on or is an active
1:21:57 element
1:21:58 and we could take that same idea and
1:22:00 take the green button and turn it into a
1:22:02 red button to suggest it being inactive
1:22:05 as well as signifying an event or
1:22:07 activity color also works as part of a
1:22:10 system
1:22:11 here for instance we have one of three
1:22:13 things selected if you were to click on
1:22:15 a blue button now would it turn to red
1:22:18 and would the red turn to blue
1:22:20 as well as showing us what's active
1:22:22 color can also show us what's inactive
1:22:25 or passive
1:22:26 here the element that's different has a
1:22:28 more muted color so we know that it's
1:22:30 the thing that is actually not working
1:22:33 rather than working
1:22:34 you could also think about how color can
1:22:36 be used to show progression
1:22:38 in this case we've got three possible
1:22:40 stages and it feels like we're
1:22:42 two-thirds of the way to completion
1:22:45 because we have two out of a possible
1:22:48 three areas lit
1:22:50 we can also look at how color can show
1:22:52 absolutely different states but within
1:22:55 the same space
1:22:56 so one button or one space can take on
1:23:00 different colors to tell us different
1:23:01 things so here for instance this button
1:23:04 is gray it's passive or inactive which
1:23:07 means the user hasn't interacted with it
1:23:09 at all but once the user goes to
1:23:12 interact with it the button becomes
1:23:14 active not activated but active so this
1:23:18 could be in a touch state or when a
1:23:20 mouse is hovering over a button for
1:23:22 instance
1:23:23 and once it's actually been pressed or
1:23:25 clicked or touched then it becomes
1:23:27 activated so the same button is telling
1:23:30 the user three different things just by
1:23:32 changing its color
1:23:35 so now let's look a little bit at shapes
1:23:38 and how we use shapes in user interface
1:23:40 design
1:23:41 we primarily use shapes in two different
1:23:43 ways in interface design
1:23:45 one is as interface elements the buttons
1:23:48 that you click on the areas of
1:23:50 navigation where you need to go
1:23:52 and the other is as design elements in
1:23:54 other words shapes that are mixed up
1:23:56 with images that are there more for look
1:23:58 and feel
1:24:00 our interface elements are primarily
1:24:01 functional so we're talking about shapes
1:24:04 that work as part of a system but are
1:24:06 also often geometric simple to
1:24:09 understand easy for the user to access
1:24:12 when we use shapes as design elements
1:24:15 they're often less systematic much more
1:24:18 emotive and sometimes more ephemeral
1:24:20 they're much more about the look and
1:24:22 feel of the interface than they are
1:24:24 about the functionality of the interface
1:24:26 and in this way they often contribute to
1:24:28 that look and feel and not to the
1:24:31 navigation itself in many ways they
1:24:33 could be viewed as non-essential
1:24:35 elements but my argument would be that
1:24:38 while they're not an essential part of
1:24:40 the navigation they are essential in
1:24:42 terms of creating a mood creating a
1:24:44 sense of individuality and creating a
1:24:47 graphic interface otherwise all
1:24:49 interfaces would look the same picking a
1:24:52 certain kind of shape can have a big
1:24:53 effect on the interface whether it's a
1:24:56 diagonal or a square or a circle for
1:24:59 instance just as a basic form might make
1:25:02 a difference between having a lot of
1:25:03 direction versus a lot of stasis versus
1:25:07 feeling very organic we use shapes for
1:25:09 our interface elements because of their
1:25:11 functionality and it's a certain kind of
1:25:14 shape that we use
1:25:15 normally ones that are geometric that
1:25:18 are simple that are systematic so that
1:25:21 they can be fast to understand and they
1:25:23 can make a system that's fast for the
1:25:25 user to visually learn and to interact
1:25:28 with we often use shapes and motifs that
1:25:30 we're familiar with as buttons in the
1:25:32 real world
1:25:34 but the key thing here is that they have
1:25:36 a relationship to what is pressing the
1:25:38 button or the active area so whether
1:25:41 that's the cursor of the mouse or
1:25:43 whether it's the finger we're building a
1:25:45 system that relates to the user and how
1:25:47 they interact with the content but we're
1:25:50 also making a system that's flexible
1:25:52 where we could label these buttons with
1:25:54 anything that we wanted to
1:25:57 in this section we're going to look at
1:25:59 how to use imagery in interface design
1:26:01 the most common use of imagery is as
1:26:03 content in your app or in your website
1:26:06 but we can also use imagery to create a
1:26:08 mood or we can use imagery as part of
1:26:10 our navigation or part of our interface
1:26:13 system
1:26:14 so let's start out by looking at imagery
1:26:16 as content
1:26:18 we live in an image-based society where
1:26:20 we're constantly surrounded by images
1:26:23 but on our screens most of those images
1:26:25 are contained
1:26:27 if you think about videos or search
1:26:29 engines or the images that we look at
1:26:31 online they're nearly always images in
1:26:33 boxes
1:26:35 so they're really content that's being
1:26:37 shown in a frame here and that content
1:26:40 can be anything
1:26:41 it's really not about being part of the
1:26:43 interface it's being the thing that
1:26:45 we're delivering to the audience so if
1:26:48 we're doing the search for instance for
1:26:49 something that we want to buy we want to
1:26:51 visually see all the different
1:26:53 variations that we could possibly have
1:26:55 and the fastest way to do that is
1:26:57 through imagery and that imagery is
1:26:59 contained and organized
1:27:02 when images break out of the box and
1:27:04 become their own world they act as
1:27:06 immersive imagery and you see this much
1:27:08 more often in games sometimes in apps
1:27:11 but in animations as well where the
1:27:14 imagery creates a whole universe a whole
1:27:16 world where things don't have to live in
1:27:18 boxes anymore the way we use contained
1:27:21 images is much more based on traditional
1:27:23 graphic design it's really based on
1:27:25 print layout where you have a grid and
1:27:28 you put things into a box but in the
1:27:30 immersive world there's a little bit
1:27:32 more fluidity it's based much more on
1:27:34 video games and animation and film and
1:27:37 you tend to have more of a correlation
1:27:39 between what the interface looks like
1:27:41 and what the content looks like
1:27:44 both contained imagery and immersive
1:27:46 imagery come in all different kinds of
1:27:48 styles so there can be illustration
1:27:51 there can be photography
1:27:52 3d renderings pretty much any style that
1:27:55 you want to make an image in
1:27:58 the benefit of the contained image is
1:28:00 that it's very pragmatic you can put
1:28:02 anything into a box and have it sit side
1:28:04 by side with anything else so you could
1:28:06 take illustrations and drawings have
1:28:09 them sit next to photographs and the
1:28:11 grid the boxes that you're putting them
1:28:13 in levels across all the content so it
1:28:16 becomes a very functional comparative
1:28:19 structure for you to use in an interface
1:28:21 design so you see it especially when you
1:28:23 need to work across a lot of content
1:28:26 that needs to be searched or work across
1:28:28 a lot of different sized platforms
1:28:31 when imagery isn't there as the content
1:28:34 it's often there to create a mood
1:28:37 you might be designing an interface for
1:28:39 an app that doesn't have a service that
1:28:41 is image based so for instance
1:28:45 uber doesn't want to show somebody
1:28:46 getting in and out of a cab but they'd
1:28:48 rather show a stylish illustration that
1:28:51 makes it feel a certain way
1:28:53 feel a little bit upmarket to set a tone
1:28:56 for what the service is you can see
1:28:58 images working really hard to create
1:29:01 moods if you look at stock photo sites
1:29:04 the whole purpose of the photography
1:29:06 here is to actually be non-specific and
1:29:08 most of the time to create
1:29:10 a feeling or a mood that the audience is
1:29:12 going to relate to rather than having a
1:29:15 specific content in the image
1:29:17 sometimes when you look at mock-ups for
1:29:19 websites they're quite often using stock
1:29:22 images to try and create the right kind
1:29:24 of ambience or mood that's going to
1:29:27 appeal to that particular audience or
1:29:29 relate to that kind of content
1:29:31 due to good old aesthetics imagery can't
1:29:34 just be content imagery always has a
1:29:37 visual form and that visual form always
1:29:40 has some style or aesthetic attached to
1:29:42 it so thereby it creates a mood here's
1:29:45 some images from calarts website and
1:29:47 while they're trying to depict the
1:29:49 events that are happening at the school
1:29:51 they're also trying to have a visual
1:29:53 form that expresses the energy and
1:29:55 exuberance of the school
1:29:57 color composition and visual aesthetics
1:30:00 can be really important in creating an
1:30:02 image that's going to engage an audience
1:30:05 and that might be done in an overt way
1:30:07 where the image looks very constructed
1:30:09 and clearly has a concept behind it as
1:30:12 in this example here or it can also be
1:30:15 done in a way that's a little more
1:30:16 invisible these images from apple are
1:30:19 very carefully constructed to show the
1:30:21 products off in their best light but to
1:30:23 also sell a lifestyle and sell an
1:30:26 aesthetic that goes along with apple
1:30:28 products so they're selling you both
1:30:30 content and mood lifestyle at the same
1:30:33 time
1:30:34 imagery can also be used as a tool for
1:30:37 navigation but most often this happens
1:30:40 in a very reduced form partly because
1:30:42 the image has to work at a small size
1:30:45 but it also has to be very recognizable
1:30:47 but imagery can do much more than just
1:30:49 being a symbol or an icon imagery can be
1:30:52 a more integral part of the interface
1:30:55 images can work really well as buttons
1:30:57 as part of your navigation system and
1:31:00 this is partly because they accurately
1:31:02 represent exactly what you're going to
1:31:04 get when you click on them there's no
1:31:06 ambiguity there
1:31:07 they can also work really well if the
1:31:09 thing you're trying to represent is very
1:31:11 difficult to describe with words but
1:31:14 easy to show with an image and as we
1:31:16 already talked about every time you use
1:31:18 an image as well as doing whatever
1:31:20 function you've assigned to it it's also
1:31:23 going to have an aesthetic function
1:31:24 there's also going to be mood and tone
1:31:27 there so for instance a map on waze
1:31:30 looks very different to a map on google
1:31:33 maps
1:31:34 waze is trying to create a visual
1:31:36 aesthetic that suits its user base and
1:31:39 suits its audience it's trying to be
1:31:41 more youthful more fun and perhaps a
1:31:44 little bit irreverent
1:31:45 but along with the aesthetics the map
1:31:48 still functions in a pragmatic way the
1:31:50 symbols in the interface all work
1:31:52 perfectly
1:31:54 so our imagery is doing multiple things
1:31:56 at the same time it's acting as mood
1:31:59 it's acting as part of the interface but
1:32:01 it's also the content in this case as
1:32:03 well because it's the map itself and the
1:32:06 cars and the accidents and everything
1:32:08 that we want to see
1:32:09 so you can see that happening here in
1:32:12 this interface designed by one of my
1:32:13 students where it's purely an
1:32:15 image-based interface
1:32:18 so the content the navigation
1:32:20 and the aesthetics are all being driven
1:32:23 by the same imagery and that imagery is
1:32:26 controlled and aestheticized to make a
1:32:28 beautiful interface
1:32:30 so you can have imagery as content
1:32:33 imagery that's there just for mood or
1:32:35 imagery that's there for navigation but
1:32:37 you can never avoid the aesthetics of
1:32:39 the image you can actually never avoid
1:32:42 the mood so even if your content is the
1:32:45 only place that you have imagery it's
1:32:47 going to have some implications for what
1:32:49 the app or the website looks and feels
1:32:51 like and the same for your navigation
1:32:54 you can't isolate the content and the
1:32:56 navigation from the aesthetics and the
1:32:59 mood of the imagery you're always going
1:33:01 to have to deal with it in one way or
1:33:03 another
1:33:04 and what this is acknowledging is that
1:33:06 we're creating visual interfaces here
1:33:08 what used to be called graphical user
1:33:11 interfaces
1:33:12 we're not creating just lists of words
1:33:15 for our content or we're not creating
1:33:17 just words for our navigation everything
1:33:20 has a form and because it has a form it
1:33:23 has an aesthetic value it can create a
1:33:26 mood and there's no way of escaping that
1:33:29 your content will always have mood and
1:33:31 your navigation or interface design will
1:33:34 always have mood as well so it's really
1:33:36 worth thinking about the visual
1:33:38 aesthetics and how you use imagery and
1:33:41 the quality of the imagery that you have
1:33:43 because you can't escape the fact that
1:33:45 it's going to have an effect on the user
1:33:49 in this section we're going to look at
1:33:51 some ideas around the use of typography
1:33:53 in the user interface
1:33:55 type can work in many different ways as
1:33:58 text it can be the content for your app
1:34:00 or your website it can act as part of
1:34:02 the interface in terms of labels and
1:34:04 buttons or it could be part of a
1:34:06 branding system to give your apple
1:34:08 website an identity
1:34:10 let's start out by looking at type as
1:34:12 content
1:34:14 why do we even read on screens anymore
1:34:16 when we have video and audio to get our
1:34:18 information through
1:34:20 well the answer is that text does quite
1:34:22 a few things that it's quite good at
1:34:24 it's language made physical text is very
1:34:28 fast so it's very accessible it's quite
1:34:30 accurate in terms of trying to describe
1:34:33 something it's very economical in terms
1:34:36 of how it works on a screen but most
1:34:38 importantly it can represent what can't
1:34:40 be depicted
1:34:42 if we think about how text translates to
1:34:44 texting we can see that the things that
1:34:47 text is good at get amplified even more
1:34:49 texting becomes faster becomes more
1:34:52 economical it starts to use images
1:34:55 emojis in with the text itself
1:34:59 on screen we start to change the very
1:35:01 structure of language
1:35:03 as well as changing what we consider to
1:35:05 be a typographic character
1:35:08 far from being eclipsed by video and
1:35:10 audio and images
1:35:12 text is becoming more of a prevalent
1:35:14 means of communication on the screen and
1:35:17 if anything it's absorbing imagery into
1:35:19 its language
1:35:21 it's also starting to reconfigure itself
1:35:24 to alter itself to work in a new digital
1:35:26 realm websites blogs texts tweets
1:35:30 they're all fundamentally text based
1:35:33 still and that text has to have a visual
1:35:36 form it has to have a typographic
1:35:38 representation so it becomes a design
1:35:40 problem it becomes part of an interface
1:35:42 design
1:35:44 we don't really think about it so much
1:35:46 but text is still one of the main
1:35:48 vehicles that we use for carrying
1:35:51 knowledge and for imparting and sharing
1:35:53 that knowledge through digital media so
1:35:56 type has an integral role in terms of
1:35:58 how it works as content but it also has
1:36:01 an integral role in terms of how it
1:36:03 works as part of the interface
1:36:06 and this is because type can do things
1:36:08 that images and icons can't so we can
1:36:10 ask ourselves the same questions why do
1:36:13 we need type as part of an interface why
1:36:15 don't we just have icons for everything
1:36:18 a lot of the answers to why we have type
1:36:20 as part of our interface are the same as
1:36:23 why we use text for content it's
1:36:25 language made physical and it has all
1:36:27 the advantages of language
1:36:29 types very fast it's accurate and clear
1:36:32 meaning and economical and also again it
1:36:35 can represent what can't be depicted so
1:36:38 let's look at some examples type is fast
1:36:40 to read
1:36:42 when we see a stop sign for instance we
1:36:44 know exactly what it means straight away
1:36:47 we barely even have to read the type we
1:36:49 recognize the form of the type and it
1:36:52 has a very accurate and clear meaning
1:36:54 there's no confusion there there's no
1:36:56 ambiguity at all stop only means one
1:36:59 thing stop and go only means one thing
1:37:02 go there's absolutely no gray area there
1:37:05 there's no interpretation and this can
1:37:08 be very useful in terms of designing an
1:37:10 interface language is very economical in
1:37:12 terms of its meaning
1:37:14 not just a single word either
1:37:16 we can take a letter and we can use that
1:37:19 to represent something so we know that
1:37:21 this i stands in for information for
1:37:24 instance
1:37:26 but we also know that p part of the same
1:37:28 alphabetic system has a totally
1:37:30 different meaning and means something
1:37:32 else and there's a level of modularity
1:37:35 and flexibility to language
1:37:38 we can add another letter to that p and
1:37:41 have the meaning totally change even
1:37:43 though the form itself hasn't changed in
1:37:46 fact the form of all three of these
1:37:48 images is the same but the meaning is
1:37:51 very very different so language has a
1:37:54 great deal of flexibility in terms of
1:37:56 how it works
1:37:57 it can also be useful in terms of
1:37:59 interface design because it can work at
1:38:01 greatly different scales sometimes
1:38:04 images don't have that same flexibility
1:38:06 and we end up making icons or symbols
1:38:08 often out of type but most importantly
1:38:11 type can represent what can't be
1:38:13 depicted and it takes a little moment to
1:38:16 unpack what this statement really means
1:38:18 but think about as an example a word
1:38:21 like democracy
1:38:22 how would you represent this word
1:38:24 visually it would actually be quite
1:38:26 difficult
1:38:27 there's not really a single iconic image
1:38:29 to represent democracy it's always going
1:38:32 to be a little bit more ambiguous as an
1:38:34 image than it is when it's represented
1:38:36 with language
1:38:38 even the words surrounding democracy
1:38:40 that might describe it government voting
1:38:42 elections politics when you turn those
1:38:45 into the form of imagery there still
1:38:47 have a they still have a lot of overlap
1:38:49 between how they might be read
1:38:52 so words are great for the accurate
1:38:54 representation of complex non-visual
1:38:57 concepts
1:38:58 like democracy for instance and this is
1:39:01 great for an interface designer because
1:39:03 quite often we'll be trying to show
1:39:06 things that are complex non-visual
1:39:09 concepts and these appear in a lot of
1:39:11 different places in menus
1:39:14 naming buttons instructions
1:39:17 in fact type is everywhere in our
1:39:19 interface because it's still the fastest
1:39:21 and most accurate way we have to
1:39:23 communicate complex and specific ideas
1:39:27 finally let's look at type as branding
1:39:30 here type has a slightly different job
1:39:32 to play it's much less about it being
1:39:35 content and about it conveying what it
1:39:37 actually says and it's much more about
1:39:40 connotation in other words what does the
1:39:42 form of the type look like what kind of
1:39:45 things does it suggest and this can be a
1:39:47 way to create a sense of identity or
1:39:50 individuality and this can lead to a
1:39:53 visual typographic brand that can be
1:39:55 really really helpful in terms of making
1:39:58 your app or your website stand out and
1:40:00 look different from everybody else or it
1:40:02 can be a way of making it build sympathy
1:40:04 to a certain kind of content the
1:40:06 combination of idiosyncratic or
1:40:09 individual form and the right kind of
1:40:11 formal connotations can lead to branding
1:40:14 typographically most often expressed as
1:40:17 a logo type so if we look at some
1:40:19 examples here we could take some large
1:40:21 companies and see what they look like
1:40:23 without any form typographic form is a
1:40:26 lot like clothes for words the meaning
1:40:29 is still there but it's dressed up with
1:40:31 a different surface a different form i'm
1:40:33 willing to guess that it's hard for you
1:40:35 to look at these words
1:40:36 and not think about the intrinsic form
1:40:39 that these logo types are normally seen
1:40:41 in we're used to seeing adidas look a
1:40:43 certain way with its round repetitive
1:40:45 forms we're used to seeing nike as both
1:40:48 a logo type a typographic form and as a
1:40:51 mark or a logo in other words in this
1:40:54 case the swoosh and we see those things
1:40:56 separately and recognize them but we
1:40:58 also see them together the logo type and
1:41:01 logo work together in harmony to form a
1:41:03 single lock up
1:41:05 there are other systems that you can use
1:41:07 to think about to create an identity
1:41:09 typographically
1:41:11 some of them could be color dependent
1:41:12 for instance netflix we see as being red
1:41:15 and black and when we look at the logo
1:41:17 type and then we look at a smaller
1:41:19 version of it a bug or a button we can
1:41:22 see that it's not the same letter form
1:41:24 that's there it's actually the color
1:41:26 that is creating the continuity between
1:41:29 the two different versions and you can
1:41:32 take this idea of flexibility or an
1:41:34 extended system to an even greater
1:41:36 extent and think about a much more
1:41:38 modular system that can be applied to a
1:41:41 lot of different artifacts or might even
1:41:43 be a system where things change
1:41:45 depending on the time of day or the time
1:41:47 of year i think google is a good example
1:41:50 of a wider system in terms of how their
1:41:53 new identity is applied to a lot of
1:41:54 different components
1:41:56 but also how they create a set of
1:41:58 sympathetic imagery that's forever
1:42:00 changing the big takeaway here is that
1:42:02 type matters if we turn these logo types
1:42:04 back to a more generic black and white
1:42:06 form we can see how much they lose in
1:42:09 terms of being recognizable and in terms
1:42:11 of their individual identity
1:42:15 in this section we're going to look at
1:42:17 icons and see how icons work as part of
1:42:20 our user interface
1:42:22 we're very familiar with icons we see
1:42:24 thousands of them every day
1:42:26 every app has to have a visual
1:42:28 representation of what that app is
1:42:31 in a small and condensed form where the
1:42:34 user can recognize it so every app has
1:42:36 an icon or symbol to represent it
1:42:39 and those icons or symbols have to
1:42:41 compete with each other and there are
1:42:43 thousands of them all vying for your
1:42:45 attention
1:42:46 trying to create visual interest and
1:42:48 trying to be recognizable
1:42:51 but there's another place where we also
1:42:53 see icons and symbols working and that's
1:42:55 in the interface itself
1:42:58 these icons were based on objects in the
1:43:00 real world and it's interesting to see
1:43:02 how little they've changed over the last
1:43:04 20 years where their form has been
1:43:06 modified the essential essence of the
1:43:09 icon has stayed the same
1:43:11 one of the things that people often get
1:43:13 confused about is the difference between
1:43:15 an icon and a symbol
1:43:17 and part of this reason is we tend to
1:43:19 use the word icon for both symbols and
1:43:21 icons so let's look at what the
1:43:23 difference is between the two of them
1:43:27 both of them represent other things
1:43:30 an icon does that by showing us a
1:43:32 picture of the thing
1:43:34 it shows us a visual representation of
1:43:36 it that is relatively realistic compared
1:43:39 to what the object is so in this case
1:43:43 here's a set of headphones and it looks
1:43:44 like the headphones but a symbol doesn't
1:43:47 necessarily look like the thing that it
1:43:49 represents
1:43:51 so for instance here's music and it's
1:43:52 represented by notes
1:43:55 so the icon is pictorial
1:43:57 whereas the symbol is non-pictorial it's
1:44:00 not an accurate visual representation of
1:44:03 the thing that is representing
1:44:05 and it would be hard to represent
1:44:06 something as broad and something as
1:44:09 abstract as music with a fixed single
1:44:12 visual image
1:44:14 so to sum it up icons
1:44:16 really do look like the thing that
1:44:18 they're representing and symbols tend to
1:44:20 not look like the thing that they're
1:44:22 representing one is pictorial and the
1:44:24 other is non-pictorial
1:44:26 so sometimes you're going to want to
1:44:28 create an icon to represent your ideas
1:44:32 and sometimes you're going to want to
1:44:33 create a symbol and it really depends on
1:44:36 what the thing is that you want to
1:44:37 represent
1:44:39 for instance a dagger is very easy to
1:44:42 show a picture of but if we're talking
1:44:44 about a concept something as abstract as
1:44:47 thought for instance it's quite hard for
1:44:49 you to think of an image of that so we
1:44:52 have to create a symbol to represent
1:44:54 that concept or that idea
1:44:57 it becomes a little confusing when
1:44:59 things are ambiguous and can work as
1:45:01 icons or symbols
1:45:03 this lightning bolt for instance could
1:45:04 be an icon because it's a picture of
1:45:06 lightning but it could also be a symbol
1:45:08 for electricity something that doesn't
1:45:10 necessarily have a visible form but
1:45:13 needs a symbol to represent it and often
1:45:16 we can assign meaning to things that can
1:45:19 make them work as icons and symbols so
1:45:22 as an icon the magnifying glass looks
1:45:24 like a magnifying glass so it represents
1:45:27 that but as a symbol it stands in for
1:45:29 the idea or concept of searching and
1:45:32 what's interesting about the magnifying
1:45:34 glass is we use this as a very common
