1:45:37 icon for two different things we use it
1:45:39 when we want to zoom in and zoom out
1:45:42 which is its iconic function but we also
1:45:44 use it when we want to type text in to
1:45:46 search for something which is its
1:45:48 symbolic function
1:45:49 our interface elements can work as icons
1:45:52 and symbols but so can our branding
1:45:54 elements here's something that we
1:45:56 instantly recognize
1:45:57 but not so much in its iconic form as
1:46:00 apple the fruit but in its symbolic form
1:46:03 as apple the company and that's to do
1:46:06 with our particular familiarity with
1:46:08 this rendering of the form of an apple
1:46:11 it becomes a symbol that is
1:46:13 representative of a company that we've
1:46:15 all learned to make a connection to we
1:46:18 don't even see an apple and think of
1:46:20 food anymore when we see it drawn in
1:46:22 this way we can only think of the
1:46:24 company so the symbolic representation
1:46:27 in this form has totally eclipsed the
1:46:29 literal depictive value of it some of
1:46:32 the best icons or symbols tend to keep
1:46:34 hold of their literal value and their
1:46:37 learnt value so let's look a little bit
1:46:39 more at how that works we can have icons
1:46:42 that work in a purely iconic way but
1:46:44 they can also overlap and become a
1:46:46 symbol and in the same way
1:46:48 symbols can overlap and become icons as
1:46:51 well
1:46:52 and if we can enter this middle ground
1:46:54 where we can get the value from both the
1:46:57 iconic meaning and the symbolic meaning
1:47:00 it gives us a much richer element to
1:47:02 work with
1:47:03 so we're trying to connect the meaning
1:47:06 between the icon and the symbol
1:47:08 to create this very rich graphic element
1:47:11 that we can use in our interface or we
1:47:13 can use in our branding the goal here is
1:47:16 to combine what is known what is
1:47:19 visually obvious
1:47:20 with what is learned in other words what
1:47:23 is associative and might not be visually
1:47:25 obvious let's look at some other
1:47:27 examples of icons and symbols that are
1:47:30 commonly used in interfaces we use a
1:47:33 pair of scissors to represent the idea
1:47:35 of cutting and pasting but in the real
1:47:37 world scissors actually function just as
1:47:40 devices for cutting and mostly just for
1:47:42 paper not for files or text or other
1:47:45 things in the digital world
1:47:46 likewise a trash can is the place that
1:47:48 we throw files away
1:47:50 we delete our files there but we're not
1:47:53 actually physically throwing anything
1:47:54 away we're dealing with digital files
1:47:57 that we're deleting off our computer but
1:47:59 a metaphor from the real world helps us
1:48:02 understand how to operate in the digital
1:48:04 world
1:48:06 likewise if we wanted to send an email
1:48:08 it doesn't actually look anything like a
1:48:10 letter but we're used to seeing this
1:48:12 symbol to represent the idea of sending
1:48:15 electronic mail
1:48:17 so we use antiquated things from the
1:48:19 real world to represent new things in
1:48:22 the digital world
1:48:23 symbolic meanings have to be learned so
1:48:26 when we look at this image of a bird we
1:48:28 don't actually connect it to a bird we
1:48:31 connect it to twitter the company so we
1:48:34 know that when we click on this bird
1:48:36 we're going to send a tweet we're not
1:48:38 going to go to a bird watching site for
1:48:41 instance
1:48:42 so there's a connection here between how
1:48:44 our interface elements might work and
1:48:46 have meaning and how our branding
1:48:48 elements work and have meaning
1:48:50 to put it another way quite often
1:48:52 there's a merging between identity and
1:48:55 interface
1:48:56 and you can see this with something like
1:48:58 twitter for instance if we look at the
1:49:00 wide range of visual representations of
1:49:03 that particular company in a symbolic
1:49:06 form
1:49:07 where any of these things could be
1:49:08 buttons and we'd know exactly what we
1:49:11 were going to get if we clicked on them
1:49:13 we're going to get to twitter we're not
1:49:15 going to get to a page that's about bird
1:49:17 songs
1:49:19 in this introductory class we're only
1:49:21 really going to touch on these ideas of
1:49:24 creating symbols and icons for both
1:49:26 interface and identity and if you were
1:49:28 doing this in the real world this would
1:49:30 be a specialization where you'd be
1:49:33 really working as an icon designer and
1:49:36 doing very little else in part of the ui
1:49:38 ux process so we can look at something
1:49:40 like google and see how much work goes
1:49:43 into really extending that system across
1:49:46 a number of different icons and creating
1:49:48 a really strong visual identity and a
1:49:51 lot of time and effort and precision is
1:49:54 put into creating each of these icons or
1:49:56 symbols so again it becomes quite a
1:49:59 specialist job within the ui ux world
1:50:02 so let's look at some simple rules for
1:50:05 what might make a good icon
1:50:07 think about what you want your icon to
1:50:09 communicate it needs an idea and it
1:50:12 needs to communicate that through its
1:50:13 aesthetics as well
1:50:15 think about its functionality it's got
1:50:17 to work at different sizes it's got to
1:50:19 be clear and legible you could think
1:50:22 about the audience it has to be
1:50:24 recognizable and it has to be
1:50:26 understandable and lastly think about
1:50:29 visual recognition this could come from
1:50:31 saturation from seeing your icon
1:50:33 everywhere or it could come from just
1:50:35 creating a simple striking and memorable
1:50:38 icon
1:50:44 [Music]
1:50:48 this week we're going to take our static
1:50:50 interface elements and begin to think
1:50:52 about how a user interacts with them
1:50:54 in other words how to bring these
1:50:56 elements a stage closer to having a life
1:50:58 on the screen
1:51:00 we'll be looking at navigational
1:51:01 conventions such as menus buttons and
1:51:04 icons in different states
1:51:07 our focus will move from what the
1:51:08 graphic interface looks like to include
1:51:11 how it works how it responds to the user
1:51:15 by adding interactivity to our static
1:51:17 designs we'll begin to think more deeply
1:51:19 about the role the designer plays in
1:51:22 shaping a user's interactive experience
1:51:26 this week we're going to continue to
1:51:28 develop the overall style and aesthetics
1:51:31 of our interface design but we're going
1:51:33 to look at it from a slightly different
1:51:34 perspective we're going to start to
1:51:36 think more about the elements of our
1:51:38 interface being active and less about
1:51:40 them just being static we're going to
1:51:43 introduce the idea of function to our
1:51:46 form so we're going to have to start to
1:51:48 think about what do these elements do as
1:51:50 well as what they look like
1:51:52 but we're not going to totally give up
1:51:54 on what the interface looks like we're
1:51:56 not going to abandon design as a tool
1:51:59 it's less about what the interface looks
1:52:01 like versus how the interface works and
1:52:04 much more about how the look of the
1:52:05 interface is connected to its function
1:52:08 or how it works at the start of this
1:52:11 class we pulled apart ui and ux we
1:52:15 separated them in order to understand
1:52:17 better how they work but to be honest
1:52:19 they're truly inseparable because the
1:52:22 form is always connected to the function
1:52:24 in some way our ui is always connected
1:52:27 to our ux
1:52:29 they have a relationship and even when
1:52:31 we want to focus on the form we can't
1:52:34 because it's connected to the experience
1:52:36 and likewise we can't focus solely on
1:52:38 the experience because it's connected to
1:52:40 the form
1:52:41 experience function and form have a
1:52:44 triangular relationship where they're
1:52:47 all connected to each other we can focus
1:52:50 on any one of these aspects of user
1:52:52 interface design and we can concentrate
1:52:55 on that one aspect but the other two are
1:52:57 always going to be there in the
1:52:58 background they're always going to have
1:53:00 some kind of input on our design
1:53:03 decisions we spent the last week
1:53:05 focusing on form and the next week we're
1:53:07 going to focus more on experience but
1:53:10 for this week we're going to let
1:53:12 function take the driving seat we're
1:53:14 going to look at logic intuition and
1:53:16 learned behavior as well as organization
1:53:19 and hierarchy within our interface
1:53:23 let's look at some basic user interface
1:53:25 functionality to begin with we could ask
1:53:28 ourselves the simplest questions
1:53:30 how does an interface work
1:53:32 how do we know how to interact with an
1:53:35 interface
1:53:36 how do we know what's interactive and
1:53:38 what's not is it all just trial and
1:53:40 error or are there rules or intuitive
1:53:44 behavior that inform the way that we
1:53:46 interact
1:53:48 let's take a very basic interface
1:53:50 element if we have these three circles
1:53:52 with three different colors what kind of
1:53:54 expectation do we have of what's going
1:53:57 to happen when we interact with them we
1:53:59 don't really know until we try we might
1:54:02 have an expectation that we think
1:54:04 something red or green or blue is going
1:54:07 to happen but we don't know what that is
1:54:09 until we touch or click on that
1:54:11 particular button but once we've
1:54:13 interacted and seen the outcome of that
1:54:16 interaction we can predict what the
1:54:18 other interactions are going to do we
1:54:21 have an expectation that's quite logical
1:54:24 so if we know that blue made blue then
1:54:26 we're pretty sure that green is going to
1:54:28 turn the background green and red is
1:54:30 going to turn the background red
1:54:32 in this very simple way we've created a
1:54:35 logical system and the benefit of this
1:54:37 logical system is that it's predictable
1:54:40 by just clicking on one button the blue
1:54:43 button
1:54:44 we've already understood how the other
1:54:46 two buttons work without even clicking
1:54:48 on them we've made a very economical
1:54:50 system for our user they didn't have to
1:54:53 expend a lot of time or energy to
1:54:55 understand how the whole system works as
1:54:58 long as we stay logical
1:55:00 once we break the system and make it
1:55:02 illogical for instance if the blue dot
1:55:04 turned the background pink we've created
1:55:07 a system that is going to take the user
1:55:08 a lot longer to learn because the system
1:55:11 has stopped being predictable we can
1:55:13 extend our idea of logic and the
1:55:16 predictable system a little bit further
1:55:18 if we click on the green dot and it
1:55:19 turns the background green the dot
1:55:22 itself could change in response it now
1:55:24 becomes black so not only does this give
1:55:27 us another color to work with it also
1:55:30 lets the user know that their action has
1:55:32 had an effect
1:55:34 one of the benefits of having a logical
1:55:36 and predictable system as part of your
1:55:38 interface is that it can be expandable
1:55:41 to the nth degree so once i know how
1:55:44 this button works i know how any other
1:55:46 similar button to this works
1:55:49 so now we're looking at a system that is
1:55:51 logical predictable and expandable the
1:55:54 next question is how do we make it
1:55:56 economical as well
1:55:58 let's take our color dot example and
1:56:01 give it a little bit more of a real
1:56:02 world application
1:56:04 say for instance we wanted to color in
1:56:06 an image and we could pick different
1:56:08 colors for different parts of the image
1:56:10 we could change the eye color to blue if
1:56:13 we pick another color we're pretty sure
1:56:15 it's going to change the eyes to
1:56:17 whatever color we select in this case
1:56:19 pink
1:56:20 and we're already being economical in
1:56:21 some small way because one click is
1:56:24 changing two items we could also have it
1:56:26 so that each click changes just one of
1:56:28 the eyes
1:56:30 let's say that we want to change other
1:56:32 parts of the face as well and color
1:56:33 those in different colors maybe we want
1:56:35 to change the mouth the eyes and the
1:56:38 hair
1:56:39 one way of doing it would be to color
1:56:41 them all ahead of time so we could just
1:56:43 simply click on whichever ones we want
1:56:45 but again while this is visually
1:56:47 predictable it's not necessarily the
1:56:49 most economical way of creating an
1:56:51 interface
1:56:53 it does make it very clear what the
1:56:55 outcome is going to be from each of our
1:56:57 interface elements but it takes up a lot
1:56:59 of real estate on the screen in a lot of
1:57:02 ways we've unpacked all the
1:57:04 possibilities of interaction and laid
1:57:06 them out for the user to see all of them
1:57:08 what does work here is the visual
1:57:11 relationship between the button and the
1:57:13 image area
1:57:15 and that's because they're basically the
1:57:17 same thing
1:57:18 one is a smaller version of the other so
1:57:21 one way to be economical would be to
1:57:23 remove the color from those elements and
1:57:25 to give the user a two-step process
1:57:28 where they're picking the particular
1:57:30 part of the image they want to color and
1:57:32 then picking the color that they want in
1:57:35 a lot of ways we're adding an extra step
1:57:37 for the user but we're giving them many
1:57:39 more options because we're working in an
1:57:42 exponential manner
1:57:43 now i can choose any of these colors for
1:57:46 any of these elements
1:57:48 i might decide that i want to change the
1:57:50 color of the mouth and then i could pick
1:57:52 whatever color i wanted so in this case
1:57:54 i'm going to decide that i want it to be
1:57:56 blue and what we've basically done here
1:57:58 is given the user 18 choices out of nine
1:58:02 elements on the screen
1:58:04 so we've basically cut the real estate
1:58:06 of our interface in half
1:58:08 and we can continue to try and do more
1:58:10 with less in this interface by using
1:58:13 visual hierarchy we don't really need
1:58:16 those colors to be that large for
1:58:18 instance in order for us to understand
1:58:20 the differences between the colors
1:58:22 we could also arrange the colors in a
1:58:24 more economical way or arrange the
1:58:27 elements of the face in a way that they
1:58:29 had a better relationship to the main
1:58:31 image
1:58:32 and we can keep reducing our on-screen
1:58:35 real estate and keep increasing our
1:58:38 options for the user by some other means
1:58:41 as well we can shrink our repeated
1:58:43 components of the face down so they
1:58:45 start to look like icons and we could
1:58:47 easily take the same amount of space for
1:58:50 six colors and turn it into a color
1:58:52 wheel where we have thousands of choices
1:58:54 all we'd really need to do to get it to
1:58:56 work is to change the cursor from an
1:58:59 arrow to an eyedropper to let the user
1:59:02 know that they can sample any color
1:59:04 and once we've given ourselves
1:59:06 permission to change the cursor we could
1:59:09 question whether we even need to have
1:59:11 the repeated small icons for the face
1:59:14 instead we could use the arrow itself to
1:59:17 select those elements on the larger
1:59:19 image and then turn the cursor into an
1:59:22 eyedropper to select the color
1:59:25 in short we've compacted a lot of the
1:59:27 users options into the tool that they're
1:59:30 using we've empowered the user to give
1:59:33 them a multitude of choices and that's
1:59:36 going to feel much better as a user
1:59:38 because you're going to feel like you're
1:59:40 in control and you create a whole system
1:59:43 based around intuition again back to our
1:59:45 colored dots once we figure out how one
1:59:47 part of this works the eyes the mouth or
1:59:51 the hair we can figure out how all the
1:59:53 parts work and we could keep going with
1:59:56 being economical with our interface we
1:59:59 could condense down and compact the
2:00:01 whole color wheel
2:00:02 into any of these parts of the image on
2:00:05 the right hand side so the color wheel
2:00:07 might only appear when you needed it so
2:00:10 rather than laying out every option for
2:00:12 the user to be visible we're compacting
2:00:14 and condensing them into the interface
2:00:17 so let's try and sum up these ideas
2:00:19 about how the interface works
2:00:21 it seems like on the one side we have
2:00:23 logic hierarchy and systems and on the
2:00:26 other side we have intuition discovery
2:00:29 and learning
2:00:30 and this seems like quite a reasonable
2:00:32 way to think about interfaces as
2:00:34 interface designers we're trying to
2:00:36 create a logical system with a hierarchy
2:00:40 that's easy for a user to understand but
2:00:42 we also want that user to be able to
2:00:45 grow within that system so that you
2:00:47 don't have to show the user every single
2:00:50 thing that the interface can do in a
2:00:52 step-by-step manner you want there to be
2:00:55 some intuitive learning on the part of
2:00:57 the user you want your interface to be
2:00:59 understandable but not 100 a generic
2:01:03 experience in a lot of ways you're
2:01:05 turning over the driving seat to the
2:01:08 user and they have to feel like they're
2:01:09 in charge if you go too far towards the
2:01:12 functional end of the spectrum you end
2:01:14 up with an interface that's boring or
2:01:16 disinteresting to the user and if you
2:01:19 forget about the function the interface
2:01:21 simply doesn't work so it's about
2:01:23 finding a balance of both
2:01:27 in this segment we're going to look at
2:01:28 some ideas surrounding invisible and
2:01:30 visible user interfaces
2:01:33 and what we're really looking at here is
2:01:35 a notion that
2:01:36 by reducing the graphic content of an
2:01:38 interface and trimming it down to just
2:01:41 its bare essentials you can create a
2:01:43 much faster level of accessibility for
2:01:46 the user to get to the content and this
2:01:48 is a strategy that we see in
2:01:50 contemporary interface design and it's
2:01:52 fairly ubiquitous at the moment and for
2:01:55 good reason as well so some of the
2:01:57 advantages of having a slimmed down
2:01:59 scaled down interface
2:02:02 first of all that is very simple and
2:02:03 direct
2:02:04 so it's very easy for the user to
2:02:06 understand there's nothing that gets in
2:02:08 the way
2:02:10 and while there's nothing that gets in
2:02:11 the way visually there's also nothing
2:02:13 that gets in the way in terms of meaning
2:02:16 a high level of clarity creates a low
2:02:18 level of user mistakes
2:02:21 a simple interface can also serve many
2:02:23 purposes it can be adoptable by a wide
2:02:26 number of people for a wide number of
2:02:28 uses and simplicity and homogeneity can
2:02:32 create a familiar and predictable
2:02:34 interface across a number of different
2:02:36 bodies of content or different kinds of
2:02:38 experiences
2:02:40 above all simplicity builds logic which
2:02:43 means that pretty much anybody can
2:02:45 figure out how to use this interface
2:02:47 very very quickly
2:02:49 so what's against these ideas what could
2:02:52 possibly be bad about them
2:02:54 well the bottom line is that they can be
2:02:56 a little bit homogenous they can be a
2:02:58 little bit too familiar and too
2:03:00 predictable perhaps too unambiguous and
2:03:04 too logical so the end result can often
2:03:06 be that the experience even when the
2:03:09 content is interesting the experience is
2:03:11 boring
2:03:12 i'm not sure if i want all my digital
2:03:15 content to look the same way i'm not
2:03:17 sure that buying cool new shoes and
2:03:19 getting a root canal should have the
2:03:21 same interface
2:03:23 so let's look at the other side of the
2:03:25 equation
2:03:26 what happens when we think about a user
2:03:28 interface that is more graphic that is
2:03:31 more entertaining engaging a much more
2:03:35 rewarding experience a much more
2:03:37 idiosyncratic
2:03:38 where are the benefits in that system
2:03:41 and where are the downfalls
2:03:43 the benefits seem mostly to be not in
2:03:45 the content itself but in the experience
2:03:48 of accessing that content so is the
2:03:51 experience of getting to that hard
2:03:53 information engaging or entertaining
2:03:56 more so than with a generic interface
2:03:59 do i end up with a much more rewarding
2:04:01 experience ordering my pair of shoes
2:04:04 through an experiential website versus a
2:04:07 more generic functional website
2:04:09 and does the value of that experience
2:04:12 create something that is more brandable
2:04:14 or marketable
2:04:15 something that is idiosyncratic or
2:04:18 individual and might be the difference
2:04:20 between me buying my pair of shoes from
2:04:22 one website or another
2:04:25 the condensed rationale here
2:04:27 is that the experience has value so you
2:04:30 might be getting the same product but
2:04:32 the experience is the thing that gives
2:04:34 it worth
2:04:36 so what are the downsides of a less
2:04:38 generic interface
2:04:41 well while that might be entertaining it
2:04:43 can also obstruct content
2:04:45 sometimes all of those bells and
2:04:46 whistles can get in the way of what you
2:04:48 actually want to do
2:04:50 and while your interface experience
2:04:52 might be very engaging there are going
2:04:54 to be times where you just want to get
2:04:56 the thing you want to get you want to
2:04:58 get straight to the content without
2:04:59 wasting any time
2:05:01 and that lack of speed and functionality
2:05:04 can turn an engaging experience into a
2:05:06 frustrating one and while this interface
2:05:08 might offer opportunities for more
2:05:10 graphic design and to be more easily
2:05:12 brandable it could also create an
2:05:14 aesthetic that can date much more
2:05:16 rapidly so instead of ending up with a
2:05:18 cool interface that is unlike anybody
2:05:20 else's you end up with something that's
2:05:22 dysfunctional and rather unnecessary you
2:05:25 can end up with an interface that
2:05:27 creates frustration for the user because
2:05:30 it obstructs them getting to the content
2:05:32 that's the whole reason that they're
2:05:34 there in the first place
2:05:36 so there are pros and cons to both the
2:05:38 hyper functional interface and the hyper
2:05:41 stylistic interface
2:05:43 and as with many of the aspects of
2:05:45 interface design that we look at and
2:05:47 examine it's not a case of one thing
2:05:50 versus the other it's more a case of how
2:05:52 do you use these aspects together so how
2:05:55 do you have both speed and style
2:05:58 there are no absolutes here it's about
2:06:01 evaluating your content and seeing what
2:06:03 kind of approach is appropriate there
2:06:06 might be times where you want a very
2:06:08 functional website with very little
2:06:10 aesthetic distraction going on
2:06:13 i don't care so much what my database or
2:06:15 search engine looks like i care about
2:06:17 how it works
2:06:19 but there are other instances where this
2:06:21 is reversed if i'm buying clothes or
2:06:24 furniture i might be much more
2:06:26 interested in the style and the
2:06:27 aesthetics of the site or an app and the
2:06:30 experience of it than i am in the
2:06:32 functionality
2:06:33 let's look at a couple of examples that
2:06:35 mix function and aesthetics in different
2:06:38 ways
2:06:39 this is a project by one of my students
2:06:42 that's a bird watching app called aviary
2:06:45 due to the content and the audience it
2:06:47 needs to be very very functional but
2:06:49 that doesn't mean it has to look boring
2:06:52 the overall design is clean and simple
2:06:55 it follows a lot of interface
2:06:56 conventions that the user would be very
2:06:58 familiar with so there's no big learning
2:07:01 curve when somebody would come to use
2:07:03 this app
2:07:04 it feels logical functional and familiar
2:07:07 all at the same time
2:07:09 but it also has enough design elements
2:07:11 in there for it to feel very
2:07:13 idiosyncratic and individual and also to
2:07:16 feel quite stylish and up market the
2:07:18 color palette and the customized icons
2:07:21 really give it its own identity so while
2:07:23 the design is very clean familiar and
2:07:25 functional there's enough of an
2:07:27 individual voice there to really give it
2:07:29 some character in this second student
2:07:32 project we can see an inversion of that
2:07:35 balance between the pragmatic and the
2:07:38 poetic
2:07:39 here the emphasis is much more on
2:07:41 creating a novel interface design and
2:07:44 aesthetics take the lead here and any
2:07:47 kind of normalized functionality has
2:07:49 just gone out of the window but that
2:07:51 doesn't mean that it doesn't work or
2:07:53 have its own logic as an interface what
2:07:56 it does mean is that the user might have
2:07:58 to spend a little bit more time with it
2:08:00 in order to figure it out and how it
2:08:02 works but there's a sense of reward
2:08:04 there the app is about adventurous
2:08:06 places to eat with unusual food so it
2:08:09 makes sense for it to have a much more
2:08:11 adventurous and unusual interface this
2:08:14 interface works because it has a great
2:08:16 relationship with its content
2:08:19 there's a refrain made popular by golden
2:08:21 krishna that the best interface is no
2:08:24 interface at all
2:08:25 and i don't think that's always true the
2:08:28 best interface is the one that has the
2:08:30 best relationship to its content the one
2:08:32 that's the most sympathetic
2:08:34 and sometimes that does mean that it's
2:08:36 invisible and sometimes it's not the
2:08:38 bottom line is the best interface is the
2:08:41 best interface and there is not one
2:08:43 solution to that because an interface
2:08:45 has to deal with so many different kinds
2:08:47 of content so the best interface is one
2:08:50 that is visible and invisible at the
2:08:52 same time but in the right proportions
2:08:55 and those proportions are going to
2:08:57 change depending on what kind of content
2:09:00 there being an interface
2:09:02 for in this section we're going to look
2:09:05 at some ideas surrounding composition
2:09:07 and structure in the user interface
2:09:10 how you arrange the elements of the
2:09:12 interface can really shape what kind of
2:09:14 experience you want the user to have
2:09:18 again we're talking about how to shape
2:09:21 content specific design here so these
2:09:24 rules won't apply for every single app
2:09:26 or website you design
2:09:28 so let's take a brief look at some of
2:09:30 these overarching concerns
2:09:32 we've looked at some issues concerning
2:09:34 the individual elements of your user
2:09:36 interface but these overall issues tend
2:09:39 to have a trickle-down effect so it's
2:09:41 worth thinking about structure and
2:09:43 composition rather than getting lost in
2:09:45 the design details of any single element
2:09:48 let's use a pragmatic example something
2:09:51 with functional content so it's a little
2:09:53 less subjective when we try and analyze
2:09:55 how it works
2:09:57 so let's look at how we might build a
2:09:59 simple calendar as an app
2:10:01 since we have functional content we're
2:10:03 probably going to need a functional
2:10:05 structure
2:10:06 so the content and the user's priorities
2:10:09 are going to drive our composition
2:10:12 we might need a small space for the
2:10:13 title but we're probably going to need a
2:10:15 much larger space for the calendar
2:10:17 itself where most of the action is going
2:10:19 to happen
2:10:20 but then we'll also need a space where a
2:10:22 user can see the events that they're
2:10:24 choosing from that calendar
2:10:26 and a small space for menu options at
2:10:29 the bottom
2:10:30 so you can see our compositional
2:10:32 structure is being totally driven by the
2:10:34 content let's take one of these sections
2:10:37 say the calendar section and develop it
2:10:39 a little bit more and we'll see how
2:10:41 functionality is really going to drive
2:10:43 all of our design decisions we have a
2:10:46 small fixed space to work with so we
2:10:49 want our type to take up as much of the
2:10:51 space as possible but still be very
2:10:53 legible it also has to be at a size
2:10:55 where it can easily be touched and the
2:10:58 events have to be easy to read we
2:11:00 probably want to know what time our
2:11:02 events are happening and we'd also want
2:11:04 them to be clearly separate from each
2:11:06 other so we don't get confused so we're
2:11:08 already creating a kind of visual
2:11:10 hierarchy even in a very limited way
2:11:13 within this functional landscape and
2:11:16 because our design palette is quite
2:11:17 limited any small changes really do make
2:11:20 a difference
2:11:22 small changes in scale or weight or
2:11:24 lines can be used to create our
2:11:26 hierarchy we might need something that's
2:11:28 visually more dramatic for a larger
2:11:30 event so we could use color for instance
2:11:33 to show the selected date and extend
2:11:35 that visual system a little bit more to
2:11:38 show other dates that might have events
2:11:40 hidden on them we could use color in a
2:11:42 more subtle way as well for instance we
2:11:44 could make the days of the week gray in
2:11:46 order to push them back since they're a
2:11:47 static element that's always there in
2:11:49 the same place
2:11:50 we could use color to build a simple two
2:11:53 color system where the static
2:11:55 information is in black and gray and the
2:11:57 active information is in green for
2:12:00 instance
2:12:01 or we could keep extending that system
2:12:03 to show more information for instance
2:12:05 the amount of events that we might have
2:12:07 on any given day
2:12:09 and if we needed to create even more
2:12:10 hierarchy we could use a subtle color to
2:12:14 show the divisions the sections that we
2:12:17 started out with that divide the title
2:12:19 from the calendar from the events
2:12:21 we could also add ancillary icons and
2:12:24 symbols to make our app feel a little
2:12:27 more believable
2:12:29 it's always useful when you're making
2:12:30 mock-ups to try and make the app look
2:12:32 like it actually works
2:12:34 so we could add some navigation symbols
2:12:37 we could add down in the bottom some
2:12:40 icons that give the app the appearance
2:12:42 of functionality at this stage we're
2:12:44 really looking at the user interface
2:12:46 design we're not thinking about the
2:12:48 functionality of this app so much it's
2:12:50 really just a visual exercise but that
2:12:53 exercise can feel a little more real
2:12:55 when we put real elements in there so
2:12:57 let's also change the language so that
2:12:59 again it looks like it's an active
2:13:01 working app we can give it a name and we
2:13:04 can add real events to our calendar so
2:13:07 functionality has been driving all of
2:13:09 our design decisions for this calendar
2:13:12 but also familiarity because this
2:13:14 calendar doesn't look that different
2:13:16 from any other calendar that might
2:13:18 appear on your phone because the basic
2:13:20 structure of it has already been figured
2:13:22 out so there's no point as reinventing
2:13:25 that but what we can do is hopefully
2:13:27 either improve on it or make our own
2:13:29 aesthetic version of it
2:13:31 in this example the composition and the
2:13:34 structure is really being driven by the
2:13:36 content and that content the filter that
2:13:38 we use to temper our design is really
2:13:41 about functionality so the content is
2:13:44 functional the structure the hierarchy
2:13:47 everything that we're looking at is
2:13:49 really to do with
2:13:50 function so while that affects our
2:13:53 larger user interface decisions it also
2:13:56 affects our smaller decisions about
2:13:58 design so when we look at color and
2:14:01 typography icons and symbols they're
2:14:04 also being tempered by the content by
2:14:06 that functionality and a calendar is
2:14:09 quite an extreme example it's at the
2:14:12 functional end of the spectrum entirely
2:14:15 so let's see what happens if we pick a
2:14:17 different kind of experience something
2:14:19 that's much less about functionality
2:14:22 and much more maybe about emotion or
2:14:25 about experience itself rather than
2:14:28 building this interface step by step
2:14:31 let's look at a finished example that
2:14:32 one of my students made
2:14:35 this is for an app called sense where
2:14:37 with a hardware extension added to your
2:14:39 phone you can record smells and mix and
2:14:43 match those smells to create sensory
2:14:45 experiences
2:14:47 because the content is based around
2:14:49 experience and exploration the interface
2:14:53 follows the same direction
2:14:56 it becomes a much more poetic experience
2:14:58 for the user rather than a purely
2:15:01 functional pragmatic one the interface
2:15:04 is image based and somewhat ambiguous it
2:15:07 has to be tried out by the user in order
2:15:09 for them to figure it out it becomes a
2:15:12 learned experiential interface rather
2:15:15 than a fixed functional interface
2:15:18 it's less concerned with looking like an
2:15:20 existing operating system
2:15:22 and more concerned with creating a
2:15:24 strong idiosyncratic individual visual
2:15:28 experience so even when there are
2:15:30 functional components to the interface
2:15:32 they don't look the same as what you
2:15:35 would expect from the regular operating
2:15:37 system the visual language of this
2:15:39 particular app takes over every aspect
2:15:42 of the design
2:15:44 a lot of the communication of the
2:15:45 interface elements is delivered through
2:15:48 imagery which means that the imagery has
2:15:50 to be meticulously controlled and
2:15:52 composed
2:15:54 instead of forefronting functionality
2:15:56 this user interface design prioritizes
2:15:59 experience mood and tone
2:16:02 in these two different examples you can
2:16:05 see how the overarching design concerns
2:16:08 trickle down to really affect every
2:16:11 aspect of the user interface design they
2:16:14 affect decisions about content about
2:16:16 composition and about structure what
2:16:19 they have in common is the ui design
2:16:22 solution is related to the content it's
2:16:25 contextually appropriate it's context
2:16:28 specific design in the last section we
2:16:31 looked at larger contextual issues and
2:16:33 now we're going to go from macro to
2:16:35 micro and look at buttons
2:16:38 these tiny components are one of the
2:16:40 most important elements of user
2:16:42 interface design so let's start by
2:16:44 asking ourselves what makes a button a
2:16:47 button
2:16:48 well pretty much anything can be a
2:16:50 button a button is just a self-contained
2:16:52 object that you know is interactive that
2:16:55 you can click on or interact with but it
2:16:58 only works as a button if the user knows
2:17:00 that it's a button in other words if
2:17:02 it's not just a shape
2:17:05 there are various visual signifiers to
2:17:07 let the user know that a button is a
2:17:09 button and not just the shape and the
2:17:11 benefit of these familiar forms is that
2:17:14 the user knows straight away that it's a
2:17:16 button so there's no ambiguity there and
2:17:18 there's no time wasted so familiar forms
2:17:21 become fast forms immediately
2:17:24 recognizable as part of the interface
2:17:27 so let's look at what kind of visual
2:17:29 traits let us know that a button is a
2:17:31 button and not just a shape
2:17:34 buttons come in many forms they might
2:17:36 not have all of these traits but they
2:17:38 probably have at least one or two of
2:17:40 them but all of these should be
2:17:42 considered when you're a user interface
2:17:44 designer working with buttons
2:17:46 if you're designing a button one of the
2:17:48 first things to think about is what size
2:17:50 it should be
2:17:52 and this relates to how it's going to be
2:17:54 used the button needs to be at a size
2:17:56 where it's legible but it also needs to
2:17:58 be at a size where either a finger or a
2:18:01 cursor can easily tap or click on it
2:18:04 buttons that are too big take up too
2:18:06 much real estate on screen and ones that
2:18:08 are too small are hard to activate
2:18:10 you should also consider the shape of
2:18:12 the button what's its function does it
2:18:15 need to have a label on it what kind of
2:18:17 screen is it going to exist on and what
2:18:20 is it going to be touched by if it's a
2:18:22 finger or a cursor that might mean a
2:18:25 different shape
2:18:26 some shapes are more economical and work
2:18:28 better as a set while other shapes might
2:18:31 be better containers for a symbol or an
2:18:34 icon that has to sit on them you could
2:18:36 think about how color works with your
2:18:38 set of buttons is it used as a signifier
2:18:41 for a particular function of a button or
2:18:43 is it part of a larger overarching
2:18:45 system
2:18:46 a common strategy to let the user know
2:18:49 that a button is a button is to give it
2:18:51 some kind of dimension to make it feel
2:18:53 like a three-dimensional object that has
2:18:55 to be pressed or interacted with this is
2:18:58 often done through a trump-loyal effect
2:19:01 using shadows and highlights to create
2:19:03 fake dimensionality a key aspect in
2:19:06 recognizing buttons in the interface is
2:19:08 where they're placed within the screen
2:19:11 they're quite often contained within a
2:19:13 certain area within a window or a bar or
2:19:16 a space that we recognize as being
2:19:18 interactive or being specifically for
2:19:21 navigation
2:19:22 so let's take some of these ideas and
2:19:24 look at them in a little more detail
2:19:27 let's start out by looking at the size
2:19:29 of our buttons and its relationship to
2:19:31 the cursor or the finger
2:19:33 the scale of your button has a
2:19:35 relationship to the thing interacting
2:19:37 with it so you could think about a
2:19:39 button on your phone having a
2:19:41 relationship in size to the tip of your
2:19:43 finger and on a computer screen having a
2:19:46 relationship to the size of the cursor
2:19:48 the size of the button is also going to
2:19:50 have a relationship to the size of the
2:19:52 screen and the resolution of the screen
2:19:55 it could also depend on how many other
2:19:57 elements you have in your interface
2:19:59 the shape of your button could be
2:20:01 informed by a few different
2:20:03 considerations the shape of it could be
2:20:05 informed by popular conventions a
2:20:08 rectangle with rounded corners was
2:20:10 prevalent at one time but now circular
2:20:12 buttons are much more prevalent and this
2:20:14 reflects a change from prioritizing the
2:20:17 computer screen to the handheld device
2:20:20 the button shape might have a
2:20:21 relationship to the text that has to sit
2:20:23 on it or the icon or image that has to
2:20:26 sit on it but it could also be
2:20:28 indicative of a certain kind of
2:20:29 functionality
2:20:31 certain shapes mean certain things so
2:20:34 when we look at this set of symbols we
2:20:36 know what their functionality is without
2:20:38 any text labeling we also recognize the
2:20:41 functionality of this set of shapes as a
2:20:44 set because we know what they mean the
2:20:46 square here only means stop when it's
2:20:48 sitting next to play pause fast forward
2:20:52 and rewind so we're not just thinking
2:20:54 about the individual shape we're
2:20:56 thinking about how those shapes work as
2:20:58 part of a system
2:21:00 and color can work in the same way it
2:21:02 can be an individual signifier of a
2:21:04 certain kind of activity but it can also
2:21:07 work as part of a larger system
2:21:09 so for instance just by coloring the
2:21:11 button even though we don't change the
2:21:13 label we might be able to imply that one
2:21:16 button is going to start something the
2:21:18 green button whereas the red one might
2:21:20 stop something one common convention
2:21:23 from the earlier days of interface
2:21:25 design is to create a sense of
2:21:27 dimensionality for buttons so that they
2:21:29 look like they can be physically pressed
2:21:32 and this was a very common strategy
2:21:34 because it helped people relate between
2:21:36 the digital world and the analog world
2:21:39 that they were familiar with this
2:21:41 strategy feels a little outdated or a
2:21:43 little antiquated now and it's been
2:21:46 superseded by instead of dimensionality
2:21:49 by using a drop shadow in order to
2:21:51 create a button that floats over the
2:21:53 surface and draws attention to itself
2:21:55 where we place our buttons on the screen
2:21:58 can also be a signifier that allows us
2:22:01 to know that they're part of a
2:22:02 navigation system
2:22:04 for instance when we put these buttons
2:22:06 at the outside edges and on their side
2:22:08 they really don't feel like buttons
2:22:10 anymore they also stop working as a set
2:22:13 because there's so much distance between
2:22:15 them
2:22:16 so almost anything can be a button if
2:22:18 the user knows that it's a button
2:22:20 and it's your job as the user interface
2:22:23 designer to let the user know that that
2:22:26 shape is a button
2:22:27 you have to let them know that without
2:22:29 them doing any work it has to be
2:22:31 immediate knowledge for the user and
2:22:34 your design decisions are very important
2:22:37 because buttons are often used to create
2:22:39 actions so it's important that you label
2:22:42 them clearly because they're going to
2:22:43 have some effect
2:22:45 the last thing that you want is an
2:22:47 ambiguous button because you could
2:22:49 delete erase or destroy something quite
2:22:52 important to somebody
2:22:54 and since our analog and digital lives
2:22:56 are increasingly intertwined your
2:22:59 actions with the interface can often
2:23:01 have actions in the real world
2:23:04 so there's quite a lot of responsibility
2:23:06 for the user interface designer to
2:23:09 create buttons that are totally accurate
2:23:12 using precise and accurate language to
2:23:14 label your buttons can really help but
2:23:17 sometimes words don't work and you're
2:23:19 going to need to use images symbols or
2:23:21 icons
2:23:23 so part of your job as the ui designer
2:23:25 is to let the user know that the button
2:23:28 is a button but also to let them know
2:23:30 exactly what that button does
2:23:32 you could think of buttons as sign posts
2:23:35 you want to be able to find them easily
2:23:37 you want to be able to get that
2:23:38 information very quickly but you also
2:23:41 want to be able to ignore them when you
2:23:43 don't need them
2:23:47 in the last segment we looked at buttons
2:23:50 and how to work with buttons and now
2:23:51 we're going to look at all the other
2:23:53 things that aren't buttons in our
2:23:55 interface so how else do we navigate
2:23:57 through our digital content if we don't
2:23:59 use buttons
2:24:00 well we have other systems in place
2:24:03 menus links fields and all of these
2:24:06 different systems have to be designed by
2:24:08 a ui designer they're all simple fast
2:24:11 functional means of interaction
2:24:14 and like buttons they rely on design
2:24:16 conventions that we're very familiar
2:24:19 with there's no point reinventing all of
2:24:21 these we actually have to work within
2:24:23 the conventions that already exist
2:24:26 because those conventions are there for
2:24:27 a reason they're very successful there's
2:24:30 some fundamental principles of graphic
2:24:32 design that we can consider in order to
2:24:35 keep our simple fast functional
2:24:37 interface working
2:24:39 we can use shape scale contrast clarity
2:24:42 and economy in order to reinforce and
2:24:45 emphasize the goals of speed and
2:24:47 function
2:24:48 so let's look at how we might apply some
2:24:51 of these design criteria to some simple
2:24:53 interface elements
2:24:55 let's start out by looking at one of the
2:24:57 simplest things of all a text field
2:25:00 on the surface of things it just looks
2:25:02 like a rectangle with some contrast from
2:25:04 the background but it can still have
2:25:06 design decisions applied to it so the
2:25:09 shape and the scale of it could change
2:25:11 for instance and become functional or
2:25:13 dysfunctional depending on what kind of
2:25:15 move we make
2:25:17 so as a designer we can change the
2:25:19 functionality of the text field but we
2:25:21 can also change the aesthetics of it as
2:25:24 well
2:25:24 by small moves we can make it feel
2:25:27 different and give the user a
2:25:29 foreshadowing of the functionality of
2:25:31 the field
2:25:33 so by giving it round corners for
2:25:34 instance we could make it feel much more
2:25:37 like it's a window for texting in
2:25:40 and once we introduce that functionality
2:25:42 to hold text we've suddenly got a
2:25:45 relationship between the text and the
2:25:47 box and now we really do have to
2:25:49 consider our shape and our scale because
2:25:52 the box now has a function so we could
2:25:55 make the text too big or too small or we
2:25:59 might have to consider how much text
2:26:01 does the box have to contain
2:26:03 is it just for sending a very tiny
2:26:06 amount of text or is it going to have
2:26:08 the capacity to send a whole paragraph
2:26:11 so again function and aesthetics are
2:26:13 intertwined we could also think about
2:26:16 whether the field has the ability to
2:26:18 change its own shape and respond the
2:26:20 amount of text that's filling it
2:26:23 and as we look at the relationship
2:26:25 between the text and the field we have
2:26:28 to now start to think about ideas of
2:26:30 clarity and economy we don't want to
2:26:33 waste space and we also want to have the
2:26:35 text to be as legible as possible so we
2:26:38 might alter the size and leading of the
2:26:41 type but we might also alter the shape
2:26:43 and proportions of the box
2:26:46 and even when we have a good
2:26:47 relationship between the size of the
2:26:49 type and the size of the box we have to
2:26:51 decide on the overall size of that
2:26:54 element within our interface once again
2:26:56 we would need more contextual
2:26:58 information about how this is going to
2:27:01 work on a screen how much text is there
2:27:04 going to be what kind of background is
2:27:06 it going to have
2:27:07 is it a single message or part of a
2:27:09 conversation once we know a little bit
2:27:12 more about the function of the design we
2:27:14 can go back to our design criteria shape
2:27:17 scale contrast clarity and economy and
2:27:20 start to let those things inform the
2:27:22 user interface design
2:27:24 right now the placement of these speech
2:27:26 bubbles isn't very economical there's
2:27:29 too much white space between them and if
2:27:31 there's two people having a conversation
2:27:33 how do we know which person is which we
2:27:36 could use color to show that
2:27:38 we could use other design conventions to
2:27:40 accentuate one voice coming from the
2:27:42 left and one from the right
2:27:44 by adding tails to the rectangles we
2:27:47 turn them into speech bubbles which is
2:27:49 also another convention that we're very
2:27:51 familiar with
2:27:53 so even in a design that we're very very
2:27:55 familiar with you can see how many small
2:27:58 decisions there are to be made by the
2:28:00 interface designer and these micro
2:28:02 decisions all contribute to the ultimate
2:28:05 look and feel of the interface
2:28:08 let's look at another example of a
2:28:09 really basic interface element let's say
2:28:12 that we have a text field for entering
2:28:15 data where the data is very limited for
2:28:18 instance entering your first and last
2:28:20 name
2:28:20 when i go to enter my name there's
2:28:22 immediately a problem my first name
2:28:25 might fit but i have quite a long last
2:28:27 name so we're definitely going to need a
2:28:29 bigger box
2:28:30 and once more as the interface designer
2:28:33 we're having to negotiate that balance
2:28:35 between aesthetics and functionality
2:28:38 and in this case we have two pieces of
2:28:41 information one typographic piece acting
2:28:44 as a label and the other acting as the
2:28:46 data that we enter
2:28:48 this is not a very economical way for us
2:28:51 to build our interface so we could think
2:28:53 about combining those two things into
2:28:56 the text field itself and now we have to
2:28:58 think a little bit more about the
2:29:00 relationship between the text and the
2:29:03 text field because they're sitting on
2:29:05 top of each other
2:29:07 also the text is now going to have to
2:29:09 have a dual function in that it's a
2:29:11 label and it's also going to change into
2:29:13 editable text
2:29:15 and we could create a number of
2:29:17 different design directions that look at
2:29:19 the relationship between the background
2:29:22 the text field and the text itself one
2:29:25 of the things you can see here is that
2:29:27 we've created different levels of
2:29:29 contrast so in some of these examples
2:29:32 the text really stands out and in other
2:29:34 examples the text field is the thing
2:29:36 that is more visible
2:29:38 it's not that any one of these examples
2:29:40 is a better solution than any of the
2:29:42 others it would again be contextual
2:29:45 depending on the other components that
2:29:46 we had and the kind of content that we
2:29:48 were working with some of these
2:29:50 solutions might work better than others
2:29:52 because they're more familiar they use
2:29:54 existing design conventions
2:29:57 and those conventions aren't always the
2:29:59 best solution but sometimes they really
2:30:02 are for instance if we think about a
2:30:04 visual convention from the digital world
2:30:07 we can look at some examples that are
2:30:09 very very successful so if you think
2:30:11 about links for instance there's a clear
2:30:14 and simple way to show a link the
2:30:18 simplest way is just to underline a
2:30:20 piece of text
2:30:22 and this is so prevalent and so
2:30:24 ubiquitous
2:30:25 and just the simplest way to do
2:30:28 something complex
2:30:30 that it's become an absolute convention
2:30:32 that we all use and understand very very
2:30:35 quickly so when we're trying to create
2:30:38 some of these interface elements there's
2:30:40 no point reinventing the wheel we don't
2:30:42 need to come up with a different
2:30:44 language to show a link for example
2:30:46 it already exists we don't need to
2:30:48 reinvent functionality but what we can
2:30:51 do is change the visual aesthetics the
2:30:54 style of those interface elements
2:30:57 so let's take another example to look at
2:31:00 let's look at a simple text menu
2:31:03 we take simple text menus for granted
2:31:05 because we use them all the time on our
2:31:07 computers but they're actually a very
2:31:09 economical and very clever way of
2:31:11 getting information to the user so let's
2:31:14 try and analyze what they do that works
2:31:16 so well so we can maybe use it for other
2:31:18 things
2:31:19 our text menu allows us to have a drop
2:31:22 down type list
2:31:23 and we can go through that list and
2:31:25 highlight whatever it is that we want to
2:31:27 choose from that list
2:31:29 but you can also have nested
2:31:31 subcategories so we can compact a lot of
2:31:34 information into that menu and make it
2:31:36 be visible and invisible whenever we
2:31:38 want so it's a highly economical system
2:31:42 our text menus always live at the top of
2:31:44 our user interface so we know where to
2:31:46 go whenever we need them and because
2:31:49 they're text based they can carry any
2:31:51 kind of function that can be described
2:31:53 by text it's also a very economical
2:31:56 system in terms of space because we can
2:31:58 contain sub-menus and only make them be
2:32:01 visible when we need them
2:32:03 they work on a kind of need to know
2:32:05 basis they remain hidden and compact
2:32:08 until we decide that we need to access
2:32:10 that information
2:32:11 drop-down text menus are
2:32:13 hyper-functional we can contain a lot of
2:32:16 information in them and a wide variety
2:32:18 of information but we can nest that
2:32:20 information and keep it hidden until we
2:32:23 need it
2:32:24 so you can see it's the main way that we
2:32:27 get a lot of our information within the
2:32:29 operating systems when we're using them
2:32:31 on a computer because it's so functional
2:32:33 and so flexible and that flexibility
2:32:36 means they can be used for almost
2:32:38 anything no matter what the content and
2:32:40 all they're really using as graphic
2:32:43 devices are words some muted color the
2:32:46 odd symbol and a few lines
2:32:49 with this very minimal palette you can
2:32:51 do almost anything
2:32:53 but of course you might not want your
2:32:55 app or website to look exactly the same
2:32:59 as the operating system
2:33:01 so you can take some of those same
2:33:02 principles of functionality and
2:33:04 compactness and redesign them to get
2:33:07 them to work with the aesthetic of your
2:33:09 apple website
2:33:11 so here's the website for the calarts
2:33:13 poster archive online it was designed by
2:33:16 a couple of my students and it needed to
2:33:18 have some drop down menus that fit the
2:33:21 aesthetic of the website but also
2:33:23 maintain the functionality of regular
2:33:26 drop down menus so they kept the same
2:33:29 kind of interaction but changed all the
2:33:32 graphic elements in the menu so the
2:33:34 typography the shape of the box how it
2:33:37 appears and disappears are all different
2:33:40 from the standard operating system it's
2:33:42 such a simple element of interface
2:33:44 design that we often don't think about
2:33:47 what we can do with it as a design
2:33:49 component so let's look at what some of
2:33:51 the variables are for our drop down text
2:33:54 menu
2:33:55 we could change the shape and the
2:33:56 proportions of the box
2:33:59 we could change the scale of the box in
2:34:01 relationship to the background elements
2:34:03 we can change the color we can think
2:34:05 about its relationship to the other
2:34:07 elements on the page
2:34:09 and while none of these design changes
2:34:11 change the functionality they do change
2:34:14 the look but they also change the
2:34:16 feeling that a user is going to have
2:34:18 when they use this menu
2:34:20 we can go into a little more detail and
2:34:22 look at some of the micro decisions that
2:34:25 might shape the look of our menu we can
2:34:27 think about the typeface what kind of
2:34:30 weight it is what kind of size the
2:34:33 leading space between the lines whether
2:34:35 it's upper or lower case the color of
2:34:38 the type and how we're going to indicate
2:34:40 a change of state when the user selects
2:34:43 one of the items from the menu in this
2:34:46 case there's no nested subcategories to
2:34:48 think about
2:34:49 but we might want to think about how the
2:34:51 category might look when we've exited
2:34:54 the drop down menu so if you look in the
2:34:56 top right hand corner here it's showing
2:34:58 us what our selection was
2:35:00 so even in something as simple and
2:35:02 familiar as a drop down menu we've made
2:35:06 many design decisions
2:35:08 we've looked at shape scale contrast
2:35:11 clarity
2:35:12 and economy
2:35:13 but none of these aspects of design
2:35:15 exist on their own they're all
2:35:18 interrelated you can't just fix one and
2:35:21 expect all the other problems to resolve
2:35:23 themselves
2:35:24 shape is related to scale is related to
2:35:27 contrast and clarity etc etc
2:35:30 and any one change you make in one of
2:35:32 these areas will have effects on the
2:35:35 other you can't change the scale without
2:35:37 affecting the clarity for instance and
2:35:39 what this means is that interface design
2:35:42 is a relational activity all of these
2:35:45 different design decisions that you're
2:35:47 making are all interconnected each
2:35:50 design decision works in relationship
2:35:53 with every other design decision so you
2:35:55 have to consider them all when you're
2:35:57 designing each element of your interface
2:36:00 but then you also have to think about
2:36:02 how each of those elements is going to
2:36:04 have a relationship to every other
2:36:06 element as well to form your complete
2:36:08 interface
2:36:10 in this last section we're going to look
2:36:12 at how some of our interface elements
2:36:15 can have various different states of
2:36:17 existence and how they can change from
2:36:19 one state to another in other words how
2:36:22 they can be both static and interactive
2:36:24 elements in our interface
2:36:26 the simplest and most common multi-state
2:36:28 object is the button a button has to
2:36:31 have different states so it can convey
2:36:33 to the user the fact that it's being
2:36:35 interacted with
2:36:37 so first of all a button can let the
2:36:39 user know it's a button by having some
2:36:41 kind of state change so the user knows
2:36:44 it's not just a static element it's an
2:36:46 interactive element
2:36:48 and secondly a button can also change
2:36:50 states to acknowledge the user's action
2:36:53 in other words to let the user know that
2:36:56 their tap or click has really worked
2:36:59 so let's look at some of these different
2:37:01 states for a simple button and look at
2:37:04 different ways that they could be
2:37:05 visually represented
2:37:07 the button has a normal state where
2:37:09 nothing is happening
2:37:11 if you put your cursor over the button
2:37:13 there should be some kind of state
2:37:15 change to let you know that the button
2:37:17 is live and this is called the hover
2:37:18 state
2:37:20 then when you actually click on the
2:37:22 button to activate it there needs to be
2:37:24 another state change to let you know
2:37:26 that the button has been pressed
2:37:28 and then the final stage is after you've
2:37:30 pressed the button there needs to be
2:37:32 some kind of change from the normal
2:37:35 state that it began in so that you know
2:37:37 that the button has been clicked on
2:37:39 not all buttons have all of these states
2:37:41 and these states can vary as well
2:37:43 depending whether you're using a mouse
2:37:45 with a cursor or whether you're using
2:37:47 your finger
2:37:48 but for the sake of this exercise let's
2:37:50 keep all four
2:37:52 so if we add some color to try and
2:37:54 denote the different stages we create a
2:37:56 hierarchy through the density of the
2:37:59 color and through the contrast with the
2:38:01 type
2:38:02 so our press state and our normal state
2:38:04 feel very different to each other
2:38:06 we can change the background color of
2:38:08 the button but we can also change the
2:38:10 color of the typography on the button as
2:38:13 well we can see from this example that
2:38:15 the normal state and the press state are
2:38:17 the buttons that have the most
2:38:19 visibility the most contrast the
2:38:21 inactive state has the least contrast
2:38:23 because we want that state to kind of be
2:38:25 ignored
2:38:26 by changing how we use the color in the
2:38:28 background we can emphasize the action
2:38:31 the activity in this case pressing and
2:38:34 make that be the most important visual
2:38:36 aspect of our four buttons
2:38:39 the trick here is to have the buttons
2:38:41 look different enough that they can
2:38:43 represent different states but look
2:38:45 similar enough so that they can belong
2:38:47 to the same family
2:38:48 you also have to consider what kind of
2:38:51 background the buttons are going to
2:38:52 exist on and whether they're going to
2:38:54 need an outline so that they'll stand
2:38:56 out on different backgrounds or not
2:38:58 in general you want to keep the buttons
2:39:00 as simple and as clean as possible
2:39:03 you could think about how much contrast
2:39:05 you want to have between each state
2:39:08 you could have a lot of contrast between
2:39:10 normal and hover for instance or it
2:39:12 could be a very subtle
2:39:14 change
2:39:15 you could also use trump loyal effects
2:39:18 to create difference between your states
2:39:20 so here you can see a different amount
2:39:23 of drop shadow within the different
2:39:25 state changes as well as a change in the
2:39:27 tonality of the buttons background color
2:39:30 so there are many different ways to
2:39:32 formally represent the different states
2:39:34 that a button might have
2:39:37 and it's interesting to look at what
2:39:38 kind of purpose these state changes have
2:39:41 for the user
2:39:43 to begin with in the normal state the
2:39:45 user has to know that the button is a
2:39:47 button they have to recognize that it's
2:39:49 something that they can click on or
2:39:51 touch or activate so there's a sense of
2:39:54 recognition knowing that the button's a
2:39:56 button
2:39:57 in the second stage the hover stage it's
2:40:00 more about confirmation if i put my
2:40:02 finger over the button for instance i
2:40:04 know that something could happen but it
2:40:06 doesn't necessarily have to happen until
2:40:09 i actually press it and enter the
2:40:11 activation stage
2:40:13 and then the last stage when the button
2:40:15 is inactive
2:40:17 could be termed as rejection because
2:40:19 we're no longer interested in that
2:40:20 button
2:40:21 we could take some of these concepts and
2:40:24 try and apply them to different kinds of
2:40:26 buttons and see what happens
2:40:29 as i mentioned earlier not every button
2:40:31 has every state and things are a little
2:40:33 different between using a mouse and a
2:40:35 cursor and using your finger on a touch
2:40:38 screen but we could take the same stages
2:40:41 and apply them to a button with a symbol
2:40:43 on it or we could apply them to a button
2:40:46 with a logo type on it and there might
2:40:48 be a number of different formal ways for
2:40:51 us to represent these four stages and as
2:40:54 i said we might not even use all four of
2:40:56 those stages but this is where the user
2:40:58 interface designer gets to decide which
2:41:01 is going to work the best
2:41:03 so let's try and see what happens if we
2:41:05 take a totally different kind of button
2:41:07 not just different content on the button
2:41:10 but a button that functions in a
2:41:12 slightly different way and interacts in
2:41:14 a different way so here's a toggle
2:41:17 button for instance it only really has
2:41:19 an on and an off as its two states
2:41:23 so the hover state might be the same as
2:41:25 the normal state
2:41:27 the activated state is clearly different
2:41:30 when we've slid the toggle all the way
2:41:32 over to the right
2:41:33 and the inactive stage is the same as
2:41:36 the normal and the hover stage
2:41:38 but let's try and slow things down and
2:41:41 see what happens when the user really
2:41:43 interacts with this toggle switch
2:41:45 there's an in-between stage that happens
2:41:48 very quickly where the user is sliding
2:41:50 the toggle from left to right to
2:41:52 activate the switch
2:41:54 and sliding it from right to left to
2:41:56 make it inactive so there's these hidden
2:41:58 stages that happen very quickly and
2:42:01 these take the place of the hover state
2:42:04 and the inactive state if we go to our
2:42:06 other set of terminology we recognize
2:42:09 the toggle button because of how it
2:42:11 looks our confirmation is that we can
2:42:13 actually move it our activation is
2:42:15 seeing it come up in green and our
2:42:17 rejection is being able to turn it back
2:42:20 off again by sliding the toggle to the
2:42:22 left
2:42:23 and the user here is switching their
2:42:24 knowledge of the interface
2:42:26 between what they see and what they do
2:42:29 so you see the toggle button you
2:42:31 interact with it to confirm you see the
2:42:34 activation and then you physically
2:42:36 interact with it again to turn it back
2:42:39 off the confirmation and rejection
2:42:42 require physical activation by the user
2:42:46 there's no real hover state here
2:42:49 because we have a finger instead of a
2:42:51 cursor for this kind of switch normally
2:42:54 so our hover state is converted into one
2:42:57 that requires physical activity it
2:42:59 requires us to actually move the toggle
2:43:02 from the left to the right
2:43:04 our hover state where the cursor was
2:43:06 still is now replaced with a motion
2:43:09 state where our finger is active
2:43:12 and this is much more common in ui
2:43:14 design for mobile devices in a lot of
2:43:17 ways the motion state has replaced a lot
2:43:20 of these conventional state changes
2:43:22 we're much more used to swiping nowadays
2:43:25 than we are to clicking
2:43:27 so if the click has been replaced by the
2:43:29 swipe it follows logically that the
2:43:32 mouse has been replaced by the finger
2:43:35 and i think this gives the user more of
2:43:37 a connection to the interface because
2:43:40 there's no mouse to mediate that
2:43:42 experience you're actually using your
2:43:44 finger right on the screen and that
2:43:46 creates the necessity for this motion
2:43:48 state and that in turn means that you
2:43:51 have to have a much more fluid
2:43:53 relationship between those states you
2:43:55 have to start to think about how your
2:43:57 interface elements are going to animate
2:44:06 [Music]
2:44:10 welcome to the fourth and final week of
2:44:12 visual elements of interface design
2:44:14 this week we're going to take our
2:44:16 individual interface components and see
2:44:18 what happens when we try to put them
2:44:19 together into a more complex structure
2:44:22 we'll be looking at how to get our
2:44:24 components to work harmoniously as a
2:44:26 family figuring out how hierarchy works
2:44:28 in the interface and discussing
2:44:30 conventions and expectations of
2:44:32 contemporary interface design
2:44:35 we'll also be examining how to navigate
2:44:37 to different screens and how to build
2:44:39 visual relationships between different
2:44:40 kinds of content but within a single
2:44:42 site
2:44:44 finally we'll be discussing different
2:44:46 platforms how to create variable content
2:44:48 for different screen sizes and looking
2:44:51 at how to organize complex bodies of
2:44:53 content into user-friendly structures
2:44:58 over the next week we're going to look
2:44:59 at how to take some of the individual
2:45:01 components of the user interface and try
2:45:04 and put them together and get them to
2:45:06 work in concert with each other
2:45:08 we'll be looking at both conceptual
2:45:10 strategies and design strategies and
2:45:13 figuring out what kind of information is
2:45:15 going to be useful when we try and make
2:45:17 a whole interface out of these various
2:45:20 pieces that we've been looking at
2:45:22 we're going to start to think about how
2:45:24 we might take some of our more
2:45:26 conceptual and theoretical knowledge
2:45:28 about the user interface and combine it
2:45:31 with some more practical knowledge to do
2:45:33 with design skills and making the
2:45:35 interface
2:45:36 there's a lot of different skills and
2:45:38 different components for us to think
2:45:40 about
2:45:41 we've looked at some very broad and
2:45:43 wide-reaching concepts and while these
2:45:45 are great for giving us some background
2:45:47 information we now need to start to try
2:45:49 and apply them to some specific
2:45:52 interfaces
2:45:53 and in a similar way we've taken the
2:45:55 user interface apart and divided it into
2:45:58 many small components or approaches to
2:46:01 design now we have to think about
2:46:03 combining all those individual pieces of
2:46:05 knowledge and starting to apply them to
2:46:07 a larger and more practical project
2:46:11 but we don't want to do that in a
2:46:12 disparate or fragmented kind of way we
2:46:15 want to build connections between all
2:46:17 these different skills and we want them
2:46:19 to work in harmony with each other
2:46:22 in a lot of ways the skills that we've
2:46:24 been looking at and the way that we've
2:46:26 been talking about them have been very
2:46:28 general and very generic
2:46:30 and i've left it open like this on
2:46:32 purpose i don't want to dictate a narrow
2:46:34 or specific way of working i'm more
2:46:37 interested in you developing a set of
2:46:39 skills that you can apply in a number of
2:46:41 different ways to different kinds of
2:46:44 content but one thing that would be
2:46:46 useful at this stage is figuring out how
2:46:49 to tie some of those skills to do with
2:46:51 making individual components with the
2:46:54 larger theoretical aspects of user
2:46:57 interface design that we've looked at
2:46:59 the things that we're calling the
2:47:00 overview if you can build a relationship
2:47:03 between these larger theoretical
2:47:05 practices and the more practical design
2:47:08 skills you'll have a really great
2:47:09 foundation for working as a ui designer
2:47:16 in this section we're going to look at
2:47:17 some larger issues about how we might
2:47:20 start to organize our content we're
2:47:22 going to try and create a hierarchy of
2:47:24 what's important and what's less
2:47:26 important we're going to begin to think
2:47:28 not just about what goes where
2:47:31 but about what goes where and why so
2:47:34 thinking about how our app or website is
2:47:36 used is actually going to help us to
2:47:38 think of a design structure we'll be
2:47:41 looking at a hierarchy of function and
2:47:43 thinking about what's important to the
2:47:45 user
2:47:46 what are the things that the user is
2:47:47 going to want to do first and so those
2:47:50 things might be more important in our
2:47:52 design layout
2:47:53 but we'll also be thinking about what's
2:47:55 not important to the user what are the
2:47:58 things that they might not want to do
2:47:59 straight away or perhaps some people
2:48:02 might not want to do those things at all
2:48:05 as well as thinking about what's
2:48:06 important to the user we'll also be
2:48:09 thinking about what's important to the
2:48:11 client which in this case might be you
2:48:14 what's the intention of the client
2:48:17 why might you be making this app or
2:48:18 website in the first place what's it
2:48:21 really meant to do
2:48:22 it's important to maintain that
2:48:24 hierarchy and keep sight of your goals
2:48:26 and your intention in making an app or a
2:48:29 website in the first place as you add
2:48:31 functionality and more options to your
2:48:33 app or website you don't want to lose
2:48:35 sight of what the goal was in the first
2:48:38 place
2:48:39 so we have these two kinds of
2:48:40 hierarchies that are going to affect our
2:48:42 content and affect our design structure
2:48:45 and ideally these two things would align
2:48:48 perfectly so the goals of the client are
2:48:51 reflected in how our interface is
2:48:53 prioritized in terms of the hierarchy of
2:48:56 the design and the content so let's look
2:48:58 at how that might work with a real world
2:49:00 example
2:49:01 the very first thing you want to do is
2:49:03 to set the scene or set the tone when
2:49:06 you first open an app or a website that
2:49:08 first impression is going to be really
2:49:10 really important you have to think about
2:49:12 what's the first thing the user sees
2:49:15 because it's going to set the tone for
2:49:16 the rest of their experience
2:49:19 so the user opens your website or app
2:49:22 they see what's in front of them they
2:49:24 form some kind of impression and opinion
2:49:26 about it
2:49:27 and then they act or engage or react to
2:49:31 what's in front of them
2:49:33 so very quickly there's three things
2:49:35 that are going on with the user they're
2:49:37 seeing
2:49:38 thinking and physically reacting let's
2:49:41 use the calarts poster website that we
2:49:44 looked at earlier and see how a user
2:49:46 first interacts with that
2:49:48 when the website first opens a selection
2:49:51 of posters is displayed and these
2:49:53 posters are pre-selected and pulled
2:49:55 randomly from a much larger group so the
2:49:58 goal is to show the user some of the
2:50:00 best posters but also have them be
2:50:02 different each time the user visits the
2:50:04 site so if we ask ourselves what's the
2:50:07 first thing the user sees when they open
2:50:09 this website the answer would be a
2:50:11 collection of really exciting and
2:50:13 energetic visual posters but the design
2:50:16 and content of this interface are also
2:50:18 going to tell the user some other things
2:50:21 as well
2:50:22 so they see all of these posters
2:50:24 but they also can tell by the way that
2:50:26 it's laid out that this is some kind of
2:50:28 collection or some kind of archive the
2:50:31 site feels like it's more concerned with
2:50:33 visual form than textual information for
2:50:36 instance so we know that it's a very
2:50:39 visually driven website
2:50:41 so the user has some kind of expectation
2:50:43 about what the rest of their experience
2:50:45 on this website is going to be like
2:50:48 in a lot of ways the initial visual
2:50:50 experience that you give the user is a
2:50:53 foreshadowing of the rest of the
2:50:55 experience that they'll have on your
2:50:56 site once the user has formed an
2:50:59 impression of your site the user is then
2:51:01 going to interact with it somehow so you
2:51:04 could ask yourself what's the first
2:51:05 thing that the user does
2:51:08 i think the first thing would be to
2:51:09 click on a poster
2:51:11 because it's a visually driven site the
2:51:13 user is probably going to get pulled
2:51:15 into that visual experience so they'll
2:51:18 either want to see more detail by
2:51:19 clicking on a poster or see more posters
2:51:22 by scrolling down the screen so let's
2:51:25 follow this through and see what happens
2:51:26 when you click on a poster
2:51:30 the poster fills the screen and what's
2:51:32 important here is that it offers a
2:51:34 different visual experience than the
2:51:36 home screen that we started on
2:51:38 so there's a significant scale
2:51:40 difference we can see much more detail
2:51:43 the visual experience is immediately
2:51:45 different
2:51:46 in a simple way we're offering the user
2:51:48 a reward for spending their time
2:51:50 clicking
2:51:51 so let's see what happens when the user
2:51:53 scrolls down to see more posters
2:51:56 here very little changes visually at
2:51:58 least we're not trying to show the user
2:52:01 details and reward them with a new
2:52:03 experience instead we're trying to show
2:52:05 them the volume and breadth of the
2:52:08 content that's available on the website
2:52:10 our goal is still to engage the user but
2:52:12 we're doing it in a different way
2:52:14 so those are our two big moves in terms
2:52:16 of interaction in our hierarchy but we
2:52:19 could ask ourselves what does the user
2:52:20 do after that they start to explore some
2:52:23 of the mid to lower level possibilities
2:52:25 for interaction
2:52:27 so this means starting to explore the
2:52:29 interface a little bit more there are
2:52:32 navigation elements in the interface
2:52:34 such as the timeline or categories or
2:52:37 the search field that are lower down in
2:52:39 the visual hierarchy but they're much
2:52:42 higher in their specific functionality
2:52:44 so you're less likely to use them when
2:52:47 you first encounter a website and you're
2:52:49 just browsing or looking around and
2:52:51 you're much more likely to use them
2:52:53 later on when you have a specific
2:52:55 intention or a specific goal that you're
2:52:57 trying to achieve
2:52:59 let's look at how these hierarchies of
2:53:01 content function and design work on a
2:53:03 handheld device
2:53:05 instead of using something where we've
2:53:06 already made an interface let's start
2:53:09 from scratch
2:53:10 let's build it up from nothing and see
2:53:12 how these ideas of hierarchy might shape
2:53:15 our structure and our interface so let's
2:53:18 pretend we're designing an app to get a
2:53:20 cap
2:53:21 let's look at what our goals might be
2:53:23 that might shape the interface
2:53:26 our goals in terms of content and
2:53:28 function could also be termed as being
2:53:30 goals for the client and goals for the
2:53:32 user and in this case those goals would
2:53:35 all be exactly the same we'd want to get
2:53:38 a cab as fast as possible so there's a
2:53:41 clear primary singular function that's
2:53:44 really going to drive the first thing
2:53:46 that the user sees on our screen if
2:53:49 we're going to get a ride as fast as
2:53:50 possible there's several things that the
2:53:53 user is going to want to see all at the
2:53:55 same time
2:53:56 first of all we're going to need to see
2:53:57 our own location where we are on the map
2:54:00 we're going to need to be able to enter
2:54:02 our destination and we're probably going
2:54:04 to want to see what cabs are nearby that
2:54:07 are going to be able to pick us up
2:54:09 and we need to have all of those three
2:54:11 things happening at the same time
2:54:14 simultaneously to have a seamless and
2:54:16 fast experience
2:54:18 when we're designing an interface like
2:54:20 this the first thing that we're really
2:54:22 doing is articulating the problem and
2:54:25 figuring out what is a good solution for
2:54:27 that we're not really thinking about any
2:54:30 specific visual solutions here we're
2:54:32 looking at the big picture
2:54:34 and trying to give a structural shape to
2:54:36 our design
2:54:38 we're still trying to answer those
2:54:40 larger questions to do with hierarchy
2:54:42 and organization
2:54:44 we're looking at what goes where and why
2:54:47 we're trying to figure out what is the
2:54:48 best visual solution and what the main
2:54:51 design elements are
2:54:53 we're thinking about the hierarchy of
2:54:55 those elements where they're going to go
2:54:57 within our interface or within our whole
2:54:59 experience so we're really looking at
2:55:02 all of these much bigger moves we're not
2:55:04 getting caught up in any design details
2:55:06 yet
2:55:10 in this section we're going to continue
2:55:12 our discussion about hierarchy and
2:55:15 composition within the interface and
2:55:17 we're going to look at some ideas
2:55:18 concerning conventions and expectations
2:55:21 for both the web and apps
2:55:24 our screens are often divided up in very
2:55:27 conventional ways and as user interface
2:55:29 designers we have to figure out how to
2:55:31 use these conventions but also create
2:55:34 some kind of variables within them in
2:55:36 terms of scale and visual attention our
2:55:39 content area is normally the most
2:55:41 important followed by branding and a
2:55:43 couple of levels of navigation or
