2:55:45 functionality before we figure out any
2:55:48 details of the design we have to figure
2:55:50 out the relationship between these four
2:55:52 different areas
2:55:54 and each of these areas is somewhat
2:55:56 self-contained so we could spend a great
2:55:58 deal of time working on the elements and
2:56:01 components of each of these individual
2:56:03 areas
2:56:04 the simplest structural division that we
2:56:06 can make is between the content that
2:56:09 might change frequently and the elements
2:56:11 that might remain stable such as the
2:56:14 branding and navigation
2:56:16 which gives us a familiar and standard
2:56:18 relationship between the header and the
2:56:21 body of our website
2:56:23 and once we've isolated these larger
2:56:25 areas or divisions we can start to think
2:56:28 about how they might be structured in
2:56:30 more detail
2:56:31 if we look at the body of this simple
2:56:33 website for instance we could have a
2:56:35 number of different things going on in
2:56:37 that one area it doesn't have to be a
2:56:39 single image or a single activity there
2:56:42 could be multiple things happening at
2:56:44 the same time and as soon as we have
2:56:46 multiple things happening our job as an
2:56:49 interface designer becomes more
2:56:50 complicated we have to start to think
2:56:53 about spacing and structure organization
2:56:56 and hierarchy conventions and familiar
2:56:59 structures can help us deal with these
2:57:01 things quite quickly and easily the web
2:57:03 is often more concerned with the content
2:57:05 that's in the structure than the
2:57:07 structure itself
2:57:09 that structure quite often just has to
2:57:11 be functional and fairly invisible so
2:57:13 it's okay if it's familiar to some
2:57:15 extent our expectation of the website is
2:57:18 that there are multiple things happening
2:57:20 on one screen we have a larger surface
2:57:22 area so it makes sense that there's more
2:57:25 going on
2:57:26 but when we start to look at a phone and
2:57:28 look at apps on our phone it's not quite
2:57:30 the same instead of four things
2:57:32 happening on one screen it's often one
2:57:35 thing happening on four screens and
2:57:37 those screens work together to form a
2:57:40 sequence of events
2:57:41 so our branding might be on a totally
2:57:43 separate screen before we get to our
2:57:45 content and even if we wanted to
2:57:47 reiterate our branding it would have to
2:57:49 be done at a smaller scale because we
2:57:51 have very little real estate on the
2:57:53 screen of the phone so where everything
2:57:55 is laid out in front of us on a web page
2:57:58 for instance
2:57:59 on an app everything is hidden or
2:58:01 condensed or sequential so quite often
2:58:05 our navigation is invisible until we
2:58:07 need it and any interaction will
2:58:09 normally eclipse the previous screen so
2:58:12 the components that make up a web
2:58:14 experience in a single page might have
2:58:16 to be spread across multiple screens for
2:58:19 an app on the phone which intrinsically
2:58:21 means we're traveling from screen to
2:58:23 screen
2:58:24 on the phone which is a little more of a
2:58:26 fluid experience which explains why even
2:58:29 though a website and an app are both
2:58:31 screen-based experiences they actually
2:58:33 feel very different even if they have
2:58:35 the same content the app feels much more
2:58:38 fluid so while our content here is
2:58:40 divided across a number of different
2:58:42 pages there's definitely going to be
2:58:44 some pages where more of the elements
2:58:47 are going to need to exist at the same
2:58:48 time
2:58:49 and again there are formal conventions
2:58:52 built around functionality that we
2:58:55 adhere to that could be where our
2:58:57 branding appears how our navigation
2:58:59 works with the swipe or even where our
2:59:01 menu bar is or what's in that menu bar
2:59:04 and these conventions are constantly
2:59:07 changing we're moving towards that more
2:59:09 fluid immersive experience we talked
2:59:11 about so we want all of our navigation
2:59:14 and other elements to be hidden and if
2:59:16 we're going to travel to other screens
2:59:18 we want to do that in a fluid way not by
2:59:21 clicking but by swiping and scrolling
2:59:24 our phones act as windows we can only
2:59:26 see a small amount of content on them
2:59:29 but we know there's a much bigger world
2:59:31 out there that we can move around in so
2:59:33 while we might only get one piece of
2:59:35 information or experience at a time we
2:59:38 know there are thousands more outside
2:59:40 the visible realm of our window and that
2:59:42 poetic explanation has some practical
2:59:45 implications we don't want anything to
2:59:47 get in the way of our window to get in
2:59:49 the way of our small amount of content
2:59:51 we're looking at our experience so our
2:59:54 menus become hidden or as invisible as
2:59:56 possible on a website our five primary
2:59:59 options might be laid out on display but
3:00:02 on an app they're condensed and hidden
3:00:04 they live in a menu that we don't see
3:00:06 until we want to and that menu itself is
3:00:09 evolving again to be more fluid so
3:00:12 instead of just being a drop down menu
3:00:14 which feels like a web convention we're
3:00:16 now much more used to having menus come
3:00:19 in from the side with a swipe and be
3:00:21 much more smooth and fluid the user
3:00:24 interface for our apps are like swiss
3:00:26 army knives they have multiple
3:00:29 functionality but everything is packed
3:00:31 away until you need that specific tool
3:00:34 and when you need a tool there's many
3:00:36 different things to choose from and they
3:00:38 generally are really good at doing just
3:00:41 one thing
3:00:42 but until you need them they remain
3:00:44 packed away they remain invisible if the
3:00:47 website lets it all hang out on display
3:00:50 the app is much more based on a
3:00:52 philosophy of being hidden until needed
3:00:55 our menus and navigation shouldn't get
3:00:57 in the way of the small visual field
3:01:00 that is our only place to show content
3:01:03 even if we wanted to unpack our options
3:01:06 there'd be no room for us to lay them
3:01:08 all out on the screen if we have more
3:01:10 than a couple of options we're going to
3:01:12 have to compact them down and create an
3:01:15 interface element that can hide them we
3:01:18 want to make sure that our user is
3:01:20 focusing on the content on our small
3:01:22 screen and because we have little real
3:01:24 estate it means that we have to feed
3:01:27 them tasks one at a time and because of
3:01:30 this our apps tend to be more focused on
3:01:33 being very successful at a very
3:01:35 localized and specific task when we need
3:01:38 more real estate for complex tasks we
3:01:41 tend to switch to the computer screen
3:01:43 and this fundamentally affects what kind
3:01:45 of content or activity works well on
3:01:48 what kind of screen for the ui designer
3:01:51 it also shapes and structures what we do
3:01:54 there are set conventions related to
3:01:56 what works best on these screens that we
3:01:58 just can't escape we have to figure out
3:02:01 how to get the most out of those
3:02:03 conventions
3:02:05 in this section we're going to look at
3:02:07 structures and grids and how those work
3:02:10 in user interface design
3:02:12 graphic designers are very familiar with
3:02:14 using grids they use them as an
3:02:16 invisible way to align elements in order
3:02:19 to get them to work together in the last
3:02:21 section we looked at how the real estate
3:02:23 of a mobile device differs greatly from
3:02:26 that of a computer screen and how this
3:02:28 affects the kind of design
3:02:30 and elements that we might have on those
3:02:32 screens
3:02:33 for the most part this meant having a
3:02:35 lot of single tasks on multiple screens
3:02:38 for a mobile device
3:02:40 and a lot of multiple tasks on a single
3:02:42 screen for say a website on a computer
3:02:44 screen
3:02:45 and this is going to have an effect on
3:02:48 how we think about structure and what
3:02:50 kind of grids we might use for these two
3:02:52 different devices a grid can be used for
3:02:55 any size screen and any amount of
3:02:57 screens it's really just a useful way
3:03:00 for the user interface designer to
3:03:02 organize the elements of their interface
3:03:04 on screen and to structure content so
3:03:07 that it works for the user so let's see
3:03:09 what kind of organization system and
3:03:11 what kind of grid works for these two
3:03:14 different screens let's start out by
3:03:16 looking at a website and let's say the
3:03:18 main place that that's going to live is
3:03:20 on a larger computer screen we've
3:03:22 already established we're going to have
3:03:23 multiple tasks on a single screen
3:03:26 and that means we're going to have more
3:03:28 elements to organize and of course we
3:03:30 have a larger space to work with some
3:03:32 basic principles of graphic design
3:03:34 composition will really help us how to
3:03:37 use grids spacing scale come up with a
3:03:40 system and be consistent within that
3:03:42 system and all of these tools are very
3:03:45 familiar to graphic designers because
3:03:47 they're the same tools we've been using
3:03:49 for print design for many years and
3:03:51 while these ideas don't translate
3:03:53 necessarily directly into the digital
3:03:56 space it's pretty close they're really
3:03:58 fundamental tools for arranging elements
3:04:01 within a space and we're going to stick
3:04:03 to this as our design centric process
3:04:06 where we're really focusing on how
3:04:08 things look and feel
3:04:10 and letting the visual nature of the
3:04:12 interface really drive some of these big
3:04:14 decisions that we're making at the start
3:04:16 of our design process let's take a real
3:04:19 world example and try to see how some of
3:04:22 these design principles have been
3:04:23 applied let's go back to calarts poster
3:04:26 archive website and let's try and
3:04:29 deconstruct it a little bit take it
3:04:31 apart and try and figure out how it was
3:04:33 built it has an overall structure that's
3:04:36 very familiar from general web design
3:04:38 and from our last class as well where
3:04:41 the two main parts of the interface are
3:04:43 divided into the header and the body and
3:04:46 even though there's no clear dividing
3:04:48 line between the two they occupy
3:04:51 different spaces for different kinds of
3:04:53 content and different kinds of
3:04:55 interaction by the user
3:04:57 the header is the place for navigation
3:04:59 and the place where the user goes to
3:05:01 make decisions about what they want to
3:05:04 see and what they want to do
3:05:06 the body is the place where the content
3:05:08 lives and the place where the user sees
3:05:10 the result of any changes that they've
3:05:13 instigated in the navigation area
3:05:16 basically the controls are at the top of
3:05:18 the screen and the results of those
3:05:20 controls show up at the bottom
3:05:22 for a site like this we've got a lot of
3:05:24 different components to organize and
3:05:27 this is quite good because it gives us a
3:05:29 chance to really see the grid we can
3:05:31 make it visible quite easily and all the
3:05:34 grid really is is a series of lines that
3:05:37 obey a certain mathematical structure
3:05:40 and these allow you to organize a lot of
3:05:42 disparate content together so you can
3:05:45 see here that all of the images align at
3:05:48 their uppermost edge you can also see
3:05:51 that there's an equal space between all
3:05:53 of the images all the vertical images go
3:05:56 from the top to the bottom of the grid
3:05:58 space that we've created for them but
3:06:00 because they're different proportions
3:06:02 they sometimes leave some blank space to
3:06:04 the right or to the top and the bottom
3:06:06 if it's a horizontal image but we
3:06:08 basically have quite a simple grid for
3:06:11 organizing both images and text
3:06:15 the grid can be divided up into certain
3:06:17 sections as well we have rows that run
3:06:20 horizontally
3:06:22 and these are separated by the captions
3:06:24 for each image
3:06:25 we have columns that run vertically and
3:06:28 once we've set a fixed value for our
3:06:30 columns and our rows
3:06:32 it's a system that is very modular so we
3:06:35 could use this on a number of different
3:06:37 size screens with a number of different
3:06:39 columns or a number of different rows
3:06:42 the space in between the columns is left
3:06:44 blank and that's called a gutter and the
3:06:47 space on the outside edge is called the
3:06:49 margins and here i've left that a little
3:06:52 wider so that all the content seems to
3:06:54 cling together a little bit more when we
3:06:56 combine the grid lines for our rows and
3:06:59 our columns we end up with two separate
3:07:01 areas the largest area is our image area
3:07:05 and even though that's defined by the
3:07:06 grid as being even our content that fits
3:07:09 into it as you can see here might not
3:07:12 always exactly fit the image area unless
3:07:15 we wanted to enlarge and crop the image
3:07:16 of course
3:07:18 so even though we're creating a modular
3:07:20 and mathematical system there are still
3:07:22 a number of variables within that system
3:07:24 so we have to make design decisions and
3:07:27 rules about how those variables are
3:07:29 going to work
3:07:30 and we can see the same thing in the
3:07:32 caption area the space that we've
3:07:34 created underneath the image area
3:07:37 where we might have a different amount
3:07:39 of text from one caption to another so
3:07:42 this is going to create another set of
3:07:44 variables so in this case you can see
3:07:46 it's creating some variation in our grid
3:07:49 the height of the caption areas is not
3:07:51 always the same
3:07:53 let's zoom in a little bit and see how
3:07:55 some of these variables work in the
3:07:56 image area and in the caption area
3:07:59 when we zoom into our grid we can see
3:08:01 that the images in the image area go all
3:08:04 the way to the edges and fill the space
3:08:07 and this is great when the proportions
3:08:09 are right for the image to do that but
3:08:11 that's not always going to be the case
3:08:13 every image has a height and a width and
3:08:16 that height and width isn't always going
3:08:18 to match the height and the width that
3:08:20 we've made with our grid we have to
3:08:22 decide how images with other proportions
3:08:25 are going to fit into our image space
3:08:27 and we're going to have to come up with
3:08:28 a set of rules to make that consistent
3:08:31 in this case i'm deciding that images
3:08:34 have to either fill the width or the
3:08:36 height of our image space so if i have a
3:08:39 square image it's going to fit the width
3:08:41 but it's going to leave even space at
3:08:43 the top and the bottom
3:08:45 but if i have a differently proportioned
3:08:47 image perhaps one that is taller and
3:08:49 skinnier it's going to leave space at
3:08:51 the left and the right side and this is
3:08:53 going to visually affect my grid
3:08:56 because my text aligns to the left i'm
3:08:58 going to decide to align my images to
3:09:00 the left as well to try and create a
3:09:02 strong visual line and keep my grid
3:09:04 intact
3:09:06 and while i'm allowing my images some
3:09:08 responsiveness to my grid i'm basically
3:09:11 trying to keep everything as even and as
3:09:13 systematic as possible if i can i want
3:09:16 to try and keep all my horizontal and
3:09:18 vertical spaces to be even
3:09:21 so the height of the image the width of
3:09:24 the image and the height and width of
3:09:26 the space between the images as well
3:09:29 and this basically creates boxes for me
3:09:31 to work within so i have a box where i'm
3:09:34 going to place my images and figure out
3:09:36 a system for how the images sit in that
3:09:38 box but i'm also going to have a box for
3:09:41 text and i'm going to have to figure out
3:09:43 a system for how the text sits in that
3:09:46 box
3:09:47 and as soon as we have text we have to
3:09:49 think about typography and getting
3:09:51 typography to look good is really about
3:09:54 making a number of micro decisions that
3:09:56 collectively contribute to good
3:09:58 typography so the first thing you can
3:10:00 ask yourself is what about the typeface
3:10:03 should i set it in helvetica or times or
3:10:06 comic sans perhaps think about what kind
3:10:09 of mood you want to have what
3:10:11 connotations you want the typeface to
3:10:13 have but also think about functionality
3:10:16 which typeface is going to be the most
3:10:18 readable at the right size and we can't
3:10:20 decide that until we try out some
3:10:22 different sizes so you can see here even
3:10:25 though the typeface is the same by
3:10:27 changing the size it changes its
3:10:29 relationship to the textbox and also
3:10:32 when the type's bigger we see the form
3:10:34 of the type so we get more of its
3:10:36 character when it's smaller it just
3:10:38 becomes functional text for reading only
3:10:41 so let's say we choose the medium size
3:10:43 text we still have to figure out its
3:10:45 relationship to the text box where is it
3:10:48 going to sit at the top in the middle or
3:10:51 at the bottom and how much space is it
3:10:53 going to have around it all of these
3:10:55 small decisions affect what the type
3:10:57 looks like but also how readable the
3:11:00 type is
3:11:01 but there's a secondary effect as well
3:11:04 depending on how we organize the type we
3:11:06 change the amount of negative space that
3:11:08 we're creating and so we build a
3:11:11 different relationship in our grid
3:11:13 between the text and the image if you're
3:11:15 working on a website that has a lot of
3:11:17 content and really needs a grid it can
3:11:19 be quite useful to start out with that
3:11:22 grid even if you're just using it with
3:11:24 dummy content
3:11:25 here's an early wireframe sketch of the
3:11:28 callouts poster archive website for
3:11:30 instance and it's before we even have
3:11:32 any images or have any information to
3:11:35 plug in there what we're trying to do
3:11:37 here is figure out our grid and our
3:11:39 system we're looking at the scale and
3:11:42 proportion of images we're looking at
3:11:44 how our captions are going to work how
3:11:46 our spacing between the images might
3:11:48 work we're also deciding which pieces of
3:11:51 information go where so we're making
3:11:53 some content and organizational
3:11:55 decisions as well as making some formal
3:11:58 design decisions and we're doing all
3:12:00 this without any real content so once
3:12:03 we've come up with the design that we
3:12:04 like we can actually fill it full of
3:12:06 content to see if it's really going to
3:12:08 work or not so just by putting images
3:12:12 into our grid and into our design we can
3:12:14 get a much better idea if it's really
3:12:16 going to function or not and remember
3:12:19 this is just a stepping stone towards a
3:12:21 final design we're really just looking
3:12:23 at big design decisions here we're
3:12:25 trying to get an idea if we can create a
3:12:28 grid spacing scale some kind of
3:12:31 consistent system that's going to work
3:12:34 for our content and we can do that
3:12:36 without really having the actual content
3:12:40 in this section we're going to talk a
3:12:41 little bit more about size and format of
3:12:44 our screens
3:12:45 we've talked previously about the
3:12:47 differences in real estate between a
3:12:49 handheld mobile device and a computer
3:12:51 screen but we've been ignoring some very
3:12:54 important things like the fact that one
3:12:56 is often vertical and the other is
3:12:58 always horizontal but most importantly
3:13:01 that they operate at radically different
3:13:03 scales
3:13:04 if i shrink my phone down to the scale
3:13:06 of my computer screen you can see just
3:13:09 quite what a difference that is
3:13:12 and while this scale is accurate in
3:13:14 terms of physical inches it's not
3:13:17 actually accurate in terms of how we use
3:13:20 these devices and how much space they
3:13:22 take up in our field of vision
3:13:24 in order to figure that out we have to
3:13:26 take into account viewing distance how
3:13:29 far is the screen away from our eyeballs
3:13:32 i might hold my phone right in front of
3:13:34 my face it might only be 10 inches away
3:13:38 but i might sit much further away from a
3:13:40 very large computer screen that could be
3:13:42 as much as 24 inches
3:13:44 so if i adjust the size of these screens
3:13:47 to take into account viewing distance
3:13:49 they're not as disparate as you think
3:13:51 within my field of vision they're not as
3:13:54 wildly different as they first appear
3:13:56 the relative scale of these two screens
3:13:59 is much closer than their physical
3:14:01 measurements in order to try and prove
3:14:04 this let's take some elements on each
3:14:06 screen and put them at the correct size
3:14:08 for that screen let's figure out what we
3:14:11 think the right size is for readable
3:14:13 type on our computer screen and let's
3:14:15 also add an estimated size for legible
3:14:18 images we can also see how many images
3:14:21 are going to fit on this screen
3:14:24 then we can take those same components
3:14:26 and estimate what their size would be on
3:14:28 a handheld device the relationship
3:14:31 between the image and text size isn't
3:14:33 that different but what you can see of
3:14:36 course is that we can fit far fewer
3:14:38 images within the window of the handheld
3:14:41 device
3:14:42 if we look at the grid on both of these
3:14:44 screen sizes we can see that it's not
3:14:47 wildly different it's really just been
3:14:50 scaled up a little bit and expanded and
3:14:53 when we compare the screens like this
3:14:55 they don't seem that different in terms
3:14:57 of scale if i use screen grabs from our
3:15:00 real world example of the poster website
3:15:03 you can see that that difference in
3:15:04 scale is even less
3:15:07 in terms of how we see things in our
3:15:09 field of vision it's really not that
3:15:11 different as we looked at before in a
3:15:14 lot of ways the phone is a smaller
3:15:16 window on a much larger world in this
3:15:19 case the larger world is the website
3:15:22 itself or the computer screen another
3:15:25 way to look at it is that both our phone
3:15:27 and our computer are windows to content
3:15:30 and they might be looking at the same
3:15:32 thing
3:15:33 but at slightly different scales
3:15:36 while there are differences between how
3:15:38 we see things with these two sizes of
3:15:40 screen there's also a structural
3:15:42 relationship between them so there are
3:15:45 often similarities as well as
3:15:47 differences
3:15:48 so then the next question is
3:15:50 which screen size do i design for first
3:15:54 which size or format is going to be the
3:15:56 most important
3:15:58 and the answer is my usual one well it
3:16:01 depends on the context it depends on the
3:16:03 content of your app or website it
3:16:06 depends on what you want it to do and
3:16:08 how it's going to be used but it also
3:16:11 depends on who's going to use it you
3:16:13 have to ask yourself from the start who
3:16:16 is my audience and what kind of platform
3:16:18 are they going to use my app or website
3:16:21 on and increasingly the answer is that
3:16:24 it's going to be used on viewed on a
3:16:26 small screen increasingly we design for
3:16:29 the handheld mobile device first and for
3:16:32 the larger computer screen second while
3:16:35 websites aren't just going to disappear
3:16:37 and all be replaced by apps we're
3:16:39 already in a phase where the website has
3:16:42 had to adapt to living in a much smaller
3:16:44 space
3:16:46 in this last class of our course we're
3:16:48 going to look at the stage in our
3:16:49 process where we might be finished with
3:16:52 our static designs and handing them over
3:16:54 to a programmer the programmer might cut
3:16:57 these pieces up and put them into a
3:16:59 working prototype so far in our classes
3:17:02 we've been thinking about interactivity
3:17:05 but we haven't really been designing
3:17:07 interactively if anything we've been
3:17:09 designing inactively or statically we've
3:17:12 been trying to envision interactivity
3:17:15 and represent it in our designs
3:17:17 but they've all been pretty static
3:17:20 and that design first process has been
3:17:23 deliberate we're coming at user
3:17:25 interface design from a visual
3:17:27 perspective from a design centric
3:17:30 process and focus we're really thinking
3:17:32 about design first
3:17:34 in many ways we've prioritized the role
3:17:37 of the user interface designer over the
3:17:40 user experience designer and as we saw
3:17:42 in our very first week these two
3:17:45 specializations are intrinsically
3:17:47 connected to each other and have a lot
3:17:49 of overlap our methodology in this
3:17:52 course is to isolate the ui so that we
3:17:55 can focus on it so that we can spend
3:17:57 more time looking at the issues to do
3:18:00 with formal design within the ui ux
3:18:03 process
3:18:04 but even if we isolate the ui from ux
3:18:07 they have to meet at some point and now
3:18:09 is the time in that process
3:18:12 we're kind of making a connection
3:18:13 between our forced division but that
3:18:16 happens in the real world sometimes as
3:18:18 well when a designer has to supply a
3:18:20 programmer with a bunch of visual assets
3:18:23 for them to make a prototype
3:18:25 so when i'm working with my students on
3:18:27 ui projects we often create as finished
3:18:31 a mock-up as we possibly can and as many
3:18:34 screens showing as many different
3:18:36 options as we can make as well i'm
3:18:38 trying to have them retain their focus
3:18:40 on graphic design and the user interface
3:18:43 but also trying to look at the context
3:18:46 of how their app for instance is going
3:18:48 to be used
3:18:49 so here's an app designed by one of my
3:18:51 students that's a tool for analyzing
3:18:54 composition in order to create a
3:18:56 realistic representation of their
3:18:58 interface design they have to design
3:19:01 many screens showing all the different
3:19:03 stages of interaction and these designs
3:19:05 could go on to be the basis for creating
3:19:08 a digital prototype the point of making
3:19:10 so many screens is that we're trying to
3:19:13 show interaction and motion and
3:19:16 engagement with the user through static
3:19:18 means through a series of static images
3:19:22 as i said before we're prioritizing the
3:19:25 graphic design aspect of the user
3:19:27 interface so let's use this project as a
3:19:30 way to recap and revisit some of the
3:19:32 things that we've learned about the user
3:19:34 interface
3:19:35 let's take a couple of these screens and
3:19:37 make them larger and then we can
3:19:39 deconstruct how they work
3:19:41 our overall division of the screen is
3:19:44 quite familiar at the top of the screen
3:19:46 we have an area to reinforce our
3:19:48 branding but also to have some
3:19:50 navigation elements some elements that
3:19:53 would remain in the same place
3:19:55 throughout pretty much any screen of our
3:19:57 app we could refer to these as our
3:20:00 global navigation
3:20:02 at the side of our screen we have a text
3:20:04 based menu and you can imagine that that
3:20:07 appears and disappears as needed
3:20:10 we have an area for content where our
3:20:12 main activity is going to take place or
3:20:15 where we're going to see the results of
3:20:16 the choices we make in the menus and at
3:20:19 the bottom of the screen we have another
3:20:21 menu that provides a set of tools that
3:20:24 could be specific to whatever content is
3:20:26 happening in that area
3:20:28 so you can see that we've already
3:20:30 established our main elements here we've
3:20:33 got a grid a structure and a set of
3:20:35 rules to work with we've partitioned and
3:20:38 organized our interface
3:20:40 and even though we're working with
3:20:42 static screens here we're not designing
3:20:44 any kind of motion we can think about
3:20:47 how our interface is going to work how
3:20:49 are our menus going to come in and out
3:20:51 of the screen how are we going to move
3:20:54 through content
3:20:55 all of these things can be considered
3:20:58 and part of our design plan without
3:21:00 necessarily implementing them in these
3:21:02 stages we can figure out what the rules
3:21:05 are for our interface
3:21:07 by dividing some of these sample screens
3:21:09 we can get knowledge that we can use
3:21:12 almost like a template to create other
3:21:14 screens
3:21:15 so any kind of division that we might
3:21:17 have in our menu
3:21:18 any kind of composition and spacing
3:21:21 arrangement to do with images or content
3:21:24 could be consistent through a number of
3:21:25 screens in the same way that our
3:21:28 movement and our motion in the interface
3:21:30 could also be consistent we can go a
3:21:32 little bit deeper in analyzing our
3:21:34 interface here if we take each of our
3:21:36 sections we can look at how the graphic
3:21:39 elements in each of those sections works
3:21:42 we have a logo type that exists in our
3:21:44 branding area and that's going to give
3:21:46 us several design considerations to
3:21:48 think about not just the logo type
3:21:50 itself but its size position and color
3:21:53 if we look at our typographic menu again
3:21:56 we have several design decisions to make
3:21:58 first of all how much space is our menu
3:22:01 going to take up
3:22:02 what kind of size should it be what kind
3:22:05 of shape how are we going to divide that
3:22:07 up what sort of typeface do we use how
3:22:10 do we use color dividers there's plenty
3:22:13 of design decisions here to give a
3:22:15 generic menu a very specific look and
3:22:17 feel
3:22:18 in our content areas most of the visual
3:22:22 work is being done by the content itself
3:22:25 the thing that is inside the box inside
3:22:28 the frame but we still have to think
3:22:30 about the size position color and
3:22:32 spacing of that content if we go back up
3:22:35 top to our navigation and branding bar
3:22:38 and think about our navigation we've got
3:22:40 plenty to think about here even though
3:22:42 it's a small area we have to think about
3:22:44 our icons what color they are the size
3:22:47 and spacing of them and those
3:22:49 considerations are also going to be the
3:22:51 same ones in our menu at the bottom our
3:22:53 content specific menu and here because
3:22:56 the tasks that we're carrying out are
3:22:58 more specific we might have to design
3:23:01 some more specific or custom icons
3:23:03 if we take all the various screens that
3:23:06 the student designed for this app and
3:23:08 lay them all out at once we can see that
3:23:10 they really work as a set there's a
3:23:12 level of consistency there from screen
3:23:15 to screen that's really important so
3:23:17 what we end up with is a really great
3:23:19 map of all our user interface elements
3:23:22 and all the different ways that they
3:23:24 could be used and at this stage we might
3:23:26 not have figured out all the different
3:23:28 ways that the user is going to interact
3:23:30 with them and all the different ways
3:23:32 that the interface is going to change
3:23:34 we're really still investigating the
3:23:36 graphic design and aesthetics of the
3:23:39 interface
3:23:40 so the next stages are going to be to
3:23:42 think much more about how the menus
3:23:44 might move how buttons or elements might
3:23:47 change states what kind of areas are
3:23:50 going to be active and generally to
3:23:52 think about how are we going to bring
3:23:54 our designs to life and we're going to
3:23:57 look at that much more in our next
3:23:59 course
3:24:00 where ui meets ux and our emphasis
3:24:03 changes from being much more on graphic
3:24:05 design and the user interface to much
3:24:08 more on structure and how the user
3:24:11 experiences the interface
3:24:13 but ui and ux will continue to inform
3:24:16 each other at every stage of the process
3:24:19 but from now on our focus is going to
3:24:21 shift a little bit more so that user
3:24:23 experience becomes the emphasis
3:24:26 and we're not abandoning our
3:24:27 design-centric process here we're just
3:24:30 taking it to the next stage to the next
3:24:32 level and once again combining your ui
3:24:36 and your ux knowledge together is what's
3:24:38 going to make you a great ui ux designer
3:24:45 [Music]
3:24:46 hi i'm michael williams
3:24:48 welcome to user experience fundamentals
3:24:51 this course provides half the foundation
3:24:53 of knowledge you need to work with uiux
3:24:56 the ux or user experience half of the
3:24:58 equation
3:24:59 if you've already taken visual elements
3:25:01 of user interface design the first
3:25:03 course in the ui ux specialization
3:25:05 offered by calarts you'll be able to
3:25:07 combine your knowledge from that course
3:25:09 with this one
3:25:10 this course focuses on ux and it's less
3:25:13 concerned with the graphic user
3:25:14 interface and much more concerned with
3:25:16 how the designer shapes the user's
3:25:18 interaction and experience
3:25:20 you could think of a ux designer as a
3:25:22 tour guide holding the hand of the user
3:25:25 making their journey easier and pointing
3:25:27 things out to them along the way
3:25:29 this course examines how content is
3:25:31 organized and structured to create an
3:25:33 experience for a user and will use a
3:25:36 condensed process as a roadmap for
3:25:38 developing ux design from start to
3:25:40 finish
3:25:41 we'll begin with a discussion about
3:25:43 ideation moving on to how to map and
3:25:45 organize goals carry out research and
3:25:48 development as well as audience research
3:25:50 and user centered design
3:25:52 we'll examine how to create a site map
3:25:54 to organize content and use non-visual
3:25:57 paper prototyping in user testing
3:26:00 we'll take all of these different areas
3:26:02 of information and research and combine
3:26:04 them into a wireframe and finally into a
3:26:07 working on-screen interactive mock-up
3:26:10 when this course is combined with the
3:26:12 first course in the specialization
3:26:14 you'll have a basic working knowledge of
3:26:16 the components of uiux
3:26:18 and also a methodology that will allow
3:26:20 you to develop an app or website from
3:26:22 conception through to digital prototype
3:26:25 in other words you'll have the many
3:26:27 pieces of compartmentalized knowledge
3:26:29 and skills you need but also a way of
3:26:31 putting those pieces together into a
3:26:33 functional process for ui ux development
3:26:37 [Music]
3:26:43 [Music]
3:26:48 why peer review is important to a
3:26:50 creative practice
3:26:52 design is rarely a solitary practice
3:26:55 as a designer you might be working with
3:26:57 clients or have a boss you need to
3:26:59 report to
3:27:00 you might also be part of a design team
3:27:02 or even managing a team of your own
3:27:05 you might be asked to present a project
3:27:07 to stakeholders at a company or pitch
3:27:09 your services to a new client
3:27:12 ultimately your design needs to stand
3:27:14 alone but in the process of making
3:27:16 design you should be testing out your
3:27:18 ideas with an audience getting feedback
3:27:21 and developing your design accordingly
3:27:25 design is very intertwined with
3:27:26 communication and to be a successful
3:27:29 designer you need to be able to talk
3:27:31 about your work to explain it and show
3:27:34 that you understand how it's working
3:27:37 in different situations it may be
3:27:39 necessary to explain your own work and
3:27:41 other people's work in a clear and
3:27:44 constructive way
3:27:46 so in this course you'll be engaging in
3:27:48 peer review to evaluate the work of your
3:27:50 peers as well as get feedback on your
3:27:52 own work
3:27:53 if you're serious about working as a
3:27:55 designer or improving your design skills
3:27:58 consider peer review as excellent
3:28:00 practice
3:28:01 in peer review you'll be asked to
3:28:04 observe and openly reflect on what is
3:28:06 working and not working in a peer's
3:28:09 graphic design submission
3:28:11 the goal of this exercise is to help
3:28:13 your fellow designers move their work
3:28:15 forward and for you to get that same
3:28:18 advice in turn
3:28:20 additionally it's for you to practice a
3:28:22 working vocabulary and discourse around
3:28:24 making graphic design
3:28:26 all of which will help you with your
3:28:29 future path as a designer
3:28:31 the next video will give you some
3:28:33 specific tips for completing peer review
3:28:36 within the specialization
3:28:40 [Music]
3:28:43 peer review tips
3:28:45 critique and feedback are essential
3:28:46 parts of the design process
3:28:49 they're an essential way to see if your
3:28:51 is communicating what you intended to an
3:28:53 audience
3:28:54 so in this video i'm going to outline a
3:28:56 few tips for completing peer review
3:28:58 assignments successfully in this class
3:29:01 participation in peer review
3:29:02 thoughtfully and meaningfully will help
3:29:04 you practice these indispensable skills
3:29:08 submitting assignments
3:29:11 read the instructions carefully
3:29:13 make sure you take a look at the review
3:29:15 criteria so you know how your assignment
3:29:18 will be assessed
3:29:19 review any examples your instructor may
3:29:21 have provided and upload exactly what
3:29:24 you are asked to do
3:29:25 if something in the instructions isn't
3:29:27 clear post your question in the course
3:29:29 forums so staff can assist you
3:29:33 make it your best work
3:29:35 this is your creation and your
3:29:37 creativity and should be an exercise
3:29:39 that demonstrates what you can do
3:29:42 practice assignments should be
3:29:44 opportunities to fail but a final
3:29:46 assignment should be something worthy of
3:29:48 your professional portfolio
3:29:50 ask for specific feedback
3:29:53 clarify what you need from your peers in
3:29:55 your review where applicable use the
3:29:58 designated comment field to ask for
3:30:00 specific feedback on your submission
3:30:02 [Music]
3:30:04 submit on time
3:30:06 refer to the due date for submitting
3:30:08 your assignment on the grades page
3:30:09 within the course
3:30:11 if you're too early or too late the peer
3:30:14 review process may not work as intended
3:30:18 reviewing submissions
3:30:21 take your time with reviewing
3:30:23 don't rush this
3:30:24 look at each part of your peers
3:30:26 submission carefully and compare what
3:30:29 has been submitted to the expectations
3:30:31 set out in the rubric
3:30:34 be objective
3:30:35 when reviewing consider your role as a
3:30:38 viewer or reader of the work
3:30:40 focus only on what the rubric asks you
3:30:42 to evaluate and try to limit your
3:30:44 personal opinions about the work in your
3:30:47 comments
3:30:49 be clear and informative
3:30:51 generic feedback such as good or okay
3:30:54 are unhelpful comments for peer reviews
3:30:57 because they don't give a your peer any
3:30:59 specific information about what was
3:31:01 working in their submission
3:31:04 likewise it doesn't help to say it
3:31:06 doesn't work or i don't like it because
3:31:09 it doesn't give your peer enough
3:31:11 information to help them reassess their
3:31:13 designs
3:31:14 try to articulate a detailed response
3:31:17 that helps to affirm your peers choices
3:31:19 in their submission or guides your peer
3:31:22 towards the goal of an assignment if it
3:31:24 appears that they are off track
3:31:27 be constructive
3:31:29 your feedback should motivate your peer
3:31:31 to make adjustments and work towards
3:31:33 improvement
3:31:35 if there's a need to correct your peer
3:31:37 be honest but it's more helpful to
3:31:39 include specific recommendations or
3:31:42 strategies to help the learner improve
3:31:45 be generous
3:31:47 recognize that everyone comes to this
3:31:49 course with a different level of
3:31:51 experience as well as a different
3:31:53 approach to making work
3:31:55 honor and value these differences
3:31:58 bad grammar and spelling shouldn't
3:32:00 contribute to a bad grade
3:32:02 please be generous
3:32:03 likewise please don't penalize students
3:32:06 for small mistakes
3:32:09 flag plagiarism and dishonest behavior
3:32:12 these violate coursera's honor code
3:32:15 if you're asked to review an assignment
3:32:17 and it appears to be plagiarized you can
3:32:19 flag it so coursera learner support are
3:32:22 notified
3:32:25 complete your reviews on time
3:32:28 refer to the due date for completing the
3:32:30 minimum number of peer reviews on the
3:32:32 grades page within the course
3:32:34 if you're too early or too late the peer
3:32:37 review process may not work as intended
3:32:41 consider reviewing more than the minimum
3:32:43 number of peers for a given assignment
3:32:46 not only is this helpful for your fellow
3:32:48 peers but it's also instructive for you
3:32:51 to see a greater range of submissions to
3:32:54 inform your own work
3:32:55 and the more you practice critique the
3:32:58 better you will get it and ultimately
3:33:00 the better your own work will be as you
3:33:02 integrate your critical abilities into
3:33:05 your own design process
3:33:09 [Music]
3:33:13 how to apply feedback to your assignment
3:33:16 so your assignment's graded and you have
3:33:18 peer feedback
3:33:20 now what
3:33:21 remember the goal of peer review is to
3:33:23 help you improve your work as a maker
3:33:25 and viewer
3:33:27 try not to see the feedback you receive
3:33:29 as either being positive or negative or
3:33:32 an affirmation
3:33:33 that you're doing something absolutely
3:33:36 right or wrong
3:33:38 through peer review you're inviting
3:33:40 other perspectives on your work to see
3:33:42 and comment on things that you might not
3:33:44 see
3:33:45 by engaging in peer review you're
3:33:47 practicing the skill you'll need in your
3:33:49 creative and professional life
3:33:51 it's important to understand how an
3:33:53 audience will react to your work
3:33:57 peer feedback should never feel like a
3:33:59 personal attack
3:34:00 if you're discouraged by some feedback
3:34:02 then step back and consider why up here
3:34:05 would react that way to your work
3:34:08 are you seeing patterns or common themes
3:34:10 in the feedback you receive is there
3:34:11 something you need to address in the
3:34:13 work
3:34:14 if you receive peer feedback that isn't
3:34:16 constructive to your work
3:34:18 that's okay it's not ideal but don't let
3:34:21 it discourage you remember the goal of
3:34:23 feedback is to help you improve if you
3:34:26 need additional feedback you can
3:34:28 resubmit
3:34:30 at the end of the day remember both
3:34:32 positive and negative feedback can be
3:34:34 useful
3:34:35 positive feedback can let you know what
3:34:38 is working what to keep as is or alter
3:34:40 only slightly in your assignment
3:34:43 negative feedback might let you know
3:34:45 what needs to be changed developed or
3:34:48 reconsidered
3:34:50 as the designer you might disagree with
3:34:52 the feedback but it's always worth
3:34:54 examining someone else's point of view
3:34:57 an outside perspective can be very
3:34:59 useful especially because designers
3:35:01 often get too close to their own work
3:35:04 and sometimes can't see when parts of it
3:35:06 aren't working or communicating properly
3:35:10 if you receive a low grade consider why
3:35:12 your peers gave you that grade
3:35:14 have they justified their grading in the
3:35:16 comments
3:35:17 consider their perspectives and try
3:35:19 incorporating their suggestions into the
3:35:21 assignment
3:35:22 and resubmit
3:35:30 [Music]
3:35:34 in this first week we're going to look
3:35:36 at what happens at the start of the ux
3:35:38 development process
3:35:40 we'll look at ideation and how to
3:35:42 articulate and structure your ideas and
3:35:44 goals for an app in other words how to
3:35:47 clarify and communicate your ideas
3:35:50 we'll look at preliminary personal
3:35:51 research and development and how to use
3:35:54 naming and language as an important part
3:35:56 of your app's identity
3:35:58 we'll also briefly touch on audience
3:36:00 research and user-centered design but
3:36:02 for the most part it'll be you that's
3:36:04 driving the ideas and design of your app
3:36:07 this week is dedicated to the initial
3:36:10 stages of developing an app and you'll
3:36:12 be working with this same content and
3:36:14 growing this project over the next four
3:36:15 weeks so it's worth spending some time
3:36:18 coming up with a good idea
3:36:24 in this first week of ux design
3:36:26 fundamentals we're going to look at
3:36:28 ideation articulation and development
3:36:32 we're going to look at the beginning
3:36:33 stages of the ui ux process
3:36:36 in our previous class we'd focused on
3:36:38 the ui aspect of ui ux the interface
3:36:42 design now we're going to focus much
3:36:44 more on the ux part the experience and
3:36:48 we're going to divide our ux process up
3:36:51 into four chronological stages
3:36:54 and these four stages are going to offer
3:36:56 you a roadmap for how to progress from
3:36:59 ideation all the way through to a
3:37:01 finished digital prototype
3:37:03 so our first week we'll look at ideation
3:37:05 articulation and development in our
3:37:07 second week we'll take those ideas and
3:37:10 start to map them out envision them and
3:37:13 test them
3:37:14 once we've got a clearer idea of what
3:37:16 goes where in our interface and what the
3:37:18 structure of our overall app is we'll
3:37:20 move on to the third stage of creating
3:37:23 wireframes and developing our user
3:37:25 interface design and in our final week
3:37:27 we'll take either wireframes or more
3:37:29 developed interface designs and turn
3:37:31 them into a digital prototype
3:37:34 before we get started i want to do a
3:37:36 quick recap of some very general
3:37:38 information from our first course
3:37:41 that course focused on ui and in order
3:37:44 to look at it in more detail we divided
3:37:46 ui and ux
3:37:48 we described ui as how the digital
3:37:50 experience looks and ux as what that
3:37:53 experience feels like
3:37:56 so ui is based much more in visual
3:37:58 design and ux a little bit more in
3:38:00 non-visual design and we're going to
3:38:02 continue this idea of separation in
3:38:05 order to examine these disciplines and
3:38:06 get more knowledge
3:38:08 with ui we focused more on form
3:38:11 aesthetics look and feel and
3:38:13 organization more graphic design
3:38:15 principles with ux we're going to look
3:38:17 much more at the intangible aspects of
3:38:20 interface design how things feel how our
3:38:23 navigation works what our story and
3:38:25 structure is
3:38:26 ui ends up being a little bit more about
3:38:29 the surface it's design driven ux tends
3:38:32 to work more with thinking about the
3:38:34 user's engagement it tends to be more
3:38:36 driven around the activity of
3:38:38 interaction and while we'll separate ux
3:38:41 from ui as a way to get a little bit of
3:38:43 focus it's never truly separated of
3:38:45 course
3:38:46 as we keep saying they're intrinsically
3:38:49 informing each other
3:38:51 so while ui ends up being a little bit
3:38:53 more tangible and ux ends up being more
3:38:56 propositional
3:38:57 we need both of these aspects of design
3:39:00 if we want to be a good ui ux designer
3:39:03 but for now ui is going to take a back
3:39:05 seat and we're going to try and look
3:39:07 much more at the ux part of the equation
3:39:10 and since we're dealing with a practice
3:39:12 that is less tangible and more
3:39:14 propositional it makes sense that we'll
3:39:16 start out looking at ideation
3:39:19 articulation and how to develop an idea
3:39:22 all four of our weeks are connected and
3:39:24 this first week really deals with the
3:39:26 thinking phase of the ux process
3:39:29 in the projects for this course you're
3:39:31 going to be working with one idea so
3:39:34 it's worth thinking a little bit about
3:39:36 where do ideas come from
3:39:38 well a lot of the times out in the real
3:39:40 world they come from a client or
3:39:42 employer as a ui ux designer you aren't
3:39:45 always coming up with your own content
3:39:47 with your own ideas but in this case
3:39:50 you're going to act as the client as the
3:39:52 employer you're going to come up with
3:39:54 your own idea for an app and the benefit
3:39:56 of this is that it can be whatever you
3:39:59 want it to be you can really use your
3:40:01 imagination to come up with a fantastic
3:40:03 idea
3:40:04 and your idea the content and the
3:40:07 concept can be something that really
3:40:09 lends itself to visual exploration it
3:40:12 doesn't have to be something that
3:40:14 already exists or that conforms to
3:40:17 current norms you're going to have the
3:40:19 ability to shape the content of your app
3:40:22 and to shape the experience as well as
3:40:24 shaping the idea you'll really be in
3:40:26 control of everything
3:40:28 and that sounds great
3:40:30 but you've still got to come up with a
3:40:31 good idea
3:40:32 so again where do good ideas come from
3:40:35 what makes an app useful or awesome
3:40:37 well quite often good ideas come from
3:40:40 good problems and while that sounds like
3:40:42 an oxymoron all it really means is that
3:40:46 it's a real problem that the app has a
3:40:48 real reason to exist but then once
3:40:50 you've started from that you need a good
3:40:52 process you need to be able to take that
3:40:54 problem and solve it with your app in a
3:40:57 good way and that's going to mean
3:40:59 figuring out exactly how you solve the
3:41:01 problem you're going to need to be able
3:41:03 to focus on exactly what it is within
3:41:06 that problem that you can solve with an
3:41:09 app as well as exactly how you're going
3:41:11 to solve it
3:41:13 so think about a good problem being one
3:41:15 that's a real problem not an invented
3:41:18 manufactured problem that nobody really
3:41:20 needs a solution to because nobody wants
3:41:23 another app that they don't really need
3:41:25 you want a problem that's actually a
3:41:27 problem and one that can be solvable by
3:41:30 an app because not every problem can be
3:41:32 solved by an app as well sometimes it's
3:41:35 okay that there's not an app for that
3:41:37 but when you do have a real problem that
3:41:39 can be solved by an app you can tell its
3:41:42 success rate by how users respond to it
3:41:45 if you have an app that's very
3:41:47 successful users really love that app
3:41:50 they have an emotional connection to it
3:41:52 so let's say we come up with a fantastic
3:41:54 idea for an app that solves a real
3:41:57 problem it isn't really worth anything
3:41:59 unless we can use a process to develop
3:42:02 that into something more than just an
3:42:04 idea ideas are cheap and the chances are
3:42:08 that 50 other people have the same idea
3:42:10 as you for an app what's going to make
3:42:12 yours different is your process you're
3:42:14 going to need to do research in order to
3:42:17 see what's already out there and figure
3:42:19 out what the best solution could be
3:42:21 you're going to need to do testing so
3:42:23 that you can make sure that it's really
3:42:25 solving the problem for your user base
3:42:27 and you're going to need to be willing
3:42:29 to revise and change your app in its
3:42:32 form and ideas as you go through this
3:42:35 process of development
3:42:37 think of a good idea as a car that
3:42:39 you're driving good process is the road
3:42:41 map that you're going to follow to get
3:42:43 to your destination and your destination
3:42:46 is a fantastic app but you're not going
3:42:49 to get there unless you stay focused and
3:42:51 don't wander off the track so there are
3:42:53 some tools for focus that you can use
3:42:55 along the way you can clearly articulate
3:42:58 your ideas and what you want to do so
3:43:01 your goals are clear you can retain that
3:43:03 clarity of intention through every
3:43:06 aspect of your process and you can check
3:43:09 to see if your app is really doing what
3:43:11 you think it is by testing it with users
3:43:14 and getting feedback so i'm going to try
3:43:16 and give you a process and a methodology
3:43:19 over the next four weeks for you to
3:43:21 develop an app
3:43:22 but what that app is is going to be
3:43:25 entirely up to you
3:43:27 the blank screen is a blank slate and
3:43:30 you can fill it with anything that you
3:43:31 want to and there's plenty of
3:43:34 nonsensical and idiotic stuff out there
3:43:37 not to mention a lot of badly designed
3:43:39 and dysfunctional apps for the next four
3:43:42 weeks you're going to work on developing
3:43:44 an app of your own and you'll be the one
3:43:46 who's going to define what the problem
3:43:48 is as well as what your solution is
3:43:51 you'll have control over all aspects of
3:43:53 your app
3:43:55 you'll be able to define what the
3:43:56 content is as well as how your app works
3:44:00 you'll be inventing your app so this
3:44:02 project is imaginary on the one hand but
3:44:04 you'll also be treating it in a very
3:44:06 real way you'll be branding and naming
3:44:08 your app mapping out all the different
3:44:11 screens and how it works and testing
3:44:13 your app as well and at the end we'll
3:44:15 make a digital prototype of your app but
3:44:18 to start with you have to figure out
3:44:20 what it is what's the idea the blank
3:44:24 piece of paper is always terrifying when
3:44:26 you can do anything sometimes you do
3:44:29 nothing so let's try and think how you
3:44:31 can invent a concept for your app let's
3:44:33 think of a framework that gives you a
3:44:35 few constraints
3:44:37 let's start out by trying to provide a
3:44:39 simple service that users actually
3:44:42 desire so maybe you can think of
3:44:44 something that would improve your own
3:44:45 life something that you would really
3:44:47 like to have as an app
3:44:49 i might be tired of going to the gas
3:44:51 station so i'd like somebody to come to
3:44:53 my house to fill my car full of gas or i
3:44:56 might just want 24-hour ice cream
3:44:59 i might have lost a pet one time and
3:45:01 wished that i'd had a service that could
3:45:03 help me find my lost pet
3:45:05 or perhaps i want to figure out how to
3:45:07 use all the groceries left in my kitchen
3:45:09 to make a new recipe your app could
3:45:12 pretty much do anything you want it to
3:45:14 and it can have whatever attitude you
3:45:16 want it to as well it might be very
3:45:18 serious and solve a real problem or it
3:45:20 might be fun it might be fantastic for
3:45:23 an invention that doesn't even exist yet
3:45:26 we're going to use this as an exercise
3:45:28 for you to learn about ui ux so don't
3:45:30 worry about this being real but think of
3:45:33 an app that you would want in your life
3:45:35 try and make it be interesting rather
3:45:37 than real and also if you think of an
3:45:40 app that you personally would want in
3:45:43 your life that solves a problem for you
3:45:45 as an individual you're not only going
3:45:48 to be the perfect user but you're going
3:45:50 to be the expert from the start
3:45:52 but spend a little time thinking about
3:45:54 your idea because it's something that
3:45:56 we're going to use as the content
3:45:59 for this process over the next four
3:46:01 weeks so while you'll be learning lots
3:46:03 of different things in the course you're
3:46:05 going to actually apply that knowledge
3:46:08 to the vehicle of your app you don't
3:46:10 have to decide until the end of this
3:46:12 week but start to think about it now
3:46:16 so we're starting out at the very
3:46:18 beginning of our project let's talk
3:46:20 about ideas and goals
3:46:23 you've been asked to come up with an
3:46:24 idea for an app that solves a problem
3:46:26 for you and this initial idea is really
3:46:30 up to you it can be whatever you're
3:46:31 interested in i can't give you that
3:46:34 initial idea but what i can do is give
3:46:36 you a way to test whether your app idea
3:46:39 is really going to work and it's okay if
3:46:41 a similar app already exists for your
3:46:43 idea because you're going to deal with
3:46:45 it in a different way
3:46:47 so let's say my initial idea for my app
3:46:50 is one that gives me recipes for food
3:46:52 that i have already in my kitchen
3:46:55 like all beginning ideas it's very broad
3:46:58 and it could go in many different
3:46:59 directions so first of all try and be as
3:47:02 specific as you can try and articulate
3:47:05 your idea as clearly as you can
3:47:08 so on the one hand you want to really
3:47:11 expand your idea you want to try and
3:47:14 explore all the different possibilities
3:47:16 that it could have but you also want to
3:47:18 look at those possibilities and decide
3:47:20 which ones you're going to use which
3:47:23 ones are really relevant
3:47:25 these two activities seem like they're
3:47:27 almost in opposition to each other but
3:47:29 when you put them together you have a
3:47:31 process and that process helps you
3:47:33 develop your idea
3:47:34 it's a simple strategy in a lot of ways
3:47:37 it's figuring out what questions to ask
3:47:40 and then answering those questions let's
3:47:42 look at my leftover food app and see
3:47:44 what kind of questions we might want to
3:47:46 ask about that
3:47:48 my starting questions could be quite
3:47:50 broad and gradually get more and more
3:47:52 focused as i define what my app really
3:47:55 is so i could start out by asking what
3:47:57 does it do and how does it work
3:48:00 quite simple and broad questions
3:48:03 i could also think about the audience
3:48:05 who's going to benefit from my app
3:48:07 and why would somebody use this in the
3:48:09 first place
3:48:11 so while these questions are broad i'm
3:48:13 going to have to come up with more
3:48:14 specific answers and those specific
3:48:17 answers are going to help me define more
3:48:19 specifically what my app is
3:48:22 and this is going to make it be
3:48:24 different from all the other apps that
3:48:25 do the same thing
3:48:27 i'm going to take it in a slightly
3:48:29 different direction
3:48:31 so let's first of all ask what does my
3:48:33 app do
3:48:34 well it creates recipes that use only
3:48:37 existing food from your kitchen
3:48:39 but it could do other things too so
3:48:41 let's explore some of those it could
3:48:43 suggest additional food to purchase for
3:48:45 recipes it could also keep track of my
3:48:48 tastes so that it could customize
3:48:50 recipes for me it could store and share
3:48:53 my recipes and maybe let me see the
3:48:55 recipes of others
3:48:57 so if your app is going to do more than
3:48:59 one thing it's useful to create a
3:49:01 hierarchy of the function of your app
3:49:04 so which of these things is the most
3:49:06 important i would say that the primary
3:49:09 goal is the first one that i came up
3:49:10 with to create recipes using existing
3:49:13 food
3:49:14 but perhaps i might need just one more
3:49:17 ingredient or two to make a really great
3:49:19 recipe
3:49:20 so i'd have to go to the store and get
3:49:22 those so suggesting additional food to
3:49:24 purchase for those recipes would
3:49:27 probably be my second most important
3:49:29 idea i think the storing and sharing of
3:49:31 recipes is also pretty important it
3:49:34 would help create a community and it
3:49:36 would also make me use the app more than
3:49:38 one time
3:49:40 and finally tracking my tastes and
3:49:42 customizing recipes just for me
3:49:44 would probably be the lowest thing in my
3:49:47 hierarchy of functionality
3:49:50 so let's take the four things that our
3:49:52 app is going to do
3:49:53 and organize them to visually represent
3:49:56 their importance
3:49:58 so we've got a hierarchy of
3:49:59 functionality and it's really important
3:50:02 that we remember this and stick to it we
3:50:04 don't want to get sidetracked and make
3:50:06 something minor be the focus of our app
3:50:09 our hierarchy of function is also a
3:50:12 hierarchy of ideas
3:50:14 we're going to spend a little more time
3:50:16 articulating that big idea
3:50:18 but all of our other ideas
3:50:20 won't go away they're just hiding behind
3:50:23 the big idea they're still there and
3:50:25 they're going to provide a supporting
3:50:27 role but they're a little less important
3:50:30 than our main idea so let's spend some
3:50:32 time focusing on that first
3:50:35 if we're clear on that big idea it's
3:50:37 easier to convey to somebody else what
3:50:40 our app is really all about and one of
3:50:43 the ways to do this is to come up with a
3:50:45 really tight and accurate one line
3:50:47 description
3:50:49 you could use the prefix myapp dot dot
3:50:52 dot and then think about how to fill the
3:50:55 rest of that sentence in so you could
3:50:57 think about how you use language and
3:50:59 tone to describe your app but also
3:51:02 just the words themselves having no
3:51:05 wasteful or extraneous language there
3:51:08 think about the specifics of each word
3:51:10 so my app now suggests delicious recipes
3:51:14 it doesn't create the recipes it
3:51:16 suggests them and notice that the
3:51:18 recipes are now delicious they're not
3:51:20 regular recipes anymore let's go back to
3:51:23 the four big questions that i asked you
3:51:25 earlier about your app
3:51:27 we're going to turn each of these into a
3:51:29 class to try and help you to answer
3:51:31 these questions
3:51:32 so our first question what does it do
3:51:35 we've already looked at in this class
3:51:37 and we've answered that by using a
3:51:40 strategy that involves ideation and
3:51:42 articulation we've now got a clear
3:51:45 one-line description of our app and a
3:51:48 bunch of supporting ideas to go along
3:51:50 with that
3:51:51 our next question is going to be how
3:51:53 does it work
3:51:54 and i don't mean how does it work
3:51:56 technically i mean how does the app
3:51:58 achieve its goal and the goal should be
3:52:00 clear in your initial statement in my
3:52:03 case it's about suggesting delicious
3:52:05 recipes and using up ingredients that
3:52:08 are already in your kitchen so i'm going
3:52:10 to need to do some research into my
3:52:12 content and into my competition i want
3:52:14 to know if something similar exists and
3:52:17 if it does i want to solve that problem
3:52:19 in a better or more engaging way we're
3:52:21 going to look more closely at who
3:52:23 benefits from our app not just the
3:52:26 audience but perhaps the client as well
3:52:29 why are we even making this app in the
3:52:31 first place is it for fun is it for
3:52:33 social good or is it to make money and
3:52:36 lastly we're going to examine a little
3:52:38 more deeply why would someone use our
3:52:40 app in the first place we're going to
3:52:42 figure out our goals and our outcomes
3:52:45 what we want the user to have achieved
3:52:47 in going through our app and we're going
3:52:49 to make sure that those goals and
3:52:51 outcomes are achieved because if they're
3:52:53 not we're going to have to go back and
3:52:55 re-evaluate all of the other questions
3:52:57 we've asked ourselves to be clear we're
3:53:00 engaged in a process here and it's not
3:53:03 always a linear one quite often it's
3:53:05 going to involve some back and forth and
3:53:08 even some modification to your ideas as
3:53:10 you develop them and refine them
3:53:13 in this section we're going to look at
3:53:14 research and development of our idea
3:53:18 we've got four main questions about our
3:53:20 idea that we're trying to answer and
3:53:23 we've got four areas that we're going to
3:53:25 investigate in order to answer those
3:53:27 questions
3:53:29 so let's look at what kind of research
3:53:31 we should do and let's look at how we
3:53:33 might develop our idea a little bit
3:53:35 further
3:53:36 chances are that if you have a good idea
3:53:38 for an app several other people have
3:53:40 also had that same good idea so one of
3:53:43 the first things that you can do is
3:53:45 check out the competition
3:53:46 it might help you to categorize what
3:53:49 other kinds of apps you're going to look
3:53:51 at
3:53:52 the most obvious place to start is apps
3:53:55 that do exactly the same thing as the
3:53:57 idea that you have
3:53:59 there are millions of apps out there so
3:54:01 the chances are that there is something
3:54:03 doing the same thing as your app but it
3:54:05 might not be very visible
3:54:07 even if there's not an app doing exactly
3:54:09 the same thing i'm willing to bet that
3:54:11 there are plenty of apps that do
3:54:12 something similar
3:54:14 and these might contain important
3:54:16 information about what to do or what not
3:54:19 to do with your app
3:54:21 these first two categories are very
3:54:23 strongly related to your idea but let's
3:54:26 see what happens when you move your
3:54:28 circle of search out a little bit
3:54:30 further you could look at what kind of
3:54:32 apps are adjacent to yours what apps
3:54:35 might have the same broader subject
3:54:37 matter but not have the same specific
3:54:40 subject as yours
3:54:42 and then you could also look at apps
3:54:43 that are totally unrelated to your
3:54:46 content and your idea
3:54:48 these could be very interesting
3:54:50 structural technical or even aesthetic
3:54:53 models that you might be interested in
3:54:55 influencing your design by looking in
3:54:57 all these areas you'll get obvious
3:55:00 predictable research but also tangential
3:55:03 unexpected research let's take my
3:55:05 leftover food app and look at some apps
3:55:08 that do exactly the same thing
3:55:10 let's say that i find three examples
3:55:13 from each of these categories i can
3:55:15 search in the app store but i can also
3:55:17 go online and see how many people are
3:55:20 using these apps and what their feedback
3:55:22 is
3:55:23 it's quite easy for me to gather visual
3:55:25 research as well i can take screenshots
3:55:27 to have a record of the designs but i
3:55:29 also need to download all of the apps
3:55:31 and go through them and actually
3:55:33 experience them so what other apps might
3:55:35 do something similar to mine i can find
3:55:38 a lot of apps to do with recipes and
3:55:40 cooking and ingredients and some of
3:55:43 these might have some smaller section
3:55:45 that deals with using leftover
3:55:47 ingredients
3:55:49 but their main focus isn't going to be
3:55:51 exactly the same as my app
3:55:53 but all of this research is still very
3:55:56 useful and that might be to do with the
3:55:58 structure it might be to do with the
3:55:59 design
3:56:00 i could be looking at these sites and
3:56:02 noting similarities that i might want to
3:56:04 repeat myself or that i might want to
3:56:07 avoid and do something totally different
3:56:10 if i broaden my circle of investigation
3:56:13 the next stage that i'm going to look at
3:56:15 are apps that are content adjacent in my
3:56:18 case this is going to be apps that might
3:56:21 deal with food or something to do with
3:56:23 food but don't really deal with recipes
3:56:26 or ingredients necessarily they might
3:56:28 deal with buying or delivering food or
3:56:31 they might have a more conceptual
3:56:33 connection
3:56:34 for instance food for all has a social
3:56:37 agenda that i could see as having a
3:56:39 connection to the agenda of my app
3:56:42 whereas yelp might be a much more useful
3:56:44 model for thinking about how a food
3:56:47 community is built online
3:56:49 finally i could look at unrelated apps
3:56:52 apps not related to my content but that
3:56:54 might inform my idea or my structure in
3:56:57 some other way
3:56:59 lifehacks for instance seems to have a
3:57:01 diy resource using philosophy that
3:57:04 matches mine or i could find some design
3:57:07 or conceptual ideas that are useful
3:57:10 in sites that deal with reusing or
3:57:12 repurposing things that seem to be trash
3:57:15 but really aren't
3:57:17 i like having these broader influences
3:57:20 because it stops your research becoming
3:57:22 too predictable
3:57:23 so we've looked at a range of different
3:57:25 connected apps ones that are very
3:57:28 specific and relate to our idea and our
3:57:30 content and ones that are further away
3:57:33 from our idea and our content
3:57:36 so now let's try and use some of that
3:57:37 research and figure out how we might
3:57:39 develop our idea a little bit further
3:57:43 i'm going to talk about this process in
3:57:45 very broad terms the process of
3:57:48 developing your idea
3:57:49 we're going to do it without really
3:57:51 talking about content so maybe you can
3:57:54 think about your idea and put it into
3:57:57 this example think about your idea as
3:58:00 being amorphous think about it as being
3:58:03 a blob or an amoeba that can change its
3:58:06 shape
3:58:07 your idea shouldn't be rigid it can be
3:58:09 manifested in a lot of different forms
3:58:12 and a lot of different structures
3:58:14 it's still your idea but the shape of
3:58:17 your idea can be very very different in
3:58:20 other words how someone sees and
3:58:22 experiences your idea one way to decide
3:58:25 on what shape your idea should have is
3:58:28 to ask yourself a series of what if
3:58:30 questions
3:58:32 what if my app was entirely image based
3:58:35 what if my app connected to a food
3:58:37 delivery service what if my app allowed
3:58:39 people to donate food to charity
3:58:42 you can always say no to any of these
3:58:44 ideas that you come up with but what's
3:58:47 important is that you test out your idea
3:58:50 and you come up with a bunch of
3:58:51 different options ideas are pretty much
3:58:54 free they just take your time and a
3:58:57 pencil and a piece of paper so it's
3:58:59 worth mapping out all the possibilities
3:59:02 all the different ways that your idea
3:59:04 could be manifested and it's worth doing
3:59:06 that now to find out what the right
3:59:08 direction is because right now your
3:59:11 ideas are free but your idea can take
3:59:13 whatever shape you wanted to
3:59:15 as we just saw there's many different
3:59:18 shapes the same idea could have by
3:59:20 thinking like this you're exploring your
3:59:22 options and you're trying to discover
3:59:25 what is special about your app and what
3:59:27 is original about your app
3:59:29 i always find a good way to do that is
3:59:32 to empty your brain
3:59:34 to map out all of the possibilities that
3:59:36 you can think of even the ones that you
3:59:39 don't think are great
3:59:40 and i do that by listing everything that
3:59:42 my app could do
3:59:44 i like to do that on a giant piece of
3:59:46 paper so that i can see all my thoughts
3:59:49 on the paper and react to them it also
3:59:52 means i can come back to them and decide
3:59:54 whether they're really good ideas or bad
3:59:56 ideas and if i can't decide that for
3:59:59 myself
4:00:00 i can very easily test the waters for my
4:00:03 ideas by talking to somebody else by
4:00:05 bouncing my ideas off another person
4:00:08 in a way even at this beginning stage
4:00:11 you're getting user feedback
4:00:13 it's the same strategy we've already
4:00:15 seen of being broad and being focused at
4:00:18 the same time
4:00:20 the broadness comes from you extending
4:00:22 the parameters of your thought
4:00:24 trying to open your mind to ideas that
4:00:27 you might not initially have thought of
4:00:29 and trying to widen your scope of what
4:00:32 you thought was a good solution to the
4:00:34 problem
4:00:35 because once you've laid out all the
4:00:37 possibilities every single way that your
4:00:40 app could be represented and everything
4:00:43 that it could do
4:00:44 then you can examine all of that
4:00:46 material all of those ideas and decide
4:00:49 precisely
4:00:50 which of those ideas is the best which
4:00:53 of those you're going to pursue and in
4:00:55 what kind of order or hierarchy you're
4:00:57 going to engage with those ideas
4:01:01 so we've started to develop and also
4:01:04 focus our idea
4:01:06 even at this early stage it's worth us
4:01:08 thinking about our audience and our
4:01:10 rationale for our app because that might
4:01:13 have some overarching consequences for
4:01:15 what our app does and how our app works
4:01:18 so all four of our questions have an
4:01:20 effect on one another and right now
4:01:22 we're going to focus on audience and
4:01:24 rationale we're going to ask who
4:01:26 benefits from my app in order to figure
4:01:29 that out we have to figure out who's the
4:01:32 audience for my app and it's no good
4:01:34 saying everybody even if everybody does
4:01:37 end up using your app it's going to
4:01:39 start out from an initial hardcore user
4:01:42 group so it's worth defining what your
4:01:44 target audience or your target user
4:01:46 group is so i'm going to take a stab at
4:01:49 describing some of the traits i think my
4:01:51 users might have
4:01:53 i think they'd definitely be people who
4:01:55 wanted to waste less food there could be
4:01:57 a few reasons for this but i think the
4:02:00 most important reason is probably going
4:02:01 to be that they're also people who want
4:02:03 to save money there's an unexpected and
4:02:06 creative aspect to making these new
4:02:08 recipes so i think my user base could
4:02:10 also be people that are inventive and if
4:02:13 i think about some of the functions in
4:02:14 my app my audience might also be people
4:02:17 who want to shop accurately for just the
4:02:19 things they need again we're going to go
4:02:22 from the general to the more specific so
4:02:24 if i look at people who want to waste
4:02:26 less i could describe them in more
4:02:28 detailed terms as being eco-conscious
4:02:31 being into recycling perhaps being
4:02:34 proactive in their lifestyle the people
4:02:37 who want to save money could be
4:02:38 described as being thrifty perhaps
4:02:40 they're on a budget or perhaps they're
4:02:42 living in a communal or shared living
4:02:44 situation for the inventive or creative
4:02:47 part i could describe my target users as
4:02:50 being people who like to cook they
4:02:52 probably have a wide taste and they
4:02:54 might be quite adventurous since they
4:02:56 don't know what they're going to get and
4:02:57 the people who want to shop accurately
4:02:59 for missing ingredients could be wanting
4:03:02 to do that because they want to save
4:03:03 space in their kitchen or save time
4:03:06 going to the supermarket or more likely
4:03:08 save money so now i've got four much
4:03:11 more detailed areas that describe some
4:03:14 of my users and i could divide these
4:03:17 into categories or if you like
4:03:20 interpersonality traits to try and
4:03:22 define the user even more closely
4:03:25 so the first set feel like they're much
4:03:28 more political considerations whereas
4:03:30 the second set feels like they're much
4:03:32 more financial they're all to do with
4:03:34 budget the third set could loosely be
4:03:37 described as being social to do with the
4:03:39 activities around cooking and eating
4:03:41 whereas the fourth set feels very
4:03:44 pragmatic it feels like it's dealing
4:03:46 with issues that are very functional
4:03:48 so we've got a little bit more specific
4:03:50 in terms of our descriptions and we've
4:03:52 also started to categorize how those
4:03:54 descriptions work we could take that a
4:03:57 stage further and we could think about
4:03:59 how we could use these directions and
4:04:01 these traits to actually invent some
4:04:04 mythical users we could invent profiles
4:04:08 of what we think typical or ideal users
4:04:11 would be and much as our one line
4:04:13 description summed up accurately our app
4:04:16 these one-line user profiles might
4:04:19 accurately describe who's using our app
4:04:22 they can be useful in terms of
4:04:24 explaining our ideas to other people but
4:04:26 they can also be really useful in terms
4:04:28 of role playing especially when it comes
4:04:31 to testing if we have a specific
4:04:33 description of somebody using our app it
4:04:36 somehow makes it feel more real
4:04:38 it's useful to have this one person
4:04:41 stand in for a whole section of users
4:04:44 we're creating an archetype or
4:04:46 stereotype that everybody can understand
4:04:49 so let's invent a user profile for my
4:04:51 app my first user is called james and
4:04:54 he's from san diego i'm going to make
4:04:56 him be young 22 years old and i'm going
4:04:59 to say that he's a student as well let's
4:05:01 say he's studying economics
4:05:04 he lives in a shared house with some of
4:05:06 his other fellow students he's pretty
4:05:08 active he likes hiking and he's really
4:05:11 into crossfit and his active lifestyle
4:05:13 is reflected in that he's a vegetarian
4:05:16 so why is james a good user for my app
4:05:19 well he fits many of my categories he
4:05:21 lives in a shared house he's 22 years
4:05:24 old and a student so he probably doesn't
4:05:25 have much money he's got a health
4:05:27 conscious lifestyle as well he's a
4:05:30 vegetarian and an economic student so i
4:05:32 think some of our politics might line up
4:05:34 too he's a good match for all four of
4:05:37 our categories let's invent another
4:05:40 sample user who also fits into our four
4:05:42 categories but is very different from
4:05:45 james here's tracy from new york she's a
4:05:48 35 year old mother she's a freelance
4:05:51 editor and she lives in a third floor
4:05:53 apartment
4:05:54 her hobbies include cycling and fencing
4:05:58 and when she has free time she likes to
4:06:00 host dinner parties she might seem very
4:06:03 different from james but there's also
4:06:05 some similarities
4:06:06 they're both quite active
4:06:08 they're both quite social and they both
4:06:11 might be on a budget as well i can
4:06:13 imagine them both using my app but in
4:06:16 very different ways
4:06:18 their emphasis within my four categories
4:06:20 isn't going to be the same
4:06:22 let's try and use this same structure
4:06:25 that helped us define our user target
4:06:28 group
4:06:29 and think about how it might help define
4:06:31 the rationale behind why someone would
4:06:33 use my app
4:06:35 our larger descriptions of the
4:06:37 personality traits of our user will
4:06:39 probably stay the same and so will our
4:06:42 categories political financial social
4:06:45 and functional but let's look at
4:06:46 rationale or motivation in our user
4:06:49 their political motivation might be to
4:06:51 make a difference to be green
4:06:54 whereas the financial motivation would
