4:06:57 be as simple as saving money to be
4:06:59 thrifty
4:07:00 the social motivation might be a little
4:07:03 bit more tricky it might be about being
4:07:05 creative but it might also be about
4:07:07 community
4:07:08 discovering and sharing recipes the
4:07:10 functional motivation is relatively easy
4:07:13 to save time and to save space
4:07:16 by following this process we've managed
4:07:18 to obtain a much more detailed
4:07:21 description of our audience and of our
4:07:23 rationale we've been inventing our users
4:07:26 but we've also been inventing our users
4:07:28 concerns and if we just stick with their
4:07:31 interests we're going to design
4:07:33 something that is entirely user centric
4:07:36 in its design
4:07:38 and that's just one part of the equation
4:07:40 that we have to consider as ui ux
4:07:43 designers
4:07:44 we also have to consider our own
4:07:46 knowledge as designers and how we're
4:07:49 going to create design centered design
4:07:52 but there's a third aspect that we don't
4:07:53 always think about and that's about the
4:07:56 client
4:07:57 they're also going to have a set of
4:07:59 goals and a set of intentions that we're
4:08:02 going to have to figure out how to deal
4:08:04 with
4:08:05 in this section we're going to look at
4:08:07 some of the goals and outcomes that we
4:08:09 want to have for our app
4:08:11 we've asked our four main questions and
4:08:14 now we're looking at why would someone
4:08:16 use my app
4:08:18 in the last section we looked a lot at
4:08:20 our app through the perspective of the
4:08:22 user and tried to define what the user's
4:08:25 role was but now we're going to turn it
4:08:27 on its head and instead of asking why do
4:08:30 i want people to use my app we're going
4:08:32 to ask what are my goals my goals as the
4:08:36 client and as the designer
4:08:38 so we're changing focus from the people
4:08:40 that are using the app to the people
4:08:42 that are making the app
4:08:44 and sometimes those sets of goals align
4:08:47 so let's look at my goals for making the
4:08:49 app first of all i'd really like to
4:08:52 reduce food wastage in the world i feel
4:08:54 like that's pretty important as a social
4:08:56 agenda but i'd also quite like to make
4:08:58 some money out of my app so i'm not
4:09:00 doing it entirely for free
4:09:02 it would be great if i could build a
4:09:04 community that was interested in the
4:09:06 same issues as i am we could share
4:09:08 knowledge and information and all
4:09:10 contribute to reducing food wastage
4:09:12 together and in general i'd like to
4:09:15 provide a useful app something that
4:09:17 people thought really had value in their
4:09:19 lives
4:09:20 i could take all of my concerns and put
4:09:23 them into the same categories that we've
4:09:25 already looked at
4:09:27 my goals as the maker aren't that
4:09:29 different from the user's goals they
4:09:31 still fit into political financial
4:09:34 social and functional categories
4:09:37 and all of my goals are interconnected
4:09:39 and interdependent upon each other
4:09:42 if my app ends up being popular then it
4:09:45 could really reduce food wastage at a
4:09:47 really high level and the more
4:09:49 successful it is and the more people
4:09:51 involved the more my chances are of
4:09:53 making money
4:09:54 and i could use that money to funnel
4:09:56 back into reducing food wastage as my
4:09:59 app grows the better chance i have of
4:10:01 building a community around it and with
4:10:03 the expansion of that community i could
4:10:05 provide a much more useful app and i
4:10:07 could reduce food wastage even further
4:10:10 so my goals are all interconnected to
4:10:12 each other and all interdependent and i
4:10:15 might view them as all being fairly even
4:10:18 fairly proportionate in terms of what
4:10:20 i'm interested in achieving
4:10:22 but that's because i'm acting as the
4:10:24 client here when a lot of the time i
4:10:27 wouldn't get to choose my client i'd
4:10:29 just be the designer so what if my
4:10:31 client was a large and greedy bank
4:10:34 what kind of goals might that bank have
4:10:36 in these four categories
4:10:38 let's make some up just for fun
4:10:41 politically i might want to promote home
4:10:42 ownership financially i'd want to make
4:10:45 money socially i might choose to build a
4:10:48 community and functionally the same
4:10:50 reason i'd want a useful app
4:10:53 and that doesn't sound too bad my bank
4:10:55 doesn't sound too greedy at all
4:10:57 wait a minute though most of the bank's
4:10:59 focus is going to be on making money 80
4:11:02 percent it's quite interested in
4:11:04 promoting home ownership still because
4:11:06 it makes money from that it's not that
4:11:08 interested in providing a useful app and
4:11:11 it might have no interest at all in
4:11:13 building a community because there's no
4:11:15 financial gain for the bank in that and
4:11:18 this is an extreme and unrealistic model
4:11:21 our digital world doesn't work like that
4:11:23 because everything is connected each of
4:11:25 these aspects has an effect on the other
4:11:28 one so even if the main focus of a
4:11:30 greedy bank is making money it might
4:11:33 only put 40 percent of its energy there
4:11:36 if you ignore the social and functional
4:11:38 aspects of your app you're going to be
4:11:40 in trouble you need to actually spread
4:11:43 everything around a little bit more
4:11:45 evenly let's see what happens at the
4:11:47 other end of the spectrum
4:11:49 instead of a large and greedy bank let's
4:11:51 see what would happen if we were making
4:11:53 an app for a small and rather nice
4:11:55 charity
4:11:56 in the broadest sense some of the goals
4:11:59 of the small and nice charity might be
4:12:01 the same as the large and greedy bank
4:12:03 and some be different
4:12:05 so financially for instance the charity
4:12:07 still needs to make money it still wants
4:12:09 to build community and provide a useful
4:12:12 app the biggest difference might be its
4:12:14 goal politically in this case to feed
4:12:17 the homeless and it might be less
4:12:19 concerned with providing a useful app
4:12:21 and more concerned with providing an
4:12:23 engaging app for its audience
4:12:25 if we think about where the emphasis of
4:12:27 the charity might be it might be much
4:12:29 more towards its goal its political goal
4:12:32 of feeding the homeless it would
4:12:34 probably have quite a high interest in
4:12:36 building a community but maybe less
4:12:38 interest in making money because it's
4:12:40 small it might not need much money to
4:12:42 keep going and it might choose to not
4:12:44 put much emphasis on creating an
4:12:46 engaging app
4:12:48 but just like our previous example
4:12:50 everything is interrelated here are
4:12:52 political financial social and
4:12:55 functional aspects of the app
4:12:57 because everything's interrelated the
4:12:59 app might actually want to make lots of
4:13:02 money so that the money can be used to
4:13:04 feed the homeless and if the app builds
4:13:07 a larger community it'll probably make
4:13:09 more money which again will let it feed
4:13:11 more people
4:13:13 you could even make an argument that
4:13:15 everything is so interrelated that the
4:13:17 percentages of the focus of the app
4:13:20 should all be even
4:13:22 if the app wasn't engaging it would
4:13:24 dramatically adversely affect the other
4:13:26 areas for instance so in some cases you
4:13:30 might end up with everything being
4:13:32 divided pretty equally
4:13:34 but it's going to be different from app
4:13:36 to app from content and subject to
4:13:39 content and subject
4:13:40 so you're going to have to figure out
4:13:42 where the emphasis of your app lies
4:13:45 because that's going to have a lot of
4:13:47 knock-on effects in terms of how your
4:13:49 content is structured and how you want
4:13:51 to shape the user's experience as they
4:13:54 travel through your app
4:13:55 it's important to remember that all of
4:13:57 the goals and outcomes in your app are
4:14:00 all tied to each other and all affect
4:14:02 each other
4:14:04 so far i've come up with an idea for my
4:14:07 app i've developed that idea and i've
4:14:09 thought about an audience and now i've
4:14:11 got to give it a name
4:14:12 in my visual elements of user interface
4:14:15 class i tried to map out many of the
4:14:17 areas where uiux takes place
4:14:20 the naming and identity part of the
4:14:22 process is highly specialized in our
4:14:25 diagram it lives over to the left hand
4:14:27 side where we're developing content
4:14:29 ideas and thinking about marketing
4:14:32 and while we might call it branding and
4:14:34 identity we're really looking at
4:14:36 something more specific here because
4:14:38 we're just looking at how to use
4:14:39 language how to use words
4:14:42 so we're not branding in terms of a
4:14:44 visual identity but we're branding with
4:14:46 language
4:14:48 so let's look at three different places
4:14:49 where we can use words as a branding
4:14:51 tool
4:14:52 the most obvious is in the name of our
4:14:54 app we're going to have to name our app
4:14:57 something
4:14:58 but we might also need a tagline and a
4:15:00 mission statement so let's look at how
4:15:03 these three things differ
4:15:05 your name is the smallest thing that
4:15:07 you're going to have to deal with it's
4:15:08 normally only a word or two your tagline
4:15:11 is often short sentence whereas your
4:15:13 mission statement can be a few lines or
4:15:15 a paragraph
4:15:17 as well as being different in scale
4:15:19 these three pieces of language are also
4:15:22 different in terms of their goals
4:15:24 you want your name to be memorable
4:15:26 original appropriate to have the right
4:15:29 kind of tone or mood and have the right
4:15:31 kind of sound to it apart from a logo
4:15:34 your name is going to be the most
4:15:35 succinct thing that's going to brand
4:15:37 your app and you're going to need it if
4:15:39 you're going to make a logo type as well
4:15:42 while you want your name to be memorable
4:15:44 and original most importantly it's got
4:15:47 to be a good fit for your product
4:15:50 your tagline is an expansion of that
4:15:52 where you want it to be catchy succinct
4:15:55 to be a summary of what your app does
4:15:57 but like your name you want it to have
4:15:59 the right kind of tone or mood
4:16:02 it offers you more language to work with
4:16:04 than just your name a sound byte rather
4:16:07 than just a single word or two
4:16:09 your tagline could be a clever
4:16:11 description of what you do or a phrase
4:16:13 that sums up your philosophy
4:16:15 your mission statement is longer still a
4:16:18 few lines or a paragraph and it gives
4:16:20 you a chance to get much more
4:16:22 descriptive about what your app does
4:16:24 you could talk about the ambitions or
4:16:26 the achievements of your app
4:16:28 but like the other two items we're
4:16:30 looking at you still need to have the
4:16:32 right tone and the right mood and that's
4:16:35 what all three of these things have in
4:16:36 common
4:16:37 they need to work as a set they need to
4:16:40 all be related to each other not just in
4:16:42 terms of their content but in terms of
4:16:45 their philosophy and how they use
4:16:46 language again tone is very important
4:16:50 we can take our familiar strategy of
4:16:53 simultaneous focus and expansion we
4:16:56 could make a mind map to get all the
4:16:58 language out of our head but then we'd
4:17:00 need to pick out what are the relevant
4:17:02 key words from all of those things that
4:17:04 we've got in front of us
4:17:06 like everything else it's part of a
4:17:08 process in general the language that we
4:17:12 use for our naming might be much more
4:17:14 creative and much more connotative
4:17:17 whereas the language we use for our
4:17:19 mission might be much more factual much
4:17:22 more descriptive
4:17:23 this kind of process and using language
4:17:25 in this way is very common in graphic
4:17:28 design particularly for branding and
4:17:30 identity projects
4:17:31 here's an example from my graphic design
4:17:34 course on coursera where you had to
4:17:36 invent a company and create an identity
4:17:39 for it i invented a startup company that
4:17:41 had a food truck selling snails
4:17:44 i made up a whole backstory but to get
4:17:47 to grips with the language that i might
4:17:48 use in my naming process i made a mind
4:17:51 map that explored all of the possible
4:17:53 areas connected to my backstory i looked
4:17:56 at french culture and french cuisine i
4:17:59 looked at the 1968 paris student
4:18:02 protests
4:18:03 i looked at french automobile design and
4:18:06 french architecture
4:18:08 from this web of words i came up with
4:18:11 three adjectives that i thought would
4:18:13 describe my company
4:18:15 having a clear set of criteria that you
4:18:17 want to communicate can be really
4:18:20 helpful when you're making an identity
4:18:22 design or when you're naming something
4:18:25 if your adjectives are successful and
4:18:27 accurate then you can use them as a
4:18:29 sounding board to test any name that you
4:18:31 come up with against them but you need
4:18:33 to have a name first in order to test it
4:18:36 so let's look at some strategies for
4:18:37 naming
4:18:38 we've already decided that naming our
4:18:41 app is much more of a creative process
4:18:43 than writing our mission statement which
4:18:45 is more descriptive
4:18:47 so it makes sense that we would take
4:18:49 slightly different strategies for both
4:18:51 of these
4:18:52 our naming might take strategies like
4:18:54 using mythology thinking what a
4:18:56 contemporary way of using languages
4:18:58 perhaps something comes from science or
4:19:01 history or fiction whereas our mission
4:19:03 statement would be much more concise
4:19:06 much more descriptive and pragmatic
4:19:09 so let's go through these naming
4:19:10 strategies one by one and look at some
4:19:13 real world solutions
4:19:15 we're going to use both app names and
4:19:17 company names as our examples
4:19:20 if i asked you to think about a branding
4:19:22 example that uses mythology for its name
4:19:24 i wonder what you'd come up with
4:19:26 i'm pretty sure you'd come up with the
4:19:28 same example as me
4:19:30 nike the greek goddess of victory and
4:19:33 well-known oregon shoemaker there's also
4:19:36 pandora who received many gifts from the
4:19:39 gods including the gift of music
4:19:41 although there's also the negative
4:19:42 connotation of the evils stored in
4:19:45 pandora's box
4:19:47 you could look at contemporary language
4:19:49 and contemporary phrases and think about
4:19:51 how to combine those you could use slang
4:19:54 misspellings and colloquialisms to
4:19:57 create a new word that has some
4:19:59 contemporary cultural meaning
4:20:02 and that might make it young and fun or
4:20:04 you could take a more scientific
4:20:05 approach and have something that's more
4:20:08 mathematical or more serious google
4:20:11 manages both by being a misspelling of
4:20:13 google you could take pieces of the
4:20:16 language like microsoft who are
4:20:18 developing software for microprocessors
4:20:21 although as technology advances your
4:20:24 name might get left behind you could
4:20:26 also take a historical approach to
4:20:28 naming is apple computers a homage to
4:20:32 apple records or does it relate to isaac
4:20:34 newton's revelations about gravity
4:20:37 or most likely that it was just the fact
4:20:39 that steve jobs visited an apple orchard
4:20:41 historically speaking bands once
4:20:43 rehearsed in garages it's funny that the
4:20:46 digital software they use to make music
4:20:48 now is on a computer and not in the
4:20:50 garage anymore
4:20:52 or your name could be totally fictional
4:20:55 totally invented what does spotify
4:20:57 actually mean
4:20:59 the connotative meaning of words and the
4:21:02 sound of them the poetics of the actual
4:21:04 language can be very powerful and if you
4:21:07 make your name up it doesn't come with
4:21:09 any baggage
4:21:11 there are lots and lots of different
4:21:12 approaches you can take to naming
4:21:15 you could think about how to combine a
4:21:17 couple of these together or just use
4:21:19 them singly we could look at
4:21:22 combinations of different words we could
4:21:24 look at very descriptive or didactic
4:21:26 strategies names that are nonsense or
4:21:29 names that just have a really great
4:21:31 sound to them combining two shorter
4:21:33 words into a longer one is a very common
4:21:36 strategy it allows you to reference two
4:21:38 known and stable things and put them
4:21:40 together to suggest the third thing
4:21:43 which is precisely what your app or
4:21:45 company does
4:21:46 so we have grub hub
4:21:48 net netflix and of course face book
4:21:52 better known as grubhub netflix and
4:21:55 facebook
4:21:56 but sometimes two words might already
4:21:58 exist as a pairing together and have a
4:22:01 more didactic meaning
4:22:03 so while opentable for example works
4:22:06 with our combination strategy it's also
4:22:08 a very didactic expression of what that
4:22:11 app does
4:22:13 but a didactic name is often not the
4:22:15 most exciting one sometimes we go in the
4:22:18 opposite direction and try and think of
4:22:20 a nonsense name or invent a word out of
4:22:22 nowhere
4:22:24 or we could think much more about how a
4:22:26 word sounds in relationship to what it
4:22:29 represents
4:22:31 to me twitter actually sounds like the
4:22:33 noise made by a million people sending
4:22:35 short messages in texts
4:22:38 so there's a wide range of things that
4:22:39 you could do to help you come up with
4:22:41 some ideas for your name
4:22:43 on the other side of our diagram is our
4:22:45 mission statement and the way that we
4:22:47 use language here is very different
4:22:50 we're really looking at something that's
4:22:51 much more concise descriptive and
4:22:54 pragmatic we're not going to look at
4:22:56 this so closely because it doesn't
4:22:57 really affect your app so much but it
4:23:00 can help you to write a fake mission
4:23:02 statement as a kind of backstory you can
4:23:04 use it as a tool to help you figure out
4:23:06 what your app might be called
4:23:08 traditionally mission statements look at
4:23:11 the history function benefits and goals
4:23:14 of a company
4:23:15 mission statements are a condensed and
4:23:17 precise description of what a company
4:23:20 wants to do
4:23:21 but could easily be applied to what an
4:23:23 app wants to do as well so here's
4:23:25 coursera's mission statement for example
4:23:28 and it makes it very clear what their
4:23:30 goals are who they work with and what
4:23:33 they offer
4:23:34 their description fulfills our criteria
4:23:37 of being concise descriptive and
4:23:39 pragmatic
4:23:40 earlier we talked about our name tagline
4:23:43 and mission statement being connected
4:23:45 together and we also looked at how the
4:23:47 three things are different
4:23:49 in terms of scale and in terms of tone
4:23:52 the tagline falls somewhere in the
4:23:54 middle of the spectrum it has to be
4:23:56 longer and have more content than your
4:23:58 name but not be as long and dry as your
4:24:00 mission statement the tagline is the
4:24:03 thing that advertises your name
4:24:05 advertises your brand it succinctly gets
4:24:08 at the heart of what your company or
4:24:10 your app does
4:24:12 whether that's thinking differently in
4:24:14 the case of apple or outsmarting traffic
4:24:17 together in the case of waze
4:24:19 or broadcasting yourself in the case of
4:24:22 youtube
4:24:23 and as before it's always a good idea to
4:24:26 check out the competition
4:24:28 so if i look at my app for using up my
4:24:30 ingredients in my kitchen i can see what
4:24:33 other people have done already i can see
4:24:36 how other people have named their apps
4:24:38 looking at the competition can be really
4:24:40 useful because you don't want to come up
4:24:42 with a name that's very similar to
4:24:44 something that already exists but you
4:24:46 might also find some strategies you
4:24:48 hadn't thought of that you can use with
4:24:50 your own language and come up with your
4:24:53 own original name
4:24:54 if you're stuck there's another option
4:24:56 available to you
4:24:58 and that's to use automated generators
4:25:01 and some of these are free online where
4:25:03 you can enter topics or parameters or
4:25:05 words and they'll automatically generate
4:25:08 a number of different combinations of
4:25:10 words for you
4:25:12 most of the time they're pretty useless
4:25:14 and i'd advise you to try and outsmart
4:25:16 the automated
4:25:17 but every now and then they might come
4:25:19 up with something really great
4:25:22 here are some automated names for my
4:25:24 leftover food app
4:25:26 and while i'm not sure any of them are
4:25:28 100 right they might give me some ideas
4:25:31 that form the basis of the name that i
4:25:32 do choose or they might help me figure
4:25:35 out my mood and tone cuisinely is pretty
4:25:38 serious whereas pop gut is pretty fun
4:25:46 [Music]
4:25:51 this week we're going to continue to
4:25:52 develop plan and test your app idea
4:25:55 we'll be sketching out more extensive
4:25:57 content and mapping it into a structure
4:26:00 we'll be thinking about what your app
4:26:02 does and how it delivers an experience
4:26:04 to the user
4:26:05 to test what aspects of your app are
4:26:07 working we'll look at how to create a
4:26:09 non-visual paper prototype and will
4:26:11 carry out some limited non-visual user
4:26:13 tests
4:26:14 while you're developing the logic of
4:26:16 your app you'll also begin to develop
4:26:18 the look and feel of it the visual
4:26:20 development process
4:26:22 we'll also look at creating a site map
4:26:24 and begin to visualize the scope and
4:26:26 structure of the entire app
4:26:28 the sitemap and interaction flowchart
4:26:30 that we'll create will give you a
4:26:32 skeleton structure that will allow you
4:26:34 to begin to imagine how your app is
4:26:36 going to function and what the
4:26:37 experience of your app is going to be
4:26:46 in this second week of ux design
4:26:47 fundamentals we're going to focus on
4:26:50 mapping testing and envisioning we're
4:26:53 taking our design idea and moving it
4:26:55 further along in our process so let's
4:26:58 look at where we are right now
4:27:00 by now you should have an idea for your
4:27:03 app that you've developed and
4:27:04 articulated so that you have a name a
4:27:07 user profile for an ideal user a set of
4:27:11 goals that you want the app to achieve
4:27:13 and a tagline in the description that
4:27:15 work with your name to accurately
4:27:17 describe your app
4:27:19 and so all of that was our ideation and
4:27:21 articulation stage and that just forms
4:27:25 one part of our entire process
4:27:27 we're going to have to take those ideas
4:27:30 and somehow envision them start to give
4:27:32 them form start to map and plan them out
4:27:35 and when we've got that stage right
4:27:37 we'll be able to start to develop the
4:27:39 visual language even further and build a
4:27:41 working digital prototype these three
4:27:44 stages in our process roughly translate
4:27:47 as being non-visual semi-visual and then
4:27:50 visual
4:27:51 in our ideas phase we were really just
4:27:53 using words to describe everything that
4:27:56 we were thinking about
4:27:58 our middle stage will still use words
4:28:00 but it'll also use other devices like
4:28:03 maps sketches and diagrams
4:28:05 in our visual stage we'll be developing
4:28:08 the user interface and using it to
4:28:11 create final designs to put into a
4:28:13 working digital prototype
4:28:15 but this week we're going to be all
4:28:17 about this semi-visual stage the stage
4:28:20 of envisioning so we'll be trying to
4:28:22 expand and represent our ideas using a
4:28:24 set of tools other than just words but
4:28:27 we'll also be careful not to dive
4:28:29 straight into trying to create a fully
4:28:32 finished user interface design with
4:28:34 finished elements we don't want to
4:28:36 invest the time in doing that until
4:28:38 we've got this second stage of our
4:28:40 process really resolved and sorted out
4:28:43 in a lot of ways this middle stage is a
4:28:45 stepping stone between coming up with
4:28:47 the idea and making the idea
4:28:50 we're really developing it and mapping
4:28:53 everything out right now and that
4:28:55 development can be a mapping of the
4:28:57 content of our app where we're trying to
4:28:59 figure out exactly what is in our app
4:29:02 but it can also be developing our ideas
4:29:05 as well
4:29:06 we're constantly going to be evaluating
4:29:08 and trying to improve upon our app and
4:29:10 our initial idea
4:29:12 to help us develop our ideas and content
4:29:15 we're going to be carrying out some
4:29:16 preliminary prototyping in a very very
4:29:19 simple way just to try and see if things
4:29:22 work the way we think they're going to
4:29:23 work
4:29:24 we'll also start to look at the
4:29:25 beginning of visual development for our
4:29:27 user interface
4:29:29 we'll be looking at beginning design
4:29:31 directions thinking about the look and
4:29:33 feel of our app rather than the
4:29:35 specifics of the user interface
4:29:38 once we've started to envision our app
4:29:40 by developing it in this way
4:29:42 we'll be able to do a second stage of
4:29:44 envisioning where we look much more at
4:29:46 wireframing at the functionality and
4:29:48 logic of our website thinking about how
4:29:51 to refine it a little bit more and
4:29:53 making sure that the pieces that we're
4:29:55 developing will work together and we'll
4:29:57 be doing that in the third week of our
4:29:58 class
4:29:59 so this second week is really the first
4:30:02 half of our envisioning process
4:30:04 it's closer to our ideation phase than
4:30:07 our prototyping phase we're still very
4:30:09 much developing our ideas and also
4:30:12 developing our content it's important to
4:30:14 remember that this is all part of a
4:30:16 process and at this stage in our process
4:30:19 we still have the ability to try
4:30:21 different things out to test things to
4:30:23 see what works and what doesn't work to
4:30:26 change things if we need to this is the
4:30:28 stage where your ideas get tested and
4:30:30 improved upon so it's important that you
4:30:33 be open-minded but that you're also
4:30:35 analytical about the work that you're
4:30:37 making and critical about it to some
4:30:39 extent
4:30:40 try and be responsive and react to the
4:30:43 things that work and the things that
4:30:44 don't work what we're really trying to
4:30:47 do here is get you to grow your app and
4:30:49 develop it into something really
4:30:51 substantial and successful
4:30:55 in the next two sections we're going to
4:30:57 look at mapping content and mapping
4:30:59 interaction we're going to break it into
4:31:01 two sections and start out by looking at
4:31:04 how to map content
4:31:06 this will help you figure out the scope
4:31:08 and structure of your app as well as
4:31:10 what is going where in terms of your
4:31:12 content
4:31:13 ui ux designers make many different
4:31:16 kinds of content maps and your content
4:31:19 map can look and feel any way that you
4:31:21 want it to
4:31:23 there's no industry standard so it's
4:31:25 really up to you
4:31:26 and you can see many different examples
4:31:28 if you do a search online and while how
4:31:31 your content map looks and works is
4:31:33 entirely up to you
4:31:35 there are some things that all good maps
4:31:37 have in common
4:31:38 primarily that your map has to make
4:31:41 sense especially since you might be
4:31:43 showing it to other people to explain
4:31:45 your app
4:31:47 so let's look at some qualities that
4:31:48 your content map should have your map
4:31:51 should have clarity so that everybody
4:31:52 can understand it very quickly it should
4:31:54 have a hierarchy so we know what's
4:31:56 important and what's not it should have
4:31:58 modularity so similar items have similar
4:32:01 visual representation and it should have
4:32:03 the potential to grow and expand
4:32:07 so let's make a generic content map with
4:32:10 generic content
4:32:11 we've got a blank page to begin with so
4:32:14 we're going to have to decide what's
4:32:15 going to go where what's going to be at
4:32:17 the heart of our map the logical place
4:32:21 to start would be with our home page
4:32:23 where a lot of our activity is going to
4:32:25 sit
4:32:26 but since we're making an app we might
4:32:28 want some kind of splash page or some
4:32:30 kind of id page that briefly occurs
4:32:33 before we get to our home page
4:32:35 let's say that there's three things that
4:32:37 we can do from our home page
4:32:39 these would all be major events or
4:32:41 activities and they'd be connected to
4:32:43 our home page so we can use a similar
4:32:46 shape to describe them and we can
4:32:48 connect them to our home page by a
4:32:50 series of lines
4:32:52 we don't know what our events are right
4:32:53 now they're just generic so we're going
4:32:56 to leave them equal in the hierarchy so
4:32:59 we've set up a system to describe our
4:33:01 content in words and in boxes and the
4:33:04 lines connecting them imply the kind of
4:33:06 activity that happens between those
4:33:08 boxes or what kind of relationship they
4:33:11 have from the arrow i know that the id
4:33:13 page pushes me onto the home page
4:33:16 and from the home page i know that the
4:33:18 connecting lines mean that i have three
4:33:20 options that i can go to and i can move
4:33:22 between those options because they're
4:33:24 also connected by a line
4:33:26 it's a very simple visual logic but one
4:33:29 that you need to be consistent with
4:33:31 so here are my sub events that are much
4:33:33 smaller things than the larger events so
4:33:35 i'm going to make those boxes smaller
4:33:37 and connect them to each of the
4:33:39 individual events that i'd have to go
4:33:41 through first
4:33:43 so i'm using scale in my geometric
4:33:45 shapes different kinds of shapes and
4:33:48 scale in my typography to create a
4:33:50 system
4:33:51 and once i've established the logic of
4:33:53 my visual language i can keep extending
4:33:56 it as i add more content
4:33:59 for instance one of my sub events might
4:34:01 lead me to a much larger event one
4:34:03 that's the same as events one two and
4:34:05 three
4:34:06 so i can show the connection and how i
4:34:08 get to that event but i can also show
4:34:11 its level of importance by using scale
4:34:14 and that larger event in turn might lead
4:34:16 me to other sub events and again i can
4:34:19 use scale to indicate their position in
4:34:21 the hierarchy
4:34:23 because we're making a generic map and
4:34:25 we're using generic terms to describe
4:34:27 our content it's visually a very flat
4:34:30 and even map right now so even though we
4:34:33 know that some things are more important
4:34:35 to others by scale we have no idea about
4:34:38 the content and how it might be grouped
4:34:40 together and we can use color as a way
4:34:42 to code that content autocode
4:34:45 functionality or types of pages so let's
4:34:48 add some color coding to our content map
4:34:51 our identity splash page and our home
4:34:54 page are pretty individual standalone
4:34:56 pages so they can have their own color
4:34:59 to signify that for the rest of our map
4:35:01 we can group similar content at similar
4:35:04 levels of engagement and activity and
4:35:07 give those a color coding system to let
4:35:09 us see that hierarchy
4:35:12 again it's a very simple technique but
4:35:14 we're using color as a coding system to
4:35:17 let us see similar kinds of event or
4:35:20 content so if we added pages that took
4:35:22 us away from our app we might want to
4:35:25 code those all the same color
4:35:27 we could also think about how we use the
4:35:29 lines and arrows between our boxes to
4:35:32 signify connections or signify traveling
4:35:35 to another space
4:35:37 because we're designing an app a lot of
4:35:39 our activity might take place on one
4:35:42 screen with pop-up menus sitting on top
4:35:44 of a background and we can show that by
4:35:47 only using the arrows when we travel to
4:35:49 another screen entirely if we had a
4:35:51 website with the same content it might
4:35:53 look the same but because it's on
4:35:55 multiple screens we'd have a lot more
4:35:57 arrows indicating traveling to a lot
4:35:59 more screens
4:36:01 we're looking at a simple diagramming
4:36:03 system here
4:36:04 it's all to do with mapping and planning
4:36:07 and being able to think about your
4:36:08 content without actually making any of
4:36:11 that content so far we've looked at a
4:36:13 generic structure with generic content
4:36:17 let's see what happens when we get rid
4:36:18 of the generic content and start to
4:36:21 actually map out a real idea for an app
4:36:24 let's start with our id page in our home
4:36:26 page but let's use one of the ideas we
4:36:28 looked at earlier which is an app to
4:36:31 help you find a lost pet we're going to
4:36:33 use the same structure that we looked at
4:36:36 with our generic map but we're going to
4:36:38 shape it and change it to fit this
4:36:40 content so let's say that our app is
4:36:43 going to do three main things three
4:36:46 large events or activities
4:36:49 for my lost pet app those three things
4:36:52 might be that i would want to search for
4:36:54 a lost pet that i might want to post
4:36:56 information about a pet that i'd lost in
4:36:58 the hope that somebody else would see it
4:37:00 and find my pet
4:37:02 or that i might want to post information
4:37:04 about a pet that i'd found in the hopes
4:37:07 that i was going to reunite it with its
4:37:09 owner
4:37:10 all of these activities would be
4:37:12 happening on one page on my home screen
4:37:15 so i might think about what kind of page
4:37:17 should that be it would make sense to me
4:37:20 for that to be a map so that i could
4:37:23 look in the area that i wanted to look
4:37:25 if i'm searching for something and i
4:37:27 could see the area that animals were
4:37:29 found or perhaps where animals had been
4:37:31 lost as well so i'm developing my
4:37:34 content organizing my content and
4:37:36 shaping my app all at the same time
4:37:39 through this process of mapping as i
4:37:42 expand my map i'm expanding the content
4:37:46 if i wanted to search for my lost pet i
4:37:49 might think about how would i do that
4:37:51 i'd need to enter a name maybe think
4:37:54 about searching by species or by date or
4:37:57 by description some of my choices might
4:37:59 have subcategories so after i picked a
4:38:02 species i might need to pick a breed or
4:38:04 i might need to add more information
4:38:06 that describes my pet just like our
4:38:09 generic content map a lot of this
4:38:11 activity would all be happening on one
4:38:13 screen and we've decided that it's going
4:38:16 to be based around a map so we're going
4:38:18 to have to come up with another idea for
4:38:20 a structure that's going to allow us to
4:38:22 float information on top of that map so
4:38:25 we might need a side menu or a separate
4:38:27 field to house that extra information
4:38:30 and if we're going to do a search we're
4:38:32 going to need somewhere to see our
4:38:34 search results as well
4:38:36 so as we're mapping out our content
4:38:38 we're also thinking about structure and
4:38:41 we're also developing the functionality
4:38:43 of our app in real life you might make
4:38:46 many different content maps with
4:38:48 different emphasis or with different
4:38:50 structures or different ways of showing
4:38:53 your idea once you have a structure that
4:38:55 you like and that's working you can
4:38:57 start to add additional information into
4:38:59 your content map
4:39:01 so i might add some outside sites that i
4:39:04 might go to from my app i might want to
4:39:06 post to facebook about my lost pet or
4:39:08 connect to the city pan perhaps you can
4:39:11 also fill out all the places that
4:39:13 content is going to exist
4:39:15 so for every section in your app you
4:39:18 show all the different pieces and
4:39:19 options that are going to be in that
4:39:21 section and this will change from
4:39:23 section to section so it'll help you
4:39:25 develop your content and develop your
4:39:27 structure so for my app searching for a
4:39:30 pet might have a slightly different
4:39:32 structure to how i would post
4:39:34 information about a pet that i'd lost
4:39:36 for instance where i might need a photo
4:39:39 and more detailed information
4:39:41 as we're mapping out all of the content
4:39:43 for our app the one thing that we're not
4:39:46 doing is thinking about design
4:39:48 we're not really making any design
4:39:50 decisions we're just creating a
4:39:52 structure and a framework and making
4:39:55 decisions about content we're looking at
4:39:57 what goes where and how it might be
4:40:00 organized and once we've figured that
4:40:02 out we can begin to think about how a
4:40:04 user might go through this content and
4:40:07 experience this content we're going to
4:40:09 start to think about how to map the
4:40:11 user's interaction and that might have
4:40:14 an effect on our content map
4:40:18 in the last class we looked at mapping
4:40:20 content in this class we're going to
4:40:23 shift focus slightly and look at mapping
4:40:25 interaction
4:40:26 and there's a lot of overlap between
4:40:28 those two kinds of maps if your app is
4:40:30 simple you might only make one map and
4:40:33 show both of these things in one map but
4:40:36 we're going to split them up and look at
4:40:38 them separately
4:40:39 the interaction map shows how the user
4:40:42 travels through your content so it's
4:40:44 much more like a flowchart in a lot of
4:40:45 ways
4:40:46 while there's overlap it sometimes has a
4:40:49 slightly different visual language to
4:40:51 its form as well just like our content
4:40:53 map your interaction map can look and
4:40:56 work however you want as long as it
4:40:58 makes sense
4:41:00 but it needs to share some of the same
4:41:01 qualities as your content map as well
4:41:04 like any map or diagram it needs to have
4:41:06 clarity so you can understand it quite
4:41:09 quickly and easily and it needs to have
4:41:11 a hierarchy
4:41:12 so that you visually understand which
4:41:14 things are important and which things
4:41:16 are less important
4:41:17 and it needs to have modularity so that
4:41:20 you can repeat the same kind of forms
4:41:22 for the same kind of content or in this
4:41:24 case for the same kind of actions
4:41:27 and it also needs to have the
4:41:28 possibility to expand or grow because
4:41:31 your app or website might change or grow
4:41:34 itself
4:41:35 so let's look at an interaction map that
4:41:37 has generic content and generic form to
4:41:40 avoid confusion let's call it a
4:41:42 flowchart of user pathways and for a
4:41:44 simple app like ours there's going to be
4:41:47 a lot of overlap with our content map so
4:41:49 if we go back to our generic content map
4:41:52 we can use that as the basis for our
4:41:54 user interaction flowchart we'd still
4:41:57 start out with the home page but let's
4:41:59 put that up at the top and show what our
4:42:01 immediate choices are as we travel
4:42:03 through this information
4:42:05 a user might choose from event one two
4:42:07 or three and then might choose
4:42:09 sub-events from any of those main events
4:42:12 we can also show the places we might go
4:42:15 off-site from our app just like we did
4:42:17 with our generic content map
4:42:19 but one of the big differences here is
4:42:22 that it's very hard for us to create a
4:42:24 generic flowchart of interaction you
4:42:26 can't map the user's path with generic
4:42:29 content
4:42:30 because the user doesn't know what
4:42:31 they're making a decision about
4:42:33 we need the content to be in there so
4:42:36 the user has something to make a
4:42:37 decision about
4:42:39 because those decisions are going to
4:42:41 determine the path that they're going to
4:42:43 take and that's what we're interested in
4:42:45 here pathways between events
4:42:48 pathways that the user is going to
4:42:50 potentially follow so let's put some
4:42:52 content in there let's use our lost pet
4:42:54 example again
4:42:56 we decided that there would be three
4:42:57 main things that our user could do from
4:42:59 the home page and the user probably
4:43:01 chooses one of those things to do and
4:43:04 our content map helped us develop the
4:43:06 idea that we were going to have an
4:43:08 actual gps map before the user made any
4:43:11 of these decisions so we should put that
4:43:13 into our user flow chart to see how it
4:43:15 affects the decision making process we
4:43:18 can give that a different kind of form
4:43:20 on our flow chart to show that it's
4:43:21 working with a different kind of
4:43:23 information it might be reading the
4:43:25 user's gps location for instance
4:43:28 with the user interaction flow chart
4:43:31 we're asking much more how do you get
4:43:33 from step one to step two the map might
4:43:36 automatically read our location but you
4:43:38 might also need to enter in your zip
4:43:40 code or address
4:43:41 we can show this visually in an either
4:43:44 or kind of way
4:43:45 from the location map we either enter
4:43:47 our location to get to our activities
4:43:50 or it reads our location automatically
4:43:53 and goes straight to those activities
4:43:56 once the user chooses a main activity
4:43:58 it's less about showing us what the
4:44:00 options are that they can choose from
4:44:02 and more about showing how they travel
4:44:05 through those options if i wanted to
4:44:07 post information about my lost animal
4:44:10 i might choose a certain order for the
4:44:12 activities and have them follow on one
4:44:14 from the other so i'd have to visually
4:44:17 show that
4:44:18 and at the end of that passage of
4:44:19 entering information there'd be a
4:44:21 different kind of interaction
4:44:24 the user would have to either confirm or
4:44:26 post that information so i might need to
4:44:29 slightly differentiate visually one
4:44:31 activity from another and i need to show
4:44:34 where the user's path took them after
4:44:36 that they probably had back to the
4:44:38 location map where they could see their
4:44:40 own post or perhaps engage in another
4:44:42 activity remember we're mapping the
4:44:45 user's interaction with our app here
4:44:48 we're not just mapping the content
4:44:50 so if i didn't want to post what i'd
4:44:52 entered i need a way to get back and
4:44:54 edit that material once again i'm
4:44:57 thinking more about the pathways and
4:44:59 traveling through the content than
4:45:01 thinking about defining the content
4:45:03 itself so i find it quite useful to have
4:45:06 a content map and have a user
4:45:08 interaction flowchart and the two of
4:45:11 them do inform each other and have a lot
4:45:13 of similarities
4:45:14 so let's make this the same as our
4:45:16 content map and add our set of links to
4:45:18 outside places and just like our content
4:45:21 map let's use color to reinforce
4:45:23 information within our diagram
4:45:26 we can use color to show different kinds
4:45:28 of content in the same way we did before
4:45:31 so light blue here for our main
4:45:32 activities and pink for the sites that
4:45:35 are away from our app
4:45:37 we can build a relationship between
4:45:39 color and shape as well here all of our
4:45:42 rectangles are places where the user has
4:45:44 to enter information
4:45:46 but the yellow rectangles are places
4:45:48 that are much more important so we've
4:45:50 created a similarity but also a
4:45:53 hierarchy within our diagram
4:45:55 and that hierarchy is about the user's
4:45:57 interaction not necessarily about the
4:46:00 content
4:46:01 here we have three main areas where the
4:46:04 user can carry out an activity
4:46:06 let's see what happens if we flesh those
4:46:08 out a little bit more it's already
4:46:10 getting quite complicated it's starting
4:46:12 to get crowded and might not even fit on
4:46:15 the page
4:46:16 and this is where the modular or
4:46:17 expandable part of our diagram really
4:46:19 comes into play now i can shrink all of
4:46:22 this information down and add other
4:46:24 information that's going to feel and
4:46:27 look exactly the same and as i add more
4:46:30 information i also have to adjust my
4:46:32 system because i want my logic and my
4:46:35 hierarchy to be consistent
4:46:38 so i could think about being consistent
4:46:40 with scale coloring with position of the
4:46:42 elements with the arrangement of them as
4:46:44 well
4:46:45 what my flowcharts really doing here is
4:46:48 mapping out all the possible decisions
4:46:50 that a user could make
4:46:52 all the possible pathways that they
4:46:53 could take so i'm going to have to lay
4:46:56 out quite a lot of information into this
4:46:58 one diagram
4:46:59 it's useful to do that in a modular and
4:47:02 consistent fashion
4:47:04 so my use of flow for posting lost and
4:47:06 posting found are very similar right now
4:47:10 so i could start by duplicating them and
4:47:12 making them exactly the same because
4:47:14 there's a little visual inconsistency
4:47:15 right now
4:47:17 so sometimes even just tidying up what
4:47:20 your diagram looks like and how you're
4:47:22 representing an interaction
4:47:24 can really help your diagram make visual
4:47:26 sense
4:47:27 as you're mapping how the user travels
4:47:30 through your content you also have to
4:47:32 make sure that you have the right
4:47:33 content in the right places
4:47:36 for instance when i'm searching for my
4:47:38 pet i might not want to enter all of the
4:47:40 same information as when i'm posting
4:47:42 something either lost or found
4:47:45 if my app doesn't work with photo
4:47:47 recognition there's no point me
4:47:49 uploading a photograph to do my search
4:47:52 the date when i lost my animal might not
4:47:54 be so important and i might not need to
4:47:56 put my content information up there yet
4:47:59 because i'm just searching
4:48:00 so as you can see as i'm figuring out my
4:48:03 user interaction it's having an effect
4:48:06 on my content so my content map and my
4:48:09 user flow chart are interwoven together
4:48:12 and they affect one another but they
4:48:14 show slightly different information so
4:48:16 when i'm searching for a lost pet for
4:48:18 instance in my flow chart i want to show
4:48:20 how the user goes through that
4:48:22 experience what information they enter
4:48:25 and when
4:48:26 so here i want to diagram that they
4:48:28 could choose between species name and
4:48:30 description and whichever one they
4:48:32 choose it would come up with a set of
4:48:34 results
4:48:36 so while there's overlap with content
4:48:38 i'm much more concerned with the arrows
4:48:41 and the pathways that are connecting
4:48:43 these different pieces of content again
4:48:46 showing the path that the user might
4:48:48 possibly take you always have to be
4:48:50 thinking what next what's the next step
4:48:53 so after i've got my results where would
4:48:55 i go next i'd probably either go back
4:48:58 and do another search if i didn't get
4:49:00 the results i wanted to
4:49:01 or if i found my pet i'd want to confirm
4:49:04 that somehow and maybe contact someone
4:49:07 to reiterate one more time what we're
4:49:09 really doing here is mapping interaction
4:49:12 we're showing how the user travels
4:49:14 through our content in the different
4:49:16 paths that they can take but as with our
4:49:19 content map we're not making any design
4:49:21 decisions yet we could take this
4:49:23 framework or structure and develop it in
4:49:26 any number of different directions
4:49:28 and while it might look and feel
4:49:30 different its structure would be the
4:49:32 same because while we're mapping out
4:49:34 these interactive pathways we're also
4:49:36 mapping out the structure of our site
4:49:39 so
4:49:40 as i mentioned there are connections
4:49:42 between our interaction flow chart and
4:49:45 our content map there are similarities
4:49:47 between the two things but they focus on
4:49:50 different kinds of information and
4:49:52 experiences
4:49:53 and they don't actually contain the same
4:49:55 information
4:49:57 if you think about our attempts even
4:49:59 from early on to separate ui from ux so
4:50:02 we could examine them more closely we're
4:50:04 kind of doing the same thing here our
4:50:07 user flowchart is trying to envision the
4:50:09 experience without any finished visuals
4:50:12 it's focusing more on the ux part of the
4:50:14 equation and while we might not be
4:50:16 making any form now what we are doing is
4:50:18 thinking creatively and inventively and
4:50:22 shaping the structure that our form is
4:50:24 eventually going to take
4:50:27 in this section we're going to use
4:50:29 non-visual paper prototypes to do our
4:50:32 very first user tests
4:50:34 in our last section we looked at mapping
4:50:37 out our content and also mapping out the
4:50:39 user's interaction
4:50:41 our content map acted as a diagram
4:50:44 showing us where all our content was
4:50:46 going to go
4:50:47 whereas our interaction map acted as a
4:50:49 flowchart showing us how a user would
4:50:52 interact with that content
4:50:54 both of these maps helped us develop our
4:50:57 idea our structure our thinking and our
4:51:00 problem solving might all have changed
4:51:02 so far we've used diagrams in geometric
4:51:05 elements but we've mostly been using
4:51:07 words as our tool for helping us develop
4:51:10 our ideas and our content
4:51:12 in our next stage we're going to look at
4:51:14 real decisions about our app in real
4:51:17 time and we're going to try and use a
4:51:19 real user to test it out but we're still
4:51:22 going to use words and we're doing that
4:51:24 because we're still in a preliminary
4:51:26 stage where we don't really have a
4:51:28 visual design yet and words are the
4:51:30 easiest way for us to express our ideas
4:51:33 we want to try and simulate an
4:51:35 interactive experience with a user we
4:51:38 want to test out our content map and our
4:51:41 flowchart of user interaction and see if
4:51:44 we were right or not
4:51:45 we're going to do this in two stages
4:51:48 first of all we're going to develop a
4:51:49 paper prototype and then we're going to
4:51:51 try and test it on a user
4:51:53 developing that paper prototype is
4:51:55 something that we can do on our own
4:51:57 that's a kind of internal stage
4:52:00 all the work we've done so far in
4:52:02 developing our app has been internal the
4:52:04 second stage will be the first time
4:52:06 we've taken our app to an external phase
4:52:09 to have a user look at it so let's look
4:52:11 at how to develop a paper prototype
4:52:13 we've already figured out a lot of
4:52:15 things about our app from the diagrams
4:52:17 that we've already made we already have
4:52:19 a map of what we think the user's
4:52:21 experience will be that diagrams all of
4:52:24 the different decisions that they can
4:52:26 make and the places that they can go
4:52:28 within our app for this basic user test
4:52:32 we don't want the user to see the whole
4:52:34 of the map and all their options we want
4:52:36 to feed them those options one at a time
4:52:39 we want the user to make decisions one
4:52:41 at a time so we're going to have to take
4:52:44 our map apart and dismantle it into
4:52:47 single sheets of paper and have each
4:52:50 sheet of paper contain just enough
4:52:52 information to get the user to the next
4:52:54 stage before they have to make a
4:52:56 decision we're breaking down and slowing
4:52:59 down the interactive process the user's
4:53:02 interaction with our app
4:53:05 for my test i took several sheets of
4:53:07 paper all the same size and made each
4:53:10 represent a decision the user had to
4:53:12 make based on the user interaction chart
4:53:14 i'd already developed we're basically
4:53:17 taking something very complex apart in
4:53:20 order to examine the individual pieces
4:53:22 more slowly and it's a strategy we've
4:53:24 used many times in this course
4:53:27 after my branding screen the user would
4:53:29 get to a screen where they have to
4:53:30 choose whether to log in use their
4:53:32 current location or enter a location
4:53:36 if they chose the login option i'd give
4:53:38 them another piece of paper where they
4:53:40 could enter their name their password or
4:53:43 perhaps log in with their google or
4:53:46 facebook id
4:53:47 if they wanted to enter their location
4:53:49 i'd hand them a piece of paper where
4:53:51 they would be entering their street
4:53:53 address the city in town perhaps the
4:53:56 state and the zip as well
4:53:58 if they chose their current location
4:54:00 there would be nothing to enter because
4:54:02 the app would already know what that
4:54:04 location was
4:54:05 in all three cases they'd be taken to
4:54:08 the next screen
4:54:09 this would be the map screen that forms
4:54:11 the main hub of activity for our entire
4:54:14 app for this simple test i'd ask the
4:54:17 user to choose between the three main
4:54:19 options they could choose to search for
4:54:21 a lost pet or they could choose to post
4:54:24 information about a lost pet or post
4:54:27 information about a pet that had been
4:54:28 found
4:54:29 if they chose to search for a pet i'd
4:54:31 give them another piece of paper
4:54:34 and here we'd be pretending that they'd
4:54:36 be entering information about the
4:54:38 species and breed name description or
4:54:41 location if it was different of the pet
4:54:43 that they were looking for
4:54:45 once they'd given me that information
4:54:47 i'd hand them another piece of paper
4:54:49 that would be the results of their
4:54:51 search so they would have a list of
4:54:53 three different dogs in this case that
4:54:56 fitted the parameters of their search
4:54:58 if the user chose any of those dogs they
4:55:01 could get more information about that
4:55:03 particular dog and since there are three
4:55:05 different choices here but with the same
4:55:08 kind of information i could use that as
4:55:10 an option to perhaps try some variations
4:55:13 i could reorder the list for example to
4:55:15 see which one the user might respond to
4:55:18 better
4:55:19 one of the great things about making a
4:55:20 paper prototype like this is that you
4:55:23 can change it or add things very very
4:55:25 quickly you can even respond to user
4:55:27 feedback in real time so here i might
4:55:30 want to add a button that would take me
4:55:32 back to the previous list if i wasn't
4:55:34 interested in one of these dogs for
4:55:36 instance let's go back to our main
4:55:38 screen and follow another path
4:55:40 if the user chose to post information
4:55:42 about a lost animal i'd hand them this
4:55:44 piece of paper with a number of
4:55:46 different pieces of information for them
4:55:48 to enter i could simulate the process by
4:55:51 asking the user to answer each of these
4:55:54 sections one by one before they could
4:55:56 post that information
4:55:58 and this whole process would be verbal
4:56:00 and would be pretend we're really trying
4:56:02 to simulate the back and forth between
4:56:04 the user and the app
4:56:07 our third option from the main map
4:56:09 screen would be posting information
4:56:11 about an animal that we'd found and this
4:56:14 would follow a similar pattern to the
4:56:16 series of interactions we had when we
4:56:18 were posting about a lost animal
4:56:21 an easy way to get started making your
4:56:23 paper prototype is to take your user
4:56:26 interaction flowchart and to just map
4:56:28 that out on single sheets of paper
4:56:31 you can make one sheet for each stage or
4:56:33 decision and you can actually lay those
4:56:35 sheets out in exactly the same structure
4:56:38 that you've already mapped out for your
4:56:40 user flow chart i like to do this in a
4:56:42 big space where i can move everything
4:56:44 around and rearrange it as i want to but
4:56:47 you could also do it in a smaller space
4:56:49 with post-it notes it's helpful to be
4:56:51 able to move things around very easily
4:56:54 and it's also helpful to do this in a
4:56:56 very rough and ready visual way
4:56:59 it helps make you less attached to each
4:57:01 individual piece of paper so you can
4:57:04 change things and alter things or
4:57:06 rearrange things without worrying about
4:57:08 losing things or screwing things up
4:57:11 the other huge benefit of working in
4:57:13 this loose style and just using words
4:57:17 is that design doesn't get in the way of
4:57:19 the content there's no distractions for
4:57:21 you but more importantly there's no
4:57:23 distractions when you come to test this
4:57:25 out on a user
4:57:27 you're trying to focus their attention
4:57:29 on decision making with limited
4:57:32 information about the content you don't
4:57:34 want design to be a distraction at this
4:57:36 point
4:57:38 this is a very simple and preliminary
4:57:40 form of user testing and we have to
4:57:42 structure our information for that test
4:57:45 in a sympathetic way
4:57:47 so we can't have a lot of information
4:57:49 going on on each of these pages you
4:57:52 might want to limit the choices to maybe
4:57:54 three things
4:57:56 you might also find that you need to
4:57:58 make extra stages in the decision making
4:58:01 process when you're going to try this
4:58:03 out with a real person and again one of
4:58:05 the benefits of working in this rough
4:58:07 and ready way is that it's very very
4:58:10 fast for you to make changes or
4:58:12 expansions to your map
4:58:14 and we're still primarily just working
4:58:17 with language here
4:58:18 i drew an image on my branding screen
4:58:21 but apart from that the only graphic
4:58:23 elements in the other screens are a few
4:58:26 boxes to indicate a hierarchy and a few
4:58:29 dots and lines to separate pieces of
4:58:32 content
4:58:33 any graphic elements should be
4:58:35 supporting actors they shouldn't be
4:58:37 taking the lead role
4:58:38 keeping your communication focused on
4:58:41 just text allows the user to imagine any
4:58:44 visual form they want to or imagine no
4:58:47 visual form at all what's important is
4:58:49 that you keep the user focused on
4:58:52 decision making you want to offer them a
4:58:55 series of paths to choose from that
4:58:57 aren't visually differentiated
4:59:00 so as you make this series of single
4:59:02 sheets
4:59:04 allow things to move around don't be
4:59:06 afraid if things change from your flow
4:59:09 chart that you've already made you can
4:59:11 always make different variations for
4:59:13 certain screens and try those out
4:59:15 nothing is fixed at this stage in the
4:59:17 process so you still have the ability to
4:59:20 allow things to change you have the
4:59:22 ability to learn things as you're making
4:59:26 laying things out like this might make
4:59:28 you come up with some new ideas or it
4:59:30 might make you rethink the structure
4:59:32 that you already had
4:59:34 in many ways you are the first user
4:59:37 who's going to test your paper prototype
4:59:39 you're the first one to experience it so
4:59:42 you can evaluate your own experience and
4:59:44 make changes
4:59:45 and once you've made those revisions
4:59:48 you'll have a set of sheets of paper
4:59:50 that you'll be able to take to a user
4:59:52 and test your app with a simple paper
4:59:55 prototype you'll be able to get your
4:59:57 first user feedback
5:00:00 in this segment we're going to continue
5:00:02 with our non-visual user testing we
5:00:04 already completed the first stage where
5:00:07 we made our paper prototype so now we're
5:00:09 going to move on to the second stage
5:00:11 where we're going to try and test that
5:00:13 prototype out on a user this might be
5:00:16 the first time you share your idea with
5:00:18 somebody else it's certainly the first
5:00:20 time you'll be getting feedback from an
5:00:22 external source for this simple test
5:00:26 your external source could be family or
5:00:28 friends or if you don't have anyone to
5:00:30 act as the user you could roleplay and
5:00:33 do it yourself pretending to be your
5:00:35 ideal user this kind of paper prototype
5:00:38 testing that we're doing is the simplest
5:00:41 kind of testing and while you might
5:00:43 learn a few things from the user it's
5:00:45 also to get you comfortable with testing
5:00:47 because you have to play a participatory
5:00:50 role in this process and if you were
5:00:52 really developing an app you'd be
5:00:54 testing regularly and you'd be testing
5:00:56 often
5:00:57 so you could think of this simple first
5:00:59 test as a precursor to more developed
5:01:02 user testing that you would carry out
5:01:04 later in the development process of your
5:01:06 app
5:01:07 and because we're fairly early in the
5:01:09 process of developing our app when we do
5:01:11 this kind of testing we're really trying
5:01:14 to figure out if there are large major
5:01:16 problems that we encounter with a user
5:01:19 there may be things that we didn't
5:01:20 anticipate as a designer that you only
5:01:23 get to see when somebody is experiencing
5:01:26 your app and the pathways of your app
5:01:28 for the first time
5:01:30 your familiarity with your own content
5:01:33 and your own idea can sometimes blind
5:01:35 you to some obvious errors or issues
5:01:39 so having a test user who doesn't really
5:01:41 know anything about your app can be
5:01:43 really helpful
5:01:44 you can have your user sit across the
5:01:46 table from you
5:01:48 you can hand them your sheets of paper
5:01:50 one at a time and ask them which option
5:01:53 they choose
5:01:54 depending on what they choose you can
5:01:56 give them the next piece of paper
5:01:58 remember we're trying to focus on
5:02:00 interaction here we're trying to see how
5:02:03 the user interacts with the app
5:02:05 so having some kind of separation
5:02:08 sitting opposite from the user for
5:02:09 instance can be quite helpful
5:02:12 also the user is playing the role of the
5:02:14 user but you're playing the role of the
5:02:16 app
5:02:17 so don't give the user additional
5:02:19 explanations of everything that's on a
5:02:21 sheet of paper if they get stuck or ask
5:02:24 you a question you can answer them
5:02:26 but for the most part you're going to
5:02:28 learn a lot more by quietly observing
5:02:31 the user interacting with the sheets of
5:02:33 paper and making decisions you're
5:02:36 playing the role of the app here so if
5:02:38 the user needs to enter data into fields
5:02:41 for instance you can just do that
5:02:43 verbally with them
5:02:45 and it'll only take you a few minutes to
5:02:47 go through all the pieces of paper so
5:02:49 you can try and find more than one
5:02:50 person to test with you
5:02:52 so let's look a little bit more closely
5:02:54 at user testing and look at the
5:02:56 relationship between the user and the
5:02:59 tester
5:03:00 the tester's job is to act as the app
5:03:03 and to give the user a series of options
5:03:05 for them to choose from the user's job
5:03:08 is to make decisions about those options
5:03:10 and give them back to the tester so you
5:03:13 have a constant loop of exchange between
5:03:16 user and tester
5:03:18 while that loop is going on the tester's
5:03:20 job is to observe the user to see which
5:03:23 decisions they make but also to see how
5:03:26 easy or difficult those decisions are
5:03:29 the user's job is to make those
5:03:31 decisions and to give the tester
5:03:33 information when needed or to tell them
5:03:36 when there's an issue
5:03:37 both the user and the tester are trying
5:03:40 to figure out how the app works the user
5:03:43 is doing this in an active way by making
5:03:45 decisions and telling it to the tester
5:03:48 and the tester is doing it in a passive
5:03:50 way by observing what the user does
5:03:53 ideally the user won't run into any
5:03:55 problems and the tester will really be
5:03:58 observing just what works
5:04:00 but it's also really useful when the
5:04:02 user finds something that doesn't work
5:04:05 because then you know it's a problem and
5:04:07 you know what you've got to fix
5:04:09 and if you run this preliminary paper
5:04:11 test with a few different users and they
5:04:14 all have the same issue they all run
5:04:16 into a problem in the same place then
5:04:18 you can be pretty sure that it's
5:04:20 actually a genuine problem and you're
5:04:22 going to have to change something with
5:04:23 your app
5:04:26 in this section we're going to look at
5:04:27 how to develop a more accurate and
5:04:29 advanced site map we already built a
5:04:32 very simple content map and that was

5:04:37 content we also built a user flowchart
5:04:41 and that was useful in showing how the
5:04:43 user would travel through our content
5:04:45 both of these examples were really tools
5:04:48 to help us develop our idea and our
5:04:50 content now we want something more
5:04:52 concrete which is our site map and we're
5:04:55 going to use that sitemap to build
5:04:57 wireframes and we'll use those
5:04:59 wireframes
5:05:00 in user testing so our sitemap needs to
5:05:03 be a little more concrete we're starting
5:05:05 to commit to the shape of our app our
5:05:08 sitemap is going to follow quite closely
5:05:11 our user flowchart we can build off that
5:05:14 and change it we need to take into
5:05:16 account any feedback or ideas that we

5:05:22 testing we're also moving towards
5:05:24 creating wireframes so i'm going to use

5:05:30 app as an example to build a site map

5:05:44 i have an auto login so if i'm a

5:05:53 depending what i choose to do on this
5:05:55 screen i'm going to get a different

5:06:09 in so for each of these possibilities no
5:06:12 matter how small i'm going to have to
5:06:14 create a separate screen so i'd need a
5:06:18 screen for my google login and i'd also
5:06:20 need a screen for my facebook login if i
5:06:23 was creating an account i'd need to add

5:06:28 perhaps address city state and zip
5:06:31 so we've already made quite a few
5:06:34 different screens and we haven't even
5:06:36 really got to our map yet to make any

5:06:40 our app there's a lot of different
5:06:42 screens here but the user would actually
5:06:44 travel through them very quickly like
5:06:46 our user flowchart let's connect the
5:06:48 screens to see what the pathways could
5:06:50 possibly be from our identity screen we
5:06:53 could go straight to the map if we
5:06:55 automatically log in if not we'd need to
5:06:58 enter some information we could type in
5:07:01 our name and our password or we could
5:07:04 use google or facebook to log into our
5:07:06 app
5:07:07 because our app is primarily going to

5:07:18 all of these screens are just about the

5:07:22 and we could color code those so we know

5:07:28 once we've logged in we're going to want
5:07:30 to get to our real map where we can
5:07:32 start to use the app if i was a

5:07:41 could enter my name and password and get

5:07:48 more information before i could get to

5:07:51 so you can see as i'm making my app i'm

5:08:04 developed and accurate sitemap once i've

5:08:40 whatever information i enter i then get

5:09:35 they might then want to go back to the

5:09:40 show up as being claimed at any stage of
5:09:42 this search process the user should be

5:09:48 to the previous screen collectively
5:09:50 these screens form another section that
5:09:53 all to do with searching for a lost pet
5:09:56 so we could color code those so we know

5:10:00 is going to help with our visual

5:10:27 and haven't found it it can help your

5:11:04 even though there's a lot of user

5:13:25 much more at visual research and look
5:13:28 and feel so far we've developed our idea
5:13:31 we've began to test our idea and we
5:13:33 began to map our idea now we need to
5:13:36 take all of these to the next stage we
5:13:39 need to develop a more definitive site
5:13:41 map we need to develop wireframes that

5:13:48 in the real world you might be working

5:13:55 have a site map wireframes and visual
5:13:58 direction
5:13:59 our sitemap is basically a more
5:14:01 developed version of our content map our
5:14:04 wireframes are generic sketches showing
5:14:06 what goes where on each frame and our
5:14:08 visual direction is concerned with the
5:14:11 design and aesthetics of our user
5:14:13 interface developing any one of these
5:14:15 areas is going to have an effect on the
5:14:18 other two
5:14:19 it's hard to create wireframes without
5:14:21 having a sitemap and knowing what it is
5:14:23 you're creating or without having some

5:14:28 know which elements are going where in

5:14:33 is the most important followed by the
5:14:35 wireframes because i really need to have

5:14:39 making my wireframes
5:14:41 and with digital tools you might be
5:14:43 developing your sitemap and your
5:14:45 wireframes at the same time visual
5:14:48 direction is often the thing that's left
5:14:50 until the end but because of my graphic
5:14:53 design background and my design centric
5:14:55 approach to ui ux i'm actually going to
5:14:58 look at the visual direction first
5:15:01 partly because i want the visual
5:15:03 direction to have more impact on my
5:15:05 wireframes and my sitemap and my
5:15:08 eventual app and also because we haven't
5:15:11 touched on the area of visual direction
5:15:13 yet within the process that we've been
5:15:15 following we've done various exercises
5:15:18 that have contributed to our site map
5:15:20 and to our wireframes our content map
5:15:23 and user flowchart will form the basis
5:15:26 of our sitemap i use a flowchart and our
5:15:28 non-visual paper prototype will inform
5:15:31 our wireframe design
5:15:33 but there's nothing so far to help
5:15:35 inform our visual direction the only
5:15:38 thing we've really done that involved
5:15:40 looking at visual direction or ui is to
5:15:43 look at our competition so we've seen
5:15:45 what other people have done in terms of
5:15:47 visual direction but we haven't really
5:15:50 done anything ourselves
5:15:52 and indeed our visual direction is much
5:15:54 more of a ui than a ux problem and i
5:15:57 cover this in much more detail in week 2
5:16:00 of visual elements of ui design so let's
5:16:03 do a fast recap on how to develop a
5:16:05 visual direction we're not trying to
5:16:08 figure out any details to the user
5:16:10 interface design here we're really
5:16:12 looking at the big picture we're trying
5:16:14 to envision what our overall design
5:16:17 direction is what the overall look and
5:16:19 feel of our app is going to be
5:16:22 and we're doing this fairly early in our
5:16:24 process
5:16:25 we're doing this before we get into the
5:16:27 details of design or before we get into
5:16:30 production but mostly before we commit
5:16:32 to a format or a structure for our app i
5:16:36 want the design to help shape our app
5:16:38 even at this early stage we're doing the
5:16:40 same thing with design that we're doing
5:16:42 with ux we're trying to imagine and
5:16:45 envision the design and the experience
5:16:48 before it actually exists we're trying
5:16:51 to visualize the invisible
5:16:53 and i'm forcing design into the equation
5:16:55 here because i'm a firm believer that
5:16:57 design can make the difference between a
5:16:59 successful and unsuccessful app
5:17:02 so let's quickly run through the example
5:17:04 that i used in the ui course here i was
5:17:07 working with the idea of a digital
5:17:09 microscope for amateur scientists that
5:17:12 utilized the camera on your phone and i
5:17:14 wanted to visualize that in two
5:17:16 different directions one that was much
5:17:19 more fun and one that was a little bit
5:17:21 more serious it's easy to see that those
5:17:23 two different directions would focus on
5:17:26 different audiences but they might also
5:17:28 fundamentally affect what the app is and
5:17:31 how it works it could affect the entire
5:17:34 structure of the app so it would affect
5:17:36 the site map and it would affect the
5:17:38 wireframe as well
5:17:40 in developing visual direction one of
5:17:42 the first things that you do is visual
5:17:45 research so you're basically collecting
5:17:47 a series of images that reflect the
5:17:50 content and the attitude and the look
5:17:53 and feel of your app so i have a digital
5:17:56 microscope for amateur scientists and i
5:17:58 want it to be fun so i'd collect images
5:18:01 that tried to reflect that i might want
5:18:04 to use illustrations rather than
5:18:05 photographs i'd be looking at both form
5:18:08 and content that worked with my idea i
5:18:11 could think about typefaces and a color
5:18:14 palette that would work with my idea as
5:18:16 well
5:18:17 and i could gather this visual research
5:18:19 into a digital space or into an analog
5:18:22 space the next stage would be to take my
5:18:24 visual references and put them together
5:18:27 in some kind of composition that would
5:18:29 give me the look and feel of my app i
5:18:32 might not use any of these individual
5:18:34 elements in my final design my goal here
5:18:38 is to create the mood of my app so this
5:18:41 mood board looks playful fun energetic
5:18:44 and probably for a younger audience by
5:18:47 contrast my serious direction should
5:18:50 obviously look much more scientific more
5:18:52 realistic because it's for a more
5:18:54 serious audience so while some of the
5:18:57 content is the same as my fun direction
5:19:00 it clearly has a very different look and
5:19:02 feel even at the research stage where
5:19:05 i'm just pulling images and again i'd
5:19:08 take my research and compose it into a
5:19:10 mood board and this feels very different
5:19:13 from our fun direction it feels much
5:19:15 more scientific for a more mature
5:19:18 audience it's much more photographically
5:19:20 based so it feels functional and real so
5:19:24 how this app functions and how it's
5:19:26 structured the very app itself could be
5:19:29 very very different from our first
5:19:31 version you can see that the design
5:19:34 direction can really affect the entire
5:19:36 app

5:20:36 in this coming week we're going to focus

5:20:40 user interface we're going to be taking
5:20:43 a giant step towards making our app seem
5:20:46 real we're going to be committing to a
5:20:48 structure and a design so let's look and
5:20:51 see where we are in our process so far
5:20:54 if you remember we broke our process
5:20:56 into three sections and we're in the
5:20:58 middle section right now we've developed
5:21:00 our ideas but we're not quite ready to
5:21:03 build a prototype we've been envisioning
5:21:05 our idea and starting to give it a
5:21:08 visual form we've successfully gone
5:21:10 through the first stage of our
5:21:12 envisioning process where we looked at
5:21:14 visual development paper prototyping
5:21:16 developing ideas and mapping content and
5:21:19 now we're moving into our second stage
5:21:22 we'll be looking at creating a set of
5:21:24 wireframes we'll be trying to create
5:21:26 functionality and logic that's
5:21:28 consistent and we'll be working on our
5:21:30 visual refinement and the continuity of
5:21:33 our entire app
