5:21:37 pieces and to prepare us for moving on
5:21:40 to the next stage where we're going to
5:21:42 create a prototype
5:21:44 in that last stage we'll be simulating
5:21:46 the final experience and creating final
5:21:49 designs
5:21:50 the stage we're in right now is geared
5:21:53 towards helping us with that final stage
5:21:56 our project is becoming more refined and
5:21:58 more concrete we're moving out of the
5:22:01 development stage and into a stage of
5:22:04 refinement
5:22:05 and this means less dramatic change is
5:22:07 going to happen with the main ideas in
5:22:10 our app we're going to become slightly
5:22:12 less flexible in what we're doing we've
5:22:15 committed to an idea a structure and a
5:22:18 form for our app but that doesn't mean
5:22:20 we're in production mode yet you need to
5:22:23 stay critical about the work that you're
5:22:25 making there's still the possibility to
5:22:28 make small changes and to shape your app
5:22:30 to improve it but those changes will be
5:22:33 smaller the further we get in this
5:22:35 process at this stage it's much more
5:22:38 about refinement and resolution as we
5:22:40 move towards our final goal of the
5:22:42 prototype
5:22:44 before we start making the form of our
5:22:46 wireframes let's look at some simple
5:22:49 theoretical guidelines that might help
5:22:51 us
5:22:52 if you look online many ui ux designers
5:22:55 will cite jacob nielsen's 10 rules of
5:22:58 user interface design and while they're
5:23:00 a little outdated and apply more to
5:23:02 interface design for software programs
5:23:05 there's still a lot of really great
5:23:07 foundational and fundamental information
5:23:10 in there for anyone designing an
5:23:11 interface and if a set of rules has
5:23:14 managed to hang around in the shifting
5:23:16 digital landscape for nearly 30 years
5:23:19 you know there's got to be some truth in
5:23:21 them
5:23:22 so let's look at nielsen's 10 rules and
5:23:24 we'll try and unpack them number one is
5:23:26 the visibility of the system status all
5:23:29 this means is let the user know what's
5:23:31 going on let the user know that the app
5:23:34 is live and working
5:23:36 number two match between the system and
5:23:39 the real world
5:23:40 what nielsen means by this is to use
5:23:43 words and forms that the user already
5:23:45 understands
5:23:46 from the real world in other words don't
5:23:49 make the user learn a whole new language
5:23:52 for the interface
5:23:53 use symbols and icons that already have
5:23:55 meaning attached to them from the real
5:23:57 world number three user control and
5:24:00 freedom what this really means is that
5:24:03 we all make mistakes so it's important
5:24:05 to make sure that the user can have
5:24:07 either undos or redos
5:24:09 or in the case of an app more likely
5:24:11 that they'll need to backtrack to an
5:24:13 earlier screen
5:24:15 number four
5:24:16 consistency and standards
5:24:19 what nielsen means here is just to use
5:24:21 repetition
5:24:22 consistency and similarity to help speed
5:24:25 things up within the interface and to
5:24:27 help develop intuition in the user so
5:24:30 you only have to learn how to work the
5:24:32 interface once after that the rest of it
5:24:35 should make sense
5:24:36 number five error prevention
5:24:39 so don't create situations where
5:24:41 mistakes happen
5:24:42 and if the user does make a mistake make
5:24:45 sure that you give them an easy way out
5:24:47 you want the user's interaction with the
5:24:49 interface to be easy and seamless
5:24:52 number six recognition rather than
5:24:55 recall so don't make the interface hard
5:24:58 work for the user the user should be
5:25:00 able to see the part of the interface
5:25:02 that they're going to interact with and
5:25:04 recognize immediately that that's the
5:25:06 place the point or site of interaction
5:25:10 so the user is reacting immediately
5:25:12 rather than going through a series of
5:25:14 remembered actions
5:25:16 number seven flexibility and efficiency
5:25:19 of use
5:25:20 think of your interface like a car
5:25:22 some users are going to want to drive
5:25:24 fast and some are going to want to go
5:25:26 slow but they're both going to want to
5:25:28 get to their destination with as little
5:25:30 fuss as possible
5:25:32 number eight
5:25:34 aesthetic and minimalist design
5:25:36 this is a familiar modernist rule you
5:25:39 don't want any unnecessary information
5:25:42 distracting you from the necessary
5:25:44 information
5:25:45 in interface design the familiar phrase
5:25:48 less is more often applies
5:25:51 number nine
5:25:52 help users recognize diagnose and
5:25:55 recover from errors and all this really
5:25:57 means is that if there's a problem
5:25:59 provide the best solution for the user
5:26:02 quickly and easily if you solve the
5:26:04 problem quickly it's like it never
5:26:06 happened
5:26:07 and finally number 10 help and
5:26:09 documentation you should provide help
5:26:12 for the user but keep it hidden until
5:26:14 they need it nobody wants instructions
5:26:16 that they don't really need so let's try
5:26:19 and keep these broader interface rules
5:26:21 in the back of our mind as we're working
5:26:24 on our wireframes
5:26:26 in this segment we're going to try and
5:26:28 answer the question what goes where in
5:26:31 our app we're going to start to look at
5:26:33 how to make wireframes in the most
5:26:36 recent stage in our process we developed
5:26:38 a site map and we'll be using this as a
5:26:41 roadmap to create our wireframes which
5:26:43 will then allow us to make a prototype
5:26:46 our sitemap ended up being a container
5:26:49 for everything we'd done before that our
5:26:51 content map our user flowchart and our
5:26:54 non-visual paper prototype
5:26:56 all gave us important information that
5:26:58 contributed to our site map now we're
5:27:01 going to take all of that information in
5:27:03 our sitemap and put it into a slightly
5:27:05 different format a more developed format
5:27:08 a series of wireframes our wireframes
5:27:11 are an in-between phase between our
5:27:13 sitemap and our prototype our wireframes
5:27:16 are more visually developed than our
5:27:18 sitemap but their main job is to map
5:27:21 what the user does when to look at a
5:27:23 hierarchy of activities and to map out
5:27:26 the user experience flow and logic in
5:27:28 other words what goes where
5:27:30 our wireframes are normally functional
5:27:33 minimal consistent and user centric
5:27:37 our wireframes will point more directly
5:27:39 towards a finished form but they're
5:27:42 still not the finished form themselves
5:27:45 don't get caught up in details in your
5:27:47 wireframe don't be too fussy or obsess
5:27:49 about small elements personally i'd even
5:27:52 avoid using a wireframe program at this
5:27:55 point i prefer to draw things myself on
5:27:58 the computer but it's entirely up to you
5:28:01 but you should bear in mind that your
5:28:02 wireframes need to be simple and clear
5:28:05 you want to create an accurate blueprint
5:28:08 for your prototype
5:28:10 when we last saw our app it was at the
5:28:12 stage of the site map
5:28:14 we'd thought through our idea and mapped
5:28:17 it out quite accurately but we'd used
5:28:20 words as a way to create the interface
5:28:23 and while we might keep a similar
5:28:25 structure for our wireframes how we
5:28:27 represent the interface is going to
5:28:30 change we're going to start to visually
5:28:32 indicate the interface we're going to
5:28:34 try and show the structure or skeleton
5:28:37 of our interface and you can do that in
5:28:39 many different ways again if you search
5:28:41 online for wireframes you'll get a wide
5:28:44 range of visual solutions
5:28:47 and you can visually indicate the
5:28:48 interface in many different ways you
5:28:51 could sketch it by hand or on the
5:28:53 computer use a limited palette so use
5:28:57 geometric shapes and lines to represent
5:28:59 your interface
5:29:00 think about how to use scale as
5:29:02 hierarchy and don't get lost in details
5:29:05 of any individual interface elements
5:29:08 using black and white or gray reducing
5:29:11 your color palette can help keep that
5:29:14 simplicity
5:29:15 think about where your elements are
5:29:16 going to live on the screen and reuse
5:29:19 your elements from screen to screen as
5:29:21 well it can help to use some existing
5:29:24 norms of interface design so you can use
5:29:26 simple generic interface elements that
5:29:29 the user is already familiar with
5:29:31 and even though our changes aren't going
5:29:33 to be so big
5:29:35 remember that we're still developing and
5:29:37 refining our app and you might discover
5:29:39 things while you make this wireframe
5:29:41 that work really well or things that
5:29:43 don't work really well and you can still
5:29:45 change them at this stage it's all part
5:29:48 of the process of growing and developing
5:29:51 your app and your interface let's start
5:29:53 out by making our easiest screen the
5:29:56 splash or identity screen i haven't
5:29:58 developed my branding yet so i'm going
5:30:00 to make a generic logo type for my
5:30:03 company my final logo type won't look
5:30:05 like this but we can look at this as a
5:30:07 generic form and we all know that it's a
5:30:10 stand-in logo type next we have our
5:30:12 login screen if we were being logged in
5:30:15 automatically we'd go straight to a map
5:30:18 the app would read your location and
5:30:20 display the map straight away and this
5:30:22 is the first time we've given any kind
5:30:24 of visual form to our main screen we can
5:30:27 use a generic map but we might also
5:30:29 think about what that map is going to
5:30:31 show
5:30:32 perhaps some indication of lost and
5:30:34 found animals if the user has to log in
5:30:37 manually they get taken to another
5:30:39 screen where they'd have to type in
5:30:42 their name and a password to create an
5:30:44 account and in my notes here i've
5:30:46 identified this as an area to test with
5:30:48 users because i'm unsure about it but
5:30:51 even on this simple login screen i'm
5:30:54 starting to create a visual logic so a
5:30:57 live text field is represented by a
5:31:00 white rectangle with a thin black
5:31:02 outline whereas a button is represented
5:31:05 by a solid gray rectangle with no
5:31:07 outline and once i establish this visual
5:31:10 logic i'm going to have to stick to that
5:31:12 same logic throughout my entire
5:31:14 wireframe if i'm asking the user to type
5:31:17 in their name and a password in a live
5:31:19 text field i'm going to need a keypad
5:31:22 to match current conventions my keypad
5:31:25 is going to pop up from the bottom and
5:31:27 it's going to look like a standard
5:31:29 generic keypad i don't even need to give
5:31:31 it too much detail there just has to be
5:31:34 enough visual information that you
5:31:35 recognize it as a keypad i also want the
5:31:38 user to have the option to log in with
5:31:40 google or facebook so that should come
5:31:43 before they hit the create account
5:31:44 button it would also make sense if those
5:31:47 two options were separate with a button
5:31:49 for each even though i'm using generic
5:31:51 forms i'm starting to make ui and ux
5:31:54 decisions as i build my wireframe my
5:31:57 next screen is the create account screen
5:32:00 this can use my same visual logic from
5:32:03 earlier with certain kinds of boxes
5:32:05 indicating certain kinds of
5:32:07 interactivity i could also add a
5:32:09 checkbox here to automatically allow my
5:32:12 app to access the user's location
5:32:15 this functionality is integral to how my
5:32:18 app is going to operate so i would
5:32:20 really encourage the user to have this
5:32:22 box checked so it would come up as
5:32:24 automatically being checked straight
5:32:26 away for instance and may have a warning
5:32:28 dialog box if you uncheck it it's
5:32:31 important that you recognize anything
5:32:33 that is crucial to the functionality of
5:32:35 your app and figure out a way to keep it
5:32:38 at the top of your ux hierarchy that
5:32:41 doesn't mean it has to be the largest
5:32:42 element but it should be one that
5:32:44 everyone encounters and you encourage
5:32:46 everyone to interact with let's pretend
5:32:49 that we're all logged in now and let's
5:32:51 start to look at our home screen i
5:32:53 decided that my app was going to use gps
5:32:56 information and display a map of
5:32:59 wherever the user was
5:33:01 so when you open my app you're going to
5:33:03 automatically have a map showing your
5:33:05 location and that map can pre-empt what
5:33:08 the user might want to look for so it
5:33:10 can automatically show them in this case
5:33:13 pets that are lost and pets that have
5:33:15 been found in the area of this
5:33:18 particular map if i put myself in the
5:33:20 position of the user this kind of
5:33:22 information would be much more useful if
5:33:25 i could see what kind of animal was
5:33:27 actually lost or found
5:33:29 and if i had lost an animal one of the
5:33:31 first things that i want to do would be
5:33:33 to get more information to see if the
5:33:36 animal that's found on this screen was
5:33:38 actually my animal so i could tap on
5:33:40 that icon and get more details about
5:33:43 that particular animal now i figured out
5:33:46 what the user wants to do i have to
5:33:48 think about how the user does that in my
5:33:50 interface a little bit more perhaps
5:33:53 there's not enough room in this smaller
5:33:55 window for me to see an image of my
5:33:57 animal or to read all the details i
5:34:00 could have a larger window that comes in
5:34:02 from the side but this takes up a lot of
5:34:04 real estate and it blocks a lot of
5:34:06 information in my map so it makes it
5:34:08 hard to go between the map and the
5:34:10 information a better solution might be
5:34:13 to put those details at the bottom of
5:34:15 the screen and that would still allow me
5:34:17 an area of the map that was very visible
5:34:20 and functional once again i'm developing
5:34:23 and refining my app as i'm making my app
5:34:26 is still going to develop even at this
5:34:28 wireframe stage and because of that
5:34:31 sometimes it's useful to work in a more
5:34:34 reductive kind of way which could even
5:34:36 be a simple computer sketch or a sketch
5:34:39 by hand i like my wireframes to be both
5:34:42 generic and to have some connection to
5:34:44 what i think the finished form is going
5:34:46 to be but that might depend on the speed
5:34:48 of your computer skills
5:34:50 let's go back to our main menu and look
5:34:53 at some other things that might happen
5:34:54 on that screen if i'm not going to
5:34:57 engage with what the app automatically
5:34:59 feeds me i'm going to want to make my
5:35:01 own decision and choose what the app
5:35:03 does next i need to have my three main
5:35:06 options housed somewhere and it makes
5:35:09 sense for those to be the main menu and
5:35:12 hidden away until i need them
5:35:14 let's pick one of these three options
5:35:16 and see how that works in a wireframe
5:35:19 let's pretend that we want to search for
5:35:21 a lost pet if we choose that option
5:35:24 we're going to have to enter several
5:35:25 different pieces of information
5:35:28 we need to be consistent with the visual
5:35:30 language that we've already used and we
5:35:32 also need to think about exactly what
5:35:34 we're asking the user to enter so name
5:35:38 description species or breed and
5:35:40 location all seem pretty solid we could
5:35:43 also have a checkbox to use the current
5:35:45 location which would be useful if you
5:35:48 were actively searching in the real
5:35:49 world i'll need a keyboard at the bottom
5:35:52 of the screen to enter my information
5:35:55 and that's going to cover up some of the
5:35:56 fields so i'm going to have to figure
5:35:58 out a system where they can move up the
5:36:00 screen once i've submitted the
5:36:03 information for my search the results
5:36:05 would come up on the main menu map
5:36:08 and as before it would be much more
5:36:10 useful if i could see the names of the
5:36:12 animals
5:36:13 and just like before i could get details
5:36:16 about each animal by tapping on them and
5:36:19 here i'd want to think about all the
5:36:21 conventions that i've already used where
5:36:23 my window is going to appear and what
5:36:25 kind of information it's going to
5:36:27 contain because i've been in a very
5:36:29 similar situation before when i worked
5:36:32 on the home screen initially my previous
5:36:35 solution was to put all of the
5:36:37 information in the pop-up window at the
5:36:39 bottom of the screen to allow a larger
5:36:42 space for the map still to be visible
5:36:44 but since i'm carrying out a slightly
5:36:46 different activity here i might want my
5:36:49 interface to look and feel a little bit
5:36:52 different it could be very similar but
5:36:55 also slightly different
5:36:57 these kind of decisions could be driven
5:36:59 by looking at how the user might want to
5:37:02 behave in these slightly different
5:37:04 scenarios the window on the right might
5:37:06 be great if you don't want to look at
5:37:08 the map but you want to flip through
5:37:10 several animals quite quickly i think
5:37:12 this is a good example that illustrates
5:37:15 how the design is growing and changing
5:37:17 and expanding as we're building our
5:37:19 wireframe in this segment we're going to
5:37:22 continue to look at working with
5:37:23 wireframes we're going to focus a little
5:37:26 bit more on consistency and details
5:37:29 we've spent quite a lot of time making
5:37:32 wireframes so now we're going to make
5:37:35 more wireframes because wireframes are
5:37:38 really important but we'll be making
5:37:40 some design decisions too we'll be
5:37:42 trying to make our wireframes consistent
5:37:45 and that consistency is in areas of
5:37:47 interaction navigation functionality
5:37:51 logic and design and these are all areas
5:37:54 that we've looked at in previous classes
5:37:57 and we might have to make a number of
5:37:59 wireframe screens to be able to compare
5:38:01 them before we can get to this kind of
5:38:03 consistency let's look at some of the
5:38:06 wireframe screens that we built in the
5:38:08 last class
5:38:09 we had three different options for how
5:38:12 our menu might appear over the top of
5:38:14 the map
5:38:15 if these windows were all having the
5:38:17 same function
5:38:18 searching for a lost pet they'd just be
5:38:21 design options they'd be variations of
5:38:24 the same thing
5:38:26 and if that was the case i'd argue that
5:38:28 the middle one was the least functional
5:38:31 it doesn't do a good job of getting out
5:38:33 of the way of the map but it also
5:38:35 doesn't do a good job of forming a
5:38:37 window where i can flick through
5:38:39 different pets
5:38:41 it seems the most dysfunctional so let's
5:38:44 see what happens when we take that away
5:38:46 and we change the function of these
5:38:48 screens let's look at our wireframe
5:38:51 design for our home screen and compare
5:38:53 that with our search screen
5:38:55 you can see that our home screen is very
5:38:58 similar to the search screen on the left
5:39:00 but different from the one on the right
5:39:02 and obviously we don't need two
5:39:05 different designs for a search screen we
5:39:07 have to pick one of them
5:39:09 so how are we going to decide which one
5:39:11 is our home screen and which one is our
5:39:13 search screen
5:39:15 one way to decide which search screen to
5:39:17 use would be to think about how the user
5:39:20 is going to use these screens what is
5:39:22 the user's priority going to be
5:39:25 on the left it feels more like you're
5:39:27 searching for a specific pet because
5:39:29 you're able to see those pets names on
5:39:31 the map
5:39:32 whereas on the right it's more visually
5:39:34 driven the pet's photo is larger and
5:39:37 you're more likely to be swiping through
5:39:39 a number of different photos on our home
5:39:42 screen the user's priority is browsing
5:39:45 it's much more of a general
5:39:47 multi-functional map
5:39:49 so we have different functions here
5:39:52 but everything looks pretty much the
5:39:54 same
5:39:55 so the question is is it too much the
5:39:58 same or is it the right level of
5:40:00 consistency you want your screens to
5:40:02 look like they belong to the same family
5:40:04 but you don't want them to look exactly
5:40:06 the same
5:40:08 if everything is too consistent the
5:40:10 experience is very flat and that's a
5:40:13 turn off for the user if we look at our
5:40:15 search for screen and our home screen
5:40:18 they're very very similar one could
5:40:20 argue that this could create user
5:40:22 confusion the user might not know which
5:40:26 part of the app they're operating in so
5:40:28 you may need a graphic device such as
5:40:30 coloring or labeling to accentuate that
5:40:33 differentiation
5:40:35 if we keep our home screen and pair it
5:40:37 with the other search screen design we
5:40:39 can see that these are different enough
5:40:41 that they look like they have different
5:40:43 functions but they look related they
5:40:46 work as siblings and the benefit of this
5:40:49 is that the user knows that they're in
5:40:50 your app because it has a clear identity
5:40:53 but they also know that they're pursuing
5:40:56 a different activity or a different
5:40:58 action
5:40:59 so as you're making different screens
5:41:01 for different areas of your app you
5:41:03 should try and get the balance between
5:41:06 difference and consistency just right
5:41:09 too different and you'll lose your app's
5:41:11 identity too much the same and you'll
5:41:14 create a flat and boring experience
5:41:17 it's useful to go back to our site map
5:41:19 and look at how we color coded different
5:41:22 areas or sections you could think of
5:41:24 these sections as being siblings my
5:41:27 section that deals with logging in might
5:41:29 look very different from my sections
5:41:31 that deal with searching but my post
5:41:34 lost and post found sections will be
5:41:36 pretty much the same and i want all of
5:41:38 my screens to have some visual
5:41:41 continuity so they feel like they all
5:41:43 belong together in one app
5:41:46 we've looked a little bit at consistency
5:41:49 so now let's look at details
5:41:51 we're actually looking at detail in
5:41:53 exactly the same areas interaction
5:41:56 navigation functionality logic and
5:41:59 design
5:42:00 just keep in mind that we're not talking
5:42:02 about details of the interface design
5:42:05 here
5:42:06 we're not quite at the stage where we're
5:42:08 going to refine the form of the
5:42:10 interface we'll be doing that soon
5:42:12 though
5:42:13 so let's take this same wireframe and
5:42:16 render it in an even simpler form that
5:42:19 way the formal design elements can be
5:42:21 ignored a little bit
5:42:23 as we've done many times before let's
5:42:26 break the interface down into all of its
5:42:28 pieces when you do that you can ask
5:42:31 yourself if each aspect of the interface
5:42:33 is working to its best potential from
5:42:36 large to small
5:42:38 and because we're working with
5:42:39 wireframes and not investing a lot of
5:42:42 time in the form of the interface we can
5:42:45 pull those interface elements around
5:42:47 quite quickly and easily and we can make
5:42:49 options and then decide which ones are
5:42:52 better we could decide the proportions
5:42:54 and size of our bottom menu
5:42:56 we could decide that we don't need
5:42:58 arrows to travel through things here
5:43:01 because the information is above in the
5:43:02 map
5:43:03 we could also decide we don't need to
5:43:05 close this particular window we could
5:43:07 just slide it up and down
5:43:10 in our map area up above we could see
5:43:12 what happens if we had icons or symbols
5:43:15 for our lost animals so even though
5:43:17 we're not getting caught up in design
5:43:19 details we're still making some
5:43:21 decisions about design
5:43:24 so when we're looking at consistency and
5:43:26 details we're looking at these two areas
5:43:29 in both our wireframe map and in the
5:43:32 beginnings of our user interface design
5:43:34 the wireframe map makes it easy to
5:43:36 compare screen to screen
5:43:39 whereas if we take any individual screen
5:43:41 we can look at the user interface design
5:43:44 and start to develop that a little bit
5:43:46 more when we put both of these things
5:43:48 back together we're heading towards more
5:43:50 finished static screens and these are
5:43:53 the screens that we're going to use to
5:43:54 build our digital prototype
5:43:58 here we are at the third and final part
5:44:00 of working with wireframes in this
5:44:03 segment we're going to look at how to
5:44:05 make a wireframe map
5:44:07 in our first section we made some
5:44:09 wireframes and in our second section we
5:44:11 refined those wireframes and now we're
5:44:14 going to take them and we're going to
5:44:16 put them into a wireframe map
5:44:18 and we've already got a blueprint for
5:44:20 this wireframe map which is our sitemap
5:44:23 so this can form the basis for our
5:44:26 wireframe map but some things have
5:44:28 probably changed when we were making the
5:44:30 wireframes so it's not going to be an
5:44:32 exact replica
5:44:34 so here are some of my more refined
5:44:37 wireframe screens but i'm going to need
5:44:39 more than just three i'm going to have
5:44:41 to make a screen of pretty much every
5:44:44 interaction that the user has in order
5:44:46 to make my wireframe map
5:44:48 so these would range from our identity
5:44:51 screen at the beginning to our main
5:44:53 navigation screen
5:44:55 login screens search screens and results
5:44:58 screens it's quite useful to put all
5:45:00 your screens side by side before you
5:45:02 make the sitemap because you can check
5:45:04 for inconsistencies
5:45:06 there might be elements that are missing
5:45:08 from certain screens for instance i
5:45:11 might need a menu on some of my screens
5:45:13 so that i could escape them and get back
5:45:15 to the previous screen if i needed to i
5:45:18 may not have been thinking about that
5:45:20 menu when i designed these earlier
5:45:22 screens i might also have just made some
5:45:24 silly mistakes there's some confusion
5:45:27 and inconsistency on these screens about
5:45:29 the positioning of the password and
5:45:31 address fields you could also start to
5:45:34 think a little bit more about navigation
5:45:36 and about how your menus are going to
5:45:38 move how will they appear and disappear
5:45:41 for instance so i might want my map to
5:45:43 be movable in every direction i'd want
5:45:46 my keyboard to pop up from the bottom of
5:45:49 the screen
5:45:50 and then to go back down and hide at the
5:45:52 bottom of the screen as well when i have
5:45:54 too much information i want that
5:45:57 information to be able to slide
5:45:59 underneath my menu elements at the top
5:46:02 my smaller menus might reveal themselves
5:46:05 with a diagonal movement from the corner
5:46:07 i might also want to swipe through a
5:46:10 series of different animals on my lost
5:46:12 and found pages when you feel that your
5:46:14 screens are resolved you have to decide
5:46:17 what kind of layout you're going to use
5:46:20 what kind of wireframe layout is going
5:46:22 to best represent the way that your app
5:46:24 works try to organize your screens in a
5:46:28 simple and logical fashion
5:46:30 but also take into account your specific
5:46:33 content there might be a special way of
5:46:36 organizing your screens that really
5:46:38 works for your app we can look at some
5:46:40 structural compositions that might
5:46:42 reflect this
5:46:44 rather than laying out all of your
5:46:45 screens in an even-handed fashion you
5:46:48 could create a pyramid structure for
5:46:50 instance this might imply one single
5:46:53 important screen with two layers of
5:46:55 interaction following on from it this
5:46:58 layout isolates the home screen but
5:47:00 shows us two fairly even areas of
5:47:03 interaction while this layout might
5:47:05 represent six areas of interaction all
5:47:09 even and centered around our home screen
5:47:12 when you decide how to layout your
5:47:14 wireframe app you could use the same
5:47:16 areas of organization that you used for
5:47:19 your sitemap for instance i had a login
5:47:22 and sign up section a number of
5:47:24 activities that i could do on my home
5:47:26 screen and then separate areas my three
5:47:29 main categories of activity
5:47:31 and as with our site map and user flow
5:47:34 chart we could connect these screens in
5:47:37 a way that we think a user is going to
5:47:39 travel through them
5:47:40 so we might end up with an
5:47:42 organizational structure that is very
5:47:45 similar to our site map but it's also a
5:47:48 much closer and more accurate
5:47:50 representation of both the ux experience
5:47:53 and the ui experience
5:47:56 you can keep expanding your wireframe
5:47:58 map to show all of the wireframes and
5:48:00 all of the sections that you've designed
5:48:03 to show the whole functionality of your
5:48:05 app and you can also print out each of
5:48:08 these screens and do another round of
5:48:11 user testing using your wireframe
5:48:13 designs and this would function in the
5:48:16 same way as our earlier paper prototype
5:48:19 where the tester would be giving options
5:48:21 to the user and the user would be making
5:48:23 decisions about how they travel through
5:48:26 the app
5:48:27 the tester would be observing what works
5:48:29 for the user and also what doesn't work
5:48:32 in reality this would probably be the
5:48:34 stage in your process where you carried
5:48:37 out your main phase of paper prototyping
5:48:40 and you'd be doing that with a number of
5:48:42 different users you'd be gathering
5:48:44 feedback from all of those users
5:48:47 collating and evaluating that
5:48:49 information making revisions to your
5:48:51 wireframes reworking them and then
5:48:54 putting them back out there for testing
5:48:56 and retesting and retesting
5:49:00 this testing phase works in a cyclical
5:49:02 way and one that can be repeated many
5:49:05 times
5:49:06 and hopefully each time you test and
5:49:08 change it makes your app better so you'd
5:49:11 be testing your wireframes with sample
5:49:14 users observing their interactions and
5:49:17 reactions
5:49:18 evaluating the results and then
5:49:21 implementing whichever of those results
5:49:23 you deemed valuable then you'd make new
5:49:26 wireframes and start this cycle all over
5:49:29 again
5:49:30 this part of our app development process
5:49:32 is optional here if you have the time
5:49:35 and if you have access to a willing
5:49:37 group of people family and friends for
5:49:39 instance then try it or once more you
5:49:42 could role play and you could act as if
5:49:45 you were the ideal user and try and give
5:49:47 yourself feedback on your own app
5:49:50 just like our wireframes are a more
5:49:52 developed version of our paper prototype
5:49:54 this round of user testing should be
5:49:56 more developed too so try to simulate
5:49:59 the actual experience of your app use
5:50:02 the frame of a phone printed out with
5:50:04 your designs inside those screens at 100
5:50:07 of scale
5:50:08 try and present the screens to a user
5:50:10 one at a time
5:50:12 and while you're making an analog
5:50:14 version of a digital experience the
5:50:16 experience part can still be quite
5:50:18 similar but remember not every
5:50:21 individual user is going to give you
5:50:23 useful feedback so evaluate the feedback
5:50:26 and see if it's worth implementing and
5:50:28 you don't have to have 50 users to get
5:50:30 good results tests have shown that 5
5:50:34 users will give you the same feedback as
5:50:36 50 so while the volume of the feedback
5:50:39 changes the actual feedback doesn't so
5:50:42 if you can find five people to test your
5:50:44 wireframe on that would be fantastic
5:50:47 in this section we're going to focus on
5:50:49 visual direction we've been looking at
5:50:52 wireframes for quite a long time and now
5:50:54 we're going to concentrate on visual
5:50:56 development we're going to start
5:50:58 thinking about what the user interface
5:51:00 actually looks like for a short time
5:51:02 we'll be putting more emphasis on ui
5:51:05 than ux but of course they always remain
5:51:08 connected let's look at what we've
5:51:10 figured out about our app already
5:51:13 we've looked at our audience and defined
5:51:15 an ideal user we've developed our
5:51:17 content with a content map we've got a
5:51:20 more finished structure from our
5:51:21 wireframe map and we started to look at
5:51:24 some initial visual research
5:51:26 we've basically got a framework and
5:51:28 structure for our app that's pretty
5:51:31 functional and resolved we have a
5:51:33 skeleton or a foundation and now we need
5:51:36 to build on top of that and that
5:51:38 involves developing the visual side of
5:51:41 our user interface
5:51:43 we're going to look at this quite
5:51:44 quickly and we're going to do it in
5:51:46 three steps we're going to look at the
5:51:48 look and feel or the initial visual
5:51:50 direction that we touched on before but
5:51:52 specifically for this app we're going to
5:51:55 take those initial designs and try and
5:51:57 refine them a little bit to create a
5:51:59 more developed and specific user
5:52:02 interface design
5:52:03 and then we'll work on refining that
5:52:05 even more to create our final ui design
5:52:08 if you need a refresher course about
5:52:11 visual design and graphic design and the
5:52:13 way it works with user interface design
5:52:16 you could revisit visual elements of ui
5:52:18 design our first course and this will
5:52:21 give you a more expanded process than
5:52:23 we're going to look at here
5:52:25 so the first thing that we're going to
5:52:26 do in creating a visual look and feel is
5:52:30 to try and meet these goals we want to
5:52:32 establish a mood a tone and a visual
5:52:35 experience that's appropriate for our
5:52:38 audience but also for the kind of
5:52:40 content of our app so it's going to be
5:52:42 different depending on what your app is
5:52:45 and who your audience is
5:52:47 right now we have wireframe designs for
5:52:50 all of our screens so our interface
5:52:53 elements are organized and arranged but
5:52:56 they're quite generic and visually
5:52:58 undeveloped we want to give them a mood
5:53:01 and a character and an attitude that
5:53:03 works for our app but we don't want to
5:53:06 lose the functionality as well let's
5:53:08 take one of our wireframe screens and
5:53:10 develop it in two different directions
5:53:13 for two different potential audiences
5:53:16 we'll use two very simple directions fun
5:53:19 and serious the goal of the fun
5:53:21 direction is to be youthful positive
5:53:24 cartoonish hip casual while the serious
5:53:27 version wants to feel utilitarian
5:53:30 practical believable authoritative as
5:53:33 we've looked at before having a set of
5:53:36 adjectives to describe your visual goals
5:53:38 can be really useful we can express
5:53:40 those adjectives through color a fairly
5:53:43 serious and dull brown versus a vibrant
5:53:46 and fun green with just one move we've
5:53:49 already created visual difference
5:53:51 between our two directions
5:53:53 we could do the same thing with
5:53:54 typography as well on the fun side we
5:53:57 might use novelty or display typefaces
5:54:00 with exuberant form they could be curvy
5:54:03 lively bouncy and generally fun
5:54:07 our typography for our serious side
5:54:10 might be much more austere more regular
5:54:13 more consistent more about functionality
5:54:16 than about playfulness and we can use
5:54:18 that typographic direction to create a
5:54:21 logo type for our app or to create a bug
5:54:24 button or icon in this case lp for lost
5:54:27 pet in the shape of a tag that a pet
5:54:29 might wear we could do the same with our
5:54:32 fun typography but here the forms are
5:54:34 much more energetic and friendly i'm
5:54:37 also using naming and language to
5:54:39 reinforce the mood get pet is much more
5:54:42 friendly than lost pet so we have a
5:54:45 graphic palette right now of words
5:54:47 typography and color but we're also
5:54:50 going to need imagery our serious images
5:54:53 could be very photographic very
5:54:55 realistic they might represent real
5:54:57 individual pets ones that are lost or
5:55:00 perhaps ones that have been found
5:55:02 through our app but the images feel
5:55:04 functional and pragmatic and realistic
5:55:07 in opposition to that our fun direction
5:55:10 might use illustration much more with
5:55:13 illustrations we can make the animals
5:55:15 seem happy and positive and upbeat and
5:55:18 we can do the same for the owners as
5:55:20 well our illustrations don't have to
5:55:22 exist in rectangles they can be
5:55:24 free-floating and energetic and we could
5:55:27 easily use them to create icons or
5:55:29 buttons so now i have a mood board for
5:55:33 each of my different directions each of
5:55:35 my different moods and they look and
5:55:37 feel
5:55:38 pretty different so now i've collected
5:55:40 all of this information even though i
5:55:42 haven't actually made a design yet i can
5:55:45 start to decide which direction is going
5:55:48 to be the best one for my app which
5:55:50 direction i want to apply to my
5:55:52 wireframes
5:55:55 in this next section we're going to be
5:55:57 developing our user interface design
5:55:59 we're going to do that through a process
5:56:02 of decision making and refinement of
5:56:04 trying out designs and seeing which ones
5:56:07 work the best with our interface
5:56:09 so let's look at where we are in our
5:56:11 process
5:56:12 we made some mood boards to map out our
5:56:15 visual direction and now we're going to
5:56:17 take elements and themes from that
5:56:19 direction
5:56:20 and apply it to our wireframes to make a
5:56:23 user interface
5:56:24 we looked at two different visual
5:56:26 directions for our app
5:56:28 both of them provide a palette of
5:56:31 graphic elements that we can apply to
5:56:33 our wireframe i want my app to appeal to
5:56:37 a slightly younger audience and to be
5:56:39 more of a light-hearted experience so
5:56:41 i'm going to choose the visual direction
5:56:44 on the right i'm going to take this
5:56:46 graphic palette and apply it in a fast
5:56:49 and fairly rough way to some sample
5:56:51 screens i'm going to make different
5:56:54 design options because i want to test
5:56:56 out and also develop my design direction
5:56:59 and be able to try it out on the
5:57:01 interface
5:57:02 so let's apply our design palette to
5:57:05 some of our wireframe screens let's
5:57:07 start out with our simple branding page
5:57:10 to match my audience and the goals of my
5:57:12 app i probably want something that's a
5:57:14 little bit positive and upbeat right at
5:57:17 the start so i'm going to use my name
5:57:19 getpet and the typography that i was
5:57:22 looking at earlier it's fun and bouncy
5:57:25 and almost feels like a wagging tale i
5:57:27 might want an image to go with it that
5:57:29 reinforces some of those same attributes
5:57:32 but perhaps that looks too youthful now
5:57:35 perhaps i've gone away from my target
5:57:37 audience i want to keep an illustration
5:57:40 but perhaps a different style and maybe
5:57:42 just a pet and not a person i like this
5:57:45 dog because he looks happy like he's
5:57:48 just been found let's add some upbeat
5:57:51 colors to our screen to try and make it
5:57:53 look less like a wireframe as you're
5:57:55 building these screens and applying your
5:57:57 design to them try different things out
5:58:00 they might not always work but it's
5:58:02 important to test out all of your
5:58:04 options if i change my image to a sad
5:58:07 dog it immediately has a very different
5:58:09 mood and all i've changed here is just
5:58:12 the expression on the dog's face all the
5:58:15 other elements have stayed the same
5:58:17 and the happy dog is much more
5:58:19 appropriate for the mood of my app but i
5:58:21 might choose to not use an illustration
5:58:23 at all i might want to reinforce my
5:58:25 branding even more with some kind of
5:58:28 logo type or bug and i could think about
5:58:30 the scale relationship of my different
5:58:33 elements to each other and also to the
5:58:35 screen size
5:58:37 i can take my very upbeat color palette
5:58:39 and apply it to some of the other
5:58:41 elements like the map for instance
5:58:43 i can use my colors as a background
5:58:45 field for pop-up windows i might want to
5:58:48 change the shape of that window and i
5:58:51 definitely want to spend some time
5:58:53 rearranging the elements within that
5:58:55 space
5:58:56 i can try out some different typography
5:58:59 and also some different button designs
5:59:01 and if you take things too far or you
5:59:03 just don't like them you can always make
5:59:05 another version
5:59:07 here i might decide i need more contrast
5:59:09 between my background and my floating
5:59:11 window i can also put in real textual
5:59:14 information and real images to try and
5:59:16 make my interface look more real now
5:59:19 that i have designs for two screens i
5:59:22 should be able to extend that logic to a
5:59:24 third screen and have them work as a set
5:59:27 i can use the same coloring system for
5:59:29 my map and i can also take the bug from
5:59:32 the first screen and reuse that i can
5:59:34 use similar typography from my second
5:59:37 screen as well so even with just three
5:59:39 screens i'm already extending and
5:59:41 applying my design rather than making
5:59:44 three very different screens i can
5:59:47 extend the design due to functionality
5:59:49 here i might need red and green labels
5:59:52 to show lost and found for instance
5:59:54 i might want to highlight the area that
5:59:56 the user is searching in and make that
5:59:59 more visible so i'm negotiating between
6:00:02 functionality and aesthetics while
6:00:04 trying to keep my design on track for my
6:00:06 audience
6:00:07 in my wireframe sketches i was testing
6:00:10 out different ways to show what kind of
6:00:12 animal was lost and found so i'm going
6:00:14 to try and do that here by using icons
6:00:17 for the different animals i might want
6:00:19 some visual hierarchy to focus on the
6:00:21 key animal that's either lost or found
6:00:24 so i might treat that element slightly
6:00:26 differently so in quite a short time
6:00:29 i've managed to take my wireframes and
6:00:31 effectively dress them up with my design
6:00:34 aesthetic but design does much more than
6:00:37 just act as a skin or a surface it can
6:00:40 really change and alter the function and
6:00:43 the structure of your app
6:00:46 in this section we're going to further
6:00:48 refine our user interface design as well
6:00:51 as finessing the design we'll be looking
6:00:53 at continuity and making sure everything
6:00:56 works the same across different screens
6:00:58 and looking at state changes for some of
6:01:00 our interface elements
6:01:02 let's look at where we are in our ui
6:01:04 development process we created a general
6:01:07 direction we developed that visual
6:01:09 direction and now we're going to
6:01:11 finalize it and finesse it our goal here
6:01:15 is to make a series of refined and fully
6:01:17 resolved screens that we can use in our
6:01:20 digital prototype
6:01:22 so let's look at the three sample
6:01:24 screens that i was designing earlier we
6:01:26 left them in a state where they were
6:01:28 fairly resolved but could probably do
6:01:31 with another round of refinement
6:01:33 our branding on our first screen seems
6:01:36 much too large for the scale of the
6:01:38 screen so we could reduce that down we
6:01:41 might choose a slightly different color
6:01:43 palette as well so we're really looking
6:01:45 at small design moves here across all
6:01:48 areas of our interface design so try and
6:01:51 consider all the small details it might
6:01:54 be about the exact curvature of a
6:01:56 rounded corner or how much contrast
6:01:59 there is between a floating window and a
6:02:01 background or the exact color or
6:02:04 position of a button
6:02:06 and remember you're striking a balance
6:02:08 between how you use design to indicate
6:02:11 functionality within the interface and
6:02:13 how you create an idiosyncratic identity
6:02:17 a look and feel that's specific to your
6:02:19 app think about how your systems work
6:02:22 across different screens
6:02:24 for instance i might need one color
6:02:27 system in my maps to show lost and found
6:02:30 but another color system to reinforce my
6:02:32 branding and a third one to show
6:02:35 interactive elements or buttons and
6:02:37 those kind of decisions might be based
6:02:39 purely around functionality
6:02:42 but i'm also trying to create an
6:02:44 aesthetic for my app as well
6:02:46 if i shift the color palette in my map
6:02:49 for example it can have quite a dramatic
6:02:51 effect
6:02:52 on how my whole app feels
6:02:55 but that difference in contrast can also
6:02:58 have an effect on functionality i might
6:03:00 be able to see my buttons more clearly
6:03:03 with a different background or a
6:03:04 different foreground color
6:03:06 some moves might create quite a large
6:03:09 visual shift but you should also look at
6:03:11 the smaller elements the details of
6:03:14 every aspect of your interface
6:03:16 on my last screen for instance i might
6:03:18 look at the relationship between the
6:03:21 icons that i have and the labels that i
6:03:23 have i've got a color coding system for
6:03:26 lost and found i've got an icon system
6:03:29 to show a different kind of animal and
6:03:31 i've got a typographic system for
6:03:33 showing the animal's name
6:03:35 so even with quite a small aspect of my
6:03:37 interface there's a lot of design
6:03:39 decisions to make
6:03:41 as you develop and refine your user
6:03:43 interface it's worth going back to your
6:03:46 wireframe map if you have screens that
6:03:49 are similar or use a lot of the same
6:03:51 elements you can develop these together
6:03:54 this will help you maintain consistency
6:03:56 and continuity across your screens and
6:03:59 across your app and if you have elements
6:04:01 appearing on multiple screens it can
6:04:04 help you create a visual system that's
6:04:06 consistent across those screens
6:04:09 in this case we might want all of our
6:04:11 maps to look the same and all of our
6:04:13 icons and labels on those maps to also
6:04:16 look the same but because we've got a
6:04:18 slightly different set of information
6:04:20 here we're going to have to come up with
6:04:22 some other icons for other animals so
6:04:25 we're extending and testing our visual
6:04:28 system at the same time
6:04:30 so rather than showing a letter to say
6:04:32 whether an animal is lost or found i can
6:04:35 indicate that with just a color system
6:04:38 the same that i'm doing elsewhere in my
6:04:40 app so i'm being consistent
6:04:43 you might find yourself going back and
6:04:45 forth between two different screens in
6:04:47 two different areas of your app and as
6:04:50 you're working in different areas you
6:04:52 might discover things where you have to
6:04:53 go back and tweak some of your earlier
6:04:56 designs it's not just interface elements
6:04:58 that you'll be changing it might be your
6:05:00 typography it might be the spacing it
6:05:03 might be how things sit within a window
6:05:06 and you want to figure out how to make
6:05:08 that consistent how to have a systematic
6:05:11 approach to your design because you want
6:05:13 all of your screens to feel like they
6:05:15 belong together in the same family if
6:05:18 you have the same element appearing on
6:05:20 more than one screen make sure that it's
6:05:22 consistent the same scale and the same
6:05:25 place
6:05:26 like your menu items for instance you
6:05:28 can also check to see if you have
6:05:30 anything that's redundant in your
6:05:32 interface in my screen on the left i
6:05:34 don't need to have the icon of the
6:05:36 animal and the animal's name
6:05:38 and even if you're using stand-in
6:05:40 artwork the way that i am with the map
6:05:43 now might be a good time to find
6:05:45 something that's a little closer to how
6:05:47 you really want the artwork to look
6:05:49 so i might want my map to look cleaner
6:05:52 and crisper and more graphic
6:05:54 and as with all design one change can
6:05:57 have a knock-on effect to other elements
6:06:00 if i change my map color there's
6:06:02 knock-on effects because i'm using it in
6:06:04 several different ways
6:06:06 so i have to adjust all the different
6:06:08 states and all the different uses of
6:06:10 that element your refinement happens on
6:06:13 a macro and micro level
6:06:15 on the micro level we might be looking
6:06:18 at our icon design if we look at our
6:06:20 icons on the right hand page for
6:06:22 instance the cat icon doesn't seem quite
6:06:25 right
6:06:26 even when we shrink it down it's still
6:06:28 too different from our icon system it's
6:06:31 too complex
6:06:33 let's enlarge a couple of icons and take
6:06:35 a look at them quickly we've made them
6:06:38 the same size but we need more visual
6:06:40 continuity than that the dog is a lot
6:06:43 simpler and a lot flatter
6:06:45 so we could make some small moves to the
6:06:48 cat icon to try and balance them out
6:06:50 let's create similarity let's make the
6:06:53 eyes work in the same way and let's make
6:06:56 the ears work in the same way as well
6:06:58 now our level of simplicity and flatness
6:07:01 seems to be quite close so these icons
6:07:04 work better as part of a set so once
6:07:07 they're set any changes i make to one of
6:07:10 them i should make to all of them so if
6:07:12 i want to make them a little bit larger
6:07:14 for instance i have to change that to
6:07:16 all of the icons again we're looking at
6:07:18 refinement continuity and consistency we
6:07:22 can take that same logic not just to our
6:07:24 icons but to the labels sitting beneath
6:07:27 them you can look at the spatial
6:07:28 relationship between the type and the
6:07:31 label as well as the relationship
6:07:33 between the label and the icon itself we
6:07:36 might even want to try out a different
6:07:37 color system to work with our map but we
6:07:40 need to do that on a global level so as
6:07:43 you can see with this example our
6:07:45 process of refinement is incremental it
6:07:48 involves a lot of small steps and even
6:07:51 though we're focusing on very small
6:07:53 details in each individual screen we're
6:07:56 also going to have to start to think
6:07:57 about how the screens work together an
6:08:00 easy way to do that is to take your
6:08:02 finished designs and drop them into your
6:08:05 wireframe map
6:08:06 this stage of the process feels a little
6:08:08 bit like completing a paint by numbers
6:08:10 picture you're going from a framework
6:08:13 that's black and white and quite generic
6:08:16 to something that is much more colorful
6:08:18 and visually compelling
6:08:20 if you have the time you can make a
6:08:22 finished design screen for every screen
6:08:24 in your wireframe and this would mean
6:08:27 that when you came to make your digital
6:08:28 prototype it's really going to feel just
6:08:31 like your finished app
6:08:36 [Music]
6:08:41 welcome to the final week of user
6:08:43 experience fundamentals
6:08:45 this week we're going to learn how to
6:08:46 take our app design and simulate the
6:08:49 experience of interactivity by making a
6:08:51 simple functioning digital prototype
6:08:53 we'll be using prototyping software to
6:08:55 put your static screens into a realistic
6:08:58 context and simulating interaction in
6:09:01 order to understand how your app feels
6:09:03 to troubleshoot and to fix any problems
6:09:06 this is the part of the course where all
6:09:08 your talents come together it's the
6:09:10 culmination of both your ui and ux
6:09:13 skills
6:09:14 your visual interface design
6:09:16 and your user interaction decisions are
6:09:18 combined together to simulate an
6:09:20 authentic app experience
6:09:22 for me this is the most exciting part of
6:09:25 uiux development when it all starts to
6:09:28 become real when you have a believable
6:09:30 prototype in your hands
6:09:32 this is the culmination of all your work
6:09:33 in this course good luck putting it all
6:09:35 together
6:09:42 here we are in the final week of ux
6:09:44 design fundamentals in this last week
6:09:47 we're going to look at how to make a
6:09:49 semi-functioning digital prototype we're
6:09:52 going to try and make our digital
6:09:53 prototype look and feel as real as
6:09:56 possible but without actually
6:09:58 programming it so let's look at where we
6:10:00 are in our process
6:10:02 we've completed the first stages in our
6:10:04 process non-visual and semi-visual and
6:10:07 we've completed the first part of our
6:10:09 prototyping phase we've made the final
6:10:11 screens that we're going to use in our
6:10:13 prototype our next stage is going to be
6:10:16 actually building that prototype which
6:10:18 connects to our final stage which we're
6:10:20 not going to deal with in this class if
6:10:23 this was a real project there'd be a
6:10:25 whole fourth phase where we would be
6:10:27 building programming beta testing and
6:10:30 revising our finished app before its
6:10:33 launch but with this class we're going
6:10:35 to end things in the second phase of our
6:10:38 prototyping section this is the phase
6:10:41 where we try and simulate the final
6:10:43 experience of our app as closely as
6:10:46 possible we refine our ui and ux and we
6:10:50 create a functioning digital prototype
6:10:52 that we could take to another level of
6:10:54 user testing if we wanted to even though
6:10:56 we're not going to use programming to
6:10:58 build our prototype we're going to need
6:11:01 some specialized software
6:11:03 but that software is pretty simple to
6:11:05 use in this last phase you want to use
6:11:09 all of the ui and ux skills that you've
6:11:11 gained throughout this course
6:11:13 and even though we're in this last phase
6:11:16 you should still keep evaluating your
6:11:18 app if there are small changes that you
6:11:20 need to make that's okay you should go
6:11:23 ahead and make them the process of
6:11:25 refinement is ongoing and continual it
6:11:28 never ends until your app launches and
6:11:31 often it continues way after that with
6:11:33 updates but our job for now is to keep
6:11:36 pressing ahead and finish building our
6:11:38 digital prototype
6:11:41 we want our digital prototype to feel as
6:11:44 real as it possibly can we want to
6:11:47 simulate the finished app as best we can
6:11:50 in order to do that we need to simulate
6:11:53 the platform that the app is going to
6:11:55 appear on we need to simulate the
6:11:57 interface simulate the interaction and
6:11:59 the sum of that is to simulate the
6:12:02 experience
6:12:03 before we get into the practicalities of
6:12:06 how to make our prototype let's look at
6:12:08 these different kinds of simulation some
6:12:11 of which we've already talked about
6:12:14 in order to simulate the platform we
6:12:16 need to think about scale we need to
6:12:18 think about physicality and we need to
6:12:20 put these together to make a visual and
6:12:23 tactile experience so our digital
6:12:25 prototype needs to be specific to the
6:12:28 device that it's going to be used on
6:12:31 our devices fill up different amounts of
6:12:34 space in our fields of vision
6:12:36 they may be vertical or horizontal and
6:12:39 we may be interacting with a hand or
6:12:42 with a mouse
6:12:43 for our prototype to feel real it needs
6:12:45 to be on the right sized screen once
6:12:48 we've chosen a physical device we need
6:12:50 to think about simulating the interface
6:12:53 and we've already done quite a lot of
6:12:55 work on this we want our user interface
6:12:57 design to be polished and believable and
6:13:00 we want the visual and verbal content
6:13:02 that we have to be believable as well
6:13:04 rather than a generic wireframe we want
6:13:07 to have actual content actual interface
6:13:10 design as close as possible to our
6:13:13 finished app
6:13:14 and once we have these static pieces at
6:13:17 the right size on the right platform we
6:13:19 have to think about how to simulate the
6:13:21 interaction that the user will have with
6:13:23 our app and again we've already done
6:13:26 some work on this we've got a wireframe
6:13:28 map that gives us a believable ux
6:13:31 experience where we've thought about the
6:13:33 paths that the user is going to take and
6:13:35 we've thought about how they might
6:13:37 interact and respond with individual
6:13:39 screens
6:13:41 the logic and flow of our user
6:13:43 interaction is already quite resolved
6:13:46 when we add together all of these
6:13:48 different kinds of simulation we should
6:13:51 be able to simulate the entire
6:13:53 experience of our app
6:13:55 so we need to have believable ui design
6:13:58 believable ux design a believable
6:14:01 platform and context and we need to have
6:14:04 all three of these
6:14:06 so believable ui design means that our
6:14:09 prototype needs to be on a screen
6:14:12 believable ux design means that our
6:14:14 prototype needs to be interactive
6:14:17 and a believable platform or context
6:14:20 means that our prototype needs to be
6:14:22 running on a real physical device
6:14:25 in a lot of ways we want to trick the
6:14:28 user into thinking that our prototype is
6:14:31 real and a fully programmed app but of
6:14:34 course it isn't and it's worth
6:14:36 remembering that we're still in the
6:14:38 testing phase here there's still the
6:14:40 possibility for change in improvement
6:14:42 because we're in this pre-programming
6:14:44 phase rather than learning how to
6:14:47 program or working with a programmer
6:14:49 we're going to use some simple digital
6:14:51 prototyping software to produce our
6:14:53 prototype
6:14:55 there's lots of different software to
6:14:56 choose from and some of it's free
6:14:59 i would suggest
6:15:00 that you find one that suits your budget
6:15:02 one that's easy to use and one that does
6:15:05 the things that you need for the sake of
6:15:07 the demos in this class i'm going to use
6:15:10 adobe xd i like this software because
6:15:13 it's easy to use i'm already familiar
6:15:15 with how other adobe products work
6:15:18 there's a lot of resources in terms of
6:15:20 templates and guides
6:15:22 it's quite a flexible tool i can
6:15:24 prototype apps or websites or pretty
6:15:26 much anything there are frequent updates
6:15:28 to the software so it's always getting
6:15:30 more and more functionality it's also
6:15:32 heading towards being an industry
6:15:34 standard in a lot of design studios
6:15:37 most of the prototyping software that's
6:15:39 out there works in a similar way so if
6:15:42 you don't use xd the knowledge is still
6:15:44 going to be transferable and easy to
6:15:46 apply to whatever program you do use
6:15:49 for the demos that we use in this class
6:15:51 we're going to keep it pretty simple you
6:15:54 can probably build a much more complex
6:15:56 digital prototype than the one that
6:15:58 we're going to build
6:15:59 if you do want to do that i'd advise you
6:16:01 to take a tutorial and check out the ui
6:16:04 kits and the wireframe kits that are
6:16:06 available for free
6:16:08 and there's a range of different ways
6:16:09 that you can build your digital
6:16:11 prototype as well how you do that might
6:16:14 depend on your area of interest or your
6:16:16 specific skill set you could use
6:16:18 pre-existing components within the
6:16:21 software to build a generic wireframe
6:16:23 and use that as your prototype you could
6:16:26 also use existing artwork and make
6:16:28 invisible interactive areas
6:16:30 or you could rebuild the whole of your
6:16:32 artwork in xd or whatever program you're
6:16:35 using there are pros and cons to all of
6:16:39 these three different approaches the
6:16:41 fastest and easiest way to build
6:16:43 something might not give the most
6:16:45 realistic simulation of your app for the
6:16:47 user and it would be nice if you can
6:16:50 utilize the interface screens that
6:16:52 you've already designed and spent time
6:16:54 on refining but you might find you have
6:16:56 to adjust or rebuild some of your
6:16:58 artwork to get the most out of the
6:17:00 software there are pros and cons to each
6:17:03 of these prototyping strategies if you
6:17:06 want to use pre-existing components to
6:17:08 build a generic wireframe for your
6:17:10 prototype the good news is it's fast to
6:17:13 build it's going to look like a
6:17:15 professional wireframe and it can work
6:17:18 in any operating system it's also going
6:17:20 to be great for testing your ux
6:17:22 the bad news is that it's going to look
6:17:25 quite generic and it's only going to
6:17:27 function as a wireframe so you won't get
6:17:28 as much information back from the user
6:17:31 testing there's no design involved
6:17:33 there's no ui so it's kind of bad for
6:17:35 testing out your ui
6:17:37 if you were going to build your
6:17:39 prototype like this you'd have a range
6:17:41 of pieces that were pre-made that you
6:17:44 could choose from in order to make your
6:17:46 wireframe and there are literally
6:17:48 hundreds of these pieces and they're all
6:17:51 very generic but they work together as a
6:17:53 set really well so if you were to make
6:17:56 your digital prototype using these
6:17:58 pieces it would look very coherent very
6:18:01 cohesive and you'd learn a lot about the
6:18:03 user's interactions the interface design
6:18:07 basically wouldn't get in the way of the
6:18:09 user experience you could also use
6:18:12 pre-existing components from a certain
6:18:14 operating system whether it's for apple
6:18:16 or android
6:18:18 let's see what some of my screens look
6:18:20 like for my lost pet app if i try and
6:18:22 build them as generic wireframes
6:18:25 first of all there's a generic map that
6:18:28 i can use so that's pretty useful
6:18:30 there are standardized icons and symbols
6:18:32 that i can use and i can color those
6:18:35 whatever i need to
6:18:37 i can scale them up and down and
6:18:38 position them wherever i want so i'm
6:18:41 able to shape the design a little bit my
6:18:44 other interface elements would be pretty
6:18:46 generic if i needed menu items they'd be
6:18:49 standard symbols and generic typeface
6:18:52 and size
6:18:54 the same would be true of other
6:18:56 information in my interface which would
6:18:58 all be standardized in terms of color
6:19:01 shape and system the benefit of this is
6:19:04 that i know that all of these pieces are
6:19:06 going to work in harmony together so i
6:19:09 end up with a functioning map although
6:19:11 it's not very visually compelling i can
6:19:14 use generic elements to make a sign up
6:19:16 screen and here the size weight and
6:19:19 color of my typography is predetermined
6:19:22 as well as the spacing between my
6:19:24 elements each component of my interface
6:19:28 is standardized as long as i make sure
6:19:30 my menu items appear in the same place
6:19:33 on different screens there's going to be
6:19:35 consistency so it's not just the
6:19:38 elements that are consistent it's how
6:19:40 i'm using them how i'm structuring my
6:19:43 page
6:19:44 so i could take the elements from my
6:19:46 first screen and quite easily copy and
6:19:48 paste them into exactly the same space i
6:19:51 can even add a keyboard that's already
6:19:53 been made for me so all i've really done
6:19:56 is copy and paste these generic elements
6:19:59 and i've already got three screens that
6:20:01 work together in harmony this kind of
6:20:04 design is great when it's used for the
6:20:06 purpose it was intended for as a
6:20:08 wireframe it's really focusing on the
6:20:11 functionality of the interface the
6:20:13 interactions
6:20:14 and not on the design if you've
6:20:16 completed all the steps in this class
6:20:18 you've already moved past this process
6:20:21 our second option is to use the artwork
6:20:24 that we've already built and we can find
6:20:26 some easy ways to make it be interactive
6:20:29 the benefits of this approach are that
6:20:31 it's fast to build it looks like your
6:20:33 design there's very little additional
6:20:35 design work to do and it's going to be
6:20:37 good for testing ui and ux
6:20:40 you might find you need to build a few
6:20:42 more user interface components to get
6:20:44 them to work with this software
6:20:46 you might also find that your designs
6:20:48 don't look exactly like whichever
6:20:50 operating system world you want them to
6:20:52 live in
6:20:54 but this might be the best and easiest
6:20:56 approach because it builds on a lot of
6:20:58 the work that you've already done
6:21:01 so let's take some of my static screens
6:21:03 that i developed earlier for my lost pet
6:21:05 app and see how those might work in this
6:21:08 scenario
6:21:09 i could import my entire screen designs
6:21:12 not one piece at a time but as one whole
6:21:15 image so straight away i've got a user
6:21:18 interface that looks quite finished and
6:21:20 quite specific to my app
6:21:22 but i'd need to fake some kind of
6:21:24 interactivity so that i can make a
6:21:27 semi-functioning digital prototype
6:21:30 most prototyping software works in the
6:21:32 same way where you're connecting one
6:21:34 page to a target page and that's
6:21:37 basically forming a link so when the
6:21:39 user taps on that page it'll take them
6:21:42 there it's basically the equivalent of
6:21:44 the arrows that we would draw between
6:21:46 our screens in our site map so this
6:21:48 might help us make a very basic
6:21:51 prototype but there's not really much
6:21:53 detail in terms of interaction there i'm
6:21:56 really just traveling from screen to
6:21:58 screen i'm not really interacting with
6:22:01 any of the interface elements
6:22:03 specifically because all of my interface
6:22:06 elements are in one image i'd have to
6:22:08 fake that interactivity i could draw a
6:22:11 box over one of my icons for instance
6:22:14 and set the fill and the line of that
6:22:16 box to be invisible i could then set a
6:22:19 target where you would go to if you
6:22:21 clicked on that icon so instead of the
6:22:24 whole screen being interactive it's
6:22:27 really just that one icon now and you
6:22:29 could continue that process with a whole
6:22:32 series of transparent boxes linking to
6:22:35 all the different screens that they
6:22:37 might need to go to with the software
6:22:40 i'm using i have some choices over the
6:22:42 kind of transition and the length of
6:22:44 transition from screen to screen as well
6:22:47 if i want to be more accurate about the
6:22:49 area that's active i can draw a specific
6:22:52 shape that relates to whatever the
6:22:54 active area in the background is so here
6:22:57 i'm drawing a circular area over a
6:22:59 circular icon and i could connect that
6:23:02 to any screen that i wanted right now i
6:23:05 have three art boards in my file that
6:23:07 reflect three different screens but if i
6:23:10 was really making this app i'd have many
6:23:12 more if i want to see whether my
6:23:14 connections are working i can use a
6:23:16 preview window to test them out
6:23:19 so if i'm on the first screen and i move
6:23:21 the cursor over the icon where i
6:23:23 designated an active area you can see
6:23:26 that the cursor changes and if i click
6:23:29 on that icon it takes me to my second
6:23:31 screen and it uses whatever transition i
6:23:34 chose in exactly the same way if i move
6:23:37 the cursor over the post button it'll
6:23:39 change to let me know that it's live and
6:23:42 if i click on it it'll take me to my
6:23:44 third screen and from there i can click
6:23:47 on my active cat icon and get back to
6:23:49 the first screen
6:23:51 so we've made a very simple interactive
6:23:53 loop between our three art boards here
6:23:56 we could have many interactive areas on
6:23:58 one screen so the dog would take me to a
6:24:01 dog screen the snake to a snake screen
6:24:03 the parrot icon to a parrot screen so
6:24:06 you'd build a network of connections to
6:24:09 simulate the interactivity let's look at
6:24:11 our third option which involves
6:24:14 rebuilding our artwork within the
6:24:16 digital prototyping software the
6:24:18 benefits here are that it will look like
6:24:20 your design you could also make it look
6:24:22 like whatever operating system it's
6:24:24 working with it's going to be a closer
6:24:26 simulation to your finished app and is
6:24:29 good for testing both ui and ux
6:24:32 in many ways it's a more refined version
6:24:34 of the strategy that we just looked at
6:24:37 but the downside is that it's going to
6:24:39 take you a lot longer to build this way
6:24:41 but it is going to give you more control
6:24:43 over the interaction in your prototype
6:24:46 ultimately it'll make it feel more real
6:24:49 so let's look at how that might work
6:24:51 with our lost pet example i could use
6:24:53 some of my existing user interface
6:24:55 components but i'd be redrawing them or
6:24:58 importing them one by one into my
6:25:01 prototyping software so i'm not starting
6:25:03 from scratch because i've got the
6:25:05 components and i've got a blueprint for
6:25:08 the layout one of the things i like
6:25:10 about this approach is that i can mix my
6:25:12 artwork for my app with the system
6:25:15 artwork to make it look a little bit
6:25:17 more real even though i'm not going to
6:25:19 make these system elements work just the
6:25:22 fact that i have them there makes you
6:25:24 think that my app is live i could also
6:25:26 use other standardized interface
6:25:28 elements and these are available from
6:25:30 free ui kits so i'm going to mix some of
6:25:34 the apple os language with my app so i'm
6:25:37 trying to get the believability from the

6:25:43 and originality of my app from my own
6:25:45 designs
6:25:47 i could also decide that i don't want to

6:25:52 and every piece that i put into this
6:25:54 prototype is going to be designed by me
6:25:58 but even in that case some pre-existing
6:26:00 familiar visual components can really
6:26:03 help the believability of your prototype
6:26:06 if i want my users to sign in with
6:26:08 facebook google or twitter it really
6:26:11 helps to have recognizable icons there
6:26:14 it makes my app feel more believable and
6:26:16 authentic i think this strategy helps
6:26:20 you build the legitimacy and familiarity
6:26:22 that you need so the user believes that
6:26:25 your prototype is really a fairly fully
6:26:28 functioning app
6:26:30 and the more screens that you build
6:26:31 using your own design components the
6:26:34 more the personality of your app is
6:26:36 going to come through
6:26:37 so we've looked at three different ways
6:26:39 for you to build your prototype which
6:26:42 way you choose to work might depend on
6:26:44 what kind of ui ux designer you really
6:26:47 are so think a little bit about where
6:26:49 your focus is
6:26:51 you might want to use pre-existing
6:26:52 components if you are less visual person
6:26:55 and more ux focused if you're more
6:26:57 concerned with the user interface you
6:26:59 probably want to rebuild your artwork
6:27:02 and if you're somewhere in between you
6:27:04 probably want to use your existing
6:27:06 artwork and work on top of it to create
6:27:08 interactive areas
