6:33:21 to my create an account screen to my
6:33:23 confirmation screen
6:33:25 i can click confirm and go to my home
6:33:28 screen my map screen my cursor tells me
6:33:31 that the parrot is the only live element
6:33:33 so i can click on that get to the parrot
6:33:36 detail screen and from there i can click
6:33:39 on the dog and get to the dog details
6:33:41 screen i've also set it up that i can
6:33:44 click back on the parrot to go between
6:33:46 these two screens because i want that
6:33:48 transition to be quite minimal i want it
6:33:51 to be seamless because it's a small jump
6:33:53 for the user
6:33:55 what we've made here is a test version
6:33:58 of our digital prototype and even though
6:34:00 it's very limited we can actually test
6:34:02 it on a real device straight away i can
6:34:06 hook my phone up to my computer and have
6:34:09 it play my preview on my phone seeing
6:34:12 the app on a real device even at this
6:34:15 preliminary stage can be really useful
6:34:19 and even though there's not much
6:34:20 interaction and it's not very specific
6:34:23 just the fact that the user is touching
6:34:25 the screen to get a response makes the
6:34:28 app feel real
6:34:29 i can also flip through all the other
6:34:31 screens that i've designed even though i
6:34:33 haven't put links in there yet or
6:34:35 connected them
6:34:37 and once i've seen my screens in this
6:34:38 simulated mode i can assess them and
6:34:41 evaluate them figure out which things
6:34:43 work which things don't and how i'm
6:34:45 going to get it to work better when i
6:34:47 build my larger prototype in this
6:34:50 segment we're going to look at some ways
6:34:52 to try and make our prototype feel a
6:34:55 little bit more realistic one thing that
6:34:57 helps with that in xd at least is
6:35:00 creating overlays so you can have a
6:35:02 window float over the top of a
6:35:04 background i'd encourage you to do
6:35:07 whatever moves you can to make your
6:35:09 digital prototype seem more realistic
6:35:13 in this case i want to try and simulate
6:35:15 a window popping up over the top of a
6:35:18 background and that background staying
6:35:20 in the same place
6:35:22 so for the screens that have the
6:35:23 floating window i'm going to delete
6:35:26 everything in the background i'm going
6:35:28 to use the same system we already looked
6:35:30 at to create a link from the icon to the
6:35:33 page but this time i'm going to choose
6:35:36 overlay rather than transition and i'm
6:35:38 going to choose slide up as the
6:35:40 transition option because i want this
6:35:43 menu to appear like it's sliding up from
6:35:45 the bottom of my screen i'm going to
6:35:47 connect a second icon from my map to an
6:35:50 overlay screen
6:35:52 so the dog icon connects to the dog
6:35:54 screen
6:35:55 and the same as before i'm going to make
6:35:57 sure it's an overlay and not a
6:35:59 transition
6:36:00 and i can tell it's an overlay screen
6:36:03 because the background is grayed out so
6:36:05 now in my preview window i can click on
6:36:08 both the dog icon
6:36:10 and the parrot icon
6:36:11 and i can have both of those windows
6:36:14 slide up over my background map
6:36:17 and that's a much closer simulation of
6:36:19 how the app is really going to work when
6:36:22 it's finished
6:36:23 you should use any techniques and tricks
6:36:25 that you can to make your app feel more
6:36:28 real whether that's in terms of
6:36:30 interaction or in terms of menu movement
6:36:33 the closer you can get to the finished
6:36:35 thing without any programming the better
6:36:39 we can use this same kind of technique
6:36:42 to create sidebar menus as well because
6:36:44 the way that these menus move is such a
6:36:47 familiar convention they're quite a
6:36:49 useful tool for us to have in creating a
6:36:52 digital prototype that feels more real
6:36:55 we're going to use the same technique
6:36:57 with overlays that we already looked at
6:37:00 on one artboard i'm going to delete
6:37:02 everything apart from the menu
6:37:05 and then i'm going to make a link to the
6:37:08 menu icon that's on my home page my map
6:37:11 page
6:37:12 i'm going to make it an overlay link so
6:37:14 that the menu can float over the top of
6:37:16 the map and i'm going to have it slide
6:37:18 out to the right so it feels like the
6:37:21 menu is coming out of the menu icon just
6:37:24 like with a real app
6:37:26 i can preview it and see if i like it or
6:37:28 not
6:37:29 clicking on the menu makes it go away so
6:37:32 it feels like i have an open and close
6:37:34 action that's quite fluid
6:37:37 one that feels quite real
6:37:39 right now in my fake menu i have
6:37:42 searched for highlighted in bold as if
6:37:45 it's active
6:37:46 if i wanted to continue to simulate this
6:37:48 interactivity i could make that be an
6:37:51 active link i can use our technique from
6:37:53 earlier of drawing a transparent box
6:37:56 over the area that i want to be
6:37:58 interactive
6:37:59 and then i can connect that link to my
6:38:02 search for screen i have to locate that
6:38:05 screen within my layout of all my
6:38:07 artboards
6:38:09 as always i can pick my transition and
6:38:11 the timing of my transition
6:38:14 since the whole screen is going to
6:38:15 change i'm going to have this transition
6:38:17 be a fast dissolve so you can see we're
6:38:21 starting to create quite a complex web
6:38:24 of connections and this is why it's
6:38:26 important for you to have your artboards
6:38:28 organized within your prototyping
6:38:30 software
6:38:32 so let's go back to our main map screen
6:38:35 and look at all the different things
6:38:36 that we can do from that screen now
6:38:39 as well as having my parrot and dog menu
6:38:42 slide up from the bottom i can also go
6:38:44 up to the menu at the top
6:38:46 and have my main menu appear
6:38:49 and if i select search for it'll take me
6:38:52 to that screen
6:38:54 another thing that we can do to create
6:38:56 more of the feeling of full
6:38:58 interactivity is to create fake fields
6:39:01 for data entry
6:39:03 it's quite simple to do this we just
6:39:05 need to create a series of progressive
6:39:08 screens where each screen adds the next
6:39:11 piece of data
6:39:12 there's no typing involved but it at
6:39:14 least takes the user through the stages
6:39:17 of filling the form out
6:39:19 as you click on each successive field it
6:39:22 fills in and then takes you to the next
6:39:25 art board so you're just adding one
6:39:27 piece of data at a time i can make it
6:39:30 feel a little more fulfilling for the
6:39:32 user by changing the text in the field
6:39:35 from a light gray to black as they enter
6:39:38 their data
6:39:39 once i press the search button it would
6:39:41 take me to a map that would show the
6:39:43 results of my search in this case it's
6:39:46 found my dog
6:39:48 if i click on the icon i get details
6:39:50 about the dog and i can choose to either
6:39:52 close that window or to confirm
6:39:55 if you think back to our user flowchart
6:39:58 we're basically making a screen for
6:40:00 every possible decision that the user
6:40:03 could make
6:40:04 the big difference is that now we're
6:40:06 following these paths through our
6:40:08 content through our app with very
6:40:10 finished visuals and a very finished
6:40:13 user interface so it feels much more
6:40:16 real so when we get feedback from a user
6:40:18 now it should be much more accurate
6:40:22 and while we're creating this simulated
6:40:24 interactivity for our user we're having
6:40:27 to create a lot more screens we're
6:40:30 expanding and refining our user
6:40:32 interface design at the same time that
6:40:35 we're really getting to grips with how a
6:40:37 user is actually going to experience the
6:40:40 real app
6:40:42 in this segment we're going to look at
6:40:44 creating some more complete pathways for
6:40:47 our digital prototype
6:40:49 so far we've made a prototype but it's
6:40:52 got fairly limited interactions
6:40:54 the user's experience is quite brief and
6:40:58 not very complete
6:40:59 to give the user a more fulfilling and
6:41:02 realistic experience of our app we're
6:41:05 going to create some more complete
6:41:07 pathways within our prototype
6:41:09 because i've organized the art boards of
6:41:11 my prototype in the same way as my
6:41:13 wireframe map i can clearly see the same
6:41:16 groupings where the user is following a
6:41:19 different path
6:41:20 where these paths have been described
6:41:23 with text or generic elements before
6:41:26 now they're clearly visually visible and
6:41:29 delineated
6:41:30 in order to build out my prototype i
6:41:33 should probably take each of these areas
6:41:36 and develop a fixed pathway for the user
6:41:39 to travel through so that they
6:41:41 experience each of these areas
6:41:44 and of course we're not going for full
6:41:46 functionality here we really just want
6:41:49 the user to travel through and
6:41:51 experience a sampling of each area so
6:41:55 we're going to need to make some more
6:41:57 screens and it begs the question how
6:41:59 many screens do you need to have in your
6:42:02 prototype
6:42:03 as a general guide i would create a
6:42:05 pathway through each important area or
6:42:08 function of your app there might be some
6:42:11 areas that you don't need to build out
6:42:13 at all
6:42:14 but the bottom line is the amount of
6:42:16 screens is really going to depend on
6:42:18 your app and how your app works but also
6:42:21 how much time you have on what stage of
6:42:24 the project you're in
6:42:26 if you feel like everything is working
6:42:27 and pretty resolved you can keep
6:42:30 building out your prototype but remember
6:42:33 we're still in a prototype phase so you
6:42:35 don't have to build everything you can
6:42:37 also keep evaluating and refining the
6:42:39 interaction
6:42:41 you might find yourself adding or taking
6:42:43 away steps within a pathway
6:42:45 and it's useful to time how long each of
6:42:48 those pathways takes the user it might
6:42:50 be much shorter or much longer than you
6:42:53 think
6:42:54 if we go back to my get pet app you can
6:42:56 see here that i've actually made a lot
6:42:59 more screens to get my pathways ready
6:43:01 i've got my login screens at the
6:43:03 beginning my home screens places where i
6:43:06 can search for my lost pet and then two
6:43:09 distinct areas where i can post a lost
6:43:12 pet or post a found pet and i've color
6:43:15 coded these green and red so you can see
6:43:18 quite easily that they're separate
6:43:20 sections
6:43:22 so i had to make between 13 and 15
6:43:25 screens to show the pathway through each
6:43:28 of these sections
6:43:30 i used the same technique of overlays
6:43:32 that we already looked at and i tried to
6:43:35 create pathways that loop back to the
6:43:37 home page or connect to each other
6:43:40 because i want to provide a more
6:43:42 complete or contained experience for the
6:43:44 user
6:43:45 in the preview window you can see that
6:43:48 i'm posting about an animal that i found
6:43:51 i made an extra screen where you can
6:43:53 upload an image from your photo album
6:43:55 which of course is faked at this point
6:43:58 because there's only one image to choose
6:44:00 from
6:44:01 but it gives the illusion of my app
6:44:03 functioning with the larger operating
6:44:05 system as well as my photo i can add
6:44:08 textual information
6:44:10 about the animal that i found one line
6:44:12 at a time
6:44:14 and even though the keyboard isn't
6:44:16 active i'm going to make the user click
6:44:18 on each of the text fields one at a time
6:44:21 because i want them to feel as if
6:44:23 they're actually entering information
6:44:25 and going through that process
6:44:27 as much as anything i'm trying to
6:44:29 simulate the timing of the experience
6:44:33 once all of the information has been
6:44:35 entered the user can post the animal
6:44:37 that they found and then they'll be able
6:44:39 to see it on their main map
6:44:42 and this conveniently takes us back to
6:44:45 our home page so we followed a complete
6:44:48 circuit or loop with the one pathway of
6:44:51 posting about a found animal
6:44:54 also now on my map i can click on the
6:44:57 green snake the animal i found and i can
6:45:00 see my information that i've entered and
6:45:02 this gives the user the feeling that the
6:45:04 entries actually worked and the data has
6:45:07 been posted
6:45:08 let's look at another pathway let's look
6:45:10 at what happens if we want to post for a
6:45:12 lost animal
6:45:14 it's pretty much exactly the same
6:45:16 process
6:45:17 i can upload my photo from a photo album
6:45:21 i can enter all the information that i
6:45:23 have about the pet that i've lost
6:45:26 and the categories for the lost pet are
6:45:28 slightly different from the found pad
6:45:31 the most important thing is that i can
6:45:33 enter a name for my pet so when my pet
6:45:36 shows up on my app it's going to show up
6:45:38 with that name in this case libby
6:45:41 whereas the snake that i found showed up
6:45:43 on the map as an icon that said
6:45:46 pet you've found having some visual
6:45:49 confirmation of the actions that i've
6:45:51 taken as a user like this can really
6:45:54 help the app feel real
6:45:56 so now i've built out a pathway for
6:45:59 almost all of my main menu options
6:46:02 the one that i've left till last is
6:46:04 profile because i feel that's a little
6:46:06 bit less important and probably a fairly
6:46:08 standard screen next i might think about
6:46:11 how these pathways are organized do they
6:46:14 follow on one from the other so the user
6:46:16 has to go through them in a specific
6:46:19 order or do i allow the user to choose
6:46:21 which pathway they want to access and in
6:46:24 which order
6:46:26 well here we are at our last class of ux
6:46:29 design fundamentals
6:46:31 we're at our final phase where we get to
6:46:33 publish and test our prototype
6:46:36 but first of all make sure that all your
6:46:38 paths are working properly in your
6:46:40 prototyping program when you've tested
6:46:42 everything and it seems good then we can
6:46:45 move on to creating a working digital
6:46:47 simulation
6:46:48 so here's my final layout document in my
6:46:51 software prototyping program
6:46:54 so you can see that i made nearly 60
6:46:57 screens just for this short prototype
6:47:00 if i want to go through all my links and
6:47:02 test them out i can use the preview
6:47:04 window
6:47:05 and i can also make a recording here if
6:47:08 i wanted to keep a record of the
6:47:09 interactions
6:47:11 i find that this is a good way for me to
6:47:13 test my prototype one last time myself
6:47:16 before i hand it off to test it with a
6:47:19 user that's never seen it before
6:47:21 i can travel through all my pathways
6:47:23 making sure that everything works and
6:47:26 also making sure that there's no dead
6:47:28 ends
6:47:29 i've arranged my fixed pathways into an
6:47:32 order that i think makes sense for the
6:47:34 user
6:47:35 after my initial splash screen they have
6:47:38 to create an account before they can get
6:47:40 to the home screen in the map
6:47:42 my menu at the top of the screen is
6:47:44 active so i can search for items in that
6:47:47 text menu or i can also click on any of
6:47:50 the icons that are on the map and
6:47:52 they're all active
6:47:54 i can open and close the information
6:47:56 windows and that feels quite realistic
6:47:59 if i spot a fanned pet that i know is
6:48:02 mine i can claim that pet
6:48:04 so here's my lost snake that i'm going
6:48:06 to claim and it takes me out to a
6:48:08 separate confirmation screen
6:48:11 and i can use my main menu to easily go
6:48:14 back to the map screen again
6:48:16 i'm basically trying to make every
6:48:18 possible activity of browsing in my home
6:48:21 screen available to the user in terms of
6:48:24 testing
6:48:25 when the user has finished exploring the
6:48:27 home screen they can choose one of the
6:48:29 three main options from the main menu
6:48:32 i made search for bold in order to force
6:48:35 the user down that pathway first
6:48:38 if you look at the overview showing
6:48:40 thumbnails of our screens you can see
6:48:42 that we've traveled through the top area
6:48:44 and now we're in the lower left area
6:48:46 that's blue
6:48:47 this is my search for area so i've
6:48:50 entered information about the animal
6:48:52 that i'm searching for and now a match
6:48:54 appears on my map
6:48:56 if it's the right animal i can claim it
6:48:59 and if it's the wrong animal i can go
6:49:01 back to my search and start over again
6:49:04 i'm trying to build the illusion of
6:49:06 choice for the user
6:49:08 i'm trying to build the illusion of full
6:49:10 interactivity when there's only really
6:49:12 two choices
6:49:14 when i've traveled all the way through
6:49:15 this path it's going to take me back to
6:49:18 my home page but the main menu is going
6:49:20 to suggest a different option now in
6:49:23 bold
6:49:24 i'm basically forcing the user to travel
6:49:27 through my three main sections in order
6:49:30 so here we are in the post found section
6:49:33 and you can easily identify this in the
6:49:36 overview of thumbnails because i've
6:49:38 color-coded all of the screens green
6:49:40 so here i can enter information and a
6:49:43 photograph of the animal that i found
6:49:46 and post it
6:49:47 once the user does that
6:49:49 an icon will appear back on the home
6:49:51 page map showing the animal that's been
6:49:54 found
6:49:55 i'm trying to prove to the user here
6:49:57 that their interactions with my app
6:50:00 actually have results
6:50:02 and of course the point of that is to
6:50:04 try and make it feel more real
6:50:06 once i've traveled through the post
6:50:08 found pathway of my prototype the menu
6:50:12 is going to change once again
6:50:14 and now it will allow me to get to my
6:50:17 last section
6:50:19 this is the red section on the right
6:50:21 hand side of my thumbnails and this is
6:50:23 where i can post information about an
6:50:26 animal that's been lost the steps here
6:50:29 are very similar to the post found
6:50:31 section
6:50:33 i'm trying to create continuity and
6:50:35 familiarity in both the interface and
6:50:38 the experience for the user i'm also
6:50:40 trying to have some slight visual
6:50:42 differences so the user knows that
6:50:45 they're in a different place
6:50:46 participating in a different activity
6:50:49 that can partly be done with words and
6:50:51 labeling but i'm also using the color
6:50:53 coding system
6:50:55 i'm hoping that the experience i'm
6:50:57 creating
6:50:58 is smooth and easy and logical for the
6:51:02 user and also engaging
6:51:04 as well as interacting with the
6:51:06 interface i want the user to really
6:51:08 engage with the content
6:51:10 so one thing that really helps with that
6:51:12 is using real text and using real images
6:51:17 or creating your text in your images so
6:51:19 that they feel real and not repeating
6:51:22 the same information twice as well
6:51:25 if you can get the user to engage with
6:51:27 the content that means they're really
6:51:29 using and experiencing your interface
6:51:33 you can see from the timer on my preview
6:51:36 window where i've been recording that
6:51:38 that whole experience was about four and
6:51:40 a half minutes to get through sixty
6:51:42 screens
6:51:43 when you've tested your prototype
6:51:44 yourself and everything seems good it's
6:51:47 time to create your finished working
6:51:49 simulation and you can do this in a
6:51:51 number of different ways as we've just
6:51:54 seen you could create a video play
6:51:56 through your prototyping software might
6:51:58 also allow you to publish a link so that
6:52:01 anybody can look at it in a web browser
6:52:04 as we've seen previously you can create
6:52:06 an interactive version a preview of your
6:52:09 app and connect your phone to your
6:52:11 computer screen or the best option is to
6:52:14 create an interactive version that's a
6:52:16 standalone so you can look at it on your
6:52:19 phone from anywhere
6:52:20 this option is the one that's going to
6:52:22 make your app feel the most real you'll
6:52:25 actually be holding it in your hand on a
6:52:28 real device
6:52:29 when you come to test your prototype
6:52:32 it's really useful to test it with a
6:52:34 user who's never seen it before that way
6:52:37 all of their responses to your ui and ux
6:52:40 decisions are going to be totally
6:52:42 genuine
6:52:43 so here's my final limited functionality
6:52:46 working digital prototype
6:52:48 i've been through a lot of stages in my
6:52:50 process to get here if i was working in
6:52:53 the real world i'd probably have
6:52:54 condensed that process
6:52:56 but for at least the first time you
6:52:58 design an app it's worth going through
6:53:00 all of those stages so you experience
6:53:03 them the next time you do this you'll be
6:53:05 able to condense your own process down
6:53:08 and choose which parts of the process
6:53:10 worked best for you
6:53:11 and if you were working on a larger
6:53:13 project you might not be involved in all
6:53:15 of these stages you might be
6:53:17 concentrating on just one part of the
6:53:19 process in a lot more detail i hope that
6:53:22 what you've seen as we've gone through
6:53:24 this process is how complicated it can
6:53:27 be to get to just this stage of making a
6:53:30 digital prototype let alone making a
6:53:33 fully functioning app
6:53:35 we've covered a lot of information in
6:53:37 this course and some of it we've gone
6:53:39 over quite quickly there are plenty of
6:53:41 areas that we could have investigated
6:53:44 much more deeply but remember this is
6:53:47 just a beginning course it's to take you
6:53:49 through that whole process so you can
6:53:51 figure out what you might want to
6:53:53 investigate in a more detailed fashion
6:53:56 and there are two more courses that
6:53:58 follow on from this one that'll help you
6:54:00 look at some of these issues in much
6:54:03 more detail i hope this course has been
6:54:05 productive for you and i hope that you
6:54:07 managed to get to this final stage of
6:54:10 producing a digital prototype
6:54:12 wherever your focus ends up being in the
6:54:15 broad field of ui and ux i wanted to
6:54:17 wish you good luck in developing your
6:54:19 app and getting your digital prototype
6:54:21 finished
6:54:28 hello and welcome to the course web
6:54:30 design strategy and information
6:54:32 architecture my name is roman jaster i'm
6:54:35 a web designer web developer and
6:54:37 instructor in the graphic design program
6:54:39 at the california institute of the arts
6:54:42 this is the third course in the ui ux
6:54:44 specialization on coursera in the first
6:54:47 half of what i designed as a two-course
6:54:49 sequence capping off this specialization
6:54:53 i hope that many of you took the
6:54:54 preceding uiux courses with michael
6:54:56 birthington
6:54:58 and that you're ready for more
6:55:00 in my courses you'll explore and
6:55:02 practice every phase of the user
6:55:03 experience design process through
6:55:05 lessons exercises and a large scale
6:55:08 project in which you will design a
6:55:10 complex website
6:55:12 in this course web design strategy and
6:55:14 information architecture you're going to
6:55:16 define your strategy outline your scope
6:55:19 and draft your site map for your project
6:55:22 and in the next course web design
6:55:25 wireframes to prototypes will give your
6:55:27 website a compelling look and feel when
6:55:30 developing wireframes and visual markups
6:55:33 while the ux concepts that i'm covering
6:55:35 will translate to many kinds of
6:55:36 interactive media like apps digital
6:55:39 kiosks or games
6:55:41 our primary focus will be on designing
6:55:43 contemporary responsive websites by the
6:55:46 end of these two courses you will have
6:55:48 created an impressive and comprehensive
6:55:50 portfolio piece that showcases ux design
6:55:53 skills
6:55:54 to guide you in your explorations you
6:55:56 will be able to peek over the shoulders
6:55:58 of two students alison and bradley who
6:56:01 completed the exact same project as you
6:56:04 you'll be able to hear them talk about
6:56:06 how they tackled each project phase and
6:56:08 you'll learn from their insights now
6:56:11 this first course that you're about to
6:56:13 begin
6:56:14 is focused on the early ux challenges
6:56:17 we'll talk about research planning
6:56:19 setting goals understanding the user
6:56:22 defining requirements and structuring
6:56:24 contents
6:56:26 you'll even get to watch a few
6:56:27 interviews that i conducted with
6:56:28 practicing designers who are going to
6:56:30 share tips and insights about user
6:56:33 experience design
6:56:35 lastly a little more about me i've been
6:56:38 working in the industry as a web
6:56:39 designer and web developer for almost 20
6:56:41 years i'm the co-founder of the design
6:56:44 studio year brigade and we work on a lot
6:56:46 of web design projects
6:56:48 we often work for educational and
6:56:50 non-profit clients as well as arts
6:56:52 organizations and artists
6:56:54 i also teach web design classes at the
6:56:56 california institute of the arts where i
6:56:58 got my own design education a little
6:57:00 over a decade ago fun fact michael
6:57:03 worthington was also my teacher in the
6:57:05 mid 2000s
6:57:07 a lot has changed in web design since i
6:57:08 created my first website way back in
6:57:10 1999.
6:57:12 what hasn't changed for me is that i
6:57:13 find the web an exciting and challenging
6:57:15 medium for designers i'm so happy that
6:57:18 you are interested in learning more
6:57:19 about ux design
6:57:21 and the web in general and i hope that
6:57:24 you will have a rewarding and fun
6:57:26 learning experience
6:57:30 [Music]
6:57:35 why peer review is important to a
6:57:37 creative practice
6:57:39 design is rarely a solitary practice
6:57:42 as a designer you might be working with
6:57:44 clients or have a boss you need to
6:57:46 report to
6:57:47 you might also be part of a design team
6:57:50 or even managing a team of your own
6:57:52 you might be asked to present a project
6:57:54 to stakeholders at a company or pitch
6:57:56 your services to a new client
6:57:59 ultimately your design needs to stand
6:58:01 alone but in the process of making
6:58:04 design you should be testing out your
6:58:06 ideas with an audience getting feedback
6:58:08 and developing your design accordingly
6:58:12 design is very intertwined with
6:58:13 communication
6:58:15 and to be a successful designer you need
6:58:17 to be able to talk about your work to
6:58:19 explain it and show that you understand
6:58:22 how it's working
6:58:24 in different situations it may be
6:58:26 necessary to explain your own work and
6:58:28 other people's work in a clear and
6:58:31 constructive way
6:58:33 so in this course you'll be engaging in
6:58:35 peer review to evaluate the work of your
6:58:37 peers as well as get feedback on your
6:58:39 own work
6:58:40 if you're serious about working as a
6:58:42 designer or improving your design skills
6:58:45 consider peer review as excellent
6:58:47 practice
6:58:48 in peer review you'll be asked to
6:58:51 observe and openly reflect on what is
6:58:53 working and not working in a peer's
6:58:56 graphic design submission
6:58:58 the goal of this exercise is to help
6:59:00 your fellow designers move their work
6:59:02 forward and for you to get that same
6:59:05 advice in turn
6:59:07 additionally it's for you to practice a
6:59:09 working vocabulary and discourse around
6:59:12 making graphic design
6:59:14 all of which will help you with your
6:59:16 future path as a designer
6:59:18 the next video will give you some
6:59:20 specific tips for completing peer review
6:59:23 within the specialization
6:59:27 [Music]
6:59:30 peer review tips
6:59:32 critique and feedback are essential
6:59:34 parts of the design process
6:59:36 they're an essential way to see if your
6:59:38 design is communicating what you
6:59:39 intended to an audience
6:59:41 so in this video i'm going to outline a
6:59:43 few tips for completing peer review
6:59:45 assignments successfully in this class
6:59:48 participation in peer review
6:59:49 thoughtfully and meaningfully will help
6:59:52 you practice these indispensable skills
6:59:56 submitting assignments
6:59:58 read the instructions carefully
7:00:00 make sure you take a look at the review
7:00:02 criteria so you know how your assignment
7:00:05 will be assessed
7:00:06 review any examples your instructor may
7:00:08 have provided and upload exactly what
7:00:11 you are asked to do
7:00:12 if something in the instructions isn't
7:00:14 clear post your question in the course
7:00:16 forums so staff can assist you
7:00:20 make it your best work
7:00:22 this is your creation and your
7:00:24 creativity and should be an exercise
7:00:26 that demonstrates what you can do
7:00:29 practice assignments should be
7:00:31 opportunities to fail but a final
7:00:33 assignment should be something worthy of
7:00:35 your professional portfolio
7:00:38 ask for specific feedback
7:00:40 clarify what you need from your peers in
7:00:43 your review where applicable use the
7:00:45 designated comment field to ask for
7:00:47 specific feedback on your submission
7:00:51 submit on time
7:00:53 refer to the due date for submitting
7:00:55 your assignment on the grades page
7:00:57 within the course
7:00:58 if you're too early or too late the peer
7:01:01 review process may not work as intended
7:01:05 reviewing submissions
7:01:08 take your time with reviewing
7:01:10 don't rush this look at each part of
7:01:13 your peers submission carefully and
7:01:15 compare what has been submitted to the
7:01:17 expectations set out in the rubric
7:01:21 be objective
7:01:22 when reviewing consider your role as a
7:01:25 viewer or reader of the work
7:01:27 focus only on what the rubric asks you
7:01:29 to evaluate and try to limit your
7:01:32 personal opinions about the work in your
7:01:34 comments
7:01:36 be clear and informative
7:01:38 generic feedback such as good or okay
7:01:42 are unhelpful comments for peer reviews
7:01:44 because they don't give a your peer any
7:01:46 specific information about what was
7:01:49 working in their submission
7:01:51 likewise it doesn't help to say it
7:01:53 doesn't work or i don't like it because
7:01:56 it doesn't give your peer enough
7:01:58 information to help them reassess their
7:02:00 designs
7:02:01 try to articulate a detailed response
7:02:04 that helps to affirm your peers choices
7:02:06 in their submission or guides your peer
7:02:09 towards the goal of an assignment if it
7:02:11 appears that they are off track
7:02:14 be constructive
7:02:16 your feedback should motivate your peer
7:02:18 to make adjustments and work towards
7:02:21 improvement if there's a need to correct
7:02:23 your peer be honest but it's more
7:02:26 helpful to include specific
7:02:28 recommendations or strategies to help
7:02:30 the learner improve
7:02:32 be generous
7:02:34 recognize that everyone comes to this
7:02:36 course with a different level of
7:02:38 experience as well as a different
7:02:40 approach to making work
7:02:42 honor and value these differences
7:02:45 bad grammar and spelling shouldn't
7:02:47 contribute to a bad grade please be
7:02:49 generous
7:02:50 likewise please don't penalize students
7:02:53 for small mistakes
7:02:56 flag plagiarism and dishonest behavior
7:03:00 these violate coursera's honor code
7:03:02 if you're asked to review an assignment
7:03:04 and it appears to be plagiarized you can
7:03:06 flag it so coursera learner support are
7:03:09 notified
7:03:12 complete your reviews on time
7:03:15 refer to the due date for completing the
7:03:17 minimum number of peer reviews on the
7:03:19 grades page within the course
7:03:21 if you're too early or too late the peer
7:03:24 review process may not work as intended
7:03:28 consider reviewing more than the minimum
7:03:30 number of peers for a given assignment
7:03:33 not only is this helpful for your fellow
7:03:35 peers but it's also instructive for you
7:03:38 to see a greater range of submissions to
7:03:41 inform your own work
7:03:43 and the more you practice critique the
7:03:45 better you will get it and ultimately
7:03:47 the better your own work will be as you
7:03:50 integrate your critical abilities into
7:03:52 your own design process
7:03:56 [Music]
7:04:00 how to apply feedback to your assignment
7:04:03 so your assignments graded and you have
7:04:06 peer feedback
7:04:07 now what
7:04:08 remember the goal of peer review is to
7:04:10 help you improve your work as a maker
7:04:12 and viewer try not to see the feedback
7:04:15 you receive as either being positive or
7:04:18 negative
7:04:19 or an affirmation
7:04:21 that you're doing something absolutely
7:04:23 right or wrong
7:04:25 through peer review you're inviting
7:04:27 other perspectives on your work to see
7:04:29 and comment on things that you might not
7:04:31 see
7:04:32 by engaging in peer review you're
7:04:34 practicing the skill you'll need in your
7:04:36 creative and professional life
7:04:38 it's important to understand how an
7:04:40 audience will react to your work
7:04:44 peer feedback should never feel like a
7:04:46 personal attack
7:04:47 if you're discouraged by some feedback
7:04:49 then step back and consider why a peer
7:04:52 would react that way to your work
7:04:55 are you seeing patterns or common themes
7:04:57 in the feedback you receive is there
7:04:59 something you need to address in the
7:05:00 work
7:05:01 if you receive peer feedback that isn't
7:05:03 constructive to your work
7:05:05 that's okay it's not ideal but don't let
7:05:08 it discourage you remember
7:05:10 the goal of feedback is to help you
7:05:12 improve
7:05:13 if you need additional feedback you can
7:05:15 resubmit
7:05:17 at the end of the day remember both
7:05:19 positive and negative feedback can be
7:05:21 useful
7:05:23 positive feedback can let you know what
7:05:25 is working what to keep as is or alter
7:05:28 only slightly in your assignment
7:05:30 negative feedback might let you know
7:05:32 what needs to be changed developed or
7:05:35 reconsidered
7:05:37 as the designer you might disagree with
7:05:39 the feedback but it's always worth
7:05:41 examining someone else's point of view
7:05:44 an outside perspective can be very
7:05:46 useful especially because designers
7:05:48 often get too close to their own work
7:05:51 and sometimes can't see when parts of it
7:05:53 aren't working or communicating properly
7:05:57 if you receive a low grade consider why
7:05:59 your peers gave you that grade
7:06:01 have they justified their grading in the
7:06:03 comments
7:06:04 consider their perspectives and try
7:06:06 incorporating their suggestions into the
7:06:08 assignment and resubmit
7:06:15 [Music]
7:06:23 to start things off i will give you a
7:06:25 brief overview of the user experience
7:06:27 process that i will teach in this course
7:06:28 sequence
7:06:30 we will begin by defining the term user
7:06:32 experience and then briefly look at the
7:06:34 five phases of ux design
7:06:36 strategy outline of scope sitemap
7:06:39 wireframes and visual mockups
7:06:42 i will also talk about the differences
7:06:44 between mobile apps and websites and the
7:06:46 differences between waterfall and agile
7:06:48 approaches to ux design
7:06:51 lastly i will introduce you to the main
7:06:52 project that you will be working on in
7:06:54 this course sequence
7:06:56 and i'll get you started on your first
7:06:57 assignment
7:06:59 oh and one more thing you will meet
7:07:01 alison and bradley the two students that
7:07:03 are completing the project alongside
7:07:04 with you
7:07:05 i think you will learn a lot from them
7:07:07 as you'll get to peek over their
7:07:08 shoulders and hear them share their
7:07:10 thought process
7:07:12 all right let's get started
7:07:20 so we will be talking a lot about ux in
7:07:22 this course
7:07:24 we should start simply by defining the
7:07:26 term
7:07:27 this is something michael already
7:07:29 touched upon in the first two courses of
7:07:31 this specialization
7:07:33 but it's always good to review such
7:07:35 fundamental concepts
7:07:37 so let's get started
7:07:40 of course ux stands for
7:07:42 user experience
7:07:44 but what does that actually mean
7:07:47 here's one way to define it the
7:07:49 experience a product or service creates
7:07:52 for people in the real world
7:07:55 that's a pretty concise definition just
7:07:57 one sentence
7:07:59 but let's dig a little deeper
7:08:01 what are the words here that stand out
7:08:04 i would say the following three
7:08:06 experience
7:08:08 people
7:08:09 real world
7:08:10 let's talk about each of these briefly
7:08:13 what happens when we have an experience
7:08:16 it leaves an impression on us some sort
7:08:18 of feeling and often a memory
7:08:21 an experience is usually also
7:08:23 accompanied by disqualifying adjective
7:08:27 you could say you had an amazing
7:08:29 experience or a horrible experience
7:08:32 or the experience could be boring or
7:08:34 delightful or charming
7:08:37 or confusing or frustrating i think most
7:08:40 of you can remember interacting with a
7:08:42 website or an app
7:08:44 that could be described in these kinds
7:08:46 of terms
7:08:48 next there's the word people
7:08:51 so this is about humans
7:08:53 that's something that one should always
7:08:54 remember as a designer
7:08:56 that your creations will affect the
7:08:58 lives of actual people
7:09:00 and wouldn't you rather that your work
7:09:02 helps and delights them then annoy and
7:09:05 frustrate them
7:09:06 and lastly there's real world
7:09:10 this is to emphasize
7:09:11 that we are not talking about
7:09:13 theoretical experiences or abstract
7:09:16 people
7:09:17 your work has real world consequences
7:09:20 and
7:09:21 if your user experience is positive the
7:09:24 websites or your app's goals are much
7:09:26 more likely to be fulfilled your users
7:09:29 will be happy and more likely to buy
7:09:31 something or sign up or spend time on
7:09:34 your sites they are also more likely to
7:09:37 return
7:09:39 now here's another thing i want to
7:09:42 emphasize
7:09:43 there's a term that often seems to stick
7:09:46 to ux like some sort of inseparable
7:09:49 partner
7:09:50 i'm talking about ui which stands of
7:09:52 course for user interface
7:09:56 you hear about ui ux as a pair a lot
7:10:00 most job descriptions asks for ui ux
7:10:02 skills the specialization is titled uiux
7:10:06 it often seems like there isn't even a
7:10:08 difference between the terms
7:10:10 but that's actually not the case
7:10:13 the author golden krishna has a really
7:10:15 great way of talking about the
7:10:17 difference between ui and ux
7:10:20 golden wrote a book called the best
7:10:22 interface is no interface it's actually
7:10:24 an inspiring book and i would recommend
7:10:26 you all read it
7:10:28 here's a page from the book
7:10:30 and here's what golden identifies as
7:10:33 ui concerns
7:10:36 navigation sub-navigation drop-downs
7:10:39 buttons links windows rounded corners
7:10:42 shadowing error messages alerts updates
7:10:45 check boxes and so on
7:10:48 and then a list of what belongs under ux
7:10:52 people happiness solving problems
7:10:54 understanding needs
7:10:56 love efficiency entertainment pleasure
7:10:59 delight smiles soul etc etc
7:11:04 there's quite a difference between these
7:11:05 lists right
7:11:06 and of course it's important to design
7:11:09 effective navigation menus and beautiful
7:11:11 buttons
7:11:12 and the decision whether to use rounded
7:11:14 corners or not or exactly which shade of
7:11:17 color to use can have significant
7:11:19 implications
7:11:21 but it's most important to remember
7:11:23 why we are designing in the first place
7:11:26 to remember that in the end our work
7:11:28 will affect the lives of people so you
7:11:31 can think about user interface design as
7:11:33 part of the greater user experience
7:11:36 design universe
7:11:37 i would argue that ui is the visual
7:11:40 manifestation of ux thinking
7:11:43 and it's important to understand that
7:11:45 there are many other concerns that have
7:11:47 to be considered to design an effective
7:11:50 and successful user experience
7:11:52 and many of these concerns will also be
7:11:55 part of this course
7:11:59 we established that user experience
7:12:01 design is at its heart user-centric
7:12:04 your main goal is to make decisions that
7:12:07 will benefit your
7:12:08 users that's often easier said than done
7:12:12 and
7:12:13 it requires that you know a few things
7:12:16 who are your users anyway and what do
7:12:19 they need
7:12:20 two key questions that you will want to
7:12:22 answer very early in the design process
7:12:25 for every project
7:12:27 and sometimes the goal for your users
7:12:29 are in direct opposition to other
7:12:32 constraints
7:12:34 what about the client's needs for
7:12:36 example
7:12:37 to illustrate this think about a website
7:12:39 like facebook
7:12:41 most people probably use the site to
7:12:44 catch up with their friends
7:12:46 facebook's number one goal as a
7:12:48 for-profit company on the other hand is
7:12:51 to show as many ads as possible in order
7:12:53 to generate income
7:12:55 a big challenge for them
7:12:57 is to show ads without annoying the user
7:13:00 or better yet
7:13:01 figure out a way to show the most
7:13:04 relevant ads so that users perceive them
7:13:07 as beneficial and not evasive
7:13:10 so when we're talking about user-centric
7:13:12 design we're also talking about the need
7:13:15 to understand humans
7:13:18 as user experience designers we need to
7:13:20 take the anatomy
7:13:22 psychology and behavior of users into
7:13:25 account at every step of the design
7:13:27 process
7:13:28 now some of this goes well beyond the
7:13:30 scope of this course
7:13:32 we'll leave it to anthropologists
7:13:34 psychologists and sociologists to study
7:13:37 the human condition
7:13:38 but here are just a few examples to show
7:13:40 you how an understanding of these
7:13:42 disciplines can help ux designers
7:13:46 how does human anatomy influence the way
7:13:49 we interact with technology
7:13:51 say if you're holding a mobile phone in
7:13:53 your right hand
7:13:55 which areas of the screen are easiest or
7:13:58 hardest to reach with your thumb
7:14:00 as you can see here the bottom right
7:14:02 corner is a lot easier to reach than the
7:14:05 top left corner
7:14:06 and there is an even more fundamental
7:14:08 question
7:14:09 which hand do most people use to hold
7:14:12 their phone
7:14:13 does it matter if they are right or
7:14:15 left-handed
7:14:17 how big does a button need to be in
7:14:19 order for us to comfortably click or
7:14:21 touch it
7:14:23 how large should text be for a
7:14:26 comfortable reading experience
7:14:29 what about people that can't distinguish
7:14:32 between certain colors
7:14:36 how many things can we concentrate on at
7:14:38 once
7:14:39 turns out that basic human psychology
7:14:41 tells us this number is about seven
7:14:43 items
7:14:44 and that fact should have implications
7:14:46 on how we design menu systems
7:14:50 another question how does color affect
7:14:52 our moods
7:14:54 in terms of user behavior
7:14:56 which device are people using at which
7:14:59 time of the day
7:15:01 what messages make people more likely to
7:15:04 say yes to an offer
7:15:07 or what makes us want to click on things
7:15:11 again these are just a few examples of
7:15:14 how an understanding of human abilities
7:15:16 and limitations can help us design
7:15:18 successful user experiences
7:15:21 so now we have a definition for the term
7:15:23 user experience
7:15:25 we also discussed the difference between
7:15:27 ux and ui
7:15:28 and we talked about the importance of
7:15:30 user-centric design
7:15:32 in the next lesson we will look at a
7:15:34 summary of the ux process that will
7:15:37 guide us through the rest of this course
7:15:39 sequence
7:15:41 this lesson will provide a brief
7:15:43 overview about the entire ux process
7:15:45 that we will be following in this course
7:15:47 this is by the way also the process that
7:15:49 i follow when i work on my own web
7:15:51 design projects so i'm definitely
7:15:53 teaching you what i practice every day
7:15:56 i want to start though by contemplating
7:15:58 what your design process could look like
7:16:00 if you just jumped right in without much
7:16:02 of a structured methodology
7:16:05 let's say you have a client who needs a
7:16:07 website and you're like great i'll just
7:16:10 need some content and i'll get started
7:16:11 on the homepage design right away
7:16:14 i'll be honest when i started out as a
7:16:15 web designer this scenario wasn't all
7:16:18 that far from what i would be doing
7:16:20 needless to say this way of working
7:16:22 wasn't ideal and once i started to
7:16:24 implement a more structured process my
7:16:26 projects turned out to be much more
7:16:28 successful and i had a much better time
7:16:30 working on them
7:16:31 one of the main problems is that things
7:16:33 can get out of hand quite quickly
7:16:37 to illustrate this i'd like to point to
7:16:39 an anecdote i found in an article from
7:16:41 the website smashing magazine the
7:16:43 article is about the importance of an
7:16:45 organized planning process
7:16:47 and the author begins this piece with an
7:16:50 account of things going bad really
7:16:52 quickly it says
7:16:54 on day one things are great you've
7:16:56 landed a new job the client is excited
7:16:58 you're stoked and the project will be
7:17:00 great first things first you have to
7:17:02 collect the main materials to begin the
7:17:04 design
7:17:05 you send the client an email asking for
7:17:07 what you need
7:17:08 on day two you get the following a tiff
7:17:11 logo and cmyk via email a set of logo
7:17:14 standards that include the rgb values
7:17:16 via a separate email
7:17:18 a disk full of photos with meaningless
7:17:20 names a fax that labels the photos
7:17:23 according to their file names
7:17:25 an email that lays out the top and
7:17:26 second level navigation
7:17:29 a phone call that makes last minutes
7:17:30 changes to the navigation
7:17:33 and email with a doc attachment full of
7:17:35 text
7:17:36 and on day three you get an email that
7:17:38 makes half of the junk you got yesterday
7:17:40 obsolete you're only three days in and
7:17:43 the project is already no fun
7:17:45 clearly no one likes working this way
7:17:48 you as the designer need a saner
7:17:50 methodology so you can be productive and
7:17:52 do great work and quite frankly the
7:17:54 client deserves a saner process also
7:17:57 i actually believe that when clients
7:17:59 seemingly do crazy things or they're
7:18:01 being difficult it's often because the
7:18:03 designer has not properly educated the
7:18:05 client to do the right thing
7:18:07 it's quite possible that your client has
7:18:09 never been involved in a website design
7:18:11 project before
7:18:13 they are looking for you as the expert
7:18:15 to offer guidance and make the process
7:18:17 productive and positive so to summarize
7:18:20 here's why we need a structured
7:18:22 predefined process that we can follow
7:18:24 for each web design project
7:18:27 you'll know exactly what to do next
7:18:29 instead of just winging it
7:18:30 and you'll have a formalized methodology
7:18:33 you can employ for every project in the
7:18:35 future
7:18:36 and you can adjust it to fit your needs
7:18:38 as you gain more experience
7:18:41 you keep the client involved and
7:18:42 accountable you'll also ensure that the
7:18:45 project stays on track and on deadline
7:18:48 your design choices are based on
7:18:50 predefined goals and target audiences
7:18:53 this is in contrast to making decisions
7:18:55 based solely on intuition
7:18:58 you'll figure out what you're building
7:19:00 and what not
7:19:01 the second part is just as important as
7:19:03 the first so for example knowing that
7:19:06 your e-commerce size will not include
7:19:08 payment processing because you want to
7:19:10 launch quickly and payment processing
7:19:12 can be quite complex
7:19:14 allows you to move forward with clarity
7:19:17 and you can look for alternatives
7:19:19 so you might for example decide to use
7:19:21 paypal to accept payments
7:19:23 and lastly you have a greater chance to
7:19:25 stay sane and have fun
7:19:28 and i think that we all do better work
7:19:29 when we have a positive working
7:19:31 experience and when we have fun
7:19:34 okay
7:19:35 now that i have hopefully convinced you
7:19:37 that a structured process is important
7:19:40 it's finally time to talk about what
7:19:42 that process actually looks like
7:19:45 the ux process as i follow it in my own
7:19:47 practice and as i will teach it to you
7:19:49 in this course sequence follows five
7:19:52 main phases
7:19:53 here they are strategy outline of scope
7:19:56 sitemap wireframes and visual mockups
7:20:00 we will cover each of these phases in
7:20:02 detail roughly one phase per week and
7:20:05 you will gain experience and a deeper
7:20:07 understanding of each phase by
7:20:08 practicing what you learn while working
7:20:10 on a project that will span the duration
7:20:12 of this course i will introduce the
7:20:14 project at the end of this week's
7:20:16 lectures
7:20:18 for now let me give you a quick overview
7:20:20 of each phase i will illustrate the
7:20:22 deliveries for each phase with examples
7:20:24 from a web design project that i have
7:20:26 worked on
7:20:27 a few years ago my company yay brigade
7:20:29 was tasked to redesign the website for
7:20:31 the pasadena conservatory of music and
7:20:34 i'll use this project's process as an
7:20:37 example
7:20:38 one last note though i have to give some
7:20:40 credit to an influential book called the
7:20:42 elements of user experience by jesse
7:20:44 james garrett i was exposed to a lot of
7:20:47 the concepts that i'm teaching in this
7:20:48 course for the first time while reading
7:20:50 this book many years ago
7:20:52 in fact the book has been one of the top
7:20:54 influential texts for the web design
7:20:57 community
7:20:58 our process starts with the strategy
7:21:00 phase
7:21:00 here we are asking a lot of fundamental
7:21:03 questions
7:21:04 like what are our goals who are our
7:21:06 users
7:21:07 what are their needs
7:21:09 here are some pages from a large
7:21:11 strategy document that we prepared for
7:21:13 the pasadena conservatory
7:21:15 you see that we defined objectives for
7:21:17 the site and determined who our users
7:21:19 are we also performed an exercise to
7:21:22 figure out what voice our website would
7:21:24 use if it was a human being
7:21:28 the strategy phase also includes visual
7:21:30 research so we look closely at a lot of
7:21:32 websites of other music schools and
7:21:35 educational
7:21:36 and non-profit organizations
7:21:40 the next phase is to create an outline
7:21:42 of scope this is basically a list of
7:21:45 content and functionality that needs to
7:21:47 be included in the site to fulfill the
7:21:49 goals set up in the strategy phase
7:21:52 next up is the site map here the content
7:21:55 and functionality from the previous
7:21:57 phase is structured into a cohesive
7:21:59 whole
7:22:00 here we also determine the navigational
7:22:02 structure of the site and we'll make
7:22:04 sure to use precise labels for the
7:22:06 navigation categories
7:22:09 speaking of labeling things for the
7:22:11 pasadena conservatory side we spent a
7:22:13 lot of time discussing the terms for
7:22:15 children and for adults
7:22:17 we knew that it was important to have
7:22:19 separate landing pages for younger and
7:22:21 older students but we also realized that
7:22:23 using the term children is not ideal for
7:22:25 teenagers
7:22:27 but we didn't want to create three
7:22:28 different landing pages for children
7:22:30 teens and adults
7:22:32 another solution we contemplated was to
7:22:34 name the first section for children and
7:22:37 teens but that seemed too clunky in the
7:22:40 end we decided to stick with children
7:22:42 and adults which though not perfect seem
7:22:44 to be the best compromise
7:22:47 the fourth phase is about creating
7:22:49 wireframes
7:22:50 here our focus shifts from the site as a
7:22:52 whole to individual web pages
7:22:55 wireframes are simple sketches of the
7:22:57 structural makeup of a web page you can
7:23:00 see here for example wireframes for the
7:23:02 homepage on the left and the for
7:23:04 children landing page on the right and
7:23:06 each of these pages was marked up to
7:23:08 address small and large screens
7:23:11 here are wireframes to show how we
7:23:13 imagine the navigation menus to work on
7:23:15 big and small screens
7:23:17 you'll notice that higher level concerns
7:23:20 such as which typefaces we will use and
7:23:23 which images and which colors are not
7:23:25 addressed here at all
7:23:27 you can think about wireframes kind of
7:23:29 like architectural blueprints they show
7:23:32 what goes where without getting hung up
7:23:34 on finer design details
7:23:37 and then lastly we have the visual
7:23:39 mock-up phase
7:23:41 now it's finally time to determine the
7:23:43 visual identity of the website
7:23:46 for graphic designers this is obviously
7:23:48 the funnest part of the process you get
7:23:50 to think about colors and typefaces
7:23:52 you'll figure out how images are treated
7:23:55 maybe you create textures or icons or
7:23:57 illustrations
7:23:59 you can see here that we worked on the
7:24:01 visual mockups for the pasadena
7:24:02 conservatory in adobe illustrator
7:24:06 i like that tool since it's easy to
7:24:07 create multiple artboards to investigate
7:24:10 different design options
7:24:12 other designers like the application
7:24:14 sketch
7:24:15 or others use photoshop or adobe xd
7:24:19 so that's it these five steps will form
7:24:22 the structure of our process in this
7:24:24 course sequence
7:24:26 you will learn about these steps in the
7:24:28 following weeks and you will use them
7:24:30 for the class project i will assign soon
7:24:33 and hopefully you will use these tools
7:24:35 in your web design work after you
7:24:37 complete the course
7:24:40 there are a few additional things i want
7:24:42 to point out in conjunction with these
7:24:44 five ux phases i think that it's helpful
7:24:47 to realize that the concerns you're
7:24:49 addressing in each of these steps shift
7:24:51 over time at first you're dealing with
7:24:53 very abstract matters and along the way
7:24:56 things become more and more concrete
7:24:59 you could also say that in the beginning
7:25:01 phases you're not at all concerned about
7:25:04 the final shape of the sides
7:25:06 when you contemplate your goals and your
7:25:08 users and the strategy phase it doesn't
7:25:10 really matter yet what kind of grid your
7:25:12 pages will eventually have or if your
7:25:15 background color will be yellow or if
7:25:17 you will employ a sans serif versus a
7:25:20 serif typeface
7:25:21 such decisions which very much determine
7:25:24 the final shape of the site are
7:25:26 addressed in later phases of the ux
7:25:28 design process
7:25:29 by the way one realization in the
7:25:31 initial strategy phase might be that you
7:25:34 shouldn't be building a website at all
7:25:36 maybe you realize that a printed piece
7:25:38 or a video are much better suited to
7:25:40 fulfill the goals of the project again
7:25:43 in the beginning of the project the
7:25:45 shape of the final product is wide open
7:25:48 also you'll notice that so far i've been
7:25:51 talking about this process in a very
7:25:52 linear fashion
7:25:54 in this diagram one project phase neatly
7:25:57 flows into the next
7:25:59 the reality however is often a little
7:26:01 bit more messy
7:26:03 in fact it's often important to
7:26:05 re-evaluate decisions made earlier as
7:26:07 new realities become clear to you
7:26:10 there should be flexibility to change
7:26:13 decisions made during an earlier phase
7:26:15 that later turn out to be untenable
7:26:18 that doesn't mean that you should change
7:26:19 your target audience or your main goals
7:26:22 at the very end of the project that
7:26:24 would probably mean that the process has
7:26:26 failed you
7:26:27 but you might for example realize that
7:26:28 your wireframes need some adjusting as
7:26:31 you work on the visual mockups
7:26:34 so the last question that you might
7:26:36 wonder is what happens after the visual
7:26:39 mock-up phase
7:26:40 well then it's time to actually build
7:26:42 the website
7:26:43 which means coding it that it can be
7:26:45 displayed in a web browser
7:26:47 or if you're working on an app it needs
7:26:49 to be coded so it can work on a mobile
7:26:51 device
7:26:52 in a later class i will be talking about
7:26:55 the coding languages that make websites
7:26:57 work namely html css and javascript
7:27:01 and i will address the question if it's
7:27:03 important as a web designer to know how
7:27:05 to code
7:27:07 i myself have a deep background in
7:27:09 coding and after the visual mockups are
7:27:11 completed i will myself start coding the
7:27:14 website i designs
7:27:16 but you might collaborate with a
7:27:18 programmer who will code the site
7:27:20 in any event after the development there
7:27:23 would be a testing phase and after that
7:27:25 the site would launch
7:27:27 so to complete the story of the pasadena
7:27:30 conservatory of music website here is
7:27:32 how the site launched a few years ago
7:27:36 and this is what the website looks like
7:27:37 today as i'm recording this video
7:27:44 let me speak quickly about two terms
7:27:46 that you might have heard about or if
7:27:48 you haven't you'll probably hear about
7:27:50 them soon as you deepen your knowledge
7:27:52 of user experience design
7:27:55 the terms are waterfall and agile and
7:27:58 they represent two different approaches
7:28:00 to ux
7:28:01 there's actually quite a bit of
7:28:02 discussion argument and controversy
7:28:05 about these terms there are factions and
7:28:08 turf wars and it's all pretty
7:28:10 fascinating and once you've had a bit of
7:28:12 experience as a ux designer you'll
7:28:14 probably want to join a side of the
7:28:16 argument yourself
7:28:18 so what do these terms actually mean
7:28:22 the waterfall approach is a more
7:28:23 traditional way of developing websites
7:28:26 and apps
7:28:27 remember all the phases we talked about
7:28:29 in the previous lesson
7:28:30 i explained how each phase is built upon
7:28:33 the previous one
7:28:34 and although there can and should be
7:28:36 some overlap it's still a fairly
7:28:39 sequential process one phase follows the
7:28:42 next and in the end there's a big launch
7:28:45 it's just like a waterfall where the
7:28:48 water starts at the top and plunges down
7:28:50 in a rather linear fashion
7:28:52 hence the name
7:28:54 for many smaller websites this process
7:28:57 makes a lot of sense you complete the ux
7:29:00 process then you develop test and launch
7:29:02 there might be smaller changes here and
7:29:04 there but the development of the project
7:29:06 is completed
7:29:08 using such a process for big and complex
7:29:11 projects however can be treacherous
7:29:14 it's tempting to want to get everything
7:29:16 completely right before showing your
7:29:18 site or app to the world
7:29:20 working with a waterfall process can
7:29:22 mean years of development before your
7:29:24 project sees the light of day
7:29:27 and since nothing is tested until late
7:29:29 in the process chances are pretty high
7:29:32 that the project went down the wrong
7:29:33 path and the final product has serious
7:29:36 flaws that cannot be easily corrected
7:29:39 and by now lots of time and money has
7:29:41 already been spent
7:29:43 that's where an agile process offers an
7:29:45 advantage
7:29:47 to stay with the water metaphor you can
7:29:49 think of agile more like the wheel of a
7:29:51 water mill
7:29:53 the agile process is circular and
7:29:56 iterative
7:29:57 instead of planning for one big launch
7:29:58 at the very end of a long process there
7:30:01 will be many launches along the way the
7:30:03 goal is not to get everything exactly
7:30:06 right the first time but to get
7:30:08 something in front of a user as quickly
7:30:10 as possible this way ideas can be tested
7:30:13 by getting actual user feedback and the
7:30:15 product can be shaped over time
7:30:18 with the agile process the product is
7:30:20 never really done instead there is an
7:30:23 ever evolving process that includes
7:30:25 cycles of user experience design
7:30:27 development testing and launch in
7:30:30 silicon valley speak people often talk
7:30:32 about mvp
7:30:34 and no this has nothing to do with the
7:30:36 most valuable player in the ux world
7:30:39 this acronym stands for minimum viable
7:30:43 products and it means that companies
7:30:45 should focus on all the aspects of a
7:30:47 product that are absolutely vital
7:30:50 everything else is secondary and can be
7:30:52 added later after the initial launch
7:30:55 to be sure a lot of startup companies
7:30:57 pride themselves using an agile
7:30:59 development process and for many apps
7:31:02 and web apps that makes absolute sense
7:31:05 think about gmail or facebook or
7:31:07 instagram for example or uber or airbnb
7:31:11 or amazon
7:31:12 these sites and services are constantly
7:31:15 being developed and they constantly
7:31:17 change they are on a never-ending
7:31:19 development cycle and they are never
7:31:21 really completed and their current
7:31:23 incarnation compared to what they were
7:31:25 when they first launched is quite
7:31:27 different
7:31:28 so where does this leave us and this
7:31:30 course well we'll be looking at things
7:31:32 through more of a water folly lens
7:31:35 since many of you will be confronted
7:31:36 with the ux process for the very first
7:31:39 time we'll talk about each phase one at
7:31:41 a time sequentially roughly one per week
7:31:45 once you complete this course sequence
7:31:47 however you should have enough
7:31:49 background in ux methodology that you
7:31:51 will be able to join an agile team
7:31:53 without problems
7:31:55 remember there will always be more to
7:31:57 learn
7:31:58 for now you should just know these terms
7:32:00 waterfall and agile and keep your ears
7:32:03 sharpened when you hear other people
7:32:04 voice their opinions about the pros and
7:32:07 cons of each
7:32:09 websites and apps seem at the surface
7:32:11 quite similar to each other
7:32:13 and they do have a lot in common
7:32:15 each is an interactive medium that is
7:32:17 accessed on a digital device
7:32:20 they also share many fundamental
7:32:22 usability patterns such as scrolling and
7:32:24 the ability to click or tap on things to
7:32:27 navigate
7:32:28 yet differences between apps and
7:32:30 websites do exist and i find that people
7:32:32 often have a hard time understanding
7:32:34 what these differences are
7:32:36 and for you as a ux designer it's
7:32:38 important to understand the advantages
7:32:40 and disadvantages of these mediums
7:32:43 you will likely encounter clients that
7:32:45 want to know your opinion whether they
7:32:47 should develop an app or a website
7:32:50 just to be clear you understand what i
7:32:52 mean when i say website and app
7:32:55 a website is accessed through a browser
7:32:57 via a website address or url
7:33:01 an app on the other hand is downloaded
7:33:03 and installed onto a device
7:33:05 if you have an iphone or an ipad you
7:33:07 would use the apple app store if you are
7:33:10 on an android device you would download
7:33:13 apps from google play what other
7:33:15 differences are there between websites
7:33:17 and apps
7:33:18 websites at least if they are coded
7:33:21 correctly as a responsive website are
7:33:23 malleable enough to work on any
7:33:25 operating system any device any screen
7:33:27 size and any browser
7:33:30 so you can easily go to let's say
7:33:32 www.google.com
7:33:35 no matter if you are on a mac computer
7:33:38 or windows pc
7:33:39 or you can be on an iphone or an android
7:33:41 phone
7:33:42 even a kindle comes with a web browser
7:33:45 and it doesn't matter if you have a
7:33:46 really small screen like a phone or a
7:33:48 medium screen like a tablet or a giant
7:33:51 desktop computer screen and it doesn't
7:33:54 really matter if you use google chrome
7:33:56 as your browser or safari or firefox or
7:33:59 microsoft edge
7:34:01 now apps are quite the opposites apps
7:34:04 are native to the specific device they
7:34:06 are running on
7:34:08 so if you are developing an app you must
7:34:10 create one version for iphones and
7:34:13 another version for android phones and
7:34:15 yet another version for windows phones
7:34:18 and so on i would also argue that the
7:34:21 web is a very democratic medium
7:34:24 its open source nature allows users to
7:34:26 have a lot of control over what to do
7:34:28 with the content of websites they visit
7:34:32 they can highlight text and copy it for
7:34:34 example they can download images from a
7:34:36 website
7:34:38 they can even install a browser
7:34:39 extension to change the way a website
7:34:42 appears and functions
7:34:43 for example you can use an ad blocker to
7:34:45 remove advertisements automatically from
7:34:48 a website
7:34:49 the functionality of apps on the other
7:34:51 hand is mostly restricted to what the
7:34:54 app designer intended
7:34:56 i'm personally very excited about the
7:34:58 medium of the web because it is such an
7:35:00 open and democratic medium
7:35:03 i also enjoy the challenge of designing
7:35:05 websites that work well on any screen
7:35:07 size
7:35:08 still there are pros and cons for each
7:35:11 so let's go through some of them here is
7:35:14 the case for apps
7:35:16 apps can have a much better integration
7:35:18 into the operating system
7:35:20 an app on your phone can have access to
7:35:23 any special device capabilities like the
7:35:26 camera flash for example or the
7:35:28 microphone or the peer-to-peer bluetooth
7:35:31 connectivity
7:35:32 for security reasons access for websites
7:35:35 to many of these advanced tools is
7:35:37 limited
7:35:38 also for apps no extra ui from the
7:35:41 browser gets in the way think about the
7:35:43 back and forward buttons that a web
7:35:45 browser constantly displays
7:35:48 apps are not handicapped by certain
7:35:50 browser limitations either
7:35:52 app developers can sometimes create
7:35:54 certain user interface solutions that
7:35:56 are just not possible in web browsers
7:36:00 if the goal for your project is to
7:36:01 create revenue
7:36:02 apps can have an advantage
7:36:05 you can sell your app through an app
7:36:06 store they don't have to be free
7:36:09 also in-app purchases are quite easy and
7:36:11 streamlined
7:36:13 because smartphone users will already
7:36:15 have entered their credit card
7:36:16 information when they set up their phone
7:36:19 and there's prestige
7:36:21 apps can be perceived as cooler and
7:36:23 sexier than websites
7:36:26 but there is a case against apps as well
7:36:29 users need to download and install an
7:36:31 app in the first place
7:36:32 that's a lot to ask from a user it
7:36:35 doesn't make sense for example to
7:36:37 install an app for a cafe let's say just
7:36:39 to find out what their opening times are
7:36:42 there are gatekeepers the apples googles
7:36:45 and microsoft's of this world
7:36:48 it can even happen that your app is not
7:36:50 approved apps are also quite expensive
7:36:52 to develop and remember that you'll need
7:36:55 a separate app for each device and
7:36:57 operating system
7:36:59 so websites have a few things going for
7:37:01 them
7:37:02 for one they are easy to access you just
7:37:04 enter a web address in your browser and
7:37:07 the site loads
7:37:09 there's no need to install anything
7:37:12 we already talked about websites working
7:37:14 across all devices platforms and screen
7:37:16 sizes
7:37:18 since the web is built upon the idea of
7:37:20 addresses and links you can easily
7:37:22 bookmark a webpage or send it to a
7:37:25 friend
7:37:26 for content that needs to be found in a
7:37:28 search engine a website is a better way
7:37:31 to go
7:37:32 websites are also more economical to
7:37:34 develop html and css are pretty easy to
7:37:37 learn and all you need is a hosting
7:37:39 account and a website address which are
7:37:42 easy to obtain
7:37:44 and here are some drawbacks
7:37:46 capabilities of websites are directly
7:37:49 related to the capabilities of web
7:37:50 browsers so our websites will only be as
7:37:53 good as the browsers that display them
7:37:56 websites are also harder to monetize
7:37:59 their open nature just makes it hard to
7:38:01 control content and there there's a
7:38:03 certain sense that web content just
7:38:05 wants to be free so users can seem
7:38:07 reluctant to pay for it
7:38:09 lastly there can be security issues
7:38:13 since
7:38:14 there aren't any gatekeepers for the web
7:38:16 it's much more of a wild west place with
7:38:19 some pretty dark corners
7:38:21 i want to share a curious historical
7:38:23 footnote
7:38:24 this is a wired magazine cover from 2010
7:38:28 the web is dead it boldly proclaims
7:38:31 at the time it seemed like apps would
7:38:33 replace websites entirely
7:38:36 that of course has not happened and with
7:38:38 better browsers and better web standards
7:38:40 the web has had quite a resurgence since
7:38:43 then
7:38:44 the question of apps versus web is
7:38:47 probably not even an either or
7:38:49 proposition these days
7:38:51 you'll notice that many companies have a
7:38:53 website and an app
7:38:55 but the bottom line might be this
7:38:58 everyone needs a website not everyone
7:39:01 needs an app
7:39:02 i have already mentioned that in this
7:39:04 course we are primarily looking at user
7:39:06 experience design through the prism of
7:39:08 designing websites but rest assured that
7:39:11 many of the tools and techniques you
7:39:12 will learn here are applicable for
7:39:14 designing apps as well
