7:45:09 welcome back everyone i hope that you
7:45:11 all came up with exciting ideas for your
7:45:13 projects
7:45:14 this week is all about strategy we will
7:45:17 talk about how to conduct research in
7:45:19 the beginning of a project and i will
7:45:21 tell you about the importance of
7:45:23 defining a target audience for your
7:45:25 website
7:45:26 we'll also discuss how to determine user
7:45:28 needs and client needs
7:45:30 the strategy that you develop in this
7:45:32 first step of the ux process will
7:45:34 influence all decisions you make further
7:45:36 down the line that's why it's so
7:45:38 important to take the time and think
7:45:40 about what you want to accomplish what
7:45:42 the goals are and how they might be
7:45:44 measured when the project launches
7:45:47 as a bonus you'll get to watch my
7:45:48 interview with craig cook founder and
7:45:51 ceo of the digital marketing agency
7:45:53 rhythm
7:45:54 craic was actually my web design teacher
7:45:56 way back in the year 2000s and he hired
7:45:59 me for my first job as a designer
7:46:01 our conversation is centered around the
7:46:03 importance of strategy as part of the ux
7:46:06 process
7:46:10 in the previous week we established the
7:46:12 centrality of the user in our design
7:46:15 process
7:46:16 in this video i want to discuss a few
7:46:18 research tools that can help you
7:46:20 figuring out how your users think and
7:46:22 behave
7:46:23 now with big stakes and big budgets come
7:46:26 a lot of fancy tools to research user
7:46:28 behavior
7:46:30 imagine for example the effort of
7:46:32 creating a new version of the mac os or
7:46:34 windows operating systems
7:46:37 the fact that both of these systems
7:46:38 combined probably touch almost everyone
7:46:40 in the world makes the stakes for such
7:46:43 projects quite high
7:46:45 it can be assumed that apple and
7:46:47 microsoft spend a lot of resources on
7:46:49 scientifically studying how users
7:46:51 interact with their software another
7:46:54 high stakes example is the website
7:46:56 healthcare.gov
7:46:57 which sees millions of users trying to
7:46:59 sign up for health insurance here's an
7:47:02 example of eye tracking experiments that
7:47:04 were conducted for this website on the
7:47:06 left you can see a heat map of where
7:47:09 users eyes spend the most amount of time
7:47:11 and on the right you'll see the pathway
7:47:14 a user's eyes traveled between different
7:47:17 areas of focus
7:47:19 now i think this is really fascinating
7:47:21 but such tools are expensive and
7:47:23 probably not available to you right now
7:47:27 as a matter of fact they are not
7:47:28 available for most smaller scale
7:47:30 projects but here's the thing
7:47:33 you can still do some lower tech
7:47:35 versions of user research yourself
7:47:38 here are a few ideas
7:47:40 you can always start by using yourself
7:47:42 as a test subject and monitor your
7:47:45 reactions to a website or app closely
7:47:49 or ask a friend to visit a website and
7:47:51 look over their shoulder
7:47:54 interview a friend or a family member
7:47:57 create a questionnaire and send it to a
7:47:59 few people you know
7:48:00 or use a third party application
7:48:04 later on in this course i will show you
7:48:06 a tool called tree jack that offers a
7:48:08 great way to test a navigational
7:48:10 structure on actual users
7:48:13 you will explore some of these methods
7:48:14 in more detail as the course progresses
7:48:17 the bottom line is that it's often
7:48:19 helpful to ask actual users for their
7:48:22 opinions and feedback
7:48:24 there are many complex and expensive
7:48:25 ways to go about this
7:48:27 but with a little ingenuity and by
7:48:29 tapping your network you can get pretty
7:48:31 far by yourself
7:48:34 in the beginning of a project it is
7:48:36 helpful to take some time to look around
7:48:38 at what already exists in this world
7:48:41 that's an essential step in every
7:48:43 designer's process
7:48:45 if you're designing a new logo you look
7:48:47 at other logos if you're designing a new
7:48:49 magazine you go to a bookstore and look
7:48:52 at a bunch of magazines
7:48:54 and if you're designing a new website
7:48:55 well you should look at a lot of other
7:48:57 websites
7:48:59 it's definitely helpful to look at the
7:49:00 direct competition of whatever project
7:49:02 you're working on
7:49:04 when my design studio worked on the new
7:49:06 site for the pasadena conservatory of
7:49:08 music we looked at the websites of a lot
7:49:10 of other music schools
7:49:12 but we also looked more generally at
7:49:14 schools and universities and non-profits
7:49:18 when we started designing the site for
7:49:20 the marciano art foundation a new art
7:49:23 museum in los angeles we looked at the
7:49:25 websites of all art museums in los
7:49:28 angeles and also comparable institutions
7:49:31 nationwide
7:49:33 at the beginning of a project it's also
7:49:35 useful to ask your client what websites
7:49:37 they like and why
7:49:40 you don't necessarily want to get into a
7:49:41 position where you are asked to copy
7:49:43 something that already exists but it's
7:49:46 good to have an understanding of what
7:49:48 your client gravitates to and what they
7:49:51 absolutely dislike
7:49:52 they will after all sign off on what you
7:49:55 eventually design
7:49:58 now you might find that looking just at
7:50:00 your direct competition isn't always all
7:50:02 that inspiring
7:50:04 i would suggest that you also get
7:50:06 inspiration by looking at the very best
7:50:08 website design out there and even more
7:50:11 important
7:50:12 as a web designer you should make it a
7:50:14 point to be aware of what is happening
7:50:16 in the web design industry at large
7:50:19 what are the most and recent cool and
7:50:21 innovative websites
7:50:23 what are the trends
7:50:25 who are the interesting makers and
7:50:26 thinkers in our community
7:50:29 what technological innovations are
7:50:31 happening
7:50:32 here are a few recommendations of how to
7:50:35 stay in the know
7:50:37 there are many showcase websites that
7:50:39 collect the most outstanding examples of
7:50:41 web design
7:50:43 decide awards
7:50:44 and note what they're doing there with
7:50:46 the 3ws is a great resource
7:50:50 it's a crowdsourced platform where users
7:50:52 vote on designs submitted
7:50:54 the projects that get the highest scores
7:50:57 get awarded honorable mentions or site
7:51:00 of the day side of the month or set of
7:51:02 the year
7:51:03 you can browse the archives to find a
7:51:05 lot of cool stuff and i would even
7:51:08 encourage you to create your own account
7:51:10 and start judging some of the entries
7:51:11 yourself this will force you to look at
7:51:14 a website critically to determine what
7:51:16 scores for design usability creativity
7:51:19 and content you would give plus it's
7:51:22 kind of fun other showcase sites you
7:51:24 should check out are site inspire
7:51:27 one page love
7:51:29 http store
7:51:30 and the webby awards
7:51:33 there's really a whole lot of them and
7:51:35 these are just some suggestions
7:51:38 another great way to stay informed is to
7:51:41 follow the leaders of the web design
7:51:42 industry and read what they have to say
7:51:45 you know the people that write books or
7:51:48 important blog posts or talk at
7:51:50 conferences
7:51:51 here are a few of the people that i
7:51:53 really like and their twitter handles
7:51:56 there are a few blogs that are
7:51:58 tremendously helpful inspiring and
7:52:00 influential
7:52:02 a list apart by the way is one of the
7:52:04 oldest web design blogs around it just
7:52:07 celebrated its 20th birthday
7:52:10 i find it super informative and an
7:52:12 amazing resource
7:52:14 in fact you will notice quite a few
7:52:16 references to articles on this side in
7:52:18 this course sequence
7:52:21 i also find it quite helpful to sign up
7:52:23 for weekly web design email newsletters
7:52:26 here are three great ones that distill
7:52:28 the most current happenings in the web
7:52:30 design world into a weekly digest
7:52:34 and if you're into podcasts here are two
7:52:36 that i really enjoy
7:52:39 the bottom line is this there's a lot of
7:52:41 information out there and much of it is
7:52:44 free you just have to go and get it
7:52:46 the sheer volume of all the resources
7:52:49 can actually be quite overwhelming
7:52:52 it takes time and energy to keep up with
7:52:54 a fast-moving industry but it pays
7:52:56 dividends to take the time to learn from
7:52:58 other people of course i probably don't
7:53:01 have to preach this so hard to you
7:53:03 you are after all taking this course
7:53:06 eager to learn
7:53:08 one of the best ways to get insight into
7:53:10 user behavior is with actual data
7:53:13 if you are redesigning an existing site
7:53:15 chances are that you have access to
7:53:17 visitor stats of the current site that
7:53:19 can tell you an awful lot about your
7:53:21 current users
7:53:23 the most common tool used to track user
7:53:26 data is google analytics
7:53:28 it's enabled by placing a tiny bit of
7:53:30 code onto every page of your site which
7:53:33 then captures user activity data for you
7:53:35 to evaluate later
7:53:37 this allows you to find answers to
7:53:39 questions like how many people visit the
7:53:41 site each day
7:53:43 as i mentioned having actual hard data
7:53:46 can be very powerful
7:53:48 but any data analysts will tell you that
7:53:50 it's important to interpret data
7:53:52 correctly
7:53:53 otherwise it can be a little bit like
7:53:55 reading tea leaves
7:53:57 for example if you found out that your
7:53:59 site had 1000 daily visitors is that a
7:54:01 lot or not does it mean the site is
7:54:04 successful or not
7:54:06 if your goal for the site was to grow
7:54:08 the amount of visitors to a hundred
7:54:10 thousand it would be quite a failure
7:54:12 on the other hand if your site only had
7:54:14 a hundred daily visitors in the past
7:54:16 this would present a tenfold increase
7:54:20 i think that website stats are best
7:54:22 approached with specific questions in
7:54:24 mind before you look at the data
7:54:27 here are some example questions that are
7:54:29 more specific
7:54:31 what percentage of my users visit the
7:54:33 site on a mobile device
7:54:36 or we ran a facebook ad last week did
7:54:39 this increase the amount of visitors
7:54:42 how many users still use internet
7:54:44 explorer
7:54:46 are my users mostly local or is there a
7:54:48 sizable worldwide audience
7:54:51 let's take a quick tour of google
7:54:53 analytics so you can get an idea of how
7:54:55 the interface works
7:54:57 we'll use as an example a website that
7:54:59 my business partner nicole and i created
7:55:01 to showcase our favorite albums of the
7:55:04 year
7:55:05 you can take a look at the website at
7:55:07 favorite albums
7:55:09 2017.yay brigade.com
7:55:12 we worked hard to make it a fun
7:55:14 interactive experience
7:55:16 now when i log on to google analytics i
7:55:20 can find out a lot about the visitors of
7:55:22 this site
7:55:24 i can see how many users are currently
7:55:26 on this site
7:55:27 right now there's just one so that must
7:55:29 be me
7:55:30 in fact
7:55:31 here you can see that the visitor is
7:55:33 from los angeles so yeah it's me
7:55:37 you can also see how many visitors came
7:55:39 to the site over time
7:55:42 here are the stats for the last week
7:55:44 or i can look at the last 30 days
7:55:47 or a custom time period
7:55:50 you can see the site launched in late
7:55:52 december
7:55:53 you can also see the total amount of
7:55:56 visitors and how long they stayed on the
7:55:58 site
7:56:00 i'd say the average interaction with the
7:56:02 side of almost two minutes is pretty
7:56:04 good
7:56:05 if it was only a few seconds i would be
7:56:07 worried that the site is not really
7:56:08 working at all
7:56:11 so what are some specific questions i
7:56:13 could
7:56:14 ask here's one
7:56:17 we entered the site and some designed
7:56:18 competitions did this make a difference
7:56:22 let's go to acquisition
7:56:24 you see that about three-quarters of
7:56:26 visitors came to the site by clicking a
7:56:28 link on another site we can explore this
7:56:31 further
7:56:32 now you see a list of the top referral
7:56:34 sources
7:56:35 i can switch to a pie chart view
7:56:38 and it's obvious that over 92 percent of
7:56:41 these referrals came from two sides
7:56:43 awards and one page love
7:56:46 exactly the sides whose competitions we
7:56:48 entered
7:56:50 so to answer my question
7:56:52 yes it did make a difference that we
7:56:54 entered the site and design competitions
7:56:57 that's helpful information for the
7:56:58 future
7:57:00 here's another question
7:57:02 i know that because of the experimental
7:57:04 nature of the site it works best on a
7:57:06 desktop computer with a mouse and not so
7:57:09 well on a mobile device
7:57:11 so the question is
7:57:13 do we have a lot of mobile viewers
7:57:15 should we spend the resources to make
7:57:18 the mobile experience better
7:57:20 i can click on audience mobile and i
7:57:23 find out that almost 95 percent of all
7:57:25 visitors were on a desktop computer
7:57:28 so we probably don't have to worry too
7:57:30 much about this issue
7:57:32 you probably agree that google analytics
7:57:34 is a really powerful and complex tool
7:57:37 it would probably take an entire course
7:57:40 all by itself if you wanted to learn all
7:57:42 of its capabilities in fact google
7:57:45 offers detailed free courses to learn
7:57:47 more about their products
7:57:49 but finding out a few fundamental facts
7:57:52 about your site's visitors isn't too
7:57:53 difficult just remember having a lot of
7:57:57 data is worth very little if you don't
7:57:59 interpret them correctly and in order to
7:58:02 interpret data you need to start with
7:58:04 specific questions that you'd like to be
7:58:07 answered
7:58:08 what are your site's user needs and
7:58:10 client needs
7:58:12 these are two fundamental questions to
7:58:13 ask and answer in order to base your
7:58:15 design decisions on a solid foundation
7:58:18 so ask yourself
7:58:20 what does the user need from the site
7:58:22 and what are the needs and goals of the
7:58:25 client
7:58:26 if you think about it if we don't know
7:58:28 the answers to these questions we don't
7:58:30 really know what to build in the first
7:58:31 place
7:58:32 all other decisions we make from here on
7:58:35 out will be determined by these
7:58:36 requirements
7:58:38 by the way let me make sure that
7:58:40 everyone understands what i mean when i
7:58:42 say user and client
7:58:45 users are the people that will access
7:58:48 the sites you can also think about them
7:58:50 as visitors or customers or their target
7:58:53 audience
7:58:54 and if it's helpful you can think of
7:58:56 their needs as visitor needs or customer
7:58:59 needs or target audience needs
7:59:02 the client is the business owner or the
7:59:04 director of an organization it's the
7:59:06 person in charge the one who hired you
7:59:08 to design their website
7:59:10 the client might be your customer but
7:59:12 they are not the customer of the website
7:59:14 you will create
7:59:16 you can also think of the client needs
7:59:18 as business needs or more generally
7:59:20 website needs
7:59:22 of course you might not be working with
7:59:24 the boss directly that really depends on
7:59:27 the size of the business or organization
7:59:30 your primary day-to-day contact might be
7:59:32 an assistant or the marketing department
7:59:34 by the way an added difficulty for
7:59:36 students at this point is that student
7:59:39 projects often don't have an actual
7:59:41 clients
7:59:42 it's true that for the project in this
7:59:44 course sequence i gave you a general
7:59:46 project brief but you came up with a
7:59:48 specific idea for your project so you
7:59:51 yourself had to play the part of the
7:59:53 client
7:59:54 that can make certain aspects a little
7:59:56 easier
7:59:57 you don't have to deal with a client who
7:59:59 might disagree with your decisions
8:00:02 but it takes away some of the real world
8:00:04 complications projects inevitably have
8:00:07 but that's okay
8:00:08 if you in fact become a web designer you
8:00:10 will have lots of opportunities to gain
8:00:12 real world experience in working with
8:00:14 actual clients
8:00:16 but now back to our discussion of user
8:00:18 needs and client needs
8:00:21 what's important at this point is that
8:00:23 these needs are stated clearly and in
8:00:25 detail so that there's no room for
8:00:27 confusion or ambiguity for you or the
8:00:29 clients
8:00:30 everyone needs to be on the same page
8:00:33 that's why you will summarize your
8:00:34 findings in a written strategy document
8:00:37 at the end of this project phase
8:00:40 also
8:00:41 stay away from overly broad statements
8:00:44 making money as a client need is nice
8:00:46 but it's way too vague a more specific
8:00:49 rephrasing could be
8:00:50 investigate how to use ads in order to
8:00:53 grow revenue
8:00:54 or
8:00:55 implement search engine optimization so
8:00:57 that more people will find the site and
8:00:59 purchase products
8:01:02 or communicate the advantages of the pro
8:01:04 plan better so that more users will
8:01:07 become paying members
8:01:09 prevent using tired cliches
8:01:12 every user wants a site that is easy to
8:01:14 navigate for example so it's not really
8:01:16 a distinguishing need
8:01:19 but the same token don't identify needs
8:01:21 that are too narrow your client might
8:01:23 love yellow backgrounds but at this
8:01:25 point it's way too early for such
8:01:26 decisions
8:01:27 a need could be for the site to create a
8:01:29 feeling of urgency and energy
8:01:32 later on you might then decide that a
8:01:34 vibrant yellow background fulfills this
8:01:36 need but again that is a decision for a
8:01:39 later phase
8:01:40 in general at this point in the process
8:01:43 you should simply identify all the needs
8:01:45 without having to come up with solutions
8:01:47 keep things open and loose
8:01:49 even though the client might tell you
8:01:51 with utmost urgency we definitely need a
8:01:53 blog
8:01:54 your job is to probe such statements a
8:01:57 little deeper by asking why
8:02:00 maybe the client's goal is to have a
8:02:02 place for news on their side
8:02:04 or they are looking for an outlet to
8:02:06 share their professional writing
8:02:08 or the site's search engine ranking
8:02:10 needs to be improved
8:02:12 all these could be good reasons for a
8:02:14 blog but again you can decide on that
8:02:16 later
8:02:17 plus you might actually discover that
8:02:19 the client simply likes the idea of a
8:02:21 blog everyone else has one right and
8:02:24 that they're not aware of the amount of
8:02:26 work it will take to constantly create
8:02:28 new blog content
8:02:30 now most of the time user needs will
8:02:33 align nicely with client needs
8:02:36 for example take a site that sells books
8:02:39 users of that site obviously want to buy
8:02:41 books and the client who owns the site
8:02:43 wants to increase the amount of orders
8:02:46 same with a website that offers online
8:02:48 classes users come to the site to learn
8:02:51 and the client wants to enroll as many
8:02:53 students as possible
8:02:55 and a news website let's say is visited
8:02:57 by users looking for interesting and
8:02:59 informative writing while the client
8:03:01 wants to have as many page hits as
8:03:03 possible
8:03:05 but there are instances when user and
8:03:07 client needs don't line up so neatly
8:03:10 when they are in fact opposing one
8:03:12 another
8:03:13 here are a few examples
8:03:15 a reader of an online newspaper wants to
8:03:17 read articles in peace but the client
8:03:19 the owner of the newspaper needs to show
8:03:21 ads for their business model to be
8:03:23 sustainable there's a tension between
8:03:25 these needs as ads can get in the way of
8:03:28 a pleasant reading experience
8:03:30 or isn't it nice when websites offer the
8:03:33 option to check out as a guest
8:03:35 it's more simple for the user they don't
8:03:38 need to think of yet another password
8:03:40 but the client might have specific
8:03:42 reasons for user accounts to be
8:03:44 mandatory
8:03:45 here's another big one
8:03:47 users often want the option to talk to a
8:03:49 human being to have their questions
8:03:51 answered and issues resolved
8:03:54 so their need is to find a phone number
8:03:56 on the website
8:03:57 it's in the client's interest however to
8:03:59 limit the amount of time support staff
8:04:01 spends on the phone they will save money
8:04:04 this way so their opposing need is to
8:04:06 design a website that answers customer
8:04:09 questions without them having to pick up
8:04:11 the phone
8:04:13 when you're working on a real world
8:04:14 project with an actual client it's
8:04:17 likely that the client will be very
8:04:19 eager to communicate their needs to you
8:04:22 but it's the actual user of the site
8:04:24 that is sometimes forgotten along the
8:04:26 way
8:04:27 it's your job as a designer to be an
8:04:29 advocate for them and to remind your
8:04:31 client that an effective user experience
8:04:34 must be centered around an understanding
8:04:36 of user needs
8:04:39 so let's finish up the segment with an
8:04:41 example
8:04:42 here are the user and client needs that
8:04:44 we identified while working with the
8:04:46 pasadena conservatory on their site
8:04:49 i invite you to pause the video for a
8:04:50 moment and to read through the lists in
8:04:53 detail
8:05:09 in the last video we established that
8:05:11 you can simply have a discussion with
8:05:12 your client in order to uncover the
8:05:15 client's needs
8:05:16 in order to understand your users needs
8:05:19 however you must determine who they are
8:05:21 who are the people that will eventually
8:05:24 use your website
8:05:26 you have to define your target audience
8:05:30 in ux speak this is also called user
8:05:32 segmentation
8:05:34 and there are different ways to
8:05:35 categorize your users into separate
8:05:37 groups
8:05:38 i will introduce four methods in this
8:05:40 video
8:05:41 we will discuss segmentation by user
8:05:43 roles demographics psychographics and
8:05:47 experience
8:05:48 when you define your target audience by
8:05:50 roles you're isolating specific groups
8:05:53 of people that share similar goals
8:05:57 so on a job side one user role would be
8:05:59 that of the job seeker
8:06:02 and these people who are looking for
8:06:04 jobs share specific user needs that must
8:06:06 be addressed for example they probably
8:06:09 need a search function but an advanced
8:06:11 set of filters to find jobs based on
8:06:14 their skill sets
8:06:16 and there would be a whole other group
8:06:18 that off the job lister with their own
8:06:20 needs
8:06:22 they for example need an interface to be
8:06:24 able to add jobs to the sites
8:06:27 or think about any social media website
8:06:29 like facebook linkedin or twitter
8:06:32 there's a big difference in user needs
8:06:34 between those
8:06:36 who have already signed up and those who
8:06:38 don't have an account yet and for a
8:06:41 school or university website let's say
8:06:43 you will have a natural segmentation of
8:06:45 users into the roles of parents students
8:06:48 and faculty
8:06:49 and users of each of these groups again
8:06:52 will have specific needs that must be
8:06:54 addressed
8:06:55 another way of defining your target
8:06:57 audience is to look at basic statistical
8:07:00 data such as gender age education
8:07:03 marital status income and location
8:07:06 depending on the specific projects you
8:07:08 are working on some of these demographic
8:07:10 properties can be more important than
8:07:13 others gender might often not matter
8:07:15 that much unless you're working on a
8:07:17 dating site let's say or a site for
8:07:19 beauty products or a fashion site
8:07:22 age might be an important distinction if
8:07:25 your site is for a preschool then you'd
8:07:27 probably be targeting parents between
8:07:29 say 25 and 45
8:07:32 and if your site sells hearing aids
8:07:35 chances are that you will have a
8:07:36 sizeable audience of older folks
8:07:40 let's look at what a demographic example
8:07:42 could look like
8:07:43 your site could target middle-aged
8:07:45 single females who are well-educated
8:07:47 with a middle-class income and who live
8:07:49 in an urban metropolis
8:07:51 or a very different target audience for
8:07:54 a different website would be this one
8:07:56 teenage boys who dropped out of high
8:07:58 school have limited financial means and
8:08:01 live in a rural environment in the
8:08:03 american midwest
8:08:05 i'm sure it's not a big stretch to
8:08:06 imagine that the decisions you make
8:08:08 later on in the process
8:08:10 such as what content features to include
8:08:13 what language to use and what visual
8:08:15 identity to design
8:08:17 would be quite different depending on
8:08:19 which of these two example audiences
8:08:21 your site targets
8:08:23 while demographics define basic
8:08:25 objective facts there's another way of
8:08:27 segmenting users which addresses
8:08:29 personal values and interests
8:08:32 these are called psychographics think
8:08:34 about people's personalities and
8:08:36 attitudes and lifestyles and hobbies
8:08:39 let's look at examples of two specific
8:08:42 psychographic profiles
8:08:44 let's say the first is politically
8:08:46 liberal vegetarian environmentally
8:08:48 conscious and politically active they
8:08:50 are likely to have a flock of backyard
8:08:52 chickens
8:08:53 and they are very interested in
8:08:55 do-it-yourself projects
8:08:57 another group seems to be quite the
8:08:59 opposite they are politically
8:09:01 conservative drive gas guzzling cars and
8:09:04 they own guns for hunting but this group
8:09:07 also owns chickens and they're
8:09:09 interested in diy projects
8:09:12 i have actually worked on a website that
8:09:14 counts both of these possible user
8:09:16 groups as different as they might seem
8:09:18 as part of their target audience i'm
8:09:20 talking about the blog root simple
8:09:22 written by kelly coyne and eric knutson
8:09:24 who provide their readers with a lot of
8:09:26 instructions on diy homestead projects
8:09:29 like building chicken coops and baking
8:09:32 bread and growing vegetables one thing
8:09:34 to point out is that while demographics
8:09:36 certainly influence psychographics they
8:09:39 do not predefine them so while it's
8:09:41 pretty likely that you will find more
8:09:43 vegetarian liberals in urban areas on
8:09:45 the west coast of the united states and
8:09:47 more gun-toting conservatives in rural
8:09:50 texas there are definitely exceptions
8:09:54 lastly you can also segment your users
8:09:56 by their level of experience with your
8:09:58 site's subject matter
8:10:00 let's say you're working on a site that
8:10:02 will display financial information about
8:10:04 the stock market
8:10:06 now is your target audience seasoned
8:10:08 stock market pros or people that are
8:10:10 just starting out to invest
8:10:13 in other words is your site for experts
8:10:15 or newbies
8:10:17 or do you intend to target both groups
8:10:20 or you might design a site that
8:10:22 advocates for a political cause
8:10:25 some of your users might be die-hard
8:10:26 supporters already
8:10:28 others might be persuadable to become
8:10:30 supporters
8:10:31 these two different audiences will have
8:10:33 different needs
8:10:35 let's finish again with an example here
8:10:38 are the target audiences we defined for
8:10:40 the pasadena conservatory separated into
8:10:43 user roles demographics and their
8:10:45 experience
8:10:47 under user roles we identified students
8:10:50 parents concertgoers funders and faculty
8:10:53 the demographic segmentation was mostly
8:10:55 concentrated on age and location so we
8:10:58 have children teenagers and adults and
8:11:00 since the school is located in pasadena
8:11:03 the target audience lives there or in
8:11:05 the greater los angeles area
8:11:07 and for experience there is a difference
8:11:09 between prospective students and those
8:11:12 students that are already enrolled
8:11:14 note that we didn't create a separate
8:11:16 psychographic profile as we didn't see
8:11:18 that as a necessity for this project
8:11:21 remember there's no reason to include
8:11:23 things that don't provide you with
8:11:24 meaningful information
8:11:27 before we wrap up this video i'll
8:11:29 briefly mention another tool that can
8:11:31 assist you with keeping your focus on
8:11:32 the target audience throughout the
8:11:34 design process
8:11:35 user personas are short imaginary
8:11:38 biographies of people who embody
8:11:40 representative members of your target
8:11:42 audience
8:11:43 you basically make up fictional
8:11:44 characters who will be used as stand-ins
8:11:47 for a larger user group you give them a
8:11:50 name and even a portrait photo
8:11:52 this allows you to breathe life into
8:11:54 dull statistics
8:11:56 and you are able to refer to your target
8:11:58 audience by name when you are trying to
8:12:00 decide something you can ask i wonder
8:12:03 what mike would think of this or
8:12:05 would jen do this or something else
8:12:08 there's a reading included in this
8:12:10 lesson that will explain user personas
8:12:12 in greater detail
8:12:14 in the end of the strategy phase you
8:12:16 should summarize all your findings in a
8:12:19 strategy document and share it with your
8:12:21 client and your team
8:12:23 you will have figured out the needs of
8:12:25 your users and your clients
8:12:27 and you will have a deeper understanding
8:12:29 about your target audience
8:12:31 the strategy document is also a good
8:12:33 place to note other research that you
8:12:35 have conducted such as a roundup of
8:12:37 similar and inspirational websites and
8:12:40 any insight from analytics data
8:12:42 with all of this in place you'll be in a
8:12:44 good position to move on to the next
8:12:46 phase
8:12:47 when this strategic information is
8:12:49 translated into requirements for content
8:12:53 and functionality
8:12:55 all right we're back talking about
8:12:57 strategy this week so we'll talk about
8:13:00 target audience
8:13:01 personas client needs and user needs

8:29:36 [Music]
8:29:42 hey everyone i hope all this well and
8:29:44 that your strategy explorations for your
8:29:46 projects proved to be fruitful
8:29:49 by now you should have a pretty clear
8:29:50 idea what your project will be about
8:29:53 and who your target audience is
8:29:55 this week we will talk about how to take
8:29:57 your user and client needs that you
8:29:59 established and create a set of content
8:30:01 and functionality requirements from them
8:30:04 in other words you're transforming your
8:30:06 overarching goals from last week into
8:30:09 specific requirements for your site
8:30:12 as a bonus you'll see my interview with
8:30:13 melissa quo who is an interaction
8:30:16 designer at google melissa was my
8:30:18 student at calarts a few years back and
8:30:21 we'll talk about how she ended up
8:30:22 working at google the way google
8:30:24 approaches ux challenges and about new
8:30:27 trends and ux software tools
8:30:32 after learning how to define strategy in
8:30:34 the lessons last week our focus now
8:30:36 shifts to determining the scope of our
8:30:38 sites
8:30:39 so that's the second step of the ux
8:30:42 process
8:30:43 and remember the distinction of moving
8:30:45 from very abstract concerns to more and
8:30:47 more concrete concerns i mentioned
8:30:50 before that in the beginning of the
8:30:51 process we're not at all concerned with
8:30:53 the final shape of the site
8:30:55 we're dealing with much more higher
8:30:57 level questions instead
8:30:59 you could even argue that at this point
8:31:01 in our process
8:31:02 at the beginning of the scope phase
8:31:04 we could be working on a brochure or a
8:31:06 book or a video as our final deliverable
8:31:09 instead of a website
8:31:11 in fact one result of the previous
8:31:13 strategy phase might be the realization
8:31:15 that a website is not the right answer
8:31:17 for the problems we're trying to solve
8:31:20 be that as it may this course is about
8:31:22 website design and the further we get
8:31:25 along the ux process the more we will be
8:31:28 concerned with the final shape of our
8:31:30 website
8:31:31 so
8:31:32 let's jump into talking about scope
8:31:36 here's a fancy way of expressing what
8:31:38 this step is all about
8:31:40 strategy becomes scope when user needs
8:31:43 and client needs are translated into
8:31:45 specific requirements for content and
8:31:48 functionality
8:31:50 here's a more diagrammatic view of
8:31:53 what's happening
8:31:54 you see that the idea here is that the
8:31:56 user needs and the client needs that we
8:31:58 established earlier directly inform the
8:32:02 content and functionality requirements
8:32:04 of the site
8:32:05 in the strategy phase we mostly answered
8:32:08 the question of why are we creating
8:32:10 something now the outline of scope phase
8:32:14 is using the insights gained to answer
8:32:17 the question what are we building
8:32:20 if you're let's say working with
8:32:22 filmmakers who just finished a movie the
8:32:24 why of the strategy phase could be
8:32:26 summarized in a very simplified way as
8:32:29 promoting the new movie
8:32:32 now the what a rebuilding question would
8:32:34 be answered with specific requirements
8:32:36 for content and functionality something
8:32:39 like a website that includes information
8:32:41 about the movie the cast screenings
8:32:43 press articles the trailer and lets
8:32:46 people sign up for a mailing list
8:32:49 at the end of the outline of scope phase
8:32:51 the specific requirements will be simply
8:32:54 listed in two columns one list for
8:32:56 content and one list for functionality
8:32:58 we'll talk more about the difference of
8:33:00 these two categories in a second
8:33:02 but let me reiterate why it's important
8:33:05 to define requirements in the first
8:33:07 place sure it's good to know what you
8:33:09 are building
8:33:10 but equally important is the question of
8:33:12 what you are not building there are many
8:33:14 constraints on a project like the
8:33:16 timeline and the budget and technology
8:33:19 constraints knowing what the site will
8:33:21 not be will help you focus your energy
8:33:23 on what you are actually building and
8:33:26 there shouldn't be any unpleasant
8:33:27 surprises in the ends when your client
8:33:29 might realize that a complex component
8:33:31 they were hoping for is not part of the
8:33:33 site
8:33:34 defining your specific requirements for
8:33:37 content and functionality in detail
8:33:39 early on will ensure that everyone's on
8:33:41 the same page about what to expect
8:33:45 so what are the differences between
8:33:48 content and functionality why do we need
8:33:50 two separate lists
8:33:52 well websites as a medium work in two
8:33:54 different ways
8:33:56 on the one hand a website is an
8:33:57 information delivery platform it lets
8:33:59 you access written text images
8:34:01 recordings movies and files
8:34:04 those are fairly straightforward and
8:34:06 passive ways of interacting with the
8:34:08 side you're mainly consuming information
8:34:12 but that's not all websites can do users
8:34:15 can also interact with them to perform
8:34:17 complex tasks like doing their taxes or
8:34:20 managing their to-do lists or buying
8:34:23 cars
8:34:24 you even have websites that let you
8:34:26 create other websites
8:34:28 this is what i'm talking about when i
8:34:30 say functionality i mean task-based
8:34:33 systems that let the user get things
8:34:36 done not just access information like a
8:34:38 description or image of a product
8:34:41 but completing tasks like customizing
8:34:44 the product and purchasing it
8:34:46 so the reason why we are making two
8:34:48 separate lists for our requirements are
8:34:50 that content and functionality
8:34:52 requirements are fundamentally different
8:34:56 content is less complex
8:34:57 sure text will need to be written and
8:35:00 photos will need to be taken and videos
8:35:02 will need to be edited but for the web
8:35:04 designer these pieces of content are
8:35:06 merely assets to be incorporated into
8:35:09 the sites
8:35:10 functionality is more complex it needs
8:35:12 to be planned and it needs to be built
8:35:15 it's a lot easier to make an about us
8:35:16 page with a few images and some text
8:35:19 than to create a shopping cart or a
8:35:21 checkout system
8:35:23 okay
8:35:24 now that we talked about the differences
8:35:26 between content and functionality let's
8:35:28 discuss how the needs we discovered in
8:35:30 the previous strategy phase help us
8:35:33 determine what to include in our website
8:35:36 let's assume that one of the user needs
8:35:38 was to get questions answered
8:35:41 what content requirements could fulfill
8:35:43 this particular user needs
8:35:46 well if you want to have your users
8:35:47 speak to a real-life person you could
8:35:50 list a phone number so that becomes a
8:35:53 content requirement it's a simple one
8:35:55 for sure but the decision whether to
8:35:57 list a phone number or not in the first
8:35:59 place might not be so simple does your
8:36:02 client have the capacity to answer phone
8:36:04 calls all day long do they have an
8:36:06 automated phone system there are
8:36:09 companies and i'm looking at you
8:36:11 facebook that purposely do not list any
8:36:14 phone numbers on their sides
8:36:16 anyway you might also give your users
8:36:19 the ability to send an email if you want
8:36:21 to give out your email address that is
8:36:24 or they could contact you through social
8:36:26 media
8:36:27 if you are monitoring these channels
8:36:29 closely if your website is for a large
8:36:31 institution it might be helpful to list
8:36:33 a directory of all employees so that
8:36:36 your users can contact the most
8:36:38 appropriate person with their questions
8:36:40 and finally you might add a frequently
8:36:42 asked questions section to the site
8:36:44 which has answers to the most commonly
8:36:47 asked questions
8:36:49 okay all of these are content
8:36:50 requirements that fulfill this
8:36:52 particular user needs again if you were
8:36:55 working on a static medium like a
8:36:56 brochure your requirements wouldn't be
8:36:59 all that different up until now
8:37:01 but we are working on a website so what
8:37:03 functionality can be built to allow
8:37:06 people to have questions answered
8:37:09 we could provide a contact form that's
8:37:11 similar to listing an email address but
8:37:14 it allows users to ask questions without
8:37:16 leaving your site
8:37:18 it also allows you to ask your users a
8:37:20 few additional specific questions that
8:37:23 they might not address when writing a
8:37:25 free form email
8:37:27 another functionality could be a live
8:37:29 chat feature
8:37:31 it obviously requires the client to have
8:37:33 people answering the chats but it's a
8:37:35 great way for customers to get answers
8:37:37 quickly
8:37:38 or how about a community forum where
8:37:40 questions are answered by other users
8:37:43 or keeping with the idea of crowdsourced
8:37:46 content on a shopping site you could add
8:37:48 the functionality for users to leave
8:37:50 product reviews
8:37:51 all of these solutions would require you
8:37:53 to plan and build systems that enable
8:37:56 the specific functionality
8:37:58 let's look at one more example quickly
8:38:00 let's say we determine that a user will
8:38:02 want to know how much shipping will cost
8:38:05 that's a reasonable user needs
8:38:07 a way to answer this need with content
8:38:09 is to simply provide a description of
8:38:12 your shipping policies
8:38:14 if you have a very simple site maybe you
8:38:16 only sell one product or if you have a
8:38:19 very simple shipping policy maybe
8:38:21 shipping is always free then this is all
8:38:24 you might need just some text explaining
8:38:26 how shipping charges are calculated
8:38:28 but on bigger ecommerce sites shipping
8:38:31 charges are often quite complex
8:38:33 so you could build a shipping calculator
8:38:35 that abstracts your complex shipping
8:38:37 formulas into something intuitive
8:38:40 maybe a form with a few questions for
8:38:42 the user to fill out
8:38:44 the hard work of calculating shipping
8:38:46 rates is then done by the site and your
8:38:49 users will be much happier
8:38:51 so to reiterate for each piece of
8:38:54 content and functionality that you add
8:38:56 to your requirements you should ask does
8:38:58 this fulfill a client needs
8:39:01 and does this fulfill a user needs
8:39:04 most importantly you must constantly
8:39:07 refer back to your strategy documents
8:39:10 that's where after all you establish the
8:39:12 client needs and user needs for the
8:39:15 sites
8:39:17 other questions that will influence your
8:39:18 decisions are about the feasibility of a
8:39:21 specific requirement
8:39:23 is it feasible technologically time-wise
8:39:26 and budget-wise
8:39:28 in our previous example we talked about
8:39:30 the shipping calculator
8:39:32 that sounds like a great idea but only
8:39:34 if the client has a budget and can
8:39:35 afford this feature
8:39:38 and only if there's enough time to build
8:39:40 it before the site's launch
8:39:43 sometimes you might come up with the
8:39:44 requirements that are just not feasible
8:39:46 due to technical constraints
8:39:48 you might want to add a full page
8:39:50 background video to your homepage to
8:39:52 fulfill the client goal of creating a
8:39:54 surprising and immersive user experience
8:39:57 but if you determine that a lot of your
8:39:58 users don't have access to high-speed
8:40:00 internet then this technological
8:40:02 constraints a slow internet connection
8:40:05 makes the requirement of a large
8:40:07 background video unfeasible before i
8:40:10 wrap up this video here's a free-flowing
8:40:12 list of all kinds of example content
8:40:14 requirements that give you an idea of
8:40:17 the kinds of things that could belong in
8:40:19 the content category
8:40:21 most websites will probably need a logo
8:40:23 and contact information and some
8:40:25 information about the company
8:40:27 an e-commerce site could also use
8:40:29 product images and product descriptions
8:40:31 featured products pricing info shipping
8:40:34 info customer reviews and frequently
8:40:36 asked questions a movie site would
8:40:38 probably include the movie trailer a
8:40:40 press kits headshots and bios a
8:40:43 restaurant would list the food menu
8:40:45 ingredients opening times directions
8:40:48 maybe the company history a school needs
8:40:50 class descriptions the academic calendar
8:40:53 event info employee directory annual
8:40:56 report pdfs and maybe blog posts
8:41:00 a podcast website could post their
8:41:02 podcast audio files and show notes and a
8:41:05 portfolio website would include an image
8:41:08 gallery and case studies and our clients
8:41:11 section
8:41:12 and some sites are required to disclose
8:41:14 their privacy policy and list site
8:41:16 credits note that all of these examples
8:41:19 have to do with text images audio video
8:41:21 and files
8:41:23 and here's a list of example
8:41:25 functionality requirements
8:41:27 some of these are pretty common like
8:41:29 sorting and filtering and searching
8:41:32 others you will find on many e-commerce
8:41:34 sites like a shopping cart and a
8:41:36 checkout system and an order history
8:41:39 there are some functionalities that
8:41:41 require others to be implemented as well
8:41:44 once you have user authorization you
8:41:46 need to think about how people will
8:41:48 create an account and recover a lost
8:41:50 password
8:41:52 once you have user comments you need to
8:41:54 think about the need to police these
8:41:56 comments
8:41:57 other items in this list are less common
8:41:59 like color correcting images pdf
8:42:02 creation and location-based content
8:42:05 but what all of these items have in
8:42:07 common is that they don't simply
8:42:08 describe content but complex systems
8:42:12 that allow users to complete certain
8:42:15 tasks
8:42:16 let's take another look at the pasadena
8:42:18 conservatory project for a real world
8:42:20 example
8:42:22 here again is the list of user and
8:42:24 client needs that came out of the
8:42:26 strategy phase let me highlight a few of
8:42:29 them to explain how these needs were
8:42:31 translated into requirements for content
8:42:33 and functionality the user need to know
8:42:36 who's on the faculty can be simply
8:42:38 answered with the list of all faculty
8:42:40 members and having bios for each teacher
8:42:42 will be helpful too
8:42:44 but since the list is rather long we
8:42:46 decided to also implement filtering
8:42:48 functionality the user can filter all
8:42:51 faculty members by the instrument they
8:42:53 teach
8:42:54 we also made the list searchable via a
8:42:56 search function
8:42:58 another user need was the question what
8:43:01 instruments are available
8:43:03 content that addresses this would
8:43:04 include a list of all instruments that
8:43:06 are being taught and information for
8:43:08 each we decided that we would also
8:43:10 design a set of icons for each
8:43:12 instrument to make the site more unique
8:43:15 on the functionality side one could
8:43:17 imagine a fun quiz that asks the user a
8:43:19 series of questions and in the end
8:43:21 suggests what instrument they should
8:43:23 learn this would be especially
8:43:25 appropriate if our strategy also
8:43:27 determined that many parents are unsure
8:43:29 what instruments their children should
8:43:31 study
8:43:32 at last this is not something we
8:43:34 actually built remember the feasibility
8:43:36 constraints one also needs to consider
8:43:38 building such a tool would be quite
8:43:40 involved and would take additional time
8:43:42 and resources one of the client needs
8:43:45 was to promote events
8:43:47 so for content we'll obviously need
8:43:50 information about the events
8:43:52 but we also need a way for people to
8:43:54 order tickets that goes into the
8:43:56 functionality column but a ticket
8:43:59 ordering system represents quite some
8:44:01 complexity you'll need to build a
8:44:03 shopping cart process payments
8:44:05 facilitate account creation login
8:44:07 functionality and password recovery so
8:44:09 we decided we would use an external
8:44:11 service for ticket purchases a service
8:44:14 like eventbrite or brown paper tickets
8:44:17 the downside of using an external
8:44:19 service is of course that you are taking
8:44:21 the user away from your site and you're
8:44:24 interrupting the seamless flow of their
8:44:26 experience
8:44:27 but the upside is that you will rely on
8:44:29 someone having figured out all the
8:44:31 complexities of selling tickets it's a
8:44:34 compromise for sure and in this case we
8:44:36 determined that it made sense to use an
8:44:39 external service
8:44:41 the client also wanted to employ
8:44:42 photography prominently throughout the
8:44:44 site well obviously then we'll need
8:44:47 great photography
8:44:48 at this point it's important to remind
8:44:50 the client that if such photography
8:44:52 doesn't already exist it's high time to
8:44:54 hire a photographer
8:44:56 later on when we're designing the visual
8:44:58 appearance of the side we'll need these
8:45:00 images another need of the client was to
8:45:04 demonstrate community
8:45:06 well as content we'll need images and
8:45:08 stories that talk about how the
8:45:10 conservatory fosters community and as
8:45:13 functionality we determined that a blog
8:45:15 would be a good way to provide users
8:45:17 access to these stories a blog requires
8:45:20 many additional functionality
8:45:22 considerations
8:45:24 we decided that we would have the
8:45:25 ability to filter the blog posts by
8:45:28 categories
8:45:29 as a side note that meant that we would
8:45:31 have to eventually choose a list of
8:45:33 appropriate categories but this is best
8:45:36 addressed in the next phase the sitemap
8:45:38 phase
8:45:39 we also decided that the posts would
8:45:41 always be sorted by date in descending
8:45:44 order so that the latest blog posts
8:45:46 would show up first and we discussed the
8:45:48 possibility of giving users the ability
8:45:50 to leave comments but comments bring up
8:45:53 another host of questions to resolve
8:45:56 would users be able to leave comments
8:45:58 anonymously or would they have to be
8:46:00 logged in
8:46:01 the latter would require us to build a
8:46:03 way for users to create accounts
8:46:06 also who will check the comments for
8:46:08 their appropriateness in terms of
8:46:10 language and content how do we prevent
8:46:12 trolls or advertising spam
8:46:14 on the opposite end of the problem
8:46:16 spectrum was this question would people
8:46:19 even want to leave comments in the first
8:46:20 place
8:46:21 a blog with no comments at all is a very
8:46:24 sad place
8:46:25 so ultimately we decided against
8:46:28 implementing comments
8:46:30 lastly i want to point out that you'll
8:46:31 encounter needs that cannot be easily
8:46:34 fulfilled by content or functionality
8:46:36 take for example the client need of
8:46:38 creating an attractive and memorable
8:46:40 website this need will be most fully
8:46:43 answered in the last phase the visual
8:46:45 mock-up phase
8:46:47 so in the end you will list all content
8:46:50 and functionality requirements in two
8:46:52 lists this represents your outline of
8:46:55 scope
8:46:56 all right it's time to talk about
8:46:58 outline
8:46:59 of scope
8:47:02 we'll go
8:47:03 through each of your projects again as a
8:47:05 reminder remind us what you're doing and
8:47:08 then let's talk about the requirements
8:47:10 for content and functionality that you
8:47:12 will
8:47:13 have your
8:47:14 that are necessary for your website
8:47:16 yeah so my project is an upscale italian

8:47:26 and your content requirements that you
8:47:28 came up with
8:47:29 yeah so for my content requirements um
8:47:33 which would be like text images and
8:47:35 videos that the user will need or will
8:47:37 be looking for are a
8:47:39 food menu that ha that showcases the
8:47:42 pasta appetizers
8:47:44 and drinks
8:47:46 um
8:47:47 images of past catered events a
8:47:50 copy or text that stresses
8:47:53 that the delivery service is only for
8:47:55 the san clear to area
8:47:57 an about us page
8:48:00 a section about testimonials and reviews
8:48:03 of the service
8:48:05 a contact and information
8:48:08 page and also frequently asked questions
8:48:11 yeah and for your functionality
8:48:13 requirements what are those to begin
8:48:15 with
8:48:16 so functionality requirements are
8:48:19 parts of the system of the website that
8:48:21 will help the user accomplish tasks
8:48:24 whereas the content requirement is more
8:48:27 of uh communicating like what it says
8:48:29 static information yeah static
8:48:31 information
8:48:32 um
8:48:33 so what do you got for functionality so
8:48:35 for functionality
8:48:37 i decided to
8:48:38 include a live chat feature for
8:48:41 customers to get help right away
8:48:44 and to be able to customize the catering
8:48:47 order
8:48:48 um
