9:03:31 hello web designers you've almost made
9:03:33 it through the first course in this
9:03:34 course sequence welcome to week four
9:03:38 with the outline of scope enhanced you
9:03:40 will now learn how to transform the
9:03:41 content and functionality requirement
9:03:43 determined last week into a navigable
9:03:45 structure
9:03:47 this structure will be visualized by
9:03:48 something called a site map
9:03:50 i'll tell you all about sitemaps and how
9:03:52 to create them
9:03:54 along the way we will also define the
9:03:56 term information architecture and i will
9:03:58 introduce you to a tool called tree jack
9:04:01 which will enable you to test your site
9:04:03 map on actual users
9:04:09 here again is our road map we've arrived
9:04:11 right in the middle of the ux process
9:04:14 ready to tackle the site map
9:04:16 this of course means that we are getting
9:04:18 more and more concrete with the shaping
9:04:20 of our final sites
9:04:21 in the site map phase we are very much
9:04:24 concerned with the structure of
9:04:25 information
9:04:27 if the last phase the outline of scope
9:04:29 phase was all about defining our
9:04:32 requirements now we are figuring out how
9:04:34 the pieces of content and functionality
9:04:37 fit together as a logical whole that the
9:04:39 user can navigate
9:04:41 at its heart it's really about defining
9:04:43 hierarchy and sequences
9:04:46 we're taking all this stuff and
9:04:48 structuring it so it can be accessed in
9:04:50 an optimal way
9:04:52 and this step isn't even all that unique
9:04:54 to websites if you think about it
9:04:57 graphic designers deal with structure
9:04:58 and hierarchy of information all the
9:05:00 time
9:05:01 i mean if you're designing a poster you
9:05:03 have to create hierarchy for the
9:05:05 elements that are on the poster and if
9:05:07 you're designing a book you'll create a
9:05:09 table of contents that guides the
9:05:11 structure of the entire book
9:05:13 the sitemap in a way represents the
9:05:15 table of contents for our website
9:05:18 it's an information flow diagram that
9:05:20 includes a bunch of boxes that are
9:05:22 connected with lines and arrows
9:05:24 we'll cover the different components of
9:05:26 a site map in detail later in this
9:05:28 lesson
9:05:29 and we'll also look at specific examples
9:05:31 of sitemaps
9:05:33 but let me stress one thing right away
9:05:36 as the table of contents for our site
9:05:38 the sitemap will show us what to include
9:05:40 for the main navigation to illustrate
9:05:43 this let's quickly add some example
9:05:45 content into this sitemap sketch here
9:05:48 you see that everything starts with the
9:05:50 home page and there are four first level
9:05:52 pages products about cart contact
9:05:56 well if this is our site map then we
9:05:59 will want to use these four links for
9:06:01 our main navigation bar
9:06:04 we'll deal with this much more in our
9:06:05 next phase during wireframing but i
9:06:08 wanted to mention that when we're
9:06:10 thinking about the site map we're
9:06:12 already laying a lot of groundwork for
9:06:14 the navigation menu of the website
9:06:17 let me introduce one more term before we
9:06:19 really get into the sitemap business
9:06:22 a lot of what we're doing during the
9:06:23 sitemap phase is often described as
9:06:26 information architecture
9:06:28 you might have heard about this term
9:06:29 before
9:06:30 there's even a job description called
9:06:32 information architect
9:06:34 and although it's been formalized as a
9:06:36 term more recently the field of
9:06:39 information architecture is quite old
9:06:41 as long as people had to communicate
9:06:43 information
9:06:44 they had to make certain choices on how
9:06:46 to structure that information i was just
9:06:49 talking about that even the medieval
9:06:51 scribes working on their illuminated
9:06:53 manuscripts had to think about how to
9:06:55 structure the writing in the context of
9:06:58 a website you can define information
9:07:01 architecture as categorizing organizing
9:07:03 and labeling information to allow users
9:07:06 to move through a website effectively
9:07:09 the writer christina wattke has another
9:07:11 way of putting it that i quite like she
9:07:14 says that information architecture is
9:07:16 deciding what the choices are what they
9:07:18 are called and where they take you when
9:07:21 you click
9:07:22 so let's discuss a few specific concerns
9:07:25 that you'll have to deal with when you
9:07:27 the information architect creates the
9:07:29 site map for your site
9:07:31 let's talk about the number of clicks a
9:07:33 user must perform before they get to
9:07:35 contents and how clicking compares to
9:07:37 scrolling and we'll also talk about how
9:07:39 many choices to offer and what to call
9:07:42 them
9:07:43 when i started out as a web designer in
9:07:45 the early 2000s there was this rule i
9:07:47 heard a lot about
9:07:49 and that was that a user should be able
9:07:51 to access every piece of content on a
9:07:53 site with just two clicks
9:07:55 less clicking can obviously benefit the
9:07:57 user experience they get things done
9:08:00 much more quickly and remember in those
9:08:02 days websites tended to load quite
9:08:04 slowly so every extra page that had to
9:08:07 be loaded slowed the user down
9:08:10 but we shouldn't conclude that a lot of
9:08:12 clicking is always a bad user experience
9:08:15 sometimes it might be wise to present a
9:08:17 series of well-structured pages instead
9:08:19 of one very complex page
9:08:22 to illustrate this think about a site
9:08:24 with many levels of information
9:08:26 a navigation with drop down menus that
9:08:28 go several layers deep allows your users
9:08:31 to get around your site with very few
9:08:33 clicks
9:08:34 and for some sites that might be the
9:08:36 most appropriate
9:08:37 but sometimes it might be better to
9:08:39 simply provide a main level navigation
9:08:42 which links to individual landing pages
9:08:44 for each primary section
9:08:47 there you can present the subsections
9:08:49 with text describing what the user can
9:08:51 expect
9:08:53 which of these options to choose could
9:08:55 depend on your user's level of
9:08:57 familiarity with the content
9:08:59 or how often they need to access
9:09:02 specific piece of information that's
9:09:04 located deep in your site's architecture
9:09:07 if they have to click five times every
9:09:09 time they access an important page they
9:09:11 will hope that they had some shortcut to
9:09:13 get there
9:09:14 but a giant drop down menu as indicated
9:09:17 on the left can quickly overwhelm users
9:09:19 that are less familiar with your site's
9:09:21 content
9:09:22 another example is designing a complex
9:09:25 form
9:09:26 this could be presented all on one page
9:09:28 no clicking required you just fill out
9:09:31 the form and send it off
9:09:32 another way to structure forms is to ask
9:09:35 a few questions at a time
9:09:37 look at the sketch on the right doesn't
9:09:39 this seem a lot more inviting than the
9:09:41 onslaught of form fields on the left
9:09:44 let's say we're developing a website to
9:09:46 help people with their taxes we could
9:09:49 ask our user all questions on one page
9:09:52 everything from their name and their
9:09:54 occupation and how much money they made
9:09:56 and how many kids they have on and on
9:09:58 all on a single page that would be
9:10:01 similar to how such forms work on paper
9:10:04 but a form like this could be quite
9:10:06 overwhelming for most users and since
9:10:08 we're working on a website we could
9:10:10 easily split up the form into many
9:10:12 different screens and start simply by
9:10:14 asking what's your name
9:10:16 it might take the user 20 or 30 screens
9:10:18 to complete the form but each screen is
9:10:21 now quite manageable
9:10:23 but let's say you are a tax expert and
9:10:25 you know exactly which form field you
9:10:27 need to change then it could get quite
9:10:30 cumbersome to click through dozens of
9:10:32 pages to find a specific field
9:10:35 so to reiterate having the user do a
9:10:37 bunch of clicking isn't necessarily bad
9:10:40 if you allow them to access information
9:10:42 in a very structured way it's only bad
9:10:44 if it's cumbersome and repetitive
9:10:47 clicking versus scrolling is a similar
9:10:49 concern
9:10:50 there are some simple sites where
9:10:52 everything can just be on one page
9:10:55 there's actually a whole category of
9:10:56 websites called one page websites on
9:10:59 those sites you don't click much at all
9:11:02 they often work more like a narrative
9:11:04 scrolling experience
9:11:07 and let me stress at this point that
9:11:09 there's absolutely nothing wrong with
9:11:11 scrolling
9:11:12 the web's foundation is based on the
9:11:14 idea that pages scroll so you don't have
9:11:17 to be afraid of scrolling you might
9:11:19 still encounter clients who are
9:11:20 concerned about this they might tell you
9:11:22 that they know for a fact that users
9:11:24 hate scrolling
9:11:26 it's up to you to educate your clients
9:11:28 think about the most popular websites
9:11:30 facebook amazon google search there is a
9:11:33 lot of scrolling involved in navigating
9:11:36 those sites
9:11:37 in fact often it's easier to scroll than
9:11:39 to click
9:11:40 especially on a laptop with a trackpad
9:11:43 or on touchscreen devices
9:11:46 think about a slideshow of images
9:11:48 if you build the interface around having
9:11:50 to click little arrows to advance the
9:11:52 slides that's actually more difficult
9:11:54 for the user than just presenting all
9:11:56 images on one page and let the user
9:11:58 scroll that's probably the reason that
9:12:00 facebook and instagram use the latter
9:12:02 option to let users navigate through
9:12:04 their friends posts
9:12:06 let's continue with the question of how
9:12:08 many choices to present to the user
9:12:10 here's something that i have mentioned
9:12:12 before this idea that the human brain
9:12:14 can hold about seven plus minus two
9:12:17 items in short term memory once you add
9:12:19 more items to a list 10 11 12 we'll have
9:12:23 forgotten what the first items were and
9:12:25 we can't make an easy decision
9:12:27 so what does that mean for our
9:12:29 information architecture we should limit
9:12:31 the amount of choices we present at one
9:12:33 time to five to nine items this is a
9:12:36 nice rule for designing a main
9:12:38 navigation don't include 15 items five
9:12:41 to nine items is a good limit
9:12:44 if you have more content your challenge
9:12:46 will be how to categorize these items
9:12:49 into subcategories that fit into a
9:12:51 smaller amount of main categories
9:12:55 here's a nice example of information
9:12:57 overloads look at the website of chaffey
9:13:00 college it's completely overwhelming to
9:13:02 make a decision here there's just so
9:13:04 much going on
9:13:06 on the right in contrast is the website
9:13:08 for columbia college there's a much more
9:13:10 limited set of options to choose from
9:13:12 here
9:13:13 and there is a sense of clarity and
9:13:15 sanity
9:13:17 lastly we have the question about what
9:13:19 to call the choices
9:13:21 it's important to create clear and
9:13:23 unambiguous labels that your target
9:13:25 audience understands
9:13:27 a user doesn't want to guess what might
9:13:29 happen when they click on a navigation
9:13:31 item
9:13:32 you want them to click with confidence
9:13:34 that they will find what they are
9:13:35 looking for in this particular section
9:13:37 of the site
9:13:39 however a competing goal is often that
9:13:42 you want to choose labels that are
9:13:43 unique and not that generic
9:13:46 that can create friction if clarity
9:13:48 suffers
9:13:49 let's look at a few examples let's say
9:13:52 we're including a blog on our site
9:13:54 we could just call it blog and be done
9:13:56 with it
9:13:57 it's a pretty well known term and users
9:13:59 will expect it to be that part of the
9:14:01 site where regular updates writing and
9:14:04 images are posted
9:14:06 but it's also a pretty broad label
9:14:08 maybe you want to use something more
9:14:10 unique and specific
9:14:12 here are some other options news stories
9:14:15 what's happening the latest there's a
9:14:18 website called slate.com and their blog
9:14:21 is called the slatest pretty clever
9:14:23 right
9:14:24 the blog for calarts the college i'm
9:14:26 teaching at is called 24 700 which is
9:14:29 part of the address of the school
9:14:32 and if you're working on a snotty punk
9:14:34 rock website maybe their blog is called
9:14:36 wtf that's not appropriate for most
9:14:39 websites but it could be quite
9:14:41 appropriate for others
9:14:43 again much depends on your target
9:14:45 audience so we are lucky that we already
9:14:47 figured out who our users are at this
9:14:49 point
9:14:51 here's another one shopping carts
9:14:53 that's a pretty standard term but it's
9:14:55 not the only one what about simply carts
9:14:58 or bag or bin or basket bucket box or
9:15:03 wallets
9:15:04 again it depends on the context of the
9:15:06 project you're working on
9:15:08 how about the about us page
9:15:11 here are some other options it could be
9:15:13 shortened to just about or be more
9:15:16 precise as in about our company
9:15:19 long labels though will be more
9:15:20 difficult to deal with when you're
9:15:22 designing the navigation bar
9:15:24 you could be super brief and just call
9:15:26 the section us
9:15:28 or depending on the type of site it
9:15:30 could be team or behind the scenes our
9:15:33 crew
9:15:34 homies
9:15:35 you get the idea
9:15:37 lastly here's a question about which
9:15:39 pronoun to use on your labels one option
9:15:42 is not to use a pronoun at all as in
9:15:45 simply account
9:15:47 but that's not all that friendly or
9:15:48 specific is it
9:15:50 so do we call the section your account
9:15:53 or is it my accounts i'll let you spend
9:15:56 a little bit more time with this
9:15:58 interesting conundrum
9:15:59 i found a great post about just this
9:16:01 question that i'd like you to read next
9:16:04 let me walk through an example sitemap
9:16:07 process so i can explain how to get from
9:16:09 a list of content and functionality
9:16:12 remember the requirements we determined
9:16:14 in the outline of scope phase to a site
9:16:16 map
9:16:18 so here we have a bunch of content items
9:16:21 let's say this is for an e-commerce site
9:16:24 the first step would be to sort these
9:16:26 items into sections of related content
9:16:30 now we have things that belong to
9:16:32 products and the shopping experience on
9:16:34 the left
9:16:36 items that belong to information about
9:16:38 the company is collected in the middle
9:16:41 and information about contacting and
9:16:43 visiting the company is on the right and
9:16:46 we have a few other things like faqs and
9:16:48 the blog as stand-alone sections
9:16:52 next let's find some umbrella terms that
9:16:54 can describe these content buckets that
9:16:57 we created
9:16:58 here we have shop faq
9:17:01 shopping cart
9:17:03 about blog and contacts
9:17:05 these labels will form the main
9:17:07 navigation of the site you know the
9:17:10 links that you'll find on top of every
9:17:12 page
9:17:13 note that we don't have a section that
9:17:15 the credits and the privacy policy fit
9:17:17 into
9:17:18 well these are pages that while they're
9:17:20 important to be included don't need to
9:17:22 be accessed from the main navigation
9:17:25 so let's decide to link to them in the
9:17:27 footer of every page
9:17:29 let's take another look at the contact
9:17:32 section
9:17:33 the last two items directions and
9:17:35 opening times could fit here
9:17:38 visiting a store can be somewhat
9:17:40 described as a form of contact
9:17:42 but it's not very precise is it
9:17:45 let's be more explicit and create
9:17:47 another category
9:17:49 now we added a section called visits
9:17:51 which will contain information for
9:17:53 directions and opening times this new
9:17:55 section will also stress that this
9:17:57 online store also has a physical
9:17:59 location users will see the word visits
9:18:02 right in the primary navigation
9:18:05 okay now let's look a little closer at
9:18:07 all the content items we have and decide
9:18:09 if we need actual pages for each
9:18:12 here i isolated all of the content notes
9:18:15 that do not need their own page
9:18:20 the product description and images and
9:18:22 customer reviews will live on the
9:18:24 product pages
9:18:26 the about text will go on the about
9:18:28 landing page
9:18:30 the employee directory will fit nicely
9:18:32 on the our team page
9:18:34 contact info and social media links can
9:18:36 be listed combined on the contact page
9:18:40 and we don't need sub pages for the
9:18:42 visit page we can list directions and
9:18:44 opening times on just one page
9:18:48 okay we cleaned up quite a bit by now we
9:18:50 are ready to build out the site map
9:18:53 we'll start with the main level items
9:18:56 they go across the top
9:18:59 but what about the order here
9:19:01 the order should make logical sense and
9:19:03 also indicate importance having to shop
9:19:06 first makes sense but you might
9:19:08 determine that it's more important to
9:19:10 list the visit section next and to move
9:19:13 the about section towards the end
9:19:15 also the faq are a little more important
9:19:18 than the blog so we'll switch those
9:19:21 and it's somewhat of a convention to
9:19:22 list the shopping cart last
9:19:25 speaking of the shopping cart
9:19:27 it's the only navigation link that has
9:19:29 two words
9:19:30 let's be consistent and rename it to
9:19:32 just cards
9:19:34 there we go our final main level
9:19:36 navigation
9:19:38 and now we add all of our secondary
9:19:40 content we determined earlier
9:19:43 here's everything that goes into the
9:19:44 shop category
9:19:46 note that the featured products and the
9:19:48 products sections both link to product
9:19:51 details
9:19:53 and the icon for product details looks a
9:19:55 little different it's a page stack to
9:19:58 indicate that there will be many
9:20:00 different product detail pages
9:20:02 the same happens with the blog posts
9:20:05 there are many different blog posts that
9:20:07 all link from the blog landing page
9:20:10 here are all the pages that need to go
9:20:12 under the about section
9:20:14 and here are all the different steps
9:20:15 that the user will need to complete
9:20:17 during the checkout process
9:20:19 note that instead of using simple lines
9:20:21 we have arrows to indicate the
9:20:23 sequential order of these items
9:20:26 a user won't be able to advance to the
9:20:28 billing info let's say before they
9:20:31 entered their shipping info
9:20:33 the arrows indicate that
9:20:36 also there's a horizontal bar on the
9:20:38 last arrow to indicate that once you
9:20:40 reach the order confirmation page you
9:20:42 can't go back to the confirm order page
9:20:46 we also have to account for the two
9:20:47 items that we're adding to the footer
9:20:50 since they will be on every single page
9:20:52 we just list them separately on the
9:20:53 bottom of our site map
9:20:55 okay we're almost done there's only one
9:20:58 little detail missing in fact
9:21:01 it's actually not so little we're
9:21:03 missing an important page
9:21:05 can you spot which one
9:21:07 it's the page that most people will land
9:21:09 on when they visit the sites
9:21:12 the home page
9:21:14 and will indicate with lines that the
9:21:16 home page links to all the other primary
9:21:19 navigation items
9:21:20 so there you have it a beautifully
9:21:23 organized site map
9:21:25 where we started
9:21:27 with a wild list of requirements
9:21:30 it took a little work to pare this down
9:21:32 into a meaningful logical and cohesive
9:21:35 whole
9:21:37 in the next video i will talk a little
9:21:39 bit more about the different elements
9:21:41 that make up a site map
9:21:44 let's quickly spend a little bit of time
9:21:46 talking about the symbols that we can
9:21:48 use to create a site map
9:21:50 in the last video you were already
9:21:52 introduced to most of them
9:21:54 in fact the sitemap vocabulary that we
9:21:56 will use in this course is pretty
9:21:58 limited
9:22:00 a simple rectangle represents a page on
9:22:02 your site
9:22:04 you're probably familiar with the idea
9:22:05 of a web page just think about an about
9:22:08 page or the contact page you will find
9:22:10 on many sites
9:22:12 usually there will be a unique url link
9:22:14 for each page
9:22:16 but for some sites the idea of a page
9:22:18 can seem a little outdated it stems from
9:22:21 the early days of the web when we were
9:22:23 navigating from one static page to
9:22:25 another
9:22:26 so today the term view or content node
9:22:29 might be more appropriate for sites that
9:22:31 rely heavily on dynamic content being
9:22:34 loaded
9:22:35 think about google maps for example
9:22:38 it's hard to think about that side as
9:22:39 having individual pages but there are
9:22:42 certainly a number of distinct views
9:22:44 that could be visualized with a simple
9:22:46 rectangle on a site map
9:22:48 a page stack is indicated with three
9:22:51 overlapping rectangles
9:22:53 you will use a page stack to account for
9:22:55 multiple pages of similar content
9:22:58 think about product detail pages news
9:23:00 items or blog posts
9:23:03 these pages will often also share a
9:23:05 common visual template
9:23:08 usually there will be a landing page
9:23:10 that will link to these detailed pages
9:23:12 symbolized as a page stack
9:23:14 we saw that in our previous example
9:23:17 where the blog landing page linked to
9:23:18 blog detail pages
9:23:22 sometimes it can be helpful to indicate
9:23:24 special content in your site map
9:23:27 are you including a link to a file maybe
9:23:29 a pdf or a zip file then use the shape
9:23:32 on the left to indicate file content
9:23:35 you might even put a little icon inside
9:23:37 to indicate what kind of file it is
9:23:40 if you include a form use a special icon
9:23:42 that communicates this
9:23:45 connectors tell us which pages are
9:23:47 connected and how we get from one page
9:23:49 to another
9:23:50 here we see that there will be a link on
9:23:52 the home page to the about us page
9:23:55 you can also use arrows to indicate a
9:23:57 sequence of pages that must be completed
9:24:00 in order
9:24:01 in this example the user must go from
9:24:03 the shopping cart to the billing info
9:24:06 before being able to go to the shipping
9:24:08 info
9:24:09 there's also a connector that indicates
9:24:11 that navigating back to the previous
9:24:13 page is no longer possible
9:24:16 you simply add a vertical line
9:24:17 perpendicular to the connector
9:24:21 you can use a dotted line to indicate
9:24:23 that a connection is based on a specific
9:24:25 condition
9:24:26 here for example a user can only
9:24:28 navigate to the my account page if they
9:24:30 are logged in
9:24:32 and lastly there is a more complex
9:24:34 structure to indicate decision points
9:24:38 this can be a helpful tool when you are
9:24:40 trying to account for paths in the
9:24:42 user's journey that depend on certain
9:24:44 yes no conditions
9:24:47 the example here is for a login routine
9:24:49 you'll see that there is a login page
9:24:51 with a login form
9:24:53 and from there we have a decision point
9:24:56 if the user's credentials are entered
9:24:58 correctly
9:24:59 the user proceeds to the my account page
9:25:02 otherwise an error page is displayed and
9:25:05 the user can navigate back to the login
9:25:07 page to try again
9:25:10 i'll finish this lesson with an example
9:25:12 site map again from the pasadena
9:25:14 conservatory of music project
9:25:17 let's look at a few things here in a
9:25:19 little more detail
9:25:21 you see that everything starts with the
9:25:23 home page and that there are a bunch of
9:25:25 pages that are linked from the home page
9:25:28 we decided to separate these pages into
9:25:30 two main groups
9:25:32 a primary group along the top about four
9:25:35 children four adults etc and a more
9:25:38 secondary one on the bottom support us
9:25:41 watch and listen contact us etc
9:25:45 the reason we did this was that we knew
9:25:47 that these groups of links would be
9:25:49 displayed with different hierarchies in
9:25:51 the navigation bar
9:25:53 and from these main navigation items
9:25:55 flow many secondary and some tertiary
9:25:57 pages
9:25:59 you'll also notice that you can annotate
9:26:01 the sitemap with as much information as
9:26:03 you deem necessary for the home page for
9:26:06 example we added a list of content items
9:26:08 that we wanted to include here the same
9:26:10 is true for the overview page in the
9:26:13 about section
9:26:15 you can see we employed a page stack a
9:26:17 few times
9:26:18 for example the faculty page links to
9:26:20 many detail pages one for each faculty
9:26:23 member
9:26:24 and you can spot a few forms too
9:26:28 in general i'd like to emphasize that
9:26:31 there isn't one right way to design a
9:26:33 site map
9:26:34 this is just one example how i deal with
9:26:37 this in my work
9:26:39 what's most important is that your site
9:26:40 map is visually clean and easily
9:26:43 understandable
9:26:45 add as much info as necessary to
9:26:47 communicate the structure of the site
9:26:49 and account for the content that will
9:26:51 have to be included
9:26:53 one final question you might have is
9:26:56 what software to use for creating
9:26:58 sitemaps again there's no rule here i'll
9:27:01 leave it up to you as you can see any
9:27:03 software that will let you easily draw a
9:27:05 bunch of rectangles and lines will do
9:27:08 the sitemap you're looking at was
9:27:10 created in adobe illustrator and i
9:27:12 definitely recommend this tool but
9:27:14 indesign would work too i suppose
9:27:16 photoshop on the other hand probably not
9:27:19 you could even use pencil and paper if
9:27:22 you have good drafting skills
9:27:24 in fact you might want to start out by
9:27:26 sketching your sitemap by hand before
9:27:29 creating the final version on the
9:27:31 computer
9:27:32 so by now we know what a site map is and
9:27:36 why it's an important part of the user
9:27:37 experience process
9:27:39 i've also talked about how we get from a
9:27:41 loose collection of content and
9:27:43 functionality requirements to a
9:27:45 structured sitemap diagram
9:27:48 in this lesson i want to introduce a
9:27:50 tool that lets you test your sitemaps
9:27:52 information architecture and solicit
9:27:55 feedback from actual users
9:27:57 the tool is called tree jack it's
9:28:00 developed by the company optimal
9:28:01 workshop out of new zealand they
9:28:04 actually make a few other helpful tools
9:28:06 for ux designers such as a first click
9:28:08 testing tool and tools for cart sorting
9:28:11 surveys and questionnaires
9:28:13 tree jag is a tree testing tool or put
9:28:17 another way an information architecture
9:28:19 validation software it basically allows
9:28:22 you to test if your users will be able
9:28:24 to find certain content on your site
9:28:27 easily
9:28:28 in a moment you'll have the opportunity
9:28:30 to test drive treejack yourself and
9:28:33 you'll also get to test your sitemap
9:28:35 that you're creating for the course
9:28:36 project
9:28:38 but let me first talk about a project
9:28:40 where we used tree jack in 2017 my
9:28:43 studio yay brigade launched the website
9:28:46 for a new art museum in los angeles
9:28:49 called the marciano art foundation if
9:28:51 you are ever in los angeles or if you
9:28:53 live close you should definitely check
9:28:55 out this place it has a cutting-edge
9:28:57 contemporary art collection and it's
9:28:59 located in a renovated masonic temple
9:29:02 the mysterious architecture alone is
9:29:04 worth a visit
9:29:06 anyway here's the home page of the
9:29:08 website that we created it's online at
9:29:10 marcianoartfoundation.org
9:29:14 and of course we created a sitemap as
9:29:16 part of our design process
9:29:18 you can see that the primary navigation
9:29:21 contains sections for about visit
9:29:23 exhibitions events etc
9:29:26 and there are subpages for each of these
9:29:28 primary links
9:29:29 and some other links will be listed in
9:29:31 the footer of every page
9:29:33 now at this point we thought it would be
9:29:35 a good idea to not just rely on our
9:29:37 intuition and expertise we wanted to put
9:29:40 this structure in front of living
9:29:42 breathing human beings to test if what
9:29:45 we created would actually work for our
9:29:47 users that's where treejack came in
9:29:51 you can find the tool at
9:29:53 optimalworkshop.com
9:29:55 treejack
9:29:57 treejack allows you to input all the
9:29:58 information of your sitemap and then
9:30:01 create a survey with specific user
9:30:03 prompts and then you can invite people
9:30:05 to complete the survey we send it out to
9:30:08 our network friends and colleagues and
9:30:10 also to a few of my students at calarts
9:30:13 here's the email we sent
9:30:15 note that we were upfront about the time
9:30:17 it would take to complete the survey
9:30:20 we were asking people for a favor so we
9:30:22 wanted to let them know it would only
9:30:24 take five minutes of their time
9:30:27 okay now that you have a little bit of
9:30:29 background i'd like you to take the same
9:30:31 tree jack survey that we sent out back
9:30:33 when we were still developing the
9:30:35 marciano website this will give you a
9:30:37 pretty good idea how tree jack works
9:30:40 and then in the next video i will share
9:30:42 some of the results we received
9:30:45 so now that you had a chance to take
9:30:47 that tree jack study yourself let me
9:30:50 show what we actually presented to the
9:30:52 client after we had people take the test
9:30:56 our findings so to say
9:30:59 we presented that in an in-person
9:31:00 meeting and we had a little deck of
9:31:03 results that we shared with our clients
9:31:06 we showed the tasks that we asked people
9:31:09 to fulfill
9:31:10 and notice here that that's kind of the
9:31:13 smart thing
9:31:15 that tree check allows you to do it's
9:31:17 one thing to look at a site map and say
9:31:20 is it good is it working and another to
9:31:24 approach a site map with motivations and
9:31:27 minds i hope that when you did the
9:31:30 survey you realized once you have a
9:31:32 clear motivation you can actually test a
9:31:35 site structure much more easily like
9:31:37 when is the museum open well let me see
9:31:39 what would i click on oh there's a visit
9:31:41 section that's probably it etc
9:31:44 so we listed our tasks we
9:31:47 showed you know the navigation
9:31:50 that uh people clicked on just so our
9:31:53 client got an idea of how the street
9:31:55 jack troll works
9:31:56 we included this age demographic
9:31:59 question
9:32:00 it's not completely necessary but it's
9:32:02 nice to know what the ages were
9:32:05 you can see that there weren't even that
9:32:07 many people it's 35 people i believe but
9:32:10 that gives you a pretty good idea
9:32:12 of seeing where problems lie overall
9:32:15 results were eighty percent successful
9:32:18 uh so out of all these people taking the
9:32:22 the survey 80 answered questions
9:32:25 correctly the directness is how direct
9:32:28 were
9:32:29 the people when they clicked on things
9:32:31 so if they first clicked on something
9:32:34 that let them down the wrong path and
9:32:35 then they backtracked to find a
9:32:38 successful answer that would be indirect
9:32:40 success so the directness was 76 and the
9:32:44 success was 80
9:32:45 pretty decent i think uh but still
9:32:48 there's 20 of people that somehow got
9:32:50 stuck with one of these questions
9:32:52 so which tasks were the problematic ones
9:32:55 here is an overview you can see that the

9:47:00 hello and welcome to the course web
9:47:02 design wireframes to prototypes my name
9:47:05 is roman jaster i'm a web designer web
9:47:07 developer and instructor in the graphic
9:47:09 design program at the california
9:47:11 institute of the arts this course is the
9:47:13 fourth and final course in the ui ux
9:47:15 specialization on coursera and a
9:47:17 continuation of the previous course web
9:47:20 design strategy and information
9:47:21 architecture
9:47:23 in the previous course we worked on the
9:47:24 first part of a large-scale website
9:47:26 project you should have your strategy
9:47:29 outline of scope and sitemap completed
9:47:33 this course will focus on the
9:47:34 application of that early ux thinking
9:47:37 into actual user interfaces
9:47:39 the creation of wireframes high fidelity
9:47:41 mockups and clickable prototypes
9:47:44 along the way we will also discuss
9:47:46 responsive web design and mobile web
9:47:47 challenges navigation design
9:47:50 web typography
9:47:52 the relationship between design and
9:47:53 programming and whether it's important
9:47:55 to know how to code
9:47:57 and i will give you a brief introduction
9:47:59 to the different technologies that make
9:48:00 the web work namely html css and
9:48:03 javascript
9:48:04 by the end of this course you will have
9:48:06 completed your website project and
9:48:08 should have a pretty sizable piece to
9:48:10 add to your portfolio of course allison
9:48:12 and bradley the two students featured in
9:48:14 the previous course will be back to
9:48:16 continue to walk us through their
9:48:18 thinking behind the projects they
9:48:20 created
9:48:21 so let's get started
9:48:24 [Music]
9:48:31 [Music]
9:48:35 why peer review is important to a
9:48:38 creative practice
9:48:40 design is rarely a solitary practice
9:48:43 as a designer you might be working with
9:48:45 clients or have a boss you need to
9:48:47 report to you might also be part of a
9:48:49 design team or even managing a team of
9:48:52 your own
9:48:53 you might be asked to present a project
9:48:55 to stakeholders at a company or pitch
9:48:57 your services to a new client
9:49:00 ultimately your design needs to stand
9:49:02 alone but in the process of making
9:49:04 design you should be testing out your
9:49:06 ideas with an audience getting feedback
9:49:09 and developing your design accordingly
9:49:12 design is very intertwined with
9:49:14 communication
9:49:15 and to be a successful designer you need
9:49:18 to be able to talk about your work to
9:49:20 explain it and show that you understand
9:49:23 how it's working
9:49:25 in different situations it may be
9:49:27 necessary to explain your own work and
9:49:29 other people's work in a clear and
9:49:31 constructive way
9:49:34 so in this course you'll be engaging in
9:49:36 peer review to evaluate the work of your
9:49:38 peers as well as get feedback on your
9:49:40 own work
9:49:41 if you're serious about working as a
9:49:43 designer or improving your design skills
9:49:46 consider peer review as excellent
9:49:48 practice
9:49:49 in peer review you'll be asked to
9:49:51 observe and openly reflect on what is
9:49:54 working and not working in a peer's
9:49:56 graphic design submission
9:49:59 the goal of this exercise is to help
9:50:01 your fellow designers move their work
9:50:03 forward and for you to get that same
9:50:06 advice in turn additionally it's for you
9:50:09 to practice a working vocabulary and
9:50:11 discourse around making graphic design
9:50:14 all of which will help you with your
9:50:17 future path as a designer
9:50:19 the next video will give you some
9:50:21 specific tips for completing peer review
9:50:24 within the specialization
9:50:28 [Music]
9:50:30 peer review tips
9:50:32 critique and feedback are essential
9:50:34 parts of the design process
9:50:37 they're an essential way to see if your
9:50:38 design is communicating what you
9:50:40 intended to an audience
9:50:42 so in this video i'm going to outline a
9:50:44 few tips for completing peer review
9:50:46 assignments successfully in this class
9:50:49 participation in peer review
9:50:50 thoughtfully and meaningfully will help
9:50:52 you practice these indispensable skills
9:50:56 submitting assignments
9:50:59 read the instructions carefully
9:51:01 make sure you take a look at the review
9:51:03 criteria
9:51:04 so you know how your assignment will be
9:51:06 assessed
9:51:07 review any examples your instructor may
9:51:09 have provided and upload exactly what
9:51:12 you are asked to do
9:51:13 if something in the instructions isn't
9:51:15 clear post your question in the course
9:51:17 forums so staff can assist you
9:51:21 make it your best work
9:51:23 this is your creation and your
9:51:25 creativity and should be an exercise
9:51:27 that demonstrates what you can do
9:51:30 practice assignments should be
9:51:31 opportunities to fail but a final
9:51:34 assignment should be something worthy of
9:51:36 your professional portfolio
9:51:38 ask for specific feedback
9:51:41 clarify what you need from your peers in
9:51:43 your review where applicable use the
9:51:46 designated comment field to ask for
9:51:48 specific feedback on your submission
9:51:50 [Music]
9:51:52 submit on time
9:51:54 refer to the due date for submitting
9:51:56 your assignment on the grades page
9:51:57 within the course
9:51:59 if you're too early or too late the peer
9:52:02 review process may not work as intended
9:52:06 reviewing submissions
9:52:09 take your time with reviewing
9:52:11 don't rush this
9:52:12 look at each part of your peers
9:52:14 submission carefully and compare what
9:52:17 has been submitted to the expectations
9:52:19 set out in the rubric
9:52:21 be objective
9:52:23 when reviewing consider your role as a
9:52:25 viewer or reader of the work focus only
9:52:28 on what the rubric asks you to evaluate
9:52:31 and try to limit your personal opinions
9:52:34 about the work in your comments
9:52:37 be clear and informative
9:52:39 generic feedback such as good or okay
9:52:42 are unhelpful comments for peer reviews
9:52:45 because they don't give a your peer any
9:52:47 specific information about what was
9:52:49 working in their submission
9:52:52 likewise it doesn't help to say it
9:52:54 doesn't work or i don't like it because
9:52:57 it doesn't give your peer enough
9:52:58 information to help them reassess their
9:53:01 designs
9:53:02 try to articulate a detailed response
9:53:05 that helps to affirm your peers choices
9:53:07 in their submission or guides your peer
9:53:10 towards the goal of an assignment if it
9:53:12 appears that they are off track
9:53:15 be constructive
9:53:17 your feedback should motivate your peer
9:53:19 to make adjustments and work towards
9:53:21 improvement
9:53:23 if there's a need to correct your peer
9:53:25 be honest but it's more helpful to
9:53:27 include specific recommendations or
9:53:30 strategies to help the learner improve
9:53:33 be generous
9:53:35 recognize that everyone comes to this
9:53:37 course with a different level of
9:53:39 experience as well as a different
9:53:41 approach to making work
9:53:43 honor and value these differences
9:53:46 bad grammar and spelling shouldn't
9:53:48 contribute to a bad grade
9:53:50 please be generous
9:53:51 likewise please don't penalize students
9:53:54 for small mistakes
9:53:57 flag plagiarism and dishonest behavior
9:54:00 these violate coursera's honor code if
9:54:03 you're asked to review an assignment and
9:54:05 it appears to be plagiarized you can
9:54:07 flag it so coursera learner support are
9:54:10 notified
9:54:13 complete your reviews on time
9:54:16 refer to the due date for completing the
9:54:17 minimum number of peer reviews on the
9:54:20 grades page within the course
9:54:22 if you're too early or too late the peer
9:54:24 review process may not work as intended
9:54:29 consider reviewing more than the minimum
9:54:31 number of peers for a given assignment
9:54:34 not only is this helpful for your fellow
9:54:36 peers but it's also instructive for you
9:54:39 to see a greater range of submissions to
9:54:42 inform your own work
9:54:43 and the more you practice critique the
9:54:46 better you will get it in and ultimately
9:54:48 the better your own work will be as you
9:54:50 integrate your critical abilities into
9:54:53 your own design process
9:54:57 [Music]
9:55:01 how to apply feedback to your assignment
9:55:04 so your assignments graded and you have
9:55:06 peer feedback
9:55:08 now what
9:55:09 remember the goal of peer review is to
9:55:11 help you improve your work as a maker
9:55:13 and viewer
9:55:15 try not to see the feedback you receive
9:55:17 as either being positive or negative
9:55:20 or an affirmation
9:55:21 that you're doing something absolutely
9:55:24 right or wrong
9:55:26 through peer review you're inviting
9:55:27 other perspectives on your work to see
9:55:30 and comment on things that you might not
9:55:32 see
9:55:33 by engaging in peer review you're
9:55:35 practicing the skill you'll need in your
9:55:37 creative and professional life
9:55:39 it's important to understand how an
9:55:41 audience will react to your work
9:55:44 peer feedback should never feel like a
9:55:46 personal attack if you're discouraged by
9:55:49 some feedback then step back and
9:55:52 consider why a peer would react that way
9:55:54 to your work
9:55:55 are you seeing patterns or common themes
9:55:58 in the feedback you receive is there
9:55:59 something you need to address in the
9:56:01 work
9:56:02 if you receive peer feedback that isn't
9:56:04 constructive to your work
9:56:06 that's okay it's not ideal but don't let
9:56:09 it discourage you remember the goal of
9:56:11 feedback is to help you improve if you
9:56:14 need additional feedback you can
9:56:16 resubmit
9:56:18 at the end of the day remember both
9:56:20 positive and negative feedback can be
9:56:22 useful
9:56:23 positive feedback can let you know what
9:56:25 is working what to keep as is or alter
9:56:28 only slightly in your assignment
9:56:31 negative feedback might let you know
9:56:33 what needs to be changed developed or
9:56:36 reconsidered
9:56:38 as the designer you might disagree with
9:56:40 the feedback but it's always worth
9:56:42 examining someone else's point of view
9:56:45 an outside perspective can be very
9:56:47 useful especially because designers
9:56:49 often get too close to their own work
9:56:52 and sometimes can't see when parts of it
9:56:54 aren't working or communicating properly
9:56:57 if you receive a low grade consider why
9:57:00 your peers gave you that grade
9:57:02 have they justified their grading in the
9:57:04 comments
9:57:05 consider their perspectives and try
9:57:07 incorporating their suggestions into the
9:57:09 assignment and resubmit
9:57:16 [Music]
9:57:16 [Applause]
9:57:19 [Music]
9:57:23 this week is all about wireframes this
9:57:26 is the first time in the process that we
9:57:27 will address the screen
9:57:29 in the previous course much of what we
9:57:31 talked about was quite abstract and
9:57:33 somewhat theoretical
9:57:35 now things are going to get much more
9:57:37 concrete as we're starting to actually
9:57:39 lay out elements for the user interface
9:57:43 this week you will learn why working on
9:57:44 wireframes first before designing high
9:57:47 fidelity mockups is a helpful
9:57:49 intermediate step i'll also talk about
9:57:52 responsive design
9:57:53 navigation systems way finding common
9:57:56 design patterns and strategies for
9:57:58 homepage design
9:58:00 and we'll discuss which tools are most
9:58:02 appropriate for creating wireframes
9:58:04 because creating those will be your next
9:58:06 homework assignment
9:58:08 as you can see there's a lot to cover so
9:58:10 let's jump right in
9:58:12 [Music]
9:58:19 we have arrived at phase four in our
9:58:21 journey along the user experience
9:58:23 process
9:58:24 that means we are ready for wireframes
9:58:27 and that also means that we are starting
9:58:28 to focus on individual pages of our site
9:58:32 so as a quick reminder in the previous
9:58:35 course we established our goals in the
9:58:36 strategy phase and then defined
9:58:39 requirements to meet these goals in the
9:58:41 outline of scope and finally we answered
9:58:43 the question of how the pieces fit
9:58:45 together
9:58:46 to create a cohesive logical structure
9:58:48 to the information of our sites
9:58:51 now during wireframing we are asking the
9:58:53 question how are the pieces presented on
9:58:56 the screen
9:58:57 so we're dealing with the design of
9:58:59 actual screens
9:59:01 in fact it's the first time in our
9:59:03 design process that we're explicitly
9:59:05 thinking about the ui the user interface
9:59:09 everything up to this point has been
9:59:11 much more big picture thinking
9:59:13 to strain a few metaphors up until now
9:59:16 we've been hovering high in the air
9:59:18 looking at overarching questions of
9:59:20 strategy requirements and structure now
9:59:23 we are ready to enter the trenches and
9:59:25 work on the interface that the user will
9:59:27 actually interact with but we are not
9:59:29 ready to design the final appearance of
9:59:32 the interface
9:59:33 instead wireframes are low fidelity
9:59:36 mockups
9:59:37 and low fidelity is a bit of a funny
9:59:40 word
9:59:41 it means simplified or low detail
9:59:45 so wireframes are simplified versions of
9:59:48 our user interface
9:59:50 here you can see an example it's from a
9:59:52 website project for the mac center in
9:59:54 los angeles which i worked on a few
9:59:56 years ago
9:59:57 you can see that wireframes involve a
9:59:59 lot of rectangular gray boxes and fairly
10:00:02 uninspiring typography
10:00:04 it's a bit drab i agree but that's by
10:00:07 design wireframes are not meant to be
10:00:10 visually exciting we're purposely
10:00:12 limiting the amount of detail we are
10:00:14 concerned with so that we don't get
10:00:16 sidetracked by having to think about
10:00:18 colors or typefaces or precise scale
10:00:21 relationships or which particular images
10:00:23 we'll use
10:00:24 we're mainly deciding which elements go
10:00:27 where on the page
10:00:28 where's the logo where's the navigation
10:00:31 where's the page title how many columns
10:00:33 do we need and what goes inside the
10:00:35 footer these kinds of questions the term
10:00:38 low fidelity is the opposite of high
10:00:40 fidelity a word you might be more
10:00:42 familiar with in the context of sound
10:00:44 quality when we say high fidelity or
10:00:47 hifi we mean high detail something
10:00:50 that's very similar to the original
10:00:52 in the context of user interface design
10:00:54 a high fidelity mockup includes all
10:00:57 those details that we're omitting during
10:00:59 wireframing such as colors and typeface
10:01:01 choices
10:01:03 you might ask do we even need this
10:01:05 intermediate step of creating wireframes
10:01:08 why not jump straight away to designing
10:01:10 high fidelity mockups the reason is that
10:01:12 wireframes give us the opportunity to
10:01:14 focus on more fundamental questions
10:01:16 about the layout of our pages without
10:01:19 getting caught up in a lot of details
10:01:22 it's maybe a little bit like making a
10:01:23 pencil sketch before painting an oil
10:01:26 painting or you can think of wireframes
10:01:28 a little bit like an architectural
10:01:30 blueprint
10:01:32 in this side-by-side comparison of the
10:01:34 wireframe and the visual mock-up of a
10:01:36 home page take a look at the main
10:01:38 headline where it says mac center for
10:01:40 art and architecture los angeles in the
10:01:43 wireframe on the left we can tell that
10:01:45 the headline is placed below a large
10:01:47 image that's by the way what the boxes
10:01:49 with the big x's represent they are
10:01:51 placeholders for the images and we know
10:01:54 that the headline will be visually more
10:01:56 prominent because in the wireframe it's
10:01:58 bigger
10:01:59 and that's all we need to decide for now
10:02:01 we don't yet have to hunt for the
10:02:03 perfect typeface or the exact shade of
10:02:05 red or the exact type size those are
10:02:08 important concerns eventually but also
10:02:11 things that can derail our design
10:02:13 process
10:02:14 maybe you've been in this situation
10:02:16 before you're trying to quickly sketch
10:02:18 out a few ideas on the computer and all
10:02:20 of a sudden you realize that you've been
10:02:22 spending the last hour looking for just
10:02:24 the right typeface that's where
10:02:26 wireframes help you out the fact that
10:02:29 they are so restrictive visually means
10:02:31 that you can focus on the big picture
10:02:33 decisions
10:02:35 and as abstract and simplified as the
10:02:37 wireframe on the left is
10:02:39 there are tons of big picture decisions
10:02:42 that have been made we know for example
10:02:44 that the logo will be on the top left
10:02:46 and there will be a horizontal main
10:02:48 navigation next to it and a more
10:02:51 secondary utility navigation in the top
10:02:54 right corner there will be three text
10:02:56 columns with images giving a brief
10:02:58 overview of the main mission of the mac
10:03:00 center each with a button that will lead
10:03:02 the user to the respective section of
10:03:04 the site
10:03:06 on the right you see how these
10:03:07 structural decisions are reflected in
10:03:09 the visual mock-up here all visual
10:03:12 design decisions have been made
10:03:14 i'll leave you with one more metaphor to
10:03:16 distinguish wireframes and visual
10:03:18 mockups
10:03:19 wireframes are a bit like x-rays of the
10:03:22 final page designs
10:03:24 they represent the underlying skeleton
10:03:26 of each page
10:03:28 while the visual markups represent a
10:03:30 beautiful skin
10:03:31 we'll talk all about visual markups in
10:03:34 the next week for now let's discuss in
10:03:36 detail all the things we have to figure
10:03:38 out during wireframing
10:03:41 here's one question that looms large now
10:03:43 that we are addressing the layout of the
10:03:45 screen during the wireframing phase
10:03:47 what is the size of our screen what is
10:03:50 the size of our canvas that we are
10:03:52 working on if you think about other
10:03:55 media the size is often the first thing
10:03:57 that is decided
10:03:59 for printed pieces like books and
10:04:01 magazines and posters we often get the
10:04:04 dimensions as part of the design brief
10:04:06 even videos have a predefined size
10:04:09 now for websites this topic is a whole
10:04:11 lot more complicated
10:04:13 and i don't think it's too much of a
10:04:15 spoiler to get right to the point
10:04:17 websites do not have one specific size
10:04:20 since they must work on a variety of
10:04:22 screen sizes and devices
10:04:24 websites should be designed to fluidly
10:04:26 respond to any viewport size and look
10:04:29 and function well no matter if you're on
10:04:31 a tiny smartphone or a giant desktop
10:04:34 computer screen instead of one size we
10:04:37 have to deal with many many sizes
10:04:41 as a small side note it's interesting to
10:04:43 mention that before the internet there
10:04:45 was one medium that routinely had to
10:04:47 deal with the issue of multiple canvas
10:04:49 sizes
10:04:50 album covers
10:04:52 for a while new music came out on
10:04:54 different formats and album cover art
10:04:56 had to work for vinyl cds and for tapes
10:05:00 and designers how to find graphical
10:05:02 solutions for these different dimensions
10:05:05 in a funny way we're looking at some
10:05:06 early response of design in these
10:05:08 examples but even then there were only a
10:05:11 handful of music packaging sizes that
10:05:14 had to be addressed
10:05:16 for websites we're dealing with a
10:05:18 bottomless and ever-expanding list of
10:05:20 devices and sizes
10:05:23 so the idea that websites have to work
10:05:25 in so many different size contexts is
10:05:28 today fully at the heart of what it
10:05:30 means to design websites
10:05:32 you could call that a limitation
10:05:35 designers can certainly be frustrated by
10:05:37 the absence of a concrete canvas size
10:05:40 it makes designing websites much more
10:05:42 complicated
10:05:43 but it's also what makes the web so
10:05:45 unique and interesting
10:05:47 and instead of thinking about it as a
10:05:49 limitation
10:05:50 think about it as an advantage
10:05:52 you can start reading a blog article on
10:05:55 your desktop computer at work then
10:05:57 continue it on your smartphone on the
10:05:59 train and then finish it on your tablet
10:06:01 device at home before going to bed all
10:06:04 while accessing the same website each
10:06:06 time
10:06:07 that's pretty cool
10:06:09 and speaking of limitations
10:06:11 try reading a printed newspaper in a
10:06:13 crowded bus or subway and you'll
10:06:15 appreciate the fact that the website
10:06:17 version of the newspaper will gracefully
10:06:20 morph to fit the palm-sized device you
10:06:22 can easily hold in a tight space
10:06:25 and yet dealing with many different
10:06:27 screen sizes makes designing websites
10:06:30 more complicated these days
10:06:32 and it used to be much simpler
10:06:35 for a long time we would access websites
10:06:37 from desktop computers and laptops and
10:06:40 that was it and for quite a while the
10:06:42 convention was to use 960 pixels for the
10:06:46 width of websites
10:06:48 that was the lowest common denominator
10:06:50 and worked for most monitors out there
10:06:52 for those screens that were bigger there
10:06:54 would be empty space on the left and the
10:06:56 right with the site's content sitting in
10:06:58 the middle of the browser
10:07:00 if the browser window got smaller the
10:07:02 website would be cut off and you would
10:07:04 have to scroll horizontally but again
10:07:06 most screens were big enough to show at
10:07:08 least 960 pixels across so designers
10:07:12 used that as the standards
10:07:14 there were even whole frameworks and
10:07:16 templates for the 960 grid when
10:07:19 designers started a new website they
10:07:21 knew how wide to make their canvas but
10:07:24 then this happens in 2007 apple released
10:07:27 the first iphone
10:07:29 and this phone was in part so successful
10:07:32 because it had a web browser that did a
10:07:34 pretty good job allowing users to serve
10:07:36 websites that were designed for the
10:07:38 desktop and you would see the website
10:07:40 displayed very small at first so it
10:07:42 would fit on the screen and then you
10:07:44 could pinch and zoom to be able to read
10:07:46 a certain section of a page
10:07:48 but it was far from an optimal user
10:07:51 experience and users quickly saw how
10:07:54 native apps were so much easier to use
10:07:57 than websites where they defined with
10:08:00 a few years after ethan mccod wrote his
10:08:03 very influential article entitled
10:08:06 responsive web design in which he laid
10:08:09 out a philosophical and technical case
10:08:11 for making websites that work well no
10:08:13 matter the size of the screen and the
10:08:16 term that ethan mccott coined in the
10:08:18 piece is what we use today to describe
10:08:21 websites that are optimized to work on
10:08:24 any screen size
10:08:25 responsive web design the blog post is
10:08:28 still online by the way on the website a
10:08:31 list of parts and i would urge you to
10:08:34 read it not only is it still a
10:08:36 meaningful manifesto for web designers
10:08:38 but it's also an interesting and
10:08:40 important historical document for the
10:08:43 field of web design
10:08:45 for a while responsive design was an
10:08:47 add-on service that web designers
10:08:50 offered for clients that cared enough to
10:08:52 create an optimized mobile experience
10:08:55 but nowadays it's really the default way
10:08:58 of creating websites contemporary
10:09:00 websites adjust seamlessly to whatever
10:09:03 width they are confronted with
10:09:06 but what does all of this mean for our
10:09:08 wireframes
10:09:10 if we don't know the screen size of our
10:09:12 users which size should we mark
10:09:15 my recommendation is to create two sizes
10:09:18 one small one for the mobile phone
10:09:20 experience and one bigger one that
10:09:23 describes what the site will look like
10:09:25 on desktop browsers
10:09:28 for the mobile size i recommend a width
10:09:30 of 320 pixels which is about the width
10:09:32 of the smallest smartphone
10:09:35 and for the desktop version use a width
10:09:37 of around 1000 pixels
10:09:39 if you are concerned about how specific
10:09:41 design elements will behave on
10:09:43 intermediate sizes or on really big
10:09:46 sizes think about giant desktop screens
10:09:49 for example then you can mark up
10:09:51 additional sizes
10:09:53 but don't think that you must somehow
10:09:54 mark up every conceivable screen size
10:09:58 that would prove to be a fool's errands
10:10:00 there are just too many possible sizes
10:10:03 okay we've been talking a lot about the
10:10:05 width of website so far you might ask
10:10:08 what about the heights how tall should i
10:10:10 make my wireframes the answer is simple
10:10:13 make them as tall as necessary to fit
10:10:16 all the content of the page you are
10:10:18 designing
10:10:19 we don't know the height of the user's
10:10:20 viewport either but remember websites
10:10:23 come with the assumption that they are
10:10:25 to be scrolled vertically so when you're
10:10:27 looking at a wireframe like this it's
10:10:29 assumed that the user will only ever see
10:10:32 part of the page design at a time and
10:10:34 that they will scroll vertically through
10:10:37 the page don't be afraid of designing
10:10:39 websites that are long and need to be
10:10:42 scrolls
10:10:43 one last issue i want to address is the
10:10:45 question of which size mobile or desktop
10:10:48 to mock up first
10:10:50 there are different opinions about this
10:10:52 and to be honest for me and my own work
10:10:55 it depends on the project i'm working on
10:10:57 but there are many who advocate strongly
10:11:00 for an approach that is called mobile
10:11:02 firsts their argument is quite
10:11:05 persuasive
10:11:06 if you focus on the smallest screen
10:11:08 first you will necessarily have to focus
10:11:11 on the most essential content and
10:11:13 functionality because the available
10:11:16 space is so restricted one thing is for
10:11:19 sure don't think about the mobile
10:11:21 experience as something optional or
10:11:24 novel
10:11:25 stats tell us that mobile web visitors
10:11:27 nowadays often outnumber those that are
10:11:30 on traditional desktop computers
10:11:32 and this trend is likely to continue if
10:11:35 you'd like to read more about the
10:11:37 thinking behind a mobile first approach
10:11:40 to web design i recommend reading an
10:11:42 excellent primer on the topic by luke
10:11:45 voblevsky
10:11:47 let's talk a little bit about different
10:11:49 navigation systems after all getting
10:11:51 around your site efficiently is one of
10:11:53 the most important criteria for a
10:11:55 successful site we already laid a lot of
10:11:58 the groundwork for navigation in the
10:12:00 sitemap phase here we were mostly
10:12:02 concerned with the question of how the
10:12:04 user would get from one point to another
10:12:07 now during the wireframing phase we have
10:12:10 to answer the question of how navigation
10:12:12 elements relate to each other and to the
10:12:14 current page and this brings up other
10:12:17 questions
10:12:18 are some links more important than
10:12:20 others
10:12:21 how are the links grouped
10:12:23 what other options are available based
10:12:25 on the current task or goals
10:12:28 and a very fundamental question for
10:12:31 every user where am i
10:12:33 this last question we will consider in
10:12:35 more detail in the next video for now
10:12:38 let's discuss how different navigation
10:12:40 systems can help us design pages that
10:12:42 make sense for your users and that let
10:12:45 them accomplish the tasks they need to
10:12:47 accomplish
10:12:48 in this video we will talk about primary
10:12:51 navigation secondary navigation utility
10:12:54 navigation and navigation to related
10:12:57 contents we'll also discuss inline links
10:13:00 something called an index and search
10:13:03 along the way i'll be referring to three
10:13:06 websites as examples for these different
10:13:08 navigation systems
10:13:09 they are sites for the pasadena
10:13:11 conservatory the marciano art foundation
10:13:14 and the mac center
10:13:15 as you probably know by now i was
10:13:17 involved in the conception design and
10:13:19 development of all of them so i have a
10:13:21 pretty good insight of why things were
10:13:23 done a certain way and i'll also use a
10:13:25 fictitious wireframe example to discuss
10:13:28 navigational concepts in detail okay
10:13:30 let's get started
10:13:32 the first one to talk about is the
10:13:34 primary navigation
10:13:36 you're probably already familiar with
10:13:37 the term it's the main navigation of the
10:13:40 site showcasing all top level sections
10:13:43 of the website
10:13:45 looking at a site map which as you
10:13:47 remember was the final outcome of the
10:13:49 previous phase determining which
10:13:51 sections make up the primary navigation
10:13:54 should be pretty easy it's the items
10:13:56 that are directly attached to the home
10:13:58 page well maybe not all of them it does
10:14:01 get a little more complicated let's
10:14:03 leave cart and account aside for the
10:14:06 moment they could be part of the primary
10:14:08 navigation but since they are somewhat
10:14:10 different from the rest of the main
10:14:12 content of the site we'll deal with them
10:14:14 separately in a moment
10:14:17 so here you can see the main navigation
10:14:19 implemented into a wireframe presumably
10:14:21 for the homepage of a site again we are
10:14:24 not indicating that the links will be
10:14:26 designed as grey buttons in a rather
10:14:28 goofy typeface this is simply a sketch
10:14:31 but while it doesn't tell us much about
10:14:33 the look and feel it does tell us the
10:14:35 order of the links in the primary
10:14:37 navigation and that it will be laid out
10:14:39 horizontally across the screen next to
10:14:42 the logo
10:14:43 in the desktop version of the site at
10:14:45 least i'll mainly focus on talking about
10:14:47 desktop here for the sake of simplicity
10:14:50 if you study a lot of websites you'll
10:14:52 notice that placing the primary
10:14:54 navigation to run horizontally across
10:14:56 the top of the page has become quite the
10:14:58 convention
10:14:59 most sites do exactly that
10:15:02 though another option would be to put
10:15:04 the primary navigation running
10:15:05 vertically on the left
10:15:07 and you'll find sites that do this as
10:15:10 well
10:15:10 and there's nothing inherently wrong
10:15:12 with that it always depends on the
10:15:14 project you're working on if you for
10:15:16 example have a large number of primary
10:15:18 links or if the labels of the links are
10:15:21 really long this vertical version might
10:15:24 work better for you but you're also
10:15:26 allocating a pretty large area of the
10:15:28 screen and entire left column only to
10:15:31 the navigation
10:15:33 so if you have a lot of other content to
10:15:34 display on your pages you'll probably
10:15:36 want to stick to a horizontal primary
10:15:39 menu
10:15:40 in our three example sites each one
10:15:42 displays the primary navigation menus
10:15:45 horizontally in the desktop version
10:15:47 here's the pasadena conservatory of
10:15:49 music sites it has about four children
10:15:52 for adults
10:15:54 new students current students events and
10:15:56 blog as part of the main level
10:15:59 navigation the marciano art foundation
10:16:01 includes abouts exhibitions education
10:16:05 visits events and the building and the
10:16:08 mac center website has visit sites
10:16:11 programming rudolf m schindler residency
10:16:14 program bookstore and supports the next
10:16:17 navigation system to talk about is the
10:16:19 secondary navigation this represents the
10:16:22 subsections of our site now some sites
10:16:25 are so simple and small that they get
10:16:27 away without any subsections and
10:16:29 secondary navigation but most sites do
10:16:32 have them and again you can look at your
10:16:34 sitemap and see pretty clearly which
10:16:36 pages belong to the secondary navigation
10:16:39 in this example the shop and about
10:16:42 sections have subpages that will need a
10:16:45 secondary navigation
10:16:46 and how could this look in our wireframe
10:16:49 well we could have a drop down menu when
10:16:52 the user hovers over a primary menu item
10:16:55 this would allow the user to get to the
10:16:57 secondary pages without having to visit
10:16:59 a primary landing page first on the
10:17:02 secondary pages we could also have a
10:17:04 secondary menu in this case i added it
10:17:07 to the left side of the screen going
10:17:09 down vertically this would allow the
10:17:11 user to quickly navigate between
10:17:13 different secondary pages and it also
10:17:15 provides a quick overview of what other
10:17:18 pages exist in this section the marciano
10:17:21 art foundation does not employ drop down
10:17:23 menus
10:17:24 the user clicks on one of the primary
10:17:26 menu links and any secondary pages are
10:17:29 listed here
10:17:30 and at the bottom of each secondary page
10:17:33 is a list of other secondary pages that
10:17:36 are part of this section the mac center
10:17:38 side makes use of simple drop down menus
10:17:42 an advantage here is that the user can
10:17:44 get to any of the secondary pages very
10:17:46 quickly and they also get an overview of
10:17:48 all the secondary content by just
10:17:51 hovering over the primary links
10:17:53 there are two sections visit and
10:17:55 bookstore that don't have subpages so
10:17:58 these don't have a drop down menu
10:18:00 the secondary navigation on the
10:18:02 secondary pages is handled horizontally
10:18:05 across the top
10:18:07 and the pasadena conservatory site
10:18:10 employs something that we at the time
10:18:12 questioned fat menus
10:18:14 when you click on a primary menu
10:18:17 you get a very large drop down that not
10:18:19 only includes links but also images
10:18:22 buttons and small text blurbs
10:18:25 we liked how we could employ typographic
10:18:27 hierarchy and create visual excitements
10:18:30 of course these fat menus are not
10:18:32 tenable on mobile devices there's just
10:18:35 not enough room for them on a small
10:18:37 screen
10:18:38 here we have a more straightforward text
10:18:40 only menu
10:18:42 let's quickly also look how the other
10:18:44 two sides deal with the mobile size
10:18:47 on the marciano side the navigation
10:18:49 collapses and we get a menu link that
10:18:51 opens up the navigation
10:18:53 on the mac center website the navigation
10:18:56 is also hidden at first and can be
10:18:57 opened with the hamburger menu icon and
10:19:00 you can also open and close the
10:19:02 secondary navigation items by tapping on
10:19:04 the main links
10:19:06 the ones that have secondary items
10:19:08 indicate this via a small triangle on
10:19:11 the right
10:19:12 so besides the primary and secondary
10:19:14 navigation we also have something called
10:19:16 utility navigation
10:19:18 there is no strict rule of what to place
10:19:20 inside a utility menu or whether you
10:19:23 even need one
10:19:24 but often important parts of the site
10:19:26 that are not directly part of the site's
10:19:28 main content will be grouped here
10:19:31 so that could be the help section or the
10:19:33 shopping cart or the login or account
10:19:36 links or the social media links
10:19:39 in our fictitious example here we could
10:19:41 argue that the cart and account links
10:19:43 while important options are somewhat
10:19:46 different from the site's main content
10:19:48 they are utilitarian in nature so these
10:19:51 would be good candidates for the utility
10:19:53 navigation
10:19:54 this navigation is often placed in the
10:19:57 top right corner beside and above the
10:20:00 primary navigation the other two links
10:20:02 we'll include here are links to our
10:20:04 social media instagram and facebook in
10:20:06 this case they will be available on
10:20:08 every single screen but they are pretty
10:20:11 discreet and do not get in the way of
10:20:13 the contents let's check in how the
10:20:15 three example sites deal with the
10:20:17 utility navigation
10:20:19 well on the marciano side the link's
10:20:21 bookstore mailing list and contact are
10:20:24 treated differently they are smaller
10:20:26 they almost look like a utility menu and
10:20:29 the reason we treated them differently
10:20:30 here was that they were somewhat less
10:20:32 important and we wanted the other six
10:20:35 main links to stand out more
10:20:37 we basically created different visual
10:20:39 hierarchies for two sets of navigational
10:20:41 items
10:20:42 the same for the pasadena conservatory
10:20:45 sites
10:20:46 here contact mailing list and donate are
10:20:49 treated differently than the primary
10:20:51 navigation links below and we also have
10:20:53 links to facebook and the rss feed here
10:20:57 on the mac center side the menu items
10:20:59 about contact links press and mailing
10:21:02 list are part of the utility navigation
10:21:05 if you count all the links in the header
10:21:06 here you'll get to 12. that's more than
10:21:09 the rule we discussed in a previous week
10:21:11 of not having more than 5 to 9 choices
10:21:14 at a time
10:21:15 and if you imagine the orange primary
10:21:17 navigation here to have a total of 12
10:21:20 items that would be visually really
10:21:22 overwhelming and it wouldn't even fit
10:21:24 horizontally
10:21:25 instead we have two menus one with seven
10:21:28 options one with five the different
10:21:31 visual hierarchy for the two sets of
10:21:33 links makes the scanning of those
10:21:35 choices much easier for the user the
10:21:37 next navigation system to talk about is
10:21:40 the related content navigation you can
10:21:43 think about this navigation in terms of
10:21:45 amazon's customers who bought this
10:21:47 product also like the following option
10:21:50 so these are shortcuts to other content
10:21:53 that are dependent on the current
10:21:55 context so it might be links to other
10:21:58 products that are related to the product
10:22:00 that the user is currently looking at
10:22:03 on the mac center website you will find
10:22:05 this sort of navigation when looking at
10:22:07 an exhibition under programming you'll
10:22:10 often find related programming in the
10:22:12 right column
10:22:14 and this makes a lot of sense for the
10:22:15 user right
10:22:16 also notice the social media share
10:22:18 buttons here they are also navigation
10:22:21 that is based on the current context
10:22:23 they allow you to share the current page
10:22:26 on facebook twitter or via email inline
10:22:29 links are simply links embedded in the
10:22:32 content itself that's what made the web
10:22:35 so famous in the first place
10:22:37 the ability to hyperlink a word to any
10:22:40 other page in the entire world wide web
10:22:43 just one piece of advice though
10:22:45 don't add too many links to your writing
10:22:48 as you might have noticed before when
10:22:49 reading wikipedia articles or certain
10:22:51 blog posts it can make the text pretty
10:22:54 distracting and difficult to read
10:22:57 okay only two more navigational systems
10:22:59 to cover an index is simply a list of
10:23:02 items sorted in some way most of the
10:23:05 time the sorting is done alphabetically
10:23:07 or chronologically
10:23:09 whenever you have a long list of options
10:23:11 let's say a list of employees or a list
10:23:13 of blog posts or a list of states it
10:23:16 makes sense to display them as an index
10:23:19 here's an example of links to previously
10:23:21 placed orders that are presented as a
10:23:24 list sorted by dates
10:23:26 a good example of an index is on the
10:23:28 pasadena conservatory side
10:23:31 when you go to about and faculty you get
10:23:33 an alphabetized list of all faculty
10:23:36 members while the order is a little more
10:23:38 complicated at first we have a small
10:23:40 list of faculty who are artist teachers
10:23:43 their order is alphabetical but they are
10:23:45 first then comes an alphabetical list of
10:23:48 everyone else notice too that you can
10:23:49 use a filter and search function here to
10:23:52 narrow down the list
10:23:54 that comes in handy if you're looking
10:23:55 for a specific name or a specific
10:23:58 department
10:24:00 on the mac center website you can look
10:24:02 at all past programming which is
10:24:04 presented as an index sorted by date
10:24:06 with the more recent events and
10:24:08 exhibitions first
10:24:10 that list by the way is so long that it
10:24:12 is paginated
10:24:14 and here too you have a few filters that
10:24:17 allow you to narrow down the list
10:24:21 and then lastly we have search
10:24:24 this is for users that want to
10:24:26 circumvent the navigation altogether
10:24:29 because they are looking for very
10:24:31 specific content via a keyword
10:24:34 the question whether a site needs a
10:24:36 search or not should have been answered
10:24:38 in the outline of scope phase
10:24:40 very small sites generally can get away
10:24:43 without a search
10:24:44 for other sites think about amazon or
10:24:47 netflix the search is the primary method
10:24:50 for users to find any content
10:24:53 so in our wireframe example here let's
10:24:56 add the search functionality as an
10:24:58 option to the utility navigation
10:25:00 that's usually a great spot for it
10:25:03 and for most sites it's appropriate to
10:25:05 have an icon often a magnifying glass
10:25:08 that will bring up a search field after
10:25:10 being clicked
10:25:12 of course if your site relies on search
10:25:14 as a primary way of navigation then you
10:25:17 probably want to display the search
10:25:18 field at all times as part of your
10:25:20 header design that way a user doesn't
10:25:23 have to perform an extra click to reveal
10:25:25 the search fields but on sites where the
10:25:28 search is merely a supplemental
10:25:30 navigation tool it's best to remove the
10:25:32 search field until it is needed that
10:25:34 makes for a less busy design and that's
10:25:37 what's happening in our three example
10:25:39 sites
10:25:40 each of them uses the magnifying glass
10:25:43 symbol located in the utility menu which
10:25:46 then reveals a large search field for
10:25:49 input
10:25:51 okay
10:25:52 we covered quite a few different
10:25:54 navigation systems and talked about how
10:25:56 they can help you to structure the
10:25:58 navigation choices for your users so
10:26:00 that they will find their way to your
10:26:03 contents next up let's talk about
10:26:05 answering the user's question where am i
10:26:08 one of the more fundamental
10:26:09 psychological fears that most humans
10:26:11 share is the fear of being lost getting
10:26:14 lost on a freeway or inside an airport
10:26:17 or inside the woods is not the idea of
10:26:19 fun for most people and we probably all
10:26:22 have a story of getting lost as children
10:26:24 and the terror that inflicted on us not
10:26:27 to mention on our parents
10:26:29 when we are designing websites we should
10:26:31 make sure that people don't get lost in
10:26:33 them because if they do get lost or even
10:26:36 if they are just a little confused of
10:26:38 where they are it will trigger a pretty
10:26:40 potent negative reaction and the
10:26:42 usability of our side suffers
10:26:45 so what are some tools and techniques
10:26:47 for us web designers to answer the
10:26:49 user's question where am i and prevent
10:26:52 them from feeling lost
10:26:54 in this video i will talk about the
10:26:56 following site identification page
10:26:59 titles you are here indicators
10:27:02 breadcrumbs links back to the homepage
10:27:04 and consistency as an example we'll use
10:27:07 one of the wireframes from the last
10:27:09 video it's a fairly simple page about
10:27:11 the employees of a company it's a
10:27:13 secondary page located in the about
10:27:15 section now by site identification i
10:27:18 mean that the user should be able to
10:27:20 tell at all times simply what site they
10:27:23 are on
10:27:24 and one of the best ways to communicate
10:27:26 this is to have the site's logo or the
10:27:28 site's title in the header on every page
10:27:32 the convention is to have it in the
10:27:33 upper left corner and i would recommend
10:27:35 that location as a starting point for
10:27:37 your design investigations
10:27:40 we'll use the three example websites we
10:27:42 looked at in the last video again here
10:27:44 you can see that there aren't any
10:27:46 surprises the logos of the organizations
10:27:49 are placed safe and securely in the
10:27:51 upper left corner sometimes no surprises
10:27:54 is a good thing it grounds the user's
10:27:56 experience when they know where they are
10:27:58 at all times
10:28:00 another important refining element is
10:28:02 the page title let the user know what
10:28:05 page they are on
10:28:07 here in this wireframe we can see the
10:28:09 page title our team is presented clearly
10:28:12 in large type towards the top of the
10:28:14 page
10:28:15 the mag center website does a good job
10:28:17 of prominently displaying the title of a
10:28:20 page as you can see here on the visit
10:28:22 page the marciano art foundation and
10:28:25 pasadena conservatory websites often use
10:28:28 a different mechanism to let the user
10:28:30 know what page they are on more on that
10:28:32 in a second but here on an upcoming
10:28:35 events page the title of the page stands
10:28:38 out clearly and this blog post here also
10:28:41 displays the title very prominently when
10:28:44 you're looking at a map in a shopping
10:28:46 center let's say one of the most useful
10:28:49 things is to know your current location
10:28:51 in relation to this map you are looking
10:28:54 for the you are here symbol websites can
10:28:57 really benefit from such indicators as
10:28:59 well
10:29:00 in your navigation you should highlight
10:29:02 the link of the section that the visitor
10:29:04 is currently on in this wireframe you
10:29:06 can see that we are currently in the
10:29:08 about section since that link in the
10:29:11 primary navigation is highlighted
10:29:13 furthermore you also see that we are on
10:29:15 the our team page in the secondary menu
10:29:19 since that link is also differently
10:29:21 formatted from the rest
10:29:23 let me quickly emphasize an important
10:29:25 points
