10:29:26 you might argue that a user will have
10:29:28 clicked the about link just a few
10:29:29 moments before so how could they already
10:29:32 have forgotten what section they are in
10:29:35 well let's assume two other scenarios
10:29:37 one they did a google search and landed
10:29:39 on the about page as the first page they
10:29:41 ever visited on your side it's helpful
10:29:43 for them to see right away where they
10:29:45 landed
10:29:46 scenario number two they went to your
10:29:49 site clicked on about and were
10:29:51 distracted by an incoming email or chat
10:29:54 then after half an hour they got back to
10:29:56 their browser and have naturally
10:29:57 forgotten where they were again nice to
10:30:00 help them out by highlighting the place
10:30:02 they are at
10:30:03 here are ways that our example sites
10:30:06 deal with this
10:30:07 by having a background color behind the
10:30:09 active link
10:30:10 or by lowering the opacity of all links
10:30:13 except for the active link
10:30:14 or by having the active link move down
10:30:17 slightly to make it different from the
10:30:18 rest
10:30:20 i can only imagine that the word
10:30:22 breadcrumbs was inspired by the original
10:30:24 grimm's fairy tale of hansel and gretel
10:30:27 in the story when the siblings were left
10:30:29 alone in the woods they tried to find
10:30:30 their way home by looking for the
10:30:32 breadcrumbs they had strewn along their
10:30:34 path earlier that didn't work out so
10:30:37 well for them damn those birds who ate
10:30:39 the bread but breadcrumbs for websites
10:30:41 are a super helpful tool for your users
10:30:44 they indicate the path that the user has
10:30:47 traveled along the information
10:30:48 architecture of your site or if a user
10:30:50 landed on this page some other way brett
10:30:53 comes communicate how this page fits in
10:30:55 with the content hierarchy in this
10:30:58 example the breadcrumb situates the our
10:31:01 team page as part of the about section
10:31:03 which in turn can be accessed from the
10:31:05 home page and a user can click on any of
10:31:08 the links in the trail to get back to a
10:31:10 prior page so far we've only looked at
10:31:13 sites that had a secondary level maybe a
10:31:15 tertiary level but you can probably
10:31:17 imagine content structures that go a lot
10:31:19 deeper the deeper your content structure
10:31:21 the more helpful breadcrumbs become
10:31:25 it's a good idea to give your users an
10:31:27 easy way to get back to the home page
10:31:30 now it's a convention that the logo of
10:31:32 this site will link back home
10:31:35 and on many sites that's the only link
10:31:37 back to the home page
10:31:39 if you want to be more explicit about
10:31:41 how to get back to the home page you
10:31:43 could add that as a link to the primary
10:31:45 navigation but as i said most sites get
10:31:48 away without doing this plus if you have
10:31:50 breadcrumbs you already have another way
10:31:52 pointing back home
10:31:54 finally one overarching strategy of
10:31:57 helping your users to orient themselves
10:31:59 is to be consistent
10:32:01 have your logo on every page of your
10:32:03 site and the same location have your
10:32:05 page title look consistent
10:32:07 use a consistent way of indicating which
10:32:09 link is currently active don't move or
10:32:12 unexpectedly change your primary
10:32:14 navigation from page to page have
10:32:16 breadcrumbs on every page and allow
10:32:19 users to link back home by clicking your
10:32:21 logo on every page
10:32:23 i'll leave you with a little fun
10:32:24 excursion to a website that showcases
10:32:27 the exact opposite of all the things we
10:32:29 just discussed
10:32:31 the site is yvette's bridal it's kind of
10:32:33 a piece of art actually a crazy
10:32:35 wonderland of late 90s inspired web
10:32:38 design but no clear navigational
10:32:40 hierarchy
10:32:42 or no hierarchy whatsoever a user can
10:32:44 all but feel lost here now i'm not
10:32:47 saying that this isn't intriguing or
10:32:49 interesting it does bring a smile to my
10:32:51 face but more in a this is so bad it's
10:32:54 really good sort of way as a piece of
10:32:57 user experience let's say you're
10:32:59 actually trying to buy a wedding gown
10:33:01 it's an utter failure and can make us
10:33:04 appreciate a well-designed usable
10:33:07 website so i want to talk about some
10:33:09 common web design patterns
10:33:11 and this is going to be a free-flowing
10:33:13 survey of some common design elements
10:33:16 that are frequently found on websites
10:33:19 and i include a lot of examples from
10:33:21 existing sites as illustrations but let
10:33:24 me explain what i mean by the word
10:33:25 design pattern
10:33:27 one definition of pattern is a repeated
10:33:29 decorative design that's not what i mean
10:33:31 here i mean the other definition for the
10:33:33 word pattern and that is a design used
10:33:36 as a guide so a design pattern is
10:33:39 basically an agreed upon structure for
10:33:42 certain design tasks so if you look at
10:33:45 this kind of generic wireframe of a site
10:33:47 you see some patterns here
10:33:50 you see there's a header with a you know
10:33:52 logo and some navigation you have a
10:33:54 content area on the right you have a
10:33:56 sidebar
10:33:57 on the left and you have a footer on the
10:33:59 bottom a header content sidebar and
10:34:01 footer you can think about them as
10:34:04 design patterns and i'm gonna talk about
10:34:06 a lot of them in this video so let's
10:34:08 start with the header that's usually
10:34:10 includes a logo a navigation maybe a
10:34:13 search and something we haven't talked
10:34:15 about which are site alerts and i'll
10:34:17 give you an example in a second so let's
10:34:20 look at a few websites here is the site
10:34:23 for the institute of domestic technology
10:34:26 and the header here includes many of the
10:34:28 elements i just mentioned there's a logo
10:34:31 the navigation right here a utility
10:34:33 navigation on the top right and a search
10:34:36 that when clicked opens up the search
10:34:39 bar what's interesting about this site
10:34:42 is that when you look at it on a mobile
10:34:44 device you actually don't get the
10:34:46 pattern again a pattern of a hamburger
10:34:50 right that we see on a lot of other
10:34:51 sides a hamburger basically
10:34:54 three lines standing in for a menu
10:34:56 button that then opens the menu on this
10:34:59 side the menu is so
10:35:01 small that we didn't need to hide the
10:35:03 navigation on small screens because it
10:35:05 always just fits that is usually not
10:35:07 true for other sites but this one is a
10:35:08 fairly small side
10:35:10 another example here is a site for an
10:35:13 organization called cell dogs
10:35:15 and what we have here is drop downs an
10:35:18 easy way to get to secondary pages
10:35:20 straight from the home page if you look
10:35:23 at this in the mobile device we have the
10:35:25 mobile menu it's not a hamburger it's
10:35:27 not three lines it's the word menu you
10:35:30 click it you get a list
10:35:33 of all the links kind of just in one row
10:35:36 this example is from the
10:35:38 ucla lab school
10:35:40 and what's interesting here is that the
10:35:42 menu when you first get to the home page
10:35:44 obviously there's a lot of fun
10:35:46 animations going on in the top half of
10:35:48 the screen but the menu is on the bottom
10:35:50 when you first get there and it also has
10:35:53 a drop down menu but since the menu is
10:35:55 on the bottom it's kind of a drop up
10:35:58 menu in a way so the secondary links go
10:36:00 to the top but if you scroll down the
10:36:03 drop down menus function in a more
10:36:05 normal way the other thing that happens
10:36:07 here is if you scroll further down the
10:36:09 page
10:36:10 the navigation the entire header
10:36:13 is sticky so that's a design pattern
10:36:15 that lets the user access the navigation
10:36:17 at all times no matter how long they've
10:36:19 scrolled down
10:36:21 on you know many sites you scroll down
10:36:23 you lose the navigation you have to
10:36:24 scroll back up if you want to go
10:36:26 somewhere else on this side we decided
10:36:29 to keep them around so again a pro for a
10:36:32 sticky navigation menu is that the links
10:36:34 will always be around and accessible the
10:36:37 reason not to do it might be that it's a
10:36:39 little distracting if you want a user to
10:36:42 focus solely on the content of each page
10:36:44 it also takes a little strip away from
10:36:46 the screen real estate right the browser
10:36:48 window is not as tall as it could be for
10:36:51 the content of your site uh it kind of
10:36:53 depends on the project what pattern to
10:36:55 implement another site we've been
10:36:56 looking at a lot the pasadena
10:36:59 conservatory of music here we
10:37:00 implemented something that at the time
10:37:02 we coined
10:37:03 fad menus
10:37:05 so if you click on a main menu you get
10:37:08 this giant drop down with some text
10:37:11 images and links at the time we kind of
10:37:14 like the energy that is created here and
10:37:16 that you have a lot of space to present
10:37:18 a lot of information in these dropdowns
10:37:20 what's interesting though is that just
10:37:22 in the last few weeks we worked with the
10:37:24 client to overhaul the website and the
10:37:27 website that's up currently as we speak
10:37:30 actually lost these fat menus and now we
10:37:33 have much more regular drop down menus
10:37:37 and they also it was part of an overhaul
10:37:39 that also changed the main level
10:37:40 navigation quite a bit
10:37:43 and you know that had been a few years
10:37:45 the needs of the clients change so we
10:37:47 redesigned this site and the fat menus
10:37:50 are no longer here let's look at one
10:37:52 more example of a header
10:37:54 this website we designed for an
10:37:56 environmental organization in los
10:37:57 angeles
10:37:58 and it's somewhat of a publication they
10:38:01 wrote a climate action framework and
10:38:03 this website presents this writing and
10:38:06 if you scroll down you get an
10:38:07 introduction to the content in the top
10:38:09 right corner you have a hamburger menu
10:38:12 so basically what happens here is that
10:38:14 the main navigation is always hidden
10:38:17 behind a menu most of the time this only
10:38:19 happens on mobile devices when there
10:38:21 isn't enough space to display the
10:38:23 navigation but here we decided that we
10:38:25 didn't want to overwhelm the viewer
10:38:27 right away so they have to do this extra
10:38:29 click usually i'm not a big fan to make
10:38:32 the user have to click on a menu link to
10:38:35 see any kind of navigation
10:38:37 but here on this side in this particular
10:38:40 instance it seemed appropriate
10:38:42 let's talk quickly about a site alert
10:38:45 that's a design pattern for breaking
10:38:47 news or something very important and
10:38:50 it's usually a strip that appears right
10:38:52 above the header
10:38:54 here is the pasadena conservatory of
10:38:56 music website and something that they
10:38:58 had at the end of the year to ask people
10:39:02 to
10:39:03 donate for their end of the year giving
10:39:06 and this will pop up on every single
10:39:07 page of the entire site until you close
10:39:11 it the user has the option to either
10:39:13 click on the button
10:39:14 or to close it and once you close it it
10:39:17 will not show on other pages so again a
10:39:20 site alert something every user
10:39:23 should see when they get to any page of
10:39:26 the site
10:39:27 now let's move on to the footer we
10:39:29 already talked about it a little bit
10:39:31 it's the area on the bottom of all pages
10:39:33 and it's a great place to include a
10:39:35 copyright statement repeat some
10:39:37 navigation links sometimes an entire
10:39:39 site map is included here you know you
10:39:42 can repeat the logo and also display the
10:39:45 social media links let's look at a few
10:39:47 examples here's the marciano art
10:39:49 foundation website if we scroll all the
10:39:52 way to the bottom
10:39:54 you'll see the
10:39:55 navigation from the header is basically
10:39:58 repeated with a couple of extra items
10:40:01 including the social media links the
10:40:03 address terms and conditions credits and
10:40:06 a copyright line here's the site for the
10:40:09 root simple blog and what's interesting
10:40:11 is that the footer here includes
10:40:13 information that can only be found in
10:40:15 the footer so about us press resources
10:40:18 and so on those links are only in the
10:40:20 footer they don't even appear in the
10:40:22 header
10:40:23 mainly because this is a blog right this
10:40:26 is about the blog posts primarily so the
10:40:29 navigation and the header just relates
10:40:31 to that
10:40:32 any kind of secondary information is
10:40:35 stuck into the footer including a little
10:40:37 blurb about what road simple is in the
10:40:39 first place
10:40:41 if we look at amazon we see a giant
10:40:44 footer and something that is often
10:40:46 called a fat footer right it takes up
10:40:49 basically an entire screen
10:40:51 and just has a bunch of links to areas
10:40:54 in the website so when you get to the
10:40:56 bottom of
10:40:57 the page and you haven't found what
10:40:59 you're looking for maybe these links
10:41:01 will help you
10:41:02 i want to show something from my
10:41:04 studio's website where we use the footer
10:41:07 to
10:41:08 create a bit of a
10:41:10 fun
10:41:11 experience and an easter egg for the
10:41:13 viewer
10:41:14 you can see that there is a cat that
10:41:16 eventually starts walking
10:41:18 across the screen on the very bottom
10:41:21 followed by a mouse and when the cat
10:41:24 passes the
10:41:25 copyright symbol it skews it a little
10:41:28 bit and when the mouse passes by on the
10:41:30 return trip it brightens it so we added
10:41:33 this as a little joyful moment and some
10:41:36 clients see it others don't but you know
10:41:38 for those who see it they hopefully are
10:41:41 a little bit delighted and it shows you
10:41:42 how you can use the bottom of the page
10:41:45 to create a bit of a surprise which
10:41:47 reminds me on the marciano website on
10:41:49 the bottom too you have this little bit
10:41:52 of a sliver that peaks to a background
10:41:55 image and if you click on it you
10:41:57 actually see
10:41:58 a big image that is hiding in the
10:42:00 background which is an alternate view of
10:42:02 the inside of the museum again it's a
10:42:05 little easter egg that we built into
10:42:07 this side
10:42:08 those who find it i hope get joy from it
10:42:11 but if you don't find it then you know
10:42:12 you're not missing something important
10:42:14 either
10:42:16 so onto the sidebar that's usually an
10:42:19 area on the left or the right side of
10:42:21 the screen that has some secondary
10:42:23 content like the navigation
10:42:25 or ads will be displayed there sidebars
10:42:28 used to be more popular and i'm
10:42:30 wondering if the rise of
10:42:32 responsive design
10:42:34 and the fact that on mobile phones you
10:42:37 don't have a sidebar because there's
10:42:39 just not enough space to have something
10:42:41 next to the content so a lot of
10:42:43 designers got used to designing on
10:42:45 mobile without sidebars and that may be
10:42:47 trickled back into the desktop
10:42:49 experience but let's look at a few
10:42:51 examples of sidebars here back at the
10:42:54 root simple website we have a sidebar
10:42:56 that has an introduction to root simple
10:42:59 links to social media books that the
10:43:02 authors have written and then eventually
10:43:04 there's a space for advertisements that
10:43:07 can be displayed here
10:43:08 and here is an example from amazon
10:43:12 i searched for something and then on the
10:43:14 left side you could call that a sidebar
10:43:17 and it basically has somewhat of a
10:43:18 secondary navigation
10:43:20 and a way to refine your search results
10:43:22 and to add some filters so that is all
10:43:26 included in a sidebar
10:43:28 and then another example from the
10:43:30 washington post i'm looking at a
10:43:32 specific article here
10:43:34 and in the sidebar on the right you can
10:43:37 see that we are presented with suggested
10:43:39 articles the most read stories in the
10:43:41 lifestyle section
10:43:43 a little ad to entice us to become a
10:43:46 paying member and then something that i
10:43:48 find quite interesting things that you
10:43:50 would usually find in the footer like
10:43:52 the copyright symbol a couple of very
10:43:54 secondary
10:43:56 menus like help and contact us policies
10:43:58 and terms of service etc that is all in
10:44:02 the sidebar here and it kind of makes
10:44:04 sense because the pages on the
10:44:06 washington post are quite long so if
10:44:09 someone is looking for that information
10:44:11 that you usually would find in a footer
10:44:14 it's kind of cool to put it into the
10:44:15 sidebar so a user gets to it quite
10:44:18 easily
10:44:19 um one thing we should also point out
10:44:22 here if you look on the left there's a
10:44:24 sticky sidebar that provides all kinds
10:44:27 of social media sharing options so that
10:44:30 is displayed at all times so if a user
10:44:33 feels that after reading or while
10:44:35 reading a story they really need to
10:44:37 share this with their friends they can
10:44:38 do that at all times so let's move on to
10:44:41 the content section of a webpage and the
10:44:44 design patterns i want to talk about
10:44:46 here are the hero slider accordions tabs
10:44:49 and something that i call 25 50 25 but
10:44:53 let's start with the hero slider and
10:44:54 that is simply a big slideshow that is
10:44:57 often included on the homepage or on the
10:44:59 top of a page
10:45:00 that provides a graphical overview
10:45:03 either of the site or the page and it
10:45:06 usually includes images sometimes it
10:45:08 includes text and sometimes includes
10:45:10 quite a bit of animation so let's look
10:45:12 at a few examples on the cell dogs
10:45:14 website you have a few images that are
10:45:16 cycled through automatically so what
10:45:18 happens is there's a new image comes in
10:45:21 it kind of dims with the gradient and
10:45:22 then some text is overlaid explaining to
10:45:26 us the story of this organization
10:45:29 oftentimes for slideshow there's this
10:45:31 design pattern that shows us how many
10:45:33 slides there are usually it's circles
10:45:36 and you can see here that there is four
10:45:38 circles one is filled at a time which
10:45:41 indicates the current slide and it kind
10:45:43 of cycles through npc which one is
10:45:47 currently active and we can even click
10:45:49 on them
10:45:50 and get to a particular slide okay and
10:45:53 back to the ucla lab school that very
10:45:57 involved animation on the top of the
10:45:59 home page you can also call a hero
10:46:02 slider somewhat of a slideshow and
10:46:04 obviously there's a lot more going on
10:46:05 text slides and elements build
10:46:08 elements flowed in and animates
10:46:12 and
10:46:13 basically what happens here though is
10:46:14 that the story of the ucla lab school is
10:46:18 being told you know what are they about
10:46:21 what makes them
10:46:22 special and unique and it's
10:46:24 all told in this very graphic
10:46:28 engaging
10:46:29 animation
10:46:30 the pasadena conservatory of music also
10:46:32 has a slideshow on the top of their
10:46:34 homepage and again this is pretty much a
10:46:36 pallet cleanser it basically introduces
10:46:39 us to the site graphically and we see
10:46:41 musicians in their teens older folks
10:46:44 playing music together young children
10:46:46 playing music and everything is very
10:46:47 colorful and there are circles around
10:46:50 and it kind of
10:46:51 sets the tone for the rest of the sides
10:46:55 onto accordions that's a design pattern
10:46:58 that as the name suggests lets the user
10:47:01 open and close certain elements on your
10:47:05 page and what it's really great for is
10:47:08 when you have a lot of content and you
10:47:09 want to first present an overview of all
10:47:12 the content
10:47:13 so when the accordions are closed you
10:47:16 see maybe the headline for that section
10:47:19 and the user can then decide to open
10:47:21 that section if they are interesting but
10:47:23 they see an overview of all the
10:47:25 headlines first instead of a giant long
10:47:28 document so let's look at a few examples
10:47:30 here
10:47:31 on the guitar page for example you get
10:47:33 to a section where it describes the
10:47:36 programs for the guitar departments and
10:47:39 there are four separate programs and you
10:47:41 can see the name of each program here
10:47:43 and on the right is an error indicating
10:47:45 that there's more information and if i
10:47:47 click on a section
10:47:48 it opens up and shows more information i
10:47:51 can open all of the sections
10:47:54 and they can also be closed but compare
10:47:57 when they are all open what the user
10:47:59 experience is now instead of seeing all
10:48:02 four programs i only see one right and i
10:48:04 have to scroll down to get an overview
10:48:07 it's much nicer to first see all four
10:48:10 programs at once and then being able to
10:48:14 choose the one that i am interested in
10:48:17 another example is on the wikipedia side
10:48:20 but here it's actually the mobile site
10:48:23 that employs the accordion on the
10:48:24 desktop side you see all the information
10:48:26 is listed normally but if we switch to
10:48:29 the mobile view
10:48:30 because you have such a smaller viewport
10:48:33 and scrolling down through a long
10:48:35 article would take a long time
10:48:38 after an introduction you actually have
10:48:40 this accordion pattern where you see the
10:48:43 headlines of each section
10:48:45 and the section is collapsed but if i
10:48:48 click or tap on it it opens up and lets
10:48:51 me read it so you basically see the
10:48:52 document outline for this article first
10:48:55 and are then able to click on the
10:48:58 section that you want to read about more
10:49:00 a little similar to accordions or tabs
10:49:04 they're also a good design pattern to
10:49:06 handle a lot of information where you
10:49:08 need to show user all the information
10:49:11 that's available without actually
10:49:12 showing at all because there isn't
10:49:14 enough space and it takes a cue from the
10:49:16 physical world from file folders right
10:49:18 that have little tabs on the side as you
10:49:20 can file documents into them that idea
10:49:23 is carried out graphically so you have
10:49:25 tabs going across and it should be very
10:49:27 obvious which one is the current one and
10:49:30 the user can click on another tab to
10:49:32 select it and then the information of
10:49:34 that tab is being shown as an example we
10:49:37 can look at the mac center website where
10:49:39 the secondary navigation is actually
10:49:41 presented in a tapped pattern here we
10:49:44 are in the site section and there are
10:49:46 four sub-sections overview schindler
10:49:48 house mackie apartments and so on and
10:49:51 those are presented as tabs so if i
10:49:53 click on a subsection
10:49:55 that's tab becomes active and shows us
10:49:58 the content of that section here's
10:50:01 another example from a site called
10:50:03 moo.com they offer printing services
10:50:07 and
10:50:08 i'm in
10:50:09 the flyer section
10:50:11 and there are tabs going across here so
10:50:13 i can look at the pricing and the design
10:50:16 guidelines and i can look at small
10:50:18 flyers square flyers long flyers and so
10:50:21 on the tab that i click on gets
10:50:23 highlighted and the content underneath
10:50:26 changes again it's a way to design a lot
10:50:29 of information in a way that it is very
10:50:32 accessible for a user
10:50:34 and lastly here is a design pattern that
10:50:36 i call
10:50:37 25 50 25 and this refers to a way of
10:50:41 splitting up your content area into
10:50:44 percentages
10:50:46 and i've seen this done on a lot of
10:50:48 sites especially sites that have a lot
10:50:50 of text content and i find it very
10:50:52 interesting so i want to talk about a
10:50:54 little bit so as you can see here you
10:50:56 have basically three columns one is 25
10:50:59 one's 50 and another one is 25
10:51:03 and that allows you to do quite a bit of
10:51:05 interesting
10:51:06 layout decisions for content for images
10:51:09 text captions pull quotes etc as you can
10:51:12 see here right some images are in the on
10:51:16 the side some images are in the middle
10:51:19 there's text that goes in the middle
10:51:21 column but then there are smaller bits
10:51:23 of text that go on either side and the
10:51:26 image on the bottom is actually
10:51:28 spanning one column and the center
10:51:31 column the way this actually works in
10:51:34 the wild let's look at a
10:51:36 really nicely designed block called the
10:51:38 great discontent they publish a lot of
10:51:40 interviews with creative people
10:51:42 and here is an example and as you can
10:51:45 see it starts with a big giant image of
10:51:48 the interview subjects then you have a
10:51:50 headline and some introduction but then
10:51:52 when you get to this lighter area this
10:51:54 25 50 25 pattern as i call it
10:51:59 really emerges you have three columns
10:52:01 the center column is bigger about 50
10:52:04 percent and then you have a column on
10:52:05 the left and the right
10:52:07 and the main text goes down the center
10:52:10 and the right and the left is taking up
10:52:13 with secondary information so a pull
10:52:16 quote or a little ad for a printed
10:52:19 magazine
10:52:20 and as we scroll down you see that one
10:52:23 image here spans the left column and the
10:52:25 center column and the right column is
10:52:27 filled up with a caption
10:52:29 then there is a giant pull quote that
10:52:31 spans all three columns
10:52:33 then it goes back into just the middle
10:52:36 column being used for the main text
10:52:38 another pull quote and so on what i like
10:52:41 about this is that it provides a nice
10:52:43 structure to deal with a lot of text and
10:52:46 images in a way that still ends up being
10:52:49 surprising and interesting for the
10:52:52 reader you can create a lot of hierarchy
10:52:54 with just these three columns and a lot
10:52:56 of different layouts
10:52:58 if you want to learn a lot more about
10:53:00 design patterns and discover even more
10:53:03 patterns i've only touched on a few here
10:53:05 i would point you to the side
10:53:07 uipatterns.com
10:53:09 the website is
10:53:11 ui-patterns.com and there's a section
10:53:13 for design patterns and it has a lot of
10:53:16 them some of them that i've covered like
10:53:18 tabs and menus and slideshows but
10:53:21 there's a lot more here to look at so i
10:53:24 invite you to go to this website and do
10:53:26 a little bit of further reading
10:53:29 i want to talk a little bit about common
10:53:31 form elements that you might find on a
10:53:33 website forms are obviously a way to
10:53:36 gather user input and let's take a look
10:53:39 at what kind of form
10:53:41 fields are possible
10:53:44 we'll start with check boxes and radio
10:53:46 buttons
10:53:47 these design patterns are really great
10:53:49 when you want the user to select
10:53:50 something from a list of predefined
10:53:52 options and checkboxes enable the user
10:53:55 to select multiple options from a list
10:53:57 it's basically an and selection so like
10:54:00 this and this and this so in this
10:54:02 example apples and oranges are selected
10:54:06 and for radio buttons you can only
10:54:08 select one option so it's an or
10:54:10 selection so like this or this or this
10:54:14 in the example apples is selected and if
10:54:17 the user were to click on oranges apples
10:54:20 would be unselected and oranges would be
10:54:22 selected and the default visual cue for
10:54:26 separating these two patterns is that
10:54:27 checkboxes are rectangular
10:54:30 and radio buttons are
10:54:32 round that doesn't have to be like this
10:54:35 in the final ui but if you have html for
10:54:38 example without any styling attached
10:54:41 then it would look very similar to these
10:54:43 wireframe examples now drop down list is
10:54:47 another design pattern for a form and
10:54:49 it's quite similar to radio buttons it
10:54:52 also allows you to choose one option
10:54:55 from a list but the options are
10:54:56 presented in a more compact space this
10:54:59 is actually quite similar to an
10:55:01 accordion you kind of see a headline in
10:55:03 this instance select fruit and you don't
10:55:06 see anything else but when you click on
10:55:07 it you see the options and you can make
10:55:10 a selection so drop-down lists are
10:55:11 really great for instances where you
10:55:13 have lots and lots of options
10:55:16 let's compare them real quick to radio
10:55:17 buttons right which one should you
10:55:19 choose if you have a list of options and
10:55:21 you want the user to select one of them
10:55:23 well radio buttons have the advantage
10:55:25 that the user is able to see all the
10:55:27 options at once without having to do
10:55:29 anything you know they don't have to do
10:55:30 an extra click the options are just
10:55:32 presented and the user can make a
10:55:35 decision
10:55:36 but the advantage of a drop down list is
10:55:39 that if you have a lot of options you
10:55:40 save some screen real estate and you
10:55:42 make things less busy so imagine asking
10:55:45 the user to select the state that they
10:55:48 live in
10:55:48 well a drop down list is pretty great
10:55:51 here because the answer is fairly simple
10:55:54 right i know which state to look for
10:55:56 it's not a difficult decision for me and
10:55:58 there are so many states there's 50 in
10:56:00 the united states so drop down this is a
10:56:02 more appropriate design pattern to use
10:56:05 next up text fields and those are pretty
10:56:07 straightforward it allows the user to
10:56:09 type in something into a field
10:56:12 for
10:56:13 free form answers and
10:56:16 they come in two flavors though there's
10:56:17 a single line field that allows the user
10:56:19 to type in a short amount of information
10:56:22 but then sometimes you want the user to
10:56:25 type quite a bit there is something
10:56:26 called a text area that allows for
10:56:29 multi-line
10:56:31 text inputs
10:56:32 one question you might ask yourself when
10:56:34 you're designing a form is what you're
10:56:36 going to do with the data
10:56:38 how are you going to process it text
10:56:41 fields are great if you don't know what
10:56:43 kind of answer the user might give
10:56:46 you know they can type whatever but if
10:56:48 you want to use the data in a more
10:56:50 structured way so you know in our fruit
10:56:53 example if we want to build a graph and
10:56:56 see which fruit is the most popular it's
10:56:58 actually easier if you have a predefined
10:57:00 list because you can process the data
10:57:03 much more easily because when people
10:57:05 type data into a text field you don't
10:57:06 know what you're gonna get you don't
10:57:07 even know in this instance if they're
10:57:09 going to write in a fruit or just
10:57:11 something random so that's something to
10:57:14 keep in mind next up we have a number
10:57:16 stepper that's pretty straightforward
10:57:18 it's a user interface element that
10:57:21 allows people to choose a whole number
10:57:23 you can have minimum and maximum numbers
10:57:27 so you could say well you know the
10:57:28 minimum is two the maximum is nine and
10:57:30 then people wouldn't be able to click
10:57:32 above and below that number and then we
10:57:35 have a slider which can be somewhat
10:57:37 similar to a number stepper but it
10:57:40 doesn't have to be numbers a slider is
10:57:42 also really great for information where
10:57:45 you want to select between two extremes
10:57:48 like speed and sour and the user can
10:57:50 indicate well what do they like do they
10:57:52 like it all the way to one side all the
10:57:54 way to the other or somewhere in the
10:57:56 middle
10:57:57 a date picker enables users to select
10:58:00 the date in the context of a calendar
10:58:03 right if you need a date to be input by
10:58:05 the user let's say they want to indicate
10:58:07 what day they want to fly somewhere or
10:58:09 what day they want to rent a vacation
10:58:10 rental first thing they need to do is
10:58:13 select the date that is oftentimes
10:58:14 easier when you have a calendar in front
10:58:16 of you you can ask people to just type
10:58:19 in numbers but the date of validation
10:58:21 also gets a little tricky right do you
10:58:22 type in the month first or the date
10:58:25 first that depends which country you're
10:58:26 in what if people type in the date that
10:58:28 doesn't exist like november 31st for
10:58:31 example so it's nice when people can
10:58:33 just click on the calendar icon and see
10:58:35 an entire calendar and you have the days
10:58:37 of the week and you can cycle through
10:58:39 each month and it's much easier to pick
10:58:41 a date and data validation is kind of
10:58:43 taken care
10:58:44 for you so once they select the date
10:58:46 that gets filled in
10:58:48 to the text box
10:58:50 on off buttons are really great for
10:58:52 binary choices so you'll see those a lot
10:58:54 in menus where people set options you
10:58:57 know do you want to receive emails from
10:58:59 us for example and there would be a
10:59:00 choice of yes or no on and off and a
10:59:03 little toggle switch is a great design
10:59:05 pattern for that
10:59:07 and then lastly we have buttons so those
10:59:10 can be clicked on and they trigger an
10:59:12 action
10:59:12 after some information has been provided
10:59:14 via other form elements it's often the
10:59:17 kind of submit this form buttons i am
10:59:20 done or go to the next screen they can
10:59:23 be different shapes they can have icons
10:59:25 to make them a little bit more clear or
10:59:27 they can even have arrows in the example
10:59:29 of getting to the next screen or going
10:59:31 to the previous screen so those are a
10:59:34 few common form elements of course there
10:59:36 are more advanced elements or hybrids
10:59:39 that you will find across websites that
10:59:43 get quite a bit more complex
10:59:45 so i just wanted to show one
10:59:48 form element that is a little bit more
10:59:50 complex from a project that i have
10:59:51 worked on a few years ago i created a
10:59:54 website that lists every single student
10:59:55 that has studied at the calarts design
10:59:57 program
10:59:59 and the website has a big search field
11:00:02 so that's basically a text input field
11:00:05 but what happens if you click and start
11:00:07 typing
11:00:08 you get a list of suggestions so that
11:00:11 marries a
11:00:13 text field with a drop down menu and
11:00:16 once you see the name that you're
11:00:17 looking for you can just select it so
11:00:19 it's a lot easier for the user once you
11:00:21 select the name the name actually
11:00:23 becomes part of that search field so
11:00:25 it's almost like a checkbox pattern this
11:00:28 name is now checked i can remove it by
11:00:30 clicking the x or i can add a new name
11:00:34 but you can see it seems like it's just
11:00:36 a text box but then it does all these
11:00:39 other more advanced and complex things
11:00:42 that are a hybrid of other form elements
11:00:45 let's talk a little bit about home pages
11:00:47 a homepage is often the very first page
11:00:49 that your users will land on as they
11:00:51 visit your website
11:00:53 so it's a pretty crucial page
11:00:55 and it makes sense to spend a little bit
11:00:57 of time asking ourselves what is the job
11:01:00 of the home page how can the home page
11:01:02 help you achieve your goals and what
11:01:05 should you include here in terms of
11:01:07 contents
11:01:08 here are a few things that in my opinion
11:01:10 a homepage needs to deliver on
11:01:13 first it should communicate the voice or
11:01:15 the brand of your website that is mostly
11:01:18 achieved with visual design so with
11:01:20 colors and images and textures etc
11:01:24 we'll have to wait until next week when
11:01:26 we start the visual design phase to get
11:01:28 to that
11:01:29 but remember that language also plays a
11:01:31 big part of communicating a brand
11:01:34 and you can set the tone right on the
11:01:36 homepage is your language friendly or
11:01:39 snotty or reserved or authoritative or
11:01:42 funny or irreverent do you use a lot of
11:01:45 words or only a few these are important
11:01:48 questions to ponder during the
11:01:49 wireframing phase
11:01:51 second the homepage should provide
11:01:52 navigation options that take your users
11:01:55 to the content of your sites the
11:01:57 homepage is also a great place to
11:01:58 introduce your products or your mission
11:02:01 to people that are visiting for the
11:02:02 first time they might be completely
11:02:05 unfamiliar with your site so ask
11:02:06 yourself what do they need to know right
11:02:09 away sometimes you only have a few
11:02:11 seconds to convince a user to stay and
11:02:13 explore further your homepage should
11:02:16 answer the basic questions of what is
11:02:18 this what can i do here where do i start
11:02:22 and lastly you can use the home page to
11:02:24 show some real content that might be the
11:02:26 latest blog post or a featured product
11:02:29 or an upcoming event or a video
11:02:32 explaining your service a download
11:02:34 button or a signup form something that
11:02:36 provides value to your users right away
11:02:40 okay so let's talk about what elements
11:02:42 should be included on a home page
11:02:45 i would argue that for the vast majority
11:02:47 of sites you will definitely want to
11:02:49 include a logo or at least the title or
11:02:52 name of the website remember we talked
11:02:54 about the user's question of where am i
11:02:57 as a fundamental concern in the
11:02:59 wayfinding lesson you'll also want to
11:03:01 include some sort of navigation both of
11:03:03 these items usually end up in the header
11:03:05 of the page
11:03:07 then there are things that most sites
11:03:08 will include on the home page like a
11:03:10 tagline that's a short and precise
11:03:13 sentence that summarizes the mission of
11:03:15 the website your call to action is also
11:03:18 often appropriate what is the most
11:03:20 important thing you want your users to
11:03:22 do
11:03:22 pick up the phone and call you well then
11:03:25 provide a phone number right on your
11:03:26 home page
11:03:27 sign up for a free trial then have a
11:03:30 sign up form or do you want your users
11:03:32 to find out more about your featured
11:03:34 product then display a learn more button
11:03:37 most sites will also have a footer with
11:03:38 secondary information such as address
11:03:41 additional links and copyright and
11:03:43 privacy statements
11:03:45 social media links are also often
11:03:46 included and most likely will find a
11:03:49 place in the header or at the footer and
11:03:51 then there are a few elements that are
11:03:53 maybes a hero image for example that's a
11:03:56 term for a big often dramatic image that
11:03:59 sets the mood for your site and shows
11:04:01 off its products or services sometimes
11:04:04 there are several hero images that are
11:04:06 displayed in a slideshow some sites have
11:04:09 a search function and as i already
11:04:11 mentioned it's often helpful to show
11:04:13 some actual content right away
11:04:15 let's check in with the apple site for
11:04:17 an example
11:04:19 here we can see most of the elements i
11:04:21 just talked about being employed right
11:04:23 on the home page
11:04:25 the logo the navigation
11:04:27 a search function
11:04:29 a featured product with a hero image and
11:04:31 a tagline
11:04:32 and very prominent calls to action learn
11:04:35 more or buy
11:04:37 there are a couple more featured
11:04:38 products as you scroll down and then
11:04:40 there's quite an extensive footer with
11:04:42 additional links phone numbers and legal
11:04:45 information the one thing that i can't
11:04:47 find on this page are any links to
11:04:49 social media
11:04:51 okay so let's look at a lot of other
11:04:53 homepage examples since we've already
11:04:55 talked about navigation and footers and
11:04:57 other lessons i want to specifically
11:05:00 focus here on the content area of the
11:05:02 home page
11:05:03 what goes here i have found in my own
11:05:05 work that sometimes it's fairly easy to
11:05:08 figure out what content should be
11:05:09 included on the homepage but at other
11:05:12 times it can almost feel that a site
11:05:14 doesn't even need a home page at all
11:05:16 so let's talk about specific strategies
11:05:18 you can employ looking around the web i
11:05:21 have discovered that there are certain
11:05:22 distinct approaches that can be
11:05:24 categorized as follows
11:05:26 table of contents featured contents
11:05:29 palette cleanser call to action
11:05:32 narrative fork in the roads and fusion
11:05:35 by the way these labels aren't an
11:05:37 official agreed-upon list or anything i
11:05:40 simply made them up although i think
11:05:41 they are pretty descriptive and it's
11:05:43 entirely possible that there are
11:05:45 additional categories in fact i invite
11:05:48 you to think about other strategies that
11:05:50 you would add here
11:05:52 let's talk about each item in this list
11:05:54 while looking at actual websites as
11:05:56 examples
11:05:57 some of these websites that i'll show
11:05:59 are sites that i have worked on myself
11:06:02 so table of contents this is a strategy
11:06:05 where the home page consists of a list
11:06:07 of links that point to the content
11:06:09 contained in the sites
11:06:11 it's a bit like a table of contents in a
11:06:13 printed magazine or book
11:06:15 let's take a look at the pioneering
11:06:17 women of american architecture side for
11:06:19 an example
11:06:20 this website is mainly a collection of
11:06:22 two dozen essays about women architects
11:06:25 that made an important contribution to
11:06:27 the field of architecture the homepage
11:06:28 simply lists the names of all women when
11:06:31 you click on a name you get a detail
11:06:33 page for the architects
11:06:35 you can also look at this list sorted
11:06:37 chronologically based on the woman's
11:06:39 life spans
11:06:40 and there's a pictorial view where the
11:06:42 list is represented by an image but
11:06:45 again this home page acts like a table
11:06:47 of contents you see a list of everything
11:06:49 that's contained in the side and you can
11:06:51 make a selection
11:06:53 the marciano art foundation website
11:06:55 doesn't actually list all content on the
11:06:57 home page but it introduces important
11:07:00 sections and acts as a jumping off point
11:07:02 to the content
11:07:04 a user basically has two ways to
11:07:05 navigate they can click on something in
11:07:08 the main navigation right away or they
11:07:10 can scroll down and get an overview of
11:07:12 the site's contents which path a user
11:07:14 chooses probably depends on how
11:07:16 experienced they are with the museum or
11:07:19 if they came to the side with a very
11:07:20 specific question people coming here for
11:07:23 the first time might want to get a
11:07:24 little bit of context about the museum
11:07:26 first
11:07:27 these users can scroll down and get an
11:07:29 overview of the most important things
11:07:32 what's on view what are the upcoming
11:07:34 events
11:07:35 what is the marciano art foundation in
11:07:36 the first place on the other hand
11:07:38 someone coming to the site wanting to
11:07:40 know the opening times of the bookstore
11:07:42 well they will probably click on
11:07:44 bookstore in the navigation and get
11:07:45 straight to the content they need here's
11:07:48 another good example the irs site
11:07:51 and for full disclosure this is not a
11:07:53 site that i had any hand in designing
11:07:55 but we can see here the table of content
11:07:57 strategy really well the most important
11:08:00 common user scenarios and tasks are
11:08:02 addressed right on the home page if i'm
11:08:04 looking to find out about my refund
11:08:06 status well there's a big link right
11:08:08 here and if i want to apply for an ein
11:08:11 well there it is
11:08:13 same thing if i'm looking for a specific
11:08:15 form
11:08:16 i would imagine that the irs uses actual
11:08:19 analytics data in deciding which topics
11:08:21 will appear on the home page
11:08:23 and these might change seasonally right
11:08:26 before april 15th when taxes are due in
11:08:28 the u.s the site might draw special
11:08:30 attention to information about filing
11:08:32 taxes right after april 15th maybe
11:08:35 there's a link about filing for an
11:08:36 extension for any stragglers who didn't
11:08:39 file on time
11:08:41 i'll show you one more website that
11:08:42 utilizes a table of content strategy but
11:08:45 not in a very imaginative way
11:08:47 alas it's one of the first sites i ever
11:08:50 designed and it's still up on the
11:08:52 internet
11:08:53 it's a tribute site for the painter
11:08:54 edward monk
11:08:56 and besides an obvious late 90s inspired
11:08:59 web design you can see here that i
11:09:01 didn't really know what to do with the
11:09:02 home page i mean there's a photograph of
11:09:04 the artist and one of his paintings but
11:09:07 then i simply added the main navigation
11:09:09 as a free flowing list of links granted
11:09:12 the hover effects are kind of fun but i
11:09:14 now realize that what doesn't work so
11:09:16 well is the fact that the main
11:09:18 navigation isn't introduced in the same
11:09:20 way it will be displayed on the
11:09:21 secondary pages
11:09:23 there's a disconnect between the home
11:09:25 page and the other pages in terms of
11:09:27 navigation layouts and it creates some
11:09:29 disorientation for the user
11:09:32 let's move on to the next strategy
11:09:34 featured content
11:09:36 here the homepage is used to display
11:09:38 some actual content right away this is
11:09:41 great for e-commerce sites and blogs
11:09:43 where you'll want to showcase either
11:09:44 featured content or the latest contents
11:09:48 so for a site like the washington post
11:09:50 and news site it makes absolute sense to
11:09:53 show the very latest and most important
11:09:55 news stories right on the home page it's
11:09:58 pretty similar to how a traditional
11:09:59 newspaper front page works
11:10:02 on amazon's home page you see some
11:10:04 featured products and product categories
11:10:07 and you'll see what's most popular or if
11:10:09 you happen to be logged in to your
11:10:11 account you get specific recommendations
11:10:14 just for you based on previous searches
11:10:16 and purchases
11:10:18 here's the blog root simple that i
11:10:19 designed a few years ago
11:10:21 and as expected the latest blog post
11:10:24 shows up first so that readers can get
11:10:26 to it right on the home page
11:10:28 and after that post they get the second
11:10:30 latest post and so on
11:10:33 lastly here's the site for the callard's
11:10:35 poster archive that my studio developed
11:10:37 in collaboration with michael
11:10:38 worthington and a few grad students here
11:10:41 the homepage gets to the point right
11:10:43 away there's a collection of posters in
11:10:45 an orderly grid
11:10:47 the user can filter this list by year or
11:10:49 subject matter and they can search for
11:10:51 something specific and of course they
11:10:53 can click on a poster for more
11:10:55 information and a bigger image
11:10:57 but much of the action happens right on
11:11:00 the home page
11:11:01 on to the next strategy
11:11:03 the palette cleanser
11:11:05 here the home page is used to set a tone
11:11:08 or a mood
11:11:09 it provides the user a moment of pause
11:11:11 before delving into the content of the
11:11:14 sites it's a little bit like the cover
11:11:16 of a book perhaps it lets you establish
11:11:18 an iconic visual introduction to your
11:11:21 sites here's the schindler lab website
11:11:23 as an example another site my studio
11:11:25 designs it's an online publication for a
11:11:28 cultural institution and we decided to
11:11:30 make a bold visual statement to set the
11:11:33 tone there's some introduction text as
11:11:35 you scroll down and you can access the
11:11:37 table of contents via a link but the
11:11:40 home page acts like a book cover for
11:11:42 this publication
11:11:44 here's the site for the construction
11:11:45 company bronze structure there are four
11:11:47 links in the main navigation about
11:11:50 projects press and contact that will
11:11:52 take you to the actual content of the
11:11:54 site the home page though uses much of
11:11:57 the real estate for a beautiful image of
11:11:59 a project that bronze structure has
11:12:01 worked on
11:12:02 this image by the way changes randomly
11:12:04 every time the home page is loaded
11:12:07 and one more
11:12:08 the website for dosa a clothing design
11:12:10 company
11:12:12 here we have a simple list of navigation
11:12:14 items to the left
11:12:15 and on the right side you have a
11:12:17 beautiful mood image
11:12:19 i think there's a place for the palette
11:12:21 cleanser but keep in mind that this
11:12:23 homepage strategy can make a website
11:12:25 feel a bit static if you want to
11:12:27 communicate that your site is always up
11:12:29 to date and not stale then you might
11:12:31 employ strategies that ensure that your
11:12:33 homepage doesn't look the same all the
11:12:35 time but again it depends on the project
11:12:38 in a little bit we'll see how you can
11:12:39 employ the palette cleanser strategy in
11:12:42 conjunction with other strategies
11:12:44 the call to action is a singular action
11:12:47 that you want the user to do before
11:12:48 leaving the sites that might be creating
11:12:51 an account or calling a phone number or
11:12:53 downloading a file starting a chat or
11:12:56 purchasing a product or service
11:12:58 here are a few examples
11:13:00 monday is a productivity tool and on
11:13:02 their home page it is very clear what
11:13:05 they want you to do next enter your
11:13:07 email and sign up for a free account
11:13:10 in fact most home pages for online
11:13:12 services will have this in common
11:13:15 get a new user to sign up or existing
11:13:18 users to log in
11:13:20 google.com is a curious case of a call
11:13:22 to action websites while there's one
11:13:24 singular thing to do here
11:13:26 search for something note that compared
11:13:29 to other search engines those that are
11:13:31 still around and those that are not
11:13:33 google doesn't show you any secondary
11:13:35 content like the most popular websites
11:13:37 or the latest news or anything else that
11:13:39 gets in between you and your search
11:13:41 results that's pretty great ux right and
11:13:44 in my opinion it's probably one big
11:13:46 reason why google search took over the
11:13:48 world even though it was one of the
11:13:50 later search engines to enter the market
11:13:53 here's another fun example donate your
11:13:55 tab
11:13:56 this service uses your computer's
11:13:58 processing power to temporarily mine
11:14:00 bitcoins but the proceeds being donated
11:14:03 to an important cause
11:14:05 and here's a ginormous call to action a
11:14:08 toggle button to turn on the mining
11:14:11 then we have the narrative as a strategy
11:14:14 for the home page here the home page is
11:14:17 used to explain or introduce the site's
11:14:20 mission this often involves scrolling or
11:14:22 a slideshow here are a few examples the
11:14:25 burden of genius side is for a
11:14:27 documentary film about the physician who
11:14:29 performed the first successful liver
11:14:31 transplant at first the homepage seems a
11:14:34 bit like a pallet cleanser
11:14:36 a big iconic photo and a title
11:14:39 but as you scroll down you get a bit of
11:14:41 a narrative in the form of captions and
11:14:44 images
11:14:45 and at the end of this sequence there's
11:14:47 a call to action to watch the trailer
11:14:50 here is the hostgator homepage
11:14:52 as you scroll down you get an
11:14:54 introduction to their hosting services
11:14:56 and they tell you how they are different
11:14:58 how to get started and what other people
11:15:00 have said in praise of their company
11:15:03 and the fun animations bring a bit of
11:15:05 surprise and joy to the experience
11:15:08 i often call what is happening here
11:15:10 narrative scrolling
11:15:12 as you scroll down the page a narrative
11:15:14 unfolds and words images and animations
11:15:18 i'm a big fan of it actually
11:15:20 and sometimes this is pushed to an
11:15:22 extreme
11:15:23 consider the website every last drop
11:15:26 this side seems to be a marriage of a
11:15:27 comic book and a movie
11:15:30 only that your scrolling controls the
11:15:32 action as you scroll down here and you
11:15:35 can see the scroll bar moving down on
11:15:36 the right
11:15:37 the character in the story wakes up and
11:15:40 starts his day he gets dressed eats
11:15:42 breakfast commutes and goes to work
11:15:46 evidently as an astronaut no less
11:15:48 along the way there are stats that tell
11:15:50 us about the importance of clean water
11:15:52 and conservation
11:15:54 alright two more strategies to cover
11:15:57 the fork in the road strategy is great
11:15:59 for sites that need to immediately send
11:16:01 users onto different paths depending on
11:16:04 what user group they belong to
11:16:06 for example the website for napster
11:16:08 which is a music streaming service
11:16:11 bifurcates the home page into two
11:16:13 different sections for two different
11:16:15 user groups
11:16:16 businesses and personal accounts
11:16:19 you must make this decision as the very
11:16:20 first thing before being able to move on
11:16:23 here's the homepage for coca-cola
11:16:25 it doesn't really provide us with much
11:16:27 information but instead we are asked to
11:16:30 choose the country we live in each
11:16:32 country then has a unique coca-cola
11:16:34 website all of its own
11:16:36 and on the website for capital investing
11:16:38 you get to choose what kind of investor
11:16:40 you are if you'd like to work with an
11:16:42 advisor or invest on your own each one
11:16:46 of these options leads to a separate
11:16:47 section of the site tailor-made for the
11:16:50 specific audience
11:16:52 and finally a homepage strategy i call
11:16:55 fusion that's simply a combination of
11:16:57 multiple strategies
11:16:59 in fact most websites will employ more
11:17:02 than one of the strategies that i
11:17:04 covered here let's look at two examples
11:17:07 on the airbnb side we can find pallet
11:17:09 cleanser
11:17:10 call to action and featured contents
11:17:13 and on the site for paymo a project
11:17:16 management service we have call to
11:17:18 action fork in the road and a narrative
11:17:22 here's the homepage for airbnb the large
11:17:24 images in the background are what i
11:17:26 would call a pallet cleanser they set
11:17:28 the tone for the sides by showcasing
11:17:30 beautiful vacation locations
11:17:33 the call to action is also very
11:17:34 prominent a big search box
11:17:37 and as you scroll down you get some
11:17:39 featured content including featured
11:17:41 homes around the world
11:17:43 by the way they also implemented an
11:17:45 interesting functionality here no matter
11:17:48 how much i scroll down
11:17:49 there's always more content that loads
11:17:52 automatically
11:17:53 that's called an infinite scroll by the
11:17:55 way
11:17:56 on the paymo side we get a big call to
11:17:58 action start a free trial
11:18:01 as you scroll down there's also a fork
11:18:03 in the roads you can choose between
11:18:05 being part of a team or being a
11:18:07 freelancer and then as you scroll
11:18:09 further there's a narrative explaining
11:18:12 the service that paymo provides
11:18:14 including nice illustrations a list of
11:18:16 problems that the software solves user
11:18:19 testimonials key features and so on
11:18:22 and then when you reach the end of the
11:18:23 page just in case there's another call
11:18:26 to action
11:18:27 alright we have made it through our
11:18:29 investigation of homepage strategies
11:18:32 this should give you a good foundation
11:18:33 and some inspiration for the homepage of
11:18:36 your project that you are designing in
11:18:37 this course so what tools might you use
11:18:40 to create your wireframes of course
11:18:42 there's pen and paper you can always use
11:18:45 that and sometimes i think it's a good
11:18:47 idea to start there you know just get
11:18:49 your thoughts out there do something
11:18:51 nimbly with your hands uh it's kind of
11:18:53 nice to start design processes with your
11:18:56 hands inside of the computer however
11:18:58 eventually i would suggest that
11:19:00 wireframes should end up in the computer
11:19:02 if only that it allows you to revise
11:19:05 them more easily you know oftentimes you
11:19:08 go to multiple phases when working with
11:19:10 a client and presenting a first set of
11:19:12 wireframes will often times lead to
11:19:14 changes that need to be implemented and
11:19:16 if you're working by hand
11:19:18 you just have to start over right or you
11:19:20 erase and everything gets kind of smudgy
11:19:22 so i like working on the computer for
11:19:25 final wireframes
11:19:27 so illustrator is another fine option
11:19:29 for a tool to create wireframes
11:19:32 it allows you to create boxes and text
11:19:34 and lines and that's kind of all we need
11:19:37 for wireframes right so the example here
11:19:40 is a wireframe i created illustrator for
11:19:42 an architecture studio and what you're
11:19:44 seeing here is the wireframe for the
11:19:47 landing page where illustrator can get
11:19:49 tricky is that it allows you to do kind
11:19:52 of too much right you put down a piece
11:19:54 of type you actually have to choose the
11:19:56 typeface and you might
11:19:58 get into this rabbit hole of you know
11:20:01 hunting for the best typeface for your
11:20:02 wireframes where as i said before it's
11:20:05 not the time to make those kind of
11:20:07 decisions so instead there are some
11:20:10 designated wireframe tools out there and
11:20:13 the one that i really like is called
11:20:15 balsamiq
11:20:17 it's made with the sole purpose to allow
11:20:19 you to create wireframes they have an
11:20:22 app that you can download and install on
11:20:23 your computer but they also have a
11:20:26 cloud-based tool that works in your
11:20:28 browser and the nice thing is that they
11:20:30 actually give you a 30-day free trial to
11:20:33 try out their software so i would really
11:20:35 encourage you to create your wireframes
11:20:37 for this class in balsamiq and i think
11:20:40 you'll find the software quite intuitive
11:20:42 and easy to learn i'm just going to give
11:20:44 you a quick tour so you'll get an idea
11:20:46 of how the software
11:20:48 works so here i am logged into balsamic
11:20:51 cloud and you can have multiple projects
11:20:53 here and you see there's already one
11:20:55 that i created which is for a previous
11:20:58 lecture right i created all the form ui
11:21:00 elements that i showed in a previous
11:21:02 lecture here in balsamiq you'll see
11:21:04 these patterns come with balsamic you
11:21:06 don't have to create them on your own so
11:21:08 if i wanted to add another checkbox item
11:21:10 here i can double click there's a
11:21:12 specific easy to learn syntax
11:21:15 so if i wanted to add another checkbox
11:21:18 item that isn't selected i can do that
11:21:21 and there it is in my wireframe
11:21:24 so let me create a quick wireframe from
11:21:27 scratch i can create a new wireframe
11:21:29 that's completely blank and add a few
11:21:31 elements and i find the best way to work
11:21:33 with balsamic is to use the quick add
11:21:35 function you just type in what you need
11:21:37 so first thing i'll need is a browser
11:21:40 window right now type in browser
11:21:42 and here in the list the ui element
11:21:44 actually shows up i click on it and
11:21:46 there it is right so now i can make it
11:21:49 bigger
11:21:50 i can double click on it there are some
11:21:52 options right
11:21:55 my site
11:21:56 my
11:21:58 site.com
11:22:00 these two things now show up in the
11:22:02 browser window and since i don't want to
11:22:04 inadvertently select this browser window
11:22:07 i can lock it by right clicking on it
11:22:09 and saying lock all right so what else
11:22:11 are we going to add here maybe a logo
11:22:13 for that i usually just use an icon
11:22:16 and a label pattern
11:22:18 there is some text underneath just an
11:22:21 empty empty icon if i click on it i can
11:22:23 label it and i can also add some links
11:22:27 so
11:22:28 there's a button bar pattern
11:22:31 i can
11:22:32 have a comma separated list here let's
11:22:34 just say about us
11:22:36 our
11:22:37 products and contacts
11:22:40 then we'll add a title
11:22:41 for the page title and maybe this about
11:22:44 us page just has a video player and a
11:22:46 caption so i can search for video add a
11:22:49 player here you know resize it however i
11:22:51 want and then let's say there's a
11:22:53 caption to the left of it just
11:22:55 describing what this video will show us
11:22:57 to search for block i'll get this
11:22:59 pattern called a block of text maybe we
11:23:01 don't know what that caption will say we
11:23:03 just know it'll say something right
11:23:05 instead of having to write it at this
11:23:07 point it'll just indicate oh there's
11:23:09 going to be some text there at this
11:23:11 point i should also show you that there
11:23:13 is actually two flavors of this
11:23:15 wireframing skin that balsamic comes
11:23:17 with if you go to the project info there
11:23:20 is a skin option right here on the
11:23:22 bottom and right now i'm using the
11:23:24 sketch skin which has quite a bit of a
11:23:26 hand drawing feel to it but there's also
11:23:28 more streamlined wireframe skin
11:23:31 that looks a little bit cleaner right so
11:23:33 it kind of depends what you prefer so
11:23:35 let's quickly create a second wireframe
11:23:37 for the our products page i can just go
11:23:40 to the page here on the left and click
11:23:42 on duplicate
11:23:44 and then i have a copy of the previous
11:23:45 wireframes i can delete the elements i
11:23:48 don't need
11:23:49 this title
11:23:50 becomes products here when i click on
11:23:53 the button bar i can change which link
11:23:55 is actually active so now our navigation
11:23:57 reflects the page that we're on
11:24:00 and here maybe we just have some
11:24:02 thumbnail images so
11:24:04 image that's just a box with an x make
11:24:07 that a certain size and i can hit
11:24:09 command d to duplicate it and now i have
11:24:12 four products that presumably when we
11:24:14 click on them go to a detail page right

3:21 that you can show either developers or
3:25 stakeholders how you envision your UI
3:28 design to behave when users interact
3:30 with it now another popular category of
3:33 tools are the low to no code tools such

3:48 these tools to make them a working

4:00 straight in these loaded nood tools
4:03 which is fine but personally I prefer to

4:23 no code tools now as far as UI design is
4:26 concerned those are the main tools that
4:28 you're going to be working in using and
4:31 for me I'm primarily working within
4:34 figma when I'm designing a UI now
4:36 because ux is such a broad term there
4:38 are other categories of tools that you

4:49 competitive analysis before you start

4:54 tools right here will help you collect
4:56 user feedback and also help you
4:59 determine how users interact with your

5:15 interface you want to test things you

Title: What is Graphic Design? Ep1/45 [Beginners Guide to Graphic Design]

0:00 Hello and welcome to this beginner's guide series to graphic design.
0:04 From what graphic design is, skills to be a graphic designer, design theory, education you need,
0:10 equipment you need, to the graphic design portfolio and interview advice,
0:15 this series is for anyone at any level. So if you're interested in graphic design
0:19 and considering becoming a graphic designer,
0:22 join me as I discuss series of graphic design topics.
0:34 So you're interested in graphic design, perhaps considering becoming a graphic designer
0:38 and looking to learn more
0:40 in this video I'm going to discuss what graphic design is. Now a lot of people on
0:46 my channel have asked me this question
0:48 mainly youngsters and people who are thinking about pursuing graphic design
0:52 and want to know what graphic design is and if it's right for them.
0:55 Now I have often found this a really hard question, as it does not really have
1:00 a simple answer without some degree of explanation.
1:03 Now if you google this it will tell you that:
1:06 'Graphic design is the art or skill of combining text and pictures in advertisements, magazines or books
1:14 So quite a simple and accurate explanation there, though I happen to think there's
1:18 more to it than that.
1:20 In this video I'm going to attempt to answer this question and give you my take on it from my experience.
1:26 so what is graphic design?
1:28 Well let's start simple.
1:30 So with graphic design we have two things:
1:32 We have graphic and design.
1:36 Graphic is visual, which is associated with art, imagination and expression, involving many mediums such as drawing painting engraving or lettering given clear and explicit detail.
1:48 So the graphic part of graphic design is made up of visual elements, the building blocks of design.
1:53 The basic visual elements that combine to create graphic design include the following:
2:00 Line, Colour, Shape, Texture, Space, Form and Typography
2:06 Through the harness of our artistic expression we choose these visual elements and arrange them on a surface in a layout
2:12 to convey an idea.
2:13 Now the second part is design, which is to decide upon the look and function of something before it is made.
2:21 So design is thinking, problem solving and practicality.... theory.
2:26 In graphic design there are rules that can be considered.
2:29 These rules are called 'The principles of design'.
2:32 These principles all have a relationship between each other
2:35 and appear in every well designed piece of work you see.
2:39 So the design principles make the design in 'graphic design'.
2:43 A good grasp of design theory will mean there is always substance behind your work.
2:48 The key principles of design are:
2:51 Contrast, Hierarchy, Alignment, Balance, Proximity, Repetition, Simplicity and Function.
2:59 When we bring both the visual elements and the design principles together we have graphic design.
3:06 Graphic design is not drawing,
3:08 graphic design is not painting, graphic design is not creating a poster
3:12 or a quick logo on your computer,
3:14 these things are the means to the end and are skills in their own right.
3:19 Graphic design is not about making things look pretty,
3:22 graphic design is the organisation and presentation of information,
3:27 developed through a creative process for a particular function.
3:31 In graphic design we must first understand information, a message or a brief
3:37 and then research, think, imagine and develop and create a solution, a concept in order to
3:43 communicate that information, tell a story, a message or idea in a visual way.
3:50 It's only once we have a solid idea developed through the design process we can use our skills with
3:55 layout, colour, typography and creative tools to bring that idea to life
4:00 Once upon a time that would have been with just the aid of a pencil or a paint brush on paper.
4:06 Today it's all those things and more. Today we also have cameras, computers and printers,
4:12 since the invention of the print press which enabled reproduction on a mass scale
4:18 graphic design has been used to communicate and spread messages worldwide.
4:22 Through the evolution of technology, today we are surrounded
4:26 by an abundance of visual communication. Today graphic design is implemented across a
4:31 broad spectrum of media both print and digital such as:
4:35 Advertising, Branding, Web design, Interface design, Magazine layout, Paper engineering and packaging, TV, Motion graphics, Infographics, Signage, Art and Installations and Digital art.
4:51 If you are looking to pursue a career in graphic design you will inevitably end up creating work across one of those sectors.
4:57 Over many decades in the past
4:59 graphic design has shaped culture through pop art, political art and commercial art.
5:04 Today graphic design has a big commercial side, it's associated heavily with commerce and
5:11 used to sell and advertise things, but that's not all it has to be.
5:15 The power of graphic design to inform, influence and instruct on a commercial level can also
5:21 be used to educate and inspire a generation
5:24 I believe graphic design is the unity of two opposing forces, artistic expression and practical application,
5:32 design theory.
5:34 As a graphic designer these two forces will always be at odds with each other in your mind.
5:39 when you can harness these two forces together in harmony,
5:43 then you can create great visual communication.
5:46 So that's an introduction to what graphic design is. For any of you that were unsure of what graphic design is.
5:52 I hope that has helped to explain.
5:55 In this video I mentioned that graphic design is made of two things
5:59 visual elements and design principles. Over the next seven episodes i'm going
6:04 to talk more about design theory and talk in detail about 'Visual elements'
6:08 that make a piece of graphic design work. In the next video I'm going to start by talking about 'Line'
6:14 as a visual element and graphic design, so see you in the next video!

0:00 Hello and welcome to this beginner's guide series to graphic design.

0:49 the basic visual elements that combine to create graphic design include the following:

1:01 Whatever work you produce be it for a magazine a poster a website or advertisement these visual
1:06 elements will play a part in your design.
1:09 in this video I'm going to discuss the first key visual element and discuss
1:14 'Line' as a visual element in graphic design.
1:17 In this video I will also be referring to some visual diagrams

1:25 accompanies this series, link is in the description. So one of the most basic
1:30 visual elements of design is the line and it should not be underestimated as
1:35 simple as lines are they can be quite versatile and be used as crucial
1:39 elements of design. Lines can be straight, curved, thick, thin, solid or dashed.
1:47 Lines can be used to add structure to a composition t0 frame information and to
1:51 divide information. Used consideratly lines can add elegance to a composition
1:56 at hierarchy and draw the eye to a specific point.
2:01 With type lines can be used to emphasise and put stress on a word a phrase or paragraph.
2:08 Lines can be used to decorate, illustrate and represent information in inforgraphics
2:14 If we think about magazines, newspapers and maps
2:17 one of the most common visual elements is the line as it works very
2:22 well to structure information.
2:24 Here are some simple horizontal lines, here we have some strokes in various
2:29 sizes and below some alternatives. A line can be made of more than a single stroke
2:34 it can be made with many individual strokes and even dots. So here are some
2:39 simple examples of how lines may be used in design. For the sake of simplicity and
2:45 to focus on the structure
2:47 I have composed these examples in black and white
2:50 if you want to take a closer look at these you can find them in the
2:53 downloadable PDF, link is in the description. So the first example is
2:58 where line plays a role of building infographics
3:02 this is a simple tree diagram made of lines in different shades. If we zoom
3:07 into the visual icons they are also lines as illustrations
3:12 so overall a simple visual with another line below as an anchor point for the associated information.
3:18 Next is an example in typography.
3:23 Here lines are used to divide and draw emphasis to a particular word.
3:27 Next is an example where lines are used to create a border. In this example there
3:34 are two stroke lines that create this border a thin and a thick. The type of
3:39 stroke lines you use in relationship to other elements can define a look and
3:44 feel to your composition. In this case we have a boutique feel.
3:48 Notice here we also have a line that elegantly divides the type.
3:53 So next is an example where lines are used to predominantly structure a composition.
3:59 but at the same time decorate. This is a simple chapter index page
4:04 here we have a large dark line running vertically down the page and
4:09 smaller lines running horizontally across the page. The horizontal lines are
4:13 passing through the vertical and inverting in colour as they do so
4:19 creating a lose grid for the type element to exist.
4:22 You can see here where the lines are defining and positioning the type elements.
4:27 Next is an example where lines can be used for structure and decoration in a
4:32 minimal elegant way. Here lines make up the board elements that frame a quote
4:38 and a line is drawn vertically aligned left creating a base to carry the paragraph next to it.
4:46 These lines though in black and white could be used to add a bit of colour onto the page as well as structure.
4:53 So next is a general example of how lines can divide information.
4:58 Now newspapers can be very complex and busy media platforms with a lot of information being presented at any time.
5:06 Here we can see were lines play a role to divide and decorate information.
5:11 At the top we have lines to separate that section from the article section below.
5:16 In this section we also have small lines, details used to decorate.
5:21 Now a line does not necessarily have to be a solid stroke a line can be created
5:27 by contrast of colour or shape on a page.
5:31 In the article below we have a big picture. Now even though there is no particular stroke applied
5:37 the solid colour fill of the image creates a line around it.
5:41 Below this we have the main body of text, in this we have an infographic made with
5:48 lines and lines that break information out such as diagrams, quotes and footers
5:53 So the last example shows lines creating decoration, texture and structure on a page
6:01 in this example there are various types of lines going on here
6:04 first we can see the physical line used to create decoration with various stroke effects applied.
6:11 In this instance the lines also create another white line
6:14 through the middle of the page as negative space. Here we have three
6:19 columns of type where the third column is breaking alignment to fit in the
6:23 space creating an interesting and dynamic layout.
6:28 So there are lots of ways line can be used in design.
6:31 These are just a few examples. So when looking at graphic design
6:35 be sure to observe how line has been considered and used as a design element.
6:40 So that is the first visual element in graphic design.
