0:00 Hello and welcome to this beginner's guide series to graphic design.
0:04 From what graphic design is, skills to be a graphic designer, design theory, education you need, equipment you need to the graphic design portfolio and interview advice.
0:14 This series is for anyone at any level.
0:17 So if you're interested in graphic design and considering becoming a graphic designer.
0:22 join me as I discuss series of graphic design topics.
0:33 So the graphic part of graphic design is made up of visual elements, the building blocks of design.
0:41 Through the harness of our artistic expression we choose these
0:44 visual elements and arrange them on a surface in a layout to convey an idea.
0:49 The basic visual elements that combine to create graphic design include the following:
0:55 Line, colour, shape, texture, space, form and typography.
1:01 Whatever work you produce be it for a magazine a poster a website or advertisement, these visual elements will play a part in your design.
1:09 In this video I'm going to discuss the second key visual and discuss colour as a visual element in graphic design.
1:17 In this video I will be referring to some visual diagrams
1:21 if you wish to follow along you can download the PDF document that
1:24 accompanies this series, link is in the description. So colour plays one of the
1:30 biggest roles in graphic design, it can give emphasis
1:33 it can be used as a mechanism of the organization, it can create impact and
1:37 create a specific look and feel in a piece of graphic design work.
1:42 When working with colour it helps to have a good knowledge of colour theory.
1:46 Colour theory provides us with practical guidance to help us mix colours and
1:50 create interesting colour combinations and it all starts with the colour wheel.
1:54 The colour wheel is a really useful tool designed to help us choose colours that work well together.
2:02 So this here is the red, yellow, blue colour model which consists of 12 colours
2:08 Now if we jump onto the Adobe colour website we can see this colour wheel
2:12 as more of a spectrum. This is an amazing resource to explore and create colour schemes.
2:20 The address for this website is color.adobe.com.
2:24 I'll be referring to this website later in this episode but for now to help explain
2:29 colour theory I'll use this simple example. If you want to take a closer
2:33 look at this color wheel and the spectrum
2:35 you can find them in the downloadable PDF, link is in the description.
2:40 So the colour wheel consists of primary colors, secondary colors and tertiary colours
2:46 and these can be split into warm and cool colours. So let's take a look at each of these.
2:52 So first is primary colours. Primary colours make up the basis for the colour wheel
2:59 here they are red, yellow and blue.
3:01 Next are the secondary colours. Secondary colours are made by mixing equal portions of the primary colours, these create green, orange and purple.
3:11 Next are the tertiary colours. Tertiary colours are made by mixing a primary colour
3:16 with a neighboring secondary colour, for example if we mix the yellow with
3:21 the orange we get a yellow orange colour in between. If we continue to mix the
3:26 primary and neighbouring colours, we fill the gaps and get the remaining tertiary colours.
3:32 Now if we separate the colour wheel we get two categories, warm and cool colours.
3:38 On the right we have the warm colours, these incorporate the red, violet colours through to yellow.
3:45 On the left we have cool colours, these incorporate the colours yellow through to indigo.
3:50 So that completes the colour wheel. Now this is a really useful tool designed to help
3:56 us choose color schemes. To help us choose interesting colour combinations
4:01 that have harmony together or create contrast there are some colour rules we can explore.
4:05 These colour rules are referred to as:
4:09 monochromatic colours, analogous colours, complementary colours and triadic colours.
4:15 So first we have monochromatic colours. Monochromatic colours are shades and tints of the same colour.
4:22 The monochromatic colour scheme is typically balance and easy on the eye.
4:28 Now if we jump onto the Adobe color website at color.adobe.com we can see
4:34 some of these colour rules on the left
4:36 if I click on monochromatic, it will generate a monochromatic colour scheme below.
4:41 Now if I click and drag the middle colour circle in the spectrum and toggle the other
4:47 it will generate new schemes.
4:49 Next we have analogous colours. Analogous colours are those found close to each other on the colour wheel.
4:56 Analogous colours typically always work well together since they have similar origins.
5:01 Like the monochromatic colors they are
5:03 also balance but typically more interesting as these colours have more contrast.
5:10 This time on the Adobe colour website if I click on Analogous it will
5:14 generate an analogous color scheme. If I click and drag the middle circle
5:20 it will generate new schemes. Notice all the colours are similar to each other.
5:25 Next we have complementary colours. Complementary colours are those found on opposite ends of the colour wheel.
5:33 Complementary colors have high contrast
5:35 which produce vibrant exciting colour schemes, as implied complementary colors
5:41 enhance each other and typically always work well together.
5:44 Back on the Adobe colour website if I click on complementary it will generate
5:49 a complementary colour scheme. If I click and drag the middle circle
5:53 it will generate new schemes.
5:56 Finally we have Triadic colours.
5:59 Triadic colours are those spaced equally on the colour wheel.
6:03 Triadic colours typically produce vibrant effects.
6:07 Back on the Adobe colour website if I click on triadic
6:11 it will generate a triadic color scheme. If I click and drag the middle circle
6:15 it will generate new schemes.
6:18 So those are some rules you can keep in mind when
6:20 exploring colours. So that is the second key visual element in graphic design.
6:26 Well I hope you enjoyed this video, if you did hit the like button on my facebook page.
6:31 If you would like to see more videos like this in future hit the subscribe button.
6:35 and you can also follow me on twitter at TastyTuts.
6:38 So the next visual element is 'Shape' in the next video I'm going to be talking
6:44 about shape as a visual element in graphic design.
6:47 See you in the next video!

URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5jprIWG8f5g&list=PLYfCBK8IplO4E2sXtdKMVpKJZRBEoMvpn&index=5&pp=iAQB

0:00 Hello and welcome to this beginner's guide series graphic design.
0:04 From what graphic design is, skills to be a graphic designer, design theory, education you need, equipment you need to the graphic design portfolio and interview advice.
0:14 This series is for anyone at any level.
0:17 So if you're interested in graphic design and considering becoming a graphic designer
0:22 join me as i discussed series of graphic design topics.
0:34 So the graphic part of graphic design is made up of visual elements the
0:38 building blocks of design. Through the harness of our artistic expression we choose these
0:43 visual elements and arrange them on a surface in a layout to convey an idea
0:48 the basic visual elements are combine to be a graphic design include the following:
0:53 Line, colour, shape, texture, space, form and typography.
1:00 Whatever work you produce be it for a magazine a poster a website or advertisement these visual
1:05 elements will play a part in your design.
1:08 In this video I'm going to discuss the third key visual element and discuss
1:12 'Shape' as a visual element in graphic design.
1:16 In this video I will be referring to some visual diagrams
1:19 if you wish to follow along you can download the PDF document that
1:23 accompanies this series, link is in the description. So everything is ultimately a shape.
1:30 All objects are composed of shapes and all visual elements are shapes in some way.
1:35 In design shapes have two dimensions and are measured by their height and width.
1:40 Shapes are defined by boundaries such as with lines or colour and can also be
1:47 created with negative space. shapes are used to add interest and substance to a
1:52 piece of graphic design work, they can be used to decorate
1:56 they can be symbolic and can be used to create patterns and textures.
2:01 Now there are two types of shape: Geometric and organic.
2:06 Geometric shapes are the shapes that can be drawn using a ruler or compass.
2:10 Weather simple or complex these produce a feeling of control or order.
2:16 Organic shapes, are shapes that can be drawn free hand or shapes found in nature.
2:21 Organic shapes weather simple or complex produce a natural feel.
2:26 Now it's important to keep in mind that shapes our signal visual elements.
2:31 In design when we begin to place shapes together we create a relationship between them.
2:36 However simple or complex it's this relationship between shapes that can
2:42 trigger feelings, convey messages, engage an audience, add emphasis to a portion of a layout and create movement.
2:50 When we group or move shapes closer or further apart
2:53 we are applying the design principle 'Proximity' later in this series we will
2:58 be learning all about the design principle proximity but for now it's
3:02 important to keep in mind
3:04 that proximity plays an important role when working with shapes.
3:09 So in design geometric and organic shapes can combine together on many levels to create
3:14 various design elements we use to communicate
3:18 One example of this is with brand logos. In logos shape is used in its purest form.
3:25 Here are some well known brand logos. If you want to take a closer look at these you
3:30 can find them in the downloadable PDF
3:32 the link is in the description. So here we have a mix of logos
3:37 some are made with geometric shapes some with organic shapes and some with a
3:41 combination of both.
3:42 We have some that are single solid shapes and some that are composed of a
3:47 few shapes that are placed near each other.
3:50 Keep in mind that proximity is playing a crucial role here in the logos that are
3:55 made with few shapes.
3:57 If we were to move some of the shapes further away or closer they would have
4:01 an entirely different look and feel. So here shape is used as a symbol.
4:07 Another example where geometric and organic shapes can come together is in typography.
4:14 A single letter in a typeface is a combination of geometric and organic shapes.
4:20 which build together to create a larger shape
4:22 in this case the letter 'R'. We learned from a young age that this shape the
4:27 letter R has a meaning. When combined with other shapes, for example the rest of the alphabet to form words
4:34 it is giving a new meaning
4:37 and when combined with a sequence of words we can form a sentence to pass on a message.
4:42 No where in design is shape used more literally to communicate visually than in typography.
4:48 It's the type of shapes that combine together that give the typeface its distinct look and feel.
4:55 Here shape is again being used as a symbol.
4:58 So taking a look in the PDF. Here I have some more examples of how shape is used in design.
5:05 In this first example we have a group of text and a geometric shape in close proximity
5:11 Now this may look simple but when you think about it
5:14 here we have a group of texts which is made of hundreds of shapes carefully arranged in half a circle
5:22 and next to it we have a single solid shape as another half circle. Overall on this page we see
5:29 a circle shape even though there is no circle shape at all anywhere on this page.
5:34 It's the careful arrangement of the type and the solid shape in close proximity
5:40 that suggests the shape of a circle. In design shape is indeed an individual
5:45 element, but as a group shapes conform compositions like this and as such
5:51 be treated as a visual element. Here shape is being used to create an overall composition to create the shape.
6:00 This can be seen in another example, here we have a page with a number of random shapes.
6:05 Now if these shapes were scattered we would just have a page of individual shapes.
6:11 But arranged any particular way with the right proximity we start to see another shape, the number three.
6:18 Here shape is being used to decorate to build, illustrate and hint at another shape on the page.
6:25 Next is an example of how shape can form a recognisable image as an illustration.
6:33 Now this image is made only of polygon shapes.
6:36 It's the colour, size and position of each and every polygon shape that gives it an appernce
6:42 to look like a Roman statue.
6:44 Now if we were to separate each shape from each other even a little bit
6:49 it would no longer look like a Roman statue.
6:51 This shows how proximity is crucial to represent or communicate in a composition.
6:59 In this next example shape is used to create something entirely different.
7:03 Here we have a number of shapes that appear to be scattered in no particular order.
7:08 We can see what looks like positive and negative spaces along with other shapes.
7:14 Its appearance is random and all the shapes seem to blend into each other.
7:19 There is nothing legible hear that stands out
7:22 only an overall look and feel. Here shape is being used to create a surface image texture.
7:29 So in this last example unlike the randomness of the image texture here we have
7:34 what looks like a regular repeating sequence of shape
7:38 this is similar to the image texture in that nothing particular stands out.
7:43 Here shape is being used to create a surface pattern texture.
7:49 So shape is what makes up the foundation of any composition. A layout can be simple made of one or few shapes
7:56 or complex made by groups of many individual shapes. In design shape can be
8:03 used in many ways, it's how they are arranged that determines the visual outcome.
8:08 When creating a composition you must always consider how the visual elements of your design
8:14 are creating shapes, how the shapes interact and work together to present and communicate the intended message.
8:21 So that is the third key visual element in graphic design.
8:25 Well I hope you enjoyed this video. If you did hit the like button on my facebook page.
8:30 Iif you would like to see more videos like this in future hit the subscribe button
8:35 and you can also follow me on twitter at TastyTuts.
8:39 So the next key visual element is 'Texture' in this video I touched on how
8:44 shape can be used to create texture. In the next video I'm going to talk in more
8:49 depth about texture as a visual element in graphic design.
8:53 So see you in the next video!

0:00 Hello and welcome to this beginner's guide series to graphic design.
0:04 From what graphic design is, skills to be a graphic designer, design theory, education you need, equipment you need to the graphic design portfolio and interview advice.
0:14 This series is for anyone at any level.
0:17 So if you're interested in graphic design and considering becoming a graphic designer
0:22 join me as I discuss series of graphic design topics.
0:34 So the graphic part of graphic design is made up of visual elements, the building blocks of design.
0:40 Through the harness of our artistic expression, we choose these
0:43 visual elements and arrange them on a surface in a layout to convey an idea.
0:49 The basic visual elements are combine to create graphic design include the following:
0:55 Line, color, shape, texture, space, form and typography.
1:01 Whatever work you produce, be it for a magazine a poster a website or an advertisement, these visual elements will play a part in your design.
1:09 In this video I'm going to discuss the fourth key visual element and discuss
1:14 Texture as a visual element in graphic design.
1:18 In this video I'll be referring to some visual examples if you wish to take a
1:22 closer look at these, you can find them in the downloadable PDF document that
1:27 accompanies this series, link is in the description. So texture is the way the
1:32 surface feels or is perceived to feel. Texture is used to create a visual tone
1:38 and can influence the look and feel of a piece of graphic design work.
1:43 Texture is used to attract or repel interest to an element
1:47 depending on the pleasantness of the texture. Now in design there are two types of texture:
1:53 Image texture and pattern texture
1:57 Image texture is generated from a combination of organic or geometric shapes and colour.
2:03 Image texture can be simple or complex and generally appears random to create a particular look and feel
2:11 Image texture tantalises the senses, the eye likes to look at image texture.
2:17 Image texture can be like the sense of smell and touch, you can't see anything in particular in it
2:22 but overall it triggers feelings of emotions and sensations of touch.
2:27 Now image texture can be environmental biological or man-made.
2:32 Here are some examples in the PDF of image texture.
2:37 Environmental texture can be wood, grain, sand, water, stone to the stars in the sky
2:44 Biological texture can be skin, fur, feathers and animal prints.
2:50 Man made texture can be paintings, illustrations, dies, cloths, papers, typography, photographic effects and so on.
3:00 Because of image textures abstract nature with the ability to trigger feelings, emotions and excite the senses.
3:06 Image textures can be used to engage and add distinct visual tone to a piece of graphic design work.
3:13 So next is pattern texture, pattern texture is again generated from organic or geometric shapes and colour
3:22 though pattern texture is mostly manufactured. Patterns can be simple or complex
3:28 but unlike image texture that generally appears random, patterns appear more structured.
3:33 Here are some examples in the PDF of pattern texture.
3:38 Repeating a formation of shape and colour will result in a pattern texture. Repeating a logo in a formation will result in a pattern texture.
3:47 Like image texture, pattern texture also tantalises the senses but in a different way.
3:53 Patterns trigger our visual senses rather than emotional senses.
3:57 Perhaps because pattern is more structured and less abstract.
4:02 Pattern is more about visual recognition
4:05 due to the shapes that repeat themselves, because of this in design pattern texture
4:10 is used and works well as decoration.
4:14 A good example of this is in branding. Pattern creates a very distinct look and feel.
4:19 and because of it's repetitive nature a brand can use this visual design element as a way to decorate and make you remember their brand.
4:27 Another example is gift wrapping paper. Now have you ever asked yourself why is
4:31 gift wrapping paper mostly patterns rather than just a single image
4:36 and why do people spend so much money on it?
4:38 One could conclude that the repetition of bright colours and images excite the visual senses.
4:45 Often a gift may lay in an environment for a while awaiting to be open such as under a Christmas tree.
4:52 The decorative nature of gift-wrap serves as a prelude to the excitement of opening a gift.
4:59 So the gift wrap could be considered as important as the gift itself.
5:03 Now some of the most iconic styles, eras of design design, fashion trends and social movements have been defined by their textures.
5:12 Examples being bauhaus patterns, neoclassical patterns, art deco textures,
5:18 psychedelic textures of the seventies, pop textures of the eighties and hip textures of the nineties.
5:24 The list goes on.
5:25 So that is the fourth key visual element in graphic design, texture.
5:30 Well I hope you enjoyed this video. If you did hit the like button on my facebook page.
5:37 If you would like to see more videos like this in future hit the subscribe button
5:40 and you can also follow me on twitter at TastyTuts.
5:44 In the next video I'm going to talk about space as a visual element in graphic design.
5:49 So see you in the next video!

0:00 Hello and welcome to this Beginner's Guide series to graphic design.
0:04 From what graphic design is, skills to be a graphic designer, design theory, education you need, equipment you need to the graphic design portfolio and interview advice.
0:15 This series is for anyone at any level.
0:18 So if you're interested in graphic design and considering becoming a graphic designer
0:23 join me as I discuss this series of graphic design topics.
0:34 So the graphic part of graphic design is made up of visual elements, the building blocks of design
0:40 Through the harness of our artistic expression we choose these visual elements and arrange them on the surface in a layout to convey an idea.
0:48 The basic visual elements that combine to create graphic design include the following:
0:53 Line, colour, shape, texture, space, form and typography.
0:59 Whatever work you produce be it for a magazine a poster a website or an advertisement
1:04 These visual elements will play an important part in your design.
1:08 In this video I'm going to discuss the firth key visual element and discuss space as a visual element in graphic design.
1:16 In this video I'll be referring to some visual examples
1:22 if you wish to take a closer look at these, you can find them in the downloadable PDF document
1:26 that accompanies this series, link is in the description.
1:31 So space creates the visual essence and dynamic of a composition.
1:36 In design there are two types of space: Positive space and negative space.
1:41 Positive space can be perceived as two dimensional or three-dimensional.
1:46 Positive space refers to the shapes of objects. It usually refers to anything that is considered the main focus of the page.
1:53 Negative space is the white space or empty space which is the part of the design that is not there, the space between the visual elements.
2:03 This can also be the background colour of a design. Negative space can be just as integral to the design as the positive space.
2:11 Negative space is important because it helps frame and contain a composition.
2:16 Negative space connects or disconnects shapes to suggest relationships between shapes.
2:23 Negative space avoids visual clutter and looks clean
2:28 which can help balance a composition and help focus the viewer on something specific.
2:34 In design what creates positive and negative space is the arrangement and visual appearance of shapes within a composition.
2:42 In two dimensional design proximity, overlap, opacity, light, and shadow and perspective can be applied to shapes to create visual dynamics.
2:52 the illusion of depth or space in a composition.
2:56 Proximity is simply the distance shapes are from one another
3:00 far apart or close together, proximity suggest relationships between shapes.
3:05 Overlap is the effect where shapes are arranged to appear to be on top of each other.
3:10 this illusion makes the top element look closer to the observer.
3:16 Opacity is the effect where objects appear transparent, degrees of opacity can make elements
3:21 like heavy or light to suggest dominance and order of closeness in a space
3:26 opacity blurs is the barriers between positive and negative space
3:32 overlapping transparent elements can create dynamics in a composition
3:37 to create an illusion of 3D and perspective.
3:40 Light and shadow can give an object a three-dimensional look, shadow can create the illusion that an object is on top of another and suggest how far apart they may be.
3:51 Perspective is created through the arrangement, creation and manipulation of shapes to look like they appear in real life.
3:58 This is the effect where shapes appear to get smaller the further away they get. Perspective can also be achieved through the use of relative sizes of objects
4:08 overlapping objects and blurring and sharpening of objects.
4:13 In design there is no way to determine the depth of the space, only the order of closeness.
4:19 In design we could use positive and negative space to create a focal point, create balance, set a visual tone and define a look and feel.
4:28 So that is the fifth key visual element in graphic design.
4:32 Well I hope you enjoyed this video if you did hit the like button on my facebook page.
4:37 If you'd like to see more videos like this and future hit the subscribe button.
4:41 and you can follow me on twitter at TastyTuts.
4:44 So the next key visual element is form. In the next video I'm going to be talking about the form as a visual element in graphic design.
4:53 So see you in the next video!

0:00 Hello and welcome to this beginner's guide series to graphic design.
0:04 From what graphic design is, skills to be a graphic designer, design theory, education you need, equipment you need to the graphic design portfolio and interview advice.
0:14 This series is for anyone at any level.
0:17 So if you're interested in graphic design and considering becoming graphic designer
0:22 join me as I discuss a series of graphic design topics.
0:33 So the graphic part of graphic design is made up of visual elements, the building blocks of design.
0:39 through the harness of our artistic expression we choose these visual elements
0:43 and arrange them on a surface in a layout to convey an idea.
0:47 The basic visual elements that combine to graphic design include the following:
0:53 Line, colour, shape, texture, space, form and typography.
0:59 Whatever work you produce be for a magazine a poster a website or an advertisement these visual elements will play a part in your design.
1:07 In this video I'm going to discuss the seventh key visual element and discuss typography as a visual element in graphic design.
1:15 In this video I'll be referring to some visual examples
1:19 If you wish to take a closer look at these, you can find them in the downloadable PDF document that accompanies the series
1:24 link is in the description.
1:26 So typography is one of the most commonly used elements in graphic design.
1:31 Type is the most direct way to communicate visually typically set either as headers or in paragraphs.
1:37 A single letter in a typeface is a combination of geometric and organic shapes which combined together to create a larger shape
1:45 in this case the letter R. This unique letter shape is an entity and is part of
1:51 a complete typeface made of other letter shapes. The characteristics of every typeface
1:56 can be broken down into what is known in design as the anatomy of type.
2:02 It's the type of shapes given to the anatomy of type in each letter that
2:06 gives the typeface its distinct look and feel.
2:10 Type is not simply about communicating words, words are important
2:14 but what can be equally as important is the style of those words the typeface
2:18 and the way they are arranged on a page. Typefaces can be part of a family of
2:24 various weights which can be used to express various tones or create visual hierarchy in the design.
2:30 Typefaces are like voices with accents and dialects
2:34 which have their own distinct pronunciations and characters
2:39 so typefaces don't just communicate literally they communicate visually
2:43 for this reason the choice of typeface used in the design is crucial to set the
2:48 intended look and feel, set a tone and add character to a piece of work.
2:53 Typefaces have evolved over time, the first type faces were designed to be practical
2:58 cut out of wooden blocks or cast in lead to work in a print press for mass production
3:04 Others have originated from various design movements
3:08 but what they all have in common is that they have all been created to fulfil a particular purpose
3:12 and express a particular look and feel. Today there are many categories of
3:18 typefaces such as serif, sans serif, slab serif, rounded, script, blackletter, decorative and abstract to name a few
3:28 with some sharing characteristics of various categories.
3:32 Design throughout history has celebrated the shape and form of type and has shown us that type
3:37 can be both functional and decorative. The way type has been arranged and the
3:41 relationship given to it to other elements in compositions has redefined the use of type in design.
3:48 So looking at the PDF here I have a few examples of functional and decorative typography.
3:55 The first set of examples showcase functional uses of type
3:59 all of these examples are used to inform and deliver a clear message.
4:03 The first example is a basic use of type justified and placed simply on a page as one block
4:11 this has a traditional feel with the use of its serif font.
4:16 The next two examples take the previous technique further
4:19 add more dynamics with more complex compositions, the examples show type in
4:24 columns and some type of elements have been increased and used in capitals to add contrast.
4:31 In the second example we have a mixture of type weights applied
4:34 to the header and sub headers to create contrast and hierarchy,
4:39 negative space has been considered to create structure and allow some type element to stand out
4:45 we also see some line elements that have been used to reinforce the visual
4:49 structure and layout with the headers and sub headers are used
4:54 the next example is simple yet dynamic, here we have a clean bold header with
4:59 justified type below in a block at a unique angle.
5:03 Overall this creates a nice contrast with direction and movement on the page.
5:08 The next example is simple and bold,
5:11 here we have a decorative font used for what could be a title or header of a magazine or booklet.
5:17 Under this we have a bold message capped into a space
5:21 sealed with two line bars top and bottom
5:24 the type has been carefully scaled to meet the same width of the top and bottom line bar.
5:30 Overall this feels bold rigid and strong, assertive.
5:36 In the last example we have a serif font with thick spines, thin cross bars with exaggerated serifs
5:42 this font feels quite classy and boutique
5:46 this has been used here in an interesting layout. Here we have some
5:51 larger line type to the top which defines the column widths of the type below
5:56 the smaller type runs alined left to each word in the larger type.
6:00 Here the contrast is not only in size but also in alignment which creates an overall dynamic layout.
6:08 So even though there are some creative aspects to these compositions
6:12 it is not enough to distract from the overall message
6:16 they only serve to complement and add sophistication to the message.
6:22 So the second set of examples showcase more creative uses of type. All of these
6:26 examples are used to inform but the priority is placed more on the creative aspect of the look and feel.
6:34 So the first examples show how print block
6:36 letters have been carefully chosen and arranged to look like a face.
6:41 Here not only are we looking at the type but also the shape and form and texture of the print blocks.
6:47 In the next example we have 3D degenerated type
6:51 unlike the functional 2D examples we have added form, shadow, perspective and depth to add visual factors which add dimension.
7:02 in the next example all emphasis is focused on the craft of the type
7:06 we are encouraged to see this as a piece of design art.
7:10 This is more about the beauty of the geometric shapes
7:14 the grid and the characteristics of the letter forms themselves.
7:18 Here the message is not what the type of saying but what the type of saying about itself.
7:22 So the next examples test the limits of order, alignment, legibility and contrast
7:29 here a lot of words pop out to us in no particular order. Due to the lack of order and focus
7:36 the types begin to merge together into an overall texture and shape on the page.
7:41 The next two examples take this to the next level
7:44 here we have almost no legibility of message to the point we stop looking for legibility
7:51 but get immersed in the hypnotic shapes.
7:54 These type compositions have become image textures and now we feel them as opposed to reading them.
8:00 So from a practical use to a more creative use.
8:04 In design type can be used to communicate literally and to tantalise the visual senses to engage emotionally.
8:11 So that is the seventh key visual element in graphic design.
8:15 Well I hope you enjoyed this video if you did hit the like button on my facebook page.
8:20 If you would like to see more videos like this in future hit the subscribe button
8:24 and you can also find me on twitter at TastyTuts.
8:28 In a previous video I mentioned that graphic design is made of two things:
8:32 visual elements and design principles.
8:36 over the past seven episodes we have been looking at the visual elements
8:40 now it's time to take a look at design principles. So over the next eight episode I'm going to be talking more
8:46 about design theory and talk in detail about design principles that make a piece of graphic design work.
8:53 So the key principles of design are contrast, hierarchy, alignment, balance, proximity, repetition, simplicity and function.
9:03 In the next video I'm going to start by talking about contrast as a design principle in graphic design.
9:09 So see in the next video!

0:00 Hello and welcome to this beginner's guide series to graphic design
0:04 from what graphic design is, skills to be a graphic designer, design theory, education you need, equipment you need, to the graphic design portfolio and interview advice.
0:15 This series is for anyone at any level.
0:18 So if you're interested in graphic design and considering becoming a graphic designer
0:22 join me as I discuss a series of graphic design topics.
0:34 Now graphic design is not simply about making things look good. In graphic design there are principles of design that can be considered.
0:41 These principles are what typically separate good design from bad design
0:46 these principles all have a relationship between each other and
0:49 appear in every well designed piece of work you see. A good grasp of design
0:54 theory or mean there is always substance behind your work.
0:58 The key principles of design are: Contrast, hierarchy, alignment, balance, proximity, repetition, simplicity and function.
1:09 Whatever work you produce be it for a magazine a poster a website or an advertisement the principles of design should be considered.
1:17 A good designer will keep these principles and guidelines in their toolkit and will
1:21 consciously use them to develop their ideas. In this video I'm going to discuss
1:26 the first key design principle and discuss contrast as a design principle in graphic design.
1:32 In this video I'll be referring to some visual examples.
1:35 If you wish to take a closer look at these you can find them in the downloadable PDF that accompanies this series
1:41 link is in the description
1:43 So contrast occurs when two or more visual elements in a composition are different. In design we use contrast
1:51 to generate impact, highlight importance, create exciting graphics and create
1:56 visual interest and dynamics.
1:59 Now context is integral to contrast. We may think that the chosen visual object in a composition says something about itself
2:06 but it is more often the visual elements around it that give it it's meaning.
2:12 For example here is a simple circle, what is it saying about itself?
2:17 Well all it says here is that I'm a circle,
2:21 but does it say how big it is? or how small it is? or how far away from us it is?
2:26 To suggest that we will need another visual element. So here is another circle this being a smaller circle
2:33 by placing this new visual object next to the original circle we now create contrast by context.
2:40 By contrast this is now saying that the original circle is bigger.
2:45 But what if I bring in another circle, this time we have another circle
2:49 the same size as the original but this time it's darker. Well this may suggest
2:54 that this circle is perhaps closer to us or more important, it certainly grabs our attention more.
3:02 So contrast creates interesting relationships between the visual elements, it can push visual elements away
3:09 connect them or complement them. Without contrast visual elements can be meaningless.
3:15 Contrast provokes our visual senses our eyes like contrast because it
3:19 grabs our attention and makes it easier to digest and make sense of what we are seeing
3:25 which is why it can be a strong method to communicate visually without the presence of type.
3:30 So looking at the PDF here I have a few examples
3:34 of contrast and how contrast can be used in design. First is contrast in shapes
3:40 here we have two shapes almost identical in scale but they both vary in characteristic.
3:47 One has a smooth surface and one has a pointed surface. Now what shape is your eye drawn too?
3:53 in this case it may be the shape with the more complex outline
3:57 the circle with the smooth surface is so simple it makes us want to look at the other shape more because it's more complex.
4:05 The next example demonstrates contrast in colour as well as shape. Even though we have a mixture
4:11 of shapes they appear in various degrees of colour. Regardless of contrast between
4:16 shapes here there is a clear contrast of colour or tone. The darker the shape the more attention it commands.
4:25 The next two examples showcase contrast in scale.
4:28 Here we have eight strokes but in different stroke sizes and a grid of circles
4:33 again we can see how context is integral to contrast here
4:38 its the visual elements around each other that give meaning to one another.
4:42 Next we have contrast in layout. The top composition appears regimented and structured
4:48 the below layout seems more free and random.
4:52 So next are some examples of contrast applied to type. In type contrast is commonly used to create hierarchy and structure
5:01 there are many methods for creating contrast in typography:
5:05 alignment, typeface, type size, colour and weight can be all considered.
5:10 In this next example we can see a word pop out from a block of type by simply being of a darker shade.
5:17 Next we can see contrast in type in a more dramatic way we have type in
5:23 various sizes weights and alignments. The type is mostly dark but there is some
5:28 white cutting through the dark solid bar and we have type cutting off and onto the page from top to bottom.
5:36 Next we have some similar type contrast but also contrasting with other visual elements.
5:42 Here the main focus is on the title word, it creates its own dynamic as
5:46 it crosses over from a light space into a dark space which our eye is mostly drawn too.
5:52 Now contrast is such a strong method of communication
5:56 it is used on some of the most important visual communication, road signs.
6:01 Here we can see how contrast is used to communicate bold simple messages.
6:06 Here we have contrast using colour to communicate hierarchy and importance .
6:11 This road sign suggesting to give priority to vehicles from the opposite direction.
6:17 In this sign we see a cigarette butt with a line through it
6:20 it suggests it's a bad thing, it's wrong, that we must not do it, we are not allowed to smoke in this area
6:28 So some examples of direct communication there.
6:31 Now some of the most iconic logos are some of the most simple and contrasting.
6:36 Just like how contrast plays it's role in road signs to communicate bold important messages,
6:42 contrast is used in logos for the same purpose of bold communication to be remembered.
6:49 So that is the first key design principles in graphic design.
6:53 Well I hope you enjoyed this video. If you did hit the like button on my facebook page.
6:58 If you'd like to see more videos like this in future hit the subscribe button
7:02 and you can also follow me on twitter at TastyTuts.
7:06 So the next key design principle is hierarchy.
7:10 In the next video I'm going to be talking about hierarchy as design principle in graphic design.
7:15 So see you in the next video!

0:00 Hello and welcome to this beginner's guide series to graphic design.
0:04 From what graphic design is, skills to be a graphic designer, design theory, education you need, equipment you need, to the graphic design portfolio and interview advice.
0:14 This series is for anyone at any level.
0:18 So if you're interested in graphic design and considering becoming a graphic designer.
0:22 join me as I discuss a series of graphic design topics.
0:34 Graphic design is not simply about making things look good.
0:39 In graphic design there are principles of design that can be considered.
0:43 These principles are what typically separate good design from bad design. These principles all have a
0:48 relationship between each other and appear in every well designed piece of work you see.
0:53 A good grasp of design theory will mean there is always substance behind your work.
0:58 The key principles of design are: Contrast, hierarchy, alignment, balance, proximity, repetition, simplicity and function.
1:08 What ever work you produce be it for a magazine, a poster a website or advertisement,
1:13 these principles of design should be considered. A good designer keeps these principles and guidelines in
1:19 their toolkit and will consciously use them to develop their ideas.
1:23 In this video I'm going to discuss the second key design principle and discuss hierarchy as a design principle in graphic design.
1:31 In this video I'll be referring to some visual examples. If you wish to take a closer look at these
1:36 you can find them in the downloadable PDF document that accompanies this series
1:39 link is in the description.
1:42 So hierarchy is the control of visual information in an arrangement
1:47 or presentation to imply importance.
1:49 Hierarchy influences the order in which the human eye perceives what it sees. In design we use hierarchy to:
1:56 Add structure, create visual organisation, create direction, add emphasis
2:02 and help a viewer navigate and digest information easily.
2:07 Hierarchy is typically created by contrast between visual elements in a composition.
2:12 Typically visual elements with highest contrast are noticed first.
2:17 Using hierarchy we can control how a viewer engages with information to ensure that
2:22 information is navigated and digested in the way it is intended.
2:27 For example where we want the eye to look first, where we want the eye to look second,
2:32 and where we want the eye to look third and so on...
2:35 Establishing clear visual hierarchy is important because it
2:39 holds the design together. Used effectively hierarchy can make a complex
2:43 message simple. In design hierarchy can manifest itself in many visual ways.
2:49 It's through the careful consideration and arrangement of visual elements that
2:53 create a clear hierarchy. Hierarchy can manifest itself in many visual ways such as
2:59 in scale. colour, contrast, space, alignment, shape and form.
3:05 So looking at the PDF here are some basic hierarchy design principles.
3:10 So the first example is hierarchy in scale. Here we have some stroke lines going from thick to thin
3:16 from top to bottom in eight steps. The hierarchy of scale in these eight steps
3:21 also suggests direction of hierarchy the flow of importance here starts from the
3:27 top and troubles down from thick to thin. Now the next example challenges this perception.
3:33 Here we have the exact same comp but this time the colour has changed.
3:38 In this example the thinnest stroke is the darkest colour and the thicker stroke is the lightest colour.
3:44 Even though the strokes are larger above the thin stroke is perceived as bolder and stronger
3:50 because it's more apparent and appears closer, more in focus.
3:54 by changing the colours we have changed the hierarchy structure.
3:58 Now it could be said that the hierarchy now travels from bottom to top
4:02 from thin to thick because the darkest colour attracts the eye first.
4:07 So this next example demonstrates hierarchy again in scale but also in contrast.
4:14 Here we see a grid of circles with a single small one
4:17 by contrast in scale and number this suggests that the larger
4:22 circles carry more important than the smaller one
4:25 Now if we modify the colour this creates a new dynamic and shifts the hierarchy.
4:30 The smaller circle is now darker. By contrast the smaller circle is now more prominent.
4:37 So in the next example we see hierarchy in colour again the shapes on
4:42 the inside are darker than those of outside so we perceive them as more prominent
4:48 tho if we change the space of these shapes. Through overlapping we now create a new hierarchy.
4:54 Hierarchy here is no longer determined by colour.
4:58 the shapes that are on top appear closer to us, are the shapes we see most clearly which are
5:03 perceived to be the most prominent. Hierarchy is now defined by space.
5:09 In the next example we have the same shapes in various colors but this time the shapes
5:13 are in various sizes with various blur effects applied.
5:17 This creates an illustration of depth unlike with space in the previous example
5:22 it's not necessarily about the shapes that appear closer to us here, but the shapes that appear the most clear to us.
5:29 So the shapes that are the most in focus do a better job of attracting the eye
5:35 thus become the most prominent. In this example hierarchy is defined by depth and focus.
5:41 So in this last example colour and alignment of shapes is suggesting perspective and movement.
5:49 Here we perceive the shapes coming closer to us.
5:52 The flow of hierarchy here starts from the perceived front and moves
5:55 towards the back into the distance. So those are some simple principles of hierarchy
6:02 Now in design depending on the simplicity or complexity of the intended message
6:07 hierarchy in design can exist in multiple forms
6:10 and compete against each other at the same time.
6:14 If we take a look at the next page in the PDF here I have some common practical design instances
6:20 where hierarchy plays a role. So for this first example I have a simple index page
6:25 here we have a lot of information
6:28 but through the consideration of alignment and space each subsection from the top
6:33 down takes a step to the right and there is adequate space separating the
6:37 individual parts. The header and sub header is a different and bolder
6:42 typeface to the body copy highlighting important breakpoints.
6:47 This all works well together to create a clear and comprehensive list easy to navigate.
6:53 Hierarchy exists here through contrast in alignment, space, colour and type face.
6:59 So next is an example of a simple type layout and some of the common settings
7:03 that are applied to distinguish hierarchy. Here we have a mixture of type alignment
7:10 typeface, type size, type weight and colour. Next is a more complex example of hierarchy.
7:17 For this example I have a letterhead. Now letterheads can come in
7:21 various designs but they typically follow the same pattern.
7:24 Here hierarchy is working in composition as well as typesetting. Letterheads will almost
7:31 always lead with a brand the logo is here in the top left looking pretty prominent.
7:37 To the right of this is the address details of the company in this instance aligned to the right
7:43 which contrasts nicely with the main message
7:46 So the top of the letterhead is pretty prominent here and communicates the
7:50 important info about the company details. Below is where the main message is placed
7:56 notice the margin here is quite large
7:59 this leaves for some nice negative space down the left side which creates a nice
8:04 clean design which also gives prominence to the logo.
8:08 Below this we have the details of who the messages is from and below this we have a footer
8:14 with some require detail. We have a small horizontal stroke to partition this away
8:19 from the message and the type size is smaller not to clash with the main message
8:24 and finally below this we have part of the brand motif as simple blocks of colour
8:31 So for a simple letterhead that is quite a lot going on here but through careful consideration of the
8:36 visual elements the order of importance is nice and clear.
8:40 So next is an example of a simple infographic.
8:45 Here hierarchy is working in two parts
8:47 it's working in the illustration and in the overall composition.
8:51 First the graphic above clearly illustrates a timeline or tree diagram.
8:56 We see the diagram starts from the base, in this instance the flow of information starts
9:01 from the bottom to the top from dark to light distinguishing the various branches.
9:07 Placed below this is a simple list which is a reference to the visual above.
9:11 So overall the illustration is the most dominant visual element here supported by the reference below.
9:18 So next is an example of where hierarchy starts to push the boundaries in more creative layouts.
9:24 First we have an index page. Here hierarchy exists in many forms
9:29 overall as small as the white type is compared to the title it still exerts more
9:35 prominence due to the contrast in colour. Here type is not treated as individual
9:40 elements but as one rigid column cutting through the loosely scattered title below.
9:46 Within the column itself we have a number of types sizes and weights which
9:50 creates contrast to distinguish order, the title is left as a playful
9:55 decorative piece in a darker tone allowing the column type to come to the front
10:01 Next we have a simple layout that could easily be a magazine article.
10:05 The composition immediately draws your attention to the large playful type layout, the article title wrapped around the body copy
10:15 Next we have a sub header and the body copy below. Contrast is created between
10:20 the sub header and the body copy by two different typefaces and sizes
10:25 and also by the right alignment of the sub header and the left alignment of the body copy.
10:31 Below this we have a small horizontal stroke signifying the end of the article
10:35 and signing off with the name of the person who wrote the article.
10:40 So the next example is where hierarchy starts to get really complex. Here I have an example of
10:47 a typical magazine cover and a newspaper cover this media is common for
10:51 communicating a lot of information in one instance.
10:55 hierarchy typically establishes itself on magazine covers through overlapping space and size
11:01 generally a magazine cover will revolve around one single main point of focus.
11:07 In this example we can see the image of the woman in the photograph is on top of the magazine title
11:14 then the header of the article which the photograph supports is on top of the photo.
11:19 So the three main important parts to this composition is: Big title, person in photo then magazine title.
11:28 Then we have some hints of what else is in the magazine
11:31 via sub headers around the figure in the picture.
11:34 We have a call out here in a circle, which compared to the other sub headers does draw your attention more
11:40 This suggests the magazine sees this article as something people want to read
11:44 perhaps to draw attention to buy the magazine. Some more prominence has been given to this article title here.
11:52 For decoration and dynamics the other titles have been applied in various typefaces
11:57 but don't typically compete too much with each other.
12:01 Next is the newspaper example.
12:03 Now unlike the magazine cover that will revolve around one single main point of focus
12:08 the newspaper will want to shout about multiple headlines at once.
12:13 Hierarchy typically establishes itself on newspaper covers through contrast in scale.
12:19 Typically on newspaper covers the most prominent article will exert the most space on the page
12:25 where less prominent articles will sit next to or around it
12:29 in this example we see the main prominent article taking up most of the
12:33 space, big photo and big typeface the smaller articles are sitting around it.
12:41 So that is the second key design principle in graphic design.
12:45 When you look at design ask yourself how has hierarchy be considered?
12:49 Is there a sense of hierarchy? What visual mechanisms have been used to create it?
12:54 and how well does it work as part of the design?
12:57 Well I hope you enjoyed this video, if you did hit the like button on my facebook page
13:02 If you'd like to see more videos like this in future hit the subscribe button
13:06 and you can also follow me on twitter at TastyTuts
13:10 So the next key design principle is alignment.
13:13 In the next video I'm going to talk about alignment as a design principle in graphic design.
13:19 So see you in the next video!

1:45 So alignment is the placement of visual elements so they line up in a composition
1:52 In design we use alignment:
1:55 To organise elements, to group elements, to create balance, to create structure, create connections between elements
2:03 and to create a sharp and clear outcome.
2:06 In design there are two alignment principles: Edge alignment and centre alignment
2:13 Edge alignment either to the left or to the right to the top or bottom.
2:17 Centre alignment as it states is aligned to a centre line down the middle or cross the horizontal.
2:24 Alignment is often an invisible line visual elements are aligned to but can
2:28 also be hinted at physically. Alignment can be used to achieve a particular look and feel.
2:35 One should always be conscious when working with alignment to achieve the intended result
2:40 So looking at the PDF here are some alignment examples
2:43 where visual elements are aligned a composition can appear clear, confident,
2:48 elegant, formal and trustworthy. Good alignment is invisible
2:54 this doesn't have to be a literal line in your design. In design one should try
2:59 and avoid the appearance of having made arbitrary decisions. When visual elements
3:03 are out of align it is noticable and can devalue a piece of work
3:08 if done unintentionally.
3:09 However if mixed alignment is intended as part of a design it can appear more
3:15 radical, dynamic, free and playful. In design alignment can be simple or complex and
3:21 is commonly achieved with the use of a grid. A grid can create an invisible
3:26 structure the invisible lines that visual elements can be placed on these
3:31 grades can ensure accurate alignment and consistency in a large piece of design work.
3:37 Today grids are typically constructed in design software as a guide when layout is created on computer.
3:44 So here are a few examples demonstrating various approaches of alignment in design.
3:50 First we have a typical layout based on a grid of three columns.
3:54 Here we can see how the line elements hint at the grid structure
3:57 while the main title and image fits nicely into the two far right columns
4:03 aligned with the article copy below. Everything in this composition appears
4:08 well aligned neat and organized. So alignment is typically used to organize
4:13 and create a degree of structure as seen in the previous examples.
4:18 However alignment can also be used in more abstract ways as part of a visual message
4:23 or to add dynamics to layout.
4:26 The next example explores alignment both vertically and horizontally which
4:30 creates a nice dynamic on the page. Here the main title is aligned to the top of the page
4:37 the cap height of the header type is defining the column widths below
4:41 the lists below then follow down the page aligned to the left of the columns
4:46 defined by the header type above.
4:48 Next is a type composition exploring both
4:51 left and right alignment of type. The contrast between alignment in each
4:56 column creates an interesting dynamic on the page.
5:00 Next is another simple type layout. On the top we have a bold header and beneath this we have a column of type.
5:06 The justified type itself is aligned to the left but the column flows down
5:12 aligned along a diagonal spine.
5:15 This adds an interesting dynamic to the composition suggesting movement and direction on a page.
5:22 This next layout is a lot more abstract and attempts to challenge the
5:26 limits of alignment. Through the implementation of contrast in type size
5:30 weight and alignment a more radical and freestyle composition is achieved here.
5:36 This last example is another abstract composition. The alignments here seem to

5:58 in both practical and more abstract design.
6:01 So that is the third key design principle in graphic design. When you look at design ask yourself
6:07 how has alignment been considered, is there a clear structure that has been used?
6:12 What grid structure might have been used to create it? and how well does it work as part of the design?
6:18 Well I hope you enjoyed this video if you did hit the like button on my facebook page
6:24 if you would like to see more videos like this in future hit the subscribe button
6:28 and you can also follow me on twitter at TastyTuts
6:31 So the next key design principle is balance.
6:34 In the next video I'm going to talk about balance as a design principle and graphic design.
6:40 So see you in the next video!

0:00 Hello and welcome to this beginner's guide series to graphic design.
0:04 From what graphic design is, skills to be a graphic designer, design theory, education you need, equipment you need to the graphic design portfolio and interview advice.
0:15 This series is for anyone at any level.
0:18 So if you're interested in graphic design and considering becoming a graphic designer
0:22 join me as I discuss a series of graphic design topics.
0:34 Now graphic design is not simply about making things look good
0:38 in graphic design there are rules that could be considered
0:41 these rules are called the principles of design and these are typically separate
0:45 good design from bad design. These principles all have a relationship
0:50 between each other and appear in every well designed piece of work you see.
0:55 A good grasp of design theory will mean there is always substance behind your work.
0:59 The key principles of design are: Contrast, hierarchy, alignment, balance, proximity, repetition simplicity and function.
1:09 Whatever work you produce be it for a magazine, a poster, a website or advertisement
1:15 the principles of design should be considered. A good designer keeps these principles as guidelines in
1:20 their toolkit and will consciously use them to develop their ideas.
1:25 In this video I'm going to discuss the fourth key design principle and discuss balance
1:31 as a design principle in graphic design. In this video I'll be referring to some
1:35 visual examples if you wish to take a closer look at these you can find them
1:39 in the downloadable PDF document that accompanies this series
1:44 link is in the description.
1:46 So balance is the visual weight of elements in a composition
1:50 We use balance to add stability, add structure, create emphasis and
1:55 to create dynamics. In design one will attempt to place visual elements in an
2:00 aesthetically pleasing arrangement or particular arrangement to fulfil a purpose
2:05 or achieve a particular look and feel. In design there are three main types of balance:
2:11 Symmetrical balance, asymmetrical balance and radium balance.
2:16 Understanding these three types of balance will help achieve the right type of visual effect in your design.
2:22 So looking at the PDF, here are some examples.
2:25 First I have some symmetrical balance examples. So symmetrical balance is
2:30 mirror image balance. If you draw a line down the center of the page all the
2:35 visual elements on one side of the screen are mirrored on the other side
2:39 they don't have to be identical visual elements but can be similar in number
2:43 colour or shape and scale. When visual elements are equal weight they are in balance.
2:50 Symmetrical balance can be used when one wishes to achieve formal design,
2:54 a sense of structure, a sense of organisation and stability.
2:59 Next is asymmetrical balance. Now asymmetrical balance itself has nothing to do with balance.
3:06 The term is used to describe a kind of balance that is not identical on
3:11 both sides of a central line, not relying on symmetry, opposite of symmetrical balance.
3:17 Asymmetrical balance occurs when several smaller visual elements on one
3:22 side are balanced by a large visual element on the other side or smaller
3:27 visual elements are placed further away from the center of the screen than
3:31 larger visual elements. Asymmetrical balance can be used when one wishes to
3:36 achieve a more casual or less planned look and feel. An asymmetrical
3:41 composition can create a feeling of tension as if the page or screen may tip
3:45 for things might slide off the side. Asymmetrical balance is more dynamic than
3:51 symmetrical balance and normally keeps the audience's attention focused on the visual message.
3:58 Next is radial balance. The third type of balance is radial balance
4:03 where all elements radiate out from a centre point in a circular fashion.
4:08 It is very easy to maintain a focal point in radial balance since all the elements
4:12 lead your eye towards the centre.
4:15 So those are the three main types of balance in design. Depending on the intended purpose
4:20 or look and feel, a designer must sense which type of balance to execute.
4:25 A designer must sense whether or not a composition is balanced or not
4:30 success is using good balance can help achieve is strong visual effect and good
4:35 quality design. So that is the fourth key design principle in graphic design.
4:41 When you look at design ask yourself how has balance been considered?
4:46 What part does balance play to create the overall design? Is there a good or bad sense of balance?
4:52 and how well does it work as part of the design?
4:56 Well I hope you enjoyed this video if you did hit the like button on my facebook page
5:01 if you would like to see more videos like this in future hit the subscribe button
5:05 and you can also follow me on twitter at TastyTuts.
5:08 So the next key design principle is proximity.
5:12 In the next video I'm going to talk about proximity as a design principle in graphic design.
5:17 So see you in next video!

0:00 Hello and welcome to this beginner's guide series to graphic design.
0:04 From what graphic design is, skills to be a graphic designer, design theory, education you need, equipment you need to the graphic design portfolio and interview advice.
0:15 This series is for anyone at any level.
0:17 So if you're interested in graphic design and considering becoming a graphic designer
0:22 join me as I discuss a series of graphic design topics.
0:34 Now graphic design is not simply about making things look good
0:38 in graphic design there are rules that could be considered
0:42 these rules are called the principles of design and these are typically separate
0:46 good design from bad design. These principles all have a relationship
0:50 between each other and appear in every well designed piece of work you see.
0:55 A good grasp of design theory will mean there is always substance behind your work.
0:59 The key principles of design are: Contrast, hierarchy, alignment, balance, proximity, repetition simplicity and function.
1:10 Whatever work you produce be it for a magazine, a poster, a website or advertisement
1:15 the principles of design should be considered. A good designer keeps these principles as guidelines in
1:22 their toolkit and will consciously use them to develop their ideas.
1:26 In this video I'm going to discuss the fifth key design principle and discuss proximity
1:31 as a design principle in graphic design. In this video I'll be referring to some
1:36 visual examples if you wish to take a closer look at these you can find them
1:40 can find them in the downloadable PDF document that accompanies this series
1:44 link is in the description.
1:46 So proximity is the grouping and shaping of objects in a in composition.
1:51 In design we use proximity for two main reasons:
1:55 First to create connections. Proximity can create relationships between visual elements
2:01 in a composition, create relevance, hierarchy, create organisation and structure.
2:08 The second reason is to dispel connections. Proximity can also be used
2:13 to suggest no relationship between visual elements, to break organisation and structure.
2:19 By moving visual elements closer together or further apart we are
2:22 applying the design principle proximity. In design these two forces can be
2:27 applied in various degrees to help achieve a particular effect or outcome to communicate a message.
2:34 Typically in design related elements should be grouped together so that they
2:38 will be viewed as a group. Unrelated elements should have distance and should
2:44 not be in close proximity to each other. Audiences will assume that elements that
2:49 are not near each other in a design are not closely related.
2:54 A good sense of proximity in design can help differentiate visual elements to reduce
2:58 visual clutter and make design more comprehensible. Proximity is influential
3:04 to the design principal balance and hierarchy. Space between visual elements
3:09 will communicate a particular dynamic on a page. Depending on the intended purpose
3:14 or look and feel a designer must sense which type of balance to execute to suggest hierarchy.
3:20 So looking at the PDF. Here are some proximity examples.
3:26 So first is a simple layout design. Now audiences should never have to work
3:32 at trying to figure out which caption goes with which graphic or whether or not a
3:37 line of text is a subtitle or a line of text unrelated to the title.
3:44 Viewers should never have to work trying to figure out the connection of information in a design.
3:49 This makes for a poor user experience and an inability to digest information.
3:55 Here we can see the same message but laid out in various different ways.
4:01 We can see which one is the most comprehensible here.
4:04 In design one should try and avoid the appearance of having made arbitrary decisions.
4:10 When visual elements appear randomly or poorly positioned it is noticeable
4:14 and can devalue a piece of work if done intentionally.
4:18 When we begin to place shapes together we create a particular relationship between them.
4:24 In this example we have three individual shapes
4:28 if placed together with just the right proximity negative spaces is made
4:32 to suggest a new visual shape entirely. This gives new meaning to the individual
4:37 shapes that make this composition. If we move them apart ever so slightly this visual
4:44 this message is lost. Now from a young age we learn shapes and symbols that are
4:49 imprinted into our memory. The alphabet for example, these shapes and symbols are
4:55 used to communicate visually as a visual language or represent meaning.
5:00 When we think of all the symbols and logos we know so well it's through the
5:04 combination of shapes and proximity that works to imprint images into our memory.
5:09 If we take some of the most simplest well known shapes and alter their proximity
5:15 they no longer have their meaning and become something entirely different.
5:20 However simple or complex it's this relationship or lack of relationship
5:24 between shapes that can trigger feelings, convey messages, engage an audience,
5:30 add emphasis to a portion of the layout and create dynamics. Proximity is a powerful
5:35 principle to use in design. A good grasp and sense of proximity can be the
5:40 difference between good and amazing design.
5:44 So that is the fifth key design principles in graphic design.
5:47 When you look at design ask yourself
5:50 How has proximity being considered? What relationships has the designer created
5:55 or dispelled? How has proximity been used to create the overall composition?
6:01 and how well does it work as part of the design?

0:00 Hello and welcome to this beginner's guide series to graphic design.
0:04 From what graphic design is, skills to be a graphic designer, design theory, education you need, equipment you need to the graphic design portfolio and interview advice.
0:15 This series is for anyone at any level.
0:18 So if you're interested in graphic design and considering becoming a graphic designer
0:22 join me as I discuss a series of graphic design topics.
0:34 Now graphic design is not simply about making things look good
0:38 in graphic design there are rules that could be considered
0:41 these rules are called the principles of design and these are typically separate
0:45 good design from bad design. These principles all have a relationship
0:50 between each other and appear in every well designed piece of work you see.
0:54 A good grasp of design theory will mean there is always substance behind your work.
0:59 The key principles of design are: Contrast, hierarchy, alignment, balance, proximity, repetition simplicity and function.
1:08 Whatever work you produce be it for a magazine, a poster, a website or advertisement
1:14 the principles of design should be considered. A good designer keeps these principles as guidelines in
1:20 their toolkit and will consciously use them to develop their ideas.
1:24 In this video I'm going to discuss the sixth key design principle and discuss repetition
1:30 as a design principle in graphic design.
1:33 So repetition is the reusing of the same or similar elements throughout a design.
1:38 Now this is not to be mistaken for repetition of visual elements as a pattern
1:44 Visual elements as a pattern is more to do with visual style or visual
1:48 artwork in an overall piece of design work.
1:51 Good design practice seeks to repeat some aspects of a design throughout
1:56 a piece of work be it for a simple or complex piece of work.
2:00 We use repetition to create a sense of unity and consistency throughout a design.
2:06 Repetition creates a particular style and creative cohesiveness,
2:10 creates emphasis, hierarchy structure and strengthens a design.
2:15 Now the ultimate goal of any piece of graphic design is to make an impression,
2:20 hopefully a lasting impression. If a design achieve this goal it will be
2:24 fulfilling its purpose to communicate and insist upon the particular message
2:29 which lingers and becomes familiar. It could be said that repetition in design
2:34 is a type of visual brainwashing the more we see of something the more we
2:39 familiarise with it, thus remember it.
2:42 Whether we like it or not repetition is impressionable. It's human nature to find
2:47 comfort and attraction to familiarity. A good example of the use of repetition in
2:53 design is it branding. In any good brand there will be a consistent use of
2:58 a graphic style or language. This can manifest in many forms such as the use
3:03 of a particular colour or colour scheme, a consistent use of a typeface or set of
3:09 chosen typefaces, shapes and motifs, patterns, alignment,
3:14 photography style, tone of voice and so on.
3:17 Now this is not all done as a coincidenc. This is a carefully
3:21 orchestrated design to create a noticeable and memorable look and feel.
3:25 It is valuable to any business that their brand is impressionable
3:30 and memorable. The same can be said for presentations, leaflets and brochures.
3:35 It really pays to maintain a visual structure throughout to maintain focus
3:39 and consistency which adds value. Another good example is in magazines if you
3:46 flick through any well designed magazine you will notice a consistent style throughout.
3:51 This is done to create a particular user experience which you
3:54 will become fond of and want to experience again and again.
3:59 So that is the six key design principle in graphic design.
4:03 When you look at design ask yourself how is repetition been considered?
4:08 What elements have been repeated and how?
4:11 Is that clear consistency? and how well does it work as part of the design?
4:16 Well I hope you enjoyed this video if you did hit the like button on my facebook page.
4:21 If you would like to see more videos like this in future hit the subscribe button
4:25 and you can also follow me on twitter at TastyTuts.

0:00 Hello and welcome to this beginner's guide series to graphic design.
0:04 From what graphic design is, skills to be a graphic designer, design theory, education you need, equipment you need to the graphic design portfolio and interview advice.
0:15 This series is for anyone at any level.
0:17 So if you're interested in graphic design and considering becoming a graphic designer
0:22 join me as I discuss a series of graphic design topics.
0:34 Now graphic design is not simply about making things look good
0:38 in graphic design there are rules that could be considered
0:42 these rules are called the principles of design and these are typically separate
0:48 good design from bad design. These principles all have a relationship
0:52 between each other and appear in every well designed piece of work you see.
0:57 A good grasp of design theory will mean there is always substance behind your work.
1:01 The key principles of design are: Contrast, hierarchy, alignment, balance, proximity, repetition simplicity and function.
1:10 Whatever work you produce be it for a magazine, a poster, a website or advertisement
1:16 the principles of design should be considered. A good designer keeps these principles as guidelines in
1:22 their toolkit and will consciously use them to develop their ideas.
1:27 In this video I'm going to discuss the seventh key design principle and discuss simplicity
1:30 as a design principle in graphic design.
1:36 So simplicity is the discipline of minimising refining or editing back a design.
1:41 The ultimate goal of any piece of graphic design is to make an impression,
1:46 hopefully a lasting impression. If a design achieves this goal
1:50 it will be fulfilling its purpose to communicate and insist upon a particular message.
1:56 More often than not in graphic design keeping it simple works really well.
2:01 In design there is a general consensus that less is more, less is more striking.
2:06 We consider simplicity to ensure that a piece of communication has maximum clarity.
2:12 We consider simplicity to create balance and impact.
2:16 Simple design is easier to understand that is more likely to make a lasting impression.
2:22 For beginners it is easily assumed that simplistic design might not be good or interesting design.
2:29 However simplicity is recognised as adding a level of function,
2:33 elegance, consideration, premium and luxury to a design.
2:38 In graphic design it's harder to take away than it is to add
2:41 This is what separates amateur designers from professionals
2:45 and takes experience confidence and discipline.
2:49 One of the hardest and most skillful disciplines in design is knowing how to
2:53 edit a piece of design. When applying simplicity in design we should avoid an
2:58 overwhelming amount of visual elements. We should try and get across one strong idea
3:04 instead of incorporating many. When considering simplicity we should remove
3:08 or edit down information and details that are not needed.
3:12 The more simplistic your design the more striking and easy to understand it will be.
3:18 Too many things going on in a composition will make for a very confusing
3:22 and bad experience. Now simplicity is not about stripping a design down to the
3:26 point its soulless. It's also about harmony and balance between visual elements
3:32 In design you want to avoid visual competition and achieve visual harmony.
3:39 Complex visual elements can indeed work to communicate
3:43 a simple message. For example an image texture or a pattern texture
3:48 may be seen as complex but these work to suggest
3:51 a mood, a feeling, a notion. Coupled with a simple logo placement or bold message
3:57 this can be striking. When you think of some of the most prestigious brands
4:01 it's common to see simplicity set on a backdrop of complex beauty.
4:07 It's a striking dynamic which can also be seen in advertisements.
4:12 Often an add will only have a few seconds to communicate a message
4:16 so must be as striking and as easy to understand as possible.
4:20 Advertisements typically seek to charm you with some sort of
4:24 dazzling visual art coupled with a simple bold tag line message.
4:28 What is seen to be simple can often be complex.
4:32 This clever achievement in design is highly sought after and is regarded as
4:36 elegant, premium and luxurious. If this visual harmony is not executed well it
4:43 may lead to be busy and confusing message which will not fulfil its purpose.
4:49 Consideration of simplicity is often seen in good brand logos.
4:53 Poor designed logos can be too complex and busy with too many colours.
4:58 Some of the best logos are some of the most simple.
5:01 So that is the seventh key design principle in graphic design.
5:05 When you look at design ask yourself how has simplicity being considered?
5:10 Is their visual harmony or visual competition?
5:14 How clear is the design? and how clear is the message?
5:17 Could it be more striking? Is there anything you could change to simplify the design?
5:22 and what impression does it make on you?
5:24 Well I hope you enjoyed this video if you did hit the like button on my facebook page.
5:30 If you would like to see more videos like this in future hit the subscribe button
5:33 and you can also follow me on twitter at TastyTuts.
5:37 So the next key design principle is function.
