0:00 Hello and welcome to this beginner's guide series to graphic design.
0:04 From what graphic design is, skills to be a graphic designer, design theory, education you need, equipment you need to the graphic design portfolio and interview advice.
0:15 This series is for anyone at any level.
0:18 So if you're interested in graphic design and considering becoming a graphic designer
0:22 join me as I discuss a series of graphic design topics.
0:34 Now graphic design is not simply about making things look good
0:38 in graphic design there are rules that could be considered
0:42 these rules are called the principles of design and these are typically separate
0:47 good design from bad design. These principles all have a relationship
0:51 between each other and appear in every well designed piece of work you see.
0:56 A good grasp of design theory will mean there is always substance behind your work.
1:01 The key principles of design are: Contrast, hierarchy, alignment, balance, proximity, repetition simplicity and function.
1:11 Whatever work you produce be it for a magazine, a poster, a website or advertisement
1:17 the principles of design should be considered. A good designer keeps these principles as guidelines in
1:22 their toolkit and will consciously use them to develop their ideas.
1:27 In this video I'm going to discuss the eighth key design principle and discuss function
1:33 as a design principle in graphic design.
1:36 So function is the consideration of the main objective for a piece of graphic work
1:41 and how well a design is explored and executed to meet that end.
1:46 The ultimate goal of any graphic design is to communicate and make an impression
1:51 but what's equally important is that the right impression is made and the
1:55 intended outcome is achieved. Earlier in this series I said that I believe
2:00 graphic design is the unity of two opposing forces:
2:04 Artistic expression and practical application, design theory.
2:09 As a creative graphic designer these two forces will always been odds with each
2:13 other in your mind. When you can harness these two forces together in harmony
2:18 then you can create great visual communication. When we create a piece of
2:22 graphic design more often than not there will be
2:25 a specific requirement that will have to be fulfilled.
2:28 Depending on the complexity of the task that could be one or many requirements.
2:32 Now in design one typically gets that requirement in the form of a brief from
2:37 a client, either written or verbally. Before a designer starts any piece of work
2:43 there is key information that will need to be understood. This will ensure that a
2:47 designer is put in the best position to produce the right creative solution.
2:53 a good and thorough brief will include at least three key things.
2:57 An overview, requirements and an intended outcome.
3:01 The overview should introduce the idea of the project.
3:04 and what exactly the design is intended for. The overview should
3:08 clearly highlight the target audience the client wishes to address with any
3:13 other relevant information which may be of importance. The requirements will
3:18 outline exactly what will be needed creatively across print and digital media.
3:23 This could be anything from a printed poster, a brochure to a website.
3:27 The intended outcome should clearly state what the client hopes to achieve
3:31 with the graphic work how it wants people to respond to the work and how it
3:35 wants people to think, feel or act. A brief may go as far as to suggest what
3:41 is required visually, a particular style or colour scheme to be used or typeface.
3:46 A brief maybe creatively limiting or open to exploration. So a brief will set out all
3:52 the challenges and boundaries a designer will have to deal with in order to
3:56 create the right quick solution. It will be the details outlined in the brief
4:01 that will influence the creative solution. Now if a piece of design fails
4:06 to deliver on what the brief asks than it fails in its function. This is where
4:11 form verses function often comes into play.
4:14 For beginners it is easily assumed that simplistic practical design
4:18 might not be good or interesting. So form, how good something looks
4:23 may be seen as a priority.
4:25 However in professional design it is understood that form follows function.
4:30 That priority should always be given to function over form, how good something looks.
4:36 During research, development and design one must always take into account
4:40 the function of the design. One of the most important things you can do is
4:45 understand your audience and the required goal.
4:49 Considering language, colour and layout
4:51 will enable you to communicate well and engage with a particular audience
4:55 to encourage the intended result. As a rule you can always
4:58 ask yourself the question why?
5:01 Why am I using that colour? Why am I using a typeface?
5:05 Why am I arranging my elements in this way?
5:08 Why am I using these shapes photos and soon on.
5:11 Is it adding anything to the piece of design or is it distracted, misleading,
5:16 thus taking away. Always ask yourself why and have a good rationale for each decision.
5:23 In design one should always try and avoid the appearance of having made
5:27 arbitrary decisions. If noticeable this can devaluate a piece of work.
5:32 However limited a brief or however creative a brief whatever is done remember
5:36 to keep in mind the function and be sure it delivers on its function.
5:41 A good designer will always strive to achieve a harmony in beauty and function.
5:47 So that is the eighth key design principle graphic design.
5:51 When you look at design ask yourself, how well does it function?
5:55 does it get across the intended message?
5:58 Could it be more striking? How clear is the message?
6:01 and how clear is the design?
6:03 Is there anything you would do to change the design?
6:07 and what impression does it make on you?
6:09 Well I hope you enjoyed this video if you did hit the like button on my facebook page.
6:14 If you would like to see more videos like this in future hit the subscribe button
6:18 and you can also follow me on twitter at TastyTuts.
6:22 So up until now I have been discussing design theory and the
6:25 essential things to know about graphic design. Now it's time to move into section two.
6:31 Now if your considering a career in graphic design
6:34 and wonder if it's right for you. In the next section I'll be answering a bunch of
6:37 key questions. So for the first video in section two I'm going to ask the question:

0:00 Hello and welcome to this beginner's guide series to graphic design.
0:04 From what graphic design is, skills to be a graphic designer, design theory, education you need,
0:10 equipment you need to the graphic design portfolio and interview advice.
0:15 This series is for anyone at any level.
0:18 So if you're interested in graphic design and considering becoming a graphic designer
0:22 join me as I discuss a series of graphic design topics.
0:34 So you're wondering if you want to focus your
0:36 creative energy into a career in graphic design. In this video I'm going to
0:40 discuss 10 key pros and cons of graphic design, being a designer and becoming a designer.
0:47 Hopefully this will help you get a good overall understanding.
0:50 So first let's talk about some pros. So number one: It's creative
0:56 If you're a person who loves being creative graphic design is a good career choice for you.
1:01 On a day to day basis you will be problem solving, coming up with cool
1:06 inventive solutions and creating a range of graphics and design ideas.
1:10 Number two: It's fun. Graphic design can be really hard work but at the same time it
1:17 can be really fun bringing your imagination to life and being creative.
1:21 Number three: It's challenging. Graphic design is by no means easy you're pretty
1:27 much problem solving most of the time. So to produce great work you will have to push yourself,
1:33 harness your skills, be resourceful, tackle complex briefs and be able to
1:37 produce great results swiftly. Number four: Its educational.
1:43 to produce original and exciting work
1:45 you're going to have to do a lot of research. Often you will find
1:49 yourself learning and discovering things you didn't previously know. Getting paid
1:53 to research and learn how cool is that!
1:56 Number 5 includes multiple creative disciplines.
2:01 There is no limit to the techniques you can use to create your work.
2:04 To name a few:
2:06 Illustration, photography, painting, screen printing, digital art, 3D modeling,
2:12 paper engineering the list goes on. One of the great things about graphic design
2:16 is that it can be diverse and you don't have to be tied to one medium.
2:22 Number six: Something to show your hard work.
2:25 So you may spend hours days or weeks working on a particular project.
2:29 At the end of it you will most likely have something to show for it.
2:33 Over time you will build a portfolio of work
2:36 you can show off and be proud of.
2:38 This will ultimately help you get more work or
2:41 a better job in future. Number seven: Expressive.
2:45 Design is a discipline. In the industry you
2:48 will not always have the freedom to be
2:50 as creative as you want.
2:52 However there will be opportunities whether your creating work
2:55 for yourself or for a client where you will have creative freedom.
2:59 On these occasions you can be
3:01 really creative with your ideas and create some work that you feel justifies
3:05 you as a designer that you can be proud of.
3:08 Number eight: Rewarding.
3:10 Being a graphic designer can be really hard work but
3:13 you really get out what you put in. If you do a great job and your client is
3:18 happy that can be really rewarding. You may enter a design competition and win
3:22 some recognition. That can be really rewarding. If you create a piece of
3:26 design like a logo that gets used to add marketing value to a client and
3:31 improve their business that can be really rewarding.
3:35 Building a great portfolio and getting a new job
3:38 and earning more revenue very rewarding.
3:41 Number nine: Collaborative.
3:43 As a designer you will be required to work alone.
3:46 but you may often have to work with other
3:48 creative professionals as part of a team.
3:51 It can be a great experience and really rewarding working with other passionate people.
3:56 Collaboration can lead to amazing work. Everyone sees things differently,
4:00 when you're bouncing ideas off another designer
4:03 some amazing ideas can be generated.
4:07 Number ten: Career prospects.
4:09 As you mature as a designer build your experience and reputation.
4:13 You can move into more senior design roles and look
4:16 to become maybe, an art director or a creative director.
4:20 You may wish to become freelance and work for yourself
4:23 or maybe start your own creative business.
4:25 In graphic design there are a lots of opportunities
4:28 to further your career.
4:29 So a lot of pros to being a graphic designer there.
4:32 On a whole graphic design is a really interesting
4:35 and fulfilling career to get into.
4:38 But like any job it's not all perfect.
4:41 So now let's talk about some cons to be a graphic designer.
4:45 So number one: Location specific. If you want to become a graphic designer
4:50 you will have to accept the prospect of having to move where the industry is and
4:54 today that's typically a city. Graphic design is not all commercial work but
4:59 a lot of jobs and a lot of well paid jobs exist in cities. So to get work and
5:04 start out you may have to move to where the work is.
5:07 Number two: Competitive.
5:10 Graphic design is very fulfilling creative and it's fun you are basically
5:14 being paid to be creative. Because of that a lot of people want to do it which
5:19 means there's a lot of competition. When starting out and looking for jobs it can
5:24 be really tough. You'll be up against lots of other candidates
5:27 which may often have more experience than you.
5:30 Number three: The road is long.
5:33 A lot of people pursue graphic design but not
5:35 everyone becomes a graphic designer or stays a designer. The path to become a
5:40 successful graphic designer can be a long and expensive one.
5:43 Unfortunately today it's not all about talent and skill, it's as much about
5:48 attitude, discipline, application and determination. Everyone's path is
5:53 different some take longer than others to get to where they want to be.
5:57 Though if one stays highly motivated disciplined and applies themselves they
6:02 will eventually achieve. The industry is competitive and one will have to keep
6:08 learning and building a better portfolio if one wishes to progress and succeed.
6:13 This may mean having to sacrifice a lot of time to achieve this.
6:17 Number four: Learning creative software tools.
6:21 To work in the design industry you will need to
6:24 be proficient in essential creative programs. There are lots of software tools
6:28 out there today and each one has a steep learning curve.
6:33 Number five: Finding the right job can be hard.
6:36 In the industry finding a job can be tough
6:39 but what can be even harder is finding the right job.
6:42 The job where you will be happy.
6:43 The job where you will get creative satisfaction. The job where you will
6:47 potentially develop your career. Some job vacancies can be pretty vague
6:52 it is only when you attend an interview you can get a good vibe for the job you are
6:56 applying for. It can be disheartening attending interviews only to learn
7:01 it's not right for you. More often than not until you are working in that role for that
7:05 agency or company you will never truly know what your role will be.
7:10 So finding the right role can be hard especially when starting out.
7:14 Number six: It's a desk job.
7:17 One of the biggest downsides to graphic design is that most
7:20 of the time you will be seated at a desk in front of a computer for long hours
7:25 so you will need to get that gym membership!
7:28 Number seven: Long hours.
7:30 Graphic design is commonly not a nine to five job.
7:34 if you're working on a big project a pitch or you're facing a tight deadline
7:38 you may be expected to work long hours
7:41 even weekends to get a job. Now if you're lucky this may not
7:45 happen very often. But if you find yourself working
7:48 for a busy agency this may be the norm.
7:51 Number eight: Working under pressure.
7:54 You may not always have as much time as you want to fully research and develop
7:58 a piece of work or simply enjoy the job. There will be times when you will have
8:03 to work the tight deadlines. This can put a lot of pressure on you and be stressful.
8:08 Number nine: Have to do things you do not want and working under others instructions.
8:14 Working in the industry you will not always get
8:17 the creative freedom you want on a project.
8:20 Sometimes you may have to take creative direction from someone more senior to
8:24 you or even the client. This can be very good if the person is willing to
8:29 collaborate and has great ideas. But can be bad if they do not. Sometimes you may
8:34 find you don't agree or even like what is
8:37 asked of you but still you will have to do it.
8:41 Number ten: Dealing with bad clients.
8:44 As a designer dealing with clients can be
8:46 really rewarding or a bit of a nightmare.
8:49 They can either let you do what you think is best, work and collaborate with you.
8:53 Or simply try and make you do what they want and this can be pretty bad if they
8:57 do not listen to you, have no good ideas or any grasp of what makes for good design.
9:03 It can be really tough on a buzz kill having to implement someone
9:06 else's bad design ideas. More often than not working with clients can be a really
9:12 positive experience but they can be times it can be a nightmare.
9:16 So there you have it those are some pros and cons to being a graphic designer.
9:22 If you'd like to take a closer look at the list discussed in this video you can
9:25 find out more in the description and in the PDF document that accompanies this
9:29 video series. The download link is in the description.
9:33 So what do you think?
9:35 Are you put off or are you inspired?
9:37 For you current designers do you have any stories
9:39 to share of your progress? Be sure to share your comments below.
9:44 Well I hope you enjoyed this video if you did hit the like button on my facebook page.
9:48 If you would like to see more videos like this in future hit the subscribe button
9:52 and you can also follow me on twitter at TastyTuts.

0:00 Hello and welcome to this beginner's guide series to graphic design.
0:04 From what graphic design is, skills to be a graphic designer, design theory, education you need,
0:10 equipment you need to the graphic design portfolio and interview advice.
0:15 This series is for anyone at any level.
0:18 So if you're interested in graphic design and considering becoming a graphic designer
0:22 join me as I discuss a series of graphic design topics.
0:34 So your thinking about getting into a creative career.
0:36 With all the creative disciplines out there you're wondering if graphic design
0:40 is right for you? In this episode I'm going to suggest a couple of key
0:44 questions you can ask yourself. I propose these questions because your answers
0:49 will give you a good indication if graphic design is something you should consider.
0:53 By the end of this video you should be able to decide if graphic
0:57 design is right for you. If you can answer yes to all the following
1:01 questions then graphic design will be a suitable creative create for you.
1:05 However if you answer no to some of them you may want to
1:09 reconsider if graphic design is right for you.
1:12 So the first question is: Are you good at drawing? To be able to draw and sketch
1:17 you will be able to communicate your ideas visually not just to yourself but to others
1:23 which can be crucial in the ideas process. Sketching is a great method to
1:28 render your imagination fast and bring your initial ideas out of your mind to
1:33 rule out anything you feel may or may not work. Every designer should be able
1:38 to draw and sketch to some degree.
1:41 Can you communicate well verbally? Indeed graphic design is all about
1:45 communicating visually but it is essential that you can also talk about
1:49 your ideas. Sometimes you will need to sell your ideas at a presentation
1:54 to win that pitch, get that job or convince a client your idea is strong.
1:59 Can you present your work? Do you have the confidence to present your work to others?
2:05 How about a room full of strangers? There will be occasions where you will have to
2:09 present your work either to other team members, clients or at an interview.
2:14 This is an essential requirement
2:16 Do you like to research? A big part of the design process is research and discovery.
2:22 To produce interesting and original work a lot of time will need to
2:26 be spent researching. Do you like the problem solve?
2:29 Graphic design is not just about creating great looking images.
2:33 Graphic design can be extremely challenging requiring a lot of research,
2:37 understanding, thinking and development. Often you will have to create exciting
2:42 visual solutions to challenging design briefs.
2:46 Are you good at conceptual thinking?
2:49 One of the most attractive qualities any employer will look for is
2:53 the ability to think outside of the box. A potential employer will not only be
2:58 looking for high quality execution of your work but high quality of ideas
3:03 Great ideas will get you far in the industry.
3:06 Can you harness your creativity into realistic solutions?
3:11 A lot of work you will have to produce as a graphic designer
3:14 will need to be practical and functional. You will not always have the
3:17 creative freedom to do whatever you want. If you are an extremely passionate
3:22 artistically creative person you will need to be able to control it. To have
3:27 good judgment and discipline to produce a result that works best for the brief.
3:32 Can you work with limitations? There will be lots of occasions where you may not
3:37 have all the creative freedom you want
3:39 are you happy working under someone else's direction, to rules grids and
3:44 guidelines set by other designers. Can you pay close attention to detail?
3:51 To achieve high standards you will need to pay closer attention to details. In most
3:55 cases you will be responsible for your work. it won't reflect well on you if
4:00 there are mistakes or errors that could have been avoided.
4:03 Are you happy creating commercial work? Most graphic design work today is commercial.
4:09 Depending on the agency or company you work for you will often have to
4:13 create work for commercial clients. Are you happy creating work for commercial
4:17 application or would you prefer to create work for more
4:21 cultural or personal artistic achievement?
4:24 Do you have a passion for typography?
4:27 Type is one of the fundamental creative elements of graphic design.
4:30 There is a lot to be learned about the anatomy of type and how to use type well.
4:35 As a graphic designer you will need to have a keen
4:38 interest in typography and be able to apply it well.
4:42 Do you like working in teams? Are you people person or do you prefer to work
4:46 alone in your own space? As a graphic designer
4:50 you will have to be able to work with others on many levels be it collaboratively,
4:54 taking instructions or giving instruction. Can you take direction from others?
5:00 You often get the opportunity to work on your own design but there will be times
5:04 especially early on in your career where you will have to take creative direction
5:09 from a senior member of staff or client.
5:12 Are you happy to work to someone else's ideas or do you prefer to work your own designs.
5:18 Can you work under pressure? You will always have to work to some
5:22 sort of deadline and often this can be tight.
5:25 This will put pressure on you to produce good creative solutions to a particular
5:29 deadline. You will have to be comfortable working to a deadline and have good time management.
5:36 Are you happy working under pressure or do you prefer to do your own thing in
5:40 your own time. Can you work to a specific brief? You will most likely always have
5:46 to work to some kind of brie. You may have a certain degree of freedom within
5:50 the brief but you will have to create something specific that a brief will require.
5:55 Are you happy working to a particular brief or do you prefer
5:58 complete and utter freedom on what you create?
6:02 Can you take criticism? You will have to be comfortable taking criticism.
6:07 It's a big part of being a graphic designer. As you work with clients or
6:11 other members of a team you may often get all things about your work you may
6:15 not like to hear. Whether it's right or wrong people will have an opinion and
6:20 more often than not it will not be personal.
6:22 You will need to keep an open mind and be objective.
6:26 Are you prepared to work on a computer most of the time?
6:30 Graphic design is typically a desk job and you will find yourself
6:33 using a computer most of the time.
6:35 Can you see yourself being creative for the foreseeable future?
6:39 Graphic design can be real fun but it is by no means an
6:43 easy job. You will have to be creative on demand to a brief and to deadline.
6:49 This can take a lot of energy and determination.
6:52 Sometimes you may have to do things you do not like. So those are some key
6:57 questions you can ask yourself. If you would like to take a closer look at the
7:01 questions discussed in this video you can find out more in the description or
7:04 in the PDF document that accompanies this video series.

0:00 Hello and welcome to this beginner's guide series to graphic design.
0:04 From what graphic design is, skills to be a graphic designer, design theory, education you need,
0:10 equipment you need to the graphic design portfolio and interview advice.
0:14 This series is for anyone at any level.
0:18 So if you're interested in graphic design and considering becoming a graphic designer
0:22 join me as I discuss a series of graphic design topics.
0:34 So you're thinking about getting into graphic design and
0:37 you're wondering how to begin. In this video I'm going to discuss and suggest a
0:41 few things you can do to help you get started. To get started I would suggest
0:46 you undertake six key steps and I would recommend you do them in the following order.
0:51 Step one: Learn research, Step two: Collect, Step three: Be inspired,
0:58 Step four: Practice, Step five: Seek education
1:02 and Step six: Apply your skills.
1:06 Now these steps can be taken at any stage in your life if young
1:09 still in school and looking to get into graphic design you can follow these steps.
1:14 If you're older perhaps you're thinking of a career change
1:17 you can also follow these steps. In this video I'm going to talk about
1:22 Step one: Learn and research. To begin I would recommend you spend some time learning
1:27 and researching. At this point I would recommend you not only look to learn
1:32 aspects of graphic design but also seek to learn aspects of other art
1:36 disciplines. I would encourage this because graphic design can often
1:40 incorporate many aspects of creativity. As well as looking at graphic design
1:45 look at photography, look at advertising, look at fashion, look at interior design,
1:51 look at architecture, look at illustration, look at contemporary art, look at fine art,
1:56 look at sculpture and so on.
1:59 Learn how artists see the world and express themselves through their craft.
2:04 See how photographers capture the world and compose their images. Look at how
2:09 fashion designers use texture and patterns on the human form.
2:13 Look at how an illustrator tells a story in a graphic novel.
2:17 Look at how an architect shapes and construct a building. Look at how an advert sells
2:23 something with a single image, a headline a metaphor or an idea. Start look and
2:29 be inquisitive about everything and ask yourself: How does it make you feel?
2:34 What does it make you think? What does the artist or designer want you to think or feel?
2:39 and how do they do it? Look all around you. Look at magazines, observed
2:45 photography and layout, look at posters, look at leaflets and flyers, look at branding
2:50 and how a theme carries itself over various mediums such as print and digital.
2:55 Look at websites, see graphic design working in a practical everyday
3:00 sense and look at how graphic design is used in an artistic sense.
3:04 Observe what has already been done and what works and try and take it all in.
3:09 Be open minded to everything. Seek to ask the question why. Always ask yourself why.
3:17 Next I would encourage you start to learn about more specific aspects of
3:21 graphic design. Learn about typography, understand the anatomy of type the
3:26 terminology of type. Familiarize yourself with type classifications. Start to study
3:33 and questioned typography in the things you see, magazines, logos, books, business cards.
3:39 Look at how it's being presented. Look at the typeface or typefaces that
3:44 have been used and how they have been used together. Look at colour look at
3:48 composition and ask yourself why those choices were made. Next research some
3:54 well known and successful graphic designers. Look at the work that made
3:58 them famous and understand why the work was successful.
4:02 Next go online and look at a variety of graphic design agencies. See the work
4:08 they showcase to understand the type of work they do and what is out there today.
4:13 So by spending some time to learn and research, this process will give you a
4:17 good understanding of what has been done in the past and what is happening now.
4:23 This should help clarify your ambitions and hopefully help you focus on the type
4:28 of work you may want to do going forward. So this is the first step on how to
4:32 begin as a graphic designer.
4:34 Well I hope you enjoyed this video If you did hit the like button on my facebook page.
4:40 and you can also follow me on twitter at TastyTuts.
4:43 So in the next video I am going to talk about the next step, step two and talk about
4:48 how collecting your inspiration can help you begin as a graphic designer.
4:53 So see you in the next video!

1:41 Start to build yourself a digital and print scrapbook for my print scrapbook I use
1:47 an A3 card binder. If you find a piece of design you like, a cool piece of
1:51 typography, a leaflet, a flyer, a poster, anything try and get hold of it and keep
1:57 it in a folder. If you find a cool image on the internet try and save it on your
2:02 computer to a dedicated scrapbook folder. Collect photos: If you cannot take the
2:09 design example away with you take a photo.
2:12 Use your smartphone to document your findings.
2:15 I actually use my Instagram account as a scrapbook
2:18 I always take pictures of cool things I like which I can come back to later
2:22 and also share with others. Seek to collect what inspires you or what you
2:27 think works well. Keep in mind that you're not trying to copy what you see
2:31 but learn and seek inspiration from it. Every now and then look what you have
2:36 collected and allow yourself to absorb what you see and learn from what others
2:41 have done before and what works.
2:44 This will help feed your subconscious and fuel your imagination.
2:49 Collect your thoughts. You will not only see cool things but you will also think
2:53 cool things. Keep a sketch pad on you at all times. Sometimes cool ideas can hit you
2:59 anytime even when you're walking down the street. Be ready to capture these.
3:04 You never know where a quick idea and they lead. By writing down all your thoughts
3:09 this will allow you to clear your mind and think of other ideas.
3:13 Collect books. If you are able to start, to purchase creative books try and buy a book a month.
3:18 Now I would recommend that they are not just about design try books on
3:23 photography, science, technology, illustration, fine art, web design,
3:28 typography and so on. The idea here is creative variety. Try and build your own
3:35 creative collection, surround yourself with it, as it can all be a great source
3:40 of inspiration to get your creative juices flowing and improve your design
3:44 theory and technical knowledge. In a future episode I will attempt to
3:49 recommend some good books you can read to help you get started. As well as

0:00 Hello and welcome to this beginner's guide series to graphic design.
0:04 From what graphic design is, skills to be a graphic designer, design theory, education you need,
0:10 equipment you need to the graphic design portfolio and interview advice.
0:15 This series is for anyone at any level.
0:18 So if you're interested in graphic design and considering becoming a graphic designer
0:22 join me as I discuss a series of graphic design topics.
0:34 So you're thinking about getting into graphic design and
0:37 you're wondering how to begin. In this video I'm going to discuss and suggest a
0:42 few things you can do to help you get started. To get started I would suggest
0:46 you undertake six key steps and I would recommend you do them in the following order.
0:51 Step one: Learn research, Step two: Collect, Step three: Be inspired,
0:57 Step four: Practice, Step five: Seek education
1:01 and Step six: Apply your skills.
1:04 Now these steps can be taken at any stage in your life if young
1:08 still in school and looking to get into graphic design you can follow these steps.
1:12 If you're older perhaps you're thinking of a career change
1:15 you can also follow these steps. In this video I'm going to talk about step three:
1:20 Be inspired.
1:21 Now I believe that inspiration is the strongest motivational force there is.
1:26 An inspired person is a motivated person. A motivated person is a creative person.
1:33 A good designer will always be seeking inspiration to fuel one's imagination
1:36 and look for creative opportunities. To stimulate your imagination and inspire
1:42 your creative career I would recommend a range of sources. So the first
1:47 inspirational source is: Found objects. So this is quite possibly the easiest and
1:52 most cost effective source of inspiration. In the last episode I spoke
1:56 about collecting everything you find interesting and keeping it in a print or
2:00 digital scrapboo. By keeping your found objects you will be able to revisit them
2:05 in future. Found objects are free sources of inspiration.
2:09 Next is: Books and subscriptions.
2:13 In the previous episode I also mentioned that a good
2:15 habit is to collect interesting books to begin your own library.
2:20 I would also recommend that they are not just about design try books on
2:24 photography, science, technology, illustration ,fine art, web design
2:30 typography and so on. The idea here is variety. In a future episode I will
2:36 attempt to recommend some good books you can read to help you get started. As well
2:41 as collecting books you can subscribe to monthly creative magazines.
2:45 These are great because they will keep you up to date with what's going on in
2:48 the creative industry. Like with found objects you can refer back to these in
2:53 future when you're in need of some exploration. If you're not able to
2:57 purchase your own books or have subscriptions then you can always visit
3:01 your local library. Next is: Online inspiration. From google image searches
3:07 to stock image web sites to creative blogs that publish every day,
3:12 to hundreds of free video tutorials.
3:14 Today the internet is one of the richest sources of inspiration and it's all at
3:19 your fingertips. Build yourself a good bookmarks library and visit your sources frequently
3:25 Next is: Everyday inspiration.
3:28 Now there is a lot that can be read in books, seen and learned on the internet.
3:32 But I would also recommend you get outside. There is nothing more refreshing
3:36 than stepping away from your desk and clearing your head. Look and observe the
3:42 world around you. See graphic design in an everyday practical context. See packaging
3:47 and its design, see magazines, see interior design and shops and stores,
3:53 see street signs and shop signs, see advertisements and see Branding and fashion.
3:58 As you see practical design start to question it.
4:02 Don't just accept things for the way they are. Ask yourself why.
4:07 Why was it presented like it was? Try and understand.
4:10 Ask yourself how do you feel? How was the experience?
4:14 Good, bad? What did the designer want you to think or feel? and how did they do it?
4:19 and did they do it well? Take pictures and make notes.
4:23 Next is: Museums galleries and exhibitions.
4:27 Try and visit art galleries or museums as
4:29 often as you can and this does not have to be about graphic design.
4:33 This could be about anything.
4:35 Try and see graphic design working in a creative context. See how an exhibit is
4:40 presented. Look at what has been used to present that exhibition. Study the
4:44 installation aspect of the presentation. Study the print aspect of the
4:49 presentation and study the digital aspect. If possible think about the
4:54 presentation and experience as a whole then ask yourself why? Why was it
4:59 presented like it was and try to understand.
5:02 Ask yourself how do you feel? How was the experience? and what did the artist or
5:07 designer want you to think? and how did they do it? Again take pictures and notes.
5:12 Next is: Creative agencies. To learn more about the industry and see what type of
5:18 work has been produced you only have to search online for some good design
5:22 agencies. This will give you a good insight and inspire you
5:26 of which direction you may want to take and what type of the agency would like
5:30 to work with. This will give you a good insight and inspire you
5:34 of which direction you may want to take and what type of agency you would like
5:38 to work in. Agencies often post up job vacancies. Here you can learn what design
5:44 agencies look for and what it takes to work in the design industry as a graphic designer.
5:50 Next is: Local colleges or universities. Now look out for open days for drop in
5:56 days at your local college or university. A lot of art schools have end of your
6:01 shows. These are great opportunities to see what creative courses are an offer
6:05 and what kind of work students produce. Seeing some of the work will get you
6:10 excited, inspired and interested.
6:13 Next is: People. So if you are attending exhibitions, galleries, local colleges,
6:19 universities and looking up design agencies. There will be a good chance you'll
6:24 get the opportunity to talk to some really interesting people.
6:27 If you get the chance to visit any local colleges or universities try and talk to
6:32 design teachers and tutors. They will often have good advice about design
6:36 education and ways into the creative industry.
6:39 If you don't get the chance to attend any open days then you
6:42 can always try and email your questions. If you can try and chat to students,
6:47 asked questions and seek to learn about their experiences on creative courses,
6:52 what they got out of it and what their plans are.
6:55 If you decide to drop into local design agencies try and talk to people who are
7:00 already in the industry. Ask questions and seek to learn about their experience
7:05 as a designer working in industry. There is a lot to be learned from creative
7:10 people and their stories can be inspiring. So the last inspiration source
7:14 I have here is: Literature. Now try and read as much as you can.
7:19 Literature stimulates the imagination which can trigger some really cool
7:23 creative ideas. Read how authors use words to describe and tell a story.
7:29 Expand your vocabulary. This will help you express yourself. So that's the third
7:34 step on how to begin as a graphic designer.
7:37 Well I hope you enjoyed this video If you did hit the like button on my facebook page.
7:42 If you would like to see more videos like this in future hit the subscribe button

0:00 Hello and welcome to this beginner's guide series to graphic design.
0:04 From what graphic design is, skills to be a graphic designer, design theory, education you need,
0:10 equipment you need to the graphic design portfolio and interview advice.
0:14 This series is for anyone at any level.
0:18 So if you're interested in graphic design and considering becoming a graphic designer
0:22 join me as I discuss a series of graphic design topics.
0:31 So you're thinking about getting into graphic design and
0:37 you're wondering how to begin. In this video I'm going to discuss and suggest a
0:42 few things you can do to help you get started. To get started I would suggest
0:46 you undertake six key steps and I would recommend you do them in the following order.
0:51 Step one: Learn research, Step two: Collect, Step three: Be inspired,
0:57 Step four: Practice, Step five: Seek education
1:01 and Step six: Apply your skills.
1:04 Now these steps can be taken at any stage in your life if young
1:08 still in school and looking to get into graphic design you can follow these steps.
1:12 If you're older perhaps you're thinking of a career change
1:15 you can also follow these steps. In this video I'm going to talk about step four: Practice.
1:22 So as you expose yourself to as much creativity as you can.
1:26 Next it will be time to start to practice and nurture your skills. First I
1:31 would strongly recommend you practice your drawing. This is the fundamental
1:35 skill in any creative career. Drawing is the basic skill of expression and
1:40 communication. Drawing is the essence of design. If you can bring your thoughts
1:45 and ideas from your mind and project them onto paper you can start to
1:49 practice the design process. If you can nurture your skill in drawing it's really
1:54 going to help you in future. So get a sketch pad or layout pad and start
1:59 sketching. Take it everywhere you can and be spontaneous. Consider using mixed media
2:05 use pencils, pens, crayons, marker pens or paint. For this I would recommend
2:12 a broad range of subject matter. Observation and still life drawing:
2:17 Draw a bowl of fruit. Draw your pet. Draw a portrait of a friend. Draw the tree a
2:22 flower in your garden. I recommend this as it will help build your expressive
2:27 Freestyle drawing skills. Copy what you see.
2:32 Draw your favorite comic book character. Find a building or picture of a building
2:36 and draw it.
2:38 Pick a typeface you like type out your name and draw your name in that font.
2:42 This will help build your technical drawing skills. So drawing is something
2:49 you can be practicing all the time. I have been practicing graphic design now
2:53 for over 10 years and I still try and draw as much as I can. So next I would
2:58 recommend you begin practicing your graphic design.
3:02 Once you're consciously learning from creativity your observing, your reading
3:06 creative books, you're collecting things you like, you're seeking inspiration and
3:10 you're practicing your drawing,
3:12 it's time to put what you have learned into practice and have a go at a small
3:16 design exercise. Now if you're a complete beginner to design I recommend you start
3:21 with a very simple and fun exercise to get you started.
3:25 So for this I would recommend you set yourself a mini brief. Now this could be
3:30 anything but for this video I'll suggest an example. If you'd like to take a
3:34 closer look at this mini brief you can find it in the downloadable PDF document
3:39 that accompanies this series. Link is in the description. So think of a local
3:44 coffee shop you like or make one up. 1. Design or redesign a logo for that
3:50 coffee shop. 2. Define some colours, a choice of fonts and create a theme, a
3:56 brand to go with your logo. 3. Take that brand and design a poster to
4:02 promote the coffee shop and include the logo. 4. Take the poster design which
4:08 should include your brand elements and design a menu for their coffee list.
4:13 So three things there. Now whatever you brief before you even think about
4:18 using a computer. First do your research, Seek your inspiration
4:22 and sketch your ideas on paper first. Do not focus on one idea. Sketch as many
4:28 various compositions as you can of the same idea. Once you have at least three
4:33 or four options of any idea.
4:35 Only then move onto the computer. So the next thing I would recommend you do is:
4:40 Practice your software skills. Once you have sketched out your design and you
4:44 know roughly what you want to do you can start to practice using software tools
4:48 to bring your design to life. For this I would recommend you use Adobe Photoshop,
4:53 Illustrator or InDesign. These are the key design programs to use. Learning these
4:59 software tools can take a while so take your time and enjoy it.
5:03 Stick with it and soon you'll get the hang of it. So that is one mini brief
5:08 idea I would recommend to practice your design process. Now to help you practice
5:13 your software skills further I would recommend you undertake an imitation
5:18 project. Find a post that you really liked or magazine article and attempt to
5:23 copy it on the computer. Study everything about it and see the creative decisions
5:28 the designer made. Try to imitate the design on the computer. You will not only
5:33 be practicing your software skills but learning about design principles.
5:38 Now another way to practice would be to undertake online briefs. Today there are
5:43 lots of websites that small businesses will go to to find designers from all
5:48 around the world to do various creative briefs. Now these sites do not require
5:53 any particular design qualifications. Here it is all about your portfolio.
5:59 You can subscribe to these, showcase your work, specify your rate and be in with
6:04 the chance to be selected. If selected this could be a good way to practice on
6:08 some small projects.
6:10 Well I hope you enjoyed this video If you did hit the like button on my facebook page.
6:16 If you would like to see more videos like this in future hit the subscribe button
6:20 and you can also follow me on twitter at TastyTuts.
6:23 So in next video I'm going to talk about the next step,
6:27 step 5 and talk about how you can build on top of your
6:30 foundation skills and seek education.
6:33 So see in the next video!

0:00 Hello and welcome to this beginner's guide series to graphic design.
0:04 From what graphic design is, skills to be a graphic designer, design theory, education you need,
0:10 equipment you need to the graphic design portfolio and interview advice.
0:14 This series is for anyone at any level.
0:17 So if you're interested in graphic design and considering becoming a graphic designer
0:22 join me as I discuss a series of graphic design topics.
0:34 So if you're interested to learn more about graphic design
0:37 the history of graphic design, design theory,
0:40 typography, good design practice and inspiration
0:43 there are a number of graphic design books I would recommend you invest in.
0:47 Now there are many books I would recommend you read here,
0:50 unfortunately too many to talk about in one episode. So to make it easy
0:56 I have created a list which you can find in the PDF document that accompanies this series.
1:01 Link is in the description. Here I have listed a range of books with web
1:05 links to find out more online. For this video I'm going to list my top ten books
1:10 for beginners and give the reasons why I would recommend you read them. Now I own
1:15 all of these books and have found them very insightful, a nice mix of design
1:20 theory and inspiration in this collection.
1:23 So before I begin I must stress that I have not been sponsored for this video
1:27 by any company or brand or publisher. All the following books
1:32 I'm about to list are what I use and would recommend.
1:35 So the first book I would recommend is: Graphic design a concise history,
1:40 by Richard Hollis.
1:42 So this is the best introduction to the history of graphic design I have read.
1:47 This was one of the recommended books on my reading list at university. Now I
1:52 would say that this book is by no means a casual read. This book covers a lot of ground
1:57 going into quite some depth of the cultural and technological developments
2:02 of graphic design across various parts of the world. If you're interested in the
2:06 history of design I recommend this book because after reading you will get a good
2:10 understanding of the evolution of design, the various design movements that
2:14 occurred in history and an appreciation of what design has become today.
2:19 This book is well organised by country and date and it's full of amazing visual
2:24 examples to observe and reflect on.
2:27 So the next book is: Graphic design school - A foundation course for graphic
2:32 designers, working in print moving image and digital media,
2:36 by David Dabner and Sandra Stewart.
2:39 So this is a great introduction and guide to the principles and practice of
2:44 graphic design.
2:45 This book is organised into two main sections. Principles and practice.
2:51 The first section deals with the fundamentals of design such as
2:55 composition, hierarchy, layout, typography
2:58 grid structure, colour and so on.
3:01 The second section put these basics into practice and gives information about
3:06 studio techniques and production issues relevant to a number of different
3:10 graphic design disciplines. At the end of this book it even goes as far as to
3:15 provide an overview of some of the different career choices
3:19 open to students entering into the graphic design field. So I would really
3:23 recommend this book if you're a beginner or student aspiring to be a graphic designer.
3:28 This book is packed with vital information.
3:31 After reading this book you will have a good working knowledge of design with a
3:36 broader view of the field of graphic design.
3:38 So my next book is: Graphic design rules, 365 essential design dos and don't,
3:45 by Stefan G. Bucher. So this is a great book for beginners packed with practical
3:51 advice with accurate and information rules
3:54 365 to be exac. I recommend this one because it's in an easy to read
3:59 format. A good read of this book will quickly bring you up to speed with some
4:03 common applications of graphic design.
4:06 You can pick this one up and put it down quickly and refer back to it easily.
4:10 So my next book is: Color design work book, a real world guide to using colour
4:16 in graphic design, by Adams Morioka.
4:20 So this is a great book for beginners to think about and explore
4:23 colour and get a good introduction to colour theory.
4:27 Besides being a very well designed book it's full of reference and inspiration
4:31 with lots of case studies supported with in depth commentary.
4:35 This book has lots to offer. So my next book is: Making and breaking the grid,
4:40 by Timothy Samara.
4:42 This book is an analysis of the construction and deconstruction of grid
4:47 based design. For a beginner
4:49 this is an interesting look at two concepts that will make you think about
4:54 structure and how to break the rules. As quoted on the back the book, in order to
4:59 effectively break the rules of grid based design, one must first understand
5:04 those rules and see them applied to real world projects. This book introduces
5:08 you to the idea of the grid and how to use it with lots of examples across
5:13 several decades with detailed information. Later in the book
5:18 you're introduced to the notion or freedom in design without the grid.
5:22 So my next book is: Thinking with type, a critical guide for designers, second
5:27 revised edition, by Ellen Lupton. This is a great book for beginners and students
5:33 who are interested in learning more about practical uses a typography across
5:38 web and print. This book showcases many typographic layouts from both modern
5:43 and classical designs which provide helpful lessons. I would recommend this
5:48 one because it's an easy read.
5:50 You will learn a little about the history of letter forms and there are
5:54 many examples illustrating some essential dos and don'ts.
5:57 So my next book is: Logo design love, by David Airey.
6:03 This is a great book for beginners who are interested in logo creation and
6:07 brand development. This book showcases in depth design process with to the point
6:13 practical tips and contains plenty of example logos with some analysis of how
6:18 they were made. This book will make you think more about the smaller details
6:22 when creating your logos and observing those made by others.
6:26 I found this one an interesting and inspirational read. So my next book is:
6:31 The graphic design exercise book, creative briefs to enhance your skills
6:36 and develop your portfolio, by Carolyn Knight and Jessica Glaser.
6:41 If you are looking to build your portfolio with some interesting projects
6:44 I would highly recommend this book. This book provides a series of challenging
6:48 design briefs that encourage your creativity across a range of genres
6:52 including logos, packaging, branding, identity, promotion, publication design,
6:59 music graphics and web design. Each brief lists the required materials and equipment
7:05 so you can pick and choose. This book offers useful information packed with
7:10 examples and things to take into consideration when working on a brief.
7:15 So a great book here to get you started creating your own work.
7:19 So my next book is: It's not how good you are,
7:22 It's how good you want to be, by Paul Arden.
7:25 So this book is not really about graphic design it's about creative thinking and
7:30 challenges the orthodox approach to work and established ideas. Written by Paul
7:35 Arden who has been an advertising executive for decades, in this book he
7:41 offers up some really interesting creative insights and motivational ideas
7:45 and ways of thinking. I recommend this book because it's a shot of inspiration.
7:51 This is a great little book you will find yourself reading over and over again.
7:55 A must for any creative collection.
7:58 So the next book is: Hegarty on creativity, there are no rules,
8:03 by John Hegarty. So my last book is again not a graphic design
8:08 book but another source of motivation inspiration and creative wisdom.
8:13 The writer, another great mind in advertising states that creativity isn't an occupation
8:19 it's a preoccupation. It is at the very core of what makes us human.
8:24 In this book John talks about the ideas that lie at the heart of creative thinking.
8:30 His message is always crystal clear and promotes the benefits of simplifying,
8:34 thinking boldly and being undaunted by challenges.
8:39 This book is a sharp and insightful reference about being creative,
8:43 definitely worth a read. So those are just a few of the books I would recommend you read.
8:49 They definitely helped me get a good grasp of design and inspired me creatively.
8:54 In fact I've only read some of those books quite recently. As a designer you
8:58 can never stop learning about your craft.
9:01 If you'd like to take a closer look at the list discussed in this video along

0:35 So you're thinking about getting into graphic design

0:42 between the age of 13 and 20,

0:47 If you're serious about working towards
0:48 a career in graphic design,
0:50 I would recommend you plan your education

1:24 my experience and opinions on your education options.

1:31 perhaps 25 plus,
1:33 you may not have the prior correct education
1:36 and you may be wondering what options are available to you.

2:00 Now, when I was 13 years old, I was good at art
2:03 and all I really cared about was being creative.
2:07 And not much has changed.
2:09 I realized I wanted to grow up
2:11 and do a career based on creativity.
2:14 Even then, I knew it was important
2:16 and I had to focus on something and apply myself.
2:19 At the time, I was very young
2:21 and did not know what creative careers you could do.
2:24 So after some career research,
2:26 I made the decision to focus my future on graphic design
2:30 as, at the time, I believed it best suited my interests
2:33 and potential skillset.
2:35 Since then, it's been quite a creative adventure.
2:38 Everything I have done since I was 13,
2:41 all my subject choices, where I went to college,
2:44 where I did my foundation course,
2:46 where I went to university, and all the jobs I have taken,
2:50 has led me to this spot.
2:52 And even though I am now into the industry
2:55 with over 10 years experience,
2:57 I'm still learning and trying to improve and succeed.
3:01 So the path to becoming a graphic designer
3:03 could be a long one and you could spend years learning.
3:06 In my opinion, there is no perfect combination of subjects
3:09 you can take to become a graphic designer.
3:12 Up until you reach university or art school,
3:15 everything you do should really be
3:17 about your personal aspiration,
3:19 learning, and creative development.
3:22 Up until your higher education,
3:23 you should keep it broad and enjoy the creative journey.
3:27 So the first question is,
3:28 what subjects should I choose at school?
3:32 As you grow up and undertake your studies in school
3:34 between the age of 11 and 16,
3:37 don't focus too much on graphic design alone.
3:40 If you know you want to be a graphic designer,
3:42 well, that's great.
3:43 But also take some time
3:45 to explore other creative disciplines.
3:47 At this stage in your life, you really want to be creative
3:51 and exercise and nurture your skills,
3:53 imagination and broaden your mind.
3:56 This will ultimately make you
3:57 a more interesting designer in future.
4:00 The subjects I would encourage you to do at this stage
4:03 is anything creative.
4:05 Subjects like art & design, music, sculpture,
4:10 English literature, poetry, design & technology,
4:14 and science.
4:15 Try and undertake subjects that will encourage your mind
4:18 to think creatively in various ways
4:21 and subjects that will encourage you to be hands on.
4:24 The work you produce here
4:25 and the qualifications you will receive
4:27 will qualify you for the next stage in your education.
4:31 So what should you do after school?
4:34 So after finishing comprehensive school, or high school,
4:37 I would recommend you consider going onto college
4:40 to continue your creative studies
4:42 and look for work experience.
4:44 As you start to attend college around the age of 16 to 18,
4:48 you will have to undertake
4:50 fewer and more specialized subjects.
4:52 Here you can start to focus on courses
4:55 that will lead you in the direction of graphic design,
4:58 but I'd still recommend you keep an open mind.
5:01 Choose courses like graphic design, graphic communication,
5:05 illustration, photography, fine art, or sculpture.
5:09 Undertake subjects that will continue to encourage your mind
5:13 to think creatively in various ways
5:16 and give you the time to focus on those disciplines
5:18 to develop your skills.
5:20 Now, as well as attending college,
5:22 I would encourage you to look for design work experience.
5:26 When you're at school, you will be mainly encouraged
5:28 to focus on your creative development and practical work.
5:32 In school, you will rarely be taught
5:34 lessons of the industry.
5:36 The sooner you can start to learn
5:37 about the design industry in real life, the better.
5:41 This will best prepare you for your future and inspire you.
5:44 If you can find a design agency
5:46 and apply to work there as an intern or part time,
5:50 even if they don't pay you
5:52 or get you involved in the design work,
5:54 seek to get involved in the research
5:56 and development on a project
5:58 or just be there to oversee and experience what it's like.
6:03 With regards to work experience,
6:04 I would recommend once you start this, do not finish.
6:08 Try and do as much work experience as you can.
6:11 Perhaps try a few agencies.
6:13 Keep active all the way through the following years
6:16 of your education, even up until your higher education.
6:20 So this, along with the work you will produce in college,
6:23 will reward you with qualifications
6:25 and result in your first portfolio,
6:28 which will prepare you for the next step.
6:30 Now, here in the UK,
6:32 after most people finish their academic studies
6:34 and achieve their A levels at the age of 18,
6:37 they can go into university.
6:40 Now, as a creative, you have a choice here.
6:42 To either go onto university,
6:44 or do what is referred to here in the UK
6:46 as a foundation course.
6:48 The foundation course
6:49 is a one-year intensive creative course
6:52 and is typically undertaken at universities or colleges.
6:55 To pursue further education
6:57 and get into university or art college,
7:00 it is highly recommended you do a foundation course
7:03 as some universities, art schools
7:06 will not accept those who have not done the course.
7:09 In 2002, I did my foundation course.
7:12 And for me, this was the most significant
7:14 and influential creative step I ever took
7:17 in my creative education.
7:19 Now, it's called a foundation course
7:21 because it's a special time for you
7:23 to mature as a person, explore your creativity,
7:26 and give you the confidence to make a decision
7:29 on what career choice you want to pursue thereafter.
7:32 Prior to this stage in college,
7:34 you may have been encouraged to specialize in your subjects.
7:37 But on the foundation course, you take a step back.
7:41 For about half of it, you're encouraged to explore
7:44 and create work across various disciplines,
7:46 such as fashion and textiles, photography,
7:50 fine art, sculpture, graphic design, illustration,
7:55 and more, depending on the course.
7:58 On the foundation course,
7:59 you begin to talk more about your work
8:02 in front of tutors and students,
8:04 you begin to attend lectures,
8:06 and are taught about art history, design theory, and so on.
8:10 For the second half of the course,
8:12 you will have to focus on one of the creative disciplines.
8:15 As you specialize in graphic design,
8:18 you will spend the rest of the year
8:19 building a portfolio of work,
8:22 which your qualification will be based on,
8:24 which will prepare you for your next step.
8:28 At this point, you will be ready
8:29 to enter into university or art college.
8:33 So do you need to go to university or art college?
8:37 Now, today it is debatable
8:39 if a university education is actually necessary
8:42 to become a graphic designer.
8:44 Some people say you do not need it, and others disagree.
8:48 I happen to think it's important for many reasons,
8:52 which I'll be talking about in depth in the next episode.
8:56 If you're fortunate to have the opportunity to go,
8:59 I would highly recommend it.
9:01 So as you start to attend university or art college
9:04 between the age of 19 or 20 plus,
9:08 you will have to choose a specific course,
9:10 or, depending on the art college,
9:12 you may be able to mix various subjects together.
9:16 For example, you may be able to do
9:18 graphic design with photography,
9:20 or graphic design with illustration.
9:23 This is where you will take anything from three years plus,
9:26 depending on whether you do it full time or part time,
9:30 to really specialize and study in graphic design.
9:33 Over the course of your higher education,
9:35 you will undertake a variety of creative briefs
9:38 that will challenge you to research
9:40 and develop your visual communication skills
9:42 across a variety of media.
9:44 You will have to present your work to a group
9:47 and talk about your work
9:48 and, perhaps for the first time,
9:50 receive actual criticism from your peers and your tutor.
9:55 You will have time to make mistakes and learn from them.
9:58 You will be encouraged to work in teams.
10:01 You will be encouraged to learn
10:03 some of the finer disciplines of graphic design
10:06 such as typography, color theory, design theory,
10:09 and learn how to use creative tools.
10:12 You will have time to talk one on one with a tutor
10:15 and get valuable feedback.
10:17 You will attend lectures and be expected to produce
10:20 a written dissertation on a subject of your choice.
10:23 At university or art college,
10:25 you are given access to opportunities and time
10:29 to mature and develop as a graphic designer.
10:32 At the end of the course, you should hopefully graduate
10:35 with a specialized portfolio of work
10:37 which will form the main basis of your qualification
10:40 and represent your talent and skill
10:43 which you will use to find a job.
10:45 This should be a symbol of all your years of study
10:49 and development you have gone through.
10:52 At the end of all this self-development,
10:54 practice and achievement,
10:56 you should hopefully be ready
10:57 to step into the wide world of the design industry
11:01 and seek to start your career.
11:03 So those are some things I recommend you keep in mind
11:06 in your creative educational journey
11:09 to best prepare you for a career in graphic design.
11:12 If you're between the age of 13 and 20
11:15 and you're currently thinking about
11:16 a career in graphic design,
11:18 I hope this video helps.
11:20 So what do you think?

Title: Do I need to go to University to be a Graphic Designer? Ep29/45 [Beginners Guide to Graphic Design]
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jw4sjBt0zK8&list=PLYfCBK8IplO4E2sXtdKMVpKJZRBEoMvpn&index=30&pp=iAQB

0:00 - Hello, and welcome
0:01 to this beginner's guide series to graphic design,
0:04 for what graphic design is,
0:06 skills to be a graphic designer,
0:08 design theory, education you need,
0:10 equipment you need,
0:11 to the graphic design portfolio and interview advice.
0:15 This series is for anyone at any level,
0:17 so if you're interested in graphic design
0:20 and consider becoming a graphic designer,
0:22 then join me as I discuss a series of graphic design topics.
0:35 So you're thinking about a career in graphic design,
0:37 and you're wondering if you really need
0:39 to go to University or art college.
0:42 In this video, I'm going to discuss
0:44 the pros and cons of seeking higher creative education.
0:48 In this video, I'll also be sharing
0:50 my experience and opinions on higher education,
0:53 why I think it's important,
0:55 and why I would encourage
0:56 anyone who has the opportunity to do it.
0:59 Now, today it is debatable
1:01 if university education is actually necessary
1:04 to become a graphic designer.
1:06 Some people say you do not need it, and others disagree.
1:11 Anyone seeking a career in creativity
1:13 will undertake their own creative journey,
1:15 to which I believe there is no right or wrong path.
1:19 I do believe that you can make a career for yourself
1:22 in graphic design, and become successful,
1:24 without going to university.
1:26 However, having said that,
1:28 I do believe that higher education is extremely important.
1:32 If you're fortunate to have the opportunity to go,
1:34 I would highly recommend it for many reasons,
1:37 of which I will be talking about later in this episode.
1:40 So to begin, I'm going to start
1:42 by talking about the cons
1:43 to going to university or art college.
1:46 So this first con is probably the most significant,
1:49 and that is cost and debt
1:52 of going to university or art college.
1:54 Now, this may vary
1:56 depending on which part of the world you live,
1:58 and the cost of the degree courses,
2:00 but currently, here in the UK,
2:02 and I believe in the U.S.,
2:04 degree courses are very expensive, well into the thousands.
2:08 It's not only just the cost of the course
2:10 you need to cover,
2:11 but also the cost of living,
2:13 especially if you're moving away
2:16 and living in student halls of residence,
2:18 or a student house.
2:19 To fund course costs and living costs,
2:22 a lot of students need to take out loans.
2:25 Over a three-year period or more on your course,
2:28 you could be looking to graduate
2:29 with 10s of thousands of pounds worth of debt.
2:33 Now, I graduated university in 2006,
2:36 and yes, I'm still paying off my student loan.
2:39 So cost and debt is shamefully high,
2:42 and one of the biggest deterrents to going to uni.
2:45 The next con is preparing for the real industry.
2:49 So traditionally, when someone goes to university,
2:52 it is seen as the end of the road in their education,
2:55 the highest qualification you can get in your field.
2:59 Now, after investing so much time and money,
3:02 one would expect higher education to prepare them,
3:04 to equip them with the skills,
3:06 and make them employable, to get a job upon graduating.
3:10 Well, for most higher education courses,
3:12 I would imagine this to be true,
3:14 but not so much with graphic design.
3:16 It is rare that a higher education
3:18 graphic design course
3:19 will adequately prepare students
3:21 to enter into the real industry.
3:24 Now, this might not be true for all.
3:26 There may be some courses that have work experience
3:29 as part of the course.
3:31 If you can find a course
3:32 offering some kind of work experience program,
3:34 then this might not apply.
3:37 Now, it could be argued
3:38 that it's not the responsibility of the university,
3:41 but to me, it feels like a bit of a letdown
3:44 to take money and churn out students
3:47 only for them to struggle to get into the industry.
3:50 At the end of the day,
3:51 that is why anyone pays to take a degree course,
3:54 because they believe it makes them
3:56 more employable and ready for the industry.
3:59 In my experience, my university was very good
4:02 at encouraging your creative skills
4:04 and conceptual design theory,
4:07 but failed to prepare students
4:09 with the practical skills and knowledge
4:11 for the harsh reality of the industry.
4:13 Thinks like how to find a job,
4:16 types of job and career choices,
4:18 preparing for an interview, and interview tips,
4:20 tailoring a portfolio, creating a CV,
4:23 personal skills to have,
4:25 technical skills required, such as software skills
4:28 and understanding how the industry works.
4:30 I have seen where fellow students
4:32 who have spent years studying design
4:34 fail to get a job,
4:36 not because they're not good,
4:38 but because they are not ready,
4:39 for the fast-paced, competitive reality of the industry.
4:44 Some might not know how to use creative tools all that well.
4:48 Some may not know how to present themselves
4:50 in their best light,
4:51 so struggle and end up giving up,
4:54 and not pursuing their career in design.
4:57 So those are some of the cons
4:58 when applying for university.
5:01 Now, I happen to believe
5:02 that higher education is extremely important.
5:05 If you're fortunate to have the operation to go,
5:08 I would highly recommend it, for many reasons.
5:11 Generally, I find that the people
5:13 who are anti-university,
5:14 or discourage people from going to university
5:17 are often people who have not
5:18 attended university themselves.
5:21 Now, I attended university.
5:23 I did my degree in graphic design
5:25 from 2003 to 2006, so I speak from experience here.
5:31 Yes, university was a huge struggle,
5:34 probably one of the most hardest times of my life,
5:36 but it was also one of the most important
5:39 and priceless stages of my life.
5:41 If it was not for my degree,
5:43 I can guarantee I probably would not be doing as well today,
5:47 or here talking to you and sharing my experience.
5:50 So one of the biggest pros of going to university
5:53 is actually one that I don't often hear spoken about,
5:57 and that is self-development.
6:00 It can take years to study graphic design,
6:03 and university can be anything
6:04 from three-years onwards.
6:07 In one's life, it can be very rare
6:09 to get such a time for personal development.
6:12 This is an opportunity
6:13 to step out of the real world for a few years
6:16 and have the freedom to develop,
6:18 mature, and grow as a designer.
6:20 University is the perfect environment
6:23 to make mistakes, receive criticism, and to learn.
6:27 These are vital years
6:29 that you will have the opportunity to create work,
6:32 specialize, and study everything about graphic design.
6:35 It's this time that will equip you
6:37 with the creative skills to enter into the industry.
6:41 Tutor time.
6:43 Tutors on your course
6:44 will already have years of expert experience
6:47 in the design industry,
6:48 and they are there to inspire you.
6:51 They will know what is good,
6:52 what works, and what does not.
6:54 This is a priceless opportunity to learn.
6:58 At university, you will get proper feedback
7:01 from tutors on your course.
7:03 If you're lucky, you will get some one-on-one time,
7:06 where tutors can advise you on your work
7:08 and help you progress in the right direction.
7:11 Challenging.
7:13 Over the course of your higher education,
7:15 you will undertake a variety of creative briefs
7:17 that will challenge you
7:18 to research and develop your visual communication skills
7:22 across a variety of media.
7:24 Creative freedom.
7:26 As soon as you enter into the industry,
7:28 you'll soon realize you won't have
7:30 as much creative freedom or opportunities as you like.
7:33 At university, you will be able
7:35 to explore your full creative potential
7:38 and respond to briefs in whatever way you wish.
7:41 You will be able to produce some really unique
7:44 and interesting work,
7:45 which you can include in your portfolio.
7:48 Team-building and communication skills.
7:51 On a good course, you will often have to work in groups
7:54 to tackle a brief.
7:55 This will put you in a position
7:57 where you will gain experience
7:58 working with others in a team.
8:00 This will encourage your teamwork
8:02 and communication skills.
8:04 As you and your fellow students produce work,
8:07 you will all, at some point,
8:08 have to present your work to each other, and talk.
8:11 You will not only be encouraged
8:13 to talk about your own work,
8:14 but also critique and discuss other people's work.
8:18 You will be expected to receive criticism, and give it.
8:22 Education.
8:24 On a course, you will be encouraged
8:25 to learn and practice
8:27 some of the finer disciplines of graphic design,
8:29 such as typography, color theory,
8:32 design theory, and learn how to use creative tools.
8:35 You will attend lectures
8:37 and be expected to produce a written dissertation
8:39 on a subject of your choice.
8:42 Past student talks and presentations.
8:45 Now, as I mentioned earlier,
8:47 it is rare that university
8:49 will adequately prepare the students
8:51 to enter into the real industry.
8:53 To me, that is a serious drawback.
8:55 Now, depending on where you do your degree,
8:57 your university may ask some of the previous students back,
9:01 to talk about their experience.
9:03 On my course, they did this,
9:05 and it was a good opportunity
9:07 to see the work they were producing
9:09 and ask some serious questions about the real industry.
9:13 Build a specialized portfolio.
9:16 At the end of the course,
9:17 you should hopefully graduate
9:19 with a specialized portfolio of work,
9:21 which will form the main basis of your qualification.
9:24 This will represent your talent and skill,
9:27 which you will use to find a job.
9:29 Degree show.
9:31 At the end of your creative course,
9:33 you will be able to display your work in a degree show.
9:36 This is where many agencies will attend
9:38 to look for fresh creative talent.
9:41 This is a great opportunity
9:43 where you can sell yourself
9:44 and talk to potential employers,
9:46 get noticed, and make contacts.
9:49 Creative qualification.
9:51 Once you graduate,
9:52 you will receive your degree qualification,
9:55 but this will not be
9:56 the only qualification you will receive.
9:58 At the end of all this self-development,
10:01 practice, and achievement,
10:03 you should hopefully be better prepared
10:05 to enter into the wide world of the design industry
10:08 and seek to start your career.
10:10 When entering into the design industry,
10:12 you will be able to say you finished a degree course,
10:15 which is recognized
10:16 as a valuable qualification to potential employers,
10:19 because it is understood
10:21 that you spent the time to specialize
10:23 and develop as a graphic designer, to a high standard.
10:27 So to conclude, my ultimate answer to this question,
10:30 "Do I need to go to university," would be yes.
10:35 Indeed, it can be very expensive,
10:37 but I cannot proclaim enough
10:38 how priceless the years you get at university are.
10:42 I can safely say that without my time at uni,
10:45 I would not have become as inspired,
10:47 as conceptual, as philosophical,
10:50 confident, or as knowledgeable
10:52 about typography and design theory as I am today.
10:55 So I would recommend university or art college to anyone.
11:00 However, at the end of the day,
11:02 it is really down to the individual
11:04 and the creative talent of the individual.
11:06 If you are talented, you're going to do well
11:09 regardless if you go to university or not.
11:12 If you don't want to go, or can't go,
11:14 it does not mean you can't still make a career for yourself.
11:17 The graphic design industry is full of various roles,
11:20 at various different levels.
11:22 You can spend that time working your way up in the industry,
11:25 and get some really valuable experience.
11:28 I do believe it is possible for someone
11:30 to make a career for themself in graphic design
11:32 without going to university.
11:35 You could spend that time
11:36 working your way up in the industry,
11:37 and get some really valuable experience
11:40 that may supersede a person with a degree.
11:43 So those are some pros and cons,
11:45 and my opinions about university and art college.
11:49 If you would like to take a closer look
11:51 at the list of pros and cons I discussed in this video,
11:55 you can find out more in the PDF document
11:57 that accompanies this video series.
11:59 So what are your thoughts on university or art college?
12:03 Do you think it's important or not?
12:06 Do you agree with the points I have raised?
12:08 And do you have any experiences you can share?
12:11 Let me know what you can think,
12:13 and share your comments below.
12:15 Well, I hope you enjoyed this video.
12:16 If you did, hit the like button on my Facebook page.
12:20 If you'd like to see more videos like this in the future,
12:22 hit the subscribe button,
12:24 and you can also find me on Twitter, @TastyTuts.
12:27 So in the next video,
12:29 I'm going to talk about the qualifications you will need
12:31 to become a graphic designer,
12:33 to enter into the industry.
12:34 So see you in the next video.

Title: Equipment to be a Graphic designer? Ep31/45 [Beginners guide to Graphic Design]
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sMgdpiqkRBQ&list=PLYfCBK8IplO4E2sXtdKMVpKJZRBEoMvpn&index=32&pp=iAQB
