Downloaded at: 2026-05-25T21:22:58.797Z

0:00 - Hello and welcome to this beginners guide series
0:03 to graphic design, from what graphic design is,
0:06 skills to be a graphic designer, design theory,
0:09 education you need, equipment you need,
0:11 to the graphic design portfolio, and interview advice.
0:15 This series is for anyone at any level,
0:17 so if you're interested in graphic design
0:20 and considering becoming a graphic designer,
0:22 then join me as I discuss a series of graphic design topics.
0:26 (upbeat music)
0:35 Graphic design is a very broad
0:37 and diverse creative discipline.
0:39 To develop your design ideas
0:40 and bring it all to life,
0:42 you will need a variety of equipment.
0:44 In this video, I'm going to discuss the equipment I use
0:47 and how I use it, all of which
0:50 I would recommend to you as a beginner.
0:52 Everything I will be talking about in this video
0:54 will be listed in the PDF series guide
0:57 with web links to find out more.
0:59 Link is in the description.
1:01 For this video, I am going to list
1:02 the various equipment into two categories;
1:05 essentials and accessories.
1:08 The essentials are equipment I would recommend
1:11 any designer as must haves
1:13 and the accessories are what you can use
1:15 depending on your choice.
1:17 So before I begin, I must stress
1:19 that I have not been sponsored for this video
1:21 by any company or brand.
1:23 All of the following products I'm about to list
1:26 are what I use and would recommend.
1:28 So let's start by looking at the essentials.
1:31 So the first, essential equipment I use
1:33 and recommend is drawing equipment.
1:36 For this, I would recommend a range of graphic pencils
1:40 and a range of drawing markers.
1:42 These are the basic drawing tools you will need
1:45 and I use during the design process;
1:48 sketching, drawing scamps, developing ideas,
1:51 or drawing storyboards.
1:53 So I use the Staedtler Mars micro graphic pencil
1:57 and I have both the 0.3 and the 0.5 millimeter range.
2:02 To develop scamps
2:03 and develop my ideas, I typically use the graphic pencil
2:06 to lay down light sketches,
2:08 and then, draw over these with a range of markers.
2:11 To draw over my initial sketches, I use a range
2:14 of black markers in various weights to create
2:17 and communicate dynamics in my sketches.
2:20 The markers I use are Staedtler
2:23 with their range of Lumo black permanent markers.
2:26 I find these really easy to work with
2:29 and they come in a range of super fine,
2:31 fine, medium, and bold.
2:34 The ink is good quality and the tips are good quality
2:37 and are really nice to draw with.
2:39 Next, I recommend you get some color pens.
2:42 For this, I would recommend a set of colored Fineliners
2:46 and a range of graphic markers,
2:48 preferably with the three-tip ends for various tip strokes.
2:52 I use Staedtler Triplus Fineliner pens.
2:56 I find these really easy to draw with, with vibrant colors.
3:00 I use these to add color to my sketches and scamps.
3:03 For more rich full-color shading, I use Letraset Promarkers.
3:08 Now, these can be quite expensive.
3:10 I generally buy them one at a time by color.
3:14 Chances are you're not going to need every color.
3:17 To start, you could consider buying
3:18 a small pack with some common colors.
3:22 So to keep all my drawing equipment together,
3:24 I use a pen case.
3:26 I use this Staedtler case which actually
3:29 comes with the Fineliner set.
3:31 This is a lightweight case which you can roll up
3:34 and keep all your additional drawing equipment in.
3:37 It's neat and compact
3:38 and I generally keep this on me at all times.
3:42 Next, I recommend you get some Tipex.
3:45 I find this helps correct and refine my scamps.
3:48 I use this Pentel Micro.
3:50 This one is great as it has a needle tip,
3:53 so you can apply it in a nice controlled way.
3:56 So that's some drawing equipment I use.
3:59 So next, I would recommend a range of graphic layout paper.
4:03 Now, graphic layout paper is great to develop ideas
4:06 and is my favorite medium to work on.
4:09 For this, I use
4:10 and would recommend a Goldline Layout Pad A4 or A3.
4:15 This layout paper is lightweight, you get a lot of sheets,
4:18 and it's semi-opaque, which means you can use it to trace.
4:21 I usually sketch out my ideas and compositions,
4:24 and trace over them to refine
4:26 and clear up a design, which I will often
4:28 scan into the computer.
4:30 When drawing more technical subjects,
4:32 like typography and geographic shapes
4:34 and designs, I use a range of grid layer paper.
4:39 Next, I recommend you get some
4:40 foldback clips in various sizes.
4:43 I find these mega useful and I carry them around with me.
4:47 As you develop ideas on paper
4:49 and gather research, you will soon build up lots of paper.
4:53 These clips are great for keeping your work in order
4:56 and allow you to be flexible to rearrange your work
4:59 and add to it.
5:00 Next, Post-it notes and sticky neon indexing bookmark tabs.
5:05 So a pretty obvious one, but I find these really useful,
5:09 not only for taking notes,
5:10 but when looking through books and research material.
5:13 These can be really handy to bookmark your references.
5:17 Next, I would recommend you get a scrapbook folder.
5:21 To me, this is really important.
5:23 As you collect inspiration material,
5:25 you're going to need somewhere to keep it all
5:27 for future reference.
5:28 For this, I use an A3 earthbound portfolio
5:32 where I place all my found objects.
5:34 This keeps them all together in one place.
5:37 I also use the foldback clips here, too,
5:39 to organize my collections into various categories.
5:43 Next, you will need a scalpel, metal ruler,
5:46 and a cutting mat.
5:47 Now, I find myself always printing
5:50 and cutting things out, whether it's for presentations
5:53 or doing development.
5:54 I use the Swann Morton number three
5:57 stainless steel scalpel handle with 10A replacement blades.
6:02 I use a Wescott stainless steel ruler
6:05 and a Am-Tech A3 cutting mat.
6:08 Next, I recommend you get a suitable camera.
6:12 Now chances are you will have a camera on your smartphone,
6:15 which is great because you will
6:16 always have it on you everywhere you go.
6:18 If not, any digital camera will do.
6:21 This is an essential bit of kit I use
6:23 to take pictures of inspiration or reference
6:26 in the field to add to my scrapbook.
6:29 Next, job folders.
6:31 As I develop research
6:33 and design ideas, I like to keep it
6:35 all in its own job folder.
6:37 This is also good to keep everything
6:38 grouped up when a project is done.
6:41 For this, I generally use clear plastic A3 cases
6:45 and file those away.
6:46 So the final piece of essential kit I recommend you get,
6:50 which is a pretty important part these days,
6:52 is an adequate computer.
6:54 Now, a lot of people ask me what's best, a PC or a Mac?
6:58 Well, today, they are both great to work on
7:00 and the software tools run the same way on both,
7:03 so this really comes down to your choice.
7:05 Here, I would recommend you get whatever
7:07 feels most comfortable to you.
7:10 But what you need to be sure of
7:11 is that whatever computer you get,
7:13 make sure it has an adequate specification
7:16 to run your creative software, enough RAM with a good CPU.
7:20 Personally, I use a Mac, but that's my personal preference.
7:25 So once you have your computer,
7:26 you will need the creative software tools.
7:29 Currently, I'm signed up to Adobe Creative Cloud.
7:33 I have the full subscription,
7:34 so I have access to all the software,
7:37 although I only use specific programs.
7:40 For you to bring your design to life,
7:41 I would recommend you get your hands
7:43 on Adobe Photoshop, Illustrator, and InDesign.
7:47 These are the big three you will need to get to grips with.
7:51 In the next video,
7:52 I will be talking about these three programs in more depth.
7:55 So that is the essential equipment I use
7:58 and I make sure that I'm never without them.
8:01 So next is my accessories list.
8:03 These are additional tools and equipment I use
8:06 and would recommend,
8:07 but they're not as vital as the essentials.
8:11 So for this, I would recommend a project folder or case.
8:15 Now, I have one very similar to this one here in A3.
8:20 I find this really useful to keep all my project work in,
8:23 where it's all compact in one place.
8:26 These are also really great for students.
8:28 You should be able to carry everything you need
8:30 in one of these when you attend a class;
8:33 your pads, your drawing equipment, and so on.
8:36 This is great to carry it all in.
8:38 Next, I would recommend highlighters.
8:42 Now, I'm obsessed with making lists.
8:44 To get things done, I make lists
8:46 and I always have an orange highlighter
8:48 to mark off what I've done.
8:50 Feels so good to mark things off, so if you're like me
8:54 and you like making lists,
8:55 I recommend you get some of these.
8:58 Next, I would recommend a scanner and printer.
9:01 Now, I tend to work a lot with my drawings and sketches.
9:05 To get these onto computer, I use a Canon CanoScan.
9:09 This is a lightweight USB-powered scanner.
9:12 Over the years, I have had a range of printers,
9:15 but right now, I'm using the A4 Canon PIXMA iP7250
9:21 color wireless inkjet printer.
9:23 This is really neat with a compact design,
9:26 takes individual ink cartridges, and prints borderless.
9:30 This range is also available up to A3 plus.
9:34 Now, if you find yourself printing large documents,
9:37 I recommend you get a telescopic transport tube.
9:40 I used one of these to store and transport large prints.
9:44 Next, I would recommend you get a graphics tablet.
9:48 Now, some may argue that this
9:49 is an essential piece of kit for a graphic designer.
9:52 Personally, I don't use my tablet all that much.
9:55 I generally only use mine when I'm doing some retouching
9:59 in Photoshop or digital drawing
10:01 or painting in Illustrator.
10:03 When doing a task like this,
10:05 I find the job is much more comfortable
10:07 and natural using a graphics tablet.
10:10 I'm currently using a Wacom tablet.
10:13 So next, you can consider getting a color calibrator
10:16 for your monitor or monitors.
10:18 Now, these are amazing for calibrating your monitor
10:21 to get more accurate color profiles,
10:24 especially if you're doing work for print.
10:26 For this, you could use the Spyder4 PRO Advanced.
10:30 If you have multiple monitors connected to your computer,
10:33 this can calibrate them all.
10:36 So the last thing I'd recommend
10:37 you get is a random crafts box.
10:40 Now, every designers should have one of these
10:42 and make sure it's full of all other stationary equipment
10:45 you would use, such as masking tape,
10:48 double-sided sticky tape, a range of glues, paints, brushes,
10:52 and so on, all of which come in handy
10:55 during to the course of a creative project.
10:57 So that is some of the equipment I use
10:59 and would recommend you acquire,
11:01 which will help you with your design process.
11:04 If you'd like to take a closer look
11:05 at the list I have mentioned in this video,
11:07 you can find out more in the PDF document
11:10 that accompanies this video series.
11:12 Download link is in the description.
11:14 So what equipment do you use?
11:16 Do you have any other equipment you would recommend?
11:19 Be sure to share your comments below.
11:22 Well, I hope you enjoyed this video.
11:24 If you did, hit the like button on my Facebook page.
11:27 If you'd like to see more videos like this in future,
11:29 hit the subscribe button.
11:31 And you can also follow my Twitter, @TastyTuts.
11:34 So to develop your design ideas,
11:36 you will need to use an array of hands-on equipment.
11:39 To bring your design to life on computer,
11:42 you will need to use creative software tools.
11:44 In the next video, I'm going to talk about
11:46 the many creative programs that are used
11:49 in the design industry, which you will want
11:51 to acquire and become proficient in.
11:53 I will also be discussing which programs are best
11:56 to use in which particular design scenario,
11:59 so see you in the next video.
12:01 (upbeat music)

0:00 - Hello, and welcome to this beginner's guide series
0:02 to graphic design.
0:04 From what graphic design is,
0:06 skills to be a graphic designer,
0:07 design theory,
0:09 education you need,
0:10 equipment you need,

0:13 and interview advice.

0:17 So if you're interested in graphic design,
0:19 you've considered becoming a graphic designer,
0:21 then join me as a I discuss a series
0:24 of graphic design topics.
0:25 (upbeat music)
0:34 Now in my experience,
0:35 I have created work across many forms of media,
0:38 from print to digital.
0:40 Being a freelancer, I go into a lot of design agencies
0:43 during the course of a year.
0:44 And I'm expected to be efficient in various software
0:47 to deliver work to deadline.
0:49 As a graphic designer, depending on what job you're doing,
0:52 you will have to use particular design software.
0:55 In this video, I'm going to discuss
0:57 the creative software tools you will need
0:59 as a graphic designer,
1:00 and which software you might use
1:02 for which design scenario.
1:04 So as a graphic designer,
1:05 the main software you will find yourself using is
1:08 Adobe InDesign, Adobe Photoshop,
1:11 and Adobe Illustrator.
1:13 As you broaden your skillset,
1:14 you may find yourself using Adobe After Effects
1:17 or Adobe Muse.
1:19 As a graphic designer, you will find yourself using
1:22 a combination of the big three,
1:24 Adobe InDesign, Adobe Photoshop, and Adobe Illustrator.
1:28 Each one of these programs has been designed
1:30 for a particular design task,
1:32 and each has their particular tools.
1:35 Depending on the complexity of your design task,
1:37 you may have to use a specific tool
1:39 and a particular program,
1:41 or use a combination of programs and tools
1:43 to develop your end result.
1:45 So now I'm going to talk a little about Adobe InDesign,
1:48 Photoshop, and Illustrator.
1:51 Adobe InDesign.
1:53 Adobe InDesign is the most powerful
1:54 desktop publishing program
1:56 and has become the industry standard,
1:58 specializing in design for layout.
2:00 InDesign provides the ability to create
2:03 and manage multi-page documents with ease.
2:06 InDesign provides powerful tools
2:08 to work with grids and typography,
2:11 while offering maximum creative freedom
2:13 to create dynamic layouts
2:14 for both print and digital media.
2:17 InDesign works by placing external links,
2:20 both raster and vector, into compositions.
2:22 These links are images or files,
2:25 otherwise prepared in Adobe Photoshop or Illustrator.
2:28 Compositions are exported out as single, portable documents,
2:31 which are distributed and can be viewed online
2:34 or sent to be printed.
2:36 The type of work you'd expect to do in InDesign is
2:39 multi-page leaflets and flyers,
2:41 posters, books, magazines,
2:45 print banners, brochures,
2:48 business cards, presentations for print or digital,
2:52 interactive PDFs, typesetting,
2:54 and bespoke prints.
2:56 InDesign is one of the key programs
2:58 any designer should have in their toolkit.
3:00 If you're new to InDesign
3:02 and wish to learn how to use the software,
3:04 I have a 13-episode beginner's guide course
3:06 on my channel.
3:08 If you'd like to find out more,
3:09 the link is in a description,
3:11 and in the PDF document that accompanies the series.
3:13 The link is in the description.
3:16 Adobe Photoshop.
3:18 Adobe Photoshop specializes in working with raster images
3:22 and creating raster graphics using layers
3:24 and applying affects with filters.
3:27 Put simply, a raster image is made
3:29 with many little square pixels.
3:31 Together, these pixels build a picture,
3:34 which we see in Photoshop.
3:35 If you're new to the subject of raster images,
3:38 I have another video dedicated
3:40 to raster image principles.
3:42 You can find the link in the description,
3:43 if you wish to find out more.
3:45 Adobe Photoshop was created specifically
3:47 for image enhancement and the modification of pixels
3:51 that make up an image.
3:52 However, since Photoshop's release,
3:54 it has been adding more and more tools
3:57 that has broadened the type of work you can do.
3:59 In Photoshop, you'll be working with images,
4:02 creating initial layouts,
4:04 and generally creating assets
4:05 to be used in other programs,
4:07 such as InDesign or Illustrator.
4:09 The type of work you'd expect to do in Photoshop is
4:12 image retouching, modification, and enhancements,
4:16 digital art and image effects,
4:18 web design layouts and image preparation,
4:21 web banners and animated gifs,
4:24 posters, simple print layouts,
4:26 and social media.
4:28 If you're new to Photoshop
4:29 and wish to learn how to use the software,
4:31 I have a 33-episode beginner's guide course
4:34 on my channel.
4:36 If you'd like to find out more,
4:37 the link is in the description
4:39 and in the PDF document that accompanies this series.
4:42 Link is in the description.
4:44 Adobe Illustrator.
4:46 Adobe Illustrator offers specialized creative tools
4:49 for creating and modifying vector graphics
4:52 and working with color.
4:53 In recent years, Illustrator has introduced tools
4:56 that make it behave like a desktop publishing program,
4:59 though has limitations when compared to InDesign.
5:02 Adobe Illustrator was designed
5:04 to create and modify vector graphics,
5:06 which are very different to raster images.
5:09 Raster images are limited to the quality
5:11 of their resolution and DPI.
5:14 Instead of being made of square pixels,
5:16 vectors are made with anchor points,
5:18 which are joined with paths
5:19 to form curves, lines, and points.
5:22 Vector shapes are mathematical expressions,
5:25 represented visually.
5:27 This means that vectors can be scaled up to any size
5:30 and will not lose any superficial quality.
5:33 Once created, a variety of color
5:35 and stroke effects can be applied.
5:37 If you're interested to learn more
5:39 about vector basics, I have a video
5:41 that explains and demonstrates this in detail.
5:44 Link is in the description.
5:46 So the type of work you would expect to do in Illustrator is
5:50 logo design, iconography,
5:52 design for T-shirts and clothing,
5:55 creating typefaces,
5:56 creating simple leaflets, flyers,
5:58 business cards, and posters,
6:00 presentations for print or digital,
6:03 infographics and blueprints and plans.
6:07 If you're new to Illustrator
6:08 and wish to learn how to use the software,
6:10 I have a 19-episode beginner's guide course
6:13 on my channel.
6:14 If you'd like to find out more,
6:16 the link is in the description,
6:17 and in the PDF document that accompanies this series.
6:21 So those are the three main programs
6:24 any designer will need.
6:26 Now I am of the opinion that none
6:27 of these programs are better than the other.
6:30 Each has been designed and developed
6:31 with a specific task in mind.
6:34 They are simply different tools in the toolbox
6:36 that can be used to tackle a particular creative task.
6:40 Used together in the right way,
6:41 there is nothing you can't create.
6:44 Now as you broaden your skillset,
6:46 there are other programs that you may want to explore.
6:49 For example, Adobe After Effects or Adobe Muse.
6:53 If you start to work with video or motion graphics,
6:56 After Effects offer specialized creative tools
6:58 to create motion graphics.
7:00 As a graphic designer myself,
7:02 I don't find myself using the advanced tools
7:04 of this program, only the basics.
7:07 For me, I find this program is great
7:09 to enhance my video presentations.
7:12 For me, I find it great for animated type,
7:15 color correcting footage,
7:17 video presentations,
7:18 video effects and transitions,
7:20 and simple animations.
7:22 So the last program I'm going to talk
7:24 about here is Adobe Muse.
7:26 In recent years, Adobe Muse has emerged
7:29 as a contender to the web design scene.
7:31 For designers, this is the dream web design product
7:35 and puts the power in the designer's hands.
7:37 Muse empowers designers to create custom
7:40 online experiences for desktop, tablet, or mobile,
7:43 without having to edit any code.
7:46 Muse is much like Adobe InDesign,
7:48 in the way you can create a layout
7:50 and import external files, which have been prepared
7:53 using Photoshop or Illustrator.
7:55 You can then export your web design
7:57 directly to your FTP server.
7:59 Adobe Muse works by exporting your web layout
8:02 into HTML 5 and JavaScript.
8:04 Muse offers some great features
8:07 like publishing with a range of typefaces,
8:10 parallax scrolling effects,
8:12 social widgets, and the ability to embed HTML.
8:16 If you're interested in web design,
8:18 I could not recommend this program enough.
8:21 So those are the programs you'll need
8:23 in order to bring your design to life.
8:26 Well, I hope you enjoyed this video.
8:27 If you did, hit the like button on my Facebook page.
8:30 If you'd like to see more videos like this in future,

8:57 So see you in the next video.

0:00 (whoosh)
0:00 (upbeat music) - Hello and welcome to this
0:01 beginners guide series to Graphic Design.
0:04 From what Graphic Design is, skills to be a Graphic Designer
0:08 design theory, education you need, equipment you need
0:11 to the Graphic Design portfolio and interview advice.
0:15 This series is for anyone at any level.
0:17 So if you're interested in Graphic Design
0:20 and considering in becoming a Graphic Designer
0:22 then join me as I discuss a series of Graphic Design topics.
0:26 (loud upbeat music)
0:35 So as you step out into the design industry
0:37 and look for jobs you will need to develop
0:39 and showcase a portfolio to demonstrate you have the skills
0:43 and showcase that you can be creative
0:45 and develop visual solutions.
0:47 The Graphic Design portfolio is very important.
0:50 It's your ticket to your next job.
0:52 Now you can spend years studying
0:54 to become a Graphic Designer.
0:56 You can spend a lot of money
0:57 on courses and higher education.
0:59 You can even achieve the best grades
1:01 and attend the best art colleges.
1:03 Though chances are when it comes down to applying for a job
1:06 it will most likely come down to your portfolio.
1:09 It's your portfolio that makes the first impression.
1:12 It will be this that they will judge you on.
1:14 No matter what grade you scored art college
1:17 if a client or potential employer in not convinced
1:20 by your portfolio
1:21 you will probably not make it through to an interview.
1:24 In my 10 years experience in the industry since graduating
1:27 I not once been asked what grade I got at university.
1:31 So I often get asked questions
1:33 about the Graphic Design portfolio.
1:35 Questions like, what makes for a good portfolio?
1:38 What's the best type of portfolio, print or digital?
1:41 Do I need a website?
1:43 How many projects do I include?
1:45 What if I don't have any projects to show?
1:47 What are employers looking for?
1:49 How much type do I include?
1:51 And what's the best programme to use to build my portfolio?
1:54 So in this video I'm going to discuss
1:56 the Graphic Design portfolio and answer these questions.
1:59 In this video I'll also be sharing my experience
2:01 and offering some tips to keep in mind.
2:04 So the Graphic Design portfolio
2:05 should clearly demonstrate your skills
2:07 show what you can do and what you have already done.
2:11 As a Graphic Designer you really want to create
2:13 the best portfolio you can.
2:15 It should be the one reason you push yourself.
2:17 The reason you try your best.
2:19 Of course we should strive to do a good job
2:22 for the client too
2:23 but you should always be pushin' to create amazing work
2:26 that you can include in your portfolio.
2:28 Because it's your work that will get you noticed
2:30 and get you a better job in future.
2:33 So let's look at some of those questions.
2:35 What makes for a good portfolio?
2:38 Now there are lots of factors that make for a good portfolio
2:41 all of which you should try and implement in yours.
2:44 So the first is don't over design your portfolio
2:47 keep it simple.
2:49 One common mistake I see especially with newcomers
2:52 is Graphic Designers over-designing their portfolios.
2:55 Now it's easy to get carried away
2:57 and want to show your skills
2:58 and creative flair on your portfolio.
3:01 But really it's not necessary.
3:03 The portfolio should be about
3:04 showing your work in its best light.
3:07 You really don't want to distract from this.
3:09 The best approach is to keep your portfolio simple.
3:12 If you have a personal brand, that's great!
3:15 Include it on the cover and back page
3:17 but try and keep the presentation layout
3:19 as clear as possible.
3:22 Consider pace in your portfolio.
3:24 Now you really want your portfolio
3:26 to work as hard as it can for you.
3:28 If you have a lot of work to show for a particular project
3:31 then spread this over a few pages.
3:34 When introducing a project
3:35 consider starting with a nice hero shot.
3:38 Allow for whoever looks at your portfolio
3:40 to really appreciate the design and details of your work.
3:44 Let your work breathe and have impact.
3:47 As your project continues over other pages
3:49 then you can include more images together.
3:52 Using this technique will control the pace of your portfolio
3:55 and encourage a viewer to pause
3:58 and digest rather than skim through.
4:01 This might mean having to increase
4:02 the page number of your portfolio
4:04 but it will make for a more sophisticated experience.
4:07 Include examples of your design process.
4:11 Now this is really important.
4:13 A potential employer will really like to see how you think
4:16 and how you approach a brief.
4:18 If you can show some good examples of your process
4:20 this will be more convincing and impressive.
4:23 It will show you take time to research
4:26 plan and think about your ideas and design.
4:29 Include in situ examples.
4:32 One of the most important things you can do
4:34 in your portfolio is show your work in situ.
4:37 This will bring your design to life
4:39 and is much more impressive than looking at flat examples.
4:42 If it's poster, show someone holding it.
4:45 If it's a mobile app, show it on a device.
4:48 If it's a brand, show it on some stationary
4:51 merchandise or packaging.
4:53 This will mean getting good quality photos of your work.
4:56 Or superimposing your work onto devices or print material.
5:01 Start on a high and end on a high.
5:04 When an interviewer tries to recall you after an interview
5:07 they will either remember your first impression
5:09 or how you ended.
5:10 Pick out two of your best projects.
5:13 The aim here is to put one of those at the front
5:15 and one at the back.
5:17 Start well and leave a good lasting impression.
5:21 Tailor your portfolio.
5:23 So as you apply for jobs
5:25 one of the smartest things you can do
5:26 is tailor your portfolio to your potential employer.
5:30 In some cases if you have too much variety in your portfolio
5:33 it may confuse or even overwhelm
5:35 the potential client or employer.
5:38 Tailoring your portfolio can be a key factor.
5:41 This can make you seem more suitable for the job.
5:43 Depending on what type of job you're applying for
5:46 you should tailor your portfolio.
5:48 If you want to be a Brand Designer
5:50 include your best brand examples.
5:52 If you want to be Motion Graphics Designer
5:54 include your best motion graphics examples.
5:57 If you wan to do UI
5:58 include your best UI examples and so on.
6:02 Now having said that
6:03 there can be roles that ask for an all-rounder.
6:06 So in that instance you may want to show a variety of work.
6:10 So remember
6:11 tailor your portfolio to your potential employer.
6:14 So those are some of the things
6:16 that make for a good portfolio.
6:18 So the next question is
6:19 what's the best type of portfolio, print or digital?
6:24 So as a Graphic Designer
6:25 you will need a strategy to get noticed
6:28 get an interview and get a job.
6:30 Today this can exist both online and offline.
6:33 This will also vary depending
6:35 on the type of designer you are
6:36 and the type of work you have.
6:38 So ideally you will need a digital portfolio
6:41 to use online to help you get noticed
6:44 and used to send over email
6:46 to possible clients or employers.
6:48 Once the digital portfolio is in the bag
6:50 you can always use it again at an interview.
6:53 Present it on your laptop or tablet device.
6:55 If you're a Digital Designer
6:57 you may only need a digital portfolio.
6:59 Though if you're more of a Print and Branding Designer
7:02 then you may wish to invest in a print portfolio.
7:05 The print portfolio is a much more impressive option
7:08 for Print Designers.
7:09 Now there is a lot more to be said on this topic.
7:12 I will be discussing this in more depth in the next episode.
7:16 So the next question
7:17 do I need a website?
7:20 As stated in the previous question
7:22 you will need a strategy to get noticed
7:24 and get an interview and get a job.
7:27 The website is a good opportunity to show your work online
7:30 get noticed and it's convenient to share.
7:34 In my experience it's better to have one than not to.
7:38 Now if your not able to set up your own site
7:40 there are so many online portfolio websites out there today
7:43 you can sign up to and create a profile.
7:46 The good thing about these sites
7:48 is that they are often looked at by recruiters and clients.
7:51 So there is always a chance you can be discovered.
7:55 As a Designer today
7:56 you may find yourself creating lots of these
7:58 to increase your exposure.
8:01 So the next question is
8:02 how many projects do I include?
8:06 Well in my opinion there is no simple answer to this
8:09 because I am not sure there is really a simple figure.
8:12 What you have to keep in mind here
8:14 is that it's not about the quantity
8:16 it's about the quality.
8:17 Instead of worrying about including too much or too little
8:21 try and focus more on including what represents your skills
8:24 and what you want to do.
8:26 As your career develops
8:27 you will no doubt create a lot of work.
8:30 Some projects will be more impressive
8:32 and interesting than others.
8:34 In the portfolio you want to project who you are
8:37 what you do and what you want to do in future.
8:40 As mentioned earlier
8:41 try to tailor your portfolio as much as you can.
8:44 Everything in your portfolio
8:45 must be your best work and interesting.
8:49 Take a step back and be objective.
8:52 Imagine you are a potential employer
8:54 looking through your portfolio.
8:56 If there is anything you have any doubts about
8:59 then edit it out!
9:01 That may mean having a small portfolio
9:03 or a large portfolio.
9:05 As long as it's strong and impressive
9:07 that's all that matters.
9:08 Next question.
9:10 What if I don't have any projects to show?
9:13 Now if you're new to Graphic Design
9:15 perhaps a student or you're looking to get into the industry
9:18 chances are you will not have many examples to show
9:21 that you have done for clients or employers in the industry.
9:25 It's common for a lot of newcomers to only have work
9:27 that has been done at school or self initiated briefs.
9:32 Keep in mind when showing work at an interview
9:35 potential employers and clients want to see what you can do
9:38 your potential and how resourceful you can be.
9:41 Employers will be impressed
9:43 by how you can find opportunity and overcome problems.
9:47 If you don't have a lot of work to show
9:49 then I would recommend you set yourself
9:50 some self initiated briefs
9:52 and look to do some work for local business.
9:55 This is a technique many Designers undertake
9:58 at some point to build their work.
10:00 Whichever field of design you want to specialize in
10:03 set yourself a brief and create a solution for it.
10:06 If you know a friend or family member with a business
10:09 seek to do some creative work for them.
10:11 Hopefully you will be able to build up some projects
10:14 to include in your portfolio.
10:17 Next question.
10:18 What are employers looking for in a portfolio?
10:22 Now this really depends
10:23 on what type of job you're looking for
10:25 and at what level.
10:26 Typically in the industry you have three significant levels
10:31 junior, middleweight, and senior.
10:33 At each level an employer will be looking for creative flair
10:37 and the ability to do the job they are requesting.
10:40 At junior level you many have just graduated from university
10:44 or looking to get your first job.
10:46 An employer will know you won't have much
10:48 industry experience so will mainly be looking for potential
10:52 creative flair, good ideas
10:54 and good demonstration of design skills.
10:57 Towards the more senior roles
10:59 employers will be focusing more on your experience
11:01 and what you have already done.
11:03 Employers will be looking for more higher quality of work
11:07 more specialized work, successful work examples
11:10 good design process examples
11:12 and good portfolio presentation.
11:15 The next question is
11:16 how much type do I include?
11:18 Now remember your portfolio is a presentation
11:21 which you are not there to make.
11:24 As much as you should want your work to speak for itself
11:27 it can help to add annotations
11:29 to help explain the context of the work.
11:32 In my experience I would say
11:34 try and keep the type down to a minimum.
11:36 For each page of your portfolio
11:38 aim to include a maximum of two sentences
11:41 to describe what the work is and how it fulfilled the brief.
11:46 In your portfolio you want the work to take the main stage
11:49 and be the main focus.
11:51 When using type, try and keep the font size to a minimum
11:55 so it does not deter from your work.
11:57 So the last question is
11:59 what's the best programme to use to build my portfolio?
12:03 Well when it comes to working with
12:04 anything layout and type-focused
12:07 I would always recommend Adobe InDesign.
12:10 As simple as the portfolio layout should be
12:12 the document itself will be complex
12:14 with many image links which you will want to edit.
12:18 In InDesign you also have the ability to create
12:21 an interactive PDF where you can embed links
12:24 to your online media.
12:25 InDesign is the best programme for this.
12:29 So those are some factors to consider

0:00 - Hello and welcome to this beginners guide series
0:03 to graphic design,
0:04 from what graphic design is,
0:06 skills to be a graphic designer,
0:08 design theory, education you need, equipment you need
0:11 to the graphic design portfolio and interview advice.
0:15 This series is for anyone at any level.
0:17 So, if you're interested in graphic design
0:20 and considering becoming a graphic designer,
0:22 then join me as I discuss this series
0:24 of graphic design topics.
0:26 (energetic synth music)
0:34 Today, graphic design has become a broad creative industry.
0:38 Over the past 10 years,
0:39 I have seen a change in the graphic design industry
0:42 and the ways we use technology to become noticed
0:44 and apply for jobs.
0:46 Through the development of technology, social media,
0:49 and a more sophisticated online experience,
0:52 graphic design is continually expanding into digital.
0:55 More designers are becoming digital
0:57 and more job sites and the process of finding a job
1:00 has become more digital.
1:02 This has changed the way we as graphic designers create
1:05 and use portfolios to apply for jobs.
1:08 In today's industry, designers are often considered
1:11 either digital designers or print designers
1:14 or integrated designers, this being a mixture of both.
1:17 Now, regardless of specialty,
1:19 to apply for a graphic design job
1:21 a portfolio of work is required.
1:24 As a graphic designer, you will need a strategy
1:26 to get noticed, get an interview, and get a job.
1:30 So, three things there,
1:31 getting noticed, getting an interview, and getting a job,
1:35 all of which I think are equally important.
1:38 Now, two questions I often get asked are
1:40 what portfolio should I use?
1:42 Print or digital?
1:44 And which one is best?
1:46 But the answers to these questions vary
1:48 depending on what type of designer
1:49 you consider yourself to be
1:51 and the approach you take to look for jobs, apply for jobs,
1:55 and present at interviews.
1:57 So, in this video, I'm going to discuss the print
1:59 and digital graphic design portfolio
2:01 and recommend which strategy is best
2:03 for which type of graphic designer.
2:06 In this video, I'll be also sharing my experience
2:08 on how I use both a print and digital portfolio
2:11 in my strategy to get jobs.
2:14 So, traditionally, you would have had
2:16 just one print portfolio.
2:18 That's all you would've ever needed,
2:20 and most of your work would've been print-based.
2:22 When applying for a job,
2:24 you may have looked for vacancies via creative magazines,
2:27 going into creative agencies,
2:28 or meeting contacts at design events.
2:31 You would've simply sent a letter
2:32 or email to a design agency.
2:35 This would include a bit about yourself, a cover letter,
2:37 and your CV with your achievements and qualifications.
2:41 If the client or agency was interested,
2:43 they would invite you in for an interview
2:45 where you would've been able to showcase
2:46 your print portfolio.
2:48 So, the traditional function of the portfolio
2:50 was to be used at the interview to get a job.
2:53 Today, we have the internet,
2:55 which has changed the way we apply for jobs
2:58 and the way clients and employers can discover new talent.
3:01 Files can be shared instantly
3:03 and also hosted to be discovered online.
3:06 Today, there are also many portfolio websites
3:09 where you can create a portfolio and host your work,
3:12 where potential clients or employers can search
3:14 and discover you.
3:16 So, this has encouraged designers to focus more on digital
3:19 and put more necessity on the digital portfolio.
3:22 Now, today, it's common
3:23 to have more than just one portfolio.
3:26 You may have your own PDF portfolio you send out over email,
3:30 you may have a personal website,
3:32 you may have work hosted on portfolio websites,
3:35 you may have a print portfolio,
3:36 or you may even use a combination of all of these.
3:40 Today, when applying for a job,
3:41 you will typically send an email
3:43 which will include your cover letter, CV,
3:45 and a digital version of your portfolio
3:48 or links to your portfolio websites.
3:50 You may even apply for jobs via design websites,
3:53 which you can attach a PDF portfolio.
3:56 Traditionally, if a client or potential employer
3:58 liked what they read in your cover letter and CV,
4:01 they would invite you in for an interview
4:03 where only then would you show your portfolio.
4:06 Today, clients and potential employers can see your work
4:09 without having to meet you first.
4:11 Now, you typically only get an interview
4:14 if they like your work.
4:16 So, the portfolio is no longer used only at interview
4:19 but is used to be sent via email to request an interview
4:22 and hosted online as a way for potential clients
4:25 and employers to discover you.
4:27 Today, the function of a portfolio
4:29 has to fulfill three purposes.
4:31 Where once it was just used at an interview to get a job,
4:35 it has now become a tool to get an interview
4:37 and get noticed online.
4:39 Today, getting noticed and getting an interview
4:42 is predominantly done online,
4:44 and getting the job is done when you're at the interview.
4:47 So, when creating a portfolio today,
4:49 designers have to address these three important things.
4:53 So, if this is the case
4:54 where applying for jobs, getting noticed,
4:56 and applying for interviews
4:58 is becoming more prominent online,
5:00 is there any need for a print portfolio anymore?
5:03 What portfolio should you use?
5:05 Print or digital?
5:07 To answer this question,
5:08 I'm going to look at these three points in more detail.
5:11 So, the first point is getting noticed online.
5:15 Now, whichever designer you consider yourself to be,
5:17 your strategy to get noticed online will be the same.
5:21 Whether you're a print designer or a digital designer,
5:23 I would recommend you develop a digital portfolio.
5:26 For this, you can consider a range of approaches.
5:30 First, I would recommend you develop your portfolio
5:32 on your computer,
5:33 as you would intend to present it at an interview.
5:36 This could be in the format of an A3 landscape presentation
5:40 with your project laid out on each page.
5:43 Once you have created your portfolio on the computer,
5:45 you can then use the images and compositions
5:48 for your own personal website.
5:50 You can then use your website address
5:52 on your social media profiles and as links in your emails.
5:56 Next, you can use the images and compositions
5:59 on portfolio websites, blogs, and social media pages.
6:03 This will put you out there, able to be discovered
6:06 and shared by potential clients and employers.
6:09 Once you have a fully developed portfolio on your computer
6:12 and a good presence online,
6:14 you can apply for jobs.
6:16 Getting an interview.
6:18 Now, again, whichever designer you consider yourself to be,
6:21 your strategy to get an interview will be the same.
6:24 Whether you're a print designer or a digital designer,
6:26 here I would recommend you use your digital portfolio.
6:30 So, at this stage, you should have lots of creative arsenal
6:33 ready to deploy.
6:34 You should ideally have one of the following,
6:37 your portfolio presentation on your computer as a PDF,
6:40 a personal website, social media pages,
6:43 or profiles on online portfolio websites.
6:46 As you compose emails and seek interviews,
6:48 you can attach your PDF
6:50 or include links to your work online.
6:52 If the client or potential employers like what they see,
6:55 they will more than likely invite you in for an interview.
6:59 Getting the job.
7:01 So, what portfolio do you use at interview?
7:04 Well, this again comes down to what type of designer
7:06 you consider yourself to be.
7:08 If you're a designer with mostly digital work,
7:10 then your potential clients or employers
7:12 will not be looking for a print portfolio,
7:15 so you could simply stick with the digital approach.
7:17 With the digital portfolio PDF already in the bag,
7:20 you can always use this again at an interview
7:23 and present on screen, on the laptop, or on a tablet device.
7:27 Now, if you're a print designer,
7:29 chances are your potential clients or employers
7:31 will be much more impressed with a print portfolio.
7:34 In my experience, the print portfolio is the most impressive
7:38 and impactful at interviews.
7:40 This is why the print portfolio
7:42 will always remain significant
7:44 and why I would recommend you have one as a print designer.
7:47 The print portfolio can come in many forms.
7:50 At art college, we were encouraged to develop
7:53 our print portfolio in a black A3+ portfolio box.
7:57 The work is displayed on A3+ paper sheets,
8:00 which are inserted into individual plastic crystal sleeves.
8:04 Now, this looks absolutely gorgeous.
8:07 I have this nice case,
8:08 and all the work is in these shiny sleeves.
8:11 This is great because it looks so much more professional.
8:15 The whole interview experience is much more tactile,
8:17 where you can spread the work around.
8:20 If you're applying for senior roles,
8:22 you should consider going all out on the print portfolio.
8:25 When you show up with something like this
8:27 to a job interview,
8:28 they know you mean business.
8:30 The only problem here with this type of portfolio
8:33 is that it can be extremely expensive
8:35 and time consuming to put together and update.
8:38 You need to buy the portfolio case, the paper,
8:41 the ink to print, you need a printer,
8:43 and the plastic crystal sleeves to put the paper into,
8:47 though, as a print designer,
8:48 I would recommend you develop a print portfolio if you can.
8:52 But if you're not able to develop a print portfolio
8:55 or don't have the time,
8:56 then I would recommend using your digital portfolio
8:59 you used to get the interview.
9:00 You can always present this on a laptop.
9:03 Now, if you're like me
9:04 and you're both a print and digital designer,
9:07 an integrated designer,
9:09 then I would recommend you use a print portfolio
9:11 along with a digital device.
9:13 I have attended interviews
9:15 where I have shown my print work in the print portfolio.
9:18 I will include images of the digital work as references
9:21 and then demonstrate the digital work or artwork
9:24 on a laptop or tablet device.
9:27 So, the best of both there.
9:29 That has always gone down well for me.
9:32 So, which portfolio is best?
9:34 In my experience, in a fast-paced, competitive industry,
9:38 the digital portfolio has become
9:40 the most convenient, versatile,
9:42 and cost-effective portfolio
9:44 at seeking to get noticed online, get an interview,
9:47 and present at an interview.
9:49 Though, having said that, if you want to impress
9:51 and create the most professional interview presentation,
9:54 I would say using the print portfolio is best
9:57 for your strategy to get the job.
9:59 It's not as important for digital designers,
10:01 but if you are a digital designer and want that wow factor,
10:04 you could consider it.
10:06 The print portfolio looks fantastic and very professional.
10:09 So, that is my experience and recommendation
10:12 with regards to the print and digital portfolio.
10:15 I happen to use both,
10:16 as they are both effective in my strategy
10:19 to get noticed and get a job.
10:21 So, what do you think?
10:22 Which portfolio do you use?
10:24 And which strategy gets you the best results?
10:27 Be sure to share your thoughts and experiences
10:29 in the comments below.
10:31 Well, I hope you enjoyed this video.
10:33 If you did, hit the like button on my Facebook page.
10:36 If you'd like to see more videos like this in future,
10:38 hit the subscribe button.
10:40 And you can also follow me on Twitter, @TastyTuts.
10:43 So, in this video, I discussed the importance
10:46 of a print and digital portfolio.
10:48 Indeed, the portfolio is very important.
10:51 It is what you are judged on for potential work.
10:54 However, there is something else potential clients
10:57 or employers will judge you on
10:58 and that is your experience.
11:01 As well as showcasing your work,
11:03 you will need to showcase your experience.
11:05 This comes in the form of a CV
11:07 that will go along with your portfolio.
11:09 In the next video,
11:10 I'm going to talk about the graphic design CV
11:13 and discuss some of the things you can keep in mind
11:15 to aid you in creating a solid CV.
11:18 So, see you in the next video.

0:00 - Hello and welcome to this beginners guide series
0:03 to Graphic Design.
0:04 From what Graphic Design is,
0:06 skills to be a Graphic Designer, design theory,
0:09 education you need, equipment you need,
0:11 to the Graphic Design portfolio and interview advice.
0:15 This series is for anyone at any level.
0:17 So, if you're interested in Graphic Design,
0:19 and consider becoming a Graphic Designer,
0:22 then join me as I discuss the series
0:24 of Graphic Design topics.
0:26 (upbeat music)
0:34 So as a Graphic Designer, when you apply for a job,
0:37 you will showcase a portfolio of your work.
0:40 This will display all your creative skills
0:42 and work you have done to date.
0:44 As I have said in previous episodes of the series,
0:47 a good portfolio is arguably the most important thing
0:50 you will need to get a job.
0:52 It is what you are judged on.
0:54 However, there is something else potential clients
0:57 or employers will judge you on and that is your experience.
1:02 Now Graphic Design can be particularly competitive.
1:05 When many applicants are applying for a position,
1:07 experience can often be the deciding factor.
1:11 So as well as showcasing your work,
1:13 you will need to showcase your experience.
1:15 This comes in the form of a CV
1:17 that will go along with your portfolio.
1:20 Now I often get asked a lot of questions
1:22 about the Graphic Design CV.
1:24 Questions like, what makes for a good design CV?
1:28 What type of CV is best, print or digital?
1:31 What should I include?
1:33 How many pages should it be?
1:34 How much type should I include?
1:37 What are employers looking for?
1:39 And what's the best program to use to build my CV?
1:42 So in this video, I'm going to discuss the Graphic Design CV
1:46 and answer these questions.
1:47 In this video, I'll also be sharing my experience
1:50 and offer some tips for you to keep in mind.
1:53 So, the Graphic Design CV should go hand-in-hand
1:56 with the portfolio.
1:57 The CV should clearly state your skills
2:00 and relevant experience.
2:02 Often when I apply for a job, I will send two documents:
2:05 the portfolio and the CV.
2:08 So let's look at some of those questions.
2:11 So the first question is, what makes for a good design CV?
2:15 There are lots of factors that make for a good CV,
2:18 all of which you should try and implement in yours.
2:21 So first is originality.
2:24 Employers will see hundreds of CVs.
2:27 This is where you have a chance to stand out
2:29 and create something a little different.
2:31 Here you can consider orientation, color,
2:35 typography, and composition.
2:37 If you have a personal brand, you can apply it here.
2:40 Well structured, now you have to imagine
2:43 that the employer is browsing through lots of CVs
2:46 at a fast pace, and chances are the employer
2:48 will be looking for something specific.
2:51 In my experience, presenting a clear hierarchy is key,
2:55 so throughout your CV, aim to create
2:57 a clear hierarchy of information.
2:59 They might only glance at your cover page for a few seconds,
3:03 so one of the first things you want them
3:04 to see are your key skills.
3:07 So, on your front cover, you should consider including
3:10 a list of your key design strengths to software expertise.
3:13 This will catch the attention straightaway.
3:16 If you meet the criteria, chances are they will put your CV
3:19 into the short list.
3:21 Now typically you will have to include details
3:23 about your experience and if you have a lot
3:26 this can mean creating a CV with a few more pages
3:29 than you may want.
3:31 If you choose to add more info,
3:32 than leave that towards the back.
3:35 If someone does not want to read it, that's fine
3:37 as long as the key facts are presented clearly first.
3:41 Keep the layout simple and clear.
3:44 Now employers will be put off if the layout
3:46 of a CV is too complex, too busy, or hard to navigate.
3:51 This will demonstrate poor design skills,
3:53 reflect badly on you, and discourage the reader.
3:56 If you have a personal brand, keep it subtle.
3:59 The purpose of your CV is to be read and digested quickly.
4:03 The reader should be able to digest key facts
4:05 in less than a minute.
4:07 Don't distract from that.
4:09 So it will help to keep your layout nice and simple.
4:13 Show you have thought about it and taken care to compose it.
4:17 This will make a good impression.
4:19 Good typography, now remember an employer
4:22 will be scrutinizing everything they see from you,
4:25 your portfolio, and your CV.
4:28 Your CV is a representation of you.
4:31 Take maximum care and make sure your typography is flawless.
4:35 If your CV is nicely balanced, easy to read,
4:38 it will make a good impression.
4:40 Errors and sloppiness will reflect badly on you.
4:43 What type of CV is best, print or digital?
4:47 Well today, it is very common to send a CV over email
4:51 or upload it to a portfolio design website
4:54 as a PDF document.
4:56 A lot of employers will be looking at these on screen
4:59 and then more than likely will print them out.
5:01 Now my advice here is to create a digital DPF
5:04 that can be both viewed on screen and printed.
5:07 This means only having to create one document,
5:10 so you could consider creating a landscape CV
5:13 as this will look nice on screen.
5:16 In your PDF, you could also include hyperlinks
5:18 to your portfolio website or social media pages.
5:22 Remember, your digital CV will also have to work in print,
5:26 so try and create a clear design with contrast
5:29 between the type and any background color you may use.
5:33 If you are smart here, you can kill two birds
5:35 with one stone.
5:37 What exactly should I include?
5:39 So in your CV, you should include in order,
5:43 your key skills, this is one of the first things
5:46 you want someone to see.
5:48 This could be a list of your design strengths
5:50 to software experience.
5:53 A personal profile, so this should be a short paragraph
5:57 about yourself between 50 and 100 words.
6:00 This is where you get to say a few words about you
6:03 as a person and as a designer.
6:05 Here you can talk about your particular passions,
6:08 interests, and what you specialize in.
6:11 This should be one of the first things they see,
6:13 so aim to have this towards the front.
6:16 Education and qualifications, this should be a breakdown
6:20 of your relevant education.
6:22 You don't need to go that far back here.
6:25 A list of your relevant education regarding art
6:28 to design will be best here.
6:31 Work experience, so depending on how much experience
6:34 you have, this will be a list of the places you have worked.
6:38 Again, only include design-related work experience
6:41 and make sure it is in order of most recent.
6:45 For each experience, provide a brief summary
6:48 of what your role was, your responsibility,
6:50 and what you achieved.
6:52 Awards, anything you can add to give you the edge
6:56 and impress your potential employer is a bonus.
6:59 If you have any design awards, be sure to include them.
7:03 Hobbies and related interests, here is another chance
7:07 to get your personality across.
7:09 If you have any related hobbies or interests,
7:12 you can include these with a brief summary of each.
7:16 So, those are some key sections to include in your CV.
7:20 So, the next question is how many pages should it be?
7:25 So as you strive to create a clear and balanced layout,
7:28 you may have to spread your CV over a few pages.
7:31 The reader can always take or leave the finer details
7:34 about your experiences towards the back.
7:37 How much type do I include?
7:40 So as part of a simple and clear layout,
7:42 try and keep type to a minimum.
7:44 Try and edit the type as much as possible.
7:47 Remember, the reader should be able to digest key facts
7:50 in less than a minute.
7:52 Aim for bite size paragraphs that are to the point
7:55 and easily read and digested.
7:58 Also when using type, try and implement a clear hierarchy.
8:03 This will help the reader navigate through your CV
8:05 and find exactly what they are looking for.
8:08 What are employers looking for?
8:11 Now this really depends on what type of job
8:13 you're looking for and at what level.
8:15 Typically in the industry, there are three
8:18 significant levels: junior, middleweight, and senior.
8:22 At junior level, you may have just graduated
8:25 from university or looking to get your first job.
8:28 An employer will know you won't have
8:30 much industry experience, so will mainly be looking
8:33 for potential, a good attitude,
8:35 an interesting character, and passion.
8:38 At more senior levels, employers will be expecting
8:41 a more unique and original CV.
8:43 Employers will be focusing more on your experience
8:46 and what you have already done.
8:48 Employers will be looking for higher education,
8:51 years of experience, a proven track record,
8:54 well known brands you may have worked for,
8:56 achievements, awards, and responsibilities
9:00 you would have had and managed.
9:02 So, the last question is what's the best program
9:04 to use to build my CV?
9:06 Well when it comes to working layout and type focused,
9:10 I would always recommend Adobe InDesign.
9:13 In InDesign, you also have the ability
9:15 to create interactive PDFs where you could embed links
9:18 to your online media.
9:20 Now if you don't have access to Adobe InDesign,
9:23 as an alternative you can use Adobe Illustrator.
9:26 With the use of artboards, Illustrator will enable you
9:29 to create a simple document like a CV with a few pages.
9:34 So those are some factors to consider
9:36 to ensure a good Graphic Design CV.
9:39 If you would like to take a closer look

0:00 - Hello, and welcome to this beginners guide series
0:03 to graphic design.

0:08 design theory,

0:34 So once you have taken the time to complete your portfolio

0:40 Today, there is an abundance of ways you can find work.
0:43 Both on the internet and offline.
0:46 Now I often get asked the question,
0:48 how do I find a graphic design job?

0:52 to answer this question
0:54 and talk from experience and give some tips
0:56 and advice to keep in mind.
0:58 So my first tip would be
1:00 move closer to the work.
1:02 Now graphic design is a commercial job.
1:05 Most of your work is going to be for commercial companies
1:08 and organizations or perhaps the odd startup.
1:11 You will also be looking to work for a good
1:13 and well established design agency.
1:15 Today, all the good design agencies are close
1:17 to where the work is.
1:18 And that is in or near cities.
1:21 One of the first things I would recommend you consider
1:24 if you haven't already
1:25 is moving closer to the place where the work is.
1:28 And that may mean living in or near a city.
1:31 So I currently live in London, UK.
1:34 One of the design capitals of the world.
1:37 Now I'm not originally from here.
1:39 I'm not even English.
1:40 I'm from a neighboring country hundreds of miles away.
1:43 Now, I have grown to love this city,
1:45 but I never originally wanted to live here.
1:47 I came here because I knew this was where the work was at.
1:51 I moved here almost 10 years ago now,
1:53 and this is where I need to stay
1:54 in order to find regular work
1:56 or move to another city.
1:59 Now of course, not all design jobs are based in the city.
2:02 There are some companies that exist outside cities.
2:05 But the fact remains that most work
2:07 is in the urban city area.
2:10 As an alternative, you could try and work from home,
2:13 or even set up a business.
2:14 But that is typically done after a few years experience.
2:17 If you're starting out new,
2:19 you may find it easier to get started where most
2:21 of the work is.
2:23 So my next tip is,
2:24 have an online strategy and get noticed.
2:27 Now traditionally,
2:28 one of the only options you would have had
2:30 was to search through design magazines
2:32 and call up agencies
2:34 or maybe visit design agencies to find a job.
2:37 Over the past few years,
2:38 I've seen a big change in the way we as graphic designers
2:41 look for design jobs.
2:43 Through the development of technology,
2:45 the internet, and social media,
2:46 I have seen a huge shift from searching offline
2:49 to searching online.
2:51 Today, not only is the internet a way to find to find a job,
2:54 but also get discovered and noticed.
2:57 Which in turn, can help you get the job.
2:59 As a graphic designer today,
3:01 you not only need a strategy to find a job
3:03 but a strategy to get noticed.
3:06 Today, you can subscribe to many portfolio websites.
3:09 Sites that will allow you to upload your work,
3:11 allow companies to post jobs,
3:13 allow you to search and apply for jobs,
3:15 and allow companies to search profiles.
3:18 In my experience, I have gotten a few jobs
3:20 from simply having a profile online.
3:22 I have received calls and have been offered work
3:25 and taken work.
3:27 So be sure to be proactive online.
3:29 Set up a portfolio profile
3:31 on as many design websites as you can.
3:34 So my next tip is, use social media.
3:37 So as well as setting up a portfolio website,
3:39 consider using social media.
3:41 This is an awesome opportunity
3:43 for you to blog and share the work you do
3:46 to a global audience.
3:48 If you're at school or art college,
3:50 you can share your creative work
3:51 through various social media platforms.
3:54 Not only can you share your work,
3:56 but you can demonstrate your design process and thinking.
3:59 You can follow and communicate with design agencies
4:02 and get recognized before you even graduate.
4:05 I have seen someone get noticed via Twitter,
4:08 get an interview, and get a job.
4:09 It does happen.
4:11 Next tip, be proactive and create your own opportunities.
4:15 So as well as taking advantage of the online resource,
4:18 you can also embrace more traditional leads.
4:21 Try creating a list of your favorite agencies
4:23 and write to them directly.
4:25 Now online application can seem a little generic.
4:29 If you write a letter to a design agency,
4:31 it will seem a lot more personal
4:33 and it will show you have put in a specific effort.
4:36 This will make a bigger impact.
4:38 So write to your agencies,
4:40 introduce yourself,
4:41 list the reasons why you like that agency,
4:43 talk about their work,
4:45 show you have a genuine interest,
4:47 declare why you think you would be amazing for them,
4:50 what you would bring to the table,
4:51 and what you would hope to achieve.
4:53 Here, you have nothing to lose.
4:55 But everything to gain.
4:57 The worst that can happen here
4:58 is you may not hear back.
5:00 But you never know, you may get offered
5:02 an interview with a chance to get the job.
5:04 So my next tip is
5:06 think outside of the box.
5:08 Now if a design agency puts out a graphic design vacancy,
5:11 you can be sure that a lot of people are going
5:13 to be applying for it.
5:15 So that design agency is going to be looking at a lot
5:17 of CVs and portfolios.
5:19 So how are you going to stand out?
5:21 So what you could consider
5:23 is doing something a little more creative.
5:25 Think tactile, think a surprise.
5:28 Think alternative.
5:30 People love surprise,
5:31 unwrapping and being presented with something hands on.
5:34 Instead of sending the typical CV and portfolio,
5:37 here you can maybe make something more unique.
5:40 Once I created my own mail pack
5:42 to send to agencies I liked
5:44 and apply for jobs.
5:46 This contained a poster and a business card
5:48 all wrapped up in a custom made envelope.
5:51 On the one side was the artwork I created
5:53 and on the other was my cover letter and CV.
5:56 On this occasion, I invested a little money
5:58 to get them printed off.
6:00 By creating something, you add value to your submission,
6:04 stand out, and show you are willing to go the extra mile.
6:07 If it's good quality,
6:08 chances are the person receiving it
6:10 will not want to throw it away.
6:12 And you will remain in their presence
6:14 to be seen again later.
6:16 So by being creative
6:17 and making something a little more unique,
6:19 you can make a lasting impression.
6:21 Next tip, apply for internships.
6:25 Now if you're new to the design industry,
6:27 it can be difficult to get that first job
6:29 without any prior experience.
6:31 To help you find a job,
6:32 I would recommend you consider applying
6:34 for as many internships as you can.
6:37 In some cases, a lot of design agencies will not be
6:40 actively looking for designers,
6:42 but will be open to internships.
6:44 I have seen a lot of designers graduate
6:47 and use internships
6:48 as a way into the industry.
6:50 If you do well, make an impression, and work hard,
6:53 they just might offer you a permanent position.
6:55 It does happen.
6:57 I myself did a number of internships
6:59 when I graduated
7:00 and it really helped me
7:02 get some solid experience under my belt to start my career.
7:05 Next tip, seek help from friends or family.
7:09 Now this sounds like an obvious one,
7:11 but nevertheless, it's one I would recommend.
7:14 One of my favorite projects
7:15 and best projects came from a recommendation
7:18 my brother-in-law gave to his friend
7:20 who wanted someone to brand their new
7:22 startup street food business.
7:24 This was a great opportunity for me
7:26 to do their entire branding
7:27 and have a good project under my belt.
7:30 So let your family and friends know
7:32 you are on the lookout for work.
7:34 You never know where it might lead.
7:36 As well as seeking help from friends and family,
7:38 I would also recommend you introduce yourself
7:40 to local businesses and proposition them
7:43 with your creative offerings.
7:45 You could go as far as to pitch them a new brand
7:47 for their business or maybe a new website.
7:50 So my final tip is, get agents to find you work.
7:54 So another way to look for work is to get others
7:56 to find work for you.
7:58 In the design industry,
7:59 you can work as a freelancer.
8:01 If you go freelance with recruitment agencies,
8:03 you can get yourself some agents
8:05 that will find work for you on a contract basis.
8:09 This is currently how I get my work.
8:11 I have a number of agents that actively find work for me.
8:14 They are aware of my skillset,
8:16 my day rate, and the type of job I'm looking for.
8:20 My agents are in contact with many clients.
8:23 And with my CV and portfolio in hand,
8:25 they will apply on my behalf.
8:27 If the client likes what they see,
8:29 they will give me the contract.
8:31 So using recruitment agencies
8:33 and agents can be a really good way to find work.
8:36 So those are some of my tips
8:38 and ways to find a graphic design job.

URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FpCqLpeCA7I&list=PLYfCBK8IplO4E2sXtdKMVpKJZRBEoMvpn&index=40&pp=iAQB

0:00 - Hello and welcome to this Beginner's Guide Series
0:03 to Graphic Design.
0:04 From what graphic design is,
0:06 skills to be a graphic designer, design theory,
0:09 education you need, equipment you need,
0:11 to the graphic design portfolio and interview advice.
0:15 This series is for anyone at any level.
0:17 So if you're interested in graphic design
0:20 and considering becoming a graphic designer
0:22 then join me as I discuss a series of graphic design topics.
0:26 (lively music)
0:34 So you applied for a design job, they are interested
0:37 and want you to go in for an interview.
0:40 For some, this can be met with mixed feelings
0:42 of excitement, extreme pressure and worry.
0:45 The design interview will possibly be the biggest challenge
0:48 you will ever face as a graphic designer.
0:51 It's the one time where you are judged
0:53 on the things you say and how you present yourself.
0:56 How you perform in the interview will determine
0:58 if you get the job or not.
1:00 Now, I often get asked the question,
1:02 "What things can I do to best prepare
1:05 for a design interview?"
1:07 So in this video I'm going to attempt
1:09 to answer this question and share some of my experiences,
1:12 and offer some tips you can do to help prepare
1:14 for a graphic design interview.
1:16 Now you can never be too prepared for a design interview.
1:19 You never know what an interviewer is going to ask you.
1:22 In my experience I find the better prepared you feel,
1:26 the more confident and relaxed you will be.
1:29 And there are lots of things you can do for this.
1:32 So my first tip would be prepare your presentation.
1:36 Now, unlike most job interviews where you might have to sit
1:39 opposite an interviewer and answer questions,
1:42 the design interview is more like a presentation.
1:45 For a graphic design interview you should be ready
1:47 to present your work and yourself.
1:50 So these are some tips to prepare for your presentation.
1:54 First tip, practice your portfolio presentation.
1:58 Now some people are gifted and can talk
2:00 about their work very well on the spot.
2:02 For others this can be very hard.
2:05 If you're like me, and struggle to talk
2:07 about your work on the spot, I would recommend
2:09 before the interview, decide what you're going to say
2:12 about your work and in what order,
2:14 and try and memorize it.
2:16 Now you may be presenting with a print portfolio
2:19 or you may be presenting on a laptop or tablet.
2:22 Be sure to practice your delivery.
2:24 This may include parts of your portfolio
2:26 you may want to zoom into on the screen.
2:28 Pages you want to pause on, links you want to showcase,
2:31 or print examples you want to pull out.
2:34 Be sure to practice your presentation a few times
2:37 either to yourself or to a friend.
2:40 If you can get someone to present to, get them to ask you
2:43 lots of difficult questions.
2:45 Get comfortable presenting in front of people
2:47 and being challenged about your work.
2:50 Get used to thinking fast
2:51 and delivering answers to questions.
2:54 By doing this you will be more confident.
2:57 You will have a good idea what you're going to say
2:59 and you will be better prepared
3:01 to deliver a smooth presentation and answer questions.
3:05 Next tip.
3:06 Be prepared to only talk about three of your projects.
3:10 Now this could catch you off guard.
3:12 Sometimes the interviewer will ask you
3:14 to only talk about three of your projects.
3:17 Now this could be for a number of reasons.
3:19 It could be down to time constraints for the interview,
3:22 or a test to see how prepared you are.
3:25 So be prepared to talk about three of your projects.
3:29 Now don't pick your favorite projects.
3:31 Make sure to pick the projects that best demonstrate
3:34 your skills and experience.
3:36 Next tip.
3:37 Tailor your portfolio.
3:39 So before the interview, keep in mind
3:41 what the design job is requiring.
3:44 One of the smartest things you can do to prepare
3:46 for an interview is tailor your portfolio
3:49 to the role you're going for.
3:50 In some cases, if you have too much variety
3:53 in your portfolio it may confuse a potential client
3:56 or employer.
3:58 Tailoring your portfolio could be a key factor.
4:01 This can make you seem more suitable
4:02 and specialized for the job.
4:05 So if the role is for a brand designer
4:07 include your best branding examples.
4:10 If the role is for a motion graphics designer
4:13 include your best motion graphics examples.
4:16 If the role is for a web designer
4:18 include your best web design examples.
4:21 Try and edit anything out that you feel is not relevant.
4:25 However, having said that, if the role is looking
4:28 for an all rounder, you may want to show a variety of work.
4:32 So remember, tailor your portfolio
4:34 to your potential employer.
4:36 Next tip.
4:38 Have the portfolio ready on device.
4:40 If you're planning to showcase your portfolio
4:42 on a laptop or tablet, make sure your devices
4:46 are fully charged and the portfolio is preloaded
4:49 on your computer or tablet.
4:51 Be ready to pull out your laptop or tablet
4:54 and present your work in seconds.
4:56 If you have accessories for your devices
4:58 to present on a TV screen, take them with you.
5:02 You may find yourself in a room with a TV screen.
5:05 Take advantage, and present your work on the big screen.
5:08 Next tip, have examples at the ready.
5:11 So as you go through your portfolio
5:13 and talk about your projects, be ready to show
5:15 any examples that will go with it.
5:18 If you plan on talking about your print project,
5:20 be sure to have examples ready to pull out and show.
5:23 If you have digital work like websites, videos, or apps,
5:27 be ready to have them open and ready to show
5:29 on your devices.
5:31 So the last tip here is have something to leave behind
5:34 after the interview.
5:36 Be sure to have something prepared to give them
5:38 before you leave.
5:40 Now this could be a simple as a business card
5:42 or something a little more creative.
5:44 Be sure it is well thought out, memorable, and unique.
5:48 Perhaps it incorporates your personal brand.
5:50 Think tactile, think surprise, think alternative.
5:54 People love surprise and wrapping,
5:56 and being presented with something hands-on.
5:59 By leaving something behind you will stand out
6:01 and show you are willing to go the extra mile.
6:04 If it's good quality, chances are the person receiving it
6:07 will not want to throw it away
6:09 and you will remain in their presence,
6:10 to be seen again later.
6:12 So by being creative and making something
6:14 a little more unique you can make a lasting impression.
6:18 So that's few things you can do to help you prepare
6:21 for your presentation.
6:23 Now one of the other important things I recommend you do
6:26 is prepare for questions.
6:28 Now for me, this is the most dreaded part of an interview.
6:32 You can be well-prepared with your presentation
6:34 but you never know what to expect
6:36 when it comes to questions.
6:38 You never know what you're going to get asked.
6:41 In my experience it helps to do as much as you can
6:43 to prepare for questions.
6:45 So if you are challenged on the spot,
6:47 you have a good answer.
6:49 In my experience, it helps to do as much as you can
6:51 to prepare for questions,
6:53 so if you are challenged on the spot,
6:54 you have a good answer.
6:56 This will enable you to make a good impression,
6:59 show you have done your research,
7:00 and have a genuine interest in the agency,
7:03 which will help you get the job.
7:05 So these are some things you can do
7:06 to help prepare you for questions.
7:08 So my first tip is, clue up on the company beforehand.
7:13 Before an interview be sure to read up on the company
7:16 you are applying for.
7:18 Take some time to research them on the Internet.
7:21 Read the history on their website.
7:23 See who is working for them.
7:24 Find out who their clients are.
7:27 These may be discussion points during the interview,
7:29 or they may ask you a question
7:31 to test your knowledge of the agency.
7:33 If you can mention a few names
7:35 or reference some of the points you have read
7:38 this will show you are proactive,
7:39 have taken time to research,
7:41 and have interest in the agency.
7:43 Next tip.
7:45 Have a few of their projects in mind to talk about.
7:48 Be prepared to talk about their work.
7:51 Research the work they have done
7:52 and have at least three projects to talk about.
7:55 Be able to talk about why you like the work,
7:58 how you think it was successful,
8:00 and why it inspired you to apply to work for them.
8:03 This will show you have a genuine interest
8:06 and a passion to work for that agency.
8:08 Next tip.
8:09 Know what your ideal project would be.
8:12 I get asked this a lot in interviews.
8:15 This is where an interviewer will want to know
8:17 a little bit more about you, your passions and ambitions.
8:20 Be prepared to talk about your ideal project.
8:24 What would the creative work be?
8:26 What would you want to produce?
8:28 Who would it be for and why?
8:30 Next tip, know what you want to achieve at the company.
8:35 At an interview you may be asked,
8:37 "What would you like to achieve at our agency?"
8:40 This is where an interviewer will be looking to see
8:42 what your intentions are, and how ambitious you are.
8:45 Be ready to talk about what you want to achieve
8:48 at the company.
8:49 This could be what you want to learn,
8:51 responsibilities you want to have,
8:53 experience you want to get,
8:55 type of work you want to produce,
8:56 and possibilities of promotion.
8:59 So my last tip is have at least six questions for them.
9:03 Now, I recommend six because some may get answered
9:06 during the course of the interview.
9:08 At the end of the interview you will always be asked
9:11 if you have any questions for them.
9:13 So this is another opportunity for you to demonstrate
9:16 you are proactive and have a genuine interest
9:18 in their agency.
9:20 So aim to have at least six good questions to ask them.
9:24 This could involve questions about the agency,
9:27 the role you are applying for,
9:28 and questions about opportunities within the agency.
9:32 Try and ask positive and well thought-out questions.
9:35 This will count for a lot in your interview
9:38 and help you get the job.
9:40 Now if you're wondering what kind of questions will be good,
9:43 in the next episode I'll be listing the type of questions
9:46 you can consider asking.
9:48 So to best prepare for the graphic design interview,
9:51 I would recommend that you prepare for your presentation
9:53 and prepare for questions.
9:55 If you would like to take a closer look
9:56 at the lists discussed in this video you can find them
9:59 in the PDF document that accompanies this video series.
