Title: Questions you’re asked at a Graphic Design interview? Ep40/45 [Beginners guide to Graphic Design]
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PAHq4uIz06M&list=PLYfCBK8IplO4E2sXtdKMVpKJZRBEoMvpn&index=41&pp=iAQB
Channel: YouTube playlist
Video ID: PAHq4uIz06M
Transcript language: English
Downloaded at: 2026-05-25T21:22:58.797Z

0:00 - Hello, and welcome to this
0:01 beginner's guide series to graphic design.
0:04 From what graphic design is,
0:06 skills to be a graphic designer,
0:08 design theory, education you need, equipment you need,
0:11 to the graphic design portfolio and interview advice.
0:15 This series is for anyone at any level,
0:17 so if you're interested in graphic design,
0:20 and considering becoming a graphic designer,
0:22 then join me as I discuss a series of graphic design topics.
0:26 (upbeat music)
0:34 So at a graphic design interview,
0:36 after presenting your work,
0:37 you will typically be asked a variety of questions.
0:40 An interviewer will want to get a good picture
0:42 of you and your ability.
0:44 They will not only be seeking to learn if you are qualified,
0:47 but also seek to learn if you're right for the team.
0:49 You will never know what questions you may get asked.
0:52 Depending on how much an interviewer wants to challenge you,
0:55 you may be tested to see how you could react
0:57 to questions on the spot.
0:58 Now, there are hundreds of possible questions
1:00 you could get asked.
1:02 One tip I would give for those preparing
1:04 for a graphic design interview
1:06 is be ready to answer difficult questions.
1:09 In my experience, the only way to prepare for this
1:12 is to create as many difficult questions as you can
1:15 and develop answers for them.
1:17 If you're then asked a difficult question at the interview,
1:20 then hopefully you will have something
1:21 to pull out and answer with.
1:23 So in this video, I'm going to share some of the questions
1:26 I have been asked at an interview and list some
1:29 of the others you can consider asking yourself
1:31 in preparation for a graphic design interview.
1:34 At an interview, you will typically
1:36 be asked a range of personal questions,
1:38 questions about the agency,
1:40 questions about your design experience,
1:42 and questions about your work experience.
1:45 So for this video,
1:47 I have broken down the questions into four categories.
1:50 Personal questions, company questions,
1:53 design questions, and work experience questions.
1:56 In this video, I'm going to list 10 common questions
2:00 for each category.
2:01 If you would like to take a closer look
2:03 at the lists I'm about to give,
2:04 you can find them in the PDF document
2:06 that accompanies this series.
2:08 The download link is in the description.
2:10 So first, I'm going to list some of the personal questions
2:13 you might expect to get asked at an interview.
2:15 So personal questions.
2:18 Why have you chosen graphic design as a career?
2:21 Who inspired you to become a graphic designer?
2:25 Name a designer who inspires you.
2:28 What are your three strengths?
2:30 And what are your three weaknesses?
2:32 Of all your projects,
2:34 which one represents you the best and why?
2:37 If you had to redo one of your projects,
2:39 which one would it be and how would you do it?
2:42 What are your goals for personal development,
2:45 and what steps have to taken to accomplish these goals?
2:48 Which brand would you love to work on and why?
2:52 Given the freedom what would your ideal project be?
2:55 What do you see yourself doing in five years?
2:58 What are your goals?
3:00 So those are some personal questions you might expect.
3:04 By asking these questions, the interviewer will be trying
3:07 to get a good idea of your character
3:09 and gage if you are a good fit for the team.
3:12 Your answers to these questions will say a lot about you.
3:16 So next we have company questions.
3:18 So the type of company questions to expect would be
3:21 what do you know about us?
3:24 Why do you want to work for this agency?
3:26 What's your favorite piece of work we have done?
3:30 Which one of our clients would you like to work on?
3:34 Why do you think you are suitable for this role?
3:37 What do you want to achieve at this agency?
3:40 What challenges are you looking for in this position?
3:43 You may be given strict parameters for your designs,
3:47 will that stifle your creativity?
3:49 How comfortable are you being told what to design?
3:54 How comfortable are you meeting and presenting to clients?
3:57 So those are some agency questions to expect.
4:00 By asking these questions, the interviewer will be trying
4:03 to see how proactive you are.
4:05 Have you taken time to research the agency?
4:08 And if you are genuinely interested
4:10 in the role at the agency.
4:12 Your answers to these questions will say a lot
4:14 about your interest and intentions to work for the agency.
4:18 So next, we have design experience questions.
4:22 So the type of design experience questions to expect
4:25 would be what were your biggest creative achievements
4:28 in your last creative role?
4:30 What is your typical approach to a design brief?
4:34 How much time should be dedicated to research?
4:37 What would your approach be
4:39 to meet a deadline of five hours?
4:42 What would your approach be
4:43 to meet a deadline for five weeks?
4:46 How many design ideas do you usually sketch
4:48 before choosing one?
4:50 Tell us a time you had to compromise on a project,
4:53 and how you dealt with it?
4:54 How would you tackle something you have no experience at?
4:58 Do you prefer working alone or in a team and why?
5:02 List some of the designs you have created
5:04 and list their successes.
5:06 So those are some design experience questions to expect.
5:10 By asking these questions, the interviewer will be trying
5:13 to get a good idea of your level of skill.
5:16 This will give them a good idea if you will be able
5:19 to slot straight into the agency
5:21 or if you will need some extra guidance or help.
5:24 Your answers to these questions will give the interviewer
5:27 a good idea of your level of experience.
5:29 So finally, we have work experience questions.
5:34 So the type of work experience questions to expect would be
5:37 why did you leave your last role?
5:40 If a client disagreed with you,
5:42 but you know it's a good idea,
5:44 how would convince them it's a good direction?
5:47 Have you ever been in a leadership role?
5:49 Please explain the situation.
5:51 How do you cope under pressure?
5:54 How do you work with others?
5:56 Tell us a time you worked in a team,
5:58 and how you worked together to accomplish a task?
6:02 Can you present to a room of 20?
6:05 How do you deal with criticism?
6:07 Which software do you prefer to work with and why?
6:11 What do you enjoy most about working as a graphic designer?
6:15 So those are some work experience questions to expect.
6:19 By asking these questions, the interviewer will be trying
6:22 to get a good idea of your character, your attitude,
6:25 and if you will easily fit into the agency.
6:28 Your answers to these questions will give the interviewer
6:31 a good idea of how you can work as part of a team.
6:34 So those are some of the questions I have been asked
6:37 at an interview and some others you could consider thinking
6:40 about before you attend a graphic design interview.
6:43 If you'd like to take a closer look
6:45 at the lists discussed in this video,
6:47 you can find them in the PDF document
6:49 that accompanies this video series.
6:51 The download link is in the description.
6:54 So what questions have you been asked
6:56 in a graphic design interview?
6:58 Do you have any other good questions?
7:00 Be sure to share them in the comments below.
7:02 Well, I hope you enjoyed this video.
7:04 If you did, hit the Like button on my Facebook page.
7:07 If you'd like to see more videos like this in the future,
7:10 hit the Subscribe button.
7:11 And you can also follow me on Twitter at TastyTuts.
7:14 So in this video, I listed some of the key questions
7:17 to prepare for for a graphic design interview.
7:20 Now at the end of an interview, you will always be asked
7:23 if you have any questions for them.
7:26 This is a great opportunity for you to demonstrate you
7:29 are proactive and have a genuine interest in their agency.
7:33 Now I would recommend you have
7:34 at least six good questions to ask them.
7:37 So in the next video,
7:39 I'm going to list a bunch of questions you can consider
7:41 asking an agency at the end of an interview.
7:44 See you in the next video.
7:46 (upbeat music)

1:47 So first I'm going to list some of the agency questions
1:50 you might want to ask.

1:56 for this agency?

2:08 or more instructional?

2:24 gets done at this agency?
2:26 What is this agency most passionate about?
2:29 What are the common attributes of your top performers?
2:33 So try and ask questions that show genuine interest
2:36 in the present and the future of the company.
2:39 Your questions will say a lot about you.
2:41 By asking these questions, you can get a good idea
2:44 if it is a good agency you want to work for.
2:47 So those are some agency questions to ask.
2:50 So next we have job role questions.
2:53 So the type of role questions you can ask could be,

3:22 What's the worst thing about working here?
3:25 What do you see as the biggest challenge
3:27 for this new candidate?
3:29 So try and ask questions that show genuine interest
3:32 in the role and give you a better picture
3:35 of what is required.
3:36 By asking these questions, you can get a good idea
3:39 if the role is right for you.
3:41 So finally we have career prospect questions.

3:59 What are the opportunities to progress?

4:06 If the candidate progresses as you like,

4:21 What have previous designers go on to do?
4:25 After everything you have seen and heard,
4:27 do you see me as a good fit for this agency?
4:30 So try and ask questions that show genuine interest

Title: Interview tips to be a Graphic Designer? Ep42/45 [Beginners guide to Graphic Design]
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0eY0qSWhrTc&list=PLYfCBK8IplO4E2sXtdKMVpKJZRBEoMvpn&index=43&pp=iAQB

Video ID: 0eY0qSWhrTc
Transcript language: English
Downloaded at: 2026-05-25T21:22:58.797Z

0:00 - Hello and welcome to this Beginner's guide series
0:03 to Graphic Design, from what graphic design is,
0:06 skills to be a graphic designer, design theory,
0:09 education you need, equipment you need,
0:11 to the graphic design portfolio, and interview advice.
0:15 This series is for anyone at any level.
0:18 So if you're interested in graphic design,
0:20 and considering becoming a graphic designer,
0:22 then join me as I discuss this series
0:24 of graphic design topics.
0:26 (upbeat music)
0:34 So you have done all your preparation,
0:36 ready for your interview.
0:37 You have prepared for your presentation,
0:40 carefully thought out answers
0:41 to a range of possible questions,
0:43 and got a good list of questions to ask,
0:45 now it's time to attend the interview.
0:47 Now the design interview
0:49 can be a big challenge to overcome,
0:51 and tough to get through.
0:52 You will be tested, and you will be judged.
0:55 In my experience, I have had good interviews,
0:57 and I have had bad interviews.
1:00 I find the more you do, the better it gets.
1:03 Now if you're new to the industry,
1:04 you may not have much interview experience.
1:07 So in this video, I'm going to share some of my experiences,
1:11 and offer some tips you can keep in mind
1:13 to help you have a successful interview.
1:15 Now every interviewer, or agency, will have their own
1:18 unique method for conductin' an interview.
1:20 In my experience, I have attended lots
1:23 and none are ever the same.
1:25 However, there is one thing that you can be sure of.
1:27 Now matter how different the role,
1:29 no matter what the agency, everyone will be judging you
1:32 on two main things, one your character,
1:35 and two your ability.
1:36 Remember, they will be lookin' for someone
1:39 who can be part of their team,
1:40 just as much as the ability to do the job.
1:43 So for this video, I'm going to separate my tips
1:45 into four sections, character tips, presentation tips,
1:49 essential dos, and essential don'ts.
1:53 So here are a number of character tips
1:55 you can consider, when undertakin' an interview.
1:57 So lets' start from the beginning.
2:00 So my first tip is, show up exactly on time,
2:03 not early, or not late.
2:06 If you arrive 10 minutes early wait outside.
2:09 Chances are, you will either be interviewed
2:11 after someone else, or the interviewer
2:13 will be taking time out of their workday to see you.
2:16 So stick to the time, you don't want to rush anyone,
2:19 or be late.
2:20 Next tip, memorize the names of the people
2:22 that you will be meeting before you arrive.
2:25 The last thing you want to do, is forget the names
2:27 of the people who are about to interview you.
2:30 When you arrive, you'll want to greet them by their names,
2:33 and talk to them throughout the interview.
2:35 This will make a good impression.
2:37 Next tip, give a good firm handshake.
2:40 First impressions are everything.
2:42 Show you mean business and you're confident
2:45 with a good handshake.
2:47 Next tip, eye contact with everyone.
2:50 So be prepared to be interviewed
2:52 by more than just one person.
2:55 Now this is generally done, so after your interview,
2:58 the people who interviewed you can compare notes.
3:01 In my experience, you will often get one
3:03 that asks all the questions,
3:04 and one that just sits quietly and observes,
3:07 or maybe asks a few questions every now and then.
3:10 Don't just talk at the person who addresses you.
3:13 When you're asked a question, deliver your answer
3:16 to everyone in the room.
3:18 When you talk and give your answer
3:19 make sure to make eye contact with everyone.
3:22 This will give off a good impression
3:24 and demonstrate good presentation and people skills.
3:28 You'll seem confident, considerate, and likable.
3:31 Next tip, take your time answering questions.
3:35 Now if there is ever a time you need your wits about you
3:37 it's at an interview.
3:39 Be prepared to answer lots of questions.
3:42 You may find some a real challenge to answer,
3:44 and this could catch you off guard.
3:46 Remember to take your time,
3:48 even if you have to pause for a second to think,
3:50 take your time.
3:51 If you cannot think of an answer,
3:53 ask if you can come back to the question later,
3:55 while you have a chance to think.
3:57 Next tip, where you lack experience,
4:00 make up for in eagerness to learn.
4:02 Now depending on your level, and what job you are going for,
4:06 you will be tested on your experience.
4:08 If you do not have a lot of experience
4:10 to answer some tough questions,
4:12 don't be afraid to admit
4:13 that you don't have that experience.
4:15 Be ready to confront it with a positive response.
4:19 Here you can reply by saying what you might do
4:21 to overcome that challenge,
4:22 and what you would hope to learn.
4:24 This will display a good attitude and willingness to learn.
4:28 So these are a few character tips.
4:30 Try and keep these in mind, as they will help you
4:32 make a good impression.
4:34 So once you and the interviewer are settled in,
4:36 it will come the time to showcase your work.
4:39 Here are some tips to present your portfolio.
4:43 So my first tip is, stand up and present.
4:47 Now this may seem obvious to some,
4:49 but you would be surprised how many times I have been told
4:52 that people don't do this.
4:54 Now remember, your interview is a presentation,
4:57 but it can also be a performance.
4:59 When asked to talk about your work,
5:01 don't just talk about it, stand up and present it.
5:04 If your interviewers are sitting down,
5:07 here you have a chance to take control.
5:09 Stand up and present your work.
5:11 You will also find that by standing up,
5:13 you will be able to speak more clear,
5:15 and breathe more clearly, which will hopefully make you feel
5:18 a little bit more at ease.
5:19 This will say a lot about your character,
5:22 you're confident, you can take control,
5:24 and you can present well, impressionable qualities
5:27 that they will remember.
5:28 So next tip, start on a high.
5:31 Be sure to kick off your portfolio presentation
5:34 with one of your most interestin' and impressive projects.
5:38 By starting with one of your most impressive projects,
5:41 you will start with confidence.
5:42 And by the time you're done talkin' about your first project
5:45 you will be nicely warmed up, settled in,
5:47 and good to carry on.
5:49 This will also make for a good first impression
5:51 of your work.
5:52 Next tip, talk in detail about your work.
5:56 Now chances are,
5:57 the interviewer will have already seen your work,
6:00 and you got the interview because they liked what they saw.
6:03 Here the interviewer will be expecting you
6:05 to talk about your work.
6:06 They will be looking to get a good idea
6:08 of your involvement in the work,
6:10 and will be taking a note of your presentation skills.
6:14 As you talk about each project,
6:16 be sure to touch on some of the following.
6:18 Say what you did, your involvement in the project.
6:22 If you worked in a team, talk about who was involved.
6:25 Talk about the challenges you faced.
6:27 Talk about your inspirations.
6:29 If you can, show your design process,
6:32 and talk about your research method.
6:34 Explain your design decisions.
6:36 Talk about how long it took to develop.
6:38 Explain how it satisfied the brief.
6:41 Did you exceed expectations?
6:43 And finally, finish with any success stories.
6:46 So my next tip is, talk calmly and anticipate questions.
6:50 As you talk about your work, you will trigger questions.
6:53 Remember to take your time, and allow the interviewer
6:56 to ask you questions.
6:59 When asked a question, try and give a clear
7:01 and direct answer.
7:03 Next tip, show print and digital examples.
7:07 If you have any print examples to go with your portfolio,
7:11 be sure to have them ready to show.
7:13 As much as in situ shots can look great in your portfolio,
7:17 interviewers love seeing the work up close
7:19 and in their hands.
7:21 Give something for the interviewer to hold and look at.
7:24 Next tip, end on a high.
7:27 When an interviewer tries to recall you after an interview,
7:31 they will either remember your first impression,
7:33 or how you ended.
7:34 Be sure to end on a high.
7:36 When closing your portfolio presentation,
7:38 be sure to put one of your best projects at the end.
7:41 This will wrap up the presentation on the highest note,
7:44 and hopefully leave a lasting impression.
7:47 So those are some presentation tips.
7:49 Try and keep these in mind as they will help you
7:52 deliver a good presentation.
7:54 So the next list of tips are essential dos.
7:57 These are the things, you really should do at an interview.
8:00 So the first tip is, ask questions.
8:04 Make an effort to be engaged in the interview.
8:07 Whenever possible try and ask questions.
8:10 This will show good communication skills.
8:12 You will have the opportunity to ask some questions
8:15 at the end.
8:16 Be sure to have at least six good ones
8:18 that will show the interviewer you have a genuine interest
8:20 in the role and the agency.
8:23 Next tip, pull out a notepad, or sketchbook.
8:27 Appear engaged and organized.
8:29 Take notes and references.
8:31 Next tip, ask for a business card.
8:34 Towards the end of the interview, before you leave,
8:37 be sure to ask for their business cards.
8:39 This will show a genuine interest in the role
8:41 and the people you have been talking to.
8:44 So the last tip is, leave your business card,
8:47 or something interesting behind.
8:49 Be sure to have something prepared to give them
8:51 before you leave.
8:53 Now this could be as simple as a business card,
8:55 or something a little more creative.
8:57 Be sure it is well thought out, memorable, and unique.
9:00 Perhaps it incorporates your personal brand.
9:03 Think tactile, think surprise, think alternative.
9:07 People love surprise, and wrapping,
9:09 and being presented with something hands on.
9:11 By leaving something behind, you will stand out,
9:14 and show you are willing to go the extra mile.
9:17 If it's good quality,
9:18 chances are the person receivin' it
9:19 will not want to throw it away,
9:21 and you will remain in their presence,
9:23 to be seen again later.
9:24 So by being creative,
9:26 and making something a little more unique,
9:28 you can make a lasting impression.
9:30 So the last list of tips here are essential don'ts.
9:34 These are some things you really should try and avoid doing,
9:36 when at an interview.
9:38 Don't criticize your last job, or people you worked with.
9:42 This is a big one.
9:43 Even if you had a really bad experience
9:45 at your previous job,
9:46 or you did not enjoy working with someone,
9:48 do not express it.
9:50 If you did have a tough time,
9:52 then explain the challenges you faced,
9:54 and the creative differences you had.
9:57 There is always two sides to each story,
9:59 and they will not know the other.
10:01 Do not moan, or criticize, because it will be a show
10:04 of bad character, appear negative,
10:07 and can put off your potential employer.
10:09 If you did have a bad experience, you can use it
10:12 as an example to show how you dealt with a tough situation.
10:15 If you can display understanding, determination,
10:18 and maturity, this will work well to impress your employer.
10:21 Next tip, don't talk about money.
10:24 Only talk about money if they bring it up first.
10:27 If you bring up the subject of money,
10:29 it will come across like you're more interested
10:31 in getting paid, than focusin' on producing great work.
10:34 It will come across unprofessional.
10:37 Next tip, don't talk about holiday.
10:40 So this is the same reason
10:42 as why you should not talk about money,
10:44 unless of course, they bring it up first.
10:46 An interviewer will want someone who is passionate
10:49 about their craft, motivated, and focused.
10:52 Talking about holiday, before you even have the job,
10:54 will come across unprofessional.
10:57 So the last tip here is, don't appear desperate
11:00 for the job.
11:01 If you really like the sound of the job,
11:03 be sure to show enthusiasm.
11:05 An interviewer will see this as a very positive sign.
11:08 But the key here is to reinforce this with good reason,
11:12 as this will strike confidence into the interviewer.
11:15 Don't just say, I really want this job.
11:17 Say something more like,
11:19 I would love this opportunity because,
11:21 and touch on some reasons.
11:23 State your interest in the kind of work they do.
11:26 Compliment their work, and explain how you would like
11:28 to aspire to produce work of the same quality.
11:32 State how you would be inspired in their agency
11:34 to produce great work, and how much you would like to work
11:37 with the creative people and the team.
11:40 If you can show genuine interest with good reason,
11:43 then you will not come across as desperate.
11:45 Try and be cool, calm, and considered.
11:48 So those are some tips
11:49 to help you get a graphic design interview.
11:52 If you'd like to take a closer look at the lists
11:54 discussed in this video, you can find out more
11:56 in the pdf document that accompanies this video series.
12:00 Download link is in the description.
12:02 So what do you think?
12:04 Do any of you have some interesting interview stories,
12:07 and tips to share?
12:08 Be sure to share your comments below.
12:11 Well I hope you enjoyed this video.
12:12 If you did, hit the like button on my Facebook page.
12:15 If you'd like to see more videos like this in future,
12:18 hit the subscribe button.
12:20 And you can also follow me on Twitter @TastyTuts.
12:23 So that concludes section three of this series,
12:26 Becoming a Graphic Designer.
12:28 Now we are going to move onto section four,
12:31 Graphic Design and Beyond.
12:33 In the first video of that section,
12:35 I'm going to talk about the design industry
12:37 and what it's like to be a graphic designer,
12:39 and the career options within the industry.
12:42 So see you in the next video.
12:44 (upbeat music)

0:00 - Hello and welcome to this beginner's guide series
0:02 to graphic design.
0:04 From what graphic design is,

0:34 So, after a successful graphic design interview,
0:37 at the design agency, one would hopefully get offered a job.
0:40 If one accepts, they will find themselves in a new job
0:44 in the design industry.
0:45 Now, like most industries,
0:46 graphic design has a position system.
0:49 This system is typically defined
0:51 by experience and ability.
0:53 Indeed, graphic design is broad
0:55 and there can be a range of specialties.
0:58 However, the position system
0:59 is pretty much the same throughout.
1:01 When entering into the design industry,
1:04 depending on education, experience and ability,
1:07 you could enter in at any level.
1:09 After accepting a job, depending on what job is undertaken,
1:13 one will find themselves at a particular point
1:16 in this position system.
1:17 As you develop your experience and build a reputation,
1:21 you can move up the career ladder.
1:23 In this video, I'm going to talk about this position system
1:27 and the typical career options within the industry.
1:29 In this video, I'll also share some of my experience.
1:33 So, in the graphic design industry,
1:34 you typically have two main role categories.
1:37 One, creative workforce roles and two, direction roles.
1:42 For the creative workforce roles,
1:44 we have intern designer, junior designer,
1:48 middleweight designer and senior designer.
1:51 For the direction roles, you have head of design
1:55 or also referred to as lead designer, art director,
1:59 senior creative and creative director.
2:03 Now, depending on the agency you work for,
2:05 there may be additional levels or, perhaps,
2:08 different terms for these.
2:09 These are the typical levels found in the industry
2:12 and can mean different things
2:13 to different agencies and designers.
2:16 The creative workforce roles will include roles
2:19 of research, creative thinking and problem solving.
2:22 The manual task of producing creative work
2:24 on computer and delivery.
2:26 In one's career, you will typically start
2:29 in the creative workforce.
2:31 And, with experience, ability and ambition,
2:33 work your way up to a directing role.
2:36 The direction roles do vary
2:38 in responsibility and specialty.
2:40 One may spend many years in the workforce as a designer
2:44 to realize what potential they have
2:46 and which directive role they may wish to pursue.
2:49 The direction roles will typically guide
2:52 and direct the creative workforce.
2:54 They will be responsible for using the skills
2:56 and talents of the creative workforce
2:58 to create a high standard creative solution.
3:02 So, now, I'm going to talk about each level in more detail.
3:05 So, first is the intern designer.
3:07 So, the intern designer role is the entry level
3:11 and depending on performance can last from a few weeks
3:15 to a few months.
3:15 This can be the first step for a designer entering
3:18 into the industry.
3:20 Internships are good for designers looking
3:22 to get crucial experience at the start of their career.
3:26 This role comes with little to no responsibility.
3:28 As an intern, you may be shadowing other designers
3:31 or helping them out with various tasks.
3:34 The type of designers that typically apply for internships
3:37 are students, graduates or newcomers to the industry.
3:41 This is the time where an agency will see
3:44 if you have what it takes to be fully employed.
3:47 If they feel you fit,
3:48 they may offer you a permanent position
3:50 as a junior designer.
3:52 When I graduated from university back in 2007,
3:56 I ended up taking a few internships.
3:58 This was good experience
4:00 and I had the opportunity to get involved in projects.
4:03 This crucial experience helped me get a job later on
4:07 as a junior designer.
4:08 So, next, we have the junior designer.
4:11 So, the junior designer role is another entry level.
4:14 The difference between the intern designer
4:16 and the junior designer level is that the junior level
4:19 is paid, whereas a lot of internships are not.
4:22 Like the internship,
4:24 this can be the first step for a designer entering
4:26 into the industry for the first time.
4:28 One of your main responsibilities will be
4:30 to be produce creative work
4:32 and help out with various design tasks.
4:35 This role comes with little responsibility
4:37 and one should be encouraged to learn
4:39 and shadow other designers.
4:41 As a junior designer,
4:42 you will be briefed by senior members of the team
4:45 and directed on what to do creatively
4:47 and encouraged to bring your ideas to the table.
4:50 As you produce your work, you will have to report
4:52 to senior members or you will be advised
4:54 on amendments if necessary.
4:57 The type of designers that typically apply
4:59 for a junior designer role
5:00 will be graduates or newcomers to the industry.
5:03 As you undertake your role as a junior designer
5:06 over a few short years,
5:08 you will learn and develop more skills
5:09 and produce more work.
5:11 With more experience, you will become more middleweight.
5:14 If you are fortunate, your employer will recognize this
5:17 and reward you with an increase in salary
5:19 and a new official title of middleweight designer.
5:23 Now, this does not always happen.
5:25 On a lot of occasions, to move up,
5:27 you have to move out.
5:28 A lot of designers will have to leave an agency
5:31 in order to practice at a higher level.
5:33 I was actually a junior designer for a number of years
5:36 in my first role.
5:37 When I realized there was no room to maneuver
5:40 in my first agency,
5:41 I decided to look for a new job.
5:43 Eventually, I moved into a new agency
5:46 as a middleweight designer.
5:48 So, next, we have the middleweight designer.
5:50 So, the middleweight designer role comes
5:52 with more responsibility and expectation.
5:55 Just like with the junior role, your main responsibilities
5:58 will be the produce creative work.
6:00 Though, at this level, you will be expected to design
6:03 and execute your work to a higher standard
6:05 and often faster.
6:06 As a middleweight designer,
6:08 you may no longer have to be briefed by senior members.
6:11 You may be trusted and expected
6:13 to deal directly with clients
6:14 or take a brief from account handlers.
6:17 As you create your work, you'll still typically
6:19 have to report and show your work
6:21 to more senior members of the team
6:23 where you will be advised on amendments if necessary.
6:26 You will still be encouraged to learn,
6:28 shadow other senior designers
6:30 and help out with various design tasks.
6:32 The types of designers that typically apply
6:34 for middleweight designer roles
6:36 are graduates or those with a few years experience
6:39 in the industry.
6:40 As you undertake your role as middleweight designer,
6:43 over a few years you will learn even more
6:45 and refine your skills further.
6:48 Perhaps, even specialize in your area of expertise.
6:51 With more experience and skill, you'll become more senior.
6:55 If you are fortunate, your employer will recognize this
6:58 and reward you with an increase in salary
7:01 and a new official title of senior designer.
7:04 Though, unfortunately, this is not always the case.
7:07 The issue in some agencies is they will already have a team
7:10 of designers, many at different levels.
7:13 If you're taken on at a particular level
7:15 and spend a few years developing your skills
7:18 and believe you are worth more
7:19 and lacking creative opportunities,
7:21 it maybe the case there is no way for you to move up
7:24 in that agency as there are already designers
7:27 in those senior roles.
7:29 If this is the case, as mentioned earlier,
7:32 you may have to leave your current agency
7:34 and apply for a more senior role in another.
7:36 I was middleweight designer at an agency
7:39 for about two years.
7:40 When I realized there was no room to maneuver,
7:43 I decided to look for a new job.
7:45 To which, I had to move out to move up.
7:48 So, next, we have the senior designer.
7:51 So, the senior designer role comes with
7:53 even more responsibility and expectation.
7:56 Just like with the junior and middleweight role,
7:59 your main responsibilities will be to use your skills
8:02 and experience to produce creative work.
8:04 At this level, you will be expected to design and execute
8:07 your own ideas to a high standard.
8:10 At this level, you may also be in part responsible
8:14 for the development of a project
8:16 and oversee junior designers
8:18 and other creative professionals
8:20 working on a project such as, motion graphics designers,
8:23 photographers, web developers and so on.
8:26 As you create your work, you will still typically
8:28 have to report and show your work to more senior members
8:31 of the team like a creative director,
8:33 who may advise on amendments if necessary.
8:36 The type of designers that typically apply
8:38 for senior design roles
8:39 are those with years of experience in the industry,
8:42 have a high standard of work with good references.
8:45 Now, generally, the senior designer role
8:48 is the highest you can achieve
8:49 in the creative workforce category.
8:51 As you undertake your role as a senior designer,
8:54 over years of practice and experience,
8:56 you will learn even more and refine your skills further.
9:00 Perhaps, even specialize in your area of expertise.
9:04 The next step for a senior designer
9:06 is into a more director role.
9:08 Now, if you are fortunate enough,
9:09 your employer will recognize this
9:11 and reward you with an increase in salary
9:14 and an opportunity for a more senior direction role.
9:17 Though, if there are no opportunities,
9:19 you may have to consider leaving your current agency
9:22 and applying for a direction role in another agency.
9:25 So, those are the main levels that exist
9:28 in the workforce category.
9:29 Now, typically, the role of a junior, middleweight
9:32 and senior designer are the same,
9:34 to produce creative work.
9:36 The main difference is between them are the expectations
9:40 of experience, quality of work and speed.
9:43 As you become more senior, you may be encouraged
9:46 to oversee other members of a creative workforce
9:48 and make important creative decisions.
9:51 The more experience you get with this,
9:54 the more you will find yourself moving towards
9:56 a direction role.
9:57 So, next, we have the head of design or lead designer role.
10:01 In my experience, I have found that this role
10:04 is not particularly common to design agencies,
10:06 but more so in in-house design teams.
10:09 I have been to some agencies where this role exists
10:12 and to others where it does not.
10:14 And, where it does the head of design or lead designer
10:18 will typically be responsible for a creative workforce,
10:21 that deadlines are met
10:22 and to maintain overall creative standards.
10:25 In my experience, this role is like a creative director,
10:29 but with more administrative responsibilities.
10:31 The type of designers that become head of design
10:34 or lead designer are those with years of experience
10:37 in the industry,
10:38 leading projects and overseeing the workforce.
10:41 The head of design or lead designer
10:43 will distribute design tasks down through the workforce
10:47 as they see fit
10:49 and oversee and direct the team.
10:51 During the course of a project,
10:52 the creative workforce will submit ideas and designs
10:56 and the head or lead designer will direct accordingly,
11:00 make creative decisions and work together
11:02 with the workforce to refine
11:04 and produce a creative solution.
11:06 Like the creative workforce, the head of design
11:09 or lead designer will still be involved
11:11 in the creative process.
11:12 But, due to increased responsibility,
11:15 may need to rely more on the production from the workforce.
11:18 A good head of design or lead designer
11:21 will not see themselves as superior to the workforce,
11:23 only, perhaps, more experienced where they can guide
11:27 the skills, talents of the workforce to produce
11:30 the best results.
11:31 So, next, we have the art director role.
11:33 So, the art director is a common role in large agencies
11:38 to create work for magazines, newspapers or packaging.
11:41 The art director will typically be responsible
11:44 for a visual style and image
11:46 and report to senior members of the team.
11:49 Unlike the head of design or lead designer role,
11:52 the art director is generally not responsible
11:56 for the workforce or to meet any specific deadlines.
11:59 The art director is free of this sort of responsibility
12:02 and is often
12:03 out on location directing photo shoots or films.
12:06 It's up to the art director to create the overall design
12:11 and direct others in a creative workforce
12:13 to artwork or layout ideas.
12:15 As a creative solution is being developed,
12:18 the art director may have to report to senior members
12:21 such as the head of design, lead designer
12:24 or creative director for approval or guidance.
12:28 The type of designers that become art directors
12:30 are those with years of experience in the industry,
12:33 have a good eye for composition and style
12:36 and like to direct photo shoots for magazines or film.
12:39 So, next, we have the senior creative.
12:42 So, the senior creative role is usually found
12:45 in large, prestigious creative agencies.
12:48 The senior creative will typically be responsible
12:51 for the concept or idea for a project
12:53 and report to a creative director.
12:56 The senior creative will often develop ideas
12:59 and draw them as scamps
13:00 which are submitted to the creative director.
13:03 These could be magazine layouts, packaging design,
13:06 photo compositions, ad layouts and storyboards for a video.
13:10 If a concept or idea is agreed on,
13:12 the creative workforce will be called upon
13:14 to develop the idea.
13:16 If there is artwork or layout to be done,
13:18 the designers will do this.
13:20 If there is a photo shoot required,
13:22 the art directors will lead this.
13:24 As work develops, it will be overseen by the senior creative
13:28 and the creative director to ensure the creative vision
13:31 is being met into a high standard.
13:34 The type of designers that become senior creatives
13:37 are those with years of experience in the industry,
13:40 have good conceptual thinking,
13:41 are good at generating original ideas
13:44 and good at communicating those ideas.
13:46 So, the last role which is the highest role
13:49 you can get in the creative industry
13:50 is the creative director.
13:52 The creative director is responsible for using the talents
13:56 of the creative workforce to create a solution.
13:59 The creative director will work with designers, artists,
14:03 copywriters, sales teams and marketers
14:06 to create a vision for a project.
14:09 Where it is the role of the designers, creatives
14:12 and art directors to design and produce ideas,
14:15 it will be for the creative director
14:17 to shape these into finished campaigns.
14:19 To become a creative director,
14:21 is not just a natural progression of the designer.
14:24 It requires a lot of experience, skill and talent.
14:28 The type of designers that become creative directors
14:31 are those with many years of experience in the industry
14:35 and demonstrate the skills of all the previous roles.
14:38 They are visionaries.
14:40 They are very good at working with people.
14:42 Talking to people and selling an idea.
14:44 They will be good conceptual thinkers,
14:47 good at generating ideas
14:48 and good at communicating those ideas to an audience.
14:52 A good creative director will never see themselves
14:54 as superior to the workforce, only, perhaps,
14:57 more experience where they can guide the skills and talents
15:00 of the workforce to produce the best results.
15:03 So, those are some of the positions
15:05 in the graphic design industry
15:07 and some of the career options to aspire to.
15:10 If you would like to take a closer look
15:12 at the list discussed in this video,

0:59 In this video, I'm going to discuss some of the options
1:02 you can consider to become a pro graphic designer
1:04 and how to take your career in a new direction.

1:10 to progress your career.
1:12 Simply become better at your job.
1:15 In my experience, I have found that by simply being good
1:17 at your job, that can take you far.
1:20 There can be a lot of slackers in the industry,
1:22 some people who do not work as hard as others.
1:25 So, you need to become as good as you can,
1:27 become proficient, organized and methodical
1:29 with your design process.
1:31 It's these qualities that will get you noticed
1:33 and make you stand out from other designers,
1:35 and this will help you get promoted
1:37 or gain the experience and skills
1:39 to progress in your career.
1:41 And this brings me to the next thing you can do,
1:43 rise the ranks.
1:45 Now, in the last episode, I spoke about the design industry
1:48 and the common positions within it.
1:50 One of the things you can do is seek to climb that ladder.
1:54 As you become more experienced
1:55 and mature within the industry,
1:57 you'll be able to progress to more senior levels
2:00 and have the opportunity to be involved
2:02 or create more interesting work.
2:04 As mentioned in the previous point,
2:06 by bettering yourself at your job,
2:08 you will put yourself in a good position to rise up.
2:12 As you become a more senior designer,
2:14 you can consider looking to transition

2:18 or a creative directive or a senior creative.
2:22 Specialize, throughout this series,
2:25 I have said on many occasions, a graphic design
2:27 is a broad, creative job, and that is true,
2:31 but many famous and well known designers are often known
2:34 for a particular skill or style.
2:36 To stand out, you could consider specializing.
2:39 Now, this is typically something a designer does
2:41 after a few years in the industry.
2:44 After one discovers a particular interest they love
2:47 or they are particularly good at,
2:48 by specializing, you can claim to be an expert
2:51 in a particular field in graphic design.
2:54 This may close the door
2:55 to some more broader work opportunities,
2:58 but this could mean you can charge more for your skill
3:00 and produce more work in that field.
3:03 Now, there are lots of fields you could specialize in,
3:05 such as branding, typography, hand lettering, web design,
3:10 app design, and motion graphics, to name a few.
3:13 Network and use social media.
3:15 As a graphic designer, today, you will need a strategy
3:19 to network both offline and online.
3:21 A good way to network offline is to attend galleries,
3:24 exhibitions, and events.
3:26 By putting yourself out there,
3:28 you will inevitably meet like-minded people
3:31 and even potential employers or clients.
3:33 Here you can introduce yourself and exchange business cards.
3:37 To network online is to use social media.
3:40 Over the past few years, I have seen a big change
3:43 in the way we, as graphic designers,
3:45 look for design jobs and interact
3:47 with potential employers and clients.
3:49 Through the development of technology,
3:51 the internet, and social media,
3:52 I have seen a huge shift from searching offline
3:55 to searching online for jobs.
3:57 In my experience, I have gotten a few jobs
3:59 from simply having a profile online
4:02 and reaching out to people.
4:03 I have received calls and have been offered and taken work.
4:07 Be sure to be proactive online.
4:09 Set up a portfolio profile on as many design websites
4:12 as you can, and network through these sites.
4:15 Not only can you share your work,
4:17 but you can demonstrate your design process and thinking.
4:20 You can follow and communicate with design industries

4:24 Become freelance.

4:40 where they are extremely good at their job
4:41 and don't need to be tied to one particular agency.
4:45 As a freelancer, you can choose your own contracts,

4:51 Because of this, a lot of designers aspire to be freelance,

5:16 This comes with the challenge to find all their clients

5:21 and financial affairs.
5:23 Quite a lot of work involved there.

5:32 Once you are registered, your agents will know
5:34 what type of work you do, how good you are
5:36 and be able to pitch you for various contracts.
5:39 Unlike independent freelance,
5:41 here you can focus entirely on the job
5:43 where all your administration and financial affairs
5:46 are done by the agency.
5:48 But this does come with a cost.
5:50 So, after a few years in the industry,

2:25 and exercise creative skills,
2:27 which will ultimately feed back into your design work.
2:30 So some courses I would recommend are screen printing.
2:35 If you sit at a desk all day in front of a computer,
2:38 then screen printing can connect you
2:40 to the more traditional, hands-on
2:42 process of graphic design.
2:44 Undertaking this course will also give you
2:46 the opportunity to create some interesting
2:48 and alternative work you can include in your portfolio.
2:52 Photography, photography is not just
2:54 about how to use a camera,
2:56 it's about how we look at things and see things.

3:02 a vital skill to build on as a graphic designer.
3:05 Life drawing and painting.
3:07 As designers, we can never be too good at drawing.
3:10 Drawing and painting is the most pure
3:13 of visual communication.
3:14 Life drawing encourages us to practice
3:16 and become more confident at drawing.
3:18 It encourages us to see and observe.
3:21 Painting will help us bring to life our imagination.
3:25 Sculpture, as well as being fun and hands-on,
3:28 sculpture makes us create, design,
3:30 and think in three dimensions.
3:33 Software courses.

## Source: how-to-actually-think-like-a-graphic-designer.txt

How To Actually Think Like A Graphic Designer!

0:00 there are three core ways that top level and good graphic designers think like and you really do not
0:05 want to ignore these three different ways of thinking as a graphic designer because if you
0:09 do try and brush them off or avoid them you're not going to get very far in this industry and
0:13 here's the first thing that you might already have experienced a client might come to you
0:17 saying we need a website that feels fresh or we want a logo that stands out but still feels
0:22 professional now this is where your problem solving mindset should be kicking in and this
0:27 is the first core way of thinking as a designer and if you don't fully understand or know that
0:33 don't worry because right now we're going to look at some real life examples so Airbnb the
0:39 world-renowned travel platform it was struggling with the same issue their old logo just wasn't
0:45 cutting it and it looked like a mattress brand and this confused its target audience the problem
0:51 they faced was not just designing something quote unquote pretty but crafting a logo that
0:56 communicated a complex message and that was belonging how do you visually communicate
1:01 belonging when the designers began working on this they were probably asking themselves how
1:08 do we make something that doesn't just look cool but actually evokes a message this is the type
1:13 of problem solving that goes beyond Aesthetics and that's the challenge we as designers have
1:18 to rise up to so the designers started to break down what Airbnb really was about it wasn't just
1:26 about homes or renting properties it was about creating a space where any anyone from anywhere
1:30 in the world could feel like they belong this led them to develop the simple yet iconic a symbol
1:37 which is more than just an initial the design is actually multi-layered the a shape doubles
1:43 as a location pin where you are basically a heart which is belonging or love and even an
1:49 abstract person in some opinions each piece was intentionally designed to represent airbnb's
1:55 brand values and solve the communication issue so even if you're Ting your ability to communicate
2:01 visually remember that problem solving is just a process airbnb's logo didn't just magically
2:07 happen it took research trial error and a deep understanding of the message to get there and
2:14 that's what we as designers are here to do solve problems in a creative and meaningful
2:20 way for this approach you need to identify the problem within the breathe for the project and
2:31 then hash out some potential solutions for the problem or problems now if you can do this you
2:36 will have access to Concepts really quickly and let me show you how that actually looks now to
2:43 demonstrate how problem Sprints work check out this logo here now I'm sure only a handful of
2:49 people know what this logo is or shall I say was so this logo was actually the design for
2:55 Uber pre 2018 and if we look at this logo what problems can we identify on the design itself
3:03 firstly it looks more like an app icon and it's not very iconic and near the irony in
3:08 that wording but it has a lot of detail and it would become kind of lost at smaller scales it
3:13 also looks a bit generic and a bit outdated to be honest Uber really wanted to become an identified
3:19 brand away from just an app and so we have those problems and to create design ideas and Concepts
3:25 we just try and fix those problems in Uber's case the removed color and strip things back to black
3:31 and white for Simplicity modernity and sleekness they also removed all of the chaotic detail and
3:37 opted for a typographic logo again for Simplicity and brand recognition so for this first approach
3:44 simply identify problems and then create fixes for those problems and that will reveal Concepts and
3:50 ideas for your project so don't for a single second Overlook or ignore the power of design
3:55 thinking in terms of problem solving visually speaking it's such a core fundamental aspect
4:01 of being a graphic designer now I'm a busy man and a busy designer and I never learned how to
4:07 code so one way that I managed to actually create websites or marketing materials that actually look
4:12 good is by using framer one thing that helps me of saving time is the library of 700 plus templates
4:18 which gives us a lot of starting options so for example this 3D template is looking pretty dope
4:27 now like I said with framer everything is just so so quick and so easy even if you're someone
4:32 like myself who just doesn't want to mess around with code and I can make Social Links here way
4:37 more interesting compared to the static appearance they have now and I can actually come in and add a
4:42 hover effect here under the effects panel there are some settings to play around with but if
4:49 you come up and hit play here look how smooth and how neat this animation looks after the
4:54 fact but if you really want to draw attention to your ctas I like the loop effect which constantly
5:03 plays out on your site and this will surely drive those clicks now there are so many more features
5:12 more tools and different ways to create stunning websites with framer and if you do want to make
5:16 these cool websites these effective websites for framer do click the link down below and check out
5:21 framer today let me ask you something have you ever looked at a design and felt something stir
5:28 inside you per perhaps Joy Nostalgia maybe even sadness that emotional impact it isn't some kind
5:35 of fluke or accident not by a long shot let's take an example we're all familiar with and
5:40 that is Coca-Cola now I don't really care about the brand or the product so don't get caught up
5:44 in those narratives we're just looking at the brand through the lens of a designer on the
5:49 video why does the advertising always evoke feelings of Happiness celebration and warmth
5:56 it's not just the iconic red color although that does does help it's the whole visual
6:02 package the typography is playful and friendly evoking a sense of nostalgia and tradition the
6:09 imagery of people smiling sharing moments together this reinforces that Coca-Cola is
6:14 about the experience and togetherness it's not just a drink in their mind that's because the
6:20 design team who put this brand identity together actually used emotional impacts via storytelling
6:27 in a graphic design visual way and it is very very important color has an incredible ability
6:32 to influence people's mood think about movie posters horror films typically use dark Moody
6:39 colors with sharp contrast to create suspense and fear while romantic comedies often use warm
6:46 inviting tones to set a light-hearted happier mood as designers we need to ask ourselves
6:54 what emotions do we want to evoke and how can we use color typography and imagery to achieve that
7:01 when you approach a project start by asking what emotional response am I aiming for here
7:07 dive deep into the psychology of color explore how typography choices can change the tone of
7:12 a message and pick imagery that reinforces the emotion you want to evoke that's how you
7:18 become not just a designer but a Storyteller if you've ever doubted your ability to connect to
7:25 an audience emotionally with your designs know that it's actually a learnable skill
7:30 look at every piece of design as an opportunity to tell a story and Spark an emotional connection and
7:36 that's the magic of visual storytelling in graphic design and when you do actually tap into it your
7:42 designs won't just speak they will move people now let's be honest here have you ever just slapped
7:48 some color onto your projects applied a couple of fonts and said okay that's the end that's the
7:53 project finished it's easy to do especially when deadlines Loom just over on the horizon but here's
8:00 the hard truth without intention behind every design element your work can easily fall flat and
8:07 if that happens you risk missing the mark entirely with your audience and your client every good
8:13 designer thinks in terms of every design Choice must have a purpose maybe you felt that doubt
8:20 creep in am I just making this look good or is there more to it that uncertainty can weigh heavy
8:27 on your mind as a designer but here's the key thing every single element in your design should
8:33 have a purpose nothing should be random no color should be applied just because you like that color
8:40 and no line should be drawn without considering how that impacts the final message of the design
8:45 think about the Nike logo it's more than just a swoosh right it's Sleek it's fast and it's Dynamic
8:53 the curved forward moving shape reinforces the Brand's message of Athletics speed and Victory
8:59 there's purpose in that simple design right there the logo isn't just visually appealing it actually
9:05 kind of tells a story if you think about it now you might be wondering how do I design with that
9:11 kind of intention behind it what does it even mean to have purpose in every single element well that
9:18 curiosity is exactly what's going to push you forward as a designer start asking questions
9:24 like why this color what does this symbolize why use this shape how does it direct the eye
9:31 or support the Brand's message why should I use this type face does it feel bold delicate
9:37 traditional or modern asking all these questions will help you further in your projects and take
9:43 color for example if you're designing a brand identity for a children's toy company you wouldn't
9:48 use muted Grays or blacks you'd lean towards bright playful Hues because those colors are
9:55 Vogue energy excitement and fun they speak to the audience and that's why intentional design is all
10:01 about communication every decision you make in the design process should be geared towards a purpose
10:08 and once you start designing with intention everything just becomes clearer you'll feel more
10:13 confident in your choices and your designs will start to resonate more deeply with your audiences
10:18 and don't get it twisted you're not designing for your client you are designing for your client's
10:22 audience and so remember it's not just about making things look pretty it's about making them
10:27 matter when you design with purpose every element becomes a piece of the puzzle working together to
10:33 deliver the right message now if you can actually approach design projects and graphic design in
10:38 general when considering these three different ways of thinking not only are you going to see
10:42 your work improve but you will be miles ahead of most designers out there while most people just
10:48 consume almost useless reals about a Photoshop tool you will have gained some deep and advanced
10:53 knowledge of how to approach actual design projects at the level of top tier agencies but
10:59 yeah don't forget that and if you want to continue your education as a graphic designer today just
11:04 click one of those videos on screen but until next time guys design your future today peace

# App Design and UX

> **Category Description:** UI/UX design principles, addictive app psychology, gamification strategies, and design techniques that make apps stand out. Covers everything from beginner mistakes to senior-level design patterns, plus the psychology behind habit-forming apps.

---

## Table of Contents

- 1. 4 levels of UI UX design (and BIG mistakes to avoid)
- 2. Every UI UX Concept Explained in Under 10 Minutes
- 3. How I Design Apps 10x Better (Free Course)
- 4. Designing Addiction  The Twisted Psychology Of Game Design
- 5. How Addictive Apps Are Made
- 6. How Apps Are Designed to Keep YOU ADDICTED
- 7. The Secret Behind Weirdly Addictive Apps
- 8. The Weird Science of Apps You Can't Stop Using
- 9. How to Make Learning as Addictive as Social Media   Duolingo's Luis Von Ahn   TED


---

## 1. 4 levels of UI UX design (and BIG mistakes to avoid)

**Source File:** `VideoTranscriber_4 levels of UI_UX design (and BIG mistakes to avoid).txt` | **Size:** 12.7 KB

I've designed apps and websites for over a decade. And when I look back at my own design journey, one thing is clear. There are patterns to the kinds of mistakes you make as a designer at every level of your career. In this video, I will highlight those patterns of mistakes, what they are, when they typically happen, and how to fix them so that you can reach your next design level faster. Here's how we'll do it. We will take a screen from a real client project in my agency portfolio and redesign it starting from an unpaid beginner level, iterating all the way to a high ticket senior level. For each level, we will call out the biggest mistakes while providing resources and guidelines so that you can avoid them.

And then by the end of the video, we'll dig into a hidden mistake that even lots of senior designers make and a solution that can really set you apart in this AI era that we're in. Let's get started. So, at the beginner level, nothing works. However, this isn't a surprise since we have less than a year of experience and we're not getting paid for our work yet. But let's dive into why it doesn't work. The first mistake in this design is something as simple as the copyrightiting. For example, do we really need four words to communicate a user's rewards? And does it make sense for the button to be about earning tokens when the action is actually about claiming voting rewards?

Clarity is key in copyrightiting, and we're not really nailing it here. Therefore, it's a fail on the first principle, which brings us to the visuals. Lots of beginners will overuse flashy gradients, blur effects, and other cool visual techniques like that. And to be fair, even mid-level designers do this, but the difference is that they make it work. More on that later. it's a fail. But what about the colors? First off, we're using too much of a very strong red color, which makes the UI straight up stressful to look at. It's called the eightoint grid system. The main idea is that anytime you use a number in your design, make it divisible by 8 or 4. Instead of 25 pixels, use 24. Instead of 11, use 12. I actually have a full video on this that you can check out after this one linked down in the description below. Anyways, unfortunately, we fail here as well. That's six out of six fails. And usually copywriting is still a big issue at this level. We still add unnecessary words and write labels that don't really make sense. ## 2. Every UI UX Concept Explained in Under 10 Minutes

**Source File:** `VideoTranscriber_Every UI_UX Concept Explained in Under 10 Minutes.txt` | **Size:** 10.2 KB

If you want to get good at UIX design,   you got to start with the basics. So,   let's begin.   This one's easiest to explain with an   example first. If I have three text and   icon pairs, but I put a container around   drinks and food, you know that they're   related and dessert is not. Then, if I   put a container around drinks, you know   that it's selected and you can toggle   over to food.

 And if I gray this text   out, you know that it's inactive and   clicking it probably won't do anything.   See how you just knew all of that? I   didn't need to write instructions on how   it worked because the UI was signifying   how things worked. Good UI has many   signifiers like a button press state,   highlights on active nav items, hover   states, or even tool tips to tell the   user what a UI affords or what it can   do.

  Now, take a look at this card. It   displays all of the information in a   logical, repeatable format, but it looks   like a spreadsheet, not a design. So,   let's give it some hierarchy, which is   pretty intuitive. We have size,   position, and color to use to our   advantage. Starting from the top, an   image always adds a great pop of color   and makes scanning super easy.

 Just like   Uber, if in this case the item is the   most important, we want that large and   bold and at the top because otherwise   it'll blend in with something like the   time and day, which can be below and   much smaller. And it's this contrast,   the difference between small and big, or   colorful and not that actually creates   the hierarchy.

 It's also why price   should be at the top, right aligned, and   blue. It's different than the rest,   which draws the eye to it. The location   should be smaller and below like this.   But instead of just from and to, we can   use icons and a line to demonstrate that   it's moving from Jamesville to Syracuse   without having to actually say that.

  Now, with that said, it's not an exact   science, and you could definitely end up   with a design that looks like this   instead. And it wouldn't be wrong, but   the same ideas apply. Most important   things near the top, bigger and colorful   is more important, and images are used   whenever possible.

 And as you start to   design more and more, you'll notice that   similar patterns arise when you need   labels or buttons, which we're going to   talk about in a minute.   Now, some people have this misconception   that all of your content needs to align   to a 12 column grid and be exactly 8   pixels apart, but these are just   guidelines.

 For example, this design   doesn't line up with any of the columns,   and that's not uncommon, especially for   custom landing pages. But for highly   structured pages like galleries, blogs   or any repeating content, they can   actually be useful for responsiveness by   giving guidelines for responsive   behavior on tablet with eight columns   and mobile with four.

 But much more   important than using excessive grids and   layouts is whites space. Letting things   breathe. For example, in a simple   section like this, the large text would   have a font size and line height like   this, and the subtext would have a font   size and line height like so. buttons   would be similar and optionally a top   announcement bar like this.

 Then simply   32 pixels between every item, but you   can also group elements that just go   together like the announcement and text   or the text and subtext which is just   another form of visual hierarchy. And   this is where we see the fourpoint grid   system actually come into play.   Everything is a multiple, not because it   inherently looks better, but because you   can always split things in half, which   creates consistency throughout a design.

  Typography is really interesting because   design is mostly just text. And for   picking a font, I can almost   unilaterally say you'll never need more   than one for any design. So find a nice   sans sarah font like the ones on screen   and stick to it. Over the last 7 years   of designing, I've rarely used any fonts   other than what's on screen, and this   shouldn't be where you're spending tons   of time.

 I'm also not a big fan of   calling any design skill a hack, but   this one kind of is. This design looks   okay right now, but our header text   feels a little too loose. If you tighten   up the letter spacing on this by about   -2 tog -3% and drop the line height to   about 110 to 120%, it instantly makes   any larger text look super pro real   fast.

 But hierarchy with typography is a   little bit more nuanced. And if we look   at our previous example, text sizes can   vary wildly. For landing pages and   websites, you generally don't want more   than six font sizes, which can have a   really large range. For things like   dashboards, that range shrinks   dramatically to where you don't normally   have text sizes larger than 24 pixels   because of the increase in information   density.

 But honestly, one of the most   important concepts of design is getting   inspiration and doing research from a   good source like today's sponsor, Mobin,   who curates hundreds of thousands of   mobile and web apps along with landing   pages, too. So, you can always pull   inspiration from real designs built by   the top design teams in the world.

 And   if you're struggling with a specific   section, just go and search for it and   instantly pull up real world examples   with links to the live sites, too. If   you're interested, you can check out   Mobin using the first link down below to   get 20% off. Now, back to the video.   Colors are difficult and everyone has   their own tastes, but I'm a big fan of   starting with one primary color.

  Generally, this is your brand color.   Then you can lighten it for a good   background or darken it for text colors.   Both good ways to incorporate subtle   color and make an otherwise drab design   look much more interesting. And with all   of that, we're already halfway to a   color ramp, which is what large   companies use to build out chips,   states, charts, and really anything   else.

 But I also think it's important as   a beginner to let the color find you.   Like an announcement bar to grab   attention or a focus state on an input   or a green new chip on a link. These are   all semantic colors, which are colors   that have meaning and provide signifiers   to the user. Blue for trust, red for   danger or urgency, yellow for warning,   and green for success.

 Make sure you use   color for purpose, not just for   decoration.   Now, designing in dark mode presents   some pretty interesting challenges for   us. First, this card is using a light   border, which creates a bit too much   contrast, so we'll lower that down. In   dark mode, we don't have shadows like   light mode.

 So, to create depth, we need   to have a lighter card than the   background. So, I'll make that   adjustment. This chip is also far too   bright, so we can dim down the   saturation and brightness and flip that   for the text to create some hierarchy.   There's also a ton of flexibility for   deep purples, reds, or greens, not just   navy blue or gray.

  But speaking of depth, shadows are a   fantastic tool to use on light mode.   This shadow, along with most, is too   strong. So reducing the opacity and   dialing up the blur helps a lot. The   strength you'll need depends on the   foreground and background, but cards   require less, while content that sits   above other content, like popovers, will   need stronger shadows.

 We can even use   these techniques with inner and outer   shadows to create effects like raised   tactile buttons. Just remember, if the   shadow is the first thing you notice on   a design, you're not using it right   now. To create strong designs, you'll   need icons. Most icons are generally too   large.

 So, the trick is to get the line   height of your font, in this case 24   pixels, and make the icons the same   size. And then tighten up the text.   Sidebar links like this are actually   just buttons without a background until   you hover, which are often called ghost   buttons. And if we isolate one and   center it, we've got a perfect   standalone button.

 These are super   common when you have a primary and   secondary CTA side by side. And a good   guideline for padding on these is to   double the height for the width. And   these can be done with or without icons,   too.   A good rule of design is when a user   does anything, there should be a   response. For example, every button   needs at least four states: default,   hovered, active, or pressed, and   disabled.

 Sometimes you'll need a   loading state, too, with a spinner.   Inputs are even more critical. You'll   need a focus state when the user clicks   in, error states with red borders, and   messages when something's wrong, and   sometimes even warning states for   optional issues. And this applies   everywhere. loading spinners when data   is fetching, success messages when an   action completes, even micro animations   on scroll or swipe.

 Every interaction   needs a response.   And that brings us wonderfully into   micro interactions. Micro interactions   are a form of feedback, but they set   things up a notch. For example, this   copy button has states and feedback when   we hover and click, but we still don't   know that we've copied anything.   Instead, if we have this chip slide up,   that's a micro interaction that confirms   our action.

 And these can range from   highly practical to much more fun and   playful.   Finally, overlays are really important   because if you screw them up, you're   going to ruin the image and the text,   too. In this case, we can't leave it as   is. So, we could add in a full screen   overlay, but it doesn't do the image   justice.

 So instead add in a linear   gradient that'll display the image then   smoothly convert into a text readable   background. Or if we're going for extra   fancy we can add in a progressive blur   on top of the gradient for an even more   modern look. And that is more or less   every UIUX concept all wrapped up in 10   minutes.

 If you want to check out Mobin,   it will be the first link down below and   you can get the link to all of the Figma   assets used in this video down there   too. Thank you so much for watching and   I will see you in the next one.

Um and what's cool about   having mascots and using AI you can make   almost infinite generations and   iterations on the same mascot.

 So here I   took Lily the ghost and I made a   variation where she has a magnifying   glass and she's searching. So you can   kind of see how in your app even an   empty state. But my pro tip for anyone who's   doing this is actually to bring on or   hire an actual artist to generate the   first version or some sort of concept   and then use that and feed it into   ChachiPT or whatever you're using to   generate as a reference and say, "Hey,   can you kind of riff off of this style?"   And that's actually what I did to   generate Lily the Ghost. I didn't just   directly say, "Can you make a ghost   mascot with a flower on her head?" Not,   I didn't do that. Instead, what I did   was I fed in this, this was a handdrawn   illustration that my fiance did of our   dog Luna, and I told it, "Okay, can you   use this as a reference and now make me   a ghost based off of this?"  So, I'll give you an   example. This is my budgeting app, Luna.   This is the best way to illustrate this.   Um, look at the tabs on the bottom. I   chose specifically lined icons, which   means that they're not filled. These are   filled icons on the right here. Um,   there is a difference between these two   and it's very subjective.

 Some people   would prefer the left, some people would   prefer the right, but the key rule   though that I see a lot of apps making   is they love to mix and match these   things unnecessarily. So, here's on the   right where we mix lined and filled   icons. So, this is a lined icon and then   the others are filled icons.

## 4. Designing Addiction  The Twisted Psychology Of Game Design

**Source File:** `VideoTranscriber_Designing Addiction_ The Twisted Psychology Of Game Design.txt` | **Size:** 17.5 KB
